<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Attempting:**
> 1. In **big-step semantics**, students frequently confuse $\Downarrow$ (final state) with the *intermediate* state $\sigma'$.
> 2. In **assignment axiom**, the *substitution* $Q[x \mapsto a]$ must replace **every free occurrence** of $x$ in $Q$, not just the first.
> 3. In **loop semantics**, omitting the **invariant** loses 4–5 marks immediately — the invariant is the heart of the proof.
> 4. In **denotational semantics**, writing $\mathbf{fix}\,F$ without naming the function $F$ explicitly is treated as an incomplete answer.
> 5. Mixing up **partial** vs **total correctness** costs 2 marks; the question almost always specifies one.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between Operational Semantics and Denotational Semantics. Mention one key advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Operational Semantics | Denotational Semantics |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Meaning defined as | Sequence of computational steps ($\longrightarrow$) | Mathematical function ($\mathcal{C}\llbracket c \rrbracket$) |
| Style | Machine-like | Abstract, compositional |
| **Key advantage** | Models intermediate states, useful for interpreters and debuggers | Highly compositional; supports formal reasoning about program equivalence and refinement |

*(Examiner's note: A clear two-row table with one correct advantage per side = full 3 marks. Vague answers citing "precision" or "rigour" without specifics = 1 mark only.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
**State the Hoare assignment axiom. What does the notation $Q[x \mapsto a]$ signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Hoare Assignment Axiom** states:

$$
\{Q[x \mapsto a]\}\ \ x := a\ \ \{Q\}
$$

The substitution $Q[x \mapsto a]$ denotes the assertion obtained by **replacing every free occurrence of the variable $x$ in the postcondition $Q$ with the expression $a$**.

**Worked example:** Given $\{?\}\ x := x + 1\ \{x > 0\}$, the precondition is $Q[x \mapsto a] = (x > 0)[x \mapsto x+1] = (x+1 > 0)$.

*(Examiner's note: For full 3 marks, the candidate must (i) write the axiom, (ii) define the substitution, and (iii) optionally show a small example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice Pattern]:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]**
For the `While` language, **write the small-step operational semantics rules for the `while` construct and the `if` construct.** Use them to **trace the execution** of the following program on the initial state $\sigma_0 = [x \mapsto 2,\ y \mapsto 5]$, showing every transition step.

$$
c \;\equiv\; \texttt{if}\ (x < y)\ \texttt{then}\ (x := x + 1;\ y := y - 1)\ \texttt{else}\ (x := 0)
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the rules** (write clearly, 2 marks):

$$
\dfrac{\mathcal{B}\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \texttt{true}}{\langle \texttt{if}\ b\ \texttt{then}\ c_1\ \texttt{else}\ c_2,\ \sigma \rangle \longrightarrow \langle c_1,\ \sigma \rangle}
$$

$$
\dfrac{\mathcal{B}\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \texttt{false}}{\langle \texttt{if}\ b\ \texttt{then}\ c_1\ \texttt{else}\ c_2,\ \sigma \rangle \longrightarrow \langle c_2,\ \sigma \rangle}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the guard** $b = (x < y)$ in $\sigma_0$ (1 mark):

$$
\mathcal{B}\llbracket x < y \rrbracket \sigma_0 = (2 < 5) = \texttt{true}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply if-true rule** (1 mark):

$$
\langle \texttt{if}\ (x < y)\ \texttt{then}\ c_1\ \texttt{else}\ c_2,\ \sigma_0 \rangle \longrightarrow \langle x := x + 1;\ y := y - 1,\ \sigma_0 \rangle
$$

**Step 4 — Execute $x := x + 1$** (1 mark):

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_0[x \mapsto 3] = [x \mapsto 3,\ y \mapsto 5]
$$

**Step 5 — Execute $y := y - 1$** (1 mark):

$$
\sigma_2 = \sigma_1[y \mapsto 4] = [x \mapsto 3,\ y \mapsto 4]
$$

**Step 6 — Final state** (1 mark):

$$
\langle c,\ \sigma_0 \rangle \longrightarrow^{*} \sigma_2 = [x \mapsto 3,\ y \mapsto 4]
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]**
**Define denotational semantics for the `While` language.** Write the semantic equation for each of: `skip`, assignment, sequential composition, and the `while` loop. Explain the role of the **least fixed point** in defining the meaning of `while`.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Denotational semantics assigns to each command $c$ a function $\mathcal{C}\llbracket c \rrbracket : \Sigma \to \Sigma_\perp$ mapping an initial state to either a final state or the undefined value $\perp$ (representing non-termination).

**Semantic equations** (3 marks — 1 each for `skip`, assignment, composition, `while`):

$$
\mathcal{C}\llbracket \texttt{skip} \rrbracket = \lambda\sigma.\ \sigma \quad \text{(identity)}
$$

$$
\mathcal{C}\llbracket x := a \rrbracket = \lambda\sigma.\ \sigma[x \mapsto \mathcal{A}\llbracket a \rrbracket \sigma] \quad \text{(state update)}
$$

$$
\mathcal{C}\llbracket c_1;\ c_2 \rrbracket = \mathcal{C}\llbracket c_2 \rrbracket \circ \mathcal{C}\llbracket c_1 \rrbracket \quad \text{(function composition)}
$$

$$
\mathcal{C}\llbracket \texttt{while}\ b\ \texttt{do}\ c \rrbracket = \mathbf{fix}\, F, \ \text{where } F(g) = \lambda\sigma.\ \text{if}\ \mathcal{B}\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma\ \text{then}\ g(\mathcal{C}\llbracket c \rrbracket \sigma)\ \text{else}\ \sigma
$$

**Role of the least fixed point** (2 marks): The function $F$ is *monotone* on the complete lattice $(\Sigma \to \Sigma_\perp,\ \sqsubseteq)$. By **Kleene's Fixed-Point Theorem**, the iterates $F^{0}(\bot),\ F^{1}(\bot),\ F^{2}(\bot),\ \dots$ form an increasing chain whose least upper bound is the **least fixed point** $\mathbf{fix}\,F$. This is the *mathematical* meaning of an infinite loop: the limit of all its finite unrollings. Choosing the *least* fixed point (not just any fixed point) guarantees that $\bot$ — non-termination — is included wherever the loop may diverge.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model]**
**State and explain the Hoare-Logic rules for:**
**(i)** the assignment statement, **(ii)** the if-then-else construct, and **(iii)** the while loop. Identify the role of the **loop invariant**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Assignment Axiom (1.5 marks):**
$$
\{Q[x \mapsto a]\}\ \ x := a\ \ \{Q\}
$$
*The precondition is the postcondition with $x$ replaced by $a$.* **Example:** For $\{y = 5\}\ x := y\ \{x = 5\}$, the substitution is $Q[x \mapsto y] = (y = 5)$, matching the precondition.

**(ii) If-Rule (1.5 marks):**
$$
\dfrac{\{P \wedge b\}\ c_1\ \{Q\}, \quad \{P \wedge \neg b\}\ c_2\ \{Q\}}{\{P\}\ \texttt{if}\ b\ \texttt{then}\ c_1\ \texttt{else}\ c_2\ \{Q\}}
$$
*Splits the proof into two sub-proofs — one for each branch.*

**(iii) While-Rule (2 marks):**
$$
\dfrac{\{P \wedge b\}\ c\ \{P\}}{\{P\}\ \texttt{while}\ b\ \texttt{do}\ c\ \{P \wedge \neg b\}}
$$
*Where $P$ is the loop invariant.* On loop exit, the invariant still holds, but $b$ is false.

**Role of the loop invariant (2 marks):**
The loop invariant $P$ is an assertion that:
- holds *before* the loop begins (base case);
- is *preserved* by one iteration of the body (inductive step: $\{P \wedge b\}\ c\ \{P\}$);
- *combined with $\neg b$* yields the desired postcondition.

It is the *inductive hypothesis* of the loop's correctness proof. **Discovering the right invariant is the central intellectual challenge** of axiomatic verification.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model]**
**Prove the following Hoare triple using the rules of Hoare logic:**

$$
\{x = 5\}\ \ \texttt{y} := \texttt{x};\ \texttt{x} := \texttt{x} + 1 \ \ \ \{x = 6 \wedge y = 5\}
$$

**Model Solution:**

We work **backwards** from the postcondition.

**Step 1 — Strip the last assignment** $x := x + 1$. Apply the assignment axiom in reverse. To obtain $\{x = 6 \wedge y = 5\}$ after $x := x + 1$, we need:

$$
\{(x = 6 \wedge y = 5)[x \mapsto x + 1]\} = \{x + 1 = 6 \wedge y = 5\} = \{x = 5 \wedge y = 5\}
$$

**Step 2 — Strip the first assignment** $y := x$. To obtain $\{x = 5 \wedge y = 5\}$ after $y := x$, we need:

$$
\{(x = 5 \wedge y = 5)[y \mapsto x]\} = \{x = 5 \wedge x = 5\} = \{x = 5\}
$$

This matches the given precondition.

**Step 3 — Forward verification tree** (3 marks for the explicit tree):

$$
\dfrac{\{x = 5\}\ \texttt{y} := \texttt{x}\ \{x = 5 \wedge y = 5\}, \quad \dfrac{\{x = 5 \wedge y = 5\}\ \texttt{x} := \texttt{x} + 1\ \{x = 6 \wedge y = 5\}}{\{x = 5\}\ \texttt{y} := \texttt{x};\ \texttt{x} := \texttt{x} + 1\ \{x = 6 \wedge y = 5\}}
$$

The right leaf is justified by the assignment axiom since the precondition is exactly $Q[x \mapsto x+1]$ with $Q = (x = 6 \wedge y = 5)$.

**Final answer:** The triple is **valid** (provable in Hoare logic). ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 – PEBMT746, Module 1):**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Program Semantics** = mathematical study of what programs *mean* (as opposed to syntax = what they *look like*).
- ☐ The three styles are **Operational, Denotational, Axiomatic** — know the one-line definition of each.
- ☐ **State** $\sigma : \text{Var} \to \mathbb{Z}$ — function from variables to integer values.

### Operational Semantics
- ☐ **Small-step:** $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \longrightarrow \gamma$ — one atomic step.
- ☐ **Big-step:** $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma'$ — start to finish in one inference.
- ☐ Be able to **trace** a program of ~5 lines step-by-step (assignment, sequence, if, while).
- ☐ For a `while` loop that terminates, SOS traces a *finite* chain; for non-termination, the chain is *infinite*.

### Denotational Semantics
- ☐ $\mathcal{C}\llbracket c \rrbracket$ is a function $\Sigma \to \Sigma_\perp$.
- ☐ Sequence = **function composition** ($c_2$ *after* $c_1$).
- ☐ `while` = **least fixed point** $\mathbf{fix}\,F$ — exists by **Kleene's theorem** on monotone maps over a complete lattice.
- ☐ Compositionality is the *defining feature* of denotational semantics.

### Axiomatic Semantics
- ☐ **Hoare triple** $\{P\}\ c\ \{Q\}$: precondition, command, postcondition.
- ☐ **Partial correctness** (default) vs **Total correctness** $[P]\ c\ [Q]$ — total adds termination.
- ☐ **Assignment axiom:** $\{Q[x \mapsto a]\}\ x := a\ \{Q\}$.
- ☐ **While rule:** $\{P \wedge b\}\ c\ \{P\}$ over $P\ \texttt{while}\ b\ \texttt{do}\ c\ \{P \wedge \neg b\}$.
- ☐ **Loop invariant** $P$ is the *inductive hypothesis* of the loop's correctness proof.
- ☐ Be able to **verify** simple programs (one loop, 1–2 assignments) end-to-end.

### Comparative Knowledge
- ☐ Operational is **good for execution**, denotational is **good for equivalence**, axiomatic is **good for verification**.
- ☐ Know the originators: **Plotkin (SOS)**, **Scott & Strachey (denotational)**, **Hoare & Floyd (axiomatic)**.
- ☐ Real-world tools: **Coq, Isabelle, Dafny** (axiomatic); **abstract interpretation in Astrée, Infer** (denotational-style); **CEK/Krivine machines** (operational).

### Exam Pattern (KTU 2024)
- ☐ Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (definitions, short comparisons).
- ☐ Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice between two complete 7+7 questions).
- ☐ **Total Module 1 weightage:** ~15–20% of the course.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Introduction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Context**
> Module 2 of *Advanced Computer Programming Techniques* transitions the learner from elementary language constructs to **generic, type-safe, and reusable** programming paradigms. This introductory lesson establishes the conceptual groundwork for the rest of the module.

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> **Module 2 — Core Focus:**
> *Generic Programming, Templates, the Standard Template Library (STL), Exception Handling, and Advanced File I/O Streams.*

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, **Module 2** focuses on elevating a programmer's ability to write **type-independent**, **reusable**, and **robust** code. Instead of writing separate functions or classes for every data type, advanced programming techniques leverage **parametric polymorphism** (templates) and **pre-validated library containers** (STL) to build scalable software systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> The primary cognitive objective of this module (mapped to **CO2 / CO3** in the KTU 2024 outcome-based framework) is for students to *design, implement, and debug* programs using advanced constructs that satisfy **industry-grade reusability and reliability** standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Control"

Imagine you own a **Universal Remote Control** at home.

| Approach | Real-World Mapping | Programming Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| One remote per device (TV, AC, Soundbar) | Duplicate code per data type | Function overloading / copy-paste logic |
| **One universal remote for ALL devices** | **Single code, works with many types** | **Generic Programming (Templates)** |

The Universal Remote does not know in advance whether it is controlling a TV or an AC. It just knows the *pattern* of commands (volume up, power off). When you "bind" it to a specific device, it adapts. This is exactly how **templates** function — the logic is written once, and the compiler binds it to the actual data type during compilation.

## 1.3 Why This Module Matters in KTU 2024

The 2024 NEP-aligned scheme places heavy emphasis on **outcome-based education (OBE)**. Module 2 directly maps to these outcomes:

- **CO2:** *Apply advanced programming constructs to solve complex computational problems.*
- **CO3:** *Develop reusable, type-safe, and efficient software modules using standard libraries.*

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Questions from this module frequently test whether you can **justify design choices** (e.g., *Why use a `vector` over an array?*) — not just write syntax. Always include the **"why"** in your answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Module 2

The module is structurally built on **three interrelated pillars**. Mastering these is mandatory for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 🔹 Pillar 1 — Generic Programming (Templates)

**Definition:** A programming paradigm where algorithms are written in terms of **types-to-be-specified-later**. The type is supplied as a **parameter** at compile time.

**Why it exists:**
- Eliminates code duplication.
- Enforces **type safety** at compile time (no runtime overhead).
- Enables **parametric polymorphism**.

**Two forms in KTU syllabus:**
- **Function Templates** — generic functions.
- **Class Templates** — generic classes (e.g., a generic `Stack<T>`).

### 🔹 Pillar 2 — Standard Template Library (STL)

The STL is a collection of **ready-made, template-based classes and functions** that provide:
1. **Containers** — data holders (`vector`, `list`, `map`, `set`, `stack`, `queue`).
2. **Iterators** — generalized pointers to traverse containers.
3. **Algorithms** — pre-written procedures (`sort`, `find`, `count`, `transform`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core STL Concept:** *Containers store data, Iterators traverse it, Algorithms process it.* These three are decoupled — any algorithm works with any container that supplies a compatible iterator. This is the **Decoupling Principle**.

### 🔹 Pillar 3 — Exception Handling & Advanced Streams

- **Exception Handling:** A structured mechanism (`try`, `catch`, `throw`) to detect and recover from runtime errors without crashing the program.
- **Advanced Streams:** Manipulators (`setw`, `setprecision`), file stream objects (`ifstream`, `ofstream`, `fstream`), and state flags (`eof()`, `fail()`).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Purpose | Time Complexity (Big-O) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Function Template | `template <typename T> T add(T a, T b)` | Generic function | $O(1)$ per call |
| Class Template | `template <class T> class Stack { T data[N]; };` | Generic class | Varies |
| `vector<T>` | `vector<int> v;` | Dynamic array | Access: $O(1)$, Insert end: $O(1)$ amortized |
| `list<T>` | Doubly linked list | Insert/erase anywhere: $O(1)$ | Access: $O(n)$ |
| `map<K,V>` | Self-balancing BST (typically Red-Black) | Key-value pairs | Insert/Search: $O(\log n)$ |
| `set<T>` | Unique sorted elements | Membership test | Insert/Search: $O(\log n)$ |
| Iterators | `vector<int>::iterator it = v.begin();` | Traversal | $O(1)$ to advance |
| `try-catch` | `try { ... } catch (T& e) { ... }` | Error handling | $O(1)$ dispatch |
| Manipulator | `cout << setw(10) << x;` | Formatted I/O | $O(1)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Rule:** When writing absolute value or set notation in your exam answer, always use the typed form — for example, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\{\,x \mid x > 0\,\}$ — *never* the bare `|` symbol, as it conflicts with KTU's electronic answer-sheet parser used in some internal assessments.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Application of Module 2 Concepts |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | Templates to write drivers for any sensor data type. |
| **High-Frequency Trading** | `vector` + custom allocators for ultra-low-latency storage. |
| **Compiler Design** | Exception handling for symbol-table error recovery. |
| **Game Development** | STL containers for entity management (players, bullets, particles). |
| **Database Engines** | `map` / `set` for B-tree-like indexing structures. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Power of Templates — From Concrete to Generic

### Step 1: The Problem — Code Duplication

Suppose we want a function that returns the maximum of two numbers. Without templates, we would have to write it for **every** type.

```cpp
int maxInt(int a, int b) { return (a > b) ? a : b; }
float maxFloat(float a, float b) { return (a > b) ? a : b; }
double maxDouble(double a, double b) { return (a > b) ? a : b; }
```

The logic is **identical** — only the type changes. This violates the **DRY principle** (*Don't Repeat Yourself*).

### Step 2: The Template Solution

We introduce a **type parameter** `T` to abstract the data type.

$$T \; \text{maxGeneric}(T \; a, \; T \; b) \; = \; (a > b) \;\text{?} \; a \; \text{:} \; b$$

The full C++ syntax:

```cpp
template <typename T>
T maxGeneric(T a, T b) {
    return (a > b) ? a : b;
}
```

### Step 3: How the Compiler Generates Code (Instantiation)

When you call `maxGeneric(5, 10)`, the compiler performs **template instantiation**:

$$\text{Call: } \texttt{maxGeneric(5, 10)} \;\Longrightarrow\; T \;\text{is bound to } \text{int} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Compiler generates } \texttt{maxInt}$$

$$\text{Call: } \texttt{maxGeneric(3.5, 2.1)} \;\Longrightarrow\; T \;\text{is bound to } \text{double} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Compiler generates } \texttt{maxDouble}$$

The reasoning, expressed as a derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Template Source} &\xrightarrow{\text{Type Binding}} \text{Concrete Source} \\
\texttt{template <typename T>} &\xrightarrow{T \leftarrow \text{int}} \texttt{int maxGeneric(int a, int b)} \\
&\xrightarrow{T \leftarrow \text{double}} \texttt{double maxGeneric(double a, double b)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is why templates are called **"blueprints"** — one blueprint, many buildings.

### Step 4: Class Template Derivation — Generic Stack

A stack works the same way regardless of what it stores. We derive the structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State:} \quad & S = \{(d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n) \mid n \le N\} \\
\text{Operations:} \quad & \\
&\text{push}(S, x) \rightarrow S' = S \cup \{x\}, \quad |S'| \le N \\
&\text{pop}(S) \rightarrow S' = S \setminus \{d_n\}, \quad \text{if } S \neq \emptyset \\
&\text{top}(S) \rightarrow d_n, \quad \text{if } S \neq \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

Where each $d_i$ belongs to **any** type $T$. The C++ realization:

```cpp
template <class T>
class Stack {
    T data[100];
    int topIndex;
public:
    Stack() : topIndex(-1) {}
    void push(T x) { data[++topIndex] = x; }
    T pop() { return data[topIndex--]; }
    T top() { return data[topIndex]; }
    bool isEmpty() { return topIndex == -1; }
};
```

### Step 5: Exception Handling — Structured Error Flow

The **control-flow derivation** of a `try-catch` block is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Program} &\rightarrow \text{try-block} \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{Normal flow} & \text{if no throw occurs} \\
\text{throw } e & \text{if error detected}
\end{cases} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Catch Handler}(T) \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
T = \text{type}(e) & \text{handle and continue} \\
T \neq \text{type}(e) & \text{propagate to outer try}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Exception handling **decouples error-detection code** (deep in a function) from **error-handling code** (usually at a higher level). This is the **separation of concerns** principle in action.

### Step 3.6: Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Construct | Mechanism | Polymorphism Type | Binding Time | Runtime Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Function Overloading | Name-based | Ad-hoc | Compile | None |
| **Function Template** | Type parameter | **Parametric** | **Compile** | **None** |
| Virtual Functions | v-table | Inclusion (subtype) | Runtime | One indirection |
| STL Containers | Templates | Parametric | Compile | None (algorithmic only) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define **generic programming**. State any two advantages of function templates over function overloading.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Generic programming is a paradigm in which algorithms are written in terms of **type parameters** that are specified later, allowing a single function or class definition to operate on multiple data types.

**Advantages over overloading (2 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Code Reusability:** A single template definition replaces multiple overloaded functions. For example, one `maxGeneric<T>(T, T)` replaces separate `maxInt`, `maxFloat`, and `maxDouble` functions.
2. **Maintainability:** Changes to the algorithm logic need to be made only once — in the template — rather than in every overloaded copy, reducing the chance of inconsistency.
3. *(Optional extra)* **Type Safety at Compile Time:** The compiler enforces type checking during instantiation, catching errors before the program runs.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the **four components of the STL** and briefly explain the role of iterators.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Standard Template Library consists of four components:

| # | Component | Role (½ Mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Containers** | Hold collections of objects (e.g., `vector`, `list`, `map`). |
| 2 | **Iterators** | Generalized pointers used to traverse container elements. |
| 3 | **Algorithms** | Pre-defined procedures like `sort`, `find`, `count` that operate on ranges. |
| 4 | **Function Objects (Functors)** | Objects that overload `operator()` to customize algorithm behavior. |

**Role of Iterators (1 Mark):**
Iterators act as a **bridge between containers and algorithms**. They provide a uniform interface (`begin()`, `end()`, `++`, `*`) so that an algorithm like `sort` can work with *any* container that supplies a compatible iterator — achieving the **decoupling principle** of the STL.

---

## 📘 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth 14 marks with a typical split of **7 + 7** across sub-parts (a) and (b). You are expected to attempt **one full question** of 14 marks OR a combination of sub-parts across questions per the "choice within a question" rule. Model solutions below are full 14-mark answers.

---

### 🅰️ Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **class templates** with a suitable example. Write a C++ program to define a class template `Array<T>` that supports element insertion, display, and finding the maximum element.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the **Standard Template Library (STL)** architecture. With neat diagrams, explain the relationship between **containers, iterators, and algorithms**. Mention any two sequence containers and two associative containers.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Concept (3 Marks):**
A class template allows the definition of a **generic class** where the data type of its members is supplied as a parameter at instantiation time. The syntax is:

```cpp
template <class T>
class ClassName { /* members of type T */ };
```

When an object is created as `Array<int> a1;`, the compiler generates a concrete class with `T = int`.

**Program (4 Marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

template <class T>
class Array {
    T arr[50];
    int size;
public:
    Array() : size(0) {}
    void insert(T val) { 
        if (size < 50) arr[size++] = val; 
    }
    void display() {
        for (int i = 0; i < size; ++i)
            cout << arr[i] << " ";
        cout << endl;
    }
    T findMax() {
        T maxVal = arr[0];
        for (int i = 1; i < size; ++i)
            if (arr[i] > maxVal) maxVal = arr[i];
        return maxVal;
    }
};

int main() {
    Array<int> intArr;
    intArr.insert(10); intArr.insert(40); intArr.insert(25);
    cout << "Int Array: "; intArr.display();
    cout << "Max = " << intArr.findMax() << endl;

    Array<float> fArr;
    fArr.insert(3.2f); fArr.insert(7.8f); fArr.insert(1.5f);
    cout << "Float Array: "; fArr.display();
    cout << "Max = " << fArr.findMax() << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Output (Expected):**
```
Int Array: 10 40 25 
Max = 40
Float Array: 3.2 7.8 1.5 
Max = 7.8
```

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**STL Architecture (3 Marks):**
The STL is built on a **decoupled, three-tier architecture**:

$$
\text{Algorithms} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Iterators} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Containers}
$$

- **Containers** store data and provide iterators.
- **Iterators** expose a uniform traversal interface to algorithms.
- **Algorithms** operate on data through iterators — *not* directly on containers.

This means `sort` can sort a `vector`, `deque`, or even a raw array — as long as iterators `begin` and `end` are supplied.

**Containers Mention (2 Marks):**
- **Sequence Containers** (linear arrangement): `vector`, `list`, `deque`
- **Associative Containers** (key-based, typically balanced BSTs): `set`, `map`, `multiset`, `multimap`

**Diagram (2 Marks):**
Refer to the **STL Triangle of Interaction** diagram in Section 4.3 above. Draw and label:

```
[Algorithms]  ⇆  [Iterators]  ⇆  [Containers]
   (sort)         (begin,++)     (vector, map)
   (find)         (end, *)        (list, set)
```

---

### 🅱️ Question B — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **exception handling**? Explain the `try`, `throw`, and `catch` mechanism with a C++ program that validates division-by-zero and out-of-range array access.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **function templates** with multiple type parameters. Write a program using a function template `compare<T1, T2>(T1 a, T2 b)` that returns the larger of two values of *different* types.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Exception handling is a **structured mechanism** in C++ to detect, signal (`throw`), and recover (`catch`) from runtime errors (exceptions) without abruptly terminating the program. It uses three keywords: `try`, `throw`, and `catch`.

**Mechanism (2 Marks):**
- The **try block** encloses code that *may* throw an exception.
- The **throw statement** signals that an exception has occurred, passing an exception object.
- The **catch handler** receives the thrown object, processes it, and the program continues.

**Program (3 Marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <stdexcept>
using namespace std;

double divide(double a, double b) {
    if (b == 0) throw runtime_error("Division by zero!");
    return a / b;
}

int main() {
    int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
    int idx;
    
    try {
        cout << "Enter index (0-4): ";
        cin >> idx;
        if (idx < 0 || idx > 4)
            throw out_of_range("Index out of bounds!");
        cout << "arr[" << idx << "] = " << arr[idx] << endl;
        
        double result = divide(100.0, 0.0);
        cout << "Result: " << result << endl;
    }
    catch (const out_of_range& e) {
        cout << "Out of Range Error: " << e.what() << endl;
    }
    catch (const runtime_error& e) {
        cout << "Runtime Error: " << e.what() << endl;
    }
    catch (...) {
        cout << "Unknown exception caught!" << endl;
    }
    return 0;
}
```

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Concept (3 Marks):**
Function templates can accept **more than one type parameter**, each independent. The syntax:

```cpp
template <typename T1, typename T2>
ReturnType functionName(T1 a, T2 b) { /* body */ }
```

The compiler instantiates a separate function for every unique combination of `(T1, T2)` used in calls.

**Program (4 Marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

template <typename T1, typename T2>
auto compare(T1 a, T2 b) -> decltype((a > b) ? a : b) {
    return (a > b) ? a : b;
}

int main() {
    cout << "compare(10, 20.5) = " << compare(10, 20.5) << endl;
    cout << "compare(100.7, 50) = " << compare(100.7, 50) << endl;
    cout << "compare('Z', 'A') = " << compare('Z', 'A') << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
compare(10, 20.5) = 20.5
compare(100.7, 50) = 100.7
compare('Z', 'A') = Z
```

**Explanation of `decltype`:**
`decltype((a > b) ? a : b)` is a **trailing return type** that lets the compiler deduce the return type based on the actual arguments, ensuring the function works seamlessly across types.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Alert**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `template` keyword** before the function/class definition → **Compilation error, full mark loss.**
> 2. **Confusing STL `set` (sorted, unique) with `multiset` (sorted, duplicates allowed)** — examiners *deliberately* test this. Memorize the difference.
> 3. **Placing `catch(...)` before specific catches** → Syntax error. The `catch(...)` handler must be **last**.
> 4. **Writing `T` instead of `<T>`** when declaring template objects (e.g., `Stack<int> s;` not `Stack s;`) → Common Part A blunder.
> 5. **Using `|` (bare pipe) in set-builder notation in answers** → May be auto-flagged by the digital evaluation parser in some KTU online tests. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in typed form.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Introduction**

- ✅ **Generic Programming** = *one definition, many types*. Templates are its primary mechanism.
- ✅ **Function Templates** abstract **algorithms**; **Class Templates** abstract **data structures**.
- ✅ Template instantiation is a **compile-time** activity — no runtime overhead.
- ✅ **STL** has **4 components**: Containers, Iterators, Algorithms, Functors.
- ✅ **Containers** are of three families: *Sequence*, *Associative*, *Container Adapters*.
- ✅ **Iterators** decouple algorithms from containers — this is the *key STL insight*.
- ✅ **Exception Handling** uses `try` → `throw` → `catch`; multiple `catch` blocks allowed; order must be *specific-to-general*; `catch(...)` is the universal handler placed **last**.
- ✅ **Standard exceptions** in `<stdexcept>`: `runtime_error`, `out_of_range`, `invalid_argument`, `overflow_error`, `underflow_error`, `length_error`.
- ✅ **File Streams**: `ifstream` (read), `ofstream` (write), `fstream` (both).
- ✅ **Manipulators** like `setw(n)`, `setprecision(n)`, `fixed`, `scientific` control I/O formatting.
- ✅ **Map vs Multimap** = unique keys vs duplicate keys; **Set vs Multiset** = same distinction for values.
- ✅ Always check stream state flags: `.eof()`, `.fail()`, `.bad()`, `.good()`.
- ✅ Use `\vert` / `\mid` (not bare `|`) in mathematical notation within KTU digital answer sheets.

> 🎓 **Final Tip:** When answering any Module 2 question in the ESE, **always state the time complexity** of any STL container you use. Examiners reward efficiency awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Names
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Names

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a "Name"

In the context of programming languages, a **Name** (also called an **Identifier**) is a sequence of characters (letters, digits, underscores, and sometimes special symbols like `$` or `@`) used to denote a program entity such as a **variable**, **constant**, **function**, **class**, **label**, **type**, or **object**.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for advanced programming paradigms, a *name* is fundamentally a **lexical token** that the language's **translator** (compiler/interpreter) maps to a **semantic attribute** through a process called **binding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:**
> A *name* in a program is a syntactic construct that is **bound** to an *attribute* (such as a value, a type, a memory location, or a behavior). The association between a name and its attribute is called a **binding**, and the rules governing *where* and *when* this binding occurs define the language's semantics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Name as a Postbox Label"

Think of names in a program like **labels on a row of post-office boxes** in a long corridor:

- The **label** (e.g., `salary`, `age`, `counter`) is the *name* you write in your code.
- The **post-box itself** is the *memory location* where the actual value (the *attribute*) is stored.
- The act of **sticking the label onto a particular box** is called **binding**.
- The **corridor section** where the label can be seen and read is the **scope** of the name.
- If you later **tear the label off and put a new one on the same box**, that represents **re-binding** (e.g., assignment).
- If you **remove the box entirely** (deallocate), but leave the label lying around, that's a **dangling reference** — a common programming error.

This analogy helps visualize the three critical aspects of a name:
1. **What it is** (lexical form)
2. **What it points to** (binding)
3. **Where it is valid** (scope)

### 1.3 Why Study Names? — KTU 2024 Syllabus Significance

> [!NOTE]
> Names are the **most fundamental abstraction** in any programming language. Without names, programmers would have to write raw memory addresses (like `0x7FFE3A40`) and there would be no way to write reusable, readable, maintainable code.

The KTU 2024 Module 2 emphasizes names because:
- They form the basis for **abstraction** (data abstraction, procedural abstraction).
- Their **binding time** determines whether a language is *statically* or *dynamically* scoped.
- They directly affect **memory management**, **execution efficiency**, and **program correctness**.
- Modern concepts like **closures**, **lambda expressions**, and **modules** are all built upon disciplined handling of names.

### 1.4 Classification of Names

Names in programming are broadly classified into:

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Identifiers** | User-defined names | `int counter;` |
| **Keywords/Reserved Words** | Predefined names reserved by the language | `int`, `if`, `while` |
| **Standard Identifiers** | Predefined names that *can* be overridden | `main`, `printf`, `cin` |
| **Literals** | Self-denoting constant names | `42`, `"Hello"`, `true` |
| **Operator Names** | Symbolic names for operations | `+`, `&&`, `<<` |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam pitfall: confusing **keywords** (cannot be redefined) with **standard identifiers** (can technically be overridden but should not be).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Binding

A **binding** is an association between a *name* and an *attribute* of that name. The set of attributes a name may possess includes:

1. **Type** (e.g., `int`, `float`, `string`)
2. **Value** (e.g., `42`, `"hello"`)
3. **Memory Location / Address** (e.g., `0x7FFE3A40`)
4. **Scope Region** (the textual portion of the program where the name is valid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Bindings:** A single name can be bound to *different attributes* at *different times*. For example, a variable name is bound to a *type* at compile time but bound to a *value* at runtime.

## 2.2 Binding Time — The "When" of Binding

The **binding time** is the moment in the translation/execution pipeline when a name is associated with its attribute. This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU examinations.

| Binding Time | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Language Design Time** | Bound when the language itself is defined | Reserved keywords like `if`, `while` |
| **Language Implementation Time** | Bound when the compiler is built | Size of `int` (often 32 bits) |
| **Compile Time (Static Binding)** | Bound during translation | Type of a variable, function signatures |
| **Link Time** | Bound when separate modules are combined | External variable addresses, library calls |
| **Load Time** | Bound when program is loaded into memory | Final memory addresses (with relocatable code) |
| **Run Time (Dynamic Binding)** | Bound during execution | Variable values, memory for `malloc` calls |
| **Unused / Unbound** | Never bound | A declared but unused local variable |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical / Formal Representation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Name → Attribute Binding** | $\text{bind} : \mathcal{N} \times \mathcal{T} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ where $\mathcal{N}$ is names, $\mathcal{T}$ is time, $\mathcal{A}$ is attribute set | Compiler symbol table construction |
| 2 | **Memory Address Formula** | $\text{Addr}(n) = \text{Base} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Offset}_i$ | Stack frame layout, array indexing |
| 3 | **Scope Depth (Lexical)** | $d(n) = $ number of enclosing blocks before the global block | Resolving shadowed identifiers |
| 4 | **Lifetime Interval** | $\text{Life}(n) = [t_{\text{alloc}}, t_{\text{dealloc}}]$ | Memory leak detection, garbage collection |
| 5 | **Reference Count** | $\text{RefCount}(x) = \sum_{n \in \text{refs}} 1$ | Alias detection, GC strategies |
| 6 | **Symbol Table Lookup** | $\text{ST}_{\text{lookup}}(n) = \text{binding} \text{ or } \bot$ | Compilation complexity $\mathcal{O}(1)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, the symbol $\bot$ ("bottom") denotes an **undefined** or **unfound** result, used in formal language semantics.

## 2.3 Scope — The "Where" of Binding

The **scope** of a name is the **region of program text** where that name is visible and can be used. There are two principal scope rules:

### A. Static (Lexical) Scoping
- Scope is determined by the **physical (textual) structure** of the program.
- A name refers to the **closest enclosing declaration** in the source code.
- Used by: **C, C++, Java, Python, Pascal, Ada**.

### B. Dynamic Scoping
- Scope is determined by the **call stack at runtime**.
- A name refers to the **most recent active binding** on the call stack.
- Used by: **early LISP, APL, SNOBOL, Perl (older modes)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Confusing *dynamic scoping* with *dynamic binding*. *Dynamic binding* refers to *when* the value is bound (at runtime), but the *scope rule* can still be static. For example, Java uses **static scope** but supports **dynamic method binding** (polymorphism).

## 2.4 Lifetime — The "How Long" of Binding

- **Scope** is a *compile-time (textual)* concept.
- **Lifetime** is a *run-time (temporal)* concept.
- A name may be in scope but not live (e.g., a `for` loop counter after the loop ends), or alive but not in scope (e.g., a static variable referenced through a pointer).

| Concept | Question Answered | Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | What is called what? | Lexical |
| **Binding** | Name ↔ Attribute association | Both |
| **Scope** | Where is the name usable? | Compile-time |
| **Lifetime** | When does the storage exist? | Run-time |

## 2.5 Name Spaces and Name Resolution

A **name space** is a set of names in which all names must be **unique** (no two entities can share a name within the same name space). Common name spaces include:

- Variables
- Functions / Procedures
- Types / Classes
- Labels
- Modules / Packages
- Macros / Templates

> [!TIP]
> **Name Resolution** is the compiler's process of finding the correct binding for a name. It uses a **symbol table** — a data structure (often a hash table) that maps names to their attributes.

## 2.6 Aliases

An **alias** occurs when two or more distinct names refer to the **same memory location**. Common sources:

- **Pointers** (`int *p = &x;` — now `p` and `x` are aliases)
- **Reference variables in C++** (`int &r = x;`)
- **Union members** (in C/C++)
- **Parameters passed by reference**
- **Module-level renaming** (`import numpy as np` in Python)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Binding-Time Analysis of a Name

Let us analyze the variable declaration in C:

```c
float salary = 5000.50;
```

### Step 1: Language Design Time
The keywords `float` and the syntax `type identifier = literal;` are defined by the C language standard (ISO/IEC 9899). The symbol `=` is bound to the assignment operation.

### Step 2: Language Implementation Time
The compiler vendor (e.g., GCC, Clang) decides that on this platform, `float` occupies **4 bytes (32 bits)** following IEEE 754 single-precision format.

### Step 3: Compile Time
The compiler reads `float salary = 5000.50;` and:
- Binds `salary` to the type `float`.
- Allocates a slot of size **4 bytes** in the symbol table entry.
- Binds the literal `5000.50` to its internal representation (a 32-bit pattern `0x459C4000`).

> [!NOTE]
> The literal `5000.50` is bound to its bit pattern at *compile time* (literal constant folding).

### Step 4: Link Time
If `salary` were declared as `extern` in another file, the linker would resolve its final memory address by combining object files.

### Step 5: Load Time
The operating system loader assigns a concrete **virtual memory address** (e.g., `0x7FFE3A40`) to the symbol `salary` in the process's data segment.

### Step 6: Run Time
The value `5000.50` is **copied** into the memory location bound to `salary`. From this point on, any write `salary = 6000;` re-binds the *value* attribute at runtime.

### Step 7: Deallocation
When the enclosing block exits, the storage for `salary` is released (its lifetime ends), even though the *name* `salary` is no longer in scope.

### Summary Table of All Bindings for `salary`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attribute of } \texttt{salary} \quad \rightarrow \quad & \text{When Bound} \\
\hline
\text{Type} = \texttt{float} \quad \rightarrow \quad & \text{Compile Time} \\
\text{Size} = 4 \text{ bytes} \quad \rightarrow \quad & \text{Implementation Time} \\
\text{Value} = 5000.50 \quad \rightarrow \quad & \text{Initialization / Run Time} \\
\text{Address} = \texttt{0x7FFE3A40} \quad \rightarrow \quad & \text{Load Time} \\
\text{Scope region} = \text{current block} \quad \rightarrow \quad & \text{Compile Time} \\
\text{Lifetime} = [t_{\text{enter}}, t_{\text{exit}}] \quad \rightarrow \quad & \text{Run Time}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Static vs Dynamic Scoping

Consider the following pseudo-code:

```
int x = 10;          // global x

function foo() {
    print x;         // (A) Which x?
}

function bar() {
    int x = 20;      // local x in bar
    foo();
}

main() {
    bar();
}
```

### Case 1: Static (Lexical) Scoping

**Step 1:** The compiler determines the textual enclosure of `(A)`. The function `foo` is defined at the **top level**, outside `bar`.

**Step 2:** The compiler searches outward from `(A)`:
- Inside `foo`? No declaration of `x`. ❌
- Inside the file / module? Yes — global `int x = 10;` ✅

**Step 3:** The compiler binds `(A)` to the **global `x`**.

**Step 4:** Output = `10`.

$$
\text{Output}_{\text{static}} = 10
$$

### Case 2: Dynamic Scoping

**Step 1:** The runtime examines the **call stack** when `foo` executes.

**Step 2:** Stack inspection (top to bottom):
- Frame for `foo`: no local `x`. ❌
- Frame for `bar`: `int x = 20;` ✅

**Step 3:** The runtime binds `(A)` to `bar`'s local `x`.

**Step 4:** Output = `20`.

$$
\text{Output}_{\text{dynamic}} = 20
$$

### Comparative Analytical Matrix

| # | Aspect | Static Scoping | Dynamic Scoping |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resolution Time** | Compile time | Run time |
| 2 | **Based On** | Source code structure | Call stack |
| 3 | **Predictability** | Highly predictable | Less predictable |
| 4 | **Implementation** | Symbol table | Runtime stack search |
| 5 | **Used By** | C, C++, Java, Python | Old LISP, APL, Perl 4 |
| 6 | **Performance** | Faster (compile-time) | Slower (runtime lookup) |
| 7 | **Debuggability** | Easier | Harder |
| 8 | **Supports Closures** | Yes (natively) | Difficult |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Symbol Table Construction

For the following C program:

```c
int x = 1;
void f() {
    int y = 2;
    {
        int z = 3;
    }
}
```

### Step 1: Global Scope Symbol Table
The compiler pushes a new scope onto the scope stack. It enters:
- `x` → type `int`, offset 0
- `f` → type `function`, offset 4

### Step 2: Function `f` Scope
A new scope is pushed. It inherits a parent pointer to the global scope.
- `y` → type `int`, offset 0 (relative to f's frame)

### Step 3: Inner Block Scope
A new scope is pushed with parent = `f`'s scope.
- `z` → type `int`, offset 0 (relative to block)

### Step 4: Block Exit
The scope containing `z` is **popped**; `z` is no longer accessible.

### Step 5: Function Exit
The scope containing `y` is popped; `y` is no longer accessible.

### Scope Tree Representation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Global Scope} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \{x, f\} \\
\quad\;\; \downarrow \text{ (parent of)} \\
\text{Function } f \text{ Scope} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \{y\} \\
\quad\;\; \downarrow \text{ (parent of)} \\
\text{Inner Block Scope} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \{z\}
\end{aligned}
$$

When looking up `z`, the algorithm searches the current scope → parent (f) → parent (global). If not found, it reports an **undefined name** error.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Analysis: Aliasing and Reference Counting

Suppose we have:

```c
int x = 10;
int *p = &x;     // p is an alias for x
int *q = p;      // q is also an alias for x
*x = 20;          // changes x
```

### Reference Count Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RefCount}(x \text{ location}) &= |\{n \mid n \text{ refers to } x\}| \\
&= |\{x, p, q\}| \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

After `free(p);` (assuming p was malloc'd), $\text{RefCount}$ would drop to 2. When the count reaches 0, the storage can be safely deallocated by a **reference-counting garbage collector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aliasing Bug Example (KTU classic):**
> If two pointers `p` and `q` alias the same memory, and one part of the code frees it through `p` while the other still uses it through `q`, the result is a **dangling pointer** — undefined behavior.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Name Binding Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Source Code] --> B[Lexer / Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Parser]
    C --> D[Symbol Table Construction]
    D --> E{Binding Time}
    E -->|Compile Time| F[Static Binding: Type, Scope]
    E -->|Link Time| G[Address Resolution]
    E -->|Load Time| H[Memory Mapping]
    E -->|Run Time| I[Dynamic Binding: Values, Pointers]
    F --> J[Executable Code]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> K[Running Process]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output / Behavior]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Scope Hierarchy (Lexical Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    GS[Global Scope: x, f]
    GS --> FS[Function f Scope: y]
    FS --> BS[Inner Block Scope: z]
    BS --> BS1[Lookup z: Found]
    BS --> BS2[Lookup y: Found in parent]
    BS --> BS3[Lookup x: Found in grandparent]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Static vs Dynamic Scoping Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Encounter name N] --> B{Specialization Rule}
    B -->|Use Source Code Structure| C[Static Scoping]
    B -->|Use Call Stack at Runtime| D[Dynamic Scoping]
    C --> E[Search Enclosing Blocks Lexically]
    D --> F[Search Stack Frames Top-Down]
    E --> G[Return Closest Lexical Match]
    F --> H[Return Most Recent Active Binding]
    G --> I[Output 10 in example]
    H --> J[Output 20 in example]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Name Categories

```mermaid
graph TD
    N[Name Categories] --> N1[Identifiers]
    N --> N2[Keywords]
    N --> N3[Standard Identifiers]
    N --> N4[Literals]
    N --> N5[Operator Names]
    N1 --> N1a[Variables]
    N1 --> N1b[Functions]
    N1 --> N1c[Classes and Types]
    N1 --> N1d[Labels and Tags]
    N2 --> N2a[Reserved: if, while, int]
    N3 --> N3a[Overridable: main, printf]
    N4 --> N4a[42, 3.14, true]
    N5 --> N5a[plus, minus, AND]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Aliasing Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Variable x: 10] -->|address 0x1000| M[(Memory Location 0x1000)]
    B[Pointer p: and x] -->|holds 0x1000| M
    C[Pointer q: and x] -->|holds 0x1000| M
    M -->|read| D[All three return 10]
    M -->|write via asterisk p = 20| E[x and q also report 20]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the scope and lifetime of a variable. Illustrate with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Scope (1 mark):** Scope is the *textual region* of a program where a name is *visible* and can be referenced. It is determined at **compile time** based on the program's lexical structure.
- **Lifetime (1 mark):** Lifetime is the *time interval* during program execution when the *storage* for a variable actually exists. It is a **run-time** concept determined by allocation and deallocation events.
- **Example (1 mark):**
  ```c
  void f() {
      int x;          // x's scope = inside f
                      // x's lifetime = from call of f to return of f
      for (int i=0;i<10;i++) {
          // i is in scope only inside the for loop
          // i's lifetime = during one iteration only
      }
      // i is OUT OF SCOPE here
  }
  ```

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by binding time? List any four different binding times with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Binding time (1 mark):** Binding time is the moment during the translation/execution pipeline when a name is associated with a particular attribute.
- **Four binding times (2 marks):**
  1. **Language Design Time** — e.g., reserved words like `if`, `while`.
  2. **Compile Time** — e.g., the type of a variable `int count;`.
  3. **Link Time** — e.g., the address of an external function `printf`.
  4. **Run Time** — e.g., the value of a variable assigned at runtime.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **dynamic scoping** with **dynamic typing** or **dynamic binding**. They are *different concepts*.
> - Many students lose marks by failing to specify **which attribute** is being bound (type, value, address, etc.).
> - When asked to compare, always use a **table format** — KTU examiners reward structured answers.
> - Always provide **both** the static and dynamic scoping outputs for any code example.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **name binding** in programming languages. Discuss in detail the different **binding times** with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable C program, explain **static (lexical) scoping** and **dynamic scoping**. Show how the same program produces different outputs under the two rules. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
A *name binding* is an association between a name (identifier) and one of its possible attributes (type, value, memory location, etc.). The process of creating and managing these associations is fundamental to language implementation and is typically realized through a **symbol table** data structure.

**Binding Time Categories (5 marks):**

1. **Language Design Time** — When the language itself is being defined. Example: In C, the keyword `while` is bound to the meaning "loop construct" by the ISO C standard.
2. **Language Implementation Time** — When a specific compiler is built. Example: GCC defines `int` as 4 bytes on x86-64.
3. **Compile Time (Static Binding)** — Example: `int x;` binds the name `x` to the type `int` during compilation.
4. **Link Time** — Example: A call to `printf` is resolved to a specific address in the standard library.
5. **Load Time** — Example: A static variable's address is finalized when the program is loaded into memory.
6. **Run Time (Dynamic Binding)** — Example: The value of `x = getchar();` is bound at runtime.
7. **Never Bound** — A declared but never used variable.

> Memory aids: more *early* binding = more *efficient* but less *flexible*; more *late* binding = more *flexible* but less *efficient*.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Program (2 marks):**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
int x = 10;          // global x

void foo() {
    printf("%d\n", x);   // (A)
}

void bar() {
    int x = 20;       // local x
    foo();
}

int main() {
    bar();
    return 0;
}
```

**Static Scoping Explanation (2.5 marks):**
The compiler determines the scope of `x` at compile time. Function `foo` is defined at the top level, outside `bar`. When the compiler encounters `x` at point `(A)`, it searches outward through the *lexical* nesting:
- Inside `foo`? — No `x` defined.
- Global scope? — Found `int x = 10;`.

Hence, the output is `10`. This is **static (lexical) scoping** — used by C, C++, Java, Python, etc.

**Dynamic Scoping Explanation (2.5 marks):**
If the language used dynamic scoping (e.g., old LISP), the binding of `x` at point `(A)` is determined at *runtime* by inspecting the *call stack*:
- Stack top (foo's frame)? — No `x`.
- Next frame (bar's frame)? — Found `int x = 20;`.

Hence, the output would be `20`.

**Comparative Table (optional, bonus):**
| Aspect | Static | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | Compile time | Run time |
| Based on | Source code | Call stack |
| Output | 10 | 20 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is a **symbol table**? Explain how it is used to support **name resolution** and **scope management** during compilation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **aliases** in programming. Discuss the problems caused by aliases with suitable code examples. How are they detected using **reference counting**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Symbol Table Definition (2 marks):**
A symbol table is a compiler data structure that stores information about names (identifiers) appearing in the source program. For each name, it records attributes such as type, scope, memory offset, and (in later phases) the runtime address.

**Operations (2 marks):**
- `insert(name, attributes)` — adds a new binding
- `lookup(name)` — retrieves the current binding
- `enter_scope()` / `exit_scope()` — manage nested scopes

**Name Resolution and Scope Management (3 marks):**
The symbol table is organized as a **stack of scopes** (often implemented as a hash table per scope, with parent pointers). When the compiler encounters a name, it searches the **innermost (current) scope first** and proceeds outward to enclosing scopes. This implements **lexical scoping** automatically. On exiting a scope, all bindings introduced in that scope are removed, ensuring no leakage.

Example: In C, when entering a function, a new scope is pushed; local variables are inserted. On function exit, the scope is popped, and the local names are no longer accessible — but if any were declared `static`, they persist in the global scope (longer lifetime).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Alias Definition (2 marks):**
An alias occurs when two or more distinct names refer to the *same memory location*. This is common in C/C++ with pointers, references, and union members.

**Code Example (2 marks):**
```c
int x = 10;
int *p = &x;
int *q = p;
*p = 30;
printf("%d %d %d", x, *p, *q);   // All three print 30
```
Here, `x`, `*p`, and `*q` are all aliases for the same memory location.

**Problems Caused (2 marks):**
1. **Dangling references** — freeing through one alias leaves others invalid.
2. **Aliasing bugs in optimization** — compilers cannot assume independent stores.
3. **Memory leaks** — reference counting fails with cyclic aliases.
4. **Confusion in reasoning** — aliasing violates the intuitive "one name, one value" model.

**Reference Counting Detection (1 mark):**
Each storage location has an integer counter $\text{RefCount}$. It is incremented each time a new alias is created and decremented each time an alias is destroyed. If the counter drops to zero, the storage can be deallocated. For example, in the code above, $\text{RefCount}(x\text{-location}) = 3$ (one for `x`, one for `p`, one for `q`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Cyclical Reference Problem:** If `p` points to a structure that contains a pointer back to `p`, the reference count never reaches zero, causing a **memory leak**. Modern garbage collectors use **mark-and-sweep** to handle this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Name** = lexical token; **Binding** = name ↔ attribute association; **Scope** = where the name is valid; **Lifetime** = how long the storage exists.
- 🔑 **Binding times** in order: Language Design → Implementation → Compile → Link → Load → Run.
- 🔑 **Earlier binding ⇒ faster execution, less flexibility. Later binding ⇒ slower, more flexible.**
- 🔑 **Static (Lexical) Scoping** = based on source code text; standard in modern languages.
- 🔑 **Dynamic Scoping** = based on call stack at runtime; rarely used today.
- 🔑 **Scope is compile-time; Lifetime is run-time** — a critical distinction.
- 🔑 **Symbol table** = the data structure used by compilers to track name bindings.
- 🔑 **Alias** = multiple names referring to the same memory location; managed via **reference counting** in some garbage collectors.
- 🔑 Common KTU pitfalls: confusing dynamic scoping with dynamic binding; conflating scope with lifetime; missing the "which attribute?" aspect of binding.
- 🔑 Remember: **name resolution** proceeds from **innermost** to **outermost** scope under lexical rules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the paradigm of structured and object-oriented programming, a **Variable** is a named symbolic identifier bound to a memory location in the Random Access Memory (RAM) of the computer system, whose associated value is permitted to change during the course of program execution. The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEBMT746) formally defines a variable as a *triple entity* consisting of a **name** (identifier), a **type** (data type), and a **value** (contents at that memory address).

A variable is characterized by four fundamental attributes:
1. **Identifier** – the symbolic name used by the programmer.
2. **Data Type** – determines the size, layout, range, and operations permitted.
3. **Value** – the actual binary data stored in memory.
4. **Memory Address** – the physical (or virtual) location where the value resides.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A variable is not merely a "container" — it is a binding between a symbolic name and a memory address, mediated by the compiler's **Symbol Table** and resolved at runtime by the loader.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **post office with numbered lockers**:
- Each **locker number** is analogous to the **memory address** of a variable.
- The **name written on the label** of the locker is the **variable identifier** (e.g., `age`, `salary`).
- The **type of locker** (small, medium, large) corresponds to the **data type** (`char`, `int`, `float`).
- The **item stored inside** the locker is the **value** of the variable.

When the postal clerk needs the value of `age`, he does not need to remember the locker number — he just reads the label. Similarly, the compiler translates the *name* to a *memory address* automatically. If you update the contents of the locker, the locker number and label stay the same, but the *value* has changed — exactly how a variable behaves during execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The identifier never changes during execution; only the value stored at the bound address may change — this is the essence of a *variable* (as opposed to a *constant*).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard sizes** (in bytes) are mandated by the KTU syllabus for the C programming language, which is the primary vehicle of assessment in PEBMT746:

- **1 Byte** = **8 bits** (the smallest addressable unit on most architectures).
- **Word size** of a typical 32-bit system = **4 bytes**.
- **Word size** of a typical 64-bit system = **8 bytes**.

> [!TIP]
> Memorizing standard type sizes is a high-frequency Part-A question in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Variable's Lifecycle

The journey of a variable from source code to runtime memory follows five structured phases:

1. **Declaration Phase** – The programmer introduces the variable by specifying its type and name (e.g., `int counter;`). The compiler reserves a slot in the **Symbol Table**.
2. **Memory Allocation Phase** – When the program is loaded into RAM, the OS/runtime allocates contiguous bytes equal to the type's size at a specific address.
3. **Initialization Phase** – An initial value is assigned (e.g., `counter = 0;`). Without explicit initialization, the variable holds a **garbage value** (undefined behavior in C).
4. **Usage Phase** – The variable is read or written in expressions, function calls, and control flow.
5. **Deallocation Phase** – When the variable's scope ends, the memory is released back to the stack/heap for reuse.

## 2.2 Classification of Variables (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Based on Scope
- **Local Variable:** Declared inside a function or block; visible only within that block.
- **Global Variable:** Declared outside all functions; visible throughout the program (file scope).

### B. Based on Lifetime (Storage Class)
- **Automatic (`auto`):** Created on entry to a block, destroyed on exit. Default for local variables.
- **Static (`static`):** Retains its value across multiple function calls. Lifetime = entire program.
- **Register (`register`):** Hints the compiler to store in a CPU register for faster access.
- **External (`extern`):** Refers to a global variable defined in another file.

### C. Based on Declaration Site
- **Primitive variables** (`int`, `float`, `char`, `double`)
- **Derived variables** (arrays, pointers, functions)
- **User-defined variables** (`struct`, `union`, `enum`)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Attribute** | **Formula / Rule** | **Unit / Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Memory occupied by `n` variables of type `T` | $S = n \times \text{sizeof}(T)$ | Bytes |
| Number of distinct values representable by `b` bits | $N = 2^{b}$ | Discrete states |
| Range of signed integer of `b` bits | $-2^{b-1} \text{ to } 2^{b-1}-1$ | Integer values |
| Range of unsigned integer of `b` bits | $0 \text{ to } 2^{b}-1$ | Integer values |
| Address of an array element `a[i]` | $\text{Addr}(a[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{sizeof}(T))$ | Bytes |
| Precision of `float` (IEEE 754) | **23** mantissa bits | ~7 decimal digits |
| Precision of `double` (IEEE 754) | **52** mantissa bits | ~15 decimal digits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the formula $\text{Addr}(a[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{sizeof}(T))$ in Part-B questions on pointer arithmetic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Variables are foundational to **embedded systems** (sensor value storage in IoT devices), **numerical simulations** (iterative solvers in MATLAB/CFD), **control systems** (PID controller state variables), and **database engines** (in-memory cache buffers). In **automotive ECU firmware**, for instance, variables hold real-time sensor readings (RPM, temperature, throttle position) updated thousands of times per second.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Address Calculation in a 1-D Array

Consider an integer array declared as:

```c
int marks[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
```

Assume the base address (address of `marks[0]`) is `2000`, and `sizeof(int) = 4` bytes.

**Step 1 — Establish the address formula.**

The compiler computes the address of the $i$-th element using the principle of **contiguous memory allocation**. For an array of type `T` with base address $B$:

$$\text{Addr}(a[i]) = B + (i \times \text{sizeof}(T))$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

For $i = 0$:

$$\text{Addr}(marks[0]) = 2000 + (0 \times 4) = 2000$$

For $i = 1$:

$$\text{Addr}(marks[1]) = 2000 + (1 \times 4) = 2004$$

For $i = 2$:

$$\text{Addr}(marks[2]) = 2000 + (2 \times 4) = 2008$$

For $i = 3$:

$$\text{Addr}(marks[3]) = 2000 + (3 \times 4) = 2012$$

For $i = 4$:

$$\text{Addr}(marks[4]) = 2000 + (4 \times 4) = 2016$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

Each successive integer element occupies the next **4 bytes** of memory because `int` requires 4 bytes on a 32-bit system. The total memory occupied is:

$$S = n \times \text{sizeof}(\text{int}) = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

This derivation is essential for KTU pointer-arithmetic problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Range of Signed Integer

**Step 1 — Total number of representable values.**

For a signed `int` stored in `b = 32` bits, we have:

$$N_{\text{total}} = 2^{b} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$$

**Step 2 — Split equally between negative and non-negative.**

By convention, the most significant bit (MSB) is the sign bit. The remaining $b-1$ bits represent magnitude:

$$N_{\text{magnitude}} = 2^{b-1} = 2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

**Step 3 — Compute the range.**

Negative side uses all $2^{b-1}$ states; non-negative side uses $2^{b-1} - 1$ states (since zero takes one slot):

$$\text{Range} = -2^{b-1} \text{ to } 2^{b-1}-1$$

$$\text{Range} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \text{ to } 2{,}147{,}483{,}647$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Storage Class Comparison

| **Storage Class** | **Keyword** | **Scope** | **Lifetime** | **Default Value** | **Storage Location** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Automatic | `auto` | Local to block | Block execution | Garbage | Stack |
| Register | `register` | Local to block | Block execution | Garbage | CPU Register (if available) |
| Static (local) | `static` | Local to block | Entire program | 0 (zero) | Stack/Static segment |
| Static (global) | `static` | File scope | Entire program | 0 (zero) | Static segment |
| External | `extern` | Global (multi-file) | Entire program | 0 (zero) | Static segment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Variable Memory Layout in RAM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: int a; float b; char c;] --> B[Compiler Front-End]
    B --> C[Symbol Table Entry]
    C --> D[Type Checking and Size Calculation]
    D --> E[Memory Allocation at Runtime]
    E --> F[Stack Segment]
    E --> G[Data Segment]
    E --> H[Heap Segment]
    F --> F1[Local variables]
    F --> F2[Function parameters]
    G --> G1[Global variables]
    G --> G2[Static variables]
    H --> H1[Dynamically allocated variables]
    H --> H2[Pointer targets]
    F1 --> I[Variable a stored at 4 bytes]
    F2 --> J[Parameter passing]
    G1 --> K[Initialized to 0 by default]
    H1 --> L[Allocated via malloc or calloc]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Lifecycle of a Local Variable

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Program enters function] --> P2[Allocate stack frame]
    P2 --> P3[Initialize local variables]
    P3 --> P4[Execute function body]
    P4 --> P5{Variable accessed?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Read or update value]
    P6 --> P5
    P5 -- No --> P7[Function returns]
    P7 --> P8[Deallocate stack frame]
    P8 --> P9[Variable destroyed - memory freed]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Scope Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Global Scope] --> X1[Variable G - accessible everywhere]
    X --> Y[Function main Scope]
    Y --> Y1[Variable L1 - accessible in main]
    Y --> Z[Function inner Scope]
    Z --> Z1[Variable L2 - accessible only in inner]
    Z1 --> Z2[Cannot access L1 directly without passing]
    Y1 --> W[Variable L1 - still in scope of main]
    X1 --> V[Variable G - shadowed if redeclared locally]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Inner blocks (deeper nested scopes) can access variables from outer blocks, but not vice versa. This is **lexical scoping**, a tested concept in KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a *variable* in C. List any four fundamental data types with their sizes on a 32-bit system.

**Model Answer:**
A variable is a named memory location whose value can change during program execution. It consists of a name, type, and value. Four fundamental data types in C on a 32-bit system are:

| **Data Type** | **Size (bytes)** | **Range** |
|---|---|---|
| `char` | 1 | -128 to 127 |
| `int` | 4 | -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 |
| `float` | 4 | 3.4E-38 to 3.4E+38 |
| `double` | 8 | 1.7E-308 to 1.7E+308 |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between *local* and *global* variables in C with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

| **Local Variable** | **Global Variable** |
|---|---|
| Declared inside a function or block. | Declared outside all functions. |
| Scope is limited to the declaring block. | Scope is the entire program. |
| Lifetime ends when block exits. | Lifetime is the entire program. |
| Default value is garbage (uninitialized). | Default value is 0. |
| Stored on the stack. | Stored in the data segment. |

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int g = 10;             /* Global variable */
void display() {
    int l = 20;         /* Local variable */
    printf("g=%d l=%d", g, l);
}
```

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) – Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the concept of *storage classes* in C. Discuss `auto`, `register`, `static`, and `extern` with examples.

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Discuss `auto` and `register` storage classes with examples and their scope, lifetime, and default values.**

**Model Solution:**

The **storage class** of a variable in C defines four properties: scope, lifetime, default value, and storage location.

**(i) Automatic Storage Class (`auto`):**
- This is the **default** class for variables declared inside a function.
- **Scope:** Local to the block in which declared.
- **Lifetime:** Exists only while the block is executing.
- **Default Value:** Garbage (undefined).
- **Storage:** Stack memory.

```c
void demo() {
    auto int x = 5;     /* equivalent to: int x = 5; */
    printf("%d", x);
}
```

**(ii) Register Storage Class (`register`):**
- Requests the compiler to store the variable in a **CPU register** for faster access.
- **Scope:** Local to the declaring block.
- **Lifetime:** Block execution only.
- **Default Value:** Garbage.
- **Storage:** CPU register (if available, otherwise treated as `auto`).
- The address-of operator `&` **cannot** be applied to a register variable.

```c
void loop_demo() {
    register int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 1000000; i++) {
        /* critical loop body */
    }
}
```

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Discuss `static` and `extern` storage classes with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**(iii) Static Storage Class (`static`):**
- When applied to a **local variable**, it retains its value between function calls.
- **Scope:** Local to the block.
- **Lifetime:** Entire program duration.
- **Default Value:** 0.
- **Storage:** Static (data) segment.

```c
void counter() {
    static int count = 0;   /* initialized only once */
    count++;
    printf("%d ", count);
}
int main() {
    counter();              /* prints 1 */
    counter();              /* prints 2 */
    counter();              /* prints 3 */
    return 0;
}
```

**(iv) External Storage Class (`extern`):**
- Used to refer to a global variable defined in **another source file** or elsewhere in the same file.
- **Scope:** Global (entire program).
- **Lifetime:** Entire program.
- **Default Value:** 0.
- **Storage:** Static segment.

```c
/* File1.c */
int shared = 100;

/* File2.c */
extern int shared;     /* declaration, not definition */
printf("%d", shared);  /* accesses the same global */
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *scope* of a static-local variable (which is still local) with its *lifetime* (which is global). Marks are deducted when these two properties are conflated. Always state them separately.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Explain *scope* and *lifetime* of variables in C. With a suitable program, demonstrate how a static local variable retains its value across function calls.

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Define scope and lifetime. Explain block scope, function scope, file scope, and program scope with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Scope** is the region of the program where a variable is *visible* (can be accessed by name). **Lifetime** is the duration during which the variable *occupies memory* and holds a valid value.

**(i) Block Scope:**
Variables declared inside a `{ }` block are accessible only within that block.

```c
{
    int x = 10;       /* x has block scope */
    printf("%d", x);  /* valid */
}
/* printf("%d", x);  /* ERROR: x not visible here */
```

**(ii) Function Scope:**
Applies exclusively to **labels** in `goto` statements — visible throughout the function regardless of nested blocks.

**(iii) File Scope:**
A global variable declared outside all functions is visible from its point of declaration to the end of the source file.

```c
int globalVar = 50;     /* file scope */
void func1() { printf("%d", globalVar); }
void func2() { printf("%d", globalVar); }
```

**(iv) Program Scope:**
A variable with `extern` linkage is visible across multiple source files of the same program.

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Write a C program to demonstrate that a `static` local variable retains its value across function calls.**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void visit() {
    static int visitCount = 0;   /* initialized only once */
    visitCount++;
    printf("Function called %d time(s)\n", visitCount);
}

int main() {
    visit();   /* Output: 1 */
    visit();   /* Output: 2 */
    visit();   /* Output: 3 */
    visit();   /* Output: 4 */
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of Output:**

The variable `visitCount` is declared as `static`. The key property is that it is **initialized only once** (when the program first loads), and its memory location in the static segment is preserved across function calls. Even though the function `visit()` exits and re-enters multiple times, `visitCount` does *not* lose its value — it is incremented from 1 to 4 across the four calls. A non-static `auto` variable would be re-initialized to 0 every time, producing output `1 1 1 1`.

**Memory Map:**
- `visitCount` is stored in the **static/data segment** of RAM, not on the stack.
- This is why its value survives the destruction and re-creation of the stack frame.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the program correctly but fail to *explain* WHY the static variable retains its value. Simply stating "because it is static" earns only partial marks. You must explicitly mention **initialization occurs only once** and **storage is in the data segment**, not the stack.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **variable** is a named memory location whose value may change during execution.
- A variable has **four attributes**: name, type, value, and memory address.
- **Local variables** have block scope; **global variables** have file/program scope.
- **Storage classes** in C are `auto`, `register`, `static`, and `extern`.
- A `static` **local** variable retains its value between function calls and is initialized only **once**.
- A `static` **global** variable has restricted linkage to its defining file.
- The `register` keyword is only a **hint**; modern compilers often ignore it.
- The `&` operator **cannot** be used on a register variable.
- Address of the $i$-th array element: $\text{Addr}(a[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{sizeof}(T))$.
- Range of signed `int` (32-bit): $-2^{31} \text{ to } 2^{31}-1$.
- Range of unsigned `int` (32-bit): $0 \text{ to } 2^{32}-1$.
- **Scope ≠ Lifetime** — always state both separately in exam answers.
- Uninitialized local variables contain **garbage**; uninitialized global/static variables default to **0**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Binding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Concept of Binding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Binding** is the process of associating an **attribute** (such as a *type*, *value*, *memory address*, or *function code*) with a **program entity** (such as a *variable*, *constant*, *function name*, or *parameter*). The exact moment at which this association is established and locked is referred to as the **Binding Time**.
