In the terminology of programming language design, every named entity in a program must be *bound* to its properties before the program can be executed. Binding is not a single event; rather, different attributes of the same entity may be bound at different times during the life cycle of a program — from language design up to program execution. The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) categorizes binding primarily along two axes: | Axis | Description | |---|---| | **What is bound?** | The attribute (type, value, location, behavior) | | **When is it bound?** | The binding time (compile, link, load, run) | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Name Tag" > [!NOTE] > Think of a **guest** in a hotel as the *program entity* (a variable). The guest has several "attributes" that get associated with them at different stages: > - **Identity** → bound when the guest is registered at the front desk (analogous to *compile time*) > - **Room Number** → bound when the receptionist hands over the key-card (analogous to *load time / runtime*) > - **Luggage Storage Slot** → bound every time the bellboy takes the bags (analogous to *dynamic runtime binding*) > > Just like in a hotel, the binding time in programming determines *how flexible*, *how safe*, *how fast*, and *how resource-hungry* the resulting program becomes. ### 1.3 Why Binding is a Pillar of Programming Language Theory Every executable statement in any language implicitly depends on bindings that have already occurred. Without bindings, the CPU would have no idea: - What **type** of data a name refers to (so it knows how many bytes to fetch). - What **memory location** to read/write. - Which **function body** to invoke when a call is made. - What **literal value** to substitute for a constant. Thus, understanding *binding* is foundational to understanding **type systems**, **memory management**, **polymorphism**, and **virtual machines** — all of which are high-weight topics in the KTU 2024 ESE. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Binding Time Spectrum A binding time is the *lifecycle stage* at which a particular attribute is fixed for a particular entity. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following ordered spectrum (earliest → latest): 1. **Language Design Time** — e.g., the meaning of `+` in C is fixed when the language is designed. 2. **Language Implementation Time** — e.g., the size of `int` in C is fixed when the compiler is written. 3. **Program Translation (Compile) Time** — e.g., the type of a variable declared in C is fixed at compile time. 4. **Link Time** — e.g., the address of a function defined in another `.c` file is fixed by the linker. 5. **Load Time** — e.g., the absolute load address of the program is fixed when the loader places it in RAM. 6. **Run Time** — e.g., the actual value of a variable entered by the user is bound only during execution. > [!NOTE] > **Golden Rule of Binding:** *A binding done at an earlier time cannot be changed at a later time without a corresponding cost (either runtime overhead, loss of flexibility, or both).* This is the central trade-off examined in KTU problems. ### 2.2 Classification of Binding — The Four Core Attributes Every program entity (variable, function, parameter) is associated with **four major attributes**, each of which has its own binding time: | # | Attribute Bound | Description | Earliest Possible Time | Latest Possible Time | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Type** | Data type (int, float, struct…) | Compile time | Runtime | | 2 | **Value** | The actual data stored | Compile time (constants) | Runtime (variables) | | 3 | **Memory Location / Address** | Where in memory the entity lives | Compile time (static) | Runtime (heap) | | 4 | **Function/Behavior** | Which code block executes on call | Compile time | Runtime (virtual dispatch) | ### 2.3 Static vs Dynamic Binding — The Core Dichotomy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Static Binding (Early Binding):** The binding is fixed **before the program runs**, typically at **compile time**. The compiler can resolve it without needing any runtime information. *Result:* faster execution, fewer runtime checks, but less flexible. > > **Dynamic Binding (Late Binding):** The binding is deferred until **runtime**. The compiler emits code that *looks up* the actual binding using metadata (e.g., a v-table) when the program executes. *Result:* slower execution, but extremely flexible — supports **polymorphism** and **runtime extensibility**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | One-Line Definition | KTU Keyword | |---|---|---| | **Static Binding** | Binding resolved at compile time | Early binding | | **Dynamic Binding** | Binding resolved at runtime | Late binding | | **Static Typing** | Variable type fixed at compile time | e.g., C, Java, C++ | | **Dynamic Typing** | Variable type fixed at runtime | e.g., Python, JavaScript | | **Static Memory Allocation** | Address fixed at compile time | Global/static variables | | **Stack Allocation** | Address bound at function-call time | Local variables | | **Heap Allocation** | Address bound on `malloc`/`new` | Runtime allocation | | **V-Table** | Lookup table used for late binding | C++ virtual functions | | **Polymorphism** | Same name, different behavior | Enabled by late binding | | **Type Binding** | `(variable, type)` pair | First binding by compiler | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compilers (GCC, Clang, javac):** Heavily rely on *early binding* for optimization — knowing the type at compile time lets them inline, unroll loops, and avoid branch mispredictions. - **Virtual Machines (JVM, .NET CLR):** Use *late binding* extensively to support dynamic class loading, reflection, and runtime polymorphism — critical for enterprise frameworks like Spring and .NET MVC. - **Embedded Systems & RTOS:** Prefer *early binding* and static allocation because the timing behavior must be 100% deterministic and the memory footprint is fixed. - **Game Engines & GUI Toolkits:** Depend on *late binding* for event handling (e.g., which function is called when a button is clicked is not known until the button is pressed at runtime). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Mathematical Formulation of a Binding A binding can be formally expressed as a **3-tuple relation** $\mathcal{B}$ that connects an entity to one of its attributes at a specific time. Let: - $\mathcal{E}$ = the set of all program entities (variables, functions, parameters). - $\mathcal{A}$ = the set of all possible attributes (type, value, address, behavior). - $\mathcal{V}$ = the set of all valid values for each attribute. - $\mathcal{T}$ = the discrete set of binding times $T = \{t_0, t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$. Then a binding is a relation: $$ \mathcal{B} \subseteq \mathcal{E} \times \mathcal{A} \times \mathcal{V} \times \mathcal{T} $$ This means a binding is a quadruple: $\big( e,\; a,\; v,\; t \big)$ such that at time $t$, the entity $e$ is associated with value $v$ of attribute $a$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Binding-Time Decision Rule Consider a variable $x$ of type `int` in a C program. The compiler must decide *when* to bind $x$ to its address. We can compare the two extreme choices: **Case 1 — Bind at compile time (static):** $$ \text{addr}(x) = \text{base} + \text{offset}_x $$ where `base` is the segment base and `offset` is fixed by the compiler. No runtime address calculation is required. The instruction generated is a single **direct addressing mode** instruction. **Cost:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per access. **Flexibility:** zero — $x$ can never be moved. **Case 2 — Bind at runtime (dynamic):** $$ \text{addr}(x) = \text{contents}(\text{ptr\_reg}) $$ The address lives inside a pointer register, which itself is loaded at runtime. Access requires one **indirection** (a memory load of the pointer first, then a load of the value). **Cost:** $\mathcal{O}(2)$ per access. **Flexibility:** full — $x$ can be relocated freely (e.g., in a garbage-collected heap). ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Walk-Through of a Polymorphic Call To see late binding in action, consider the classic KTU favorite: a base-class pointer calling a derived-class method. **Step 1 — Compiler phase (early binding decision):** The compiler sees `ptr->draw();` but **does not know** which `draw()` to bind. It therefore emits code that says: *"at runtime, look up the correct function in the v-table."* **Step 2 — Object construction (runtime phase):** When a `Circle` object is created, its v-table pointer `vptr` is set to point to the `Circle` v-table. **Step 3 — The actual late binding (runtime phase):** $$ \text{addr}(\text{draw}) = \text{contents}\big(\text{addr}(\text{vptr}) + \text{slot\_offset}\big) $$ The CPU reads the v-table, fetches the function pointer, then jumps to it. **Step 4 — Cost summary:** $$ T_{\text{late}} = T_{\text{vptr\_load}} + T_{\text{slot\_load}} + T_{\text{indirect\_call}} $$ $$ T_{\text{early}} = T_{\text{direct\_call}} $$ $$ \therefore \quad T_{\text{late}} \;=\; 3 \cdot T_{\text{memory}} + T_{\text{jump}} \quad\text{vs}\quad T_{\text{early}} \;=\; T_{\text{jump}} $$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Binding Strategies | Property | Early Binding (Static) | Late Binding (Dynamic) | |---|---|---| | **Binding Time** | Compile time | Run time | | **Speed** | **Faster** (direct call / addressing) | **Slower** (indirections involved) | | **Flexibility** | Low — fixed at compile time | High — resolved as needed | | **Code Size** | Smaller (no metadata) | Larger (v-tables, type info) | | **Compiler Support** | Type checking, optimization | Deferral of resolution | | **Supports Polymorphism** | No (compile-time only) | Yes (runtime dispatch) | | **Memory Footprint** | Lower | Higher (metadata tables) | | **Typical Use** | Statically-typed languages, embedded | OOP languages, interpreters, VMs | | **Example** | C function call, Java `final` method | C++ `virtual`, Java instance method | | **Error Detection Time** | **Compile time** (safer) | **Runtime** (errors surface later) | ### 3.5 The Binding-Time Trade-off Function Let $P$ be the *program attribute* (e.g., type), $t$ be the binding time, and $C(t)$ be the combined cost: $$ C(t) \;=\; \alpha \cdot R(t) \;+\; \beta \cdot F(t) \;+\; \gamma \cdot M(t) $$ where: - $R(t)$ = runtime overhead cost (increases as $t$ moves later). - $F(t)$ = flexibility gained (increases as $t$ moves later). - $M(t)$ = memory-metadata cost (increases as $t$ moves later). - $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are **weight factors** decided by the language designer. > **Interpretation:** A *systems language* chooses $\alpha$ large (penalizing late binding) and $\beta$ small — favoring early binding. A *scripting/AI language* chooses $\beta$ large — favoring late binding even at the cost of speed. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Binding-Time Spectrum (Earliest → Latest) ```mermaid flowchart LR n0["Language Design Time"] --> n1["Language Implementation Time"] n1 --> n2["Program Translation Time"] n2 --> n3["Link Time"] n3 --> n4["Load Time"] n4 --> n5["Run Time"] n5 --> n6["Impossible Later"] style n0 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c style n1 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c style n2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style n3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style n4 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style n5 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style n6 fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each box is a binding time. An attribute bound earlier (left) is **frozen** and cannot be changed by later events. An attribute bound later (right) provides **flexibility** at the cost of runtime machinery. ### 4.2 Static vs Dynamic Binding — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Code Statement] --> B{Compiler Sees Call?} B -- Can resolve
type and target? --> C[Static Path: Early Binding] B -- Cannot resolve
virtual or generic? --> D[Dynamic Path: Late Binding] C --> C1["Direct address / call emitted"] C1 --> C2["Single instruction: CALL function_addr"] C2 --> Z[CPU Executes Target] D --> D1["Emit v-table lookup code"] D1 --> D2["Load vptr from object header"] D2 --> D3["Load function pointer from slot"] D3 --> D4["Indirect CALL through register"] D4 --> Z style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style D fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style Z fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 ``` ### 4.3 Memory-Address Binding Pipeline (Three-Stage) ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["Stage 1: Compile Time
Symbolic Address Generated
Example: variable x"] --> s2["Stage 2: Link/Load Time
Relocatable Address Assigned
Example: x -> OFFSET 0x1004"] s2 --> s3["Stage 3: Run Time
Physical Address Computed
Example: x -> PHYS 0x00401004"] s3a["Alternative Heap Path
malloc at runtime
x -> ptr returned at line 42"] -.-> s3 style s1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style s2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style s3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style s3a fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ### 4.4 Mapping Attributes to Binding Times (Entity-Level View) ```mermaid flowchart TD ent["Program Entity: variable x"] --> a1["Attribute 1: Type
Bound at: COMPILE TIME"] ent --> a2["Attribute 2: Value
Bound at: RUN TIME (or compile time if constant)"] ent --> a3["Attribute 3: Memory Address
Bound at: LINK/LOAD TIME (static) or RUN TIME (heap)"] ent --> a4["Attribute 4: Scope/Visibility
Bound at: COMPILE TIME"] style ent fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8 style a1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style a2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style a3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style a4 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **binding** in the context of programming languages. List any **two** major binding times during a program's life cycle. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Binding** is the association of an attribute (such as a *type*, *value*, *memory address*, or *function code*) with a program entity (such as a *variable* or *function name*). > > **Two major binding times:** > 1. **Compile Time Binding** — e.g., the type of a C variable `int x;` is bound to `x` by the compiler. (1½ marks) > 2. **Run Time Binding** — e.g., the value stored in `x` is bound only when the program executes an assignment statement. (1½ marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **static binding** and **dynamic binding** with one example each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Binding (Early) | Dynamic Binding (Late) | |---|---|---| | **When** | Compile time | Run time | | **Speed** | Faster | Slower (indirections) | | **Example** | A non-virtual C++ function call resolved at compile time | A C++ `virtual` function call resolved at runtime through the v-table | (1½ marks for the table; 1½ marks for the examples.) --- ### 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]* **(a)** Explain the **concept of binding** in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the **binding time spectrum** from language design time to run time. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Binding is the association of an attribute with a program entity. The point in the software life cycle at which this association is established is called the **binding time**. The binding-time spectrum, from earliest to latest, is as follows: 1. **Language Design Time** — Properties fixed by the language spec, e.g., precedence of `*` over `+` in C. (1 mark) 2. **Language Implementation Time** — Properties fixed when the compiler/interpreter is built, e.g., size of `int = 4 bytes` in GCC. (1 mark) 3. **Program Translation (Compile) Time** — Types of variables, scope rules, bound by the compiler. (1 mark) 4. **Link Time** — Cross-module references resolved, e.g., a `printf` call in `main.c` linked to `libc.so`. (1 mark) 5. **Load Time** — Absolute memory addresses of the program fixed when the OS loader places it in RAM. (1 mark) 6. **Run Time** — Variable values, heap allocations, and polymorphic dispatches bound during execution. (1 mark) 7. **Diagram** (1 mark): ``` Language Design → Implementation → Compile → Link → Load → Run (earliest) (latest) (most efficient) (most flexible) ``` **(b)** Compare **static and dynamic binding** with reference to the following: binding time, execution speed, flexibility, memory requirement, and an example in C++. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Criterion | Static (Early) Binding | Dynamic (Late) Binding | |---|---|---| | **Binding time** | Compile time | Run time | | **Execution speed** | Faster — direct CALL instruction | Slower — needs v-table lookup | | **Flexibility** | Low — fixed at compile time | High — supports polymorphism | | **Memory requirement** | Lower (no v-table) | Higher (v-table per class) | | **C++ example** | `obj.show();` where `show()` is non-virtual | `Base *p = new Derived; p->show();` where `show()` is `virtual` | In C++, when a method is declared `virtual`, the compiler emits code to resolve the call at runtime using the object's v-table pointer. For non-virtual methods, the call is bound at compile time to the statically-known function address. (3 marks for the table, 4 marks for the explanation and C++ example.) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** What is **type binding**? Explain the difference between **static type binding** and **dynamic type binding** with one example language for each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Type binding** is the association of a data type with a variable name. It determines how much memory is allocated and what operations are legal on that variable. - **Static Type Binding:** The type of a variable is fixed at compile time and cannot change during execution. (2 marks) - **Example Language:** C or Java. - **Example Code Concept:** `int x;` — once declared, `x` is always an `int` throughout its scope. - **Dynamic Type Binding:** The type of a variable is determined at runtime and may change as different values are assigned. (2 marks) - **Example Language:** Python or JavaScript. - **Example Code Concept:** `x = 5` (int) followed by `x = "hello"` (str) — the same variable holds different types at different times. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Static Type Binding | Dynamic Type Binding | |---|---|---| | Type decided at | Compile time | Run time | | Type errors caught at | Compile time | Run time | | Performance | Higher | Lower (needs type tags) | | Flexibility | Lower | Higher | | Example | C, Java | Python, JavaScript | **(b)** With the help of a **v-table diagram**, explain how **late binding** is implemented for **virtual functions in C++**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** When a class contains one or more `virtual` functions, the C++ compiler generates a **virtual function table (v-table)** for that class. Each object of the class carries a hidden pointer `vptr` that points to the v-table of its actual class. **V-Table Diagram (3 marks):** ``` Class Base Class Derived (inherits Base) +-------------------+ +-------------------+ | vptr ---------+ | | vptr ---------+ | +---------------|---+ +---------------|---+ | | v v +-------------------+ +-------------------+ | slot 0: Base::foo | | slot 0: Derived::foo | | slot 1: Base::bar | | slot 1: Base::bar | +-------------------+ +-------------------+ Base* p = new Derived(); p->foo(); // LATE BINDING in action ``` **Step-by-step execution of `p->foo();` (4 marks):** 1. Compiler sees a virtual call — emits indirect call code. 2. At runtime, the CPU reads `p->vptr` (the v-table pointer of the actual `Derived` object). 3. CPU fetches the function pointer at `slot 0` of the v-table → finds `Derived::foo`. 4. CPU performs an indirect CALL to that address → `Derived::foo` executes. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Do NOT confuse** *binding of address* with *binding of type* — they are two different attributes of the same variable. > - **Do NOT state** that late binding is "always bad" — it is *necessary* for runtime polymorphism. The question tests whether you understand the trade-off, not whether you blindly prefer one. > - **Always show the v-table** in answers about virtual functions — examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for the diagram. > - **In C++**, if the base-class method is **not** declared `virtual`, then the call is bound *statically* even with a base pointer. Many students lose marks by assuming every base-pointer call is virtual. --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Binding** = associating an attribute with a program entity. - ✅ **Binding time** = the moment this association is locked in. - ✅ The **binding-time spectrum** runs from *Language Design Time* (earliest) to *Run Time* (latest). - ✅ **Static (Early) Binding** = at compile time → fast but inflexible. - ✅ **Dynamic (Late) Binding** = at run time → flexible but slower and memory-hungry. - ✅ The **four core attributes** of any entity that get bound: *Type*, *Value*, *Address*, *Behavior*. - ✅ **V-tables** are the standard mechanism for late binding in C++. - ✅ **Statically-typed** languages (C, Java) bind types early; **dynamically-typed** languages (Python, JS) bind types late. - ✅ **Memory-address binding** can be at compile time (static globals), at call time (stack locals), or at `malloc`/`new` time (heap). - ✅ The **trade-off** is always *speed* (early) **vs** *flexibility* (late) — and language designers pick a position on this spectrum based on their goals. - ✅ **KTU tip:** When asked to "explain binding with an example," *always* include a code snippet **and** state the binding time explicitly for each attribute.

topics.title: Scope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In computer programming, **Scope** refers to the **region or context within a program** where a particular identifier (variable, function, class, or object) is **valid, visible, and accessible** to the compiler or interpreter. It defines the **boundaries of name resolution** — i.e., the area of source code where a declared name can be used without ambiguity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Scope is the textual region of a program where a name (identifier) binding is valid. It is a compile-time (static) or runtime (dynamic) property that determines the visibility and lifetime of variables, functions, and other named entities.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT746), **Scope** is treated as a foundational concept that governs:
1. **Name Visibility** — Where a name can be referenced.
2. **Name Binding** — How a name maps to an entity (variable, function, etc.).
3. **Lifetime / Storage Duration** — How long the entity exists in memory.
4. **Conflict Resolution** — How the compiler resolves name collisions in nested regions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Building 🏢**
>
> Think of a large corporate office building with multiple floors and rooms:
>
> - **Global Scope = The Building's Main Lobby Notice Board** — A notice placed here is visible to *every* employee in the building.
> - **Local Scope = A Private Cubicle Notice** — A memo pinned inside a specific cubicle is only visible to the person working in that cubicle.
> - **Block Scope = A Whiteboard inside a Meeting Room** — Once the meeting ends and the room is closed, the whiteboard is erased. The notice existed only during that block.
> - **Function Scope = A Department** — Only members of that department (function body) can see the department-internal announcements.
> - **Nested Scope = An Office inside a Department inside the Building** — The innermost office can see all outer announcements (downward lookup), but the lobby cannot see the private cubicle memo (no upward access).
>
> Just as you cannot walk into a closed office and read its whiteboard from the lobby, a program **cannot access a variable outside its scope**.

---

## 1.3 Why Scope Matters in Advanced Programming

| Aspect | Without Proper Scope Management | With Proper Scope Management |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Variables persist unnecessarily, wasting RAM | Variables are reclaimed as soon as scope ends |
| **Security** | Any function can tamper with any data | Sensitive data is encapsulated in restricted scopes |
| **Maintainability** | Name collisions cause silent bugs | Predictable, modular, reusable code |
| **Concurrency** | Shared state causes race conditions | Thread-local scoping enables parallelism |
| **Debugging** | Tracing bugs across thousands of lines is painful | Localized scope simplifies trace analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The examiner frequently tests the difference between **Scope** (a *compile-time* concept) and **Lifetime** (a *runtime* concept). Remember: a variable can be **in scope but not alive** (e.g., a `static` local variable before its first call), or **alive but not in scope** (e.g., a global variable hidden by a local redeclaration).

---

## 1.4 Types of Scope (Master Classification)

The KTU syllabus categorizes scope into the following **canonical types**:

1. **Local Scope** — Inside a function or block.
2. **Global Scope** — Outside all functions, at file/program level.
3. **Block Scope** — Inside `{}` braces (C99+, C++, Java, ES6+ JS).
4. **Function Scope** — Throughout the entire function body (e.g., `var` in JavaScript).
5. **Class Scope** — Inside a class definition (C++, Java, C#).
6. **Namespace Scope** — Inside a named namespace (C++).
7. **Function Prototype Scope** — Only inside a function parameter list declaration.
8. **File Scope** — Visible only within a single translation unit (C `static` globals).

---

## 1.5 SCOPE vs. LIFETIME vs. VISIBILITY — The Holy Trinity

These three terms are **frequently confused** in KTU exams. The distinction is critical:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope** = *Where* the name can be referenced (textual region of source code).
> **Lifetime (Storage Duration)** = *When* the storage exists in memory (runtime duration).
> **Visibility (Linkage)** = *Whether* the name is hidden by another name of the same kind.

| Concept | Question Answered | Determined At | Example (C++) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | "Where can I write this name?" | Compile time | `int x;` inside `{}` is visible only inside `{}` |
| **Lifetime** | "When does memory exist?" | Runtime | `static int x;` lives until program termination |
| **Visibility** | "Is the name shadowed?" | Compile time | Local `x` shadows global `x` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: The LEGB Rule (Python — Most Tested in KTU Labs)

Python uses the **LEGB Rule** for name resolution, which is one of the most frequently asked questions in KTU 2024 module tests.

**LEGB** stands for the order in which Python searches for a name:

1. **L — Local**: Names assigned within the current function (innermost).
2. **E — Enclosing**: Names in any enclosing functions (for nested functions).
3. **G — Global**: Names assigned at the top-level of a module or declared `global`.
4. **B — Built-in**: Pre-defined names in the `builtins` module (e.g., `print`, `len`, `range`).

### 2.1.1 The `global` and `nonlocal` Keywords

- `global x` — Tells Python: "Treat `x` as referring to the module-level name. Assigning to it modifies the global."
- `nonlocal x` — Tells Python: "Treat `x` as referring to the nearest enclosing scope (excluding global)."

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting the `global` declaration when assigning to a global variable inside a function. Python will *unintentionally* create a new local variable, causing `UnboundLocalError` at runtime.

---

## 2.2 C/C++ Scoping Rules (The Compiler's Perspective)

C and C++ enforce **static (lexical) scoping** — the scope is determined by the **physical layout of the source code**, not by the runtime call stack.

### 2.2.1 Block Scope Rules
- A variable declared inside a block `{ ... }` is visible from its point of declaration to the closing brace.
- Nested blocks can **shadow** outer variables.
- A variable declared inside a `for` initialization (C99+) is limited to the loop.

### 2.2.2 Storage Class Specifiers (KTU Favorite Topic)

| Specifier | Scope | Lifetime | Default Value | Storage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `auto` | Block | Block | Garbage | Stack |
| `register` | Block | Block | Garbage | CPU Register (hint) |
| `static` | Block | **Entire Program** | **0** | Data Segment |
| `extern` | File / Global | **Entire Program** | **0** | Data Segment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Local Variable Magic:** A `static` variable inside a function is **scoped locally** (only the function can see it) but **lives for the entire program duration**. Its value persists across multiple function calls — this is a classic KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 2.3 Java Scoping Rules

Java has **strict block scoping** with no `register` or `extern`. Key features:
- **Method-local variables**: Scope = block where declared.
- **Class-level fields**: Scope = entire class (subject to access modifiers).
- **`this` keyword**: Refers to the current object instance.
- **No global variables** in Java (everything lives inside a class).
- **Effectively final** variables can be captured by lambdas.

---

## 2.4 JavaScript Scoping (The `var` vs `let` vs `const` Saga)

This is a **highly tested** area in KTU's web/frontend modules.

| Keyword | Scope | Hoisted? | Reassignable? | Redeclarable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `var` | **Function** scope | Yes (`undefined`) | Yes | Yes |
| `let` | **Block** scope | Yes (TDZ) | Yes | No |
| `const` | **Block** scope | Yes (TDZ) | **No** | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TDZ = Temporal Dead Zone:** Variables declared with `let`/`const` are hoisted but **not initialized**. Accessing them before the declaration line throws `ReferenceError`. This is a KTU favorite!

---

## 2.5 The Shadowing Phenomenon

**Variable shadowing** occurs when a variable declared in an inner scope has the **same name** as a variable in an outer scope. The inner variable temporarily *hides* the outer one.

```c
// C Language Example
#include <stdio.h>
int x = 100;          // Global scope

void demo() {
    int x = 50;       // Local scope — shadows the global x
    printf("%d", x);  // Prints 50
}

int main() {
    printf("%d", x);  // Prints 100
    demo();
    return 0;
}
```

In C++, a nested class can shadow an outer class name. In Java, method parameters can shadow instance fields (use `this.fieldName` to disambiguate).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Concept | C / C++ | Java | Python | JavaScript |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default scope of variable | Block | Block | Function/Module | `var`=Function, `let`=Block |
| 2 | Global declaration keyword | `extern` / definition | N/A (use class) | `global` | Implicit (no `var`) |
| 3 | Static local persistence | ✅ `static int` | ❌ (use class field) | ✅ via closure | ✅ via closure |
| 4 | Block scope (`{}`) | ✅ (C99+) | ✅ | ✅ (no block, but comprehensions have it) | ✅ with `let/const` |
| 5 | Nested function access | Limited | ❌ (use lambda) | ✅ (closure + `nonlocal`) | ✅ (closure) |
| 6 | Hoisting | ❌ (declaration only) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (`var` only) |
| 7 | TDZ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (`let`/`const`) |
| 8 | Name resolution order | Lexical, nearest first | Lexical, nearest first | **LEGB** | Lexical, nearest first |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How Scope is Used |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (C)** | `static` locals preserve sensor calibration between interrupts. |
| **Operating Systems (C)** | `static` globals in kernel modules limit linkage to file — prevents symbol pollution. |
| **Web Development (JS)** | IIFEs and `let` blocks prevent global namespace pollution. |
| **Data Science (Python)** | LEGB rule resolves variable shadowing in Jupyter notebooks (very common bug). |
| **Game Development (C++)** | RAII + block scope ensures deterministic resource cleanup. |
| **Compiler Design** | Symbol tables use nested scope stacks (one per block). |
| **Cybersecurity** | Encapsulation through scope prevents unauthorized data leakage. |

---

## 2.8 Symbol Table & Scope Stack (Compiler Internals)

Compilers maintain a **symbol table** for each scope. When entering a block, a new table is pushed onto the **scope stack**. When leaving the block, the table is popped.

> **Visualization (Compiler's View):**
>
> ```
> Global Scope        →  [x, y, function: main]
> └── main() Scope    →  [a, b]
>     └── if-block    →  [temp]     ← pushed
>     └── (if ends)   →  [a, b]     ← temp popped
> └── func() Scope    →  [param: p, q]
> ```

This stack-based approach makes **lexical scoping** O(1) per lookup and trivially supports recursion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Traces & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section contains exhaustive code walkthroughs and analytical reasoning steps. Since "Scope" is a behavioral programming concept, derivations here take the form of **execution traces, memory maps, and resolution chains** rather than algebraic equations.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Trace #1: LEGB Resolution in Python

Consider the following Python program — a classic KTU module test question:

```python
x = "global"                    # G - Global scope

def outer():
    x = "enclosing"             # E - Enclosing scope
    def inner():
        x = "local"             # L - Local scope
        print("Inner sees:", x)
    inner()
    print("Outer sees:", x)

outer()
print("Global sees:", x)
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1 — Module Load:**
Python executes the module body top-to-bottom.
- `x = "global"` creates a binding in the module's `__dict__`.
- The function objects `outer` and (later) `inner` are created.
- Neither function is called yet — no inner scopes are active.

**Step 2 — Call `outer()`:**
- A new local namespace is created for `outer`.
- `x = "enclosing"` binds `x` in `outer`'s namespace.
- `inner` is defined and binds to `outer`'s local namespace.
- `inner()` is called → new local namespace for `inner`.

**Step 3 — Call `inner()`:**
- `x = "local"` binds `x` in `inner`'s local namespace.
- `print("Inner sees:", x)` → Python looks up `x`:
  - **L** check: Found `"local"` ✅ → Prints `Inner sees: local`.

**Step 4 — Return from `inner()`:**
- `inner`'s namespace is destroyed.
- Back in `outer`, `print("Outer sees:", x)`:
  - **L** check in `outer`: Found `"enclosing"` ✅ → Prints `Outer sees: enclosing`.

**Step 5 — Return from `outer()`:**
- `outer`'s namespace is destroyed.
- `print("Global sees:", x)`:
  - **L** check at module level: not found.
  - **G** check in module: Found `"global"` ✅ → Prints `Global sees: global`.

### Output
```
Inner sees: local
Outer sees: enclosing
Global sees: global
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Trace #2: The `nonlocal` Keyword in Action

```python
def counter():
    count = 0
    def increment():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count
    return increment

c = counter()
print(c())  # ?
print(c())  # ?
print(c())  # ?
```

### Step-by-Step Resolution

**Step 1 — `counter()` call:**
- Creates local namespace for `counter` with `count = 0`.
- Defines `increment`, returns the function object.
- `counter`'s namespace **does not die** because `increment` holds a reference to it (closure).

**Step 2 — `c()` first call (which is `increment()`):**
- **L** check: `count` not in `increment`'s local scope.
- Without `nonlocal`, Python would create a *new* local `count` and raise `UnboundLocalError` on `count += 1`.
- With `nonlocal count`, Python treats `count` as a reference to `counter`'s namespace binding.
- `count = 0 + 1 = 1` → returns `1`.

**Step 3 — `c()` second call:**
- `count = 1 + 1 = 2` → returns `2`.

**Step 4 — `c()` third call:**
- `count = 2 + 1 = 3` → returns `3`.

### Output
```
1
2
3
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This is how Python implements **encapsulation and persistence** without classes. The `nonlocal` keyword is the bridge between local and enclosing scopes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Trace #3: C `static` Local Variable

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void counter() {
    static int count = 0;   // Initialized only ONCE
    count++;
    printf("Call %d: count = %d\n", count, count);
}

int main() {
    counter();
    counter();
    counter();
    return 0;
}
```

### Memory Map Analysis

**Step 1 — Program starts:**
- `static int count` is allocated in the **Data Segment (BSS/Initialized)**.
- Initial value: `0` (only on the first call, not on every entry into `counter`).

**Step 2 — First `counter()` call:**
- The variable is in scope (line 4), but its storage already exists in the data segment.
- The assignment `count = 0` is **skipped** on subsequent calls.
- `count` becomes `1` → prints `Call 1: count = 1`.

**Step 3 — Second `counter()` call:**
- Storage still alive, value still `1`.
- `count` becomes `2` → prints `Call 2: count = 2`.

**Step 4 — Third `counter()` call:**
- `count` becomes `3` → prints `Call 3: count = 3`.

### Output
```
Call 1: count = 1
Call 2: count = 2
Call 3: count = 3
```

### Scope vs. Lifetime Summary
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Scope** | Only inside `counter()` (lines 4–7) |
| **Lifetime** | Entire program execution (data segment) |
| **Visibility from `main`?** | No — it is hidden outside `counter` |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Trace #4: JavaScript Hoisting and TDZ

```javascript
console.log(a);  // Line 1
console.log(b);  // Line 2

var a = "I am var";
let b = "I am let";
```

### Step-by-Step Trace (Hoisting Phase)

**Step 1 — Compilation / Hoisting:**
The JavaScript engine scans the entire function/module and creates memory for all declarations:
- `var a` is **hoisted and initialized** to `undefined`.
- `let b` is **hoisted but uninitialized** → placed in **Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)**.

**Step 2 — Line 1: `console.log(a)`:**
- `a` is `undefined` (hoisted with default init) → prints `undefined`.

**Step 3 — Line 2: `console.log(b)`:**
- `b` is in TDZ → throws `ReferenceError: Cannot access 'b' before initialization`.

### Output
```
undefined
ReferenceError: Cannot access 'b' before initialization
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume `let` is "not hoisted". The truth is: `let` **is hoisted, but in TDZ**. This distinction is frequently tested.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Trace #5: Java Block Scope in `for` Loops

```java
public class ScopeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            int x = i * 10;
            System.out.println("i=" + i + ", x=" + x);
        }
        // System.out.println(i); // COMPILE ERROR: i not in scope
        // System.out.println(x); // COMPILE ERROR: x not in scope
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Resolution

**Step 1 — Enter `for` loop:**
- `i` is declared in the `for` initialization block.
- `i` is **only** visible within the `for(...)` parenthesis **and** the loop body.

**Step 2 — Enter loop body `{ }`:**
- `int x` is declared inside the body block.
- `x` is only visible from its declaration to the closing `}` of the body.
- **New `x` is allocated on stack for every iteration.**

**Step 3 — Loop iteration ends:**
- `x` goes out of scope → memory reclaimed (stack popped).
- `i` updates and is re-checked — still in scope.

**Step 4 — Loop ends:**
- `i` goes out of scope.
- The commented `println` statements would cause **compilation errors**, not runtime errors.

### Output
```
i=0, x=0
i=1, x=10
i=2, x=20
```

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | C | C++ | Java | Python | JavaScript |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lexical scoping** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Dynamic scoping** | ❌ (unless macro hack) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (mostly; `this` is dynamic) |
| **Block scope with `{}`** | ✅ (C99+) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (partially) | ✅ (`let`/`const`) |
| **Static locals** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (use class) | ✅ (via closure) | ✅ (via closure) |
| **Global keyword** | Implicit + `extern` | Implicit + `::` | N/A (class-based) | `global` | Implicit (no `var`) |
| **Nested function** | ❌ (GCC extension) | ❌ (use lambda) | ❌ (use lambda) | ✅ | ✅ |
| **`nonlocal`/`this`-like** | N/A | N/A | `this` | `nonlocal` | `this` (dynamic) |
| **Hoisting** | ❌ (declarations) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (`var`) |
| **TDZ** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (`let`/`const`) |

---

## 3.7 Algorithm: Name Resolution in a Nested Scope Tree

The compiler/interpreter executes the following algorithm for every name lookup:

**Step 1.** Start at the **innermost active scope** (the current block/function).

**Step 2.** Search the symbol table of the current scope for the name.
- If found → return the binding (with shadowing precedence).

**Step 3.** If not found, move to the **parent (enclosing) scope**.

**Step 4.** Repeat Steps 2–3 until the **global scope** is searched.

**Step 5.** If still not found, check the **built-in** namespace (Python) or report **`Undeclared Identifier` / `ReferenceError`** (C/JS).

**Complexity Analysis:**
- **Time:** $\mathcal{O}(d)$, where $d$ = scope depth (number of nested blocks).
- **Space:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per lookup (just stack traversal).

$$T_{\text{lookup}}(d) = \mathcal{O}(d), \quad S_{\text{lookup}} = \mathcal{O}(1)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Nested Scope Hierarchy (Python LEGB Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    B[Built-in Scope<br/>print, len, range] --> G[Global Scope<br/>x = global, outer]
    G --> O[Enclosing Scope<br/>outer function<br/>x = enclosing]
    O --> I[Local Scope<br/>inner function<br/>x = local]
    I --> R[Name Resolution Flow<br/>L E G B]
    R --> L1[Step 1: Search Local]
    L1 --> L2{Found?}
    L2 -- Yes --> DONE[Return Local Binding]
    L2 -- No --> E1[Step 2: Search Enclosing]
    E1 --> E2{Found?}
    E2 -- Yes --> DONE2[Return Enclosing Binding]
    E2 -- No --> G1[Step 3: Search Global]
    G1 --> G2{Found?}
    G2 -- Yes --> DONE3[Return Global Binding]
    G2 -- No --> B1[Step 4: Search Built-in]
    B1 --> B2{Found?}
    B2 -- Yes --> DONE4[Return Built-in Binding]
    B2 -- No --> ERR[NameError Raised]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Scope Lifetime Comparison (C static vs auto)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PROG[Program Lifetime]
        direction TB
        A1[Function Called] --> A2[auto variable created on stack]
        A2 --> A3[auto used inside function]
        A3 --> A4[Function Returns]
        A4 --> A5[auto variable destroyed]
    end

    subgraph STATIC[Static Local Lifetime]
        direction TB
        S1[Program Starts] --> S2[static variable allocated in data segment]
        S2 --> S3[First function call: initialized to 0]
        S3 --> S4[Subsequent calls: value preserved]
        S4 --> S5[Function returns: variable remains in memory]
        S5 --> S6[Next call: same value reused]
        S6 --> S7[Program Ends]
        S7 --> S8[static variable deallocated]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: JavaScript Variable Lifecycle (var vs let vs const)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Source Code Parsed] --> H1[var declarations hoisted<br/>initialized to undefined]
    START --> H2[let declarations hoisted<br/>placed in TDZ]
    START --> H3[const declarations hoisted<br/>placed in TDZ]

    H1 --> EXE1[Execution starts]
    EXE1 --> ACC1[Access allowed<br/>returns undefined]
    ACC1 --> ASS1[var x = value<br/>assignment happens]

    H2 --> EXE2[Execution reaches declaration line]
    EXE2 --> TDZ1[Outside TDZ: initialized]
    TDZ1 --> ACC2[Access allowed<br/>returns value]

    H2 -.->|Before declaration line| ERR1[ReferenceError: TDZ violation]

    H3 --> EXE3[Execution reaches declaration line]
    EXE3 --> TDZ2[Outside TDZ: initialized]
    TDZ2 --> ACC3[Access allowed]
    ACC3 -.->|Attempt reassign| ERR2[TypeError: Assignment to constant]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Compiler Symbol Table Stack Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STK[Scope Stack during execution of main]
        direction TB
        TOP[Top of Stack: Current Block Scope<br/>Symbol Table: temp, i]
        MID1[Level 1: Function Scope<br/>Symbol Table: a, b, x]
        MID2[Level 2: Global Scope<br/>Symbol Table: x, y, main, func]
        BOT[Bottom: Built-in / Predefined<br/>Symbol Table: printf, malloc]
    end

    TOP --> MID1
    MID1 --> MID2
    MID2 --> BOT

    LOOKUP[Name Lookup Request: 'x'] --> SEARCH1[Search TOP]
    SEARCH1 --> F1{Found?}
    F1 -- No --> SEARCH2[Search MID1]
    SEARCH2 --> F2{Found?}
    F2 -- Yes --> RES1[Return MID1 binding: local x]
    F2 -- No --> SEARCH3[Search MID2]
    SEARCH3 --> F3{Found?}
    F3 -- Yes --> RES2[Return MID2 binding: global x]
    F3 -- No --> SEARCH4[Search BOT]
    SEARCH4 --> F4{Found?}
    F4 -- No --> ERR3[Compiler Error: undeclared identifier]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Scope vs Lifetime vs Visibility (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Identifier Declared] --> B[Has Scope]
    A --> C[Has Lifetime]
    A --> D[Has Visibility]

    B --> B1[Compile-time concept]
    B1 --> B2[Textual region in source code]
    B2 --> B3[Examples: local, global, block, class]

    C --> C1[Runtime concept]
    C1 --> C2[Duration in memory]
    C2 --> C3[Examples: static, automatic, dynamic]

    D --> D1[Linked to shadowing rules]
    D1 --> D2[Hidden by inner declaration?]
    D2 --> D3[Examples: shadowed, accessible, hidden]
```

---

## 4.6 Architecture Block Diagram: Multi-File Program Scope (C/C++)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FILE1[File 1: main.c]
        direction TB
        G1[Global int x]
        F1[static int file1_secret]
        FN1[function main]
    end

    subgraph FILE2[File 2: helper.c]
        direction TB
        G2[Global int y]
        F2[static int file2_secret]
        FN2[function helper]
    end

    subgraph HEADER[File 3: shared.h]
        direction TB
        EXT[extern int x]
        DECL[function prototypes]
    end

    FILE1 -.->|includes| HEADER
    FILE2 -.->|includes| HEADER
    HEADER -.->|links x across files| FILE1
    HEADER -.->|links x across files| FILE2

    F1 -.->|Cannot access| F2
    F2 -.->|Cannot access| F1
    G1 -.->|Visible in both via extern| G2
```

---

## 4.7 Scope Resolution Flowchart (General Algorithm)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Program needs to resolve name N] --> CUR[Start at current scope]
    CUR --> CHK{In current scope?}
    CHK -- Yes --> USE[Use this binding]
    CHK -- No --> UP[Move to enclosing scope]
    UP --> CHK2{More enclosing scopes?}
    CHK2 -- Yes --> CHK
    CHK2 -- No --> GLOBAL[Check global/module scope]
    GLOBAL --> CHK3{Found?}
    CHK3 -- Yes --> USE
    CHK3 -- No --> BUILTIN[Check built-in scope]
    BUILTIN --> CHK4{Found?}
    CHK4 -- Yes --> USE
    CHK4 -- No --> ERR[Error: Undefined Name]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Scope" of a variable. How is it different from "Lifetime" of a variable?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Scope** is the region of the program (textual/lexical) where a variable's name is **valid and can be referenced**. It is a **compile-time** concept.

**Lifetime** (or **storage duration**) is the period during program execution for which the **memory** allocated to the variable **remains reserved**. It is a **runtime** concept.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Scope | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Compile-time (static) | Runtime (dynamic) |
| Defines | Name visibility | Memory existence |
| Determined by | Source code structure | Storage class / allocation |
| Example | `int x;` inside `{}` | `static int x;` lives till program end |

**Example illustrating the difference:** A `static` local variable has **local scope** (only the function can see it) but **global lifetime** (lives till program ends).

> **Marking Note (KTU):** Award **1 mark** for the definition of scope, **1 mark** for lifetime definition, **1 mark** for the example/comparison table.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the LEGB rule in Python? List its four levels in order.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **LEGB rule** defines the order in which Python resolves a variable name during execution. Python searches for a name in the following sequence:

1. **L — Local:** The current function's local namespace.
2. **E — Enclosing:** Names in any enclosing function scopes (for nested functions).
3. **G — Global:** The top-level (module-level) namespace.
4. **B — Built-in:** The `builtins` module containing pre-defined names like `print`, `len`, etc.

The first scope where the name is found wins. If the name is not found in any of the four levels, Python raises a `NameError`.

> **Marking Note (KTU):** Award **1 mark** for each correct level and **0.5 mark** for the description.

---

## 📗 PART B: 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of the two choices provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of variable scope in C with suitable examples. What is a `static` local variable? Why is it useful? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program to demonstrate the difference between **local**, **global**, and **static local** variables, including the concept of **variable shadowing**. Show the output and explain each line. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Types of Scope in C:**

**1. Block Scope:**
A variable declared inside a block `{ ... }` is visible from its declaration to the closing brace. Nested blocks can shadow outer variables.

```c
void func() {
    int x = 10;       // x visible from here
    {
        int y = 20;   // y only in inner block
        int x = 30;   // shadows outer x
    }
    // y not visible here
}
```

**2. Function Scope:**
Labels used with `goto` have function scope. Also, in older C (pre-C99), variables declared at the start of a function were visible throughout the function. In C99+, block scope is the default.

**3. File Scope (Global):**
Variables/functions declared outside all functions have file scope. By default, they have **external linkage** (visible to other translation units). Use `static` to restrict linkage to the file.

**4. Function Prototype Scope:**
Parameter names in a function declaration are visible only within the prototype.

```c
int add(int a, int b);   // a, b have prototype scope
```

**5. Static Local Variable:**
A variable declared with the `static` storage class inside a function:
- Has **local scope** (only the function can see it).
- Has **program-wide lifetime** (value persists across calls).
- Is **initialized only once** (default `0`).

**Why is it useful?**
- **Encapsulation:** Hides state from other functions.
- **Persistence:** Maintains state between calls without using globals.
- **Thread safety:** Each function call sees the same data without global namespace pollution.

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark per type (5 types → 5 marks), 1 mark for `static` local definition, 1 mark for usefulness.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**C Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int global_var = 100;  // Global scope

void demonstrate() {
    int local_var = 10;        // Local scope
    static int static_var = 0; // Static local: scope=local, lifetime=global
    
    local_var += 1;
    static_var += 1;
    global_var += 1;
    
    printf("Local: %d, Static: %d, Global: %d\n", 
           local_var, static_var, global_var);
}

int main() {
    printf("Initial global: %d\n", global_var);
    demonstrate();
    demonstrate();
    demonstrate();
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

| Call | local_var | static_var | global_var | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `demonstrate()` 1st | 10→11 | 0→1 | 100→101 | `Local: 11, Static: 1, Global: 101` |
| `demonstrate()` 2nd | 10→11 | 1→2 | 101→102 | `Local: 11, Static: 2, Global: 102` |
| `demonstrate()` 3rd | 10→11 | 2→3 | 102→103 | `Local: 11, Static: 3, Global: 103` |

**Output:**
```
Initial global: 100
Local: 11, Static: 1, Global: 101
Local: 11, Static: 2, Global: 102
Local: 11, Static: 3, Global: 103
```

**Variable Shadowing Demonstration:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int value = 50;

int main() {
    int value = 20;  // Shadows global
    printf("Inner: %d\n", value);  // Prints 20
    {
        int value = 5;  // Shadows outer local
        printf("Deepest: %d\n", value);  // Prints 5
    }
    printf("Outer: %d\n", value);  // Prints 20
    return 0;
}
```

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for program logic, 2 marks for correct output, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for shadowing example.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse `static` local with `static` global. The former is **scoped locally**; the latter is **scoped globally with internal linkage**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students forget that `static` locals are **initialized only once** — they often write `static int x = 0;` and then conclude "x is always 0", which is wrong after the first increment.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not drawing a **memory map** distinguishing stack (auto) vs data segment (static). This is a 2-mark deduction if missing in board exams.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **LEGB rule** in Python with an example involving **nested functions**, `global`, and `nonlocal` keywords. Show the output and trace the scope chain step by step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the scoping rules of **C, Java, and Python** in a tabular form. Write a program in each language to demonstrate **block scope inside a `for` loop** and show that the loop variable is not accessible outside the loop. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Python Program Demonstrating LEGB + `global` + `nonlocal`:**

```python
# Global scope
message = "I am GLOBAL"

def outer():
    # Enclosing scope
    message = "I am ENCLOSING (outer)"
    
    def inner():
        # Local scope
        global message           # Forces reference to global
        nonlocal_message = "I am LOCAL (inner)"
        print("1. Inner sees:", message)  # Refers to global (due to global)
        message = "Modified by inner"
    
    def inner_nonlocal():
        nonlocal message          # Refers to outer's message
        print("2. inner_nonlocal sees:", message)
        message = "Modified by inner_nonlocal"
    
    inner()
    print("3. After inner, outer sees:", message)
    inner_nonlocal()
    print("4. After inner_nonlocal, outer sees:", message)

outer()
print("5. After all, global sees:", message)
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Action | Lookup | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `outer()` called | — | New scope created for `outer` |
| 2 | `inner()` called | `message` | L: no, E: "ENCLOSING", but `global` forces skip → G: "GLOBAL" |
| 3 | `inner()` modifies `message` | — | Global `message` becomes "Modified by inner" |
| 4 | Back in `outer`, print | `message` | E: "ENCLOSING" (untouched) |
| 5 | `inner_nonlocal()` called | `message` | L: no, E: "ENCLOSING" via `nonlocal` |
| 6 | `inner_nonlocal()` modifies | — | E's `message` becomes "Modified by inner_nonlocal" |
| 7 | Back in `outer`, print | `message` | E: "Modified by inner_nonlocal" |
| 8 | Back in module, print | `message` | G: "Modified by inner" |

**Output:**
```
1. Inner sees: I am GLOBAL
2. inner_nonlocal sees: I am ENCLOSING (outer)
3. After inner, outer sees: I am ENCLOSING (outer)
4. After inner_nonlocal, outer sees: Modified by inner_nonlocal
5. After all, global sees: Modified by inner
```

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for program, 2 marks for trace table, 2 marks for output, 1 mark for conclusion.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparative Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | C (C99+) | Java | Python |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default scope | Block | Block | Function/Module |
| Loop variable scope | Limited to `for` block | Limited to `for` block | Limited to `for` block |
| Global keyword | Implicit + `extern` | N/A (use class static) | `global` |
| Static local | ✅ | ❌ (use class) | ✅ (via closure) |
| Block scope `{}` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (partial) |
| Inner function | ❌ (GCC ext.) | ❌ (use lambda) | ✅ |
| `nonlocal` equivalent | N/A | N/A | `nonlocal` |
| Strict encapsulation | ❌ | ✅ (access modifiers) | ⚠️ (convention) |

**Block Scope in `for` Loop:**

**C (3 marks):**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        int sq = i * i;
        printf("i=%d, sq=%d\n", i, sq);
    }
    // printf("%d", i);  // ERROR: 'i' undeclared
    return 0;
}
```

**Java (3 marks):**
```java
public class ForScope {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            int sq = i * i;
            System.out.println("i=" + i + ", sq=" + sq);
        }
        // System.out.println(i);  // COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

**Python (3 marks):**
```python
for i in range(3):
    sq = i * i
    print(f"i={i}, sq={sq}")
# print(i)  # NameError: name 'i' is not defined (in interactive mode)
# In a function/script, i leaks to enclosing scope (Python quirk)
```

**Output (all three):**
```
i=0, sq=0
i=1, sq=1
i=2, sq=4
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** In Python, the `for` loop variable **does** leak to the enclosing function/module scope (unlike C/Java). Students often incorrectly claim "Python also blocks it". The correct statement: *Python blocks it only at the **interactive REPL** level; in scripts, it leaks to the enclosing function scope.*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In C, the `for` loop variable scope was introduced in **C99**. Students writing C89-style code (declaring `int i;` outside the `for`) get zero scope benefit — a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing `static` in C (storage class) with `static` in Java (class-level field modifier). They are **completely different concepts** despite the same keyword.

---

## ⚠️ KTU 2024 Examiner's General Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Scope Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Scope and Lifetime** — KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. Always mention both aspects.
> 2. **Forgetting the `global` / `nonlocal` declaration** in Python — code that *would* work in C++ fails in Python.
> 3. **Missing the memory map** — When asked about `static` locals, always draw a table showing **stack vs. data segment** allocation.
> 4. **Ignoring TDZ** — In JavaScript `let`/`const` questions, students forget the Temporal Dead Zone.
> 5. **Not showing program output** — For 14-mark code questions, the expected output is **mandatory**. A correct program without output gets only partial marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Scope** = *where* a name is valid (compile-time, textual region).
- [x] **Lifetime** = *when* memory exists (runtime, storage duration).
- [x] **Visibility** = *whether* a name is shadowed by another.
- [x] **Local scope** = inside function/block; ends at closing brace.
- [x] **Global scope** = outside all functions; visible everywhere.
- [x] **Block scope** = inside `{ }`; supported in C99+, Java, JS (`let`/`const`).
- [x] **Static local** in C: local scope + program lifetime; initialized once.
- [x] **LEGB rule** in Python: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in.
- [x] **`global` keyword** = refers to module-level variable inside a function.
- [x] **`nonlocal` keyword** = refers to nearest enclosing (non-global) scope.
- [x] **Hoisting in JS** = `var` is hoisted with `undefined`; `let`/`const` are hoisted to TDZ.
- [x] **TDZ** = Temporal Dead Zone; access before declaration throws `ReferenceError`.
- [x] **Shadowing** = inner scope variable hides outer scope variable of same name.
- [x] **Symbol table** is maintained per scope; scope stack enables O(1) name lookup.
- [x] **Python quirk** = `for` loop variable leaks to enclosing function scope (in scripts).
- [x] **C99 enhancement** = `for(int i=0; ...)` limits `i` to the loop block.
- [x] **Java has no globals** = everything is encapsulated in classes.
- [x] **`static` in C ≠ `static` in Java** — different semantics despite same keyword.
- [x] **Closure** = function + its lexical environment (Python, JS).
- [x] **Name resolution is one-pass, lexically nested** in all major languages.

---

## 🎯 Quick-Reference Mnemonics for Exam Day

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **"L-E-G-B"** | Python scope order: Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in |
| **"Scope = text, Life = time"** | Scope is compile-time, Lifetime is runtime |
| **"Static local = local eyes, global life"** | Scoped locally, persists globally |
| **"TDZ = declared but dead"** | `let`/`const` are hoisted but unusable until declaration line |
| **"Shadow = inner wins"** | The most local scope always wins name resolution |

---

✅ **End of Notes — Module 2: Scope**

topics.title: Scope and Lifetime
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Scope and Lifetime ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Scope** (also called **visibility** or **lexical extent**) is the region of a program text where a particular name (identifier) is valid and can be used to refer to the entity it denotes. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, scope is a *compile-time* (static) property governed by where the declaration appears in the source code. > [!NOTE] > **Lifetime** (also called **duration** or **extent**) is the period of execution during which a particular variable (storage) exists in memory and holds a value. Lifetime is a *run-time* (dynamic) property determined by the storage class and the rules of the language's memory model. In the C language (the canonical KTU reference), the standard categorizes identifiers into **four kinds of scopes** and **four storage durations**. These are the exact terms used in the ISO C standard and reproduced verbatim in KTU question papers. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of a Software Company Building 🏢** > > - **Scope** is like the **"access card"** an employee holds. The card only works on certain floors and rooms. An intern may have access only to the 2nd-floor lab (block scope), while the CEO has access to every floor (file scope). The card does not "exist" outside the building — it was *declared* in HR. > > - **Lifetime** is like the **"employment duration"** of a worker. A contract worker (*automatic storage*) joins when a project starts and leaves when it ends. A permanent employee (*static storage*) stays from the company founding until the company shuts down, retaining the same desk and documents. This analogy maps directly to memory: - **Scope** → Which lines of code can "see" the variable. - **Lifetime** → How long the variable physically occupies RAM. ### KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights | KTU Term | Standard Term | Time Dimension | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Scope** | Visibility / Lexical Extent | **Compile-time** (Static) | | **Lifetime** | Duration / Storage Duration | **Run-time** (Dynamic) | | **Linkage** | Name Binding Across TU's | **Link-time** (Static) | > [!NOTE] > A **single identifier** has *one* scope, *one* linkage, and *one* storage duration — but these three properties are **independent** of each other. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Kinds of Scope in C > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus, students must be able to identify and apply the **four scope rules** of the C language. **(a) Block Scope** - A name declared inside a `{ ... }` block (or in the parameter list of a function definition) has **block scope**. - The name is visible from the point of declaration to the closing `}` of the enclosing block. - Inner blocks may *shadow* (hide) outer declarations. **(b) Function Scope** - Applies only to **goto labels**. - A label is visible throughout the entire function body, even *before* its textual declaration. - Labels cannot be redeclared within the same function. **(c) Function Prototype Scope** - Applies only to parameter names declared in a *function prototype* (forward declaration). - Visible only within the prototype declaration itself; names are immaterial to the caller. **(d) File (Global) Scope** - A name declared **outside** of any block or function has file scope. - Visible from the point of declaration to the **end of the translation unit** (the source file). ### 2.2 The Four Storage Durations (Lifetimes) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lifetime** is the run-time counterpart of storage class. KTU 2024 expects familiarity with all four durations. **(a) Static Storage Duration** - Memory is allocated **once** when the program starts and persists until the program terminates. - The variable is initialized **exactly once**, before any other code runs (or at first encounter if not explicitly initialized). - All **file-scope** variables and variables declared with the `static` keyword have this duration. **(b) Automatic Storage Duration** - Memory is allocated upon **entry to the block** where the variable is declared, and deallocated upon **exit**. - If not explicitly initialized, the value is **indeterminate** (garbage). - Default for local variables; can be forced via the `auto` keyword. **(c) Allocated (Dynamic) Storage Duration** - Memory is allocated via `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `realloc()` and released via `free()`. - Lifetime spans from allocation to deallocation; the storage is **not bound to any scope**. **(d) Thread Storage Duration** *(C11 / KTU Advanced)* - Exists from thread creation to thread termination. - Declared with the `_Thread_local` specifier. ### 2.3 Storage Class Specifiers — The Five Pillars | Specifier | Scope (default) | Lifetime | Initial Value | Addressable? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `auto` | Block | Automatic | Garbage (uninitialized) | Yes | | `register` | Block | Automatic | Garbage | **No** (hinted to CPU register) | | `static` | Block **or** File | **Static** | **Zero** (uninitialized) | Yes | | `extern` | File | **Static** | Zero (uninitialized) | Yes | | `typedef` | Syntactic | (No storage) | (No storage) | N/A | ### 2.4 Linkage — The Hidden Third Dimension > [!NOTE] > **Linkage** determines whether multiple declarations of the same name refer to the **same entity** across translation units (`.c` files). - **No Linkage** — Unique to the block; never shared (e.g., local `auto` variables). - **Internal Linkage** — Visible within a single translation unit only (achieved via `static` at file scope). - **External Linkage** — Visible across translation units (default for file-scope non-`static` variables and functions). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Rule of Thumb | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Scope = Visibility | Determined by *placement* of `{ }` | | 2 | Lifetime = Duration | Determined by *storage class* | | 3 | Static local → Lifetime = Program | Lifetime ≠ Scope | | 4 | `static` inside function | Retains value across calls | | 5 | `extern` reference | Refers to **definition** elsewhere | | 6 | Function parameters | Always **block scope**, **automatic** duration | | 7 | Uninitialized `static` | Defaults to **0** (zero-initialized) | | 8 | Uninitialized `auto` | Holds **garbage** (indeterminate) | | 9 | `register` address `&` | **Illegal** — KTU favorite trick question | | 10 | Global `static` | Internal linkage (file-private) | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems (KTU ECE/EEE crossover)**: Choosing `static` for peripheral state registers prevents stack overflow in ISR callbacks. - **Operating Systems**: Kernel variables use **static duration** for device descriptors that must outlive any single function call. - **Game Engines / Real-Time Graphics**: Avoiding `static` in render loops prevents memory leaks and ensures deterministic frame budgets. - **Library Design**: `static` linkage enables **information hiding** — internal helper functions in a `.c` file are not exported, preventing namespace collisions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Scope Resolution via Shadowing Let us trace the visibility of `x` through a multi-block program in C. ```c #include int x = 10; /* (1) File-scope x — external linkage, static lifetime */ void demo(void) { printf("%d\n", x); /* (2) Refers to (1) — prints 10 */ } int main(void) { int x = 5; /* (3) Block-scope x — automatic lifetime, shadows (1) */ printf("%d\n", x); /* (4) Refers to (3) — prints 5 */ { int x = 99; /* (5) Inner block-scope x — shadows (3) */ printf("%d\n", x); /* (6) Refers to (5) — prints 99 */ } /* (5) destroyed here */ printf("%d\n", x); /* (7) Refers to (3) again — prints 5 */ return 0; } /* (3) destroyed here */ ``` **Analytical Walkthrough (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1** — Declaration `(1)` creates a file-scope identifier `x` with **external linkage** and **static lifetime**. It occupies memory for the entire program run, starting at program load. **Step 2** — At call site `(2)`, the compiler resolves `x` by searching the enclosing scopes. Since `demo()` is at file scope, the search finds `(1)`. **Output: `10`**. **Step 3** — Entering `main()`, declaration `(3)` creates a new automatic variable `x`. By the **scope shadowing rule**, this new `x` hides the file-scope `x` for the duration of `main()`. **Step 4** — At `(4)`, the nearest visible `x` is `(3)`. **Output: `5`**. **Step 5** — Inside the inner block, declaration `(5)` again shadows `(3)`. A *new* automatic variable is allocated on the stack. **Step 6** — At `(6)`, the innermost `x` is `(5)`. **Output: `99`**. **Step 7** — At `(7)`, after the inner block closes, `(5)` is deallocated. The visible `x` is now `(3)` again. **Output: `5`**. **Step 8** — After `main()` returns, `(3)` is destroyed. The file-scope `x` `(1)` is reclaimed at program termination. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Lifetime vs Scope: The `static` Local Variable ```c #include void counter(void) { static int c = 0; /* (A) Static local — block scope, STATIC lifetime */ int t = 0; /* (B) Auto local — block scope, AUTOMATIC lifetime */ c++; t++; printf("c=%d t=%d\n", c, t); } int main(void) { counter(); /* call 1 */ counter(); /* call 2 */ counter(); /* call 3 */ return 0; } ``` **Analytical Walkthrough:** **Step 1** — Both `c` and `t` have **block scope** (visible only inside `counter()`). **Step 2** — `c` has **static lifetime** — it is allocated **once** at program start and zero-initialized before `main()` runs. Initialization `static int c = 0` happens only at this single moment. **Step 3** — `t` has **automatic lifetime** — allocated and initialized each time the block is entered; deallocated when the block exits. **Step 4** — Execute call 1: - `c` increments to 1 (retains value across calls). - `t` increments to 1 (fresh allocation). - **Output: `c=1 t=1`** **Step 5** — Execute call 2: - `c` increments to 2 (still the same memory). - `t` increments to 1 (brand-new automatic storage). - **Output: `c=2 t=1`** **Step 6** — Execute call 3: - `c=3 t=1`. - **Output: `c=3 t=1`** **Final Output:** ``` c=1 t=1 c=2 t=1 c=3 t=1 ``` This example **crystallizes the KTU high-yield distinction**: - **Scope ended** when the function returned. - **Lifetime of `c` continued** — it survived across calls because it has static duration. - **Lifetime of `t` ended** — it was born and died within each call. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Identifier Properties When the KTU examiner asks *"What is the scope / lifetime / linkage of variable X?"*, apply this matrix: $$ \text{Declaration Location} = \begin{cases} \text{Outside all functions} & \Rightarrow \text{File scope} \\ \text{Inside a function, no specifier} & \Rightarrow \text{Block scope, automatic lifetime} \\ \text{Inside a function, with \texttt{static}} & \Rightarrow \text{Block scope, STATIC lifetime} \\ \text{Inside a function, with \texttt{extern}} & \Rightarrow \text{Block scope, refers to external defn} \\ \text{Function parameter} & \Rightarrow \text{Block scope, automatic lifetime} \\ \end{cases} $$ $$ \text{Default Initial Value} = \begin{cases} \text{Static duration} & \Rightarrow \mathbf{0} \\ \text{Automatic duration, uninitialized} & \Rightarrow \mathbf{\text{Indeterminate (garbage)}} \\ \end{cases} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Scope Hierarchy Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Identifier Declared"] --> B{"Location?"} B -- "Inside block or function parameter" --> C["Block Scope"] B -- "Function prototype parameter only" --> D["Function Prototype Scope"] B -- "Label for goto" --> E["Function Scope"] B -- "Outside all blocks" --> F["File Scope"] C --> G{"Storage Class?"} G -- "auto or default" --> H["Automatic Lifetime"] G -- "register" --> I["Automatic Lifetime, no & op"] G -- "static" --> J["Static Lifetime"] G -- "extern" --> K["References External Definition"] F --> L{"static keyword?"} L -- "Yes" --> M["Internal Linkage"] L -- "No" --> N["External Linkage"] H --> O["Default Value: Garbage"] J --> P["Default Value: Zero"] M --> P N --> P ``` ### 4.2 Lifetime Visualization Timeline ```mermaid gantt title Lifetime of Variables Across Program Execution dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Static Lifetime FileScopeX (file) :active, fsx, 0, 10 StaticLocalC (counter) :active, slc, 0, 10 section Automatic Lifetime Main_X (main) :done, mx, 1, 7 InnerBlock_X (inner) :crit, ibx, 3, 5 Counter_T (counter) :done, ct, 2, 4 Counter_T_2nd (counter) :done, ct2, 5, 7 section Dynamic Lifetime MallocBlock (heap) :active, mb, 4, 8 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Scope-Lifetime-Linkage Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph COMPILE["COMPILE-TIME (Static)"] SCOPE["SCOPE
Where is the name visible?"] LINKAGE["LINKAGE
Is it the same name elsewhere?"] end subgraph RUN["RUN-TIME (Dynamic)"] LIFETIME["LIFETIME
When does storage exist?"] end SCOPE -- "determines" --> A1["Identifier Resolution"] LINKAGE -- "determines" --> A1 A1 -- "drives" --> LIFETIME subgraph EX["EXAMPLES"] E1["int x inside main
Block scope, no linkage, auto lifetime"] E2["static int y inside fn
Block scope, no linkage, static lifetime"] E3["int z at file scope
File scope, external linkage, static lifetime"] E4["static int w at file scope
File scope, internal linkage, static lifetime"] end A1 --> E1 A1 --> E2 A1 --> E3 A1 --> E4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *Distinguish between **scope** and **lifetime** of a variable in C. Illustrate with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Scope** is the region of program text where an identifier is visible (compile-time, lexical property). (1 mark) - **Lifetime** is the duration during which the variable's storage exists in memory (run-time property). (1 mark) - **Example:** A `static` local variable inside a function has **block scope** (visible only inside that function) but **static lifetime** (persists across function calls until program termination). An automatic local variable has block scope but is destroyed when the block exits. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is the default initial value of a `static` local variable versus an automatic local variable? Why does this matter?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - A `static` local variable is **zero-initialized** by default before program startup. (1 mark) - An automatic local variable, if uninitialized, contains an **indeterminate (garbage)** value. (1 mark) - This matters because using a garbage value leads to **undefined behavior** — a common source of bugs and security vulnerabilities. Static initialization is a safe default for persistent counters/flags. (1 mark) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **four kinds of scope** and **four storage durations** in C. Provide one example declaration for each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the following C program. Predict the output and justify each line by analyzing scope, lifetime, and linkage. **(7 Marks)** ```c #include int g = 100; // L1 void alpha(void) { g += 5; printf("alpha: g=%d\n", g); } int main(void) { int g = 50; // L2 { int g = 25; // L3 printf("inner: g=%d\n", g); } printf("outer: g=%d\n", g); alpha(); alpha(); return 0; } ``` **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 Marks** | # | Scope | Example | Lifetime | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Block scope** | `int x;` inside `main()` | Automatic | | 2 | **Function prototype scope** | `int f(int a, int b);` in prototype | None at runtime | | 3 | **Function scope** | `goto cleanup;` label | None at runtime | | 4 | **File scope** | `int counter;` outside functions | Static | | # | Storage Duration | Example | Lifetime Span | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Static** | `static int x;` | Program start → program end | | 2 | **Automatic** | `auto int x;` or default | Block entry → block exit | | 3 | **Allocated** | `malloc(100);` | `malloc()` → `free()` | | 4 | **Thread** | `_Thread_local int x;` | Thread start → thread end | **(b) — 7 Marks — Output Trace** - **L1**: `g = 100` — file scope, external linkage, static lifetime. (1 mark) - **L2**: Inside `main()`, `g = 50` — block scope, no linkage, automatic lifetime. **Shadows** L1. (1 mark) - **L3**: Inside inner block, `g = 25` — shadows L2. (1 mark) - Line 1 prints `inner: g=25` (L3 is visible). (1 mark) - After inner block ends, L3 is destroyed. Line 2 prints `outer: g=50` (L2 visible). (1 mark) - `alpha()` is called; inside `alpha()`, no local `g` exists, so `g += 5` refers to L1. (1 mark) - After two calls: `g` becomes 110. So lines 3 and 4 print `alpha: g=105` and `alpha: g=110`. (1 mark) **Final Output:** ``` inner: g=25 outer: g=50 alpha: g=105 alpha: g=110 ``` --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Retest]** **(a)** Explain **storage class specifiers** in C with a comparison table covering `auto`, `register`, `static`, and `extern`. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a C program using a `static` local variable that maintains a **persistent count** of how many times a function has been called. Show that the count survives across multiple invocations, while an automatic local does not.
