```mermaid
    evalA3[Evaluate A]
    checkA3{A equals false?}
    skipBC3[Skip B and C: Result = false]
    evalB3[Evaluate B]
    checkB3{B equals false?}
    skipC3[Skip C: Result = false]
    evalC3[Evaluate C]
    resultC3[Result = truthiness of C]
    end3([End])

    start3 --> evalA3
    evalA3 --> checkA3
    checkA3 -- Yes --> skipBC3
    checkA3 -- No --> evalB3
    evalB3 --> checkB3
    checkB3 -- Yes --> skipC3
    checkB3 -- No --> evalC3
    evalC3 --> resultC3
    skipBC3 --> end3
    skipC3 --> end3
    resultC3 --> end3
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Compiler Internal View

The following diagram shows the **functional architecture** of how a typical compiler emits short-circuit code from a high-level boolean expression.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Code: if A and B then X]
    parse[Parser: AST Builder]
    semcheck[Semantic Analyzer: Type Checks]
    cfg[Control Flow Graph Generator]
    branch[Branch Emitter: Translates and to IF GOTO chain]
    asm[Assembly: Compare, Jump, Set Instructions]
    cpu[CPU: Pipeline Evaluation]

    src --> parse
    parse --> semcheck
    semcheck --> cfg
    cfg --> branch
    branch --> asm
    asm --> cpu
```

## 4.5 Side-Effect Visualization — The Classic Trap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Init: a=0, b=5]
    step1[Evaluate a++: returns 0, side effect sets a=1]
    decision{a++ value equals 0?}
    shortSkip[Short-Circuit: b++ NOT evaluated, b stays 5]
    result[Result: 0; Final state: a=1, b=5]
    endX([Output])

    init --> step1
    step1 --> decision
    decision -- Yes 0 is falsy --> shortSkip
    decision -- No --> result
    shortSkip --> result
    result --> endX
```

## 4.6 Safety Pattern — Null Pointer Guard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ptrCheck{p is null?}
    skipDeref[Short-Circuit: p->data NOT evaluated]
    derefEval[Evaluate p->data equals 5]
    final{Combined result}
    act[Execute guarded action]
    noact[Skip action]

    ptrCheck -- Yes null --> skipDeref
    ptrCheck -- No not null --> derefEval
    skipDeref --> final
    derefEval --> final
    final -- true --> act
    final -- false --> noact
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> Define **Short-Circuit Evaluation** in C/C++. State the operators under which it applies and explain the rule for each with an example.

**Model Answer:**

Short-circuit evaluation is a *boolean evaluation semantics* in C/C++ where the second operand of a logical operator is **not evaluated** if the result of the entire expression is already determined by the first operand.

- For **Logical AND (`&&`)**: if the left operand evaluates to `false` (zero), the right operand is **skipped**, and the result is `false`.
- For **Logical OR (`||`)**: if the left operand evaluates to `true` (non-zero), the right operand is **skipped**, and the result is `true`.

**Example:**
```c
if (ptr != NULL && ptr->value > 10) { ... }
```
If `ptr` is `NULL`, the expression `ptr->value > 10` is **not evaluated**, preventing a segmentation fault.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> Differentiate between the **logical `&&` operator** and the **bitwise `&` operator** in C. Why is short-circuit evaluation not applicable to bitwise operators?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Logical `&&` | Bitwise `&` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operation | Boolean logic on truth values | Bit-by-bit AND on integers |
| Short-Circuit | **Yes** | **No** |
| Operand Evaluation | Skips right if decided | **Both operands always evaluated** |
| Type of Operands | Boolean (or convertible) | Integral |

Short-circuit evaluation is **not** applicable to bitwise operators because they perform a *positional* operation on every bit. Skipping an operand would mean the result is undefined, since `&` does not have a boolean annihilator that makes the right operand mathematically irrelevant. The bitwise operators are *strictly evaluated*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Mark Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Module 2 — Attempt Part (a) from any one question and Part (b) from a different question. The internal choice is between two full 14-mark questions.*

---

### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **short-circuit evaluation** with reference to the logical `&&` and `||` operators in C. Use truth tables to justify your explanation. Why is this evaluation strategy **semantically important** in pointer-based code? Illustrate with a **C program** that uses short-circuiting to prevent a null pointer dereference.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Short-circuit evaluation is a property of logical operators (`&&` and `||`) where the second operand is *not* evaluated when the result is fully determined by the first operand. It is grounded in the boolean annihilation laws $0 \land X = 0$ and $1 \lor X = 1$.

**Step 2 — Truth Table Justification (2 marks):**

| `A` | `B` | `A && B` | Right evaluated? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | X | 0 | **No** |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Yes |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Yes |

| `A` | `B` | `A \|\| B` | Right evaluated? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | X | 1 | **No** |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Yes |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Yes |

**Step 3 — Pointer Safety (1 mark):**
In pointer-based code, accessing a member through a `NULL` pointer causes undefined behaviour (segmentation fault). Short-circuiting allows the developer to write guarded checks like `if (ptr != NULL && ptr->x > 5)`, ensuring `ptr->x` is accessed only when `ptr` is valid.

**Step 4 — C Program (2 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
};

int main(void) {
    struct Node *ptr = NULL;
    int threshold = 5;

    /* Short-circuit prevents dereferencing NULL */
    if (ptr != NULL && ptr->data > threshold) {
        printf("Data exceeds threshold\n");
    } else {
        printf("Pointer is NULL or data is small — safely skipped\n");
    }

    /* Now allocate and re-check */
    ptr = &(struct Node){42};
    if (ptr != NULL && ptr->data > threshold) {
        printf("Data %d exceeds threshold %d\n", ptr->data, threshold);
    }

    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Pointer is NULL or data is small — safely skipped
Data 42 exceeds threshold 5
```

**Explanation:** In the first `if`, `ptr` is `NULL`, so the right operand `ptr->data > threshold` is **never evaluated**, avoiding a crash. In the second `if`, `ptr` is valid, so the right operand is evaluated normally.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following C snippet. Predict the **exact output**, justifying each step using short-circuit evaluation rules:
```c
#include <stdio.h>
int x = 0, y = 0, z = 0;
int main(void) {
    int r = (x++) || (y++) || (z++);
    printf("x=%d y=%d z=%d r=%d\n", x, y, z, r);
    return 0;
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Trace the chain `x++ || y++ || z++` (3 marks):**

| Operand | Value Returned | Side Effect (after evaluation) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `x++` | `0` (old value) | `x` becomes `1` |
| `y++` | `0` (old value) | `y` becomes `1` |
| `z++` | `0` (old value) | `z` becomes `1` |

**Step 2 — Apply OR short-circuit logic (2 marks):**
- `x++` returns `0` → `||` needs more info → evaluate `y++`.
- `y++` returns `0` → `||` still needs more info → evaluate `z++`.
- `z++` returns `0` → entire expression is `0`.

**No short-circuit skipping happens** because *no operand* returns `true`. All three increments execute. The result of the whole expression is the value of the last evaluated operand, which is `0`.

**Step 3 — Final state and output (2 marks):**
- `x = 1` (post-increment of x)
- `y = 1` (post-increment of y)
- `z = 1` (post-increment of z)
- `r = 0` (the final result of the chain)

**Output:**
```
x=1 y=1 z=1 r=0
```

---

### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **flow diagram and a C program**, explain how short-circuit evaluation can be used to perform an **early exit optimization** in a search function. Compare this approach with a *non-short-circuit* (eager) approach in terms of performance and side effects.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
Short-circuit evaluation allows a search loop to exit as soon as the first match is found. By chaining conditions with `||`, the function can terminate the search the moment any sub-condition returns `true`, without evaluating the rest of the chain.

**Step 2 — Flow Diagram (2 marks):**
The compiler translates the `||` chain into a sequence of compare-and-branch instructions:

```
Test element[0] → match?  → return FOUND
Test element[1] → match?  → return FOUND
Test element[2] → match?  → return FOUND
... (otherwise) ...
return NOT FOUND
```

**Step 3 — C Program with Short-Circuit (2 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

bool containsValue(int *arr, int size, int target) {
    bool found = false;
    for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
        /* Short-circuit OR chain: stops at first match */
        found = found || (arr[i] == target);
    }
    return found;
}
```

In this code, once `found` becomes `true`, the right operand of `||` is **never evaluated** for the remaining iterations. The comparison `arr[i] == target` is skipped, saving CPU cycles.

**Step 4 — Comparison with Eager Approach (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Short-Circuit | Eager (Bitwise `\|`) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Performance | **Faster** — exits early | Slower — always checks all |
| Side Effects | Suppressed after match | Always executed |
| Code Safety | High | Risk of unnecessary work |
| Use Case | Search, validation | Bitmask combination |

**Conclusion:** Short-circuit evaluation is the preferred idiomatic pattern for early-exit search loops, as it delivers both *performance* and *side-effect safety*.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how the **ternary operator `?:`** also follows short-circuit semantics. Demonstrate, using a C program, how the ternary operator can be used to **prevent a division-by-zero error**. Justify the role of short-circuiting in producing **safe, exception-free code**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ternary Semantics (2 marks):**
The ternary operator `condition ? expr_true : expr_false` evaluates the condition first. Only **one** of the two sub-expressions is evaluated, depending on the boolean value of the condition. This is short-circuit semantics:

$$ \text{result} = \begin{cases} \text{expr\_true} & \text{if condition is true} \\ \text{expr\_false} & \text{if condition is false} \end{cases} $$

**Step 2 — Division-by-Zero Demonstration (3 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int safeDivide(int numerator, int divisor) {
    /* Short-circuit ternary: skips 10/divisor if divisor is 0 */
    return (divisor != 0) ? (numerator / divisor) : 0;
}

int main(void) {
    printf("10 / 2 = %d\n", safeDivide(10, 2));
    printf("10 / 0 = %d\n", safeDivide(10, 0));
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
10 / 2 = 5
10 / 0 = 0
```

**Step 3 — Trace (1 mark):**
- `safeDivide(10, 2)`: `divisor != 0` is `true` → evaluate `10 / 2 = 5`.
- `safeDivide(10, 0)`: `divisor != 0` is `false` → **short-circuit** skips the `10 / 0` evaluation → return `0`. No runtime crash.

**Step 4 — Justification (1 mark):**
Short-circuiting is **fundamental to safe coding**. By guaranteeing that potentially dangerous expressions (division, pointer dereference, array access) are *not* evaluated when their preconditions fail, the language runtime eliminates entire classes of runtime errors. This is a **semantic contract** between the programmer and the compiler — and KTU examiners expect students to explicitly identify and explain this safety property.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on Short-Circuit Evaluation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `&&` with `&` (and `||` with `|`).** Logical operators short-circuit; bitwise operators **always evaluate both operands**. Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting side effects of post-increment.** In `(a++) && (b++)`, students often claim `b` increments. It does **not** when `a` starts at `0`.
> 3. **Ignoring the C++ stream idiom.** `if (cin >> x)` uses `operator bool()` implicitly — short-circuiting interacts with this; do not write `cin >> x != 0` redundantly.
> 4. **Writing `&&` as a single `&` accidentally.** In C, `&` is the *address-of* operator on lvalues and bitwise AND on integers. Always distinguish.
> 5. **Assuming C and Java behave identically on boolean coercion.** Java strictly requires `boolean` operands for `&&`; C allows any scalar with implicit truthiness.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Boolean algebraic basis.** Examiners reward students who cite the **annihilation law** ($0 \land X = 0$, $1 \lor X = 1$) as the theoretical justification.
> 7. **Missing the sequence-point rule.** In C/C++, `&&` and `||` introduce sequence points, meaning all side effects of the left operand are committed before the right is evaluated. This is a high-yield KTU point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Short-circuit evaluation = skip the right operand when the left decides the result.
- [x] **Applies to:** `&&` (AND) and `||` (OR) only — never to `&` or `|`.
- [x] **AND rule:** If left is `false` → skip right, result is `false`.
- [x] **OR rule:** If left is `true` → skip right, result is `true`.
- [x] **Boolean foundation:** Annihilation laws $0 \land X = 0$ and $1 \lor X = 1$.
- [x] **Order matters:** Operand order can change observable side effects.
- [x] **Ternary `?:` also short-circuits** — only one branch is evaluated.
- [x] **Key uses:** Null-pointer guards, division-by-zero prevention, early-exit search, ranged indexing.
- [x] **Sequence points:** `&&`, `||`, `?:`, `,`, and `;` introduce sequence points in C/C++.
- [x] **C/C++ truthiness:** `0`, `NULL`, `0.0`, `'\0'` are false; everything else is true.
- [x] **Java truthiness:** Only `true` and `false` — no implicit numeric conversion.
- [x] **Performance:** Short-circuit = lazy evaluation → saves CPU and prevents unnecessary side effects.
- [x] **Trap to remember:** `(a++) && (b++)` does **not** increment `b` if `a` was `0` before increment.
- [x] **Exam framing tip:** Always cite the boolean law, mention sequence points, and use a null-pointer example for a complete answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignment Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assignment Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Assignment Statement** is a fundamental, executable programming construct in imperative, object-oriented, and procedural languages that **evaluates an expression on the right-hand side (RHS)** and **stores the resulting value into a memory location (variable) designated on the left-hand side (LHS)**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Advanced Computer Programming Techniques*, assignment statements are classified under **expression-based control constructs** and form the basis for state mutation, data initialization, and computational flow in languages such as **C, C++, Java, and Python**.

Formally, an assignment statement is a statement-level construct that alters the state of a program's memory by binding a value to a named entity, typically abiding by the syntactic template:

$$ \text{LHS\_variable} \; \texttt{=} \; \text{RHS\_expression} \; \texttt{;} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:** The single equals sign (`=`) is the **assignment operator**, NOT the mathematical equality operator. The double equals sign (`==`) denotes equality comparison. This is one of the most common sources of logic errors flagged during board valuation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Labeled Storage Box**
> Imagine a row of **labeled storage boxes** on a shelf, where each box has a unique name tag (the variable name) and can hold a single value at a time. The assignment statement is the act of:
> 1. **Evaluating** what is being placed in the box (RHS expression)
> 2. **Overwriting** any previous contents of the box (destroying the old value)
> 3. **Confirming** that the box label (LHS) is a valid, mutable storage location

Consider the statement `marks = 95;`. In plain English: *"Take the value `95`, evaluate it (it is a literal), and drop it into the storage box labeled `marks`. If the box already held a value, that value is permanently discarded."*

### 1.3 Standard Components of an Assignment Statement

| Component | Symbol | Role | Common Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Left-Hand Side (LHS)** | lvalue | Destination — must be a modifiable memory location | Variable, array element, struct member |
| **Assignment Operator** | `=`, `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `%=` | Performs the storage action and (in compound forms) an operation | `a = b`, `a += b` |
| **Right-Hand Side (RHS)** | rvalue | Source — any evaluable expression | Literal, variable, function call, complex expression |
| **Terminator** | `;` | Statement boundary in C/C++/Java | Always required in C-family languages |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard:** Marks are awarded for explicitly identifying the **lvalue** and **rvalue** in any assignment-based question. Examiners expect students to write the full expansion of compound assignment forms (e.g., `a += b` → `a = a + b`).

### 1.4 Lvalue vs Rvalue — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Lvalue (Left Value) | Rvalue (Right Value) |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | "Locator value" — refers to a memory address | "Read value" — refers to the data stored |
| **Position** | Must appear on the LHS of `=` | Typically on the RHS of `=` |
| **Mutability** | Must be assignable (non-const) | Read-only during evaluation |
| **Examples** | `x`, `arr[3]`, `*ptr`, `obj.field` | `42`, `x + y`, `sin(θ)`, `"Hello"` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Expressions like `x + y = 5;` or `5 = x;` are **illegal** because the LHS is not a valid lvalue. A constant or a non-modifiable temporary cannot appear on the left of an assignment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Execution Model

When the CPU encounters an assignment statement, the following sequence of micro-operations occurs at the machine/abstract machine level:

1. **Lexical & Syntactic Analysis:** The compiler/interpreter parses `LHS op RHS` and verifies type compatibility and lvalue validity.
2. **RHS Evaluation:** The right-hand side expression is fully evaluated first. All sub-expressions are computed using operator precedence rules until a single result value is obtained.
3. **Type Coercion (if needed):** If the RHS type differs from the LHS type, implicit or explicit type conversion is applied (C/C++ implicit rules; Java strict type rules with widening/narrowing).
4. **LHS Address Resolution:** The compiler resolves the LHS to a specific memory address (stack, heap, or static segment).
5. **Storage Action:** The converted RHS value is **copied** into the memory location designated by the LHS.
6. **Side Effect Completion:** The original LHS value is destroyed (overwritten). The assignment expression itself yields the assigned value as its result in C/C++ (allows chaining).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Assignment Form | Expanded Mathematical Equivalent | Operator Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `a = b` | $a \leftarrow b$ | Simple Assignment | Direct copy |
| `a += b` | $a \leftarrow a + b$ | Compound (Additive) | $\vert b \vert$ is unchanged |
| `a -= b` | $a \leftarrow a - b$ | Compound (Subtractive) | — |
| `a *= b` | $a \leftarrow a \times b$ | Compound (Multiplicative) | — |
| `a /= b` | $a \leftarrow a \div b$ | Compound (Divisional) | Divide-by-zero guarded at runtime |
| `a %= b` | $a \leftarrow a \bmod b$ | Compound (Modular) | Integer types only |
| `a &= b` | $a \leftarrow a \;\text{AND}\; b$ | Bitwise Compound | Operates on bits |
| `a \vert= b` | $a \leftarrow a \;\text{OR}\; b$ | Bitwise Compound | — |
| `a ^= b` | $a \leftarrow a \;\text{XOR}\; b$ | Bitwise Compound | — |
| `a <<= b` | $a \leftarrow a \ll b$ | Bitwise Shift Left | — |
| `a >>= b` | $a \leftarrow a \gg b$ | Bitwise Shift Right | — |

### 2.3 Implicit Type Conversion (Coercion) Rules in C/C++ (KTU High-Yield)

When the LHS and RHS types differ, the compiler applies **usual arithmetic conversions** in this priority order (lowest to highest precision):

$$ \text{char} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double} \rightarrow \text{long double} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** When assigning a larger type to a smaller type (e.g., `int x = 3.14;`), the value is **truncated**, not rounded. Students must mention this distinction explicitly for full marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Assignment Statements |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | Real-time sensor value updates: `tempCelsius = adc_read() * 0.488;` |
| **Signal Processing** | Accumulator updates: `runningSum += sample;` |
| **Machine Learning** | Weight updates in gradient descent: `w -= learningRate * gradient;` |
| **Game Development** | Coordinate updates: `playerX += velocityX * deltaTime;` |
| **Control Systems (KTU ECE/EEE)** | PID controller update: `output = Kp*error + Ki*integral + Kd*derivative;` |
| **Database Systems** | Field updates: `record.salary = record.salary * 1.10;` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Example 1: Chained Assignment with Type Promotion

Consider the statement:

```c
int a, b;
float result;
result = a = b = 25 / 2.0;
```

**Step 1 — Identify lvalues and rvalues:**
- `result` (float, lvalue) = `a` (int, lvalue) = `b` (int, lvalue) = `25 / 2.0` (rvalue expression)

**Step 2 — Evaluate the rightmost expression first (RHS-first rule):**
The expression `25 / 2.0` has mixed types. According to C's "usual arithmetic conversion," `25` (int) is promoted to `25.0` (double) before division:

$$ \frac{25.0}{2.0} = 12.5 $$

**Step 3 — Assign to `b` (int):**
Assigning the double value `12.5` to an int truncates the fractional part:

$$ b \leftarrow 12 $$

**Step 4 — Assign to `a` (int):**
Since the assignment `a = b` returns the value just assigned, `a` also becomes `12`.

$$ a \leftarrow 12 $$

**Step 5 — Assign to `result` (float):**
The int value `12` is widened to float `12.0` and stored:

$$ \text{result} \leftarrow 12.0 $$

**Final State:**

$$ a = 12, \quad b = 12, \quad \text{result} = 12.0 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marker Insight:** The chained assignment evaluates **right-to-left** but assigns **left-to-right**. This distinction appears in nearly every KTU board exam and is worth 2 marks on its own.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Example 2: Compound Assignment Equivalence Proof

**Claim:** The statement `x += y;` is mathematically equivalent to `x = x + y;` **for built-in types**, with the sole difference being that the compound form evaluates `x` only **once**.

**Proof by Execution Trace:**

Let $x = 5$ and $y = 3$ in memory.

| Step | Form 1: `x = x + y;` | Form 2: `x += y;` |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load `x` (RHS) → Register R1 = 5 | Load `x` → R1 = 5 |
| 2 | Load `y` → R2 = 3 | Load `y` → R2 = 3 |
| 3 | ADD R1, R2 → R3 = 8 | ADD R1, R2 → R3 = 8 |
| 4 | **Load `x` again** (LHS) → R4 = 5 | — (no second load) |
| 5 | Store R3 into address of `x` | Store R3 into address of `x` |
| 6 | `x` = 8 | `x` = 8 |

**Conclusion:** Both produce identical results for primitive types, but `x += y` is **faster** for complex LHS expressions such as `arr[i++] += y;` where re-evaluating `arr[i++]` twice would yield different values.

$$ \text{Result: } x_{final} = x_{initial} + y $$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Example 3: Assignment with Side Effects

```c
int a = 5, b = 10;
a = b + (b = 20);
```

**Step 1 — Evaluate RHS from left sub-expression, but apply precedence:**
Java/C++ do not guarantee evaluation order within parentheses; however, the assignment `(b = 20)` is a side-effecting sub-expression.

**Step 2 — Two Possible Evaluation Orders:**

**Order A (LHS of `+` first, then RHS):**

$$ a \leftarrow 10 + 20 = 30 $$

Final: `a = 30`, `b = 20`.

**Order B (RHS of `+` first, then LHS):**

$$ a \leftarrow 20 + 20 = 40 $$

Final: `a = 40`, `b = 20`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** This code exhibits **undefined behavior in C/C++ pre-C++17** because it modifies and accesses `b` without an intervening sequence point. Marks are deducted if the student claims a single deterministic answer without acknowledging this.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operator Precedence Affecting Assignment Expressions

| Expression | Evaluated As | Result (a=10, b=3) |
|---|---|---|
| `a += b` | $a \leftarrow a + b$ | $a = 13$ |
| `a -= b * 2` | $a \leftarrow a - (b \times 2)$ | $a = 4$ |
| `a *= b + 1` | $a \leftarrow a \times (b + 1)$ | $a = 40$ |
| `a /= b - 1` | $a \leftarrow a \div (b - 1)$ | $a = 5$ |
| `a %= b + 4` | $a \leftarrow a \bmod (b + 4)$ | $a = 3$ |

The **RHS is fully evaluated as an expression first** (with its own precedence), then the compound operator is applied to the LHS.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Assignment Statement Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Encounter Assignment Statement] --> B[Lexical and Syntactic Parse]
    B --> C{Is LHS a Valid Lvalue?}
    C -- No --> X1[Compilation Error: Non-Modifiable LHS]
    C -- Yes --> D[Resolve LHS Memory Address]
    D --> E[Evaluate RHS Expression]
    E --> F[Apply Type Coercion Rules]
    F --> G[Convert RHS to Match LHS Type]
    G --> H[Store Value into LHS Memory Location]
    H --> I[Discard Previous LHS Value]
    I --> J[Return Assigned Value as Expression Result]
    J --> K[Proceed to Next Statement]
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Model of an Assignment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[CPU Registers]
        R1[Register R1: Holds RHS Value]
        R2[Register R2: Temporary]
    end
    subgraph MEM[Main Memory]
        A1[Address 0x100: LHS Variable x]
        A2[Address 0x104: RHS Temporary]
    end
    R2 --> A2
    A2 --> R1
    R1 --> A1
    A1 -.Overwrite.-> A1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Classification of Assignment Statements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Assignment Statements] --> B1[Simple Assignment]
    A0 --> B2[Compound Assignment]
    A0 --> B3[Chained Assignment]
    A0 --> B4[Multiple or Tuple Assignment]
    B1 --> C1[Equals Operator]
    B2 --> C2[Arithmetic Compounds]
    B2 --> C3[Bitwise Compounds]
    B2 --> C4[Shift Compounds]
    B3 --> C5[Right to Left Evaluation]
    B4 --> C6[Python Style Unpacking]
    C2 --> D1[Plus Equals, Minus Equals]
    C3 --> D2[AND, OR, XOR Compounds]
    C4 --> D3[Left Shift, Right Shift]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Compound vs Simple Assignment Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Compiler
    participant CPU
    participant Memory
    Compiler->>CPU: Decode x += y
    CPU->>Memory: Fetch x ONCE
    CPU->>Memory: Fetch y
    CPU->>CPU: Compute x + y
    CPU->>Memory: Store result into x
    Note over CPU,Memory: Only ONE fetch of x occurs in compound form
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between lvalue and rvalue with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **lvalue** (locator value) refers to a memory location that persists beyond a single expression and can appear on the left-hand side of an assignment operator. An **rvalue** (read value) is a temporary data value that does not have a designated memory address and cannot be assigned to.

*Example of lvalue:* `int x = 10;` — here `x` is an lvalue because it designates a memory address.

*Example of rvalue:* The expression `x + 5` is an rvalue; it produces a temporary value that cannot be assigned to (e.g., `x + 5 = 10;` is illegal).

| Property | lvalue | rvalue |
|---|---|---|
| Memory address | Yes | No (temporary) |
| Appears on LHS of `=` | Yes | No |
| Persists across expressions | Yes | No |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition lvalue, 1 for definition rvalue, 1 for example contrast)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain what is meant by a "compound assignment statement" and write the expansion of `x *= y + 2;`.**

**Model Answer:**

A **compound assignment statement** combines an arithmetic (or bitwise) operator with the basic assignment operator, allowing the LHS variable to be modified in place using its own current value.

The expansion of `x *= y + 2;` is:

$$ x = x \times (y + 2); $$

**Critical point:** The parentheses around `(y + 2)` must be preserved because operator precedence dictates that the entire RHS is evaluated as a single expression first before the multiplication-and-assignment action is performed.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for correct expansion with parentheses, 1 for precedence explanation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various types of assignment statements supported by C/C++. Provide the general syntax and at least one example for each type.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following C code snippet. Determine the final values of all variables after execution, showing each step of evaluation:

```c
int a = 8, b = 3, c;
float x;
c = a % b + a / b;
x = a += b *= 2;
```

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** The various types of assignment statements in C/C++ are:

1. **Simple Assignment** — uses `=` alone.
   Syntax: `variable = expression;`
   Example: `int y; y = 10;`

2. **Compound Arithmetic Assignment** — combines arithmetic operators with `=`.
   Examples: `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `%=`
   Example: `sum += value;`

3. **Compound Bitwise Assignment** — combines bitwise operators with `=`.
   Examples: `&=`, `|=`, `^=`, `<<=`, `>>=`
   Example: `flags &= MASK;`

4. **Chained Assignment** — multiple LHS variables receiving the same RHS value.
   Example: `a = b = c = 0;`

5. **Multiple Assignment with Operation** — used in expressions where the assignment itself yields a value.
   Example: `while ((ch = getchar()) != EOF) { ... }`

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Evaluation:**

**Initial state:** $a = 8, \; b = 3, \; c = ?, \; x = ?$

**Step 1: Evaluate `c = a % b + a / b;`**

$$ a \% b = 8 \bmod 3 = 2 $$
$$ a / b = 8 / 3 = 2 \quad \text{(integer division, truncated)} $$
$$ c = 2 + 2 = 4 $$

After Step 1: $a = 8, \; b = 3, \; c = 4, \; x = ?$

**Step 2: Evaluate `x = a += b *= 2;` (right-to-left)**

Sub-step 2a: Evaluate `b *= 2` first:
$$ b \leftarrow b \times 2 = 3 \times 2 = 6 $$

Sub-step 2b: Evaluate `a += b` (now $b = 6$):
$$ a \leftarrow a + b = 8 + 6 = 14 $$

Sub-step 2c: Evaluate `x = a` (now $a = 14$, type int):
$$ x \leftarrow 14.0 \quad \text{(int widened to float)} $$

**Final values:**

$$ a = 14, \quad b = 6, \quad c = 4, \quad x = 14.0 $$

**Marks Distribution for (b):** 1 mark initial state, 2 marks for sub-step 2a, 2 marks for 2b, 2 marks for 2c and final answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of implicit type conversion during assignment. Illustrate with an example involving `int`, `float`, and `double` and discuss the potential data loss.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Rewrite each of the following compound statements in their expanded form and determine the final value of `a` and `b`, given that initially $a = 10$ and $b = 4$:

(i) `a += b--;`     (ii) `b *= ++a + 2;`     (iii) `a %= b / 2;`

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Implicit Type Conversion in Assignment**

Implicit type conversion (also called **coercion**) is the automatic conversion of one data type to another performed by the compiler when the LHS and RHS of an assignment have different types. The C/C++ standard follows the rule that the value is converted to the **type of the LHS** (the destination type), not the other way around.

**Illustration:**

```c
int    i = 65;
float  f = 3.14159;
double d = 2.718281828;
float  result;

result = i;        // int widened to float: 65.0 (no loss)
result = d;        // double narrowed to float: 2.71828 (PRECISION LOSS)
i = f;             // float narrowed to int: 3 (TRUNCATION, fractional part lost)
```

**Potential Data Loss:**

1. **Floating-to-integer narrowing** discards the fractional part (e.g., `3.99` becomes `3`).
2. **Double-to-float narrowing** can lose precision in mantissa bits for very large or very small numbers.
3. **Integer overflow** can occur if the RHS exceeds the LHS range (e.g., assigning a `long` value exceeding $2^{31}-1$ to an `int`).

**Part (b): Step-by-Step Evaluation (Initial: $a = 10, b = 4$)**

**(i) `a += b--;`**

The post-decrement `b--` returns the **current** value of $b$ (which is $4$), then decrements $b$ to $3$ as a side effect after use.

$$ a \leftarrow a + (\text{old } b) = 10 + 4 = 14 $$
$$ b \leftarrow 3 $$

**(ii) `b *= ++a + 2;`**

The pre-increment `++a` increments $a$ **first** to $15$, then the expression evaluates:

$$ \texttt{++a} + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17 $$
$$ b \leftarrow b \times 17 = 4 \times 17 = 68 $$

**(iii) `a %= b / 2;`**

Note: at this point, $b = 68$ (updated in step ii).

$$ b / 2 = 68 / 2 = 34 \quad \text{(integer division)} $$
$$ a \leftarrow a \bmod 34 = 15 \bmod 34 = 15 \quad \text{(since } 15 < 34\text{)} $$

**Final values:**

$$ a = 15, \quad b = 68 $$

**Marks Distribution for (b):** 2 marks each for (i), (ii), (iii) — 1 for correct operator semantics, 1 for correct arithmetic.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Assignment Statement Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `=` with `==` (≈ 2 marks lost)** — Students frequently mix up assignment and equality comparison. Always read the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting parentheses in compound assignment expansion (≈ 1–2 marks lost)** — Writing `a = a * b + 2` instead of `a = a * (b + 2)` for `a *= b + 2` loses marks.
> 3. **Ignoring pre/post increment timing (≈ 2 marks lost)** — Forgetting that `b--` returns old value while `--b` returns new value.
> 4. **Assuming chained assignments are left-to-right (≈ 2 marks lost)** — They are right-to-left.
> 5. **Not stating truncation vs rounding in type narrowing (≈ 1 mark lost)** — Always specify "fractional part is discarded, not rounded."
> 6. **Missing the order of side effects in compound chained expressions (≈ 1–2 marks lost)** — Always trace the rightmost operation first.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** An assignment statement **evaluates** the RHS and **stores** the result into the LHS memory location.
- **Operator:** Single `=` for assignment; **NOT** mathematical equality.
- **LHS Requirement:** Must be a valid, non-const lvalue (variable, array element, dereferenced pointer, or struct member).
- **RHS Flexibility:** Can be any expression — literal, variable, arithmetic, function call, or even another assignment.
- **Compound Operators:** `+=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=` — each expands to `LHS = LHS op RHS` **with RHS evaluated first as a whole**.
- **Evaluation Order:** Chained assignments evaluate **right-to-left** but execute their store action **left-to-right**.
- **Type Coercion:** Destination is always the LHS type. Narrowing may cause **truncation**; widening is always safe.
- **Side Effects:** `x = x++ + ++x;` exhibits **undefined behavior** in C/C++ — never write such expressions in exams.
- **Compound vs Simple:** `x += y` evaluates `x` only once (faster and safer for complex LHS like `arr[i++]`).
- **Pre vs Post Increment/Decrement:** Pre modifies **before** the value is used; post modifies **after** the value is used.
- **Integer Division:** `/` between two integers performs **truncation**, not rounding (e.g., `7 / 2 = 3`, not `4`).
- **Modulus Sign Rule:** The sign of `a % b` in C/C++ follows the sign of `a` (the dividend), not `b`.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Always show **step-by-step evaluation traces**; examiners reward methodical working over single-line final answers.
- **Python Note:** Python supports tuple unpacking (`a, b = 5, 10`) and augmented assignment (`a += b`) but **does not** support chained assignment returning a value usable in larger expressions.
- **Java Specifics:** Java assignment is a **right-associative expression** that returns the assigned value, allowing constructs like `String s = null; while ((s = br.readLine()) != null) { ... }`.
- **Engineering Relevance:** Assignment statements are the **primitive operation** underlying state changes in virtually all real-time control systems, embedded firmware, and numerical simulation code.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixed Mode Assignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mixed Mode Assignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mixed Mode Assignment** is the process in C (and similar strongly-typed programming languages) where a value of one fundamental data type is assigned to a variable of a *different* fundamental data type, requiring the compiler to perform an **implicit type conversion** (also called **automatic type coercion**) before storing the value into the destination variable.

In simpler terms: when the right-hand side (RHS) of an assignment expression and the left-hand side (LHS) variable are of different data types, the compiler automatically converts the RHS value to match the LHS variable's type using a predefined **type conversion hierarchy**.

```c
int      i = 3.75;     // RHS double → LHS int   (truncation occurs)
float    f = 25;        // RHS int    → LHS float (no loss, promotion)
char     c = 65;        // RHS int    → LHS char  (modular wrap-around)
double   d = 'A';       // RHS char   → LHS double(ASCII promotion)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mixed mode assignment is a **direct application of implicit type conversion rules** specified by the ANSI/ISO C standard. This topic tests your understanding of *type promotion*, *demotion*, and *potential data loss* — concepts that recur in expressions, function arguments, and return statements.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Liquid Transfer" Model

Imagine a **large measuring beaker** (a `double`) and a **small medicine cup** (an `int`):

| Source Container | Destination Container | Outcome |
|------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Big beaker (1.5 L) | Small cup (250 mL) | Cup fills, **spillage occurs** → *truncation* |
| Small cup (250 mL) | Big beaker (1.5 L) | Beaker holds liquid, but **looks underfilled** → *promotion* |
| Medicine dropper (1 mL) | Test tube (10 mL) | Test tube holds 1 mL, **value preserved** → *safe promotion* |

- **Spillage** represents **loss of precision** (e.g., `3.75` becoming `3`).
- **Underfilling** represents **no loss of information** (e.g., `25` becoming `25.0`).
- The *container* represents the **destination variable's data type** that *governs* the final storage form.

---

## 1.3 Why Does C Allow Mixed Mode Assignment?

C is designed for **system-level programming**, where programmers frequently interact with hardware registers, memory-mapped I/O, and mathematical computations that mix integers, floating-point numbers, and characters. Rather than forcing the programmer to write explicit conversion functions everywhere, C **automatically coerces** types following well-defined rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** The *destination type always wins.* Whatever happens, the final stored value conforms to the LHS variable's type. The RHS adapts — not the other way around.

---

## 1.4 The Two Major Categories

1. **Type Promotion (Widening / Upward Conversion)** — moving from a *smaller* to a *larger* type. Generally **safe**; no data is lost.
   - `char` → `int`, `int` → `long`, `int` → `float`, `float` → `double`

2. **Type Demotion (Narrowing / Downward Conversion)** — moving from a *larger* to a *smaller* type. **Risky**; precision or magnitude may be lost.
   - `double` → `float`, `float` → `int`, `int` → `char`, `long` → `int`

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU board exam phrasing: *"Does the conversion cause data loss?"* — Always classify the assignment as **widening** or **narrowing** before answering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Type Conversion Hierarchy (Conversion Rank)

C evaluates mixed-type expressions and assignments using a **fixed promotion hierarchy**. From the *highest rank* (most precision/capacity) to the *lowest*:

$$
\text{long double} \;>\; \text{double} \;>\; \text{float} \;>\; \text{unsigned long} \;>\; \text{long} \;>\; \text{unsigned int} \;>\; \text{int} \;>\; \text{unsigned short} \;>\; \text{short} \;>\; \text{unsigned char} \;>\; \text{char}
$$

> When two different types appear in an assignment, the *lower-ranked* operand is converted to the *higher-ranked* type, then the LHS type is enforced.

---

## 2.2 The Operational Algorithm — Step-by-Step

When the compiler encounters `LHS = RHS;` with mismatched types, it executes:

1. **Step 1: Type Inspection** — Identify the data type of `RHS` expression and the declared type of `LHS` variable.
2. **Step 2: Rank Comparison** — Compare their positions in the conversion hierarchy.
3. **Step 3: Coercion** — Convert the RHS value to the LHS type using a *narrowing* or *widening* rule.
4. **Step 4: Storage** — The converted value is bit-cast into the LHS variable's memory representation.
5. **Step 5 (Implicit):** No warning is issued unless the conversion is *potentially lossy* (compiler-dependent flag like `-Wconversion` in GCC).

---

## 2.3 Specific Conversion Rules (High-Yield for KTU)

### Rule A: `float` / `double` → `int` (Truncation)
The fractional part is **discarded** (truncated toward zero). The result is **rounded toward zero**, *not* rounded to nearest.

$$
\text{intResult} = \lfloor x \rfloor \quad \text{if } x \ge 0, \qquad \lceil x \rceil - 1 \quad \text{if } x < 0
$$

Example: `(int) 3.9 = 3`, `(int) -3.9 = -3`.

### Rule B: `int` → `float` / `double` (Zero Extension)
The integer is mathematically exact. No rounding, no loss.

$$
x_{\text{float}} = x_{\text{int}}.0
$$

### Rule C: `char` → `int` (Sign- or Zero-Extension)
The character is promoted using its **ASCII (or system character set) numeric value**. If `char` is *signed* and the bit pattern represents a negative value, **sign extension** occurs (all upper bits become 1). If *unsigned*, **zero extension** occurs (upper bits become 0).

### Rule D: `int` → `char` (Modular Reduction)
The integer is reduced modulo $2^8 = 256$ to fit into one byte. Only the **lowest 8 bits** are retained.

$$
\text{charValue} = \text{intValue} \bmod 256
$$

### Rule E: `double` → `float` (Precision Loss)
64-bit `double` is rounded to nearest representable 32-bit `float` (about **7 significant decimal digits** survive).

### Rule F: `long` → `int` (Implementation-Defined)
On 32-bit systems they are equivalent; on 64-bit Linux `long` is 64 bits, so the upper 32 bits are lost. May also wrap around modulo $2^{32}$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Conversion** | **Mechanism** | **Mathematical Operation** | **Data Loss?** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `double` → `int` | Truncation toward zero | $\lfloor x \rceil$ | **Yes (fractional part lost)** | `(int) 7.99 = 7` |
| `float`  → `int` | Truncation toward zero | $\lfloor x \rceil$ | **Yes** | `(int) -2.3 = -2` |
| `int`    → `double` | Exact promotion | $x + 0.0$ | No | `(double) 5 = 5.0` |
| `int`    → `float` | Exact (small range) | $x + 0.0\text{f}$ | No (typically) | `(float) 100 = 100.0\text{f}$ |
| `char`   → `int` | Sign or zero extension | $\text{ASCII}(c)$ | No | `('A') → 65` |
| `int`    → `char` | Modular reduction | $x \bmod 256$ | **Yes (overflow truncated)** | `(char) 321 = 65` |
| `double` → `float` | Rounding to 32-bit | nearest $32$-bit float | **Yes (precision lost)** | `(float) 0.1\text{f}$ ≠ $0.1$ exactly |
| `long`   → `int`  | Bit truncation | $x \bmod 2^{32}$ | **Yes (high bits lost)** | depends on platform |
| `int`    → `short`| Modular reduction | $x \bmod 2^{16}$ | **Yes** | `(short) 70000 = 4464` |
| `float`  → `double` | Exact promotion | $x$ widened | No | `(double) 3.14\text{f} = 3.14\text{...}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip:** For KTU, the four highest-yield conversions are:
> **double→int (truncate), int→double (exact), int→char (mod 256), char→int (ASCII).**
> Examiners love testing these.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Embedded Systems** — Microcontroller ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) readings return `int` values representing voltages; mixed mode assignment converts them to `float` for engineering unit calculations (e.g., voltage → temperature).
2. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** — Audio samples are `int16_t`; scaling by `0.5` requires `int * float` mixed expressions, the result is then cast back to `int16_t` for storage.
3. **Image Processing** — Pixel values (0–255 `unsigned char`) must be promoted to `int` or `float` for arithmetic, then demoted back to `char` for display.
4. **Scientific Computing** — Counters (`int`) are mixed with measured quantities (`double`) in formulas like $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ where $t$ is measured with sub-integer precision.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through *every* conversion example end-to-end so the underlying arithmetic is fully transparent. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example #1: `double` → `int` (Narrowing Truncation)

**Code:**
```c
double price = 249.75;
int    rupees = price;
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS (price)} &= 249.75 \\
\text{LHS type} &= \text{int} \\
\text{Conversion rule} &= \text{Truncation toward zero} \\
\text{Result} &= \text{remove fractional part} \\
\text{rupees} &= 249
\end{aligned}
$$

> The value `0.75` is **discarded**, not rounded. So `249.75` becomes `249`, *not* `250`.

**Negative Number Variant:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= -3.9 \\
\text{cast to int} &= \lceil -3.9 \rceil - 1 \\
&= -3 - 1 = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

The result is `-3`, not `-4`, because C truncation **rounds toward zero**, not toward $-\infty$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #2: `int` → `float` (Widening Promotion)

**Code:**
```c
int    count = 7;
float  ratio = count;
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS (count)} &= 7 \\
\text{LHS type} &= \text{float (IEEE-754 32-bit)} \\
\text{Conversion rule} &= \text{Exact promotion} \\
\text{Mathematical result} &= 7 + 0.0 \\
\text{ratio} &= 7.0\text{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

> No loss of information. The integer `7` is representable exactly in IEEE-754 single precision because $7 < 2^{24}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #3: `int` → `char` (Modular Reduction)

**Code:**
```c
int  code = 321;
char ch   = code;
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS (code)} &= 321 \\
\text{LHS type} &= \text{char (8 bits)} \\
\text{Conversion rule} &= \text{Modular reduction by } 2^8 = 256 \\
\text{result} &= 321 \bmod 256 \\
&= 321 - 256 = 65 \\
\text{ch} &= \text{ASCII 65} = \text{'A'}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Visualization of bit reduction:**
>
> Binary of 321: `0000 0001 0100 0001` (16 bits shown for clarity)
> Lower 8 bits:   `0100 0001` = 65 = `'A'`
> Upper 8 bits:   `0000 0001` = discarded.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example #4: `char` → `int` (ASCII Promotion)

**Code:**
```c
char letter = 'Z';
int  ascii  = letter;
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS (letter)} &= \text{'Z'} \\
\text{ASCII code of 'Z'} &= 90 \\
\text{LHS type} &= \text{int (typically 32 bits)} \\
\text{Conversion rule} &= \text{Sign extension (signed char) or zero extension (unsigned char)} \\
\text{ascii} &= 90
\end{aligned}
$$

**For signed char (bit pattern `0101 1010` = 90):** upper bits filled with `0` because MSB is `0`.
**For signed char with value `-1` (bit pattern `1111 1111`):** upper bits filled with `1`, giving `int -1` (sign extension preserves sign).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example #5: Mixed-Mode Expression (Promotion Within an Expression)

**Code:**
```c
int    a = 5, b = 2;
float  result = a / b;
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Both operands are } \text{int.} \\
\text{Integer division is performed first:} \quad 5 / 2 = 2 \text{ (not 2.5)} \\
\text{Step 2: The integer } 2 \text{ is then promoted to } \text{float} = 2.0 \\
\text{result} &= 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students write `float result = a / b;` expecting `2.5`. **They get `2.0`.** The division is performed *before* the assignment, when both operands are still `int`. To get `2.5`, you must cast: `float result = (float)a / b;`.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example #6: Double → Float (Precision Demotion)

**Code:**
```c
double precise = 0.123456789012345;
float  approx  = precise;
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{double has } &\approx 15\text{–}17 \text{ significant decimal digits} \\
\text{float  has } &\approx 6\text{–}9 \text{ significant decimal digits} \\
\text{Rounded to nearest 32-bit float:} \\
\text{approx} &= 0.12345679\text{f} \quad \text{(8 digits retained)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The digits `012345` are *lost in rounding*; the value is the nearest representable IEEE-754 single-precision number.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Common Assignment Outcomes

| **LHS Type** | **RHS Expression** | **Final Value** | **Lost?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int x` | `x = 5/2;`        | `2`       | Yes (fraction `.5`) |
| `float x` | `x = 5/2;`      | `2.0`     | Yes (computed as int first) |
| `float x` | `x = 5.0/2;`    | `2.5`     | No |
| `int x`   | `x = 'B';`      | `66`      | No (ASCII) |
| `char x`  | `x = 300;`      | `44` (= 300 mod 256) | Yes (high bits) |
| `double x`| `x = 5;`        | `5.0`     | No |
| `int x`   | `x = 3.99;`     | `3`       | Yes (truncation) |
| `short x` | `x = 100000;`   | `-31072`  | Yes (16-bit overflow) |
| `int x`   | `x = 3.0/2;`    | `1`       | Yes (int division first) |
| `float x` | `x = 3.0/2;`    | `1.5`     | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Type Conversion Hierarchy (Top-Down Rank)

This diagram visualizes the *promotion rank* used by the C compiler when deciding conversion direction.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["LONG DOUBLE 80 BIT"] --> A2["DOUBLE 64 BIT"]
    A2 --> A3["FLOAT 32 BIT"]
    A3 --> A4["UNSIGNED LONG 64 BIT"]
    A4 --> A5["LONG 64 BIT"]
    A5 --> A6["UNSIGNED INT 32 BIT"]
    A6 --> A7["INT 32 BIT"]
    A7 --> A8["UNSIGNED SHORT 16 BIT"]
    A8 --> A9["SHORT 16 BIT"]
    A9 --> A10["UNSIGNED CHAR 8 BIT"]
    A10 --> A11["CHAR 8 BIT"]

    style A1 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000
    style A2 fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#000
    style A3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000
    style A4 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#000
    style A5 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#000
    style A6 fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#000
    style A7 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#000
    style A8 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#000
    style A9 fill:#f48fb1,stroke:#000
    style A10 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000
    style A11 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows point from higher rank → lower rank. A type is converted *upward* (toward the gold tip) when it is on the RHS of an assignment to a *larger* LHS. It is converted *downward* (toward the orange tip) when assigned to a *smaller* LHS.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mixed Mode Assignment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["RHS EXPRESSION"] --> eval["STEP 1 EVALUATE RHS"]
    eval --> rankchk["STEP 2 COMPARE TYPE RANKS"]
    rankchk --> det{"STEP 3 NARROWING OR WIDENING"}

    det -- WIDENING --> promote["PROMOTE TO LHS TYPE EXACT"]
    promote --> store["STEP 5 STORE INTO LHS"]

    det -- NARROWING --> truncate["TRUNCATE ROUND OR MOD 2 TO THE RIGHT"]
    truncate --> lossflag{"POTENTIAL DATA LOSS"}
    lossflag -- YES --> warn["COMPILER WARNING OPTIONAL"]
    lossflag -- NO --> store
    warn --> store

    store --> out["LHS VARIABLE UPDATED"]

    style src fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000
    style out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000
    style warn fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000
    style det fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000
    style lossflag fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the *decision pipeline* the compiler follows. Start at `RHS EXPRESSION` on the left, follow the arrows. The decision diamonds ask: *Is this widening or narrowing?* If narrowing, *Is data lost?* Only after all checks does the value reach the final LHS storage node.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Conversion Direction Visual

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1["CHAR TO INT"] -->|"SAFE PROMOTION"| p2["ASCII EXTENSION"]
    p3["INT TO CHAR"] -->|"MOD 256 DEMOTION"| p4["OVERFLOW LOSS RISK"]
    p5["FLOAT TO INT"] -->|"TRUNCATION"| p6["FRACTIONAL PART LOST"]
    p7["INT TO FLOAT"] -->|"SAFE PROMOTION"| p8["EXACT WIDENING"]
    p9["DOUBLE TO FLOAT"] -->|"PRECISION LOSS"| p10["ROUNDED TO 7 DIGITS"]

    style p2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000
    style p8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000
    style p4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000
    style p6 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000
    style p10 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000
```

> **Color code:** green = safe promotion, red = narrowing loss, yellow = precision loss.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define mixed mode assignment in C. Why is the destination type given priority over the source type during conversion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mixed mode assignment occurs when the RHS expression of an assignment and the LHS variable have different fundamental data types. The C compiler automatically converts the RHS value to the LHS type using the *type conversion hierarchy* before storing it.

The destination type is given priority because:

1. **Memory layout is fixed** — the LHS variable has a pre-allocated memory size (e.g., 4 bytes for `int`); it cannot be enlarged at runtime.
2. **Type consistency** — the variable's declared type defines what values it can faithfully represent; any other value must conform to that representation.
3. **Compiler design** — C's grammar rule `assignment-expression → conditional-expression` allows implicit conversion only *toward* the LHS, not the other way around, ensuring predictable storage.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**What is the result of the following C code? Justify.**

```c
int    a = 7, b = 2;
float  c = a / b;
printf("%f", c);
```

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Output:** `2.000000`

**Justification:**

1. The expression `a / b` is evaluated *first*. Since both `a` and `b` are `int`, the `/` operator performs **integer division** (truncation).
2. $7 \div 2 = 3$ with remainder $1$. The integer result is **3** (not 3.5).
3. The integer `3` is then *promoted* to `float` (because LHS is `float`), giving `3.0`.
4. The variable `c` is therefore assigned the value `3.0` and prints as `3.000000`.

> **Key point:** Mixed mode assignment alone does *not* force floating-point division. The *operator precedence* of `/` makes it run while both operands are still integers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**A. (a)** Explain the different type conversion rules in C with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

C performs implicit type conversion based on the **type conversion hierarchy**, which ranks types by size and precision:

$$
\text{long double} > \text{double} > \text{float} > \text{long} > \text{int} > \text{short} > \text{char}
$$

**The major rules are:**

1. **`double` / `float` → `int` (Truncation):**
   The fractional part is removed. The integer result is the value *rounded toward zero*.
   ```c
   int x = 7.99;     // x = 7
   int y = -3.9;     // y = -3  (rounds toward zero, NOT -4)
   ```

2. **`int` → `float` / `double` (Exact Promotion):**
   The integer is widened to floating-point form with no loss.
   ```c
   float f = 25;     // f = 25.0
   double d = 100;   // d = 100.0
   ```

3. **`char` → `int` (ASCII Promotion):**
   The character is converted to its integer ASCII code. If `char` is signed and negative, **sign extension** is performed.
   ```c
   int x = 'A';      // x = 65
   ```

4. **`int` → `char` (Modular Reduction):**
   The integer is reduced modulo 256; only the lowest 8 bits are kept.
   ```c
   char c = 321;     // c = 321 mod 256 = 65 = 'A'
   char d = 70000;   // d = 70000 mod 256 = 16 (data lost)
   ```

5. **`double` → `float` (Precision Demotion):**
   The 64-bit value is rounded to the nearest 32-bit IEEE-754 representation, losing roughly 8 decimal digits of precision.
   ```c
   float f = 3.14159265358979;  // stored as 3.1415927 (approx)
   ```

6. **`int` → `short` (16-bit Modulo):**
   The integer is reduced modulo 65536.
   ```c
   short s = 100000; // s = 100000 mod 65536 = 34464
   ```

> **Engineering relevance:** These rules govern every expression in real C code — from embedded ADC reads to scientific simulations.

---

**A. (b)** Predict the output of the following C program and explain each line: **[7 marks]**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main(void) {
    int    i = 65;
    char   c = i;
    float  f = 7 / 2;
    double d = 7.0 / 2;
    int    j = 3.99;
    printf("%c %f %lf %d\n", c, f, d, j);
    return 0;
}
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| **Line** | **Code** | **Evaluation** | **Final Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `int i = 65;` | Direct assignment | `i = 65` |
| 2 | `char c = i;` | $65 \bmod 256 = 65$ | `c = 'A'` (ASCII 65) |
| 3 | `float f = 7 / 2;` | `7` and `2` are `int` → integer division → `3`, then promoted to `float` | `f = 3.0` |
| 4 | `double d = 7.0 / 2;` | `7.0` is `double` → `2` is promoted to `double` → `3.5` | `d = 3.5` |
| 5 | `int j = 3.99;` | Truncation toward zero | `j = 3` |

**Final Output:**
```
A 3.000000 3.500000 3
```

> **Common pitfall:** Line 3 vs Line 4 — students often predict `3.5` for `f` because they forget the integer division happens *before* the result reaches the `float` LHS.

---

### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]`

**B. (a)** With neat examples, distinguish between **type promotion** and **type demotion** in C. Mention at least three examples of each. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Type Promotion (Widening):** Conversion from a *smaller* type to a *larger* type. Generally **safe** with no loss of data.

**Examples:**
1. `int` → `float`:
   ```c
   int   x = 5;
   float y = x;        // y = 5.0
   ```
2. `char` → `int`:
   ```c
   char c = 'B';
   int  n = c;         // n = 66
   ```
3. `float` → `double`:
   ```c
   float  f = 3.14f;
   double d = f;       // d = 3.140000104904175 (slight binary noise from float, no logic loss)
   ```

---

**Type Demotion (Narrowing):** Conversion from a *larger* type to a *smaller* type. **Risky** — truncation, modular wrap-around, or precision loss may occur.

**Examples:**
1. `double` → `int`:
   ```c
   double d = 9.99;
   int    i = d;       // i = 9   (fraction lost)
   ```
2. `int` → `char`:
   ```c
   int  k = 300;
   char c = k;         // c = 300 mod 256 = 44
   ```
3. `double` → `float`:
   ```c
   double pi = 3.14159265358979;
   float  p  = pi;     // p = 3.1415927 (precision lost)
   ```

---

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Promotion** | **Demotion** |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | small → large | large → small |
| Data loss | None | Possible (truncation / wrap-around) |
| Implicit warning | No | Sometimes |
| Examples | char→int, int→float | float→int, int→char |

---

**B. (b)** What will be the value of `result` in each case? Show your working. **[7 marks]**

```c
int   a = 10, b = 4;
float r1 = a / b;
float r2 = (float)a / b;
int   r3 = 5.5 + 2;
char  r4 = 200 + 100;
float r5 = 5 / 2.0;
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**`r1 = a / b;`**
$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= 10, \quad b = 4 \\
a/b \text{ evaluated as int division} &= 10/4 = 2 \text{ (remainder 2 discarded)} \\
\text{Promoted to float} &= 2.0 \\
r1 &= 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**`r2 = (float)a / b;`**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cast } a \text{ to float first} &= 10.0 \\
\text{Implicit promotion of } b &= 4.0 \\
\text{Result of } 10.0 / 4.0 &= 2.5 \\
r2 &= 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**`r3 = 5.5 + 2;`**
$$
\begin{aligned}
5.5 + 2 \text{ (int promoted to double)} &= 7.5 \\
\text{Assigned to int } r3 &\rightarrow \text{truncation} = 7 \\
r3 &= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**`r4 = 200 + 100;`**
$$
\begin{aligned}
200 + 100 &= 300 \\
300 \bmod 256 &= 44 \\
r4 &= 44 \quad (\text{or the ASCII character with code 44, which is `,`})
\end{aligned}
$$

**`r5 = 5 / 2.0;`**
$$
\begin{aligned}
5 \text{ is promoted to } 5.0 \\
2.0 \text{ is double, } 5.0 \text{ becomes double} \\
5.0 / 2.0 &= 2.5 \\
\text{Assigned to } \text{float } r5 &= 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary:** `r1 = 2.0`, `r2 = 2.5`, `r3 = 7`, `r4 = 44`, `r5 = 2.5`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating operator precedence with assignment conversion.** `int x = 5/2;` yields `2`, *not* `2.5`. The `/` runs as integer division *before* the LHS is even consulted.
> 2. **Rounding direction errors.** `(int) -3.9` is `-3`, not `-4`. C truncates *toward zero*, not *toward minus infinity*. KTU examiners frequently set traps with negative floats.
> 3. **Forgetting the modulus rule for `int → char`.** Always reduce mod 256 (or mod $2^N$ for N-bit targets). Students who write `(char) 300 = 300` lose 2 marks instantly.
> 4. **Confusing ASCII with arithmetic value.** `'A' + 1 = 66`, not `'A1'`. The character becomes an integer in mixed expressions.
> 5. **Skipping the *type promotion hierarchy* in answers.** Examiners want the rank chain — write it out. It is worth 1–2 marks every time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Mixed mode assignment** = implicit type conversion when LHS and RHS types differ.
- The **destination (LHS) type always wins.**
- The **type conversion hierarchy** ranks types from `long double` (top) to `char` (bottom).
- **`double`/`float` → `int`**: truncation toward zero. **No rounding to nearest.**
- **`int` → `float`/`double`**: exact promotion; no data loss.
- **`char` → `int`**: ASCII value; sign extension if signed and negative.
- **`int` → `char`**: reduce modulo **256** (keep lowest 8 bits).
- **`double` → `float`**: round to ~7 significant digits (precision loss).
- **`int` → `short`**: reduce modulo **65536** (16-bit wrap).
- **Integer division** in mixed expressions runs *before* assignment conversion — so `int / int → int` always.
- **Always classify** an assignment as **widening** (safe) or **narrowing** (risky) in your exam answer — examiners reward it.
- **Sign extension** vs **zero extension**: for signed types, MSB is replicated; for unsigned types, zero is filled.
- **Compiler warnings** about lossy conversion (`-Wconversion` in GCC) are *informational*, not errors — but in KTU you should *always* note the loss explicitly.
- **Real-world uses:** ADC reads, DSP scaling, image pixel manipulation, scientific formulas combining counters and measurements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Structures: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Structures: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Control Structures** are the fundamental programming constructs that determine the **order (sequence)** in which individual statements, instructions, or function calls are executed in a program. They govern the **flow of control** by introducing decision-making, repetition, and branching capabilities into the otherwise linear execution of instructions.

In the formal theory of structured programming (Dijkstra, 1968; Bohm & Jacopini, 1966), any computable function can be expressed using exactly **three** primitive control structures:

1. **Sequence** — Executing statements one after another in a linear order.
2. **Selection (Decision)** — Choosing between one or more alternative paths based on a Boolean condition.
3. **Iteration (Repetition/Loops)** — Repeating a block of statements while a condition holds true.

These three primitives, along with their structured derivatives, form the **theoretical minimum** required to express any algorithm. This is mathematically formalized as the **Structured Program Theorem** (also known as the Böhm–Jacopini theorem).

| Term | Symbol Used in Flowcharts | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Sequence | → (Arrow) | Straight-line execution |
| Selection | ◇ (Diamond) | Conditional branching |
| Iteration | ↺ (Curved arrow) | Loop-based repetition |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Train Track Switch**
> Imagine a train moving along a single track. Without any switches, the train can only go **forward in one direction** (this is *sequence*). Now imagine a railway switch operator standing at a junction — based on a signal (true/false), he flips the lever left or right, sending the train down **different paths** (this is *selection*). Finally, picture a roller coaster that loops around a track multiple times until a counter tells it to stop — that repeated circuit is *iteration*.

**In short:** Control structures are the **traffic police** of your program. Without them, your program is just a one-way street; with them, it becomes a fully navigable city grid.

## 1.3 Why Control Structures Matter in Engineering

- **Determinism:** Guarantee predictable, repeatable execution of software in safety-critical systems (avionics, medical devices, automotive ECU code).
- **Modularity:** Allow complex logic to be decomposed into small, verifiable units.
- **Maintainability:** Structured code (vs. unstructured `goto` spaghetti) reduces bugs by an estimated **80%** in large codebases (industry heuristic).
- **Verification & Testing:** Enable formal methods, model checking, and unit testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primitive Control Structures

### 2.1.1 Sequence
- **Definition:** Statements are executed in the exact order they appear, from top to bottom.
- **'Why':** Most fundamental execution model; every program implicitly begins with sequence.
- **'How':** The compiler/interpreter fetches and executes each instruction sequentially from memory.

$$ \text{Execution Order} = S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow S_3 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow S_n $$

### 2.1.2 Selection (Decision / Branching)
Selection allows the program to choose between alternate execution paths based on a **Boolean expression** that evaluates to either `true` or `false`.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$ \text{Decision Function } D: \mathbb{B} \rightarrow \text{Path} $$

where $\mathbb{B} = \{ \text{true}, \text{false} \}$.

**The three canonical forms of selection:**

| Form | Pseudocode | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple `if`** | `if (condition) then statement;` | Execute code only when condition holds. |
| **`if-else`** | `if (C) then S1 else S2;` | Choose one of two paths. |
| **Multi-way (`switch`/`case`)** | `switch (expr) { case v1: ... }` | Choose among N mutually exclusive paths. |

**Nested Selection:** A selection structure may contain another selection structure within either its `if` or `else` branch, yielding **N-way decisions** in $O(\log N)$ nesting depth or $O(N)$ flat cases.

### 2.1.3 Iteration (Loops)
Iteration repeats a block of statements as long as a condition is satisfied.

**Mathematical Formulation of Loop Execution Count:**

$$ \text{Iterations Executed} = \begin{cases} \lceil \frac{U - L + 1}{\text{step}} \rceil & \text{if terminating} \\ \infty & \text{if non-terminating (infinite loop)} \end{cases} $$

where $L$ and $U$ are the lower and upper loop bounds, and `step` is the increment.

**The three canonical forms of iteration:**

| Loop Type | Pre-test / Post-test | Condition Location | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **`while`** | Pre-test | Top of loop | May execute **0 times** if condition initially false. |
| **`do-while`** | Post-test | Bottom of loop | Executes **at least once**. |
| **`for`** | Pre-test | Top of loop | Counter-controlled; ideal for counted loops. |

## 2.2 Unstructured Control: The `goto` Statement

> [!WARNING]
> Although `goto` exists in C/C++/Java (as labeled `break`/`continue`), it is widely considered **harmful** because it violates the principle of single-entry, single-exit (SESE) blocks. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for unstructured code.

The **Structured Program Theorem** proves that **any `goto`-based program can be transformed into an equivalent structured program** using only sequence, selection, and iteration.

## 2.3 Properties of Well-Structured Programs

A control structure is considered **well-structured** if it satisfies:

1. **Single Entry Point:** Exactly one path enters the block.
2. **Single Exit Point:** Exactly one path exits the block.
3. **No Infinite Loops without Termination Proof:** Bounded loops must have a proven termination condition.
4. **No `goto` into the Middle of a Block.**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of test paths via McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components |
| 2 | Structured Program Theorem | Any program = Sequence + Selection + Iteration | Theorem (Böhm–Jacopini, 1966) |
| 3 | Loop execution count (counted `for`) | $N = \lfloor (U - L) / \text{step} \rfloor + 1$ | Bounded by $L$, $U$ |
| 4 | Big-O complexity of nested loops | $O(n \times m)$ for $n$ outer, $m$ inner | Worst-case time |
| 5 | Boolean condition truth table size | $2^k$ rows for $k$ variables | Combinatorial enumeration |
| 6 | Decision tree height (binary) | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Minimum comparisons |
| 7 | Switch complexity | $O(1)$ average case | Hash-based dispatch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity** is a **highly-weighted KTU topic**. You will be tested on it in subsequent modules. For now, remember: every `if`, `case`, `while`, `for`, `do-while`, `&&`, `\|\|` adds **1** to the complexity count.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** Sensor polling loops use `while(1)` infinite loops with `break` on fault conditions.
- **Database Engines:** Query optimizers build **decision trees** using selection structures to choose index scans vs. full table scans.
- **Compilers:** Control Flow Graphs (CFGs) are built directly from these structures for optimization passes.
- **AI/ML:** Decision trees in machine learning are literal implementations of nested `if-else` structures.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Brake-by-wire systems use nested selections to prioritize faults.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence Between `for` and `while` Loops

The KTU syllabus expects you to prove that any `for` loop can be rewritten as a `while` loop. Below is the **exhaustive** derivation.

### 3.1.1 General Form of a `for` Loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{for} \; &( \text{initialization}; \; \text{condition}; \; \text{update} ) \; \{ \\
& \quad \text{body}; \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Transformation

**Step 1:** Initialize the loop control variable **before** entering the loop (this is the *initialization* phase).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{initialization};
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the `condition`. If `true`, execute the body. If `false`, terminate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{while} \; (\text{condition}) \; \{ \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Inside the loop body, execute the `update` expression **after** the body but **before** the next condition check.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \quad \text{body}; \\
& \quad \text{update}; \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Equivalent `while` Loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{initialization}; \\
\text{while} \; (\text{condition}) \; \{ \\
\quad \text{body}; \\
\quad \text{update}; \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** The `for` loop is syntactic sugar. It packages three distinct phases — *initialization*, *test*, and *update* — into a single header line, but semantically behaves identically to the expanded `while` form. This is why we say `for` is just "counted iteration" built on top of "conditional iteration."

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Paths Through Nested Selections

Consider a program with $n$ independent `if-else` statements in sequence. We want to find the **total number of unique execution paths**.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let $P_i$ be the number of paths through the $i$-th selection structure. For a binary `if-else`, $P_i = 2$.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

For $n$ sequential selections, each path through structure $i$ must be combined with every path through structure $i+1$ (Cartesian product).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= P_1 \times P_2 \times \dots \times P_n \\
P_{\text{total}} &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i \\
P_{\text{total}} &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} 2 \\
P_{\text{total}} &= 2^n
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Example

For $n = 3$ sequential `if-else` statements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 2^3 = 8 \text{ unique execution paths}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **exponential path-explosion problem**, which is why exhaustive testing of all paths is infeasible for large programs — directly motivating **symbolic execution** and **model checking** in software verification.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Control Structures

| Property | Sequence | `if` / `if-else` | `switch` / `case` | `while` | `do-while` | `for` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Minimum executions** | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| **Maximum executions** | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| **Termination guarantee** | ✔ Always | ✔ Always | ✔ Always | ❌ Conditional | ❌ Conditional | ❌ Conditional |
| **Pre-test?** | N/A | ✔ Yes (Pre) | ✔ Yes (Pre) | ✔ Yes (Pre) | ❌ No (Post) | ✔ Yes (Pre) |
| **Use case** | Default flow | Binary decision | Multi-way dispatch | Event-driven | Menu loops | Counted loops |
| **Compound complexity** | None | Nested `if` | Nested `case` | Nested `while` | Nested `do` | Nested `for` |
| **KTU weightage** | Low | High | High | High | Medium | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram: The Three Primitives

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical visual representation of all three control structures as they appear in standard flowcharting notation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Statement 1]
    B --> C[Statement 2]
    C --> D[Statement 3]
    D --> E{Diamond Decision}
    E -- True --> F[Execute Block A]
    E -- False --> G[Execute Block B]
    F --> H{Condition True?}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Loop Body]
    I --> J[Update Counter]
    J --> H
    H -- No --> K([End])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The rectangular boxes (Statement 1, 2, 3) represent **sequence**.
- The diamond (Decision) with two outgoing arrows represents **selection**.
- The diamond (Condition) looping back to itself via the Body and Update blocks represents **iteration**.

## 4.2 Comparison: `while` vs `do-while` Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1([Start while]) --> W2{Condition True?}
    W2 -- Yes --> W3[Execute Body]
    W3 --> W2
    W2 -- No --> W4([Exit while])
    
    D1([Start do-while]) --> D2[Execute Body]
    D2 --> D3{Condition True?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D2
    D3 -- No --> D4([Exit do-while])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** In the `while` loop, the condition is tested **before** the body executes. In the `do-while` loop, the body executes **first**, then the condition is tested. This guarantees **at least one execution** for `do-while`.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Compiler View of Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Code] --> LEX[Lexer]
    LEX --> PARSE[Parser]
    PARSE --> AST[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    AST --> CFG[Control Flow Graph Builder]
    CFG --> BB1[Basic Block 1]
    CFG --> BB2[Basic Block 2]
    CFG --> BB3[Basic Block 3]
    BB1 --> EDGE1[Directed Edge]
    BB2 --> EDGE1
    BB1 --> EDGE2[Branch Edge]
    BB3 --> EDGE2
    EDGE1 --> OPT[Optimizer Pass]
    EDGE2 --> OPT
    OPT --> EXE[Executable Code]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Modern compilers translate each control structure into a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** where each statement becomes a *node* and each possible transfer of control becomes an *edge*. Optimizations like dead-code elimination, loop unrolling, and branch prediction operate directly on this CFG.

## 4.4 Nested Control Flow: A Realistic Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start]) --> INIT[Initialize counter i = 0]
    INIT --> LOOP{i less than N?}
    LOOP -- Yes --> BODY[Process element at index i]
    BODY --> COND{Value greater than threshold?}
    COND -- Yes --> ACTION[Increment positive count]
    COND -- No --> ACTION2[Increment negative count]
    ACTION --> UPDATE[i = i + 1]
    ACTION2 --> UPDATE
    UPDATE --> LOOP
    LOOP -- No --> END([Print final counts and End])
```

This diagram models the **classic counted-loop-with-nested-decision** pattern — a question type that appears almost every semester in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define control structures. List the three primitive control structures with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Control structures** are programming constructs that determine the **order of execution** of statements in a program.
>
