**Step 4.** Without `break`, the loop always runs exactly 1000 iterations:

$$
T_{\text{no-break}} = 1000 \cdot T_{\text{iter}}
$$

**Step 5.** Speedup in the **best-case**:

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{best}} = \frac{T_{\text{no-break}}}{T_{\text{best}}} = \frac{1000 \cdot T_{\text{iter}}}{1 \cdot T_{\text{iter}}} = 1000\times
$$

This demonstrates the **engineering utility of `break`**: it converts a fixed-cost loop into a *best-case-fast, worst-case-same* construct.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Trace: `exit()` Cleanup Chain

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
void h1(void) { printf("h1\n"); }
void h2(void) { printf("h2\n"); }
int main(void) {
    atexit(h1);
    atexit(h2);
    printf("main\n");
    exit(7);
    return 0;
}
```

**Execution flow:**

**Step 1.** `atexit(h1)` registers `h1` in the **atexit handler stack** (LIFO).

**Step 2.** `atexit(h2)` registers `h2` on top of `h1`.

**Step 3.** `exit(7)` is invoked. The runtime performs the following (per ISO C §7.22.4.4):

$$
\text{Exit Sequence} = \Big[ \text{call handlers in LIFO order} \,\Big] \;\rightarrow\; \text{flush + close stdio streams} \;\rightarrow\; \text{\_Exit(status)}
$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, output order is:

```
main
h2       ← registered last, called first
h1
```

**Step 5.** Program terminates with status **7** (the value passed to `exit()`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Pitfall**: `exit()` *does* invoke `atexit` handlers, but `abort()` *does NOT*. This is a frequent KTU viva question.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Diagram — Unconditional Branching Constructs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Unconditional Branching in C / C++"]:::root
    A --> B["Intra-Function Transfers"]:::group
    A --> C["Inter-Function Transfers"]:::group
    A --> D["Program-Termination Transfers"]:::group

    B --> B1["goto label"]:::leaf
    B --> B2["break"]:::leaf
    B --> B3["continue"]:::leaf
    B --> B4["return value"]:::leaf

    C --> C1["longjmp env, val"]:::leaf
    C --> C2["throw exception C++"]:::leaf

    D --> D1["exit status"]:::leaf
    D --> D2["abort void"]:::leaf
    D --> D3["_Exit status"]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef group fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0ea5e9,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef leaf fill:#fef3c7,color:#1f2937,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:1px;
```

```mermaid
    S2 --> S3["Statement 3"]:::node
    S3 --> S4["Statement 4"]:::node

    S3 -. "goto L5" .-> S5
    S3 -. "break" .-> S6["Statement 6 / After Loop"]:::node

```

## 4.3 Cleanup-Chain Block Diagram (Exit Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["main calls exit"]:::mnode
    M --> H2["atexit Handler 2 last registered runs first"]:::hnode
    H2 --> H1["atexit Handler 1 runs next"]:::hnode
    H1 --> FL["Flush all open stdio streams"]:::fnode
    FL --> CL["Close all open stdio streams"]:::cnode
    CL --> EX["_Exit status"]:::enode

```

## 4.4 Program-Counter State-Transition Block Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SeqExec
    SeqExec: Sequential Execution
    SeqExec --> SeqExec: PC = PC + sizeof(instr)
    SeqExec --> UncondJump: Unconditional Branch Reached
    UncondJump: Unconditional Jump
    UncondJump --> SeqExec: PC = address of target label
    SeqExec --> FuncReturn: return Encountered
    FuncReturn: Function Return
    FuncReturn --> SeqExec: PC = saved return address
    SeqExec --> ProgTerm: exit or abort
    ProgTerm: Program Termination
    ProgTerm --> [*]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Differentiate between **conditional** and **unconditional branching** in C. List any four unconditional branching constructs with one-line descriptions.

**Model Answer (Expected Key Points):**
- **Conditional branching:** Control transfer depends on evaluation of a Boolean/relational/logical expression. Examples: `if`, `switch`, ternary `?:`.
- **Unconditional branching:** Control transfer occurs the moment the statement is executed, with no predicate evaluated. The decision is *implicit* in the code path itself.
- Four unconditional constructs:
  1. `goto label;` — jumps to a label in the same function.
  2. `break;` — exits the innermost loop or `switch`.
  3. `continue;` — skips remainder of current loop iteration.
  4. `return value;` — returns from current function to caller.
- (Bonus point for `exit()`, `abort()`, or `longjmp()`.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
What is the difference between `exit(0)` and `return 0;` in a C program? Which standard header must be included to use `exit()`?

**Model Answer (Expected Key Points):**
- `return 0;` in `main()` transfers control to the runtime startup routine and supplies `0` as the exit status. It also implicitly invokes the normal exit sequence (atexit handlers, stream flush) via `main`'s epilogue.
- `exit(0)` is a **library function** that *explicitly* invokes the normal termination sequence and may be called from **any function**, not just `main`.
- Both ultimately terminate the program; however, `exit()` can be called from any scope, while `return 0` is valid only inside a function.
- Required header: **`<stdlib.h>`**.
- (Bonus: `abort()` does *not* run `atexit` handlers and raises `SIGABRT`.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Module 2 carries **internal choice** in the ESE. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both are 14 marks: typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the `goto` statement in C with its syntax, rules, and **two valid engineering use cases**. Provide a code example showing how `goto` is used for **centralized error handling** in a function that allocates three resources. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the difference between `break` and `continue` with suitable C code examples. Also show, with a program fragment, how `break` causes an unconditional exit from a **nested loop** structure. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Syntax:**
```c
goto label;
...
label:
    statement;
```

**Rules:**
1. The label is a user-defined identifier followed by a colon.
2. Both `goto` and the label must lie in the *same function*.
3. Jump must not bypass an automatic-variable initialization into its scope (causes undefined behaviour).

**Centralized Error-Handling Example:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
int process(void) {
    FILE *fa = fopen("a.txt", "r");
    if (!fa) goto cleanup;
    FILE *fb = fopen("b.txt", "r");
    if (!fb) goto cleanup;
    FILE *fc = fopen("c.txt", "r");
    if (!fc) goto cleanup;

    /* ... do real work ... */
    fclose(fc);
    fclose(fb);
    fclose(fa);
    return 0;

cleanup:
    if (fc) fclose(fc);
    if (fb) fclose(fb);
    if (fa) fclose(fa);
    return -1;
}
```

**Two Valid Use Cases:**
1. **Centralized cleanup** in functions that acquire multiple resources (kernel-style).
2. **Breaking out of deeply nested loops** when the search succeeds deep inside.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

`break` exits the **innermost enclosing loop** entirely; `continue` skips the **remainder of the current iteration** and re-tests the loop condition.

```c
/* break example */
#include <stdio.h>
int main(void) {
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        if (i == 3) break;
        printf("%d ", i);     // prints 0 1 2
    }
    return 0;
}

/* continue example */
#include <stdio.h>
int main(void) {
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        if (i == 3) continue;
        printf("%d ", i);     // prints 0 1 2 4
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Nested-loop `break`:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main(void) {
    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
            if (i == 1 && j == 1) {
                printf("Breaking at i=%d j=%d\n", i, j);
                break;                  // exits only the inner j-loop
            }
            printf("(%d,%d) ", i, j);
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}
```
**Output:**
```
(0,0) (0,1) (0,2)
(1,0) Breaking at i=1 j=1
(2,0) (2,1) (2,2)
```

> Note: The `break` here exits only the innermost loop (the `j`-loop), so the `i`-loop continues.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the `return` statement. How does it differ from `exit()`? Provide a C program demonstrating that `return` from `main` invokes the same cleanup chain as `exit()`. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What are the purposes of `atexit()`, `abort()`, and `_Exit()`? Compare them in a tabular form. Write a short program that registers two cleanup handlers using `atexit()` and shows their **LIFO invocation order**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**`return` statement:**
- Used to exit a function and optionally return a value to the caller.
- In `main()`, `return expr;` is *equivalent* to `exit(expr);` because the runtime startup code invokes the same termination sequence when `main` returns.

**Difference between `return` and `exit()`:**

| Aspect | `return` | `exit()` |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Exits only the current function | Terminates the entire program |
| Callable from | Any function | Any function |
| `atexit` handlers | Run only when returned from `main` | Always run |
| Header | None | `<stdlib.h>` |

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
void bye(void) { printf("Cleanup!\n"); }
int main(void) {
    atexit(bye);
    return 0;                  /* triggers atexit handler same as exit(0) */
}
```
**Output:** `Cleanup!`

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

| Function | Purpose | Runs atexit handlers? | Sends signal? | Header |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `exit(status)` | Normal program termination | **Yes** (in LIFO order) | No | `<stdlib.h>` |
| `abort()` | Abnormal program termination | **No** | **Yes** (`SIGABRT`) | `<stdlib.h>` |
| `_Exit(status)` | Immediate termination, no cleanup | **No** | No | `<stdlib.h>` |

**LIFO Demonstration Program:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
void handlerA(void) { printf("Handler A invoked.\n"); }
void handlerB(void) { printf("Handler B invoked.\n"); }
int main(void) {
    atexit(handlerA);          // registered first
    atexit(handlerB);          // registered second
    printf("Main: about to exit.\n");
    exit(0);
}
```
**Output:**
```
Main: about to exit.
Handler B invoked.    ← registered last, called first (LIFO)
Handler A invoked.
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `break` scope**: Many students write *"break exits all loops"*. It does **NOT** — it exits only the **innermost** enclosing loop. To break out of nested loops, you must use a flag + `goto` or `return`.
> 2. **Confusing `exit()` and `abort()` cleanup behaviour**: `exit()` runs `atexit` handlers; `abort()` does NOT. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting `<stdlib.h>`**: Code using `exit()`, `abort()`, or `atexit()` without including `<stdlib.h>` is **non-portable** and loses 1 mark on ESE.
> 4. **Spaghetti-code code in `goto` answers**: Examiners penalize excessive `goto` use. Always demonstrate a **structured, justified** use (e.g., cleanup, FSM).
> 5. **`goto` into a block**: If you `goto` past a variable declaration into its scope, the behaviour is **undefined** — examiners love this trick question.
> 6. **Forgetting that `return` from `main` invokes cleanup**: Students often wrongly claim `return` does NOT run `atexit` handlers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- 🔹 **Unconditional branching** transfers control *without* any predicate test.
- 🔹 The **5 primary C constructs**: `goto`, `break`, `continue`, `return`, `exit()`.
- 🔹 `break` → **exits innermost** loop/switch. `continue` → **skips rest** of current iteration.
- 🔹 `goto` is **function-scoped** — cannot cross functions (use `longjmp` instead).
- 🔹 `return` from `main` ≡ `exit(value)` — both run `atexit` handlers.
- 🔹 `exit()` runs `atexit` handlers in **LIFO order**; `abort()` does **NOT**.
- 🔹 `_Exit()` is the **immediate** termination (no cleanup at all).
- 🔹 The C standard forbids jumping past a variable's initialization into its scope (undefined behaviour).
- 🔹 Legitimate uses of `goto`: **centralized cleanup**, **deep-loop exit**, **finite-state machines**.
- 🔹 Every unconditional branch ultimately lowers to a **direct PC assignment** in assembly.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guarded Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Guarded Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Guarded Commands** are a mathematical notation for the formal specification and derivation of programs, introduced by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in his seminal 1975 paper *"Guarded commands, nondeterminacy, and formal derivation of programs"*. A guarded command is a syntactic construct of the form:

$$G \rightarrow S$$

where **G** is a Boolean expression called the **guard** and **S** is a statement list (the command). The statement **S** is *eligible* for execution *only* when the guard **G** evaluates to `true`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard Phrasing):**
> A *guarded command* is a language construct that pairs a Boolean precondition (the *guard*) with a statement (the *command*). The command is enabled for selection if and only if the guard evaluates to true, allowing the abstraction of selection and iteration in a formally verifiable, non-deterministic manner.

The notation defines **two fundamental control structures**:

1. **Alternative (Selection) Construct** — used in place of the conventional `if-then-else`.
2. **Repetitive (Iteration) Construct** — used in place of the conventional `while` loop.

The two structures are written using the *guarded command set* notation. Two special symbols are used:
- **▯** (the *shill* symbol) — separates alternative guarded commands within a set.
- **FI** and **OD** — mirror the opening `IF` and `DO` to delimit the construct (palindromic bracketing, Dijkstra's stylistic choice).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Traffic Signal with a Greedy Sensor" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a smart traffic intersection. The intersection accepts a vehicle (executes a command) **only** if the corresponding lane sensor (the *guard*) detects that a vehicle is waiting (evaluates to `true`). If multiple sensors trigger simultaneously, the controller **does not pre-commit** to any single lane — it selects one *non-deterministically* and fairly. The vehicle is released only when the guard permits it. Similarly, a guarded command constructs a set of candidate actions, each "guarded" by a condition; the action is "guarded in" — that is, protected / enabled — only by a true guard.

In plain English, a guarded command is a **conditional instruction** that does not commit to a single path at the syntactic level. The selection of which command to execute is *deferred* to execution time and may be *non-deterministic* — meaning any eligible command is acceptable. This freedom from forced choice is precisely what makes guarded commands ideal for *formal program derivation*, where correctness is proved *before* committing to a specific implementation.

## 1.3 Key Properties (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Guarded Command Semantics:**
>
> 1. **Non-determinism** — When multiple guards evaluate to true, the language permits *any* one of the corresponding commands to be selected. The programmer is not forced to dictate a specific choice.
> 2. **Atomicity of Selection** — The selection of a guard and the execution of its associated command are an *atomic* event; no intermediate state changes occur between evaluation and execution.
> 3. **Failure-Stop Semantics** — In the alternative construct, if *no* guard is true, the program *aborts* (a deliberate "proof obligation" forcing the programmer to handle all cases, including invalid states).

These three properties are the formal bedrock of guarded commands and recur in every KTU question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Alternative Construct (`IF ... FI`)

The alternative construct generalises the conventional `if-then-else` chain. Its syntax is:

$$\text{IF} \;\; G_1 \rightarrow S_1 \;\; \square \;\; G_2 \rightarrow S_2 \;\; \square \;\; \dots \;\; \square \;\; G_n \rightarrow S_n \;\; \text{FI}$$

where each $G_i$ is a guard (Boolean expression) and each $S_i$ is a statement list.

**Operational Semantics (Step-by-Step):**

1. The set $\{G_1, G_2, \dots, G_n\}$ of guards is evaluated against the *current program state*.
2. The set of *eligible* commands is identified: $\mathcal{E} = \{ S_i \mid G_i \equiv \text{true} \}$.
3. If $\mathcal{E} \neq \emptyset$ (the empty set), one $S_i \in \mathcal{E}$ is selected **non-deterministically** and executed.
4. If $\mathcal{E} = \emptyset$ (no guard is true), the construct **aborts** — equivalent to a `STOP` or unreachable state in formal proof terms.
5. After the selected $S_i$ completes, control passes to the statement following `FI`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Aborts on Empty Set" Rule Matters:**
> This rule is *not* a deficiency — it is a *design feature*. By forcing the programmer to ensure that at least one guard is true at every reachable point, the language guarantees **exhaustiveness** of the case analysis. This is why guarded commands are popular in **safety-critical systems** (avionics, railway signalling, medical devices) and the basis of languages like **SPARK** (a formally verifiable subset of Ada).

## 2.2 The Repetitive Construct (`DO ... OD`)

The repetitive construct generalises the conventional `while` loop. Its syntax is:

$$\text{DO} \;\; G_1 \rightarrow S_1 \;\; \square \;\; G_2 \rightarrow S_2 \;\; \square \;\; \dots \;\; \square \;\; G_n \rightarrow S_n \;\; \text{OD}$$

**Operational Semantics:**

1. The guards are evaluated against the current state.
2. If *all* guards are false, the construct terminates successfully and control passes to the statement following `OD`.
3. If at least one guard is true, an enabled command is selected **non-deterministic ally** and executed.
4. Control then returns to the start of the construct, and the guards are re-evaluated.
5. The loop continues until *all* guards become false — this is the **termination condition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Termination via the "Decreasing Variant" Principle:**
> For a `DO` construct to *guarantee* termination, the programmer must exhibit a *variant* (a well-founded natural number or ordinal) that strictly decreases with each iteration. This is the algorithmic counterpart of the **Well-Ordering Principle** in number theory and is the cornerstone of Dijkstra's program-correctness methodology.

## 2.3 Weakest Precondition Calculus

Dijkstra's guarded commands come with a rigorous **predicate transformer** semantics. The *weakest precondition* $wp(\text{Cmd}, Q)$ is the weakest condition $P$ such that execution of `Cmd` from a state satisfying $P$ is guaranteed to terminate in a state satisfying postcondition $Q$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Weakest Precondition $wp(S, Q)$ | Termination Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Skip** | $Q$ | Always terminates |
| **Abort** | `false` | Never guaranteed to terminate |
| **Assignment** $x := E$ | $Q[x \mapsto E]$ (substitute $E$ for $x$ in $Q$) | Always terminates |
| **Sequencing** $S_1 ; S_2$ | $wp(S_1, wp(S_2, Q))$ | Both must terminate |
| **Alternative** `IF G[]S FI` | $\displaystyle \bigvee_{i} (G_i \;\land\; wp(S_i, Q))$ | At least one $G_i$ must be true |
| **Repetitive** `DO G[]S OD` | $\displaystyle \bigvee_{k \ge 0} \; H_k(Q)$ (limit of a Kleene chain) | All guards eventually false |

> [!NOTE]
> The repetitive construct's precondition is the **greatest fixpoint** of the equation $X \;\equiv\; (\neg G_1 \land \neg G_2 \land \dots \land Q) \;\lor\; \bigvee_i (G_i \;\land\; wp(S_i, X))$. KTU 14-mark questions frequently test the ability to *derive* this fixpoint for a small loop.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | SPARK/Ada guarded commands used to derive avionics software whose correctness is *proved*, not just tested. |
| **Railway Signalling (EN 50128)** | SIL-4 train-control software (e.g., Metro systems) uses guarded command derivations. |
| **Cryptographic Protocols** | The TLS 1.3 specification was developed using formal guarded-command-style state machines. |
| **Compilers** | Instruction selection in modern compilers (e.g., LLVM's SelectionDAG) uses guarded command patterns to encode peephole rewrites. |
| **Model Checkers** | Internal representations in tools like SPIN and CBMC are guarded command systems. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Guarded commands bridge the gap between *algorithm* (mathematical specification) and *program* (executable code). They are a foundational topic in formal methods, appearing in nearly every advanced programming paradigms course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Max of Two Numbers

**Specification (KTU 2024 standard form):**

Given two integers $a$ and $b$, derive a guarded command program to compute $m = \max(a, b)$.

**Step 1 — Define the postcondition.**

$$Q: m = \max(a, b) \equiv (m \ge a \land m \ge b \land (m = a \lor m = b))$$

**Step 2 — Identify the two mutually exclusive cases.**

$$\text{Case 1: } a \ge b \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = a$$
$$\text{Case 2: } b > a \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = b$$

**Step 3 — Write the alternative construct with guards.**

We pair each case with its corresponding assignment, *guarding* each by the case condition:

$$\text{IF} \;\; a \ge b \rightarrow m := a \;\; \square \;\; b > a \rightarrow m := b \;\; \text{FI}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the precondition (proof obligation).**

We need $\forall a, b: \; wp(\text{IF}, Q) \equiv \text{true}$. Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
wp(\text{IF}, Q) &\equiv (a \ge b \;\land\; wp(m := a, Q)) \;\lor\; (b > a \;\land\; wp(m := b, Q)) \\
&\equiv (a \ge b \;\land\; a = \max(a, b)) \;\lor\; (b > a \;\land\; b = \max(a, b))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Resolve the disjuncts.**

In the first disjunct, $a = \max(a, b) \equiv a \ge b$, so the conjunct reduces to $(a \ge b \land a \ge b) \equiv a \ge b$.

In the second disjunct, $b = \max(a, b) \equiv b > a$, so the conjunct reduces to $(b > a \land b > a) \equiv b > a$.

$$wp(\text{IF}, Q) \;\equiv\; (a \ge b) \;\lor\; (b > a) \;\equiv\; a \ge b \lor b > a \;\equiv\; a \ne b \;\lor\; a = b \;\equiv\; \text{true}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The proof obligation is satisfied for *all* integer inputs. The program is therefore **correct by construction**, not by testing.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Integer Division by Repeated Subtraction

**Specification:** Compute $q$ and $r$ such that $a = b \cdot q + r$ with $0 \le r < b$, assuming $a \ge 0$ and $b > 0$.

**Step 1 — Initialise.** Set $q := 0$ and $r := a$. The invariant is:

$$P: a = b \cdot q + r \;\land\; 0 \le r$$

**Step 2 — Loop body.** If $r \ge b$, decrease $r$ by $b$ and increase $q$ by $1$:

$$r \ge b \;\rightarrow\; r := r - b;\; q := q + 1$$

**Step 3 — Termination guard.** Loop while $r \ge b$:

$$\text{DO} \;\; r \ge b \;\rightarrow\; r := r - b;\; q := q + 1 \;\; \text{OD}$$

**Step 4 — Prove the invariant is preserved.**

$$\begin{aligned}
wp(r := r - b;\, q := q + 1, \; P) &\equiv wp(r := r - b,\; wp(q := q + 1, P)) \\
&\equiv wp(r := r - b,\; a = b(q+1) + r \;\land\; 0 \le r) \\
&\equiv a = b(q+1) + (r - b) \;\land\; 0 \le r - b \\
&\equiv a = bq + r \;\land\; b \le r
\end{aligned}$$

Disjoined with the guard $r \ge b$:

$$(r \ge b) \;\land\; (a = bq + r \;\land\; b \le r) \;\equiv\; r \ge b \;\land\; P$$

The invariant is preserved.

**Step 5 — Show termination.** The variant is $v = r$ (a non-negative integer). Each iteration strictly decreases $v$ by $b > 0$. By the well-ordering of $\mathbb{N}$, the loop terminates. Upon termination, the negation of the guard holds: $r < b$. Combined with $P$, this yields $a = bq + r$ and $0 \le r < b$, which is the desired postcondition $Q$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Guarded Command Constructs

| Construct Type | KTU Use Case | Verification Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| `IF` with mutually exclusive guards | Decision tables (e.g., grading systems) | Show that $\bigvee_i G_i \equiv \text{true}$ over the input domain |
| `IF` with non-exclusive guards | Search and scheduling | Show at least one $G_i$ true at every reachable state |
| `DO` with decreasing variant | Counting, summation | Exhibit $v: \text{State} \to \mathbb{N}$ that strictly decreases |
| Nested `IF` inside `DO` | Multi-phase algorithms | Prove variant decreases per outer iteration; inner `IF` case analysis |
| `DO` with skip-style body | Polling/event loops | Variant is bounded by external input; prove boundedness separately |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Valuation Trap:**
> Students often write *deterministic* (ordered) selection, e.g., "evaluate $G_1$, else $G_2$". This is **not** a guarded command — it is a conventional `if-else`. KTU questions specifically test your recognition that **selection is non-deterministic when multiple guards are true**. The marker *will* deduct marks if you omit the non-determinism clause.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Guarded Command Construct

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *evaluation–selection–execution* pipeline of a guarded command construct. This is the canonical KTU-exam block diagram style.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Entry to Construct] --> B[Snapshot Current State]
    B --> C[Evaluate All Guards G1 to Gn]
    C --> D{At Least One Guard True?}
    D -- No --> E[Abort or Skip based on Construct Type]
    D -- Yes --> F[Build Eligible Set E = Si where Gi is true]
    F --> G[Non Deterministic Selection of Ssel from E]
    G --> H[Execute Selected Command Ssel Atomically]
    H --> I{Construct Type?}
    I -- IF Alternative --> J[Transfer Control to Statement After FI]
    I -- DO Repetitive --> K[Re evaluate Guards from Updated State]
    K --> C
    E --> L{Construct Type?}
    L -- IF Alternative --> M[STOP Abort: proof obligation violated]
    L -- DO Repetitive --> N[Transfer Control to Statement After OD]
    J --> O[Exit]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Alternative Construct (`IF`)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Eval
    Eval --> EligibleOne: Exactly One Gi True
    Eval --> EligibleMany: Multiple Gi True
    Eval --> Abort: All Gi False
    EligibleOne --> Execute: Select S_i
    EligibleMany --> Execute: Non Deterministic Pick
    Execute --> [*]: Control Past FI
    Abort --> [*]: STOP Abort
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Repetitive Construct (`DO`)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EvalLoop
    EvalLoop --> Eligible: Some Gi True
    EvalLoop --> Terminate: All Gi False
    Eligible --> Pick: Non Deterministic Selection
    Pick --> ExecuteLoop
    ExecuteLoop --> EvalLoop: Re evaluate
    Terminate --> [*]: Control Past OD
```

## 4.4 Mapping to Conventional Constructs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conventional IF ELSE IF] --> B[Guarded IF Construct]
    C[Conventional WHILE Loop] --> D[Guarded DO Construct]
    B --> E[Non Deterministic Selection]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Formal Correctness by Construction]
    F --> G[Engineering Application in Safety Critical Systems]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in a KTU Exam:**
> When the question says "draw the control-flow diagram", the *state-transition* style (Sections 4.2 and 4.3) is preferred for guarded commands. The *block-flow* style (Section 4.1) is preferred when the question asks to "explain the operational semantics". KTU markers specifically check whether the student has drawn **two arrows back from the body of `DO` to the guard evaluation step** — this is the visual signature of the repetitive construct.

## 4.5 Trace Diagram for the Max Example (Worked Trace)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant State as Program State
    participant Guard as Guard Evaluator
    participant Sel as Non Det Selector
    participant Exec as Executor
    State->>Guard: a = 5, b = 3
    Guard->>Sel: Eligible Set = {S1} since a greater or equal b true
    Sel->>Exec: Pick S1
    Exec->>State: m := 5
    Note over State: Postcondition: m = max(5,3) = 5 satisfied
    State->>Guard: a = 2, b = 7
    Guard->>Sel: Eligible Set = {S2} since b greater a true
    Sel->>Exec: Pick S2
    Exec->>State: m := 7
    Note over State: Postcondition: m = max(2,7) = 7 satisfied
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define a guarded command. Explain, with an example, the meaning of the symbol "▯" as used in guarded command notation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *guarded command* is a syntactic construct of the form $G \rightarrow S$, where $G$ is a Boolean expression called the *guard* and $S$ is a list of statements (the *command*). The command $S$ is eligible for execution only when $G$ evaluates to `true`.

The symbol **▯** is called the *shill*. It is used to **separate two or more alternative guarded commands** within a single `IF` or `DO` construct. It denotes logical disjunction at the construct level: each `$G_i \rightarrow S_i \;\square\; G_j \rightarrow S_j$' represents two *parallel* candidates, either of which may be selected. *Example:* In the construct

$$\text{IF} \;\; x > 0 \rightarrow y := x \;\; \square \;\; x \le 0 \rightarrow y := -x \;\; \text{FI}$$

the **▯** separates the two alternatives for computing the absolute value of $x$. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*State the rule of "non-determinacy" in guarded commands. Why is this property essential for formal program derivation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The *non-determinacy rule* states that when more than one guard in an `IF` or `DO` construct evaluates to `true`, the implementation is permitted to select **any one** of the corresponding commands for execution. The selection is not bound to a particular ordering, and the programmer need not pre-specify which one will be chosen.

This property is essential for formal program derivation because it allows the programmer to specify **what** must be accomplished (the *postcondition*) without prematurely committing to *how* it is accomplished (the *algorithm*). This separation makes the proof of correctness cleaner: at each step, one proves that at least one guarded command refines the specification, and only later is the non-determinism resolved into deterministic code. Non-determinacy also supports the use of *weakest-precondition* reasoning, which assumes the *most favourable* scheduling. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Question Paper Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the syntax and semantics of the **alternative (`IF ... FI`)** and **repetitive (`DO ... OD`)** guarded command constructs with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive a guarded command program to compute the *greatest common divisor* (GCD) of two positive integers $m$ and $n$ using the *repetitive* construct. Prove the correctness of your program using the weakest-precondition method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The **alternative construct** has the form

$$\text{IF} \;\; G_1 \rightarrow S_1 \;\; \square \;\; G_2 \rightarrow S_2 \;\; \square \;\; \dots \;\; \square \;\; G_n \rightarrow S_n \;\; \text{FI}$$

Operationally: (i) the guards are evaluated; (ii) the *eligible set* of commands $\{S_i \mid G_i \equiv \text{true}\}$ is identified; (iii) if the set is non-empty, one command is selected **non-deterministically** and executed; (iv) if the set is empty, the construct *aborts*. Control then passes to the statement following `FI`. (3.5 marks)

*Example (absolute value of x):*

$$\text{IF} \;\; x \ge 0 \rightarrow y := x \;\; \square \;\; x < 0 \rightarrow y := -x \;\; \text{FI}$$

The **repetitive construct** has the form

$$\text{DO} \;\; G_1 \rightarrow S_1 \;\; \square \;\; G_2 \rightarrow S_2 \;\; \square \;\; \dots \;\; \square \;\; G_n \rightarrow S_n \;\; \text{OD}$$

Operationally: the guards are evaluated and an enabled command is selected and executed; the guards are then re-evaluated. The loop terminates when *all* guards are false. (3.5 marks)

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Specification:** Given positive integers $m, n$, compute $g = \gcd(m, n)$.

**Invariant:** $P: g \mid m \;\land\; g \mid n \;\land\; g = \gcd(m, n)$ — but a *constructive* invariant using the *Euclidean algorithm* is:

$$P: m > 0 \;\land\; n > 0 \;\land\; \gcd(\text{old } m, \text{old } n) = \gcd(m, n)$$

**Guard and command design:** The Euclidean algorithm repeatedly subtracts the smaller from the larger. We express this as two alternative commands inside a `DO` loop:

$$\text{DO} \;\; m > n \rightarrow m := m - n \;\; \square \;\; n > m \rightarrow n := n - m \;\; \text{OD}$$
$$\text{IF} \;\; m > n \rightarrow g := m \;\; \square \;\; n \ge m \rightarrow g := n \;\; \text{FI}$$

**Proof of invariant preservation (using weakest preconditions):**

For the first command:

$$\begin{aligned}
wp(m := m - n,\; P) &\equiv (m - n) > 0 \;\land\; n > 0 \;\land\; \gcd(\text{old } m, \text{old } n) = \gcd(m - n, n) \\
&\equiv \gcd(\text{old } m, \text{old } n) = \gcd(m, n) \quad \text{(since } \gcd(m-n, n) = \gcd(m, n)\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

Disjoined with the guard $m > n$, the invariant $P$ is preserved. (3.5 marks for proof)

**Termination:** The variant is $v = m + n$, a non-negative integer. Each iteration strictly decreases $v$ by at least the value of the smaller of $m, n$, which is positive. By the well-ordering of $\mathbb{N}$, the loop terminates. (1.5 marks)

**Postcondition on exit:** Both guards are false, so $m \le n$ and $n \le m$, hence $m = n = g = \gcd(\text{old } m, \text{old } n)$. (2 marks)

### Question B — Option (ii) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** With a clear syntactic and semantic description, discuss Dijkstra's *non-deterministic* alternative command. How does it differ from a conventional `if-else` chain? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a guarded command program to sort two variables $x$ and $y$ such that the postcondition $x \le y$ holds. Verify your program formally using the weakest-precondition calculus. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Syntactic description:** Dijkstra's alternative command has the form

$$\text{IF} \;\; G_1 \rightarrow S_1 \;\; \square \;\; G_2 \rightarrow S_2 \;\; \square \;\; \dots \;\; \square \;\; G_n \rightarrow S_n \;\; \text{FI}$$

**Semantic description:** A guard $G_i$ is *open* if it evaluates to `true`; the corresponding command $S_i$ is then *eligible*. The construct executes *one* of the eligible commands non-deterministically, or aborts if no guard is open. (3.5 marks)

**Comparison with conventional `if-else`:** A conventional `if-else` chain imposes a strict *left-to-right* evaluation order and commits to the *first* matching branch. Dijkstra's construct, by contrast:

- **Does not commit** to a specific order — any eligible command may run.
- **Requires exhaustive coverage** — failing to cover a state causes an abort, surfacing a logical error rather than silently skipping it.
- **Supports formal proof** — the weakest precondition $\bigvee_i (G_i \land wp(S_i, Q))$ cleanly captures "all paths lead to $Q$".

(3.5 marks)

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Specification:** Sort two variables $x, y$ such that postcondition $Q: x \le y$ holds.

**Guarded command program:**

$$\text{IF} \;\; x > y \rightarrow \text{tmp} := x;\; x := y;\; y := \text{tmp} \;\; \square \;\; x \le y \rightarrow \text{skip} \;\; \text{FI}$$

**Weakest-precondition verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
wp(\text{IF}, Q) &\equiv (x > y \;\land\; wp(\text{cmd}_1, Q)) \;\lor\; (x \le y \;\land\; wp(\text{skip}, Q)) \\
&\equiv (x > y \;\land\; wp(\text{cmd}_1, x \le y)) \;\lor\; (x \le y \;\land\; x \le y) \\
&\equiv (x > y \;\land\; y \le x) \;\lor\; (x \le y) \quad \text{(after command 1 executes: } x_{\text{new}} = y_{\text{old}}, y_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{old}}\text{)} \\
&\equiv \text{true}
\end{aligned}$$

(5 marks for the derivation; 2 marks for stating the final postcondition is `true`, i.e., the program is correct for all $x, y$.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on Guarded Commands:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the shilling symbol "▯" with logical AND or sequential composition** — it is the *separator* of alternatives, not a connector. (Deduct 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Forgetting the "abort if no guard true" clause** — guarded commands do *not* silently fall through. (Deduct 2 marks.)
> 3. **Treating `DO` like a conventional `while`** — the variant must be *explicitly exhibited* in 14-mark questions. (Deduct 2 marks if missing.)
> 4. **Omitting the non-determinism clause** — saying "the *first* true guard is chosen" is wrong. (Deduct 1 mark.)
> 5. **Using "=" instead of "→" in the guard** — the arrow is *mandatory* syntax. (Deduct 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 6. **Skipping weakest-precondition steps in 14-mark derivations** — KTU markers award marks for *each substitution step*; do not collapse the algebra. (Deduct up to 3 marks.)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Guarded Commands (Module 2)**
>
> - **Originator:** Edsger W. Dijkstra, 1975. The notation is mathematical, language-independent.
> - **Primitive form:** $G \rightarrow S$ — a guard paired with a command.
> - **Two constructs:** `IF ... FI` (alternative) and `DO ... OD` (repetitive).
> - **Separator symbol:** `▯` (shill) — separates alternative guarded commands.
> - **Alternative construct semantics:** evaluate all guards; if ≥1 is true, pick one command non-deterministically and execute; if none true, *abort*.
> - **Repetitive construct semantics:** evaluate all guards; if ≥1 is true, pick one command non-deterministically and execute, then re-evaluate; if none true, *terminate normally*.
> - **Non-determinism:** when multiple guards are true, *any* eligible command may be selected.
> - **Weakest precondition of `IF`:** $\bigvee_i (G_i \land wp(S_i, Q))$.
> - **Weakest precondition of `DO`:** greatest fixpoint of $X \equiv (\neg G_i \land Q) \lor \bigvee_i (G_i \land wp(S_i, X))$.
> - **Termination proof obligation for `DO`:** exhibit a *well-founded variant* (typically a non-negative integer that strictly decreases per iteration).
> - **Use cases:** safety-critical systems (SPARK/Ada for avionics, EN 50128 for rail), compiler instruction selection, formal specification languages.
> - **Common KTU marks-earning move:** always state the *invariant*, the *variant*, and verify both preservation and termination — this triumvirate guarantees full marks on 14-markers.
> - **Memorise the symbols:** `→` (enables), `▯` (alternative), `FI` (end of IF), `OD` (end of DO). Mixing these up costs marks instantly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Subprograms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Subprograms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subprogram (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *subprogram* is a **named, parameterized, and reusable program unit** that performs a specific, well-defined task. It is invoked (called) by other program units and, upon completion, **returns control** to the point of invocation. In programming language theory, subprograms are classified into two principal categories: **Functions** (which return a value) and **Procedures** (which execute commands without returning a value).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and structured programming paradigms, a subprogram encapsulates a logically independent computation, supporting the engineering principles of **modularity, abstraction, and code reuse**. The two main subprogram types are:

- **Function** → A subprogram that is invoked by name and **returns a single value** to the caller (e.g., `sqrt(x)`, `max(a, b)`).
- **Procedure** → A subprogram that is invoked by name and **does not return a value**, instead producing results through parameters or side effects (e.g., `printDetails()`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Understanding:**
> Think of a subprogram as a **vending machine** in an office cafeteria.
> 1. **You (the caller)** press a button and insert specific inputs (parameters).
> 2. **The machine (the subprogram)** performs an internal operation without exposing its inner workings (abstraction).
> 3. **The output slot** delivers a result (return value) — like a soft drink.
> 4. You walk away with your drink and continue your day (control returns to the caller).
>
> Each press of the button is a **subprogram call**; the recipe inside the machine is the **subprogram body**; the buttons are the **formal parameters**; and the coins you insert are the **actual parameters (arguments)**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Characteristics of Subprograms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Defining Properties of a Subprogram:**
> 1. **Single Entry Point** — Execution of a subprogram always begins at the first executable statement; there is exactly one entry.
> 2. **Single Exit Point (in well-structured designs)** — Control normally returns to the caller at exactly one point (except for abnormal termination).
> 3. **Parameters and Local Variables** — A subprogram has its own formal parameter list and locally declared variables with limited lifetime.
> 4. **Re-entrancy and Recursion Support** — A correctly designed subprogram can be called from within itself or from overlapping code paths.
> 5. **Black-Box Behaviour** — The caller need not know the implementation; only the **interface (signature)** is required.

## 1.4 Why Subprograms Matter — Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications of Subprograms:**
> - **Embedded Systems (e.g., KTU-aligned IoT labs):** Modular firmware uses subprograms for sensor polling, actuator control, and state machines.
> - **Compiler Construction:** Lexical analyzers and parsers are built as cooperating subprograms.
> - **Numerical Methods:** Functions like `gaussElimination()`, `newtonRaphson()` isolate complex algorithms.
> - **Operating Systems:** System calls (`fork()`, `exec()`) are essentially subprograms exposed to user space.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Subprogram — The General Form

A subprogram in a typical high-level language (C, C++, Java, Python, Ada) consists of **two distinct components**:

| Component | Description | Visibility |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Subprogram Declaration (Prototype)** | Specifies the subprogram's name, return type, and parameter list. Does NOT allocate memory for code. | External (visible to all units) |
| **Subprogram Definition (Body)** | Contains the actual implementation — local variable declarations and executable statements. | Definition is unique (one per program unit) |

### 2.1.1 Subprogram Call Mechanism

The act of invoking a subprogram is called a **subprogram call** (or *function invocation*). The call involves:

1. **Binding of actual parameters to formal parameters** (parameter passing).
2. **Saving the execution context** of the caller (return address, register state).
3. **Transfer of control** to the subprogram's entry point.
4. **Execution of the body** in a newly created activation environment.
5. **Return of a value** (for functions) and **restoration of caller's context**.

## 2.2 Parameter Passing Mechanisms — The Heart of Subprograms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:**
> Parameter passing is the most frequently examined concept in Module 3. The KTU board typically asks students to *trace* or *compare* mechanisms using a small program and a parameter table.

The five canonical parameter passing models are summarized in the table below. The choice of mechanism affects whether the **caller's actual argument is modified** and whether **aliases** are created.

| Mechanism | Direction of Data Flow | Caller's Variable Modified? | Alias Created? | Typical Languages |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Pass-by-Value (in mode)** | Caller → Subprogram | No | No | C, Java, Python (for immutable args) |
| **Pass-by-Reference (inout mode)** | Caller ↔ Subprogram | Yes | Yes | FORTRAN, Pascal `var`, C++ `&` |
| **Pass-by-Value-Result (inout mode, copy-in/copy-out)** | Caller ↔ Subprogram (at call and at return) | Yes | No (during execution) | Ada `in out`, some FORTRAN compilers |
| **Pass-by-Name (macro-like textual substitution)** | Substituted textually at each use | Yes | Yes (conceptually) | Algol 60, C++ template tricks |
| **Pass-by-Result (out mode)** | Subprogram → Caller (at return only) | Yes (output) | No | Ada `out` parameter |

### 2.2.1 Formal vs. Actual Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Distinction (often tested):**
> - **Formal Parameter** — The variable name declared in the subprogram's header (e.g., `x` in `void foo(int x)`). It acts as a *placeholder*.
> - **Actual Parameter (Argument)** — The expression or variable supplied by the caller at the call site (e.g., `5` or `count` in `foo(5)`, `foo(count)`). It is the *concrete value or location*.

The **binding** between these two is the act of association, performed either **positionally** (by order) or **by keyword** (Ada, Python support this).

## 2.3 Scope, Lifetime, and Visibility Rules

These three properties determine which variables a subprogram can access and for how long:

| Property | Definition | Standard Mechanism |
|----------|------------|--------------------|
| **Scope (Visibility)** | The textual region of the program where a name is accessible. | Static scope (lexical) or Dynamic scope |
| **Lifetime (Extent)** | The duration during execution for which storage exists. | Static allocation, Stack-dynamic, Heap-dynamic |
| **Binding Time** | The phase at which a name is bound to a storage location. | Compile time vs. Run time |

### 2.3.1 Local vs. Global Variables

- **Local Variable** — Declared *inside* the subprogram body. Its scope is confined to that subprogram, and its lifetime spans the subprogram's execution. Allocated on the **stack frame** of the subprogram.
- **Global (Non-Local) Variable** — Declared outside all subprograms. Visible to all subprograms in the file (unless shadowed). Lifetime is the entire program execution. Allocated in the **static data segment**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Quick-Reference Table — Memory & Parameter Costs:**

| Quantity / Concept | Formula or Rule | Unit / Notes |
|--------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Stack frame size of a subprogram | $S_{frame} = S_{params} + S_{locals} + S_{saved} + S_{ret}$ | Bytes |
| Parameter passing cost (by value) | $C_{val} = 2 \cdot T_{copy} + 0$ | Time units (copy in, no copy back) |
| Parameter passing cost (by reference) | $C_{ref} = T_{copy(addr)} + 0$ | Cheaper for large structures |
| Parameter passing cost (by value-result) | $C_{vr} = 2 \cdot T_{copy} + T_{copy\_out}$ | Higher cost, no aliasing during call |
| Subprogram nesting depth bound | $D \le \frac{S_{stack}}{S_{frame}}$ | $S_{stack}$ = total stack size |
| Recursion memory overhead | $M_{recur} = N \cdot S_{frame}$ | $N$ = recursion depth |
| Positional argument binding count | $\frac{n!}{(n-k)!}$ | For $k$-out-of-$n$ reordering |
| Activation record return address offset | $\text{offset}_{RA} = S_{params} + S_{locals} + S_{saved}$ | Bytes from frame base |

## 2.5 Activation Record (Stack Frame) — The Heart of Subprogram Execution

When a subprogram is called, the runtime system constructs an **Activation Record** (also called a *stack frame*) on the call stack. The classical layout (left to right represents low-to-high memory addresses) is:

$$\boxed{\text{Activation Record} = \big[\, RA \;\vert\; DYN\_LINK \;\vert\; STAT\_LINK \;\vert\; Saved\_Registers \;\vert\; Local\_Vars \;\vert\; Parameters \,\big]}$$

Where:
- $RA$ = Return address (where to resume the caller).
- $DYN\_LINK$ = Pointer to the caller's activation record (dynamic link, supports dynamic scope).
- $STAT\_LINK$ = Pointer to the static parent (supports lexical scope, used by closures).
- $Saved\_Registers$ = CPU state preserved across the call.
- $Local\_Vars$ = Local variables of the subprogram.
- $Parameters$ = Space for the formal parameters (sometimes at the top, depending on calling convention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The activation record is the *physical embodiment* of subprogram abstraction on modern hardware. Languages like C use a fixed-size frame; languages with closures (Python, JavaScript) add a heap-allocated environment to support **first-class functions**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Traces & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Positional vs. Keyword Argument Binding

Let $n$ denote the number of formal parameters of a subprogram. We wish to derive the number of **distinct valid binding mappings** from actual arguments to formal parameters.

### 3.1.1 Positional Binding

In positional binding, the $i$-th actual argument is bound to the $i$-th formal parameter. Hence there is exactly **one** mapping for each call, but the *number of possible orderings* of $n$ distinct arguments into $n$ positions is given by the permutation count:

$$
N_{positional} = n!
$$

For $n$ formal parameters with $k$ arguments reordered (where $k \le n$):

$$
N_{positional}(n, k) = \frac{n!}{(n - k)!}
$$

**Derivation steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{The first argument can occupy any of the } n \text{ formal slots.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{The second argument has } (n-1) \text{ remaining choices.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Continuing for } k \text{ arguments, multiply:} \\
N & = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \ldots \times (n-k+1) \\
N & = \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} (n - i) \\
N & = \frac{n!}{(n - k)!}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Keyword (Named) Binding

In keyword binding (e.g., `foo(name='Alice', age=21)` in Python or `Foo(Age => 21, Name => "Alice")` in Ada), each actual argument carries an explicit name. The total number of distinct bindings is **2 to the power of $n$**, because each formal parameter is either supplied or omitted (assuming all are optional):

$$
N_{keyword} = 2^{n}
$$

**Derivation (using the sum rule of combinatorics):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Each of } n \text{ parameters can be either present (1) or absent (0).} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{By the multiplication rule, total combinations} = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdots 2 \; (n \text{ times}) \\
N & = 2^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Example Computation

Suppose $n = 3$ formal parameters $(a, b, c)$:
- Positional: $N = 3! = 6$ valid orderings.
- Keyword: $N = 2^{3} = 8$ possible supplied subsets (including the empty call).

## 3.2 Worked Trace: Pass-by-Value vs. Pass-by-Reference

Consider the following C-like pseudocode. We trace the values of `a` and `b` in `main` after calling `swap(a, b)`.

**Program Listing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{void swap (int x, int y) } \{ \\
& \quad \text{int temp = x;} \\
& \quad \text{x = y;} \\
& \quad \text{y = temp;} \\
& \text{\}} \\
& \\
& \text{int main()} \{ \\
& \quad \text{int a = 5, b = 10;} \\
& \quad \text{swap(a, b);} \\
& \quad \text{printf("a = %d, b = %d", a, b);} \\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.1 Case 1 — Pass-by-Value

**Initial state:** `main` has `a = 5, b = 10`. The call `swap(a, b)` causes a **copy** of `a` and `b` to be passed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before call:} \quad & a = 5, \; b = 10 \\
\text{Copy into formal parameters:} \quad & x = 5, \; y = 10 \\
\text{After body of swap executes:} \quad & x = 10, \; y = 5 \\
\text{After return to main:} \quad & a = 5, \; b = 10 \quad (\textbf{unchanged, only local copies were swapped})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Output:** `a = 5, b = 10` — the swap *appears* to fail from the caller's perspective.

### 3.2.2 Case 2 — Pass-by-Reference

In C++ syntax, declare `void swap(int &x, int &y)`. The formal parameters `x` and `y` are now **aliases** (references) to the caller's `a` and `b`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before call:} \quad & a = 5, \; b = 10 \\
\text{Aliases created:} \quad & x \to a, \; y \to b \\
\text{After body of swap executes:} \quad & a = 10, \; b = 5 \\
\text{After return to main:} \quad & a = 10, \; b = 5 \quad (\textbf{swapped successfully})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Analytical Matrix of Side Effects

| Mechanism | `a` after swap | `b` after swap | Alias? | Safety |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|--------|--------|
| Pass-by-Value | **5** | **10** | No | Safe (no side effects) |
| Pass-by-Reference | **10** | **5** | Yes | Risk of unintended modification |
| Pass-by-Value-Result | **10** | **5** | No (during call) | Safe, but subtle |
| Pass-by-Name | **10** | **5** | Yes (textual) | Risky, hard to optimize |

## 3.3 Derivation: Stack Frame Memory Layout for Nested Calls

Consider three nested subprogram calls: `main()` → `level1()` → `level2()`. We derive the **dynamic chain length** and **static chain length** for lexical scoping.

### 3.3.1 Definitions

- **Dynamic Chain (Call Stack)**: A sequence of activation records linked in the order of *execution-time* calls. Length = current call depth.
- **Static Chain (Lexical Linkage)**: A sequence of activation records linked according to the *textual nesting* of the source program. Used to resolve non-local variable references under static scoping.

### 3.3.2 Length Computation

If the subprogram $P$ is nested inside $Q$, and $Q$ is nested inside $R$, the **static depth** of $P$ is 2 (counting from outermost $R$ as 0). When $P$ is executing, the static chain length to access a name declared in $R$ is:

$$
L_{static} = d_P - d_{R} = 2 - 0 = 2
$$

Where $d_X$ denotes the static nesting depth of subprogram $X$.

For the dynamic chain at the same moment:

$$
L_{dynamic} = \text{current call depth} = 2
$$

**The two values may differ** if a subprogram is called from a non-statically-enclosing scope. This is precisely the case where **dynamic scope** (e.g., early Lisp, shell scripts) gives different answers from **static (lexical) scope** (e.g., modern Python, Java, C).

### 3.3.3 Cost of Non-Local Access

The cost of accessing a non-local variable $V$ declared at static depth $d_V$, from a subprogram executing at static depth $d_P$ ($d_P > d_V$):

$$
T_{access} = (d_P - d_V) \cdot T_{deref} + T_{fetch}
$$

Where $T_{deref}$ is the cost of one pointer dereference (following one static link) and $T_{fetch}$ is the cost of reading the variable's value from the activation record.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** Deeply nested procedures (a hallmark of legacy Pascal code) can suffer from non-local access latency. This is why modern languages (Java, Go) prefer flat or class-based scoping.

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Maximum Recursion Depth

Suppose the call stack is **$1$ MB = $1024 \times 1024$ bytes** and each activation record occupies **$128$ bytes**. Compute the maximum recursion depth before a stack overflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{max} & = \left\lfloor \frac{S_{stack}}{S_{frame}} \right\rfloor \\
D_{max} & = \left\lfloor \frac{1048576 \; \text{bytes}}{128 \; \text{bytes}} \right\rfloor \\
D_{max} & = 8192 \; \text{calls}
\end{aligned}
$$

After 8192 nested calls, the 8193rd call would attempt to push a frame beyond the stack's mapped region, triggering a **segmentation fault** (Unix) or a **StackOverflowError** (JVM).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Subprogram Call Flow (Activation Record Lifecycle)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete lifecycle of a subprogram call, from invocation to return.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Caller executes subprogram call statement"] --> B["Evaluate actual arguments"]
    B --> C["Bind actuals to formals per parameter passing model"]
    C --> D["Push return address onto runtime call stack"]
    D --> E["Allocate activation record on stack"]
    E --> F["Save caller register state into activation record"]
    F --> G["Transfer control to subprogram entry point"]
    G --> H["Execute subprogram body line by line"]
    H --> I{"Return statement encountered?"}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J["Place return value in agreed register or memory"]
    J --> K["Restore caller register state from activation record"]
    K --> L["Pop activation record from stack"]
    L --> M["Transfer control back to return address"]
    M --> N["Caller resumes with return value available"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **diamond node** at "Return statement encountered?" represents a decision branch; if no return is reached, the loop continues executing the body.
> - The **rectangular nodes** represent state transitions in the runtime system.
> - The **final node** indicates that the caller can now use the return value (e.g., assign it: `result = foo();`).

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Parameter Passing Mechanism Comparison

This Mermaid block diagram contrasts the three most-tested parameter passing mechanisms: pass-by-value, pass-by-reference, and pass-by-value-result.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CallerScope["CALLER MEMORY SPACE"]
        A["Actual Argument: x = 5"]
    end
    subgraph SubprogScope["SUBPROGRAM ACTIVATION RECORD"]
        B["Formal Parameter: p"]
    end
    A -- "Pass-by-Value: copy of 5 sent" --> B
    B -- "Modify p to 10" --> A
    A -- "Pass-by-Reference: address of x sent" --> B
    B -- "Indirectly modifies x to 10" --> A
    A -- "Pass-by-Value-Result: copy in, then copy out at return" --> B
    B -- "Local p modified; x updated only at return" --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Note:** Each sub-block represents a distinct **memory region** in the program. The arrows indicate the *direction* of data movement and *when* it occurs (call time vs. return time).

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Activation Record Stack Growth (Nested Calls)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top["Top of Stack: Newest Activation Record"]
    Mid1["Middle Frame: Parent Subprogram"]
    Mid2["Lower Frame: Caller of Parent"]
    Bottom["Bottom of Stack: Main Activation Record"]
    Top --> Mid1
    Mid1 --> Mid2
    Mid2 --> Bottom
    SP["Stack Pointer (SP) moves down as frames are pushed"]
    SP -.-> Top
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> The **stack pointer (SP)** is a CPU register that always points to the topmost (most recent) activation record. As subprograms are called, SP moves *toward lower addresses* (assuming a downward-growing stack, the convention on x86 and ARM). When a subprogram returns, the frame is popped, and SP moves *back up*.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Static vs. Dynamic Scope Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Reference to variable 'count' inside subprogram P"]
    X --> Q{"Which scope rule applies?"}
    Q -- "Static (Lexical) Scope --> S1["Search outward through lexical ancestors of P"]
    Q -- "Dynamic Scope --> S2["Search through runtime call chain: caller of P, then its caller, etc."]
    S1 --> R1["Found in enclosing block of P (compile-time decision)"]
    S2 --> R2["Found in most recent activation record on call stack (run-time decision)"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:**
> Students often confuse "static scope" with "static storage." These are **orthogonal** concepts. A variable can have static storage (lifetime = entire program) yet be accessible only through dynamic scope (e.g., certain Fortran COMMON blocks accessed via the call stack).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024, Model Question]**

### **Q1.** Differentiate between *formal* and *actual* parameters with an example. Why is the distinction important in subprogram design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Formal parameters** are the variable names listed in the *header* of a subprogram definition. They act as placeholders.
> **Example:** `int power(int base, int exp)` — here `base` and `exp` are formal parameters.
>
> **Actual parameters** (or *arguments*) are the specific values or expressions supplied by the caller at the *call site*.
> **Example:** `power(2, 10)` — here `2` and `10` are actual parameters.
>
> **Importance:** The distinction decouples the subprogram's *interface* (which uses formals) from its *usage* (which uses actuals), enabling **reusability** and **abstraction**. The same subprogram can be called with different actuals without rewriting the body. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Model Question]**

### **Q2.** What is an *activation record*? List any four components stored in it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **activation record** (or *stack frame*) is a data structure created on the runtime call stack each time a subprogram is invoked. It stores all information required for the subprogram's execution and the eventual restoration of the caller's state.

**Four components (1/2 mark each):**

1. **Return Address (RA)** — the instruction in the caller to resume at.
2. **Dynamic Link** — pointer to the caller's activation record.
3. **Saved Registers** — CPU state preserved across the call.
4. **Local Variables** — storage for variables declared inside the subprogram.

(Any four valid components accepted. Total: 3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** OR-type choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **five different parameter passing mechanisms** in detail with suitable examples. Compare them based on (i) direction of data flow, (ii) whether the caller's variable is modified, and (iii) whether aliasing occurs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Pass-by-Value:** A copy of the actual argument's value is passed to the subprogram. The subprogram's formal parameter is a *local variable* initialized to this copy. The caller's variable is **not modified**. No aliasing.
> *Example (C):* `void foo(int x) { x = 10; }` — calling `foo(a)` does not change `a` in the caller.

> **Pass-by-Reference:** The *address* of the actual argument is passed. The formal parameter becomes an *alias* of the caller's variable. Modifications inside the subprogram directly affect the caller. Aliasing occurs.
> *Example (C++):* `void foo(int &x) { x = 10; }` — calling `foo(a)` sets `a = 10` in the caller.

> **Pass-by-Value-Result (Copy-in / Copy-out):** A copy is made at call time (like pass-by-value), the subprogram operates on the local copy, and at return time, the final value of the formal is copied back to the actual argument. The caller's variable **is modified**, but **no aliasing** exists during execution.
> *Example (Ada):* `procedure Foo(X : in out Integer)`.

> **Pass-by-Name:** The actual argument is *textually substituted* into the subprogram body wherever the formal parameter appears. This is essentially a macro-style expansion. Side effects on the actual argument can be surprising.
> *Example:* Algol 60 used this mechanism.

> **Pass-by-Result:** Unlike pass-by-value, no initial value is copied in. The formal parameter acts as a local variable; at return, its value is copied *out* to the caller's variable. Used in Ada `out` mode.

**Comparison Table (1.5 marks):**

| Mechanism | Data Flow Direction | Caller Modified? | Alias? |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|---------|
| Pass-by-Value | In | No | No |
| Pass-by-Reference | In / Out | Yes | Yes |
| Pass-by-Value-Result | In / Out | Yes (at return) | No (during call) |
| Pass-by-Name | In / Out (textual) | Yes | Yes (conceptual) |
| Pass-by-Result | Out | Yes (output only) | No |

(7 marks: 1 mark per mechanism × 5 = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for the comparison table)

---

**Q. (b)** What is an **activation record**? Draw and explain the structure of a typical activation record for a subprogram. How are local variables and parameters allocated in the activation record? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> An **activation record** is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the **call stack** when a subprogram is invoked. It holds all the runtime data needed to execute the subprogram and to restore the caller's state upon return.

> **Structure of a Typical Activation Record:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top (high address)} \quad & \rightarrow \text{Parameters} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Return Address (RA)} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Dynamic Link (old SP)} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Static Link (lexical parent pointer)} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Saved Registers} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Local Variables} \\
\text{Bottom (low address)} \quad & \rightarrow \text{Temporaries / Spill Space}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Allocation of Local Variables:** The compiler reserves a fixed offset for each local variable within the activation record, computed at compile time. At runtime, the stack pointer is decremented by the frame's total size, effectively carving out space for these locals.

> **Allocation of Parameters:** Parameters are typically placed at *fixed offsets from the top* of the frame. This is so that the caller, who writes the actual arguments at known offsets before the call, can be sure the callee will find them at the expected locations (calling convention).

> **Key Operations:**
> - **Prologue** (entry): push frame, save registers, allocate locals.
> - **Epilogue** (exit): restore registers, pop frame, return to RA.

(7 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks structure diagram/description + 2 marks allocation explanation + 2 marks prologue/epilogue)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** Compare **static (lexical) scoping** and **dynamic scoping** with a suitable example. Explain which one is used in modern programming languages and why. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Static (Lexical) Scoping:** The scope of a variable is determined by the *textual structure* of the program. A name refers to the variable declared in the *nearest enclosing block* in the source code, regardless of the runtime call chain.

> **Dynamic Scoping:** The scope of a variable is determined by the *runtime call chain*. A name refers to the most recent binding of that name on the call stack, regardless of textual nesting.

> **Example (Pascal-like pseudocode):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{var x : Integer;} \\
& \text{procedure A;} \\
& \quad \text{var x : Integer;} \\
& \quad \text{begin x := 1; B; end;} \\
& \text{procedure B;} \\
& \quad \text{begin print(x); end;} \\
& \text{begin x := 0; A; end.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> - Under **static scoping**: `B` prints `1` (it uses the `x` declared in `A`, the nearest enclosing scope in the source).
> - Under **dynamic scoping**: `B` prints `0` (it uses the most recent `x` on the call stack, which is the global `x = 0` since `A`'s local `x` has gone out of scope by the time `B` is called... but in many classic textbook examples, dynamic scoping would resolve to `1` if `A`'s frame is still active).

> **Modern Language Choice:** Static scoping is used in **almost all modern languages** (C, C++, Java, Python, JavaScript, Rust, Go). Reasons:
> 1. **Predictability** — A programmer can determine variable bindings by reading the code, without executing it.
> 2. **Efficiency** — The compiler can resolve non-local references at *compile time* using static links, avoiding expensive stack searches.
> 3. **Modularity** — Subprograms can be reasoned about in isolation.

(7 marks: 2 marks definitions + 2 marks example + 3 marks modern language reasoning)

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the concept of **subprogram nesting** and **recursion**. Write a recursive subprogram to compute the factorial of a number and trace its execution for `factorial(4)`, showing the activation record stack at each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Subprogram Nesting:** When a subprogram is declared *inside* another subprogram, it is said to be *nested*. The inner subprogram has access to the local variables of its enclosing subprogram (under static scoping). Languages like Pascal, Ada, and Python support nested subprograms; C and Java do not (Java uses inner classes for similar effect).

> **Recursion:** A subprogram is *recursive* if it calls itself, either directly (`P` calls `P`) or indirectly (`P` calls `Q` which calls `P`). Each recursive call creates a *new activation record*, with its own copy of local variables.

> **Recursive Code for Factorial:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{int factorial(int n) \{ } \\
& \quad \text{if (n <= 1) return 1;} \\
& \quad \text{else return n * factorial(n - 1);} \\
& \text{\}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Trace for `factorial(4)`:**

| Call # | Argument `n` | Action | Returns To | Stack Depth |
|--------|--------------|--------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | 4 | Calls `factorial(3)` | — | 1 |
| 2 | 3 | Calls `factorial(2)` | Call 1 | 2 |
| 3 | 2 | Calls `factorial(1)` | Call 2 | 3 |
| 4 | 1 | Base case, returns 1 | Call 3 | 4 → 3 |
| 3 | 2 | Returns $2 \times 1 = 2$ | Call 2 | 3 → 2 |
| 2 | 3 | Returns $3 \times 2 = 6$ | Call 1 | 2 → 1 |
| 1 | 4 | Returns $4 \times 6 = 24$ | `main` | 1 → 0 |

> **Final Result:** `factorial(4) = 24`.

(7 marks: 1 mark nesting definition + 1 mark recursion definition + 2 marks code + 3 marks trace table)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Subprogram Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing formal and actual parameters** — A single mark is deducted each time the terms are swapped in the answer. Always use the *exact* textbook terminology.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention aliasing** in parameter passing comparisons — The KTU answer key *always* awards 1 mark for the aliasing column in the comparison table.
> 3. **Omitting the base case** in recursive function traces — The examiner will deduct 1 mark and mark the trace as "incomplete" if the base case is not shown.
> 4. **Static vs. dynamic scope confusion** — Many students write "static" when they mean "stack-allocated" or "compile-time bound." The correct term for *compile-time textual scope* is **lexical** (or **static**) scoping; for *run-time call-stack-based scope* it is **dynamic** scoping.
> 5. **Activation record components** — Do not list the *program counter* and *return address* as the same thing; some boards distinguish them.
> 6. **Missing arrow directions** in parameter passing diagrams — The KTU paper often asks for a "direction of data flow" diagram; be sure to mark *in-mode* and *out-mode* arrows clearly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fundamentals of Subprograms:**
>
> - [x] A **subprogram** is a named, parameterized, reusable program unit; it has a single entry and (normally) a single exit.
> - [x] **Function** returns a value; **procedure** does not.
> - [x] **Formal parameters** appear in the definition header; **actual parameters** are passed at the call site.
> - [x] **Five parameter passing mechanisms:** pass-by-value, pass-by-reference, pass-by-value-result, pass-by-name, pass-by-result.
> - [x] Pass-by-**value**: no side effects, no aliasing, lower cost for small types.
> - [x] Pass-by-**reference**: side effects, aliasing, cheaper for large structures.
> - [x] Pass-by-**value-result**: side effects, no aliasing *during* the call (but at return).
> - [x] **Activation record** is allocated on the call stack at each subprogram invocation.
> - [x] Key activation record fields: **RA, Dynamic Link, Static Link, Saved Registers, Locals, Parameters**.
> - [x] **Static (lexical) scope** uses textual nesting; **dynamic scope** uses the runtime call chain.
> - [x] Modern languages (C, C++, Java, Python) use **static scoping** for predictability and efficiency.
> - [x] **Recursion** is supported by allocating a new activation record for each call; the stack grows until the base case unwinds.
> - [x] Maximum recursion depth: $D_{max} = \lfloor S_{stack} / S_{frame} \rfloor$.
> - [x] **Local variables** are stack-dynamic; **global variables** are static (lifetime = entire program).
> - [x] Always label parameter passing diagrams with **direction of data flow** and indicate **aliasing**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues for Subprograms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Issues for Subprograms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Issues for Subprograms** refer to the fundamental questions a programming language designer must resolve when defining how subprograms (functions/procedures) are declared, invoked, parameterized, scoped, and linked at both **compile-time** and **run-time**. According to KTU's *Advanced Computer Programming Techniques* syllabus (PEBMT746, Module 3), these issues dictate the semantics, efficiency, and expressiveness of modular code in languages ranging from C and Pascal to Ada, Java, and C#.

The principal design issues that any language must address are:

1. **What is the referencing environment of a subprogram (local, nonlocal, global)?**
2. **How are parameters passed between caller and callee?**
3. **Can subprograms themselves be passed as parameters?**
4. **Can subprograms be overloaded?**
5. **Can subprograms be generic (parametrically polymorphic)?**
6. **Should subprograms be allowed to be nested, and how deeply?**
7. **Can subprograms be called indirectly through references?**
8. **Should subprograms support separate and independent compilation?**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Protocol 🍳**

Think of your program as a **large restaurant kitchen**:

- **Subprograms** = Individual chefs (each specializes in one dish).
- **Calling a subprogram** = Head chef yells "Make the soup!" to a junior chef.
- **Parameters** = The recipe card and ingredients handed to the junior chef.
- **Local referencing environment** = The junior chef's own cutting board, knives, and pots — private to him.
- **Nonlocal environment** = Shared pantry shelves he can also reach.
- **Pass-by-value vs pass-by-reference** = Either handing the chef a *photocopy* of the recipe (he can scribble on it freely) or handing him the *original* (any change affects the head chef's copy).
- **Overloading** = One chef named "Ali" who can cook both Biryani *and* Pasta — same name, different "ingredients" determine which dish.
- **Generic subprograms** = A chef who can cook *any* curry as long as you specify the main vegetable.
- **Closures** = A chef who carries his private spice box even when sent to another kitchen.

> **Standard Metrics (KTU frequently tests these terms):**
> - **Static Depth** of a subprogram = lexical nesting level (compile-time)
> - **Dynamic Depth** = number of active subprogram calls at runtime
> - **Chain Offset / Static Link** distance = fixed compile-time integer

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Foundational Subprogram Design Issues

### Issue 1 — Local Referencing Environment
When a subprogram is called, it needs access to its **own locals** (the *local environment*) and possibly **nonlocal names** (the *nonlocal referencing environment*). The two questions are:

- What is the boundary between *local* and *nonlocal*?
- How is a nonlocal name *located* at runtime?

The classic solution (used by **Algol 60**, **Pascal**, and explained by **Ada**) is the **Static Link (Access Link)** approach. Each activation record carries a pointer to the activation record of its **static parent**.

$$\text{Static Depth of Subprogram} = \text{Number of lexically enclosing scopes}$$

To access a variable at static depth $d$ from a subprogram at static depth $n$, follow the static-link chain $(n - d)$ times.

### Issue 2 — Parameter Passing Methods
There are **five** classical semantic models (Sebesta's taxonomy, which KTU follows):

| # | Method | Direction | Copy Made? | Alias Possible? | KTU Frequency |
|---|--------|-----------|------------|-----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Pass by Value** | IN | Yes (on call) | No | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | **Pass by Result** | OUT | Yes (on return) | No | ★★★ |
| 3 | **Pass by Value-Result** | IN-OUT | Yes (both) | No | ★★★ |
| 4 | **Pass by Reference** | IN-OUT | No (address) | **Yes** | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | **Pass by Name** | IN-OUT | None (textual substitution) | **Yes** | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliases** occur when the same memory cell can be accessed by *two different names* (e.g., one parameter passed by reference and another parameter pointing to the same address). Aliases are the #1 source of obscure bugs in pass-by-reference languages.

### Issue 3 — Parameters that are Subprograms
If a subprogram `P` is passed as a parameter to `Q`, then `Q` can call `P`. The question is: **what referencing environment does P use when called from Q?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Closures** solve this by bundling the subprogram code with its referencing environment into a single object — a technique foundational to **functional languages** (Lisp, Haskell) and modern **lambda expressions** (C++, Java, Python, C#).

### Issue 4 — Overloaded Subprograms
**Ad-hoc polymorphism.** The compiler differentiates subprograms with the same name by examining the **type signature** of the parameters. The selection algorithm uses:
- Number of parameters
- Type of each parameter
- Order of parameter types

### Issue 5 — Generic (Polymorphic) Subprograms
**Parametric polymorphism.** A single subprogram definition operates on values of *any* type. C++ templates, Java generics, and C# generics all implement this. KTU typically asks: *"How does a generic differ from an overloaded subprogram?"*
