| Feature | Overloaded | Generic |
|---|---|---|
| Distinct copies of code | **Yes** (one per signature) | **No** (one parametric version) |
| Type checking | At call site | At instantiation |
| Code bloat | High | Low to moderate |
| Compile-time mechanism | Name mangling | Template expansion / type erasure |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every** definitional formula, semantic rule, and static-link arithmetic that has appeared in past KTU End-Semester Examinations for this module.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|
| Static Depth | $D_s = \text{nesting depth of enclosing scopes}$ | Integer (compile-time) |
| Dynamic Depth | $D_d = \text{calls currently on the stack}$ | Integer (run-time) |
| Chain Offset (static-link distance) | $\Delta = D_s(\text{caller}) - D_s(\text{target})$ | Integer (hops) |
| Subprogram overhead (call cost) | $T_{call} = T_{push} + T_{link} + T_{exec} + T_{return}$ | Time units |
| Pass-by-value memory cost | $C_v = 2 \cdot \text{size}(x)$ | Bytes (input + local copy) |
| Pass-by-reference memory cost | $C_r = \text{size}(\text{pointer}) = 8 \text{ bytes (64-bit)}$ | Bytes |
| Pass-by-name evaluation | **Thunk** = zero-argument procedure created at call site | Code object |
| Closure size | $S_{closure} = S_{code} + S_{environment}$ | Bytes |
| Static link (semantics) | points to activation record of **lexical parent** | Pointer |
| Dynamic link (semantics) | points to activation record of **caller** | Pointer |
| Activation record fields | Return address, parameters, locals, **dynamic link**, **static link** | Mixed |

> **Real-World Utility:** Static links enable fast lexical scoping in compilers for Pascal, Ada, and ML. Closures power JavaScript, Python lambdas, and React hooks. Overloading and generics together form the backbone of reusable standard libraries (e.g., `std::vector<T>` in C++).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Locating a Nonlocal Name Using the Static Link Chain

**Problem Setup:** A subprogram `A` at static depth $D_s = 3$ needs to access variable `X` declared at static depth $D_s = 1$. How many static-link hops are required, and what is the algorithm?

### Step 1 — Identify the static depth of the referencing subprogram
$$D_s(\text{caller}) = 3$$

### Step 2 — Identify the static depth of the variable's declaration
$$D_s(\text{target}) = 1$$

### Step 3 — Compute the chain offset (number of static-link traversals)
$$\Delta = D_s(\text{caller}) - D_s(\text{target}) = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ hops}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the traversal algorithm

\begin{aligned}
\text{current} &= \text{activation record of A} \\
\text{step 1:} \quad \text{current} &\leftarrow \text{static\_link(current)} \quad (D_s = 2) \\
\text{step 2:} \quad \text{current} &\leftarrow \text{static\_link(current)} \quad (D_s = 1) \\
\text{step 3:} \quad \text{offset within current} &= \text{compile-time-constant for X} \\
\text{final:} \quad \text{address of X} &= \text{base(current)} + \text{offset}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> The **offset** within the activation record is determined at **compile time** because the compiler knows the layout of the enclosing scope. Only the **base** requires runtime chain traversal. This is why lexical scoping is fast.

### Step 5 — Complexity Analysis
The worst-case access time for a name at depth 0 (global) is:
$$T_{access} = O(D_s(\text{caller})) = O(\text{nesting depth})$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why some languages (e.g., early Fortran) used **dynamic scoping** — they avoided static-link traversal entirely by searching the dynamic call stack, but at the cost of correctness and clarity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Parameter Passing Semantics: Step-by-Step Model

Consider the following C-like fragment used to illustrate pass mechanisms:

```c
void swap(int a, int b) {
    int t = a;
    a = b;
    b = t;
}
int main() {
    int x = 5, y = 10;
    swap(x, y);
    // What are x and y?
}
```

### Case A — Pass by Value (C, Java primitives)

\begin{aligned}
\text{On call:} \quad a &\leftarrow \text{copy}(x) = 5 \\
b &\leftarrow \text{copy}(y) = 10 \\
\text{During swap:} \quad a &= 10, \quad b = 5 \\
\text{On return:} \quad x &= 5, \quad y = 10 \quad \text{(unchanged)}
\end{aligned}

### Case B — Pass by Reference (Pascal `var`, C++ `int&`)

\begin{aligned}
\text{On call:} \quad a &\leftarrow \text{address}(x) \\
b &\leftarrow \text{address}(y) \\
\text{During swap:} \quad \text{write}(a) &\rightarrow \text{write}(x) = 10 \\
\text{write}(b) &\rightarrow \text{write}(y) = 5 \\
\text{On return:} \quad x &= 10, \quad y = 5 \quad \text{(swapped)}
\end{aligned}

### Case C — Pass by Value-Result (Ada `in out`)

\begin{aligned}
\text{On call:} \quad a &\leftarrow \text{copy}(x) = 5, \quad b \leftarrow \text{copy}(y) = 10 \\
\text{After body:} \quad a &= 10, \quad b = 5 \\
\text{On return:} \quad x &\leftarrow a = 10, \quad y \leftarrow b = 5
\end{aligned}

### Case D — Pass by Name (Algol 60)

The actual text of `x` and `y` is *substituted* textually into the body of `swap` before execution:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Body becomes:} \quad \text{int } t &= x; \\
x &= y; \\
y &= t;
\end{aligned}

Execution produces $x = 10, y = 5$, **identical to pass-by-reference here**, but they differ when aliasing occurs.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pass-by-Reference vs Pass-by-Name Under Aliasing

Let `x = 5` and consider the call `swap(x, x)` in pass-by-reference and pass-by-name.

| Step | Pass-by-Reference | Pass-by-Name |
|---|---|---|
| Initial state | $x = 5$ | $x = 5$ |
| `t = a;` | $a$ is `x`, so $x = 5$, then $t = 5$ | Textual: `t = x;` → $t = 5$ |
| `a = b;` | $a$ and $b$ both point to `x` → $x = 5$ | `x = x;` → $x = 5$ |
| `b = t;` | $b$ points to `x` → $x = 5$ | `x = t;` → $x = 5$ |
| **Final** | $x = 5$ | $x = 5$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pass-by-reference and pass-by-name **agree on simple cases** but **disagree when aliasing is present** in more complex expressions involving array indexing or function calls. The famous Jensen's Device in Algol 60 exploits pass-by-name to implement call-by-name for `sum(int i, int lo, int hi, int term)` where `term` uses the live index `i`.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Comparison: Static vs Dynamic Scoping

| Property | Static (Lexical) Scoping | Dynamic Scoping |
|---|---|---|
| Binding of free variables | **Compile time** | **Run time** |
| Mechanism | Static links, display vector | Search dynamic call stack |
| Speed of nonlocal access | $O(\text{depth})$ via links | $O(\text{dynamic depth})$ worst case |
| Predictability | High | Low — depends on call history |
| Languages | C, Java, Python, Haskell, Pascal | Early Lisp, APL, Snobol, Perl `local` |
| Effect of `eval`/REPL | Predictable | Often surprising |
| KTU exam weight | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Static Link Chain — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts how a subprogram at static depth 3 walks the static-link chain to reach a variable at static depth 1. The runtime stack of activation records is shown, each pointing to its **lexical parent's** activation record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["AR_Main StaticLink_null Depth0"] --> B1["AR_SubA StaticLink_to_Main Depth1"]
    B1 --> C1["AR_SubB StaticLink_to_SubA Depth2"]
    C1 --> D1["AR_SubC StaticLink_to_SubB Depth3"]
    D1 -- "hop 1" --> C1
    C1 -- "hop 2" --> B1
    B1 -- "locate X with offset 12" --> X1["Variable X at Depth1 base plus 12"]
    A1 -- "ReturnAddress" --> RET1["Return to main caller"]
    B1 -- "DynamicLink_to_Main" --> A1
    C1 -- "DynamicLink_to_SubA" --> B1
    D1 -- "DynamicLink_to_SubB" --> C1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** To access variable `X` declared in `SubA` from inside `SubC`, the runtime follows the static-link chain: `SubC → SubB → SubA`, then uses the compile-time constant **offset** of `X` within `SubA`'s activation record.

---

## 4.2 Parameter Passing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Function call encountered"] --> P1{"Parameter is primitive?"}
    P1 -- "Yes" --> P2["Pass by value default in Java C sharp Python"]
    P1 -- "No" --> P3{"Is it a large struct or object?"}
    P3 -- "Yes" --> P4{"Need to modify it in callee?"}
    P4 -- "Yes" --> P5["Pass reference or pointer to avoid copy"]
    P4 -- "No" --> P6["Pass by reference for read only efficiency"]
    P3 -- "No" --> P7["Pass by value"]
    P5 --> P8["Potential aliasing risk: check call site"]
    P6 --> P8
    P2 --> P9["Safe: no aliasing, no side effect on caller"]
    P7 --> P9
```

---

## 4.3 Activation Record Layout (Subprogram Stack Frame)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FR["Activation Record of SubC"]
        F1["Local Variables of SubC"]
        F2["Parameters of SubC"]
        F3["Return Address points to caller instruction"]
        F4["Dynamic Link points to caller's AR"]
        F5["Static Link points to lexically enclosing AR"]
    end
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> F5
    F5 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The static link is what distinguishes lexically scoped languages (Pascal, Ada) from dynamically scoped ones (early Lisp, APL). The dynamic link exists in *every* language to support stack unwinding on function return.

---

## 4.4 Closure Architecture (Subprogram Passed as Parameter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CL["Closure Object passed to Q"] --> CC["Code Pointer to function P"]
    CL --> CE["Captured Environment: list of references to enclosing variables"]
    CE --> EV1["env1 x equals 10"]
    CE --> EV2["env2 y equals 20"]
    Q["Function Q receives closure"] --> INV["Q invokes P via code pointer"]
    INV --> EVAL["P runs with its ORIGINAL lexical environment intact"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why closures matter:** Without them, passing a nested function would cause it to "lose" its surrounding variables. Closures are the foundation of **JavaScript callbacks**, **Python decorators**, **C++ `std::function` with captures**, and **Java lambdas with effectively-final variables**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*With a suitable example, explain the term **"pass-by-reference"**. Mention one situation where it can lead to subtle bugs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In pass-by-reference, the address (not the value) of the actual parameter is passed to the formal parameter. Any modification made to the formal parameter inside the subprogram directly modifies the caller's variable.

For example, in Pascal:

```pascal
procedure inc(var n: integer);
begin n := n + 1; end;
```

Calling `inc(x)` permanently changes `x` in the caller.

**Subtle bug scenario (1 mark):** If two reference parameters happen to point to the same variable (e.g., `inc(x, x)` where both parameters refer to `x`), they become **aliases**, and updates through one parameter can silently overwrite updates through the other, producing surprising results. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between **overloaded subprograms** and **generic subprograms** with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Overloaded subprograms** share the same name but have different **type signatures** (different number or types of parameters). The compiler generates **separate code** for each signature and selects the correct one at the call site. Example: `int abs(int)` and `double abs(double)` in C++.

- **Generic subprograms** use **type parameters** (e.g., `template<class T> T identity(T x)`) so that a *single* parametric definition works for any type. The compiler typically **instantiates** one copy per type used, but the source code is written only once. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often confuse **static depth** with **dynamic depth** — remember: static depth is *lexical* (compile-time), dynamic depth is *runtime call stack*.
> - When drawing static-link chains, students frequently mislabel the dynamic link as the static link. Dynamic link → **caller's** AR. Static link → **lexical parent's** AR.
> - For parameter passing, never claim "Java passes objects by reference" — Java passes **object references by value**. This costs marks every semester.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **static link (access link) method** for implementing lexical scoping. How is a nonlocal variable at static depth $d$ located by a subprogram at static depth $n$? Derive the chain-offset formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **static link** is a pointer stored in every activation record that points to the activation record of the **lexically enclosing** subprogram. This contrasts with the **dynamic link**, which points to the activation record of the **caller**.

```
Diagram (represent as in Section 4.1):
   SubC (depth 3) ──static_link──> SubB (depth 2)
   SubB (depth 2) ──static_link──> SubA (depth 1)
   SubA (depth 1) ──static_link──> Main (depth 0)
```

**Chain-Offset Derivation:**

Let $D_s(P)$ denote the static depth of subprogram $P$, and let $X$ be a variable declared in the scope of $Q$ where $D_s(Q) = d$. A subprogram $P$ at static depth $n$ accesses $X$ by traversing the static-link chain:

$$\Delta = D_s(P) - D_s(Q) = n - d \text{ hops}$$

In each hop:

\begin{aligned}
\text{step}_1 &: \text{current} \leftarrow \text{static\_link}(\text{current}) \\
\text{step}_2 &: \text{current} \leftarrow \text{static\_link}(\text{current}) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\text{step}_{n-d} &: \text{current} \leftarrow \text{static\_link}(\text{current}) \\
\text{final} &: \text{address}(X) = \text{base}(\text{current}) + \text{compile\_time\_offset}(X)
\end{aligned}

The **offset** within the activation record is fixed at compile time because the compiler knows the layout of the enclosing scope. Only the base pointer requires runtime traversal. Worst-case access time is therefore:

$$T_{\text{access}} = O(D_s(P)) = O(\text{nesting depth})$$

This is significantly faster than the naive $O(D_d)$ required by dynamic-scope stack searches. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Compare the **five classical parameter passing methods** (value, result, value-result, reference, name) in terms of direction of data flow, copying cost, aliasing risk, and effect when the same actual parameter is passed to two formal parameters. Illustrate with a `swap` example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Method | Direction | Copy? | Alias Risk | `swap(x, x)` final value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Value** | IN | On call | None | $x = 5$ (unchanged) |
| **Result** | OUT | On return | None | $x = 5$ (no input) |
| **Value-Result** | IN-OUT | Both | None | $x = 5$ (effectively) |
| **Reference** | IN-OUT | No (address) | **Yes** | $x = 5$ |
| **Name** | IN-OUT | No (thunk) | **Yes (textual)** | $x = 5$ |

**Key observations for the `swap(x, x)` case:**

1. **Pass-by-value and value-result:** The two formal parameters are *separate copies*. After `t = a; a = b; b = t;` with `a=5, b=5`, the result is $t=5, a=5, b=5$. Copying back yields $x = 5$.

2. **Pass-by-reference:** Both `a` and `b` are **aliases** of `x`. Execution: $t = x = 5$; $a = b$ becomes $x = x$ (no change); $b = t$ becomes $x = 5$. Final: $x = 5$.

3. **Pass-by-name:** Textual substitution: `t = x; x = x; x = t;`. With $x = 5$: $t = 5$, $x = 5$, $x = 5$. Final: $x = 5$.

Pass-by-reference and pass-by-name **agree on simple cases** but **diverge when aliasing involves expressions** that have side effects (e.g., `swap(A[i], A[j++])`). The compiler implements pass-by-name via **thunks** — zero-argument procedures that re-evaluate the actual parameter expression on every use. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** What is a **closure**? Explain how closures enable the correct referencing environment for subprograms passed as parameters. Give an example in C++, Java, or Python. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **closure** is a data object that packages together:
1. A pointer to the executable code of a subprogram, and
2. A reference to the **referencing environment** in which that subprogram was originally defined.

When a subprogram is passed as a parameter, the receiver must be able to call it with all its *original* nonlocal references intact. Without a closure, those references would point to a now-deallocated activation record.

**Mechanism:**
- The closure constructor captures all free variables of the subprogram.
- These captured variables are stored in an environment record (often on the heap, not the stack).
- The receiver invokes the subprogram through the closure, restoring its original scope.

**C++ Example:**

```cpp
auto make_adder(int x) {
    return [x](int y) { return x + y; };  // [x] captures x by value
}
auto add5 = make_adder(5);
std::cout << add5(3);  // prints 8
```

Here `add5` is a closure: it carries the code of the lambda and the captured value `x = 5`. Even after `make_adder` returns and its stack frame is gone, the closure preserves the binding.

**Why it's needed in KTU perspective:** Closures make **first-class functions** possible — functions as values, parameters, return values, and data structure members. This is the foundation of **functional programming**, **callbacks**, and **event-driven GUIs**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Discuss the design considerations for **generic (parametrically polymorphic) subprograms** versus overloaded subprograms. What are the trade-offs in terms of code size, type checking, and runtime efficiency? Mention how C++ templates and Java generics implement these. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Design Trade-off Table:**

| Aspect | Overloaded Subprograms | Generic Subprograms |
|---|---|---|
| Source code | One definition per signature | One parametric definition |
| Compiler output | One object per signature | One object per instantiation type |
| Code bloat | Moderate (one per usage) | Can be large (one per type) |
| Type checking | At call site, static | At instantiation point |
| Runtime cost | None (resolved at compile time) | None (resolved at compile time) |
| Flexibility | Limited to declared signatures | Infinite (any type satisfying constraints) |

**C++ Template Implementation:**

```cpp
template <typename T>
T max(T a, T b) { return (a > b) ? a : b; }
```

The compiler performs **template instantiation** — for `max<int>` and `max<double>`, two separate functions are generated. This is **type erasure at source level but not at binary level**.

**Java Generic Implementation:**

```java
public <T extends Comparable<T>> T max(T a, T b) { ... }
```

Java uses **type erasure** — only one bytecode version of `max` exists, with casts inserted at the call site. This avoids code bloat but sacrifices some runtime type information.

**Engineering trade-off:**
- C++ templates = fast runtime, larger binary.
- Java generics = smaller binary, casts at runtime, no primitive type generics.
- C# generics = best of both — reified generics with one specialized copy per value type.

**Key KTU takeaway:** Generics are the more *expressive* and *DRY* (Don't Repeat Yourself) mechanism, while overloading is simpler and more *explicit*. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Bullet Bank — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ☐ **Five parameter passing methods:** value, result, value-result, reference, name — know direction, copy, alias risk.
- ☐ **Pass-by-name** is implemented via **thunks** (zero-argument functions), not direct copies.
- ☐ **Static depth** = lexical nesting level; **Dynamic depth** = call stack depth at runtime.
- ☐ **Static link** → lexical parent's AR; **Dynamic link** → caller's AR.
- ☐ Chain offset formula: $\Delta = D_s(\text{caller}) - D_s(\text{target})$.
- ☐ **Closures** bundle code + captured environment; required for first-class functions.
- ☐ **Overloading** = ad-hoc polymorphism (one name, multiple signatures).
- ☐ **Generics** = parametric polymorphism (one parametric definition, many type instantiations).
- ☐ **Java passes object references by value**, NOT by reference — a common exam trap.
- ☐ **Pass-by-reference and pass-by-name** agree on simple cases but differ with aliasing involving side-effecting expressions.
- ☐ **Separate compilation** allows subprograms to be compiled independently into object files and linked later.
- ☐ **Activation record** contains: parameters, return address, dynamic link, static link, locals.
- ☐ **C# generics** are reified (specialized at runtime per value type); **Java generics** use type erasure.
- ☐ The **Jensen's Device** exploits pass-by-name for `sum` loops with side-effecting index expressions.
- ☐ **Separate compilation** vs **independent compilation** — independent means no shared state at compile time.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** When a question says "explain with a diagram," always include an activation-record stack with explicit static-link and dynamic-link arrows. Examiners award full marks only when the diagram distinguishes these two link types clearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Referencing Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Local Referencing Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Local Referencing Environment (LRE)** of a subprogram is the set of *all entities* (variables, constants, parameters, subprograms, types) that the subprogram can legally reference at run-time, restricted to those whose *bindings* are established within or directly accessible to that subprogram.

In the terminology of **block-structured languages** (Algol, Pascal, Ada, modern C/C++/Java), the LRE consists of:

1. The **local variables** declared inside the subprogram body.
2. The **formal parameters** passed into the subprogram.
3. All entities **inherited from enclosing scopes** (parent blocks, package bodies, enclosing classes), subject to visibility rules.
4. **Built-in / predefined entities** of the language (e.g., `Integer`, `Sin`, `Cos`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The LRE is *established* at the moment the subprogram is *activated* (i.e., when its activation record is pushed onto the run-time stack) and is *destroyed* when the subprogram terminates. This life-cycle is the foundation of stack-dynamic storage management.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Cabin" Model

Imagine a software company as a **building**:

- The **building itself** = the *global scope* (all global variables, library functions).
- Each **office cabin** = a subprogram.
- Items *inside* a cabin (the desk, the files on the desk) = **local variables** of that subprogram.
- Items pinned on the *cabin wall* from outside (a notice from the manager) = **non-local references** (variables from the enclosing scope).
- A **name plate** on the door = the subprogram's formal parameters.

When you enter a cabin, you can see and use *everything inside it plus whatever is on its walls (inherited visibility)*. The moment you step out, the cabin is locked, its contents are thrown away, and a new occupant can move in with a fresh set of items. **This is exactly how a Local Referencing Environment behaves at run-time.**

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Quick Revision)

- **Local Variable** — A variable declared within a subprogram; its scope is bounded by the subprogram's body.
- **Non-Local Variable** — A variable visible in a subprogram but declared in an enclosing scope.
- **Scope (Static / Lexical Scope)** — The textual region of the program where a name is visible.
- **Extent (Lifetime / Dynamic Scope)** — The interval of execution time during which the variable's storage exists.
- **Activation Record (AR)** — A stack-allocated data structure holding the LRE of an active subprogram.
- **Static Link (Access Link)** — A pointer in each AR to the AR of the *statically enclosing* subprogram instance, used to reach non-local entities.
- **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** — A pointer to the AR of the *caller* (the subprogram that invoked the current one).
- **Nesting Depth** — The number of enclosing static scopes between a subprogram and the global scope.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU pitfall: **Scope** is a *compile-time (textual)* concept, while **Extent** is a *run-time (temporal)* concept. Many students confuse the two — examiners routinely test this distinction for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Layers of an LRE

A subprogram's LRE is composed of three layered sub-environments, evaluated in strict order during name resolution:

1. **Local Declarations Layer** — Variables, constants, types, nested subprograms declared *inside* the current subprogram body.
2. **Formal Parameter Layer** — Names introduced through the subprogram's parameter list (these are bound at call time to actual arguments).
3. **Inherited (Non-Local) Layer** — All entities visible from the *statically enclosing* subprogram, recursively up to the global scope.

**Resolution Rule:** When a name is referenced, the compiler/runtime searches layer 1 → layer 2 → layer 3 (innermost first). The *first match* wins. This is the **most-close-nesting rule** and is the cornerstone of block-structured name binding.

## 2.2 Static vs Dynamic Scoping — A Critical Distinction

| Property | Static (Lexical) Scoping | Dynamic Scoping |
|---|---|---|
| Visibility determined by | **Program text** | **Call sequence at run-time** |
| Binding time | Compile time | Run time |
| Default in modern languages | **Yes (C, C++, Java, Python, Ada, Pascal)** | No (rare; found in early LISP, APL, some scripting dialects) |
| Implementation | Static links / display vectors | Association list / dynamic chain traversal |
| Predictability | **High** — behaviour is text-driven | Low — depends on call history |
| KTU exam weightage | **High** | Medium (contrast question) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LRE is *always* a static-scope construct in KTU's syllabus. Dynamic scoping is mentioned only as a *contrast* — examiners will frame questions assuming lexical scoping unless explicitly told otherwise.

## 2.3 Activation Record Anatomy (The Physical Home of the LRE)

Each subprogram invocation creates an **Activation Record** on the run-time **call stack**. The LRE lives inside this AR. The standard layout (bottom → top of stack) is:

| Offset from AR Base | Field | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Return Value | Storage for the value the subprogram returns to its caller |
| 1 | Actual Parameters | Copy of (or pointer to) the arguments passed in |
| 2 | Saved Dynamic Link | Pointer to the caller's AR (used to restore stack on return) |
| 3 | Saved Static Link | Pointer to the *statically enclosing* AR (used to reach non-local entities) |
| 4 | Saved Return Address | Instruction pointer inside the caller to resume execution |
| 5 | Local Variables | All locally declared variables of the current subprogram |
| 6 | Temporaries | Intermediate results of expressions |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rule Sheet

The following are the **core equations and rules** examiners expect a student to be able to reproduce from memory:

| ID | Formula / Rule | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | $\text{Depth}(P) = \text{Depth}(\text{parent}(P)) + 1$ | Nesting depth of subprogram $P$ relative to its enclosing scope |
| **R2** | $\text{LRE}(P) = \text{Locals}(P) \cup \text{Params}(P) \cup \text{Visible}(\text{Ancestors}(P))$ | Composition of the LRE |
| **R3** | $\text{Address}(x) = \text{Base}(\text{AR}(P)) + \text{Offset}(x)$ | Run-time address of variable $x$ in the AR of $P$ |
| **R4** | $\text{Offset}(x) = \sum_{i<k} \text{Size}(v_i) + \text{AlignPad}$ | Compile-time computed offset of the $k$-th local |
| **R5** | $\text{Display}[i] = \text{AR of the deepest static ancestor at depth } i$ | Display-vector optimization for fast non-local access |
| **R6** | $\text{NonLocalAccessCost}_{\text{link}} = O(n)$ where $n = \text{Depth}(P)$ | Cost of static-link traversal |
| **R7** | $\text{NonLocalAccessCost}_{\text{display}} = O(1)$ | Cost when a display vector is maintained |
| **R8** | $\text{TotalStackHeight} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \text{ARSize}(S_j)$ | Peak call-stack memory for $k$ concurrent active subprograms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Static-link and display mechanisms are the real-life foundations of *closure capture* in functional languages (Scheme, Haskell), *enclosing instance references* in Java inner classes (`OuterClass.this`), and *captured variables* in C\# lambdas and C++11 `[&]` capture lists. A solid grasp of LRE design directly translates to writing correct, efficient callbacks and event handlers.

## 2.5 Lifetime vs Visibility — Two More Pairs to Know

| Concept | Question it answers | Answered at |
|---|---|---|
| **Lifetime (Extent)** | *When* does the storage for a variable exist? | Run time |
| **Scope (Visibility)** | *Where* in the program text can the name be used? | Compile time |
| **Reachability** | Can the *current* code path *physically access* a variable? | Run time (depends on LRE chain) |

A variable can be **in scope but not yet alive** (e.g., declared later in the same block — illegal in Java, legal in C with hoisting rules). A variable can be **alive but not in scope** (e.g., a local of a function that has returned — unreachable from outer code, garbage-collected later).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Static-Link Resolution (Full Trace)

Consider the following Algol-60-style pseudocode (used in KTU model papers as the canonical LRE example):

```
PROGRAM Demo;
  VAR x : INTEGER;          (* Global, Depth 0 *)

  PROCEDURE P;
    VAR a : INTEGER;        (* Local to P, Depth 1 *)

    PROCEDURE Q;
      VAR b : INTEGER;      (* Local to Q, Depth 2 *)
    BEGIN
      a := 10;              (* Non-local reference into P's LRE *)
      x := 20;              (* Non-local reference into global LRE *)
      b := 30;              (* Local reference, Depth 2 *)
    END Q;

  BEGIN
    CALL Q;
  END P;

BEGIN
  CALL P;
END Demo.
```

**Step 1 — Identify the static nesting tree.**

The static structure is a chain: `Demo` (depth 0) $\rightarrow$ `P` (depth 1) $\rightarrow$ `Q` (depth 2). Each arrow represents a *static parent* edge.

**Step 2 — Build the call chain at run-time.**

The execution trace is: `Demo` calls `P`; `P` calls `Q`. Hence the **dynamic call stack** is, from bottom to top: `AR(Demo)` $\rightarrow$ `AR(P)` $\rightarrow$ `AR(Q)`.

**Step 3 — Set up the static links.**

When `Demo` is activated, its AR's static link is `NIL` (or to itself, depending on convention). When `P` is called from `Demo`, the callee's static link is set to point to the AR of the *statically enclosing* subprogram, i.e., `AR(Demo)`. When `Q` is called from `P`, the callee's static link is set to `AR(P)`.

The resulting pointer graph is:

$$
\text{AR(Q)}.\text{static\_link} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AR(P)}.\text{static\_link} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AR(Demo)}.\text{static\_link} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{NIL}
$$

**Step 4 — Resolve each reference inside `Q`.**

- `b` — Look in `AR(Q)` locals. **Found at offset $\text{AR(Q)}.\text{base} + 0$.** No link traversal needed.
- `a` — Not in `AR(Q)`. Follow static link to `AR(P)`. Look in `AR(P)` locals. **Found at offset $\text{AR(P)}.\text{base} + 0$.** One link hop.
- `x` — Not in `AR(Q)`. Follow static link to `AR(P)`. Not in `AR(P)`. Follow static link to `AR(Demo)`. Look in `AR(Demo)` globals. **Found at offset $\text{AR(Demo)}.\text{base} + 0$.** Two link hops.

**Step 5 — Compute access cost using Rule R6.**

$$
\text{Cost}(b) = 0 \text{ hops}, \quad \text{Cost}(a) = 1 \text{ hop}, \quad \text{Cost}(x) = 2 \text{ hops}
$$

In general, the cost equals the number of enclosing static scopes between the reference site and the declaration site, which equals $\text{Depth}(\text{decl}) - \text{Depth}(\text{ref site})$, but is **always non-negative**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Display Vector Optimization

A **display vector** $D$ is a global array of pointers maintained by the run-time system, where $D[i]$ always points to the AR of the *currently active* subprogram at static depth $i$.

**Step 1 — Activation of `Demo` (depth 0).**

Set $D[0] = \text{AR(Demo)}$. All other $D[i]$ are `NIL` for $i > 0$.

**Step 2 — Activation of `P` (depth 1) by `Demo`.**

Save the old $D[1]$ (currently `NIL`) and set $D[1] = \text{AR(P)}$. $D[0]$ is untouched.

**Step 3 — Activation of `Q` (depth 2) by `P`.**

Save the old $D[2]$ (currently `NIL`) and set $D[2] = \text{AR(Q)}$.

**Step 4 — Reference resolution inside `Q` using the display.**

To access a non-local entity declared at static depth $d$, the runtime simply does:

$$
\text{Address}(x) = D[d].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(x)
$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address}(b) &= D[2].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(b) \\
\text{Address}(a) &= D[1].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(a) \\
\text{Address}(x) &= D[0].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Each access is exactly **one indirection plus one base-plus-offset addition**, irrespective of nesting depth. This realises **Rule R7**: $O(1)$ non-local access cost. The trade-off is $O(1)$ extra work *per subprogram call* to save/restore one display slot, plus $O(\text{max depth})$ storage for the display itself.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Formal Derivation of AR Size

Suppose a subprogram $S$ has $n$ local variables with sizes $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$ bytes, plus overhead fields totaling $H$ bytes, and alignment requirement $A$ (typically $A = 4$ or $A = 8$ bytes for 32/64-bit architectures).

**Step 1 — Compute raw payload size.**

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = H + \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i
$$

**Step 2 — Round up to the next alignment boundary.**

$$
S_{\text{AR}} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{raw}}}{A} \right\rceil \cdot A
$$

**Step 3 — Example calculation.**

Let $H = 24$ bytes (5 fields $\times$ 4 bytes + 4 bytes for return value), $n = 3$ with $s_1 = 4, s_2 = 8, s_3 = 4$, and $A = 8$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{raw}} &= 24 + (4 + 8 + 4) = 24 + 16 = 40 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{AR}} &= \left\lceil \frac{40}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 8 = 5 \cdot 8 = 40 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

So this AR consumes exactly 40 bytes on a 64-bit-aligned stack. The total stack height for a recursive call depth of $k$ would be $40k$ bytes, per **Rule R8**.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Demonstrating Dynamic Scoping Failure

Consider the same program but interpreted under **dynamic** scoping. The only change is that non-local lookups follow the *dynamic* (call) chain rather than the *static* (textual) chain.

Suppose in some hypothetical language, `Q` is called from a different path:

```
PROGRAM Demo2;
  VAR x : INTEGER;
  PROCEDURE R; VAR x : INTEGER; BEGIN x := 99; CALL Q; END R;
  PROCEDURE Q; BEGIN PRINT x; END Q;
  BEGIN x := 0; CALL R; END Demo2.
```

Under **static** scoping, `Q` prints `0` (it sees the global `x`). Under **dynamic** scoping, `Q` follows the dynamic chain back to `R`'s AR and prints `99`. **Same source code, different output** — this is precisely why lexical scoping is preferred for predictability, and why the LRE in KTU's syllabus is built on static-link / display semantics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Call Stack with Static Links

The following diagram visualises the run-time call stack for the Worked Example 1 (Demo → P → Q). Each activation record is shown with its dynamic link (downward arrow) and static link (curved side arrow).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stack0["RUN-TIME CALL STACK  (Top of Stack on the Left)"]
        direction TB
        arQ["AR_Q  Depth 2\nReturnValue\nActualParams\nDynLink to AR_P\nStaticLink to AR_P\nRetAddr\nLocal b"]
        arP["AR_P  Depth 1\nReturnValue\nActualParams\nDynLink to AR_Demo\nStaticLink to AR_Demo\nRetAddr\nLocal a"]
        arDemo["AR_Demo  Depth 0\nReturnValue\nActualParams\nDynLink to NIL\nStaticLink to NIL\nRetAddr\nGlobal x"]
    end

    linkQ1[ ] -.Dynamic Link.-> arP
    linkQ2[ ] -.Static Link.-> arP
    linkP1[ ] -.Dynamic Link.-> arDemo
    linkP2[ ] -.Static Link.-> arDemo

    arQ --- linkQ1
    arQ --- linkQ2
    arP --- linkP1
    arP --- linkP2

    refA["Reference to a inside Q"] -- "Follow StaticLink" --> arP
    refX["Reference to x inside Q"] -- "Follow StaticLink twice" --> arDemo
    refB["Reference to b inside Q"] -- "Local lookup" --> arQ
```

**How to read the diagram:**

- The **vertical column** of ARs represents the dynamic call stack (Q is the most recent call, sitting on top).
- **Dynamic links** (dashed straight arrows) trace the call chain back to the caller.
- **Static links** (dashed curved arrows) trace the lexical (textual) parent chain.
- Inside `Q`, accessing `a` requires *one* static-link hop (to `P`); accessing `x` requires *two* hops (to `P` then to `Demo`); accessing `b` requires *zero* hops (local).

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — LRE Resolution Algorithm

The following flowchart captures the exact decision sequence a compiler or interpreter follows when resolving a name reference against the Local Referencing Environment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Name Reference Encountered"]) --> localCheck{"Is name declared\nin the CURRENT\nsubprogram body?"}
    localCheck -- "Yes" --> useLocal["Bind to local variable\nO(1) access via AR base + offset"]
    localCheck -- "No" --> formalCheck{"Is name in the\nformal parameter list?"}
    formalCheck -- "Yes" --> useFormal["Bind to formal parameter\nO(1) access via AR base + offset"]
    formalCheck -- "No" --> followStatic{"Follow static link\nto enclosing AR"}
    followStatic --> ancestorCheck{"Reached enclosing AR\nsuccessfully?"}
    ancestorCheck -- "Yes, name found there" --> useNonLocal["Bind to non-local entity\nO(depth) using static link\nOR O(1) using display vector"]
    ancestorCheck -- "Reached NIL" --> useBuiltin{"Is name a built-in\nlanguage entity?"}
    useBuiltin -- "Yes" --> useBuiltin2["Bind to built-in\ne.g. Integer, Sin, Sqrt"]
    useBuiltin -- "No" --> undeclared["ERROR Undeclared Identifier\nCompilation fails"]

    useLocal --> end1(["Resolution Complete"])
    useFormal --> end1
    useNonLocal --> end1
    useBuiltin2 --> end1
    undeclared --> end1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — Static vs Dynamic Scoping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph staticChain["STATIC SCOPING  Lexical Parent Chain"]
        sQ["AR_Q  Static depth 2"] -- "Static Link" --> sP["AR_P  Static depth 1"]
        sP -- "Static Link" --> sDemo["AR_Demo  Static depth 0"]
    end

    subgraph dynamicChain["DYNAMIC SCOPING  Call-Time Chain"]
        dQ["AR_Q  Invoked by P"] -- "Dynamic Link" --> dP["AR_P  Invoked by Demo"]
        dP -- "Dynamic Link" --> dDemo["AR_Demo  Caller is NIL"]
    end

    note1["In static scoping, Q looks at AR of its TEXTUAL parent P, even if invoked via R at runtime"]
    note2["In dynamic scoping, Q looks at AR of its CALLER P, which may differ from the textual parent"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue for the student:** In static scoping the two chains *usually* coincide; they diverge only when the call path crosses a textual boundary. KTU exam questions frequently use such a divergence to test whether the student has internalised the difference.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between the scope and the lifetime of a variable. Show with a small code snippet how a variable can be in scope but not yet alive, and vice-versa.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Scope (1 mark):** The textual region of the program where a name is *visible* and can be legally referenced. Determined at **compile time** by the language's scoping rules.
- **Lifetime / Extent (1 mark):** The interval of execution time during which *storage* is allocated for a variable. Determined at **run time** by the storage-allocation strategy.
- **Example (1 mark):**
  - *In scope but not yet alive:* A `for`-loop index `i` declared in the loop header is in scope throughout the loop body, but storage is allocated only when the loop begins executing; before that, it is not alive. (Java, C++.)
  - *Alive but not in scope:* A local variable of a function that has returned is still allocated on the stack briefly (until the AR is popped) but is no longer accessible because no name binds to it from any active scope.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is a static link in an activation record? Why is it needed even when a display vector is maintained?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Static Link (1 mark):** A pointer stored in each activation record that points to the activation record of the *statically enclosing* subprogram instance. It is the textual parent in the source code, not necessarily the caller.
- **Why needed with display (1 mark):** The display vector can be corrupted if a subprogram is invoked as a parameter or returned as a value (a *downward funarg*). The static link in the AR itself is robust against such complications because it is set at activation time from the caller's display and never overwritten.
- **Practical use (1 mark):** Closures in functional languages, Java inner classes (`OuterClass.this`), and C++ lambda captures all rely on a static-link–like hidden pointer to maintain correct non-local access.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students habitually lose 2–3 marks by (i) confusing **static** and **dynamic** links, (ii) forgetting to mention the **return address** field, and (iii) drawing activation record boxes without *labelling* each field. Always label every field of the AR explicitly, mention both kinds of links separately, and trace the resolution of at least two non-local references for full credit.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **Local Referencing Environment (LRE)** of a subprogram in a block-structured language. List all the components that constitute an LRE, and describe how they are physically realised at run-time using an **activation record**. Draw a labelled diagram of the activation record and indicate the role of the static link and the dynamic link. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following pseudocode. For each of the variables `a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e` referenced inside the body of subprogram `Inner2`, state the **static depth of declaration**, the **number of static-link hops** required to access it, and the **final address expression** (using a display vector `D`). Show all your working. **(7 marks)**

```
PROGRAM Outer;
  VAR a : INTEGER;
  PROCEDURE Middle;
    VAR b : INTEGER;
    PROCEDURE Inner1;
      VAR c : INTEGER;
      PROCEDURE Inner2;
        VAR d : INTEGER;
      BEGIN
        (* reference site *)
        a := b + c + d;
      END Inner2;
    BEGIN
      CALL Inner2;
    END Inner1;
    VAR e : INTEGER;
  BEGIN
    CALL Inner1;
  END Middle;
BEGIN
  CALL Middle;
END Outer.
```

**Model Solution for B-A (a):**

1. **Definition of LRE (1 mark):** The LRE of a subprogram is the set of all entities (variables, parameters, types, nested subprograms) that the subprogram can reference at run-time, comprising its own locals, its formal parameters, and all non-local entities inherited from enclosing static scopes.

2. **Components of LRE (2 marks):**
   - Local variables declared in the subprogram body.
   - Formal parameters declared in the subprogram's parameter list.
   - Non-local entities from the enclosing scopes (lexical parents).
   - Built-in / predefined entities of the language.

3. **Physical realisation — Activation Record (2 marks):** At run-time, every subprogram invocation creates a fresh activation record on the call stack. The AR contains storage for all locals, copies of (or pointers to) actual parameters, a return value slot, the return address, a dynamic link to the caller's AR, and a static link to the statically enclosing AR. (See the labelled diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

4. **Static vs Dynamic link (2 marks):**
   - **Dynamic link** points to the AR of the *caller* (the subprogram that invoked the current one). Used to restore the stack frame on return.
   - **Static link** points to the AR of the *statically enclosing* subprogram (the lexical parent in the source text). Used to reach non-local entities under lexical scoping. The two are *different* — a subprogram can be called from a non-ancestor in the source.

**Model Solution for B-A (b):**

- **Static nesting depths:**
  - `Outer` $\rightarrow$ depth 0
  - `Middle` $\rightarrow$ depth 1
  - `Inner1` $\rightarrow$ depth 2
  - `Inner2` $\rightarrow$ depth 3
  - The reference site is inside `Inner2` at depth 3.

- **Declaration depths and hop counts (5 marks):**

  | Variable | Declared in | Declaration depth $d$ | Reference depth | Hops = $\text{RefDepth} - d$ | Address expression |
  |---|---|---|---|---|---|
  | $a$ | `Outer` | 0 | 3 | 3 | $D[0].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(a)$ |
  | $b$ | `Middle` | 1 | 3 | 2 | $D[1].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(b)$ |
  | $c$ | `Inner1` | 2 | 3 | 1 | $D[2].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(c)$ |
  | $d$ | `Inner2` | 3 | 3 | 0 | $D[3].\text{base} + \text{Offset}(d)$ |
  | $e$ | `Middle` | 1 | 3 | — | **NOT accessible** — declared *after* `Inner1` in the source text, so it is not in the LRE of `Inner2` under Algol-60's declaration-before-use rule |

- **Hops using static links only (would be):** 3, 2, 1, 0, and undefined for $e$ respectively. With the display vector (Rule R7), every access is $O(1)$ regardless of hop count.

- **Key insight (1 mark):** The variable `e` is unreachable from `Inner2` even though it is declared in an enclosing scope, because it is textually *after* `Inner1`. The LRE respects declaration order, not merely nesting.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`  *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **static (lexical) scoping** with **dynamic scoping**. Illustrate with a concrete program fragment in which the two strategies produce different outputs for the same input. State, with justification, which scoping rule the C, Java, and Python languages use, and which implementation mechanism (static link or display vector) gives constant-time non-local access. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive, step by step, the formula for the **total size of an activation record** in bytes for a subprogram with $n$ local variables of sizes $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$, a fixed overhead of $H$ bytes, and an architecture-mandated alignment of $A$ bytes. Use the formula to compute the total peak stack memory consumed by a recursive function that calls itself $k$ times before any returns occur, given $H = 32$, $n = 4$, $(s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4) = (4, 8, 4, 8)$, $A = 8$, and $k = 10$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for B-B (a):**

1. **Definitions (2 marks):** Static scoping binds a name to its declaration by examining the *source-code textual structure* at compile time. Dynamic scoping binds a name by examining the *runtime call stack* when the reference is encountered.

2. **Concrete contrasting example (2 marks):** (Same shape as Worked Example 4.)
   - Under static scoping: `PRINT x` inside `Q` outputs the global `x = 0`.
   - Under dynamic scoping: `Q` walks the dynamic chain, finds `x = 99` in `R`'s AR, and prints `99`.

3. **Languages (2 marks):** C, Java, Python, C++, Ada, Pascal, Haskell, Go, Rust — all use **static (lexical) scoping** as the default. Bash and some early Lisps exhibit dynamic scoping.

4. **Constant-time mechanism (1 mark):** The **display vector** achieves $O(1)$ non-local access by maintaining a global array of pointers to currently active ARs at each static depth. Static links alone give $O(n)$ access where $n$ is the static depth.

**Model Solution for B-B (b):**

1. **Derivation of the AR size formula (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{raw}} &= H + \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \\
S_{\text{AR}} &= \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{raw}}}{A} \right\rceil \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

The raw size is the sum of overhead and locals. The aligned size rounds the raw size up to the next multiple of $A$ to satisfy architecture alignment constraints (otherwise, misaligned accesses cause hardware exceptions or severe slowdowns).

2. **Numerical substitution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{raw}} &= 32 + (4 + 8 + 4 + 8) = 32 + 24 = 56 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{AR}} &= \left\lceil \frac{56}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 8 = 7 \cdot 8 = 56 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

(56 is already a multiple of 8, so no padding is needed.)

3. **Peak stack memory (2 marks):** Applying **Rule R8**, with $k = 10$ concurrent (un-returned) recursive activations:

$$
\text{PeakStack} = k \cdot S_{\text{AR}} = 10 \cdot 56 = 560 \text{ bytes}
$$

The total call-stack memory required is **560 bytes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in B-B (b):** Students often forget to apply the ceiling operation for alignment and lose 1 mark. Always show $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ explicitly, and verify divisibility of the raw size by $A$ before declaring no padding is needed.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LRE = Locals + Parameters + Inherited (Non-Local) Entities** (Rule R2). Know the formula cold.
- **Scope is compile-time / textual; Lifetime is run-time / temporal.** This is the single most-tested distinction in Part A.
- **An Activation Record holds the LRE physically.** It contains at minimum: return value, actuals, dynamic link, static link, return address, locals, temporaries.
- **Static link = lexical parent AR; Dynamic link = caller AR.** They are not the same — they differ whenever a subprogram is called from a non-ancestor.
- **Most-Close-Nesting Rule:** Innermost matching declaration wins during name resolution.
- **Static-link traversal cost** = nesting depth difference, $O(n)$ (Rule R6).
- **Display vector** gives $O(1)$ non-local access (Rule R7) at the cost of one save/restore per call.
- **Static scoping is the default** in C, C++, Java, Python, Ada, Pascal, Go, Rust. Dynamic scoping is rare and exam-only.
- **Closures, Java inner classes, C++ lambdas, and C# delegates** all rely on the static-link concept to capture non-local bindings.
- **AR Size formula:** $S_{\text{AR}} = \lceil (H + \sum s_i) / A \rceil \cdot A$. Always include alignment.
- **Peak stack memory:** $\text{Rule R8} = k \cdot S_{\text{AR}}$ for $k$ concurrent un-returned activations.
- **Two AR fields to NEVER forget in a diagram:** Static link AND dynamic link (separate arrows, separate labels).
- **Examiner-favourite trick:** A variable declared in an enclosing scope but *textually after* the nested subprogram is **not** in the LRE of the nested subprogram (declaration-before-use rule in Algol/Pascal/Ada).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameter Passing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Parameter Passing Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition In programming language theory, **Parameter Passing Methods** (also termed **Parameter Binding Mechanisms**) refer to the semantic mechanism used by a programming language to bind actual arguments (supplied at the call site) to formal parameters (declared in the function/subroutine signature). The choice of mechanism determines whether the called procedure receives a **copy of the value**, a **reference to the storage location**, or a **re-evaluated expression**, and directly governs the visibility, mutability, and side-effect behavior of data shared between the caller and the callee. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEBMT746 Module 3:** > Students must master **Call by Value**, **Call by Reference**, **Call by Address (Pointer)**, **Call by Value-Result**, and the conceptual evaluation of **Call by Name** with reference to activation records, copy-restore semantics, and parameter binding time. This topic carries direct weightage in ESE Part B questions (typically 7 or 14 marks). --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you want a colleague to update a project file stored on the office shared drive. | Mechanism | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | **Call by Value** | You **photocopy the file** and hand over the duplicate. The colleague edits the copy; your original remains untouched. | | **Call by Reference / Address** | You **share a direct link** to the file on the drive. Any edit the colleague makes instantly reflects in your original. | | **Call by Value-Result** | You hand over a **photocopy**, the colleague edits the copy, and upon return, you **overwrite your original** with the modified copy. | | **Call by Name** | The colleague receives a **set of instructions** ("whenever you need the data, re-read it from my desk"). Each access fetches the *current* value. | > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters in Engineering:** In embedded systems, real-time operating systems (RTOS), and high-performance computing (HPC), the wrong parameter passing strategy can cause **race conditions, stack overflows, or unintended aliasing** — a 1-bit logic error in pointer-based calls can corrupt an entire RTOS scheduler table. --- ### Taxonomy of Parameter Passing Mechanisms (KTU 2024 Classification) Programming languages implement one or more of the following binding strategies: 1. **Call by Value** — Value-copy semantics. 2. **Call by Reference** — Alias semantics (Fortran, Pascal `var`). 3. **Call by Address (Pointer)** — Explicit address-passing (C, C++). 4. **Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore)** — Hybrid: copy in, copy out (Ada `in out`, Fortran `intent(inout)`). 5. **Call by Result** — Copy-out only (Ada `out`). 6. **Call by Name** — Late binding via thunk (Algol 60). 7. **Call by Need (Lazy Evaluation)** — Thunk + memoization (Haskell, Miranda). > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** C and C++ use *Call by Value* by default but expose *Call by Address* through pointers. Java uses a hybrid — primitives are *Call by Value*, while object references are effectively *Call by Value of the reference* (a subtle distinction students frequently misstate). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Five Binding Phases Every parameter passing mechanism operates across **three temporal phases**: | Phase | Description | Affected Mechanisms | |---|---|---| | **(i) Binding Time** | When the argument-expression is evaluated and bound to the formal parameter. | All (eager for Value/Ref; deferred for Name) | | **(ii) Access Semantics** | Whether the formal parameter shares storage (alias) with the actual argument. | Reference → yes; Value → no | | **(iii) Return Semantics** | How (or whether) the modified formal parameter value is propagated back to the caller. | Value-Result → yes; Value → no; Reference → automatic | ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How Each Mechanism Works #### A) Call by Value 1. The actual argument expression is **evaluated** in the caller's environment. 2. The resulting **r-value** (a copy) is placed onto the callee's **activation record** (stack frame). 3. The callee operates exclusively on this local copy. 4. On return, the copy is **discarded** — the caller's original is untouched. > [!NOTE] > In C, every primitive `int`, `float`, `char`, `struct` argument is passed by value unless its address (`&`) is explicitly passed. #### B) Call by Reference 1. The actual argument's **l-value** (storage address) is bound to the formal parameter. 2. The formal parameter becomes an **alias** of the actual argument. 3. Any write to the formal parameter is **immediately visible** to the caller. 4. No explicit dereferencing is required (language handles it). #### C) Call by Address (Pointer) 1. The **address** of the actual argument is pushed onto the stack. 2. The callee's formal parameter is a pointer variable. 3. The callee must **explicitly dereference** (`*ptr`) to read/write. 4. Behavior is functionally equivalent to *Call by Reference* in single-threaded contexts. #### D) Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore) 1. **Entry:** Actual argument's r-value is copied into the formal parameter (like Call by Value). 2. **Execution:** Callee operates on the local copy (modifications are local). 3. **Return:** Final r-value of the formal parameter is **copied back** into the actual argument's storage. 4. The callee never observes concurrent updates to the original during execution. #### E) Call by Name 1. The argument **expression** (not value) is bound to the formal parameter. 2. A **thunk** (a deferred closure) is created, capturing the caller's environment. 3. Each reference to the formal parameter **re-evaluates** the original expression. 4. Late binding means the parameter sees the *latest* value at access time. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Mechanism | Binding Time | Storage Sharing | Update Visible to Caller? | Re-evaluation? | KTU Typical Question | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Call by Value** | Before call | No (local copy) | No (read-only effect) | No | "Justify with example" — 7 marks | | 2 | **Call by Reference** | Before call | Yes (alias) | Yes (immediate) | No | "Differentiate from Call by Value" — 7 marks | | 3 | **Call by Address** | Before call | Indirect (via pointer) | Yes (via deref) | No | "Illustrate with C program" — 14 marks | | 4 | **Call by Value-Result** | Entry: copy-in; Exit: copy-out | Transient | Yes (only on return) | No | "Trace output with swap" — 14 marks | | 5 | **Call by Result** | Exit: copy-out | Transient | Yes (only on return) | No | "Compare with Value-Result" — 7 marks | | 6 | **Call by Name** | At each use (late) | No (expression re-evaluated) | Yes (latest value) | **Yes (every access)** | "Algol 60 Jensen's Device" — 14 marks | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Answers:** "Call by Reference" (Pascal/Fortran) is a **language-level alias** — no explicit dereferencing syntax. "Call by Address" (C/C++) requires **explicit pointer dereferencing**. Examiners *do* deduct marks if these are conflated. --- ### 2.4 Engineering Utility | Domain | Mechanism Preferred | Justification | |---|---|---| | Embedded Firmware (C) | Call by Address | Zero-copy access to hardware registers (e.g., `*(volatile uint32_t*)0x40021000`) | | Scientific Computing (Fortran) | Call by Reference | Avoids costly array copies for large matrices | | Functional Languages (Haskell) | Call by Need | Enables infinite data structures, lazy evaluation | | Safe OOP (Java) | Call by Value (of reference) | Eliminates pointer aliasing, preserves encapsulation | | Real-Time Systems (Ada) | Call by Value-Result | Deterministic copy semantics, no hidden aliasing hazards | > [!TIP] > **Real-world note:** In Linux kernel programming, the **`container_of` macro** exploits *Call by Address* semantics to reverse-engineer struct pointers — a classic interview question derived from this module. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Trace Matrix — The Classic "Swap" Problem The `swap(a, b)` problem is the canonical KTU examination testbed because it **visually differentiates** every parameter passing mechanism. Let us construct an exhaustive trace. **Initial State:** $$a = 5, \quad b = 10$$ #### Case 1: Call by Value ```c void swap(int x, int y) { int t = x; x = y; y = t; } ``` **Step-by-Step Trace:** - **Activation record of `swap`** receives copies: $x_{\text{local}} = 5$, $y_{\text{local}} = 10$. - $t = x_{\text{local}} = 5$. - $x_{\text{local}} = y_{\text{local}} = 10$. - $y_{\text{local}} = t = 5$. - On return, stack frame is **popped and discarded**. - Caller's original values remain: $a = 5$, $b = 10$. **Swap fails.** #### Case 2: Call by Address (C with Pointers) ```c void swap(int *x, int *y) { int t = *x; *x = *y; *y = t; } ``` **Step-by-Step Trace:** - Push addresses: $x = \&a$, $y = \&b$. - $t = *x = *( \&a ) = 5$. - $*x = *y \Rightarrow *( \&a ) = *( \&b ) = 10 \Rightarrow a = 10$. - $*y = t = 5 \Rightarrow *( \&b ) = 5 \Rightarrow b = 5$. - Final state: $a = 10$, $b = 5$. **Swap succeeds.** #### Case 3: Call by Reference (Pascal-Style Pseudocode) ```pascal procedure swap(var x, y: integer); var t: integer; begin t := x; x := y; y := t end; ``` - Formal parameters `x` and `y` are **compile-time aliases** for `a` and `b`. - All writes are direct: $a \leftarrow 10$, $b \leftarrow 5$. - Final state: $a = 10$, $b = 5$. **Swap succeeds**, with zero pointer syntax. #### Case 4: Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore) **Activation:** - Copy-in: $x_{\text{local}} \leftarrow a = 5$, $y_{\text{local}} \leftarrow b = 10$. - Swap locally: $x_{\text{local}} = 10$, $y_{\text{local}} = 5$. - Copy-out at return: $a \leftarrow x_{\text{local}} = 10$, $b \leftarrow y_{\text{local}} = 5$. - Final state: $a = 10$, $b = 5$. **Swap succeeds.** --- ### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Aliasing and the "Jensen's Device" (Call by Name) **Jensen's Device** is the textbook KTU illustration of Call by Name, originally from Algol 60. Consider summation: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Real function signature (Algol 60):} \quad &\texttt{procedure sum(i, lo, hi, term);} \\ &\quad \texttt{value lo, hi;} \\ &\quad \texttt{integer i, lo, hi;} \\ &\quad \texttt{real term;} \\ &\quad \texttt{begin real acc;} \\ &\quad \texttt{acc := 0;} \\ &\quad \texttt{for i := lo step 1 until hi do acc := acc + term;} \\ &\quad \texttt{sum := acc} \\ &\quad \texttt{end;} \end{aligned}$$ **Call site:** $$\texttt{sum(i, 1, 100, a[i])}$$ **Derivation of Execution Semantics:** 1. At call time, the **expression** `a[i]` is **not evaluated**. Instead, a **thunk** $\tau$ is created that, when invoked, computes `a[i]` using the **caller's current value of `i`**. 2. Inside the loop, each access to `term` triggers $\tau$: $$acc_k = acc_{k-1} + \tau(i_k) = acc_{k-1} + a[i_k]$$ 3. Since $i$ is incremented **inside the same scope** where `term` references it, `a[i]` is re-evaluated *with the latest `i`* at every iteration. 4. The final accumulated value is: $$\text{sum} = \sum_{k=1}^{100} a[k]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this is significant:** Call by Name enables *true procedural abstraction* — the same summation routine works for `a[i]`, `a[i]*a[i]`, or `1/a[i]`, with the expression re-evaluated lazily. This is the conceptual ancestor of **lambda functions** and **lazy evaluation** in modern functional programming. --- ### 3.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Table (Analytical Matrix) | Test Scenario | Call by Value | Call by Reference | Call by Address | Call by Value-Result | Call by Name | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Swap succeeds? | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Aliasing possible? | No | Yes | Yes (via ptr) | Transient | No (re-eval) | | Concurrent update visible? | No | Yes | Yes | No (snapshot) | Yes (latest) | | Syntax burden | None | None | High (`*`, `&`) | None | Hidden (thunk) | | Implementation cost | Low | Medium | Medium | High (2 copies) | Highest | | Predictability | Highest | Medium | Medium | Medium | Lowest | | Used in | C primitives | Pascal, Fortran | C, C++ | Ada | Algol 60 | --- ### 3.4 Worked Example — Trace with `Call by Reference` (Fortran-style) **Problem:** Given the C-equivalent pseudocode using Pass-by-Reference semantics, predict the output. ```fortran SUBROUTINE INC(X, Y) X = X + 1 Y = Y + 2 END SUBROUTINE PROGRAM MAIN A = 5 B = 10 CALL INC(A, B) PRINT *, A, B END PROGRAM ``` **Trace:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Before call:} \quad & A = 5, \quad B = 10 \\ \text{Call INC(A, B):} \quad & X \equiv A, \quad Y \equiv B \quad \text{(aliasing)} \\ \text{Line 1: } X = X + 1 \quad &\Rightarrow A = A + 1 = 6 \\ \text{Line 2: } Y = Y + 2 \quad &\Rightarrow B = B + 2 = 12 \\ \text{After call:} \quad & A = 6, \quad B = 12 \end{aligned}$$ **Output:** `6 12` --- ### 3.5 Worked Example — Pointer-based Call by Address (C) ```c #include void update(int *p, int n) { *p = *p * n; n = n + 1; } int main(void) { int a = 4, b = 3; update(&a, b); printf("a=%d b=%d\n", a, b); return 0; } ``` **Trace:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Before call:} \quad & a = 4, \quad b = 3 \\ \text{Call update(\&a, b):} \quad & p = \&a, \quad n = b = 3 \quad \text{(local copy of } b \text{)} \\ \text{Line 1: } *p = *p * n \quad &\Rightarrow a = a \times 3 = 12 \\ \text{Line 2: } n = n + 1 \quad &\Rightarrow n_{\text{local}} = 4 \quad \text{(only local!)} \\ \text{After call:} \quad & a = 12, \quad b = 3 \end{aligned}$$ **Output:** `a=12 b=3` > [!NOTE] > Notice that **modifying `n` inside the callee does NOT affect `b` in the caller**, because `n` is itself a local copy of `b` (Call by Value of the int `b`). Only `*p` modifies the caller's `a`. This is a *mixed semantics* test case that KTU examiners love. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Activation Record (Stack Frame) Layout for Each Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CallerStack["CALLER ACTIVATION RECORD"] A1["a = 5
b = 10"] end subgraph CallByValue["CALLEE FRAME — Call by Value"] V1["x = 5 (COPY)"] V2["y = 10 (COPY)"] end subgraph CallByRef["CALLEE FRAME — Call by Reference"] R1["x -> ALIAS to a"] R2["y -> ALIAS to b"] end subgraph CallByAddr["CALLEE FRAME — Call by Address"] P1["ptr_x = &a"] P2["ptr_y = &b"] end A1 -. "push r-values" .-> V1 A1 -. "push r-values" .-> V2 A1 -. "push l-values (aliases)" .-> R1 A1 -. "push l-values (aliases)" .-> R2 A1 -. "push addresses" .-> P1 A1 -. "push addresses" .-> P2 V1 -. "NO writeback on return" .-> A1 V2 -. "NO writeback on return" .-> A1 R1 -. "writes propagate live" .-> A1 R2 -. "writes propagate live" .-> A1 P1 -. "writes via deref" .-> A1 P2 -. "writes via deref" .-> A1 ``` --- ### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing a Parameter Passing Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Function Definition"]) --> Q1{"Does callee need to
modify caller's data?"} Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Is the argument
large (array, struct)?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q3{"Does the language
support references?"} Q2 -- "No (small primitive)" --> V["Call by VALUE"] Q2 -- "Yes (large object)" --> C["Call by CONST REFERENCE
or POINTER"] Q3 -- "Yes (C++, Pascal)" --> R["Call by REFERENCE"] Q3 -- "No (pure C, Java)" --> A["Call by ADDRESS
(pointer)"] R --> End(["Implementation Selected"]) A --> End V --> End C --> End ``` --- ### 4.3 Lifecycle Diagram — Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller participant Stack as Stack Frame Caller->>Stack: Entry: COPY IN a=5, b=10 to x, y Note over Stack: x=5, y=10 (local copies) Stack->>Stack: Swap locally: x=10, y=5 Note over Stack: x=10, y=5 (still local) Stack-->>Caller: Exit: COPY OUT a<-x=10, b<-y=5 Note over Caller: a=10, b=5 (now modified) ``` --- ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Jensen's Device (Call by Name) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CallSite["Call Site"] Expr["Expression: a[i]
(NOT evaluated yet)"] Lo["lo = 1"] Hi["hi = 100"] end subgraph Thunk["THUNK (Closure)"] T["term := lookup a at caller's current i"] end subgraph SumProc["SUM Procedure Body"] Loop["for i := lo step 1 until hi"] Acc["acc := acc + term"] end Expr --> Thunk Lo --> SumProc Hi --> SumProc SumProc -->|each iteration| Thunk Thunk -->|returns a[i]| Acc Acc --> SumProc ``` --- ### 4.5 Side-by-Side Memory Architecture Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["Call by Value Memory"] A1["Caller: a=5"] A2["Callee stack: x=5
(INDEPENDENT COPY)"] end subgraph B["Call by Reference Memory"] B1["Caller: a=5"] B2["Callee stack: x -> POINTER to a"] end subgraph C["Call by Address Memory"] C1["Caller: a=5"] C2["Callee stack: ptr=&a"] end A1 --- A2 B1 -. "alias arrow" .-> B2 C1 -. "address arrow" .-> C2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagrammatic Reading for KTU Answers:** When answering "Explain with diagram" type 7-mark questions, the examiner expects a **stack frame illustration** showing (a) caller's local variables, (b) callee's formal parameters, and (c) an explicit arrow indicating copy, alias, or address semantics. The diagrams above are exam-grade blueprints. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between **Call by Value** and **Call by Reference**. Mention one programming language that supports each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Call by Value | Call by Reference | |---|---|---| | What is passed? | A **copy** of the argument's value | An **alias / address** of the argument | | Modification visibility | Local to callee only | Immediately reflected in caller | | Safety | High (no aliasing) | Lower (aliasing hazards) | | Example language | C (default for primitives) | Pascal (`var` keyword), Fortran | > **Example:** In C, `int add(int a, int b)` uses Call by Value; in Pascal, `procedure add(var a, b: integer)` uses Call by Reference. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** What is **Call by Address**? How is it different from **Call by Reference**? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Call by Address:** The **memory address** of the actual argument is explicitly passed, and the formal parameter is a **pointer variable** that the programmer must dereference (`*ptr`) to read/write the caller's variable. It is the default mechanism in C/C++ when the programmer uses `&` and `*`. - **Difference:** Call by Reference (Pascal, Fortran) is a **language-managed alias** requiring **no explicit dereferencing syntax**. Call by Address requires the programmer to handle pointer arithmetic and dereferencing manually. - **Trade-off:** Call by Address offers fine-grained control but introduces risks of dangling pointers, null dereferences, and memory leaks. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Mistake 1:** Confusing "Call by Reference" with "Call by Address" — these are **NOT** the same. Lose **2 marks**. > - **Mistake 2:** Writing "Java is Call by Reference" — **INCORRECT**. Java is *always* Call by Value, even for object references (the reference is copied). Lose **1–2 marks**. > - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting to draw the **activation record / stack frame** in 14-mark trace questions. Lose **2–3 marks**. > - **Mistake 4:** Not tracing the output line-by-line. Examiners award partial marks for correct intermediate steps. Always show **at least 3 trace steps**. > - **Mistake 5:** Stating "C uses Call by Reference" without qualification. The correct statement is *"C uses Call by Value by default; Call by Address is achieved by explicitly passing `&variable`."* Lose **1 mark**. --- #### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]` **Q. (a)** Explain **Call by Value** and **Call by Reference** parameter passing techniques with suitable C and Pascal examples. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Call by Value:** - A copy of the actual argument's value is passed to the formal parameter. - Modifications inside the callee do **not** affect the caller's variable. - Used by default in C for all primitive types. ```c #include void increment(int x) { x = x + 1; printf("Inside: x = %d\n", x); } int main(void) { int a = 5; increment(a); printf("Outside: a = %d\n", a); return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Inside: x = 6 Outside: a = 5 ``` **Call by Reference (Pascal):** - The formal parameter becomes an **alias** of the actual argument. - Modifications inside the callee are **immediately visible** to the caller. - Implemented via the `var` keyword in Pascal. ```pascal program example; var a: integer; procedure increment(var x: integer); begin x := x + 1; writeln('Inside: x = ', x) end; begin a := 5; increment(a); writeln('Outside: a = ', a) end. ``` **Output:** ``` Inside: x = 6 Outside: a = 6 ``` **Comparison (2 marks):** | Feature | Call by Value | Call by Reference | |---|---|---| | Safety | Safe, no side effects | Risk of unintended modification | | Performance | Costly for large data (copy overhead) | Efficient (no copy) | | Readability | Clear data flow | Hidden aliasing | **(7 marks total)** — Awarded: Definition (2), C example (2), Pascal example (2), Comparison (1). --- **Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **activation record layout** for a function called using **Call by Address**. Write a C program to swap two integers using this mechanism and trace the output. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Activation Record Diagram (2 marks):** ``` HIGH ADDRESS +-------------------+ | Return address | +-------------------+ | ptr_x = &a | <-- callee's formal parameter +-------------------+ | ptr_y = &b | <-- callee's formal parameter +-------------------+ LOW ADDRESS CALLER'S ENVIRONMENT +-------------------+ | a = 5 | +-------------------+ | b = 10 | +-------------------+ ``` **C Program (3 marks):** ```c #include void swap(int *x, int *y) { int t; t = *x; // t = 5 *x = *y; // a = 10 *y = t; // b = 5 printf("Inside swap: *x=%d, *y=%d\n", *x, *y); } int main(void) { int a = 5, b = 10; printf("Before swap: a=%d, b=%d\n", a, b); swap(&a, &b); printf("After swap: a=%d, b=%d\n", a, b); return 0; } ``` **Trace (2 marks):** | Step | Operation | a | b | *x | *y | t | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Initial | 5 | 10 | — | — | — | | 2 | `swap(&a, &b)` called | 5 | 10 | 5 | 10 | — | | 3 | `t = *x` | 5 | 10 | 5 | 10 | 5 | | 4 | `*x = *y` | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 5 | | 5 | `*y = t` | 10 | 5 | 10 | 5 | 5 | **Output:** ``` Before swap: a=5, b=10 Inside swap: *x=10, *y=5 After swap: a=10, b=5 ``` **(7 marks total)** --- #### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **Call by Value-Result** parameter passing mechanism with an example. How does it differ from **Call by Reference**, and why is it preferred in certain real-time systems? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** Call by Value-Result (also called *Copy-Restore* or *Call by Copy-In Copy-Out*) is a hybrid mechanism where: 1. **At entry**, the actual argument's value is copied into the formal parameter (like Call by Value). 2. **During execution**, the callee operates exclusively on its local copy. 3. **At return**, the final value of the formal parameter is copied back to the actual argument's storage location. **Example (3 marks):** ```c /* Simulating Value-Result semantics */ void increment(int x) { x = x + 10; /* Final value of x will be written back to a on return */ } int main(void) { int a = 5; increment(a); /* a is now 15 */ } ``` **Pseudocode Trace:** | Phase | a (caller) | x (callee copy) | |---|---|---| | Entry | 5 | 5 (copied from a) | | During | 5 | 15 (after `x = x + 10`) | | Return | 15 (restored from x) | discarded | **Difference from Call by Reference (1 mark):** - In Call by Reference, modifications are **immediately visible** during execution (live aliasing). - In Call by Value-Result, modifications are **invisible during execution**; they propagate only at return time. This eliminates aliasing hazards in concurrent contexts. **Why Preferred in Real-Time Systems (1 mark):** Real-time and safety-critical systems (avionics, automotive ECU software) prefer Call by Value-Result (e.g., Ada `in out`) because: - **Determinism:** No hidden aliasing between caller and callee. - **Composability:** Side-effect analysis is straightforward — no "spooky action at a distance." - **Verification:** Easier to formally verify using Hoare logic / SPARK Ada. **(7 marks total)** --- **Q. (b)** With reference to Algol 60, explain the **Call by Name** parameter passing mechanism. Illustrate with **Jensen's Device** and show how the expression `a[i]` is re-evaluated in the caller's scope. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** Call by Name is a **lazy-binding** mechanism where the actual argument is passed as an **unevaluated expression** (a thunk). Each access to the formal parameter **re-evaluates** the original expression in the caller's current environment. **Jensen's Device (3 marks):** ```algol procedure sum(i, lo, hi, term); value lo, hi; integer i, lo, hi; real term; begin real acc; acc := 0; for i := lo step 1 until hi do acc := acc + term; sum := acc end; ``` **Call:** ```algol sum(i, 1, 100, a[i]); ``` **Step-by-Step Derivation of Re-Evaluation (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{At call:} \quad & \text{thunk } \tau \leftarrow \lambda.\, a[i] \text{ (capturing caller's } i \text{)} \\ \text{Iteration 1 (i=1):} \quad & acc_1 = 0 + \tau(i=1) = 0 + a[1] \\ \text{Iteration 2 (i=2):} \quad & acc_2 = acc_1 + \tau(i=2) = a[1] + a[2] \\ & \vdots \\ \text{Iteration 100 (i=100):} \quad & acc_{100} = a[1] + a[2] + \dots + a[100] \end{aligned}$$ **Key Insight:** Every access to `term` re-invokes `τ`, which reads `a[i]` with the **latest** value of `i` from the caller's scope. The expression is *never* evaluated at call time — only at point of use. **(7 marks total)** --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3** - ✅ **Six mechanisms** to memorize: Value, Reference, Address, Value-Result, Result, Name. - ✅ **C defaults** to Call by Value; Pointer-based Call by Address requires `&` and `*`. - ✅ **Java is *strictly* Call by Value** — object references are passed *by value of the reference* (NOT Call by Reference). - ✅ **Fortran** traditionally uses Call by Reference; modern Fortran uses `intent(in)` / `intent(out)` / `intent(inout)` attributes. - ✅ **Pascal** uses `var` for Call by Reference and default for Call by Value. - ✅ **Ada** explicitly classifies parameters: `in` (Value), `out` (Result), `in out` (Value-Result). - ✅ **Call by Name** is a historical Algol 60 concept; modern descendants include **lazy evaluation** in Haskell (`call by need` with memoization). - ✅ **Activation record / stack frame diagrams** are mandatory for full marks in 7-mark comparison questions. - ✅ **The Swap problem** is the universal testbed — know all four traces (Value fails, Reference succeeds, Address succeeds, Value-Result succeeds). - ✅ **Aliasing** = multiple names for the same storage; occurs in Reference, Address, and (transiently) Value-Result. - ✅ **Determinism vs. Efficiency trade-off:** Reference is fast but risky; Value is safe but costly for large data. - ✅ For **14-mark questions**, always include: (1) definition, (2) diagram, (3) code, (4) trace table, (5) comparative conclusion. - ✅ **Examiner's keyword triggers:** "side effect," "aliasing," "activation record," "copy-restore," "thunk," "re-evaluation," "determinism."

topics.title: Parameters that are Subprograms
topics.content:
