> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Parameters that are Subprograms # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Advanced Computer Programming Techniques** (per KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBMT746, Module 3), a **Subprogram Parameter** refers to a formal parameter of a subprogram (procedure or function) whose corresponding actual parameter is itself another subprogram. This is a direct implementation of the **First-Class Function** paradigm, where subprograms can be passed as arguments, returned as results, stored in variables, and constructed at runtime. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *parameter that is a subprogram* is a formal parameter declared with a subprogram profile, allowing the called subprogram to invoke the passed subprogram indirectly. The actual parameter must match the parameter profile's signature (name, number, order, and types of parameters, plus return type for functions). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you run a **restaurant kitchen** (the *calling subprogram*). Instead of fixing the recipe, you tell your chef: *"Tonight, I might give you a recipe for Biryani or a recipe for Pasta — I'll hand you the recipe card when you start cooking."* - The **recipe card** = the *subprogram* being passed as a parameter. - The **cooking process** = the *calling subprogram*. - The **chef** doesn't need to know the recipe in advance — they just follow whatever card is handed over, as long as the format (ingredients list, steps) matches the kitchen's standard. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** A subprogram parameter allows one function/procedure to *receive another function/procedure* as input, enabling highly flexible, generic, and reusable code — the foundation of **callback functions, event handlers, and functional programming**. ### 1.3 Types of Subprogram Parameters (KTU-Standard Classification) | # | Type | Description | |---|------|-------------| | 1 | **Formal Subprogram Parameters** | Declared as subprograms inside the parameter list of another subprogram | | 2 | **Default Parameters** | Subprograms that supply a default actual parameter if one is omitted | | 3 | **Keyword Parameters** | Actual parameters matched to formal parameters by name, not position | | 4 | **Optional Parameters** | Parameters that may be omitted during the call | | 5 | **Variable Number of Parameters (Variadic)** | Subprogram accepts an arbitrary number of actual parameters | > [!TIP] > The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **Formal Subprogram Parameters** and **Default Parameters** as primary learning outcomes. Master these two for guaranteed marks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Formal Subprogram Parameters A **formal subprogram parameter** is itself declared as a subprogram. The passed actual subprogram must have a **compatible parameter profile and, for functions, a compatible return type**. **Generalized Syntax (Ada-style KTU Reference):** ```ada procedure Outer ( Formal_Sub : in procedure (X : in Integer; Y : out Float) ); ``` **Step-by-Step Operational Logic:** - **Step 1 — Declaration:** The outer subprogram declares the formal parameter with a complete subprogram profile. - **Step 2 — Invocation:** When `Outer` is called, the caller passes the *name* of an existing subprogram whose profile exactly matches. - **Step 3 — Binding:** The formal parameter is **bound** to the actual subprogram for the duration of the call. - **Step 4 — Indirect Call:** Inside `Outer`, the formal subprogram parameter is invoked like a regular subprogram call. - **Step 5 — Return:** On exit, the binding is released. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** This is the *only* way in older languages (like Ada-83) to pass behavior into a subprogram without using object-oriented polymorphism. It is the ancestor of modern **lambda functions** and **function pointers** in C/C++. ### 2.1.2 Parameter Profile Matching Rules For the actual subprogram to be a valid match, the following must hold: $$ \text{Match} \iff \begin{cases} \text{Name} = \text{Don't Care (positional)} \\ \text{Number of Parameters} = \text{Identical} \\ \text{Order of Parameters} = \text{Identical} \\ \text{Types of Parameters} = \text{Identical or Subtype-Equivalent} \\ \text{Mode (in/out/in out)} = \text{Compatible} \\ \text{Return Type (functions)} = \text{Identical} \end{cases} $$ ### 2.1.3 Default Parameters **Default parameters** are those for which the formal parameter declaration includes a *default expression* used when the caller omits the actual parameter. **Operational Logic:** - **Step 1 — Define Default:** A default value/expression is bound to the formal parameter at declaration time. - **Step 2 — Detect Omission:** If the caller provides fewer actual parameters, the compiler/runtime substitutes defaults for the trailing omitted ones. - **Step 3 — Evaluate Default:** The default expression is evaluated in the caller's scope (most languages) or the callee's scope (Ada). - **Step 4 — Pass Forward:** The evaluated default is then passed as if it were a normal actual parameter. > [!WARNING] > In KTU exams, students often confuse *default* with *optional*. **Optional = may or may not be passed; Default = has a fallback value when not passed.** They are related but distinct concepts. ### 2.1.4 Keyword (Named) Parameters In keyword parameter passing, actual parameters are tagged with the *name* of their corresponding formal parameter, decoupling order from the call site. **Example Syntax:** ```python def Build(Name, Age, City): # declaration pass Build(City="Kochi", Age=22, Name="Anu") # keyword call ``` ### 2.1.5 Variable Number of Parameters (Variadic Functions) Variadic subprograms accept an **arbitrary number of arguments** using ellipsis (`...`) or packing arguments into a sequence/array. **Generalized Form:** $$ \text{Signature} : f(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n, \ldots : V) \rightarrow R $$ Where $V$ is the variadic tail collecting zero or more extra values of compatible type. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Syntax/Rule | Real-World Engineering Use | |---|---------|-------------|----------------------------| | 1 | Subprogram Parameter Declaration | `formal: in subprogram_profile` | Numerical integration routines that accept any integrand | | 2 | Parameter Profile Match | Same #, order, type, mode | Compiler-level type-checking for callback registration | | 3 | Default Parameter | `formal: in T := Default_Value` | GUI library default styling (e.g., `Button(label="OK", color="Blue")`) | | 4 | Keyword Parameter | `Call(name1 => val1, name2 => val2)` | API design for self-documenting calls (HTTP request builders) | | 5 | Optional Parameter | `formal: in T := NULL` or `?` marker | Configuration functions with optional overrides | | 6 | Variadic Parameter | `formal: in T...` | Logging (`log("Error:", code, msg, ts)`), `printf` formatting | | 7 | First-Class Function | `var f := procedure(x); end;` | Event listeners, map/filter/reduce, dependency injection | | 8 | Closure Capture | Inner function captures outer-scope variable | React hooks, callback factories, debouncing functions | | 9 | Currying | `f(a)(b)(c)` via partial application | Functional pipelines, point-free style DSLs | | 10 | Higher-Order Function | A function that takes/returns functions | `map`, `filter`, `reduce` in every modern language | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems (C):** Function pointers are passed to interrupt service routines and state machines. - **Web Frameworks (Python/JS):** Express middleware, React event handlers, Django ORM querysets — all use subprogram parameters. - **Numerical Computing (Fortran/MATLAB):** Integrators (`quad`, `ode45`) accept the integrand as a function handle. - **GUI Toolkits (Java/C#):** `ActionListener` and `EventHandler` are essentially subprogram parameters under the hood. - **AI/ML (Python):** Callbacks for early stopping, learning rate schedulers, and custom loss functions in TensorFlow/PyTorch. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks for a "real-world example," think of one numerical, one GUI, and one systems-level use case to demonstrate breadth. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Formal Subprogram Parameter (Ada-Style Pseudocode) **Problem Statement:** Design a generic numerical procedure `Integrate` that accepts an arbitrary function $f(x)$ and computes $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ using the trapezoidal rule. **Step 1 — Declare the Outer Procedure with a Subprogram Parameter** ```ada procedure Integrate ( F : in function (X : Float) return Float; -- subprogram parameter A, B : in Float; -- regular parameters N : in Integer := 100; -- default parameter Result : out Float ); ``` **Step 2 — Derive the Trapezoidal Rule Formula** The trapezoidal rule approximates the integral as a sum of $N$ trapezoids of equal width. $$ h = \frac{b - a}{N} $$ The integral is approximated as: $$ \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\Bigl[f(a) + 2\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} f(a + i \cdot h) + f(b)\Bigr] $$ **Step 3 — Translate to Subprogram Logic** ```ada procedure Integrate(F, A, B, N, Result) is H : Float := (B - A) / Float(N); Sum : Float := (F(A) + F(B)) / 2.0; -- F is invoked as a regular call begin for I in 1 .. N-1 loop Sum := Sum + F(A + Float(I) * H); end loop; Result := H * Sum; end Integrate; ``` **Step 4 — Calling the Procedure with a Concrete Subprogram** ```ada function Sin_X(X : Float) return Float is begin return Math.Sin(X); end Sin_X; Integrate(F => Sin_X'Access, A => 0.0, B => 3.14159, Result => Area); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice the keyword call syntax `F => Sin_X'Access`. This is a *named actual parameter* matched to the formal `F`. The `'Access` attribute is Ada's mechanism for obtaining a *reference* to the subprogram. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Default Parameters (Python Illustration) **Step 1 — Declare with Defaults** ```python def Send_Notification(Message, Recipient="Admin", Channel="Email", Priority=1): print(f"To: {Recipient} | Channel: {Channel} | Priority: {Priority} | Msg: {Message}") ``` **Step 2 — Calls with Varying Numbers of Arguments** ```python Send_Notification("Server Down") # Uses all 3 defaults Send_Notification("Login Failed", "Anu") # Override Recipient Send_Notification("Backup Done", Channel="SMS") # Keyword parameter for Channel ``` **Step 3 — Evaluation Order of Defaults** Default expressions are evaluated **left-to-right at call time**, in the **caller's namespace**, in the order they are needed. $$ \text{DefaultEvalOrder} : \text{TrailingLeftmostSkipped} \rightarrow \text{Evaluate} \rightarrow \text{Bind} $$ **Trace Table:** | Call | `Message` | `Recipient` | `Channel` | `Priority` | |------|-----------|-------------|-----------|------------| | 1 | "Server Down" | "Admin" (default) | "Email" (default) | 1 (default) | | 2 | "Login Failed" | "Anu" (overridden) | "Email" (default) | 1 (default) | | 3 | "Backup Done" | "Admin" (default) | "SMS" (overridden) | 1 (default) | ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Closures and Captured Variables (JavaScript Illustration) **Step 1 — A Counter Factory Using a Subprogram Parameter** ```javascript function Make_Counter(Step = 1) { // Step is a default parameter let Count = 0; // outer-scope variable return function() { // inner subprogram (closure) Count += Step; // captures Count and Step return Count; }; } let C1 = Make_Counter(); let C2 = Make_Counter(5); console.log(C1(), C1(), C2(), C2()); // 1, 2, 5, 10 ``` **Step 2 — Mathematical Representation of a Closure** A closure $C$ can be modeled as a tuple: $$ C = ( \text{Code}, \text{Environment} ) $$ Where $\text{Environment}$ is a frozen snapshot of the variable bindings in the lexical scope at the time the closure was created. **Step 3 — Show the Binding Table** | Closure | Captured Variable | Initial Value | Final Value After 2 Calls | |---------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------------| | $C_1$ | `Count` | 0 | 2 | | $C_1$ | `Step` | 1 | 1 (unchanged) | | $C_2$ | `Count` | 0 | 10 | | $C_2$ | `Step` | 5 | 5 (unchanged) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Each invocation of `Make_Counter` creates a *new* environment. Therefore, `C1` and `C2` are independent closures with independent `Count` variables — a direct consequence of **lexical scoping**. ## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Variadic Functions **Step 1 — C-style Declaration** ```c int Sum(int Count, ...); ``` **Step 2 — Internally, the variadic arguments are accessed via the `` macros** ```c #include int Sum(int Count, ...) { va_list Args; va_start(Args, Count); int Total = 0; for (int i = 0; i < Count; i++) { Total += va_arg(Args, int); } va_end(Args); return Total; } ``` **Step 3 — Mathematical Model of Variadic Invocation** $$ \text{Sum}(n, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i $$ Where $n$ is the first explicit parameter (Count), and $x_1 \ldots x_n$ are the variadic tail. **Step 4 — Trace Table** | Call | Count | Variadic Args | Computed Sum | |------|-------|---------------|--------------| | `Sum(3, 10, 20, 30)` | 3 | 10, 20, 30 | 60 | | `Sum(5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5)` | 5 | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 | 15 | | `Sum(0)` | 0 | (none) | 0 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Subprogram Parameter Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD caller["Caller Scope
Main Subprogram"] actual1["Actual Subprogram 1
Sin_X"] actual2["Actual Subprogram 2
Cos_X"] actual3["Actual Subprogram 3
Exp_X"] outer["Outer Subprogram
Integrate"] formal["Formal Subprogram Parameter
F : in function Float -> Float"] bind["Binding Mechanism
Profile Match Check"] invkA["Invoke F at a"] invkB["Invoke F at a+h"] invkC["Invoke F at a+2h"] invkD["Invoke F at b"] result["Compute Result
h * Sigma"] caller --> actual1 caller --> actual2 caller --> actual3 actual1 --> bind actual2 --> bind actual3 --> bind bind --> formal formal --> outer outer --> invkA outer --> invkB outer --> invkC outer --> invkD invkA --> result invkB --> result invkC --> result invkD --> result ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The caller chooses among multiple candidate subprograms. Only those whose **profile** matches the formal parameter `F` are accepted by the binding mechanism. Once bound, `Integrate` invokes `F` indirectly at multiple points (the trapezoidal rule's sample points) and aggregates the results. ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Default Parameter Resolution ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller as Caller Subprogram participant Compiler as Compiler/Runtime participant DefaultPool as Default Expression Store participant Callee as Callee Subprogram Caller->>Compiler: Call Foo(Msg='Hi') Compiler->>Compiler: Detect missing args (Recipient, Channel, Priority) Compiler->>DefaultPool: Fetch default for Recipient DefaultPool-->>Compiler: 'Admin' Compiler->>DefaultPool: Fetch default for Channel DefaultPool-->>Compiler: 'Email' Compiler->>DefaultPool: Fetch default for Priority DefaultPool-->>Compiler: 1 Compiler->>Callee: Invoke Foo('Hi', 'Admin', 'Email', 1) Callee-->>Caller: Returns / Side Effects ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Profile Matching Decision ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> ReceiveActual ReceiveActual --> CheckName: name ignored CheckName --> CheckCount: pass CheckName --> Reject: fail CheckCount --> CheckOrder: count matches CheckOrder --> CheckTypes: order matches CheckOrder --> Reject: order mismatch CheckTypes --> CheckModes: types match CheckTypes --> Reject: type mismatch CheckModes --> CheckReturn: modes compatible CheckModes --> Reject: mode conflict CheckReturn --> Bind: return type matches CheckReturn --> Reject: return type mismatch Bind --> [*] Reject --> [*] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **decision tree a KTU examiner expects** when you draw a profile-matching flowchart. Memorize the six checks in order: **Name → Count → Order → Type → Mode → Return Type**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between a formal subprogram parameter and a default parameter with a suitable example.* **Model Answer:** A **formal subprogram parameter** is a parameter of a subprogram whose type is itself a subprogram. It allows the caller to pass a procedure or function as an argument, enabling the callee to invoke the passed subprogram indirectly. *Example:* A numerical `Integrate(F)` routine that accepts any function `F` whose profile matches `Float -> Float`. A **default parameter** is a formal parameter that has a pre-defined fallback value used when the caller does not supply a corresponding actual argument. *Example:* `Send_Alert(Msg, Channel="Email")` — if the caller omits `Channel`, `"Email"` is used automatically. | Aspect | Formal Subprogram Parameter | Default Parameter | |--------|----------------------------|-------------------| | Type | Subprogram | Scalar / structured type | | Purpose | Pass behavior | Provide fallback value | | Binding | Match by profile | Match by position or keyword | *(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for distinction table.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is a closure? Explain with a one-line example.* **Model Answer:** A **closure** is a first-class subprogram value that *captures and carries* the lexical environment (variable bindings) of the scope in which it was created, even after that scope has exited. **One-line example:** ```javascript const adder = (x) => (y) => x + y; // inner arrow fn captures x ``` Here, `(y) => x + y` is a closure that remembers the value of `x` from its enclosing scope. *(3 marks: 2 for definition, 1 for example.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only one example and skip the *comparison table* or the *trace*. KTU 2024 scheme awards **7 marks per sub-part**, and the standard split is **3 marks for definition/concept + 2 marks for example + 2 marks for diagram/table/trace**. Skipping the diagram/table forfeits 2 marks. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]** *Answer the following:* **(a)** Explain the concept of *parameters that are subprograms* in detail. Discuss the rules for parameter profile matching. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a suitable example in any high-level language, explain *default parameters* and *keyword parameters*. Show how they differ from positional parameters. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** A parameter that is a subprogram is a formal parameter whose *type is a subprogram*. This is the foundation of **higher-order functions** and **first-class functions**, and is essential for callback-driven designs (event listeners, numerical routines, map/filter/reduce). **Rules for Parameter Profile Matching:** For an actual subprogram to be a valid substitute, the compiler/runtime performs a sequence of six checks: 1. **Number of Parameters:** Exact match in count. 2. **Order of Parameters:** Parameters must appear in the same order (when bound positionally). 3. **Types of Parameters:** Each formal-actual pair must be type-compatible (identical or subtype-related). 4. **Mode Compatibility:** Modes (`in`, `out`, `in out`) must not conflict — an `in` formal cannot receive an `out` actual. 5. **Return Type (functions only):** The function's return type must be identical to the formal profile. 6. **Name (irrelevant):** Names are *not* compared; only the profile matters. **Illustration (Ada syntax):** ```ada procedure Process (Op : in procedure (X : in out Integer)) is begin Op(10); end Process; ``` **Trace of Profile Match for `Op => Increment'Access`:** | Check | Increment's Profile | Op's Profile | Result | |-------|---------------------|--------------|--------| | Count | 1 | 1 | ✅ | | Order | (X: in out Integer) | (X: in out Integer) | ✅ | | Type | Integer | Integer | ✅ | | Mode | in out | in out | ✅ | | Function Return | N/A | N/A | ✅ | | **Verdict** | — | — | **Accept** | *(7 marks: 3 for concept + 2 for rules + 2 for trace table.)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Default Parameters:** Parameters that have a pre-assigned value used if the caller omits them. ```python def Connect(Host, Port=8080, UseTLS=True, Timeout=30): print(f"Host={Host} Port={Port} TLS={UseTLS} Timeout={Timeout}") ``` **Keyword Parameters:** Actual parameters are tagged with the formal name, allowing arbitrary order. ```python Connect("api.example.com", Timeout=10, UseTLS=False) ``` **Positional vs Keyword vs Default — Comparative Matrix:** | Feature | Positional | Keyword | Default | |---------|------------|---------|---------| | Order matters | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | Must supply value | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Optional | | Readability | Lower | Highest | High | | Compiler support | Universal | Python, Ada, Fortran | Most modern languages | | Use case | Internal calls | Public API calls | Configuration with sensible defaults | **Trace of the Call Above:** | Formal | Actual Provided | Value Bound | |--------|----------------|-------------| | `Host` | "api.example.com" (positional) | "api.example.com" | | `Port` | (omitted → default) | 8080 | | `UseTLS` | False (keyword) | False | | `Timeout` | 10 (keyword) | 10 | *(7 marks: 2 for each concept + 1 for table + 2 for trace.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B]** *Answer the following:* **(a)** What are *first-class subprograms*? Explain with reference to subprogram parameters and closures. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design a procedure that uses a subprogram parameter to compute the area under a curve $f(x)$ over $[a,b]$ using Simpson's 1/3 rule. Show the integration formula and a sample trace. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** **First-Class Subprograms:** A subprogram is *first-class* in a language if it can be: - Assigned to a variable - Passed as a parameter to another subprogram - Returned as a result from a subprogram - Constructed at runtime (e.g., anonymous lambda) **Languages with first-class subprograms:** Python, JavaScript, Haskell, Lisp, Scala, Kotlin, C# (with delegates), modern C++ (with `std::function`). **Subprogram Parameters (Reiteration):** When a function `G` accepts a function `F` as a parameter, `F` is a *subprogram parameter*. This is the basis of higher-order functions like `map`, `filter`, `reduce`. **Closures:** A closure is a first-class subprogram paired with its lexical environment. It allows the function to "remember" the values of variables from the scope in which it was created. **Comprehensive Example (JavaScript):** ```javascript function Power(n) { return function(x) { return x ** n; }; // closure captures n } const Square = Power(2); const Cube = Power(3); console.log(Square(5), Cube(2)); // 25, 8 ``` **Comparative Table:** | Property | First-Class Subprogram | Closure | |----------|------------------------|---------| | Can be assigned to variable | ✅ | ✅ | | Can be passed as argument | ✅ | ✅ | | Can be returned | ✅ | ✅ | | Captures environment | ❌ (not necessarily) | ✅ (mandatory) | | Typical use | `map`, `filter` | Currying, factories | *(7 marks: 2 for definition + 2 for subprogram parameter link + 2 for closure + 1 for table.)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Simpson's 1/3 Rule Formula:** For an even number $N$ of subintervals on $[a, b]$, with step size $h = (b-a)/N$: $$ \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\Bigl[f(x_0) + 4f(x_1) + 2f(x_2) + 4f(x_3) + \cdots + 4f(x_{N-1}) + f(x_N)\Bigr] $$ In compact form: $$ \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\Biggl[f(x_0) + f(x_N) + 4\sum_{i=1,3,5,\ldots}^{N-1} f(x_i) + 2\sum_{i=2,4,6,\ldots}^{N-2} f(x_i)\Biggr] $$ **Code (Ada-style Pseudocode):** ```ada procedure Simpson_Integrate ( F : in function (X : Float) return Float; A, B : in Float; N : in Integer; -- N must be EVEN Result : out Float ) is H : Float := (B - A) / Float(N); Sum : Float := (F(A) + F(B)) / 2.0; begin for I in 1 .. N-1 loop if I mod 2 = 0 then Sum := Sum + 2.0 * F(A + Float(I) * H); -- even index: weight 2 else Sum := Sum + 4.0 * F(A + Float(I) * H); -- odd index: weight 4 end if; end loop; Result := (H / 3.0) * Sum; end Simpson_Integrate; ``` **Sample Trace — Compute $\int_0^2 x^2\,dx$ with $N=4$:** Step size: $h = (2-0)/4 = 0.5$ | $i$ | $x_i$ | $f(x_i)=x_i^2$ | Weight | Contribution | |-----|-------|----------------|--------|--------------| | 0 | 0.0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | | 1 | 0.5 | 0.25 | 4 | 1.0 | | 2 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 2 | 2.0 | | 3 | 1.5 | 2.25 | 4 | 9.0 | | 4 | 2.0 | 4.0 | 1 | 4.0 | $$ \text{Sum} = 0 + 1.0 + 2.0 + 9.0 + 4.0 = 16.0 $$ $$ \text{Result} = \frac{0.5}{3} \times 16.0 = \frac{8.0}{3} \approx 2.6667 $$ **Analytical Check:** $\int_0^2 x^2\,dx = \frac{x^3}{3}\Big|_0^2 = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.6667$ ✅ *(7 marks: 2 for formula + 2 for code + 2 for trace + 1 for verification.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 3: Parameters that are Subprograms** - **Definition:** A subprogram parameter is a formal parameter whose actual value is itself a subprogram. - **Parameter Profile Match:** Six checks — Name (ignored), Count, Order, Type, Mode, Return Type. - **First-Class Subprograms:** Can be assigned, passed, returned, and created at runtime. - **Default Parameters:** Provide a fallback value when the actual argument is omitted; evaluated at call time, left-to-right. - **Keyword Parameters:** Match by name rather than position; improve readability for public APIs. - **Optional Parameters:** May or may not be passed; typically implemented via default values or `NULL`/`None` sentinels. - **Variadic Functions:** Accept an arbitrary number of arguments via ellipsis (`...`); require a count or sentinel for safe traversal. - **Closures:** A first-class function + its captured lexical environment. Foundation of currying and partial application. - **Higher-Order Functions:** Functions that take or return functions — `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, `sort` with custom comparators. - **Profile Mismatch Handling:** Always check modes and types *before* invoking; mismatches cause compile-time errors in static languages, runtime exceptions in dynamic ones. - **Real-World Use Cases:** Numerical integration (pass integrand), event listeners (pass callback), GUI button clicks (pass handler), map/reduce pipelines. - **Exam Triggers:** If you see *"design a procedure that accepts another function"* → use **formal subprogram parameter**; if you see *"design an API with sensible defaults"* → use **default + keyword parameters**; if you see *"function that remembers state"* → use **closure**. - **Common Pitfall:** Confusing **default** with **optional** — defaults always have a value; optionals may have a `NULL`/`None` sentinel. - **Syntax Watch:** In Ada use `'Access`; in C use `&function_name`; in Python/JS just use the function name; in C++ use `std::function<...>`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Module 3 Notes — Parameters that are Subprograms.** > *You are now equipped to answer any 3-mark or 14-mark KTU question on this topic with definition + example + diagram/table + trace.*

topics.title: Calling Subprograms Indirectly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calling Subprograms Indirectly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **Advanced Computer Programming Techniques (PEBMT746)**, **Calling Subprograms Indirectly** is formally defined as a subroutine invocation mechanism in which the target subprogram is **not referenced by its declared identifier at the call site**, but instead is selected dynamically through a **pointer**, **reference**, **handle**, or **callable object** that is dereferenced at runtime to resolve the actual executable code path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Indirect subprogram invocation (also called indirect call, late binding, or callback-driven dispatch) is a mechanism where the called address is supplied at runtime through a variable of pointer/reference/callable type, enabling polymorphic behaviour, plug-in architectures, and higher-order functional programming constructs.*

The five principal forms recognised in the PEBMT746 syllabus are:

| # | Form | Native Language Construct | Bind Time |
|---|------|--------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Function Pointer** | `int (*fp)(int)` in C / C++ | Runtime |
| 2 | **Subroutine Reference** | `\&mysub` in Perl | Runtime |
| 3 | **First-Class Callable** | Lambdas, `def` objects in Python | Runtime |
| 4 | **Delegate / Closure** | `delegate` in C#, `lambda` in Java | Runtime |
| 5 | **Virtual Dispatch (vtable)** | `virtual` methods in C++/Java | Runtime |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a hotel reception desk and you want someone to come fix your room's air-conditioner. Instead of **directly calling the technician "Ramesh"** (a direct call), you tell the receptionist:

> *"Whoever is on duty today, send them up to room 304."*

The receptionist checks the **duty roster** (a *pointer* or *reference*), finds the available technician — say *Suresh* today, *Ramesh* tomorrow — and dispatches them. **You never hard-coded the name**; you used an *intermediary reference* that gets resolved **at the moment of the call**.

That intermediary reference is exactly what an **indirect call** is in programming: a *named slot* (variable, pointer, callable object) that holds the *address* of the actual function. When execution reaches the call site, the runtime looks up the slot, fetches the address, and jumps to it. **The slot's contents can change between calls, so the same call site can invoke different subprograms** at different times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Word Memory Hook (for KTU viva)**
> 1. **Pointer** – holds the address
> 2. **Dereference** – retrieve the function
> 3. **Invoke** – call through the slot

## 1.3 Why Indirect Calls Exist — Engineering Motivation

- **Decoupling** — the caller does not need to be recompiled when a new function is plugged in.
- **Polymorphism** — the same interface can trigger different behaviours (Strategy Pattern, Observer Pattern).
- **Callbacks** — library code (e.g. `qsort`, GUI event loops) can call back into *user-supplied* logic.
- **Higher-Order Functions** — functions that accept or return other functions (`map`, `filter`, `reduce`).
- **Plugin Architectures** — dynamically loaded modules register themselves into a function table.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Performance Metric (Bold Constant)**
> A direct call typically costs **~1–2 CPU cycles** for the `CALL` instruction. An indirect call costs **~5–20 cycles** due to an additional **pointer dereference** plus a potential **branch predictor miss**. Indirect-call-induced *CPU pipeline flushes* are responsible for **15–25 % of all branch mispredictions** in large C/C++ binaries (measured on x86-64 architectures, Intel/AMD).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — Six Logical Steps

An indirect subprogram call is executed by the runtime through the following sequence:

1. **Declaration Phase** — A variable of *pointer-to-function*, *reference-to-subroutine*, or *callable* type is declared, e.g. `int (*fp)(int);` in C.
2. **Binding Phase** — The address of an existing (named) subprogram is assigned to the variable: `fp = □`.
3. **Storage Phase** — The binding is held in the variable; the slot is *mutable*, so a different function can later overwrite it.
4. **Dereference Phase** — At the call site, the language runtime reads the address currently held in the variable.
5. **Invocation Phase** — Control is transferred to the dereferenced address with the supplied arguments.
6. **Return Phase** — Execution resumes at the instruction *after* the call site, identical to a direct call.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Step 4**
> Without dereferencing, the runtime would interpret the slot as data (a function-object) rather than as code. The dereference operator `(*fp)(x)` in C or the implicit call `fp(x)` in Perl/Python forces a transition from *data* to *executable address*.

## 2.2 Mechanism in Three Paradigmatic Languages

### 2.2.1 C / C++ — Function Pointers

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int square(int n)        { return n * n; }
int cube  (int n)        { return n * n * n; }

int main(void) {
    /* Declaration: pointer-to-function taking (int) -> int */
    int (*op)(int);

    op = square;          /* Binding */
    printf("%d\n", op(5)); /* Indirect call -> 25 */

    op = cube;            /* Rebinding */
    printf("%d\n", op(5)); /* Indirect call -> 125 */
    return 0;
}
```

### 2.2.2 Perl — Subroutine References

```perl
sub square { $_[0] * $_[0] }
sub cube   { $_[0] * $_[0] * $_[0] }

my $op = \□        # Take a reference
print $op->(5), "\n";     # Indirect call  -> 25

$op = \&cube;             # Rebind
print $op->(5), "\n";     # Indirect call  -> 125
```

### 2.2.3 Python — First-Class Functions (Higher-Order)

```python
def square(n): return n * n
def cube(n):   return n * n * n

op = square                # Function is a first-class object
print(op(5))               # Indirect call  -> 25

op = cube                  # Rebind to a different callable
print(op(5))               # Indirect call  -> 125
```

## 2.3 The "How" — Internal Memory Layout

When the compiler emits a function pointer, it stores a **machine-code address** (e.g. `0x00000000004011A6` on a 64-bit system). The pointer variable occupies **8 bytes** on a 64-bit platform. The dereference is therefore equivalent to a normal memory load followed by an indirect `JMP`/`CALL`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Runtime Address Resolution Cost (Bold Metrics)**
> - **Direct call:** 1 instruction (`CALL rel32`), ~**1 cycle** branch-predictor hit.
> - **Indirect call:** 2–3 instructions (`MOV reg, [addr]` + `CALL reg`), ~**5–15 cycles** if predicted, ~**20+ cycles** on misprediction (full pipeline flush of ~200 instructions in modern x86 pipelines).
> - **Virtual call (C++ vtable):** 1 indirect load + indirect call, ~**10–20 cycles** average, notorious for **I-cache pollution** in large object hierarchies.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Notation / Syntax | Memory Footprint | Typical Cost |
|---|---------|-------------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Function pointer (C) | `R (*fp)(args)` | 8 B (64-bit) | O(1) dereference + CALL |
| 2 | `typedef` for fp | `typedef R (*fp_t)(args);` | – | Compile-time alias |
| 3 | Array of fp (dispatch table) | `fp_t table[N];` | N × 8 B | O(1) table lookup |
| 4 | Subroutine ref (Perl) | `\&name` | Scalar (SV *) | O(1) |
| 5 | Anonymous sub (Perl) | `sub { ... }` | Closure CV | O(n) where n = captured vars |
| 6 | Lambda (Python) | `lambda x: x*x` | PyObject * | O(1) call, O(n) alloc |
| 7 | Bound method (Python) | `obj.method` | PyObject * | O(1) call |
| 8 | `std::function` (C++) | `std::function<R(args)>` | 16–32 B (small-buffer) | O(1) call, may heap-allocate |
| 9 | Delegate (C#) | `delegate R D(args);` | 8 B (method ptr) + 8 B (target) | O(1) |
| 10 | Closure capture size | $\sum \text{sizeof}(c_i)$ | Variable | O(k) where k = captured symbols |
| 11 | Branch misprediction penalty | $\text{cycles}_{\text{flush}}$ | – | $\approx \mathbf{15\text{–}20}$ cycles on x86-64 |
| 12 | Address-of operator | `&func` (C), `\&sub` (Perl) | – | O(1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Table Safety Note** — All vertical bars `|` inside cell contents have been replaced with `,` or the `;` separator to preserve table integrity. For mathematical absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux Kernel** — Uses function pointers extensively in `struct file_operations`, allowing each device driver to plug in its own `open()`, `read()`, `write()` implementations without changing the VFS layer.
- **GUI Frameworks** — Tkinter / Qt / GTK maintain *signal-to-slot* tables; user-defined Python callables are stored as PyObjects and invoked when widgets emit events.
- **Numerical Libraries** — `scipy.integrate.quad` accepts a user-supplied Python function as its first argument — a textbook higher-order function built on indirect call.
- **Web Servers** — Node.js / Express route handlers are stored in a hash table mapping URL patterns to *function references*, dispatched per request.
- **Operating Systems** — Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) are stored in the **Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)**; the CPU performs an *indirect* call to the handler pointed to by the interrupt vector.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Model of an Indirect Call

Let us model the runtime cost of executing an indirect call. Define:

- $T_d$ = time (in CPU cycles) for a **direct** call
- $T_i$ = time (in CPU cycles) for an **indirect** call when the branch predictor is correct
- $T_m$ = time (in CPU cycles) for an **indirect** call when the branch predictor is *wrong* (misprediction flush penalty)
- $p$ = probability of branch-predictor **hit** ($0 \le p \le 1$)
- $n$ = number of indirect call sites in a hot loop (e.g. inside a `qsort` comparator callback)

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Expected Cost per Indirect Call

The expected (mean) cost of a single indirect call is the weighted sum of hit and miss scenarios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T_{\text{indirect}}] &= p \cdot T_i \;+\; (1-p) \cdot T_m
\end{aligned}
$$

On a typical Intel Skylake-class core, we plug in the bold numerical constants:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_i &\approx \mathbf{5} \text{ cycles} \\
T_m &\approx \mathbf{20} \text{ cycles} \\
p &\approx \mathbf{0.85} \text{ (85\% prediction accuracy)} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow E[T_{\text{indirect}}] &= 0.85 \cdot 5 + 0.15 \cdot 20 \\
&= 4.25 + 3.00 \\
&= \mathbf{7.25 \text{ cycles}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Total Cost in a Hot Loop of Size $N$

If the loop iterates $N$ times and performs exactly **one** indirect call per iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}}(N) &= N \cdot E[T_{\text{indirect}}] \\
&= N \cdot (p \cdot T_i + (1-p) \cdot T_m)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N = 10^6$ (one million sort operations), the *amortised* cost becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}}(10^6) &= 10^6 \times 7.25 \\
&= \mathbf{7.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ cycles}} \\
&\approx \mathbf{2.42 \text{ ms}} \quad \text{on a } \mathbf{3.0 \text{ GHz}} \text{ CPU}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Cost Overhead vs. Direct Call

Define the **overhead ratio** $R$ as the fractional slowdown relative to a direct call ($T_d = 1$ cycle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{E[T_{\text{indirect}}]}{T_d} - 1 \\
&= \frac{7.25}{1} - 1 \\
&= \mathbf{6.25} \;\; (\text{i.e.\ a } 625\% \text{ overhead})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the KTU Examiner**
> The $625\%$ figure looks alarming, but remember that $T_d = 1$ cycle is the *idealised* direct-call cost. In real code, the called function itself executes thousands of cycles, so the **amortised overhead shrinks below 1\%** for any non-trivial subroutine. The analysis is still valuable for understanding why **devirtualisation** is a major compiler optimisation.

## 3.2 Worked Example — C qsort Comparator (Indirect Call in Action)

The C standard library function `qsort` performs **one indirect call per element comparison**. Below is the canonical usage and a runtime cost estimate.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int compare_ints(const void *a, const void *b) {
    return (*(const int *)a - *(const int *)b);
}

int main(void) {
    int arr[] = { 7, 2, 9, 1, 5 };
    size_t n = sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0]);

    /* Indirect call: qsort invokes compare_ints via a function pointer */
    qsort(arr, n, sizeof(int), compare_ints);

    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    putchar('\n');
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1** — `compare_ints` is passed as a pointer (`&compare_ints` implied by array-to-pointer decay of the function name).

**Step 2** — `qsort`'s internal loop, for each pair $(i, j)$, executes:

$$
\text{result} \;\leftarrow\; \text{call}_{\text{indirect}}(\text{comp}, \&\text{arr}[i], \&\text{arr}[j])
$$

**Step 3** — For $N = 5$ elements, the number of comparisons is $N \log_2 N \approx 5 \times 2.32 \approx 11.6$, so $qsort$ performs approximately **12 indirect calls** to `compare_ints`.

**Step 4** — Total indirect-call cost (using the derived formula):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sort}} &= N \log_2 N \cdot E[T_{\text{indirect}}] \\
&\approx 11.6 \times 7.25 \\
&\approx \mathbf{84 \text{ cycles}} \;\; \approx \mathbf{28 \text{ ns}} \;\; \text{on a } 3 \text{ GHz CPU}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Direct Call | C Function Pointer | C++ `std::function` | Perl Sub Ref | Python Callable |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|----------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Type safety** | Strong | Weak (void *) | Strong | Dynamic | Dynamic |
| **Allocates heap?** | No | No | Possibly (large closure) | Possibly | Yes (PyObject) |
| **Can rebind?** | No (constexpr) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Captures state?** | No | No | Yes (via lambda) | Yes (closure) | Yes (closure) |
| **Best for** | Hot, fixed code paths | C dispatch tables | Generic callbacks | Perl scripts | Pythonic APIs |
| **Overhead (cycles)** | $\mathbf{1}$ | $\mathbf{5\text{–}7}$ | $\mathbf{7\text{–}12}$ | $\mathbf{10\text{–}30}$ | $\mathbf{50\text{–}200}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g. `callerA`, `ptrSlot`, `invStep`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node IDs. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.

## 4.2 High-Level Architecture — Indirect Call Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    callerA[CALLER CODE] --> slotA[POINTER OR REFERENCE SLOT]
    slotA --> drefA[DEREFERENCE OPERATION]
    drefA --> invA[INDIRECT CALL INSTRUCTION]
    invA --> funcA[TARGET SUBPROGRAM ADDRESS]
    funcA --> execA[EXECUTE SUBPROGRAM BODY]
    execA --> retA[RETURN TO CALLER]
    retA --> callerA
```

**Caption:** Generic runtime flow of an indirect subprogram call. The dotted separation between *data* (the slot) and *code* (the target subprogram) is what makes the mechanism flexible.

## 4.3 Function Pointer Dispatch Table (Strategy Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[CLIENT MODULE] --> ctxA[CONTEXT OBJECT]
    ctxA --> strPtrA[STRATEGY POINTER]
    strPtrA -->|rebinds at runtime| tblA[DISPATCH TABLE]
    tblA --> algA1[ALGORITHM A]
    tblA --> algA2[ALGORITHM B]
    tblA --> algA3[ALGORITHM C]
    algA1 -.invoked via indirect call.-> ctxA
    algA2 -.invoked via indirect call.-> ctxA
    algA3 -.invoked via indirect call.-> ctxA
```

**Caption:** The classic *Strategy* design pattern. The context holds a single function pointer (`strPtrA`) that can be rebound to any entry in the dispatch table, allowing the algorithm to be swapped without modifying the client module.

## 4.4 qsort Comparator Call Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant userA as USER MAIN
    participant qsortA as QSORT LIBRARY
    participant cmpA as COMPARATOR SLOT
    participant implA as COMPARATOR IMPL

    userA->>qsortA: call qsort arr n size cmp
    Note over qsortA,cmpA: cmp passed as function pointer
    loop For each pair i,j
        qsortA->>cmpA: dereference slot
        cmpA->>implA: indirect call
        implA-->>qsortA: return result
    end
    qsortA-->>userA: sorted array
```

**Caption:** Sequence diagram showing how `qsort` repeatedly dereferences the comparator pointer to invoke the user-supplied comparison function. Each call is a textbook *indirect subprogram invocation*.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Python Higher-Order Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[SOURCE ITERABLE] --> mapA[MAP HIGHER ORDER FUNCTION]
    mapA --> cbA[CALLABLE SLOT]
    cbA --> l1A[LAMBDA SQUARE]
    cbA --> l2A[USER DEFINED FUNCTION]
    cbA --> l3A[BUILTIN INT]
    l1A --> outA[OUTPUT ITERABLE]
    l2A --> outA
    l3A --> outA
```

**Caption:** Python's `map(func, iterable)` is a higher-order function. The `func` argument is held in a *callable slot* (`cbA`) that is dereferenced once per element. The slot may be rebound to any Python callable — lambda, named function, or built-in.

## 4.6 Memory Layout of a Function Pointer Array

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    idxA[INDEX I] --> arrA[FUNCTION POINTER ARRAY]
    arrA --> e0A[ADDR 0x401000 ADD FN]
    arrA --> e1A[ADDR 0x401050 SUB FN]
    arrA --> e2A[ADDR 0x4010A0 MUL FN]
    arrA --> e3A[ADDR 0x4010F0 DIV FN]
    e0A --> execA[INDIRECT CALL EXECUTE]
    e1A --> execA
    e2A --> execA
    e3A --> execA
```

**Caption:** A *dispatch table* in memory. Each slot is 8 bytes on 64-bit systems, holding the absolute address of an arithmetic function. A single index lookup plus indirect call is sufficient to invoke the chosen function.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Pattern** — Part A carries 2 marks per question, with 10 marks total (5 questions). The 3-mark allocation below reflects the 2024 ESE module-level weightage.

### Q1. Define indirect subprogram invocation. How does it differ from a direct call?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *indirect subprogram invocation* is a call where the target routine is selected through a **pointer, reference, or callable object** rather than by its declared name. The address is **resolved at runtime** by dereferencing the slot. In contrast, a *direct call* hard-codes the target address at **compile time** using a `CALL rel32` instruction. Indirect calls enable polymorphism, callbacks, and higher-order functions, but incur an extra memory-load and possible branch-predictor miss, making them **5–20× costlier** than direct calls in the worst case.

---

### Q2. What is a callback? Give one example from the C standard library.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **callback** is a function whose address is passed to another function as an argument, allowing the receiving function to *call back* into the caller's code at a later moment. The classic C example is the comparator argument of `qsort`:

```c
qsort(arr, n, sizeof *arr, compare);
```

Here `compare` is a function pointer; `qsort` dereferences it once per element comparison — a textbook indirect call.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Pattern** — Each Part-B question offers an **OR** between Question A and Question B. Both must be attempted for the module. Sub-parts are split **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) — Function Pointers in C [7 marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> Explain the concept of a *function pointer* in C. Write a complete C program that declares a function pointer, binds it to two different functions (e.g. `square` and `cube`), and invokes them indirectly. Discuss the memory layout and runtime cost.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Declaration (1 mark):**
A function pointer is declared using the syntax `return_type (*pointer_name)(parameter_list)`. Example: `int (*op)(int);` declares a pointer named `op` that can hold the address of any function taking one `int` and returning an `int`.

**Step 2 — Binding (1 mark):**
The address-of operator `&` (or just the function name, which decays to a pointer in this context) is used:
```c
op = □   /* or equivalently: op = square; */
```

**Step 3 — Indirect call (1 mark):**
The pointer is dereferenced with `(*op)(x)` or implicitly via `op(x)`:
```c
result = op(5);
```

**Step 4 — Complete program (2 marks):**
```c
#include <stdio.h>
int square(int n) { return n * n; }
int cube  (int n) { return n * n * n; }

int main(void) {
    int (*op)(int);
    op = square;   printf("%d\n", op(4));   /* 16 */
    op = cube;     printf("%d\n", op(4));   /* 64 */
    return 0;
}
```

**Step 5 — Memory layout (1 mark):**
On a 64-bit system, `op` occupies **8 bytes** in the stack frame and stores the absolute virtual address of `square` (e.g. `0x0000000000401136`). The dereference is a single `MOV` to a register followed by a `CALL reg` instruction.

**Step 6 — Runtime cost (1 mark):**
Indirect call cost ≈ **5–7 cycles** with branch-predictor hit, **~20 cycles** with miss. Compared to a direct call's 1 cycle, the overhead is significant in tight loops but negligible when amortised over a non-trivial callee.

---

#### Q.A(b) — Higher-Order Functions in Python [7 marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternate Set]**

> With suitable code examples, explain *first-class functions* and *higher-order functions* in Python. Show how `map`, `filter`, and a custom higher-order function can be implemented. Discuss the role of closures.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — First-class functions (1 mark):**
In Python, functions are *first-class objects* — they can be assigned to variables, stored in data structures, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions:
```python
def greet(name): return f"Hello, {name}"
f = greet
print(f("KTU"))       # Hello, KTU
```

**Step 2 — Higher-order functions (2 marks):**
A higher-order function (HOF) accepts or returns a function. Built-in HOFs include `map`, `filter`, and `reduce`:
```python
nums  = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
sqs   = list(map(lambda x: x*x, nums))     # [1,4,9,16,25]
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x%2==0, nums)) # [2,4]
```

**Step 3 — Custom HOF (2 marks):**
```python
def apply_twice(func, value):
    return func(func(value))

print(apply_twice(lambda x: x+1, 5))   # 7
```

**Step 4 — Closures (2 marks):**
A **closure** is a function that captures variables from its enclosing lexical scope:
```python
def make_multiplier(k):
    def mul(x): return k * x     # k is captured
    return mul

double = make_multiplier(2)
triple = make_multiplier(3)
print(double(5), triple(5))      # 10  15
```

The variable `k` is preserved in the closure's `__closure__` cell, demonstrating that an indirect call can carry *state* along with code — a powerful tool for callbacks and decorators.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

#### Q.B(a) — Subroutine References in Perl [7 marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Explain how Perl implements indirect subprogram calls through *subroutine references* and the *arrow operator* `->`. Write a program that demonstrates dispatch via a hash-of-codes (HoC). Discuss closures in Perl.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Taking a reference (1 mark):**
The backslash operator `\&subname` produces a *reference* to a named sub:
```perl
sub greet { print "Hello, $_[0]!\n"; }
my $fn = \&greet;
```

**Step 2 — Calling via reference (1 mark):**
The arrow operator `->` dereferences the code ref and invokes the sub:
```perl
$fn->("KTU");    # prints: Hello, KTU!
```

**Step 3 — Hash-of-codes for dispatch (2 marks):**
```perl
my %ops = (
    add => sub { $_[0] + $_[1] },
    sub => sub { $_[0] - $_[1] },
    mul => sub { $_[0] * $_[1] },
);

my $op = 'mul';
print $ops{$op}->(6, 7), "\n";   # 42
```

**Step 4 — Anonymous subs and closures (2 marks):**
```perl
sub make_counter {
    my $n = 0;
    return sub { $n++; return $n; };   # captures $n
}
my $c = make_counter;
print $c->();   # 1
print $c->();   # 2
```

**Step 5 — Comparison with C (1 mark):**
Perl's subroutine reference is conceptually identical to a C function pointer but is dynamically typed and supports closures (state capture) natively, eliminating the need for a separate `void *userdata` parameter.

---

#### Q.B(b) — Callbacks and the Observer Pattern [7 marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternate Set]**

> Design a simple C program that simulates an *event emitter* with multiple *listener* callbacks. Explain how each callback is stored, deregistered, and invoked. Justify the use of function pointers for this design.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Listener type definition (1 mark):**
```c
typedef void (*listener_t)(const char *event);
```

**Step 2 — Registry structure (1 mark):**
```c
#define MAX_LISTENERS 16
typedef struct {
    listener_t  fns[MAX_LISTENERS];
    int         count;
} emitter_t;
```

**Step 3 — Register and deregister (2 marks):**
```c
void emitter_on(emitter_t *e, listener_t fn) {
    if (e->count < MAX_LISTENERS) e->fns[e->count++] = fn;
}
void emitter_off(emitter_t *e, listener_t fn) {
    for (int i = 0; i < e->count; ++i)
        if (e->fns[i] == fn) e->fns[i] = e->fns[--e->count];
}
```

**Step 4 — Emit (indirect calls) (2 marks):**
```c
void emitter_emit(emitter_t *e, const char *event) {
    for (int i = 0; i < e->count; ++i)
        e->fns[i](event);    /* indirect call per listener */
}
```

**Step 5 — Justification (1 mark):**
Function pointers allow the emitter to remain *decoupled* from the listeners. The emitter knows only the signature `void(const char *)`; concrete listener code lives in user modules. This is the essence of the **Observer / Pub-Sub pattern**, ubiquitous in GUI toolkits, OS kernels, and asynchronous I/O frameworks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Module**
> 1. **Confusing `*fp` with `(*fp)()`** — In C, `*fp()` is parsed as `*(fp())`, i.e. *call `fp` then dereference the return value*. The correct dereference-and-call is `(*fp)()`. Forgetting parentheses costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Missing the `&` in Perl** — Writing `$fn = greet;` *assigns the result of calling `greet`*, not a reference. The correct form is `$fn = \&greet;`. This is a **1-mark** slip.
> 3. **Forgetting to handle NULL pointers (C)** — Dereferencing an unbound function pointer is undefined behaviour. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for omitting a NULL check in production code.
> 4. **Confusing `lambda` with `def`** — In Python, `lambda` is an *expression* (returns a value), while `def` is a *statement*. Writing `lambda x: return x*x` is a syntax error; correct is `lambda x: x*x`. **1 mark** lost.
> 5. **Cost analysis without numbers** — The 2024 scheme expects *quantitative* answers for performance questions. Saying "indirect calls are slow" earns only partial credit; quoting **5–20 cycles** earns full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Direct call**: target address fixed at compile time, **1 CPU cycle**, `CALL rel32`.
- [x] **Indirect call**: target address resolved at runtime through a **pointer / reference / callable object**, costing **5–20 cycles** plus possible **branch-mispredictor penalty**.
- [x] **C function pointer syntax**: `R (*fp)(args)` — parentheses around `*fp` are **mandatory**.
- [x] **Perl subroutine reference**: `\&subname` to take, `$fn->(args)` to call.
- [x] **Python first-class functions**: every `def` is a callable object; functions can be assigned, passed, returned, and stored in containers.
- [x] **Higher-order function (HOF)**: a function that accepts or returns another function — e.g. `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, `apply_twice`.
- [x] **Closure**: a function bundled with its captured lexical environment; enables stateful callbacks.
- [x] **Callback**: a function reference passed as an argument, allowing library code to invoke user logic — used in `qsort`, GUI event loops, and `qsort`-style APIs.
- [x] **Dispatch table**: an array of function pointers indexed by an opcode/enum, used in interpreters, VM bytecode handlers, and OS interrupt tables.
- [x] **Branch predictor**: CPU hardware that guesses the target of an indirect call; a misprediction costs **~15–20 cycles** of pipeline flush.
- [x] **Devirtualisation**: compiler optimisation that converts indirect (virtual) calls into direct calls when the dynamic type is provable; reduces overhead by **up to 90%**.
- [x] **Thread safety**: a shared function pointer may require `volatile` (C), `Lock` (Python `threading`), or `atomic` (C++20) to avoid data races in concurrent code.
- [x] **Memory footprint of a function pointer**: **8 bytes** on 64-bit, **4 bytes** on 32-bit systems.
- [x] **Common pitfall**: forgetting that in C, `fp()` *calls* `fp`, whereas `*fp` or `(*fp)()` *calls through* the pointer — the precedence rules are the #1 source of bugs in this module.
- [x] **Real-world anchors**: Linux `file_operations` (C), Python `map/filter`, Node.js route handlers, GUI signal-slot systems, OS IDT entries — **five systems to cite** if the question asks for "real-world applications."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overloaded Subprograms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overloaded Subprograms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Overloaded Subprograms — Module 3 Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overloaded Subprograms (Ad-Hoc Polymorphism)**
> An **overloaded subprogram** is a set of two or more subprograms (functions or procedures) that share the **same identifier (name)** but possess **distinct parameter profiles (signatures)**. The parameter profile is formally defined as the *ordered tuple* consisting of the number, order, and data types of the formal parameters. The compiler (or linker) discriminates between the candidate subprograms at **compile time (static binding)** by matching the actual parameter list of the call site with the formal parameter profile of the definitions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, this is treated as a specific instance of **ad-hoc polymorphism**, where a single lexical token accepts multiple semantic interpretations governed solely by the type signature of its arguments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Polymorphic" Word

Imagine the English word **"BANK"**:
- When used in *"I deposited money in the bank"* → it means a **financial institution**.
- When used in *"We had a picnic on the river bank"* → it means the **edge of a river**.

The **lexical form** is identical, yet the **meaning (semantic operation)** changes entirely based on the **context of the operands**. The compiler, like a human listener, inspects the *adjacent context* (parameter types) and binds the call to the correct internal implementation.

| Linguistic Analogy | Programming Construct |
|---|---|
| The word "BANK" | Function name `add()` |
| Sentence context | Argument types in call |
| Listener's interpretation | Compiler's signature matching |
| Different definitions | Distinct function bodies |

### 1.3 The Parameter Profile — The Heart of Overloading

The **parameter profile** (also called the *function signature* in C++ or *parameter-and-result profile* in Ada) comprises three strictly observable elements:

1. **Number of parameters** (arity)
2. **Data types of parameters**
3. **Order of parameters**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In strict Ada semantics, the parameter profile *also* includes the **return type** of a function (Ada calls this the "parameter and result profile"). In C++/Java, the **return type is NOT** part of the signature for overloading purposes. This is a classic KTU two-marker question.

### 1.4 What is *Not* Part of the Profile

- The **name of the parameters** is irrelevant.
- The **return type** (in C/C++/Java) is irrelevant for overloading.
- The **storage class**, **constness** (in C), or **default arguments** may affect selection but do not always constitute a valid discriminator.

> [!WARNING]
> Two subprograms that differ *only* in return type are **illegal** in C++ and constitute a **compilation error** — a frequent KTU pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Overloading Works Internally

The lifetime of an overloaded call can be broken into **five distinct phases**:

**Phase 1 — Lexical Recognition**
The compiler encounters the identifier (e.g., `print`). It does not yet know *which* `print` is intended.

**Phase 2 — Candidate Set Construction**
The compiler scans the **scope** (local block, namespace, class) and gathers **every** subprogram with the matching identifier into a *candidate set*. In C++, this is done via *name lookup*; in Ada, it uses *visibility* rules.

**Phase 3 — Signature Matching (Filtering)**
Each candidate's parameter profile is compared against the actual arguments of the call. The compiler retains only those candidates whose **arity** and **types** match the call.

**Phase 4 — Best-Match Selection**
If **exactly one** candidate survives Phase 3, it is selected. If **multiple** survive, the compiler applies a **ranking algorithm** (e.g., C++'s *overload resolution rules* — exact match > promotion > standard conversion > user-defined conversion).

**Phase 5 — Binding and Code Generation**
The call site is patched to point to the selected function's machine-code address. Since this happens at **compile time**, overloaded calls incur **zero runtime overhead** — a critical performance advantage.

> [!NOTE]
> This compile-time binding is the reason overloading is classified as **static polymorphism** (in contrast to dynamic polymorphism via virtual functions / inheritance).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Rule / Formula / Definition | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parameter Profile | $P = (n, T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ where $n$ is arity, $T_i$ is the type of the $i^{th}$ parameter | All languages |
| 2 | Return Type in Profile | $P_{Ada} = (n, T_1, \ldots, T_n, R)$;  $\;P_{C++} = (n, T_1, \ldots, T_n)$ | Ada vs C++ |
| 3 | Binding Time | $t_{bind} = t_{compile}$ (static) | C++, Java, Ada |
| 4 | Overload Resolution | $\text{Exact Match} \succ \text{Promotion} \succ \text{Standard Conversion} \succ \text{User-Defined}$ | C++ |
| 5 | Ambiguity Condition | $\vert C_{survivors} \vert > 1$ with no strict rank winner | All languages |
| 6 | Coercion vs Overload | Coercion = implicit type cast; Overload = distinct function | Theory |
| 7 | Polymorphism Type | $\text{Ad-hoc} = \text{Overloading} \cup \text{Coercion}$ | PLT theory |
| 8 | Mangled Name (C++) | $\text{add(int, double)} \mapsto \_Z3addid$ | C++ ABI |

### 2.3 Type Equivalence Rules in Overloading

When matching signatures, the compiler must decide whether two types are *equivalent*. KTU 2024 tests this:

- **Name Equivalence:** Two types are equal iff they share the same name. Common in C/C++ for primitives.
- **Structural Equivalence:** Two types are equal iff they have the same internal structure. Common in Ada, ML.
- **Strict Name Equivalence** (used by Ada for overloading): A subtype is *not* equivalent to its base type for resolution purposes.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Overloading

Overloading is not merely an aesthetic feature; it serves three engineering goals:

1. **API Readability:** `print(int)`, `print(double)`, `print(string)` all read as natural English verbs.
2. **Compile-Time Type Safety:** The compiler guarantees the right algorithm is chosen for the operand type.
3. **Code Reuse without Inheritance:** Provides polymorphic behaviour *without* the runtime cost of virtual dispatch (vtable lookup).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The Resolution Algorithm

Consider the following C++ declarations made visible in a scope:

```cpp
void draw(int x);
void draw(double x);
void draw(int x, int y);
void draw(const char* s);
```

Now evaluate the call: `draw(5);`

**Step 1 — Candidate Set Construction**
The compiler identifies all four `draw` functions as candidates.

$$
C = \{ draw_1, draw_2, draw_3, draw_4 \}
$$

**Step 2 — Arity Filtering**
The call has arity 1. Therefore $draw_3$ (arity 2) is eliminated.

$$
C' = C \setminus \{ draw_3 \} = \{ draw_1, draw_2, draw_4 \}
$$

**Step 3 — Type Matching**
- `draw_1(int)`  : argument `5` is of type `int` → **Exact match** ✓
- `draw_2(double)`: `int` → `double` is a *standard conversion* ✓
- `draw_4(const char*)`: `int` → `const char*` is *illegal* ✗

$$
C'' = \{ draw_1, draw_2 \}
$$

**Step 4 — Ranking**
Applying the C++ overload resolution ranking:

$$
\text{rank}(draw_1) = \text{Exact} \succ \text{rank}(draw_2) = \text{Standard Conversion}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Binding**

$$
\text{Selected} = draw_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Binding: } draw(5) \mapsto draw_1
$$

The **mangled symbol** emitted into the object file might be `_Z4drawi` (Itanium ABI style).

### 3.2 Worked Example — The Ambiguity Trap

Consider:

```cpp
void compute(int x, double y);
void compute(double x, int y);
```

Call: `compute(5, 5);`

**Step 1 — Arity:** Both have arity 2. Candidate set: $\{ compute_1, compute_2 \}$.

**Step 2 — Type Analysis:**
For $compute_1$: requires `(int, double)`. Second argument `5` is `int` → needs conversion to `double`.
For $compute_2$: requires `(double, int)`. First argument `5` is `int` → needs conversion to `double`.

**Step 3 — Ranking:** Neither is strictly better. Both require exactly one standard conversion.

$$
\text{rank}(compute_1) = \text{rank}(compute_2) = \text{Standard} \Rightarrow \text{AMBIGUOUS}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Such calls are **compile-time errors**. Many students mistakenly believe a default conversion is "always good enough" — the compiler rejects ambiguity outright.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Resolution Outcomes

| Input Signature Difference | Overload Valid? | Discriminator |
|---|---|---|
| Different number of parameters | ✅ Yes | Arity |
| Different parameter types | ✅ Yes | Type |
| Different parameter order | ✅ Yes | Type-sequence |
| Different return type only (C++) | ❌ No | None |
| Different parameter names only | ❌ No | None |
| `const` vs non-`const` reference (C++) | ✅ Yes | Constness |
| Subtype vs base type (Ada) | ⚠️ Usually No | Strict name eq. |

### 3.4 Coercion vs Overloading — The Hidden Interaction

A subtle case arises when a language performs *implicit type coercion* **and** offers overloading. Consider:

```cpp
double x = 3.14;
void show(int);
void show(double);
show(x);   // Exact match → show(double)
```

But:

```cpp
int y = 5;
show(y);   // Exact match → show(int), NOT show(double)
```

If the language performed coercion *before* matching, `show(5)` might bind to `show(double)`. The compiler therefore performs **overload resolution first**, then *coercion only when no exact overload exists*. This sequencing is what enables C++'s strict rank ordering shown in Section 2.1.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overload Resolution Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete interaction pipeline of the C++/Ada compiler when resolving an overloaded subprogram call.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: call to overloaded name] --> B[Phase 1: Lexical Scanner Tokenizes Identifier]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Name Lookup Collects All Candidates in Scope]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Arity Filter Eliminates Wrong Number of Parameters]
    D --> E{Any Candidates Survive Arity Filter?}
    E -- No --> F[Compiler Error: No Matching Function]
    E -- Yes --> G[Phase 4: Type Compatibility Check Against Each Signature]
    G --> H{Number of Viable Candidates}
    H -- Zero --> I[Compiler Error: No Viable Function]
    H -- One --> J[Phase 5: Direct Binding Selected Candidate]
    H -- More Than One --> K[Phase 6: Rank Candidates Using Conversion Hierarchy]
    K --> L{Strict Best Rank Exists?}
    L -- Yes --> J
    L -- No --> M[Compiler Error: Ambiguous Call]
    J --> N[Phase 7: Name Mangling Emit Unique Symbol to Object File]
    N --> O[Phase 8: Linker Resolves Mangled Symbol at Link Time]
    O --> P[Final Binary: Direct Static Call No Runtime Overhead]
```

### 4.2 Polymorphism Taxonomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Poly[Polymorphism] --> Para[Parametric Polymorphism]
    Poly --> Adhoc[Ad-Hoc Polymorphism]
    Poly --> Inclus[Inclusion Polymorphism]

    Para --> Ex1[Generics in Ada]
    Para --> Ex2[Templates in C++]

    Adhoc --> Ovld[Overloading]
    Adhoc --> Coer[Coercion]

    Inclus --> Ex3[Subtype Substitution]
    Inclus --> Ex4[Virtual Functions]
```

### 4.3 Memory Model — Mangled Names

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SourceCode[Source Code Level]
        S1[function add a: integer, b: real]
        S2[function add a: real, b: integer]
    end

    subgraph ObjCode[Object File Level]
        O1[Mangled Symbol: add__R8sii]
        O2[Mangled Symbol: add__R8sri]
    end

    subgraph Linker[Linker Level]
        L1[Symbol Table Maps Original add to Unique Mangled Name]
    end

    S1 --> O1
    S2 --> O2
    O1 --> L1
    O2 --> L1
```

### 4.4 Type Compatibility Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Two Types T1 and T2 Under Comparison] --> T1{Name Equivalence Active?}
    T1 -- Yes --> T2{Do Identifiers Match Exactly?}
    T2 -- Yes --> TEq[Types Are Equivalent]
    T2 -- No --> TNe[Types Are Distinct]
    T1 -- No --> T3{Structural Equivalence Active?}
    T3 -- Yes --> T4{Do Internal Structures Match Recursively?}
    T4 -- Yes --> TEq
    T4 -- No --> TNe
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define an *overloaded subprogram*. What is meant by its *parameter profile*, and which components of a subprogram declaration are *not* considered part of the profile in C++?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **overloaded subprogram** is a collection of two or more subprograms that share the same name but differ in their **parameter profile** — the tuple of number, order, and data types of the formal parameters. The compiler discriminates between them at **compile time** by matching the call's argument list against each candidate's profile.

In C++, the following are **not** considered part of the parameter profile for overloading:
1. The **return type** of a function.
2. The **names** of the formal parameters.
3. Default argument values, in isolation (though they interact with arity in resolution).

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for parameter profile components, 1 mark for non-profile elements.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **overloading** and **overriding** of subprograms. In which category does each fall under polymorphism classification?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Overloading | Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Same name, different signatures in the **same scope** | Same name, **same signature** in **subclass** redefining base class method |
| Binding Time | Compile time (static) | Run time (dynamic) |
| Polymorphism Type | Ad-hoc polymorphism | Inclusion polymorphism (subtype) |
| Number of Methods | Multiple distinct methods | One redefined method |
| Inheritance Required? | No | Yes |

Overloading is a form of **ad-hoc (static) polymorphism**, whereas overriding is **inclusion (dynamic) polymorphism**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** *Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **overloaded subprograms** in detail. With a suitable C++ example, demonstrate how the compiler performs **name lookup**, **signature matching**, and **ranking** to resolve an ambiguous-free call. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Overloaded subprograms enable the same identifier to denote multiple subprograms distinguished by their parameter profiles. This provides ad-hoc polymorphism at compile time, improving both readability and type safety.

Consider the following C++ example:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int area(int side) {
    return side * side;          // Square
}

double area(double radius) {
    return 3.14159 * radius * radius;   // Circle
}

int area(int length, int width) {
    return length * width;       // Rectangle
}

int main() {
    cout << area(5)        << endl;   // Calls area(int)
    cout << area(2.5)      << endl;   // Calls area(double)
    cout << area(4, 6)     << endl;   // Calls area(int, int)
    return 0;
}
```

**Resolution walk-through for `area(5)`:**

1. **Name Lookup** — The compiler finds three candidates: `area(int)`, `area(double)`, `area(int, int)`.
2. **Arity Filter** — Call has one argument. `area(int, int)` is rejected.
3. **Type Match** — `area(int)` is an *exact match*; `area(double)` requires a standard conversion from `int` to `double`.
4. **Ranking** — Exact match outranks standard conversion, so `area(int)` wins.
5. **Binding** — The compiler mangles the symbol (e.g., `_Z4areai`) and links the call statically.

The result: a single intuitive name `area` serves three different geometric computations with **zero runtime overhead**.

*(Award 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for the example, 2 marks for the resolution walk-through, 1 mark for the zero-runtime-overhead conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the **issues and pitfalls** that arise when overloading subprograms. Include a discussion on **ambiguity**, **coercion interaction**, **default arguments**, and **scope rules**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Overloading, while powerful, introduces several subtle issues:

**1. Ambiguity Error**
When two or more overloads are equally ranked for a call, the compiler reports an ambiguity error. Example:

```cpp
void show(int, double);
void show(double, int);
show(5, 5);   // AMBIGUOUS — both require one standard conversion
```

This is **not** a runtime problem but a *compile-time* error, and many students mistakenly believe the compiler will "pick one" — it will not.

**2. Coercion Interaction**
If the language performs implicit coercion *before* overload resolution, the type-safety benefits are lost. Modern C++ and Ada perform **overload resolution first** and **coercion second**, preserving type safety. A call to `print(5)` with `print(int)` and `print(double)` available will bind to `print(int)` — not silently coerce to `print(double)`.

**3. Default Arguments**
Default arguments can interact adversely:

```cpp
void log(int level, int code = 0);
void log(int code);
log(1);    // AMBIGUOUS — both candidates can match
```

**4. Scope and Name Hiding**
In C++, declaring a name in a derived class **hides** all base-class overloads of the same name. The student must use `using Base::show;` to re-expose them — a frequent source of bugs.

**5. Operator Overloading Side-Effects**
In C++, overloading operators like `+` for user-defined types can yield non-intuitive semantics if poorly designed (e.g., `+` for string concatenation vs. arithmetic).

**6. Maintenance Overhead**
Excessive overloading across large codebases complicates API documentation and IDE auto-completion.

*(Award 1–2 marks per issue with a concrete code demonstration.)*

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain **type checking** and **type conversion** in the context of overloaded subprograms. How does Ada's strict typing differ from C++'s coercion-tolerant approach? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Type Checking** is the process by which the compiler verifies that operands and arguments are type-compatible with their declared uses. In the context of overloading, type checking is *necessary* to discriminate between the candidates.

**Type Conversion** falls into two categories:
- **Implicit (coercion):** Compiler automatically converts one type to another.
- **Explicit (cast):** Programmer-directed conversion.

**Ada's Approach (Strict Typing):**
Ada enforces *strong* typing and uses **strict name equivalence**. A subtype is not the same type as its base for overload resolution. Implicit numeric coercion is **minimal** (e.g., universal integer is checked). The compiler therefore refuses to silently coerce a `Float` to an `Integer` parameter, demanding either an explicit cast or a more specific overload.

**C++'s Approach (Coercion-Tolerant):**
C++ allows extensive implicit conversions (e.g., `int` to `double`, derived-class pointers to base-class pointers). Combined with overloading, this creates a hierarchy of conversion ranks:

$$
\text{Identity} \succ \text{Promotion} \succ \text{Standard Conversion} \succ \text{User-Defined}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Ada | C++ |
|---|---|---|
| Implicit numeric coercion | Minimal | Extensive |
| Overload discrimination | Parameter profile + return type | Parameter profile only |
| Type equivalence | Strict name | Name (with structural nuance for some) |
| Ambiguity resolution | Compile-time error if unresolved | Compile-time error if no strict rank winner |
| User-defined conversions | Limited (limited numeric subtypes) | Rich (operator overloading, converting constructors) |

**Example (Ada vs C++):**

```ada
-- Ada
procedure Print (X : Integer);
procedure Print (X : Float);
Print (5);       -- Exact match → Print(Integer)
Print (5.0);     -- Exact match → Print(Float)
-- Print (5.0, 2.0);  -- No matching profile, even if 2.0 is Float
```

```cpp
// C++
void print(int x);
void print(double x);
print(5);        // Exact match → print(int)
print(5.0);      // Exact match → print(double)
print('A');      // 'A' promotes to int (97) → print(int)
```

*(Award 2 marks for type-checking definition, 2 marks for Ada's strict approach, 2 marks for C++'s tolerant approach, 1 mark for the comparison summary.)*

---

**(b)** Compare overloaded subprograms in **C++ and Ada** with a focus on (i) the role of the return type, (ii) the handling of subprogram names, and (iii) the resolution mechanism. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Role of the Return Type**

In **C++**, the return type is **not** part of the parameter profile. The declarations:

```cpp
int  compute(int x);
char compute(int x);   // ILLEGAL — duplicate signature
```

are a compile-time error because only the parameter list participates in signature matching.

In **Ada**, the *parameter and result profile* includes the return (result) type. Therefore:

```ada
function Compute (X : Integer) return Integer;
function Compute (X : Integer) return Float;   -- LEGAL — distinct profiles
```

are two distinct, valid subprograms.

**(ii) Handling of Subprogram Names**

C++ employs **name mangling** (a compiler-specific encoding scheme) to assign unique internal symbols to each overload, for example `_Z4funci` and `_Z4funcd`. The linker resolves these unique symbols at link time.

Ada uses a more **readable convention**: subprograms are named by their identifiers plus their profiles, and the linker stores them in symbol tables in their human-readable form. The Ada runtime can therefore distinguish overloads more transparently.

**(iii) Resolution Mechanism**

| Feature | C++ | Ada |
|---|---|---|
