| Resolution phase | Compile time | Compile time |
| Type equivalence | Name-based for primitives | Strict name equivalence |
| Implicit conversions | Extensive ranking hierarchy | Minimal; mostly rejected |
| Ambiguity | Compile error | Compile error |
| Generic subprograms | Templates (`template<class T>`) | Generics (`generic`) |

C++ overload resolution follows the rigorous ranking:

$$
\text{Exact} \succ \text{Promotion} \succ \text{Standard} \succ \text{User-Defined}
$$

Ada's resolution is more rigid: the compiler selects the unique candidate whose parameter profile matches the call *exactly*; if none matches exactly, an ambiguity or "no match" error is reported unless universal types are involved.

**Conclusion:** C++ provides *flexibility* through coercion-based ranking; Ada provides *safety* through strict typing. Both achieve ad-hoc polymorphism but optimize for different engineering values.

*(Award 2 marks for return-type discussion, 2 marks for naming mechanism, 3 marks for resolution comparison.)*

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Overloading Questions:**
> 1. **Returning a value alone does NOT make an overload valid in C++** — many students lose 2 marks by writing `int foo()` and `double foo()` as distinct overloads. They are NOT.
> 2. **Do not confuse overloading with overriding** — overloading is within the same scope (or class), whereas overriding occurs across inheritance hierarchies.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the compile-time (static) binding** of overloading costs the 1-mark keyword credit.
> 4. **In Ada questions, the return type IS part of the profile** — students trained on C++ often omit this and lose full credit.
> 5. **Ambiguous calls are compile errors, not runtime errors** — never say "the program crashes at runtime" for an overload ambiguity.
> 6. **Overloading is NOT the same as default arguments** — default arguments vary the *arity* at the call site but do not constitute a separate subprogram body.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **overloaded subprogram** shares its name but differs in its **parameter profile**: number, order, and types of parameters.
- ✅ Binding is performed at **compile time** — zero runtime overhead.
- ✅ It is a form of **ad-hoc polymorphism**, distinct from **inclusion polymorphism** (overriding) and **parametric polymorphism** (generics/templates).
- ✅ In **C++**, the return type is **not** part of the profile; in **Ada**, it **is** part of the parameter-and-result profile.
- ✅ The compiler performs: **Name Lookup → Arity Filter → Type Match → Ranking → Binding**.
- ✅ Conversion ranking in C++: `Exact ≻ Promotion ≻ Standard ≻ User-Defined`.
- ✅ Ambiguity arises when multiple candidates have **equal** best rank — the compiler reports an **error**.
- ✅ Coercion is applied **only after** overload resolution fails to find an exact match.
- ✅ Overloading is **not** the same as overriding (inheritance hierarchy) or as default arguments.
- ✅ Symbol mangling (C++) or profile-based naming (Ada) gives each overload a **unique internal identity** for the linker.
- ✅ C++ provides *flexibility*; Ada provides *type safety* — both achieve static polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generic Subprograms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Generic Subprograms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Generic Subprogram** is a subprogram (function or procedure) that is parameterized by one or more *type parameters* (or generic formal parameters) in addition to the usual value/result parameters. The body of the subprogram is written *once* in terms of these abstract type parameters, and the compiler **automatically generates a distinct concrete (specific) subprogram for every distinct type combination with which the generic is instantiated** during compilation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEBMT746 Module 3):**
> Generic subprograms are a cornerstone of **parametric polymorphism**. They allow a single algorithm to be applied to data of many types without resorting to *ad-hoc polymorphism* (overloading) or *subtype polymorphism* (inheritance). The study of generics unifies three ideas: **abstraction (parameterization), reusability (one body, many uses), and type safety (compile-time checking).**

Mathematically, a generic subprogram $G$ with type parameter $T$ can be modeled as a *family* of functions:

$$G : \forall T \in \mathcal{C}.\; (T, T, \dots) \rightarrow R(T)$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of types for which $G$ is legal (the *constraint set*), and $R(T)$ is the result type — itself possibly dependent on $T$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Socket Wrench"

Imagine a **single, adjustable socket wrench** that can grip nuts and bolts of *any* size or shape — metric, imperial, hex, star — without you owning a separate wrench for each. The *wrench body* (the algorithm) is generic; the *bolt size* (the data type) is the parameter.

- The **wrench = the generic subprogram body**
- The **bolt size passed in = the type parameter (actual generic parameter)**
- The *moment* you attach it to a 10 mm bolt, the wrench becomes a *10 mm wrench* (this is **instantiation**).
- Attach it to a 17 mm bolt, and you get a *17 mm wrench* — a *different physical subprogram* for a *different type*.

You wrote one body; you got many tools. That is exactly what generic subprograms provide to a programmer.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Generic Formal Parameter** | A placeholder for a type (or subprogram/package) that appears in the generic subprogram's declaration header. |
| **Generic Actual Parameter** | The concrete type (or value/subprogram) supplied at the point of *instantiation*. |
| **Instantiation** | The compiler's act of producing a *concrete copy* of the generic body specialized for the actual parameter(s). |
| **Constraint** | Rules that restrict the kinds of types that can be used as actual generic parameters (e.g., must be a numeric type, must support `$<$ operator). |
| **Parametric Polymorphism** | The property by which one body works uniformly for a parametric family of types. |
| **Ad-hoc Polymorphism** | A contrasting approach — overloading — where separate bodies are written for each type. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generic ≠ Overloaded.** In *overloading*, you write **N different bodies** for N types. In *generics*, you write **one body** and the compiler synthesizes N specialized bodies automatically. The conceptual density (lines of source) is $O(1)$ in generics but $O(N)$ in overloading.

### 1.4 Where Generics Live — Language Survey

| Language | Generic Mechanism | Binding Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Ada** | `generic` … `procedure/function` | Compile-time (explicit instantiation) |
| **C++** | Function templates (`template<class T>`) | Compile-time (implicit instantiation) |
| **Java** | Generic methods (`<T> T foo(T x)`) | Compile-time (type erasure at runtime) |
| **C#** | Generic methods (`<T> T Foo<T>(T x)`) | JIT/Compile (reified generics) |
| **Haskell** | Polymorphic functions (`id :: a -> a`) | Fully static, type-class constrained |

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEBMT746) emphasizes **Ada-style generics** and **C++-style templates** as canonical models, because they are the cleanest pedagogical examples of the concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Part Structure of a Generic Subprogram

Every generic subprogram has three logical layers:

1. **Generic Formal Part (Header)** — declares *what* is parameterised.
2. **Subprogram Specification** — the *signature* the generic will expose.
3. **Subprogram Body** — the executable code, written abstractly in terms of the formals.

The *header* is what distinguishes a generic from an ordinary subprogram.

### 2.2 Why Generics? — The "Why" Behind the Mechanism

- **Reusability** : A library author writes the `Swap` algorithm **once**; it ships to millions of users who instantiate it for `int`, `float`, `std::string`, `MyClass`, etc.
- **Type Safety** : Unlike C-style `void*` macros, generics *preserve* the type — there is **no unsafe cast**, and the compiler checks each instantiation.
- **Performance** : Because instantiation is a *compile-time* expansion, the generated code is **as efficient as a hand-written specialized version** (no boxing, no virtual dispatch overhead).
- **Maintainability** : Bug fixes propagate to *all* instantiations automatically.

### 2.3 Binding Times — The Heart of the Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binding Time = the moment at which a property of a variable/parameter is fixed in memory.** Generics shift *type binding* from **run-time (dynamic)** to **compile-time (static)**, eliminating a whole class of runtime overhead.

| Aspect | Non-Generic (Dynamic) | Generic (Static) |
|---|---|---|
| Type of parameter | Bound at call site, possibly via vtable | Bound at instantiation (compile) |
| Code generated | One body, many call-site coercions | One specialized body *per type* |
| Run-time overhead | Possible (boxing, RTTI) | **None** for primitive instantiations |
| Type safety | Weaker (casts needed) | **Stronger** (compile-time check) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement / Rule | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Generic Declaration Form (Ada)** | `generic [formal_part] subprogram_specification` | Must precede every Ada generic subprogram. |
| 2 | **Generic Declaration Form (C++)** | `template <class T [, …]>` followed by ordinary function | Template parameter list is the formal part. |
| 3 | **Constraint** | Predicate $C(T)$ restricting the set of admissible actual types, e.g., `T is discrete` or `T has operator<` | Without constraint, instantiation may fail at *semantic* check time. |
| 4 | **Instantiation Rule** | $G\langle T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n \rangle \;\Rightarrow\; G_{T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n}$ (a fresh copy) | Each distinct type argument produces a fresh, independent subprogram. |
| 5 | **Code Bloat** | $\text{CodeSize} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert G_{T_i} \vert$ | A *trade-off*: faster execution vs. larger binary. |
| 6 | **Implicit Instantiation (C++)** | Triggered automatically by the compiler at first use | Reduces programmer burden; C++ default. |
| 7 | **Explicit Instantiation (Ada, C++ explicit form)** | Programmer issues `instantiate G<Integer>` | Used for separation of generic definition from use (e.g., library builds). |
| 8 | **Separate Compilation Issue** | In Ada, a generic's body must be *visible* at instantiation point (no separate `.o` like ordinary subprograms) | KTU 2024 frequently tests this. |
| 9 | **Generic of a Generic** | $G_1$ takes a subprogram $S$ as a generic parameter; $G_1\langle S \rangle$ can be reused for many $S$ | Powerful for *strategy pattern* implementations. |
| 10 | **Java Type Erasure** | $\text{erased}(G\langle T \rangle) = G$ (raw type at runtime) | Means Java generics do NOT have true compile-time code generation like C++ templates. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Numerical libraries (BLAS/LAPACK)**: A `gemm` (matrix multiply) generic kernel can be instantiated for `float`, `double`, `complex<float>`, `complex<double>` — the same loop nest, four high-performance specializations.
- **Embedded systems**: A generic `RingBuffer<T, N>` ensures type-safe circular buffers in firmware without `void*` casts (a major source of CVEs).
- **Compilers**: Almost every compiler's AST, IR, and SSA representation is built around generic data structures (e.g., LLVM's `ilist<T>`, `SmallVector<T, N>`).
- **Network protocols**: A generic `Checksum<T>` (where T is `uint8_t`, `uint16_t`, `uint32_t`) prevents bugs that plague C-style checksum routines.

### 2.6 Classification of Generic Subprogram Parameters

Generic formal parameters are **not** restricted to types. They can be:

1. **Type parameters** — `T`, `U`, … (most common)
2. **Value parameters** — compile-time constants (e.g., array bound `N` in `template<class T, int N> class Array`)
3. **Subprogram parameters** — passing an operator or function as a generic parameter (Ada: `with function "<"(L,R : T) return Boolean`)
4. **Package parameters** — passing an entire module (Ada-specific)

This hierarchy is critical: passing *subprogram parameters* lets you write truly generic algorithms (e.g., a generic `Sort` that takes a comparison function as a generic parameter).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — The Generic Swap (Ada)

We walk through the *complete lifecycle* of a generic subprogram, from declaration to instantiation to machine code.

**Step 1 — Generic Declaration (header + specification)**

```ada
generic
   type Item is private;        -- generic formal TYPE parameter
procedure Swap (X, Y : in out Item);
```

- `generic` keyword opens the formal part.
- `type Item is private;` introduces a *type parameter* `Item`. The `is private` constraint means "any non-limited type that supports assignment and copy."

**Step 2 — Generic Body**

```ada
procedure Swap (X, Y : in out Item) is
   Temp : Item;
begin
   Temp := X;
   X   := Y;
   Y   := Temp;
end Swap;
```

The body references `Item` *abstractly* — it has no idea whether `Item` will turn out to be `Integer`, `Float`, `MyRecord`, etc.

**Step 3 — Instantiation**

```ada
procedure Swap_Int is new Swap (Integer);
procedure Swap_Flt is new Swap (Float);
```

The `new` keyword triggers **explicit instantiation**. The compiler now performs a *conceptual substitution*:

$$
\text{Swap}\langle \text{Integer} \rangle \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Swap\_Int} : (\text{in out Integer},\; \text{in out Integer})
$$

**Step 4 — Call Site**

```ada
Swap_Int (A, B);   -- A, B : Integer
```

This is *identical* in efficiency to a hand-written `Swap_Int`. There is no run-time dispatch, no boxing, no `void*`.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The C++ Template Equivalence

**Step 1 — Template declaration**

```cpp
template <class T>
void swapGeneric(T& x, T& y) {
    T temp = x;
    x = y;
    y = temp;
}
```

**Step 2 — Implicit Instantiation**

```cpp
int  a = 3, b = 4;          swapGeneric(a, b);     // generates swapGeneric<int>
float x = 1.5, y = 2.5;     swapGeneric(x, y);     // generates swapGeneric<float>
std::string s1 = "hi", s2 = "bye";
                              swapGeneric(s1, s2);   // generates swapGeneric<std::string>
```

The compiler synthesizes *three* distinct functions in the object file:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object File} = \{ & \text{swapGeneric}\langle\text{int}\rangle(\text{int\&},\;\text{int\&}), \\
                       & \text{swapGeneric}\langle\text{float}\rangle(\text{float\&},\;\text{float\&}), \\
                       & \text{swapGeneric}\langle\text{std::string}\rangle(\text{std::string\&},\;\text{std::string\&}) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ada vs C++ Generic Semantics

| # | Property | Ada Generics | C++ Templates |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Discovery of actual parameters** | Explicit (`new Swap (Integer)`) | Implicit (from call-site arguments) |
| 2 | **Type-checking of body** | Done *once* on the generic body, respecting constraints | Done *twice* — on the abstract body AND on each instantiation |
| 3 | **Separate compilation of body** | Not allowed (body must be visible) | Allowed (explicit instantiation + `extern template`) |
| 4 | **Permitted constraints** | Rich (`is private`, `is range <>`, `is digits <>`, `with function`) | Implicit (via SFINAE / C++20 concepts) |
| 5 | **Parameter kinds** | Types, values, subprograms, packages | Types, non-type values (integers, pointers, enums) |
| 6 | **Failure mode** | Instantiation error: `type does not match constraint` | Substitution Failure Is Not An Error (SFINAE) — silent elimination |
| 7 | **Origin philosophy** | Designed for *safe, deliberate* genericity (1970s ALPHARD/Ada lineage) | Designed for *maximum expressiveness* (1980s Stepanov/Musser lineage) |

### 3.4 Generic Subprogram of a Subprogram — The Highest Form

A generic can take another *subprogram* as a generic parameter — this is the formal mechanism for passing algorithms (think: function pointers, but *type-safe* and *inlined*).

**Ada style:**

```ada
generic
   type Item is private;
   with function "<" (L, R : Item) return Boolean;  -- subprogram as parameter
procedure Generic_Sort (A : in out array (Positive range <>) of Item);
```

You can now do:

```ada
procedure Int_Sort is new Generic_Sort (Integer, Standard."<");
```

The compiler generates a `Sort` that uses the *passed-in* comparison. **One sort algorithm, infinite comparison functions** — the ultimate in reuse.

### 3.5 Constraint Derivation — Why Constraints Matter

Suppose you write:

```ada
generic
   type T is private;
function Max (A, B : T) return T;
```

with body `if A < B then return B else return A end if;`.

The body **requires** that `T` has an operator `$<$`. If the user instantiates `Max` with a type that *does not* have `$<$` (e.g., a complex record with no comparison), the compiler will reject it — but at *instantiation time*, not at generic definition time. This is called **deferred constraint checking**.

A **constrained** version is safer:

```ada
generic
   type T is private;
   with function "<" (L, R : T) return Boolean;   -- constraint!
function Max (A, B : T) return T;
```

Now the constraint $C(T) \equiv (\text{T has operator} \;<)$ is *statically* checked at the generic declaration site, providing earlier, clearer diagnostics.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unconstrained Max instantiation: } & \text{Fail-time} = \text{Instantiation} \\
\text{Constrained Max instantiation:   } & \text{Fail-time} = \text{Generic declaration read}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lifecycle of a Generic Subprogram (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *complete* journey from generic source code to a concrete machine-code instantiation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Generic Source Code: generic type T plus body f of T"] --> B["Compiler Reads Generic Declaration"]
    B --> C{"Is Body Semantically Valid for all T in Constraint Set C?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Compilation Error: Constraint Violation"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Generic Body Stored Symbolically"]
    E --> F["Encounter Instantiation: f less than Integer greater than"]
    F --> G["Substitute T = Integer into Body"]
    G --> H["Type Check Concrete Body"]
    H --> I{"Type Check Pass?"}
    I -- "No" --> J["Instantiation Error"]
    I -- "Yes" --> K["Emit Concrete Code: f integer version"]
    E --> L["Encounter Instantiation: f less than Float greater than"]
    L --> M["Substitute T = Float into Body"]
    M --> H
    K --> N["Linker: Final Executable contains f integer version and f float version"]
    H --> N
```

### 4.2 Constraint Satisfaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GenericHeader["Generic Formal Part"]
        P1["type T is private"]
        P2["with function star left bracket T right bracket return T"]
    end
    GenericHeader --> ConstraintSet["Constraint Set C of T = types supporting assignment and star operator"]
    ConstraintSet --> ActualTypeCheck{"Actual Type T_actual satisfies C?"}
    T1["Integer: has star operator yes, assignable yes"] --> ActualTypeCheck
    T2["Complex number: has star operator yes, assignable yes"] --> ActualTypeCheck
    T3["Abstract Record X: has star operator NO"] --> ActualTypeCheck
    T4["Limited type: assignable NO"] --> ActualTypeCheck
    ActualTypeCheck -- "Yes" --> Accept["Instantiation Accepted: f_T_actual generated"]
    ActualTypeCheck -- "No"  --> Reject["Constraint Violation: compile-time error reported with line number"]
```

### 4.3 Generic vs Overloaded — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Overloading["Overloading Paradigm O of N"]
        O1["Programmer writes Swap_Int"]
        O2["Programmer writes Swap_Float"]
        O3["Programmer writes Swap_String"]
        O4["Compiler picks correct one at call site by argument type"]
    end
    subgraph Generics["Generic Paradigm O of 1"]
        G1["Programmer writes Swap of T: one body"]
        G2["Compiler synthesizes Swap_Int, Swap_Float, Swap_String at instantiation"]
        G3["Call site invokes the correct concrete version"]
    end
    Overloading -.contrasts.- Generics
```

### 4.4 Generic Instantiation Memory & Code Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Source["Source Layer"]
        S1["swapGeneric template less than class T greater than void swapGeneric T ampersand x T ampersand y"]
    end
    Source --> Compiler["Compiler"]
    Compiler --> IntSpec["swapGeneric int version: takes int ampersand int ampersand"]
    Compiler --> FloatSpec["swapGeneric float version: takes float ampersand float ampersand"]
    Compiler --> StrSpec["swapGeneric std string version: takes std string ampersand std string ampersand"]
    IntSpec --> Code["Code Segment: 3 specialized copies"]
    FloatSpec --> Code
    StrSpec --> Code
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Define a *generic subprogram*. How does it differ from an *overloaded* subprogram? Illustrate with a one-line example in C++.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **generic subprogram** is a subprogram parameterised by *type parameters* (and optionally *value* or *subprogram* parameters), such that the *same* body can be instantiated for different actual type arguments, with the compiler automatically generating a type-specialised copy for each distinct instantiation. By contrast, an **overloaded** subprogram consists of *N separately written bodies* — one per type — that happen to share the same identifier. The compiler selects among them by argument-type matching at the call site, but the bodies are independent.

**Illustration (C++):**

```cpp
template <class T> T add(T a, T b) { return a + b; }   // generic: 1 body
int add(int a, int b);                                  // overload: separate body
```

In generics, **one body serves all types**; in overloading, **each type needs its own body**. (3 marks: 1 def + 1 contrast + 1 example)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **List *three* kinds of generic formal parameters in Ada. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ada permits **four** kinds of generic formal parameters (any three suffice for full marks):

1. **Type parameter** — `type T is private;` (the most common; e.g., any non-limited type with assignment).
2. **Value parameter** — `N : Natural;` (compile-time constant, used for array sizes).
3. **Subprogram parameter** — `with function "<"(L,R : T) return Boolean;` (injects an operation into the generic).
4. **Package parameter** — `with package P is new Q (<>);` (parameterises by an entire module).

Example block:

```ada
generic
   type Item     is private;                              -- (1) type
   Size         : Positive;                              -- (2) value
   with function Compare(L,R : Item) return Boolean;      -- (3) subprogram
procedure Sort_Generic (A : in out array (1..Size) of Item);
```

(3 marks: 1 mark per kind with example)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, PEBMT746 Module 3]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define a generic subprogram. Explain the **three logical parts** of a generic subprogram's structure. With a neat diagram, illustrate the **lifecycle** of a generic subprogram — from source code to compiler-generated concrete instances.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Write an **Ada-style generic procedure** that swaps two elements of *any* type. Show two explicit instantiations (one for `Integer`, one for a user-defined record). Also write the **equivalent C++ function template**. Explain why the Ada form requires the generic body to be visible at the instantiation point, while C++ does not.

**Model Solution (a):**

A **generic subprogram** is a subprogram whose signature contains *generic formal parameters* — placeholders for types, values, or subprograms — which are bound to concrete entities at *instantiation time*, causing the compiler to generate one specialised subprogram per distinct binding.

**The three parts:**

1. **Generic Formal Part** — declares the *formals* (e.g., `type T is private`).
2. **Subprogram Specification** — the ordinary signature that follows the formal part (e.g., `procedure Swap(X, Y : in out T);`).
3. **Subprogram Body** — the executable algorithm, written in terms of the formals.

*Lifecycle* (see SECTION 4.1): Source → compiler reads generic → symbolic body stored → at instantiation, type is substituted → concrete body type-checked → machine code emitted → linked into final executable.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Ada:**

```ada
generic
   type T is private;
procedure Generic_Swap (X, Y : in out T);

procedure Generic_Swap (X, Y : in out T) is
   Temp : T;
begin
   Temp := X;
   X   := Y;
   Y   := Temp;
end Generic_Swap;

-- User-defined record
type Point is record
   Xc, Yc : Float;
end record;

-- Instantiations
procedure Swap_Int    is new Generic_Swap (Integer);
procedure Swap_Point  is new Generic_Swap (Point);
```

**C++:**

```cpp
template <class T>
void genericSwap(T& x, T& y) {
    T temp = x;  x = y;  y = temp;
}
```

**Why Ada requires body visibility (and C++ doesn't, with `extern template`):**

Ada's *renaming* and *separate compilation* model treats a generic as a *template* that is *expanded* at every instantiation site. Because the *only* way to validate the generic is to examine the body, the Ada compiler mandates the body to be *physically present* in the same compilation unit as the instantiation. C++, in contrast, supports *explicit instantiation declaration* (`extern template void swapGeneric<int>(int&, int&);`) which instructs the compiler *not* to generate the specialisation in this translation unit — the linker resolves it from another `.o` file where the template *was* instantiated. This makes C++ generics more "library-friendly" but introduces a two-phase name-lookup subtlety.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, PEBMT746 Module 3]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **concept of constraints** on generic formal type parameters. With an example in Ada, explain the difference between an *unconstrained* and a *constrained* generic. Why are constraints said to "shift failure from instantiation to declaration time"?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the *binding time* implications of generics. Using a comparison table, show how generics differ from dynamic polymorphism (subtype polymorphism / inheritance). Give one engineering scenario where generics are *strictly better* than inheritance.

**Model Solution (a):**

A **constraint** is a predicate over the set of admissible actual generic parameters. In Ada, constraints may be:

- **Type category** constraints: `is private`, `is limited private`, `is discrete (<>)`, `is integer`, `is float`, `is fixed point`, `is array (Index range <>) of Element`, `is access`, `is new Parent (<>)`.
- **Subprogram presence** constraints: `with function "..."`.
- **Value** constraints (implicit in `is range <>`).

**Unconstrained vs Constrained — Ada example:**

```ada
-- UNCONSTRAINED
generic
   type T is private;
function Max_U (A, B : T) return T;

function Max_U (A, B : T) return T is
begin
   if A < B then return B else return A end if;       -- requires operator< on T
end Max_U;
```

If the user instantiates `Max_U` with a type that has *no* `$<$` operator, the error is reported at *instantiation time* (after the body has been analysed).

```ada
-- CONSTRAINED
generic
   type T is private;
   with function "<" (L, R : T) return Boolean;       -- explicit subprogram constraint
function Max_C (A, B : T) return T;

function Max_C (A, B : T) return T is
begin
   if "<"(A, B) then return B else return A end if;
end Max_C;
```

Now the `$<$` is a *generic formal parameter*, supplied at instantiation. Errors are flagged the moment the *generic declaration is read*, before any user instantiates it. This is what we mean by **shifting failure to declaration time**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Binding Time Implications:**

In a generic subprogram, the *type* of the parameter is bound at **compile time**, at the point of instantiation. The generated machine code is *monomorphic* — there is *exactly one* type signature per emitted function. Consequently:

- **No v-table indirection** at call site.
- **No boxing** of primitive values into wrapper objects.
- **No runtime type checks** beyond the usual static guarantees.
- **Code bloat**: every distinct instantiation produces a new copy in the binary, so binary size grows linearly with the number of types.

**Comparison Table — Generics vs Dynamic Polymorphism (Inheritance):**

| # | Aspect | Generics | Inheritance (Subtype Polymorphism) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Type binding | **Compile-time** (static) | **Run-time** (dynamic, via v-table) |
| 2 | Generated code | One specialised copy per type | One body, called through a v-pointer |
| 3 | Performance | Equivalent to hand-written monomorphic code | One indirect call per invocation |
| 4 | Type safety | Strong (compile-checked) | Weaker (possible `ClassCastException`) |
| 5 | Binary size | Larger (code bloat) | Smaller (shared code) |
| 6 | Restrictive subtyping | Not required (works on unrelated types) | Requires an `is-a` relationship |
| 7 | Adding a new type | Provide a new instantiation | Edit the class hierarchy |

**Engineering scenario where generics are strictly better — `std::vector` in C++:**

Consider a numerical simulation library that must store large arrays of `float` and `double`. Using **inheritance**, you would have to allocate each element on the heap as a polymorphic `Number*` — losing spatial locality, suffering one virtual call per element access, and triggering 10x–100x slowdown on vectorised inner loops. Using **generics** (`std::vector<float>`, `std::vector<double>`), the compiler generates two distinct, monomorphic storage classes that store elements *contiguously in memory* with *no indirection* — enabling SIMD auto-vectorisation and cache-friendly traversal. Generics are the *only* way to get both **type safety** and **peak performance** for primitive types.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks in PEBMT746 Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Confusing generics with overloading** — *instantiation synthesises a new copy*; overloading does *not*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation.
> 2. **Forgetting the `is private` constraint on Ada type parameters** — `type T;` is **illegal** in Ada. The constraint is mandatory. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Claiming Java generics "generate specialised code"** — they do **not**. Java performs **type erasure** at runtime. Generics in Java are a *compile-time-only* fiction (mostly). Conflating them with C++ templates loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Writing `void Swap<T>(T a, T b)`** — the `$<$T$>$` belongs in the *template parameter list*, not in the function name. Syntax errors are heavily penalised.
> 5. **Forgetting to instantiate explicitly in Ada** — unlike C++, you *cannot* call a generic without a `new` line. The compiler will say "no visible subprogram `Swap`."
> 6. **Stating "generics are slower"** — this is **wrong** for compile-time-bound generics; they are *as fast* as monomorphic code. Examiners mark this as a conceptual error.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Generic Subprogram** = a subprogram with *type parameters* (and possibly value/subprogram parameters) that the compiler specialises at instantiation.
- The three parts of a generic: **formal part, specification, body**.
- **Constraints** restrict the set of admissible actual types; they shift errors from instantiation to declaration time.
- **Instantiation** = `new Swap(Integer)` (Ada) or implicit from call site (C++ templates, Java).
- Generics ≠ Overloading: **one body** vs **N bodies**.
- Generics ≠ Inheritance: **static polymorphism** vs **dynamic polymorphism**; generics are faster, larger; inheritance is slower, smaller.
- **Type erasure** in Java means generics there are *compile-time only* — no specialised bytecode is generated.
- **Code bloat** is the *only* major drawback of generics: $N$ instantiations $\Rightarrow$ $N$ copies in the binary.
- **Generic of a subprogram** is the most powerful form: lets the generic algorithm be *parameterised by the operation it applies* (e.g., comparison in `Sort`).
- Ada's **separate compilation rule** mandates generic body visibility; C++ offers `extern template` to relax this.
- **Language support** checklist to remember: Ada, C++, Java, C#, Haskell — all support generics, with subtle semantic differences.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues for Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design Issues for Functions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of programming language design (specifically following the taxonomy of Sebesta's *Concepts of Programming Languages*, which forms the backbone of KTU's PEBMT746 syllabus), **Design Issues for Functions** refers to the set of fundamental, language-level decisions that a language designer must resolve in order to define how subprograms (functions/procedures) operate at runtime. These decisions govern: 1. The mechanism of **parameter passing** (how arguments are bound to formal parameters). 2. The semantics of **local referencing environments** (how names are resolved inside a subprogram). 3. The treatment of **side effects** (whether a function may modify state outside its local scope). 4. The implementation and semantic correctness of **recursion**. 5. The rules for **overloading, default parameters, and variable-length argument lists**. 6. The status of subprograms as **first-class citizens** (closures, higher-order functions, deep vs. shallow binding). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The university expects students to articulate *why* each design issue exists, *how* a chosen mechanism affects program semantics, and *what trade-offs* the designer must accept. Pure rote memorization of mechanisms (e.g., "Java uses call by value") is **insufficient** — you must justify the choice. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Post-Office" Model Imagine every function call as a transaction at a **Smart Post-Office counter**: - **The Counter (Activation Record):** Each call opens its own private counter. The clerk (CPU) works only on documents placed on *this* counter. - **The Envelope (Parameter Passing):** When you hand over a form, the clerk may either **photocopy it** (call by value — original untouched), **note down the locker number** (call by reference — original can be modified), or **write a forwarding address** (call by name — looked up when actually used). - **The Stamp Book (Local Referencing Environment):** The clerk refers to a *local* stamp book kept in the counter drawer. If a stamp is not found, she consults a hierarchy of drawers (nested static scopes). - **Side Effect:** If the clerk, while stamping, also erases a chalkboard in the lobby, that is a **side effect** — observable outside the counter. - **Recursion:** A clerk may call another counter clerk for help; while she is away, her counter is preserved on a stack. When the helper finishes, she resumes. - **Closure:** A clerk who, after finishing her shift, keeps a "diary" referencing letters from her counter can still use them later — she is a *first-class* citizen with a captured environment. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Every design issue for functions answers a single overarching question: *When a subprogram is invoked, what exactly is copied, shared, preserved, or hidden between the caller and the callee?* ### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics - **Stack Frame Size:** Typically **64 bytes – 1 KB** depending on architecture (saved registers, return address, locals). - **Parameter Slots:** Most ABIs reserve **4–8 bytes per parameter slot** (32-bit vs. 64-bit). - **Recursion Depth Limit:** Default stack size in most OS processes is **1 MB – 8 MB**, limiting recursive calls to roughly **10,000–100,000** frames before stack overflow. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Canonical Design Issues Below is the **structured logic** a KTU examiner expects you to reproduce under any question on this topic. #### 2.1.1 Side Effects - **Definition:** A side effect occurs when a subprogram modifies any state observable outside its own local scope (e.g., a global variable, a reference parameter, a file, I/O). - **Pure functions** have *no* side effects and depend only on their inputs → essential for **functional programming** (Haskell, pure parts of ML/F#). - **Why it matters:** Side effects make reasoning, parallelization, and optimization (common subexpression elimination, memoization) significantly harder. #### 2.1.2 Parameter Passing Methods This is the **most heavily tested sub-topic** in KTU ESE. The five classical mechanisms are: 1. **Call by Value (In mode)** — The actual parameter is *evaluated* and its value is copied into the formal parameter's storage. The formal has its *own* memory cell; modifications are local. 2. **Call by Reference (Inout mode)** — The address of the actual parameter is passed; the formal becomes an *alias* of the actual. Modifications are visible to the caller. 3. **Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore / Inout mode)** — A hybrid: copy in on entry, copy out on return. 4. **Call by Name (Inout mode, textual substitution)** — The actual parameter is *textually substituted* into the callee body and re-evaluated on every use (Algol 60). 5. **Call by Need (Lazy Evaluation)** — Like call by name, but each actual is evaluated **at most once** and the result memoized (Haskell). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Distinction:** Call by reference vs. Call by value-result — they differ when a parameter is aliased to another. Call by reference propagates updates *immediately*; call by value-result propagates them only at function return. #### 2.1.3 Local Referencing Environment - **Local variables** are allocated within the **activation record instance (ARI)** on the run-time stack. - **Lifetime** of a local = duration of activation (created on call, destroyed on return). - **Visibility / Scope** of a local = the textual region where the name is bound (static/dynamic scope). - **Static (Lexical) Scoping:** A name refers to its declaration in the *nearest enclosing block* in the source code. (Used by C, Java, Python, Haskell.) - **Dynamic Scoping:** A name refers to the *most recent* active binding at runtime. (Used by original Lisp, APL, SNOBOL4, bash.) #### 2.1.4 Recursion - A function that calls itself (directly or indirectly). - Requires the run-time system to maintain **separate activation records** for each pending call. - **Tail Recursion:** When the recursive call is the *last* operation, the compiler can reuse the current frame (tail call optimization) → O(1) space instead of O(n). #### 2.1.5 Overloading & Generic Subprograms - **Overloading:** Two or more subprograms share a name but differ in parameter types (Ada, C++). - **Generic (Template) Subprograms:** A *parameterized* subprogram, instantiated at compile time for each type (Ada generics, C++ templates, Java generics). #### 2.1.6 Functions as First-Class Values & Closures - A subprogram may be: (a) assigned to a variable, (b) passed as a parameter, (c) returned from a function. Such languages treat functions as **first-class citizens** (Lisp, Haskell, JavaScript, Python). - A **closure** is a first-class function paired with its **referencing environment**. Two binding strategies: - **Deep Binding:** The environment at the time the closure is *created* is captured. - **Shallow Binding:** The environment at the time the closure is *invoked* is used. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Reference Table for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize the semantics row-by-row.** | # | Parameter Passing Method | Formal Receives | Caller Variable Affected on Exit? | Re-evaluation per Use? | Aliased Parameters Consistent? | Languages Using It | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Call by Value | Copy of value | No (if no global alias) | No | Always consistent | C, Java, Pascal (default) | | 2 | Call by Reference | Address (alias) | Yes | No | May give inconsistent order | Fortran, Pascal `var`, C++ `&` | | 3 | Call by Value-Result | Copy of value | Yes (on return) | No | May differ from call by reference | Ada `out`, Fortran | | 4 | Call by Name | Textual substitution (thunk) | Yes | Yes (thunk re-runs) | May differ from both | Algol 60 | | 5 | Call by Need | Memoized thunk | Yes | No (first time only) | Consistent | Haskell (lazy) | > **Notation Note:** For absolute-value or magnitude expressions in exams, KTU scripts prefer `\vert x \vert` over `|x|` to keep them readable in plain text. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems:** Call by value is preferred to guarantee predictability and bounded stack usage. - **Parallel Computing:** Pure functions (no side effects) are the cornerstone of MapReduce, GPU kernels, and actor models (Erlang/Elixir). - **Compiler Design:** Tail call optimization directly affects the performance of functional language compilers (GHC, OCaml) and even JavaScript engines (V8). - **Cybersecurity:** Call by name and dynamic scoping are notorious sources of *referential opacity* and *race conditions* — a key reason modern languages restrict them. - **Formal Verification:** Pure, side-effect-free functions are the *only* construct over which formal proofs of correctness are tractable. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Activation Record Instance (ARI) — The Heart of a Function Call > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation Goal:** Show the precise memory layout of a stack frame and the order in which the runtime system pushes fields during a function call. This is a guaranteed 7-mark derivation in KTU 2024 ESE. #### 3.1.1 Formal Layout of an Activation Record For a typical block-structured language (C / Java / Pascal family), the activation record instance on the run-time stack contains the following fields, ordered from **low address (top of stack)** to **high address (bottom of stack)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ARI} &= \Big\{ \text{RetAddr},\; \text{DynamicLink},\; \text{StaticLink},\; \\ &\quad\quad \text{Parameters},\; \text{Locals},\; \text{Temporaries} \Big\} \end{aligned} $$ - **RetAddr:** Address in the caller's code to return to. - **DynamicLink:** Pointer to the ARI of the *caller* (forms the call chain). - **StaticLink:** Pointer to the ARI of the *enclosing static parent* (only in nested scopes). - **Parameters:** Slots for incoming arguments. - **Locals:** Local variables declared in the function body. - **Temporaries:** Compiler-generated scratch space for intermediate expressions. #### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Call Protocol Consider a call `y = f(a, b);` where `f` is defined as `int f(int p, int q) { int x; ... }`. **Step 1 — Evaluate Arguments (Caller Side)** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= \text{evaluate } a \\ t_2 &= \text{evaluate } b \end{aligned} $$ The compiler emits code to place the evaluated values in either the callee's parameter slots (caller-evaluated, as in C) or pushes them on the stack in reverse order. **Step 2 — Save Caller State** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{save RetAddr} &\leftarrow PC_{\text{return}} \\ \text{save DynamicLink} &\leftarrow BP_{\text{caller}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Establish New Frame** $$ \begin{aligned} BP_{\text{new}} &\leftarrow SP_{\text{new}} \\ SP &\leftarrow SP - \text{frameSize}(f) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Initialize Parameters** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{slot}[p] &\leftarrow t_1 \\ \text{slot}[q] &\leftarrow t_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Execute Body** Allocate locals, evaluate expressions, and write to `ReturnValue` slot. **Step 6 — Epilogue (Return)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{copy ReturnValue} &\rightarrow y \\ SP &\leftarrow BP_{\text{callee}} \quad \text{(pop frame)} \\ BP &\leftarrow \text{DynamicLink of callee} \\ PC &\leftarrow \text{RetAddr} \quad \text{(jump back)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this order?** The dynamic link is saved *before* the base pointer (BP) is overwritten so that on return, the runtime can restore the caller's environment in a single pop. This is the canonical "Callee-Save / Caller-Save" ABI contract. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Trace — Recursion Call Stack (Factorial) > **Question of the Day:** Trace the activation stack for `fact(3)` in C, assuming static (lexical) scoping. #### 3.2.1 Source ```c int fact(int n) { if (n <= 1) return 1; return n * fact(n - 1); } ``` #### 3.2.2 Activation Stack Growth (Calls) | Step | Call | n value | Stack Frames (top → bottom) | Operation | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `fact(3)` | 3 | `[fact(3)]` | `n<=1` false → calls `fact(2)` | | 2 | `fact(2)` | 2 | `[fact(2), fact(3)]` | `n<=1` false → calls `fact(1)` | | 3 | `fact(1)` | 1 | `[fact(1), fact(2), fact(3)]` | `n<=1` true → **returns 1** | | 4 | Pop `fact(1)` | — | `[fact(2), fact(3)]` | `2 * 1 = 2` returned to `fact(3)` | | 5 | Pop `fact(2)` | — | `[fact(3)]` | `3 * 2 = 6` returned to main | #### 3.2.3 Stack Space Complexity $$ \begin{aligned} S(n) &= n \times \text{frameSize} + O(1) \\ &= \Theta(n) \end{aligned} $$ For a **tail-recursive** rewrite `factTail(n, acc) { if (n<=1) return acc; return factTail(n-1, n*acc); }`, an optimizing compiler performs *tail call elimination*: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\text{tail}}(n) &= 1 \times \text{frameSize} = \Theta(1) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the difference?** In the non-tail version, the multiplication `n * fact(n-1)` must happen *after* the recursive call returns, so each frame must be preserved. In the tail version, the multiplication is an *argument* to the next call — the current frame is no longer needed and can be reused. --- ### 3.3 Comparative Proof — Call by Reference vs. Call by Value-Result > **Theorem (KTU Exam Classic):** When parameters are *not* aliased, call by reference and call by value-result produce **identical results**. When parameters *are* aliased, they may differ. #### 3.3.1 Aliased Case (No Conflict) Program: ```c void f(int x, int y) { x = x + 1; y = y + 2; } int a = 5; f(a, a); ``` | Method | Trace | Final value of `a` | |---|---|---| | Call by Value | `x=5, y=5` (copies) → `x=6, y=7` → copies discarded | **5** | | Call by Reference | `x, y` both alias `a` → first update sets `a=6`, then `a=8` | **8** | | Call by Value-Result | `x=5, y=5` (copies) → `x=6, y=7` → on return, copy out in order: `a=6`, then `a=7` | **7** | **Observation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CBV: } & a = 5 \\ \text{CBR: } & a = 8 \\ \text{CBVR: } & a = 7 \end{aligned} $$ The three results are **pairwise distinct**, proving that aliased parameters expose a genuine semantic difference between the mechanisms. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; node labels contain **raw uppercase text only** (no markdown, no `|` symbols, no nested tables). ### 4.2 Run-Time Stack of a Recursive Call (Top-of-Stack View) ```mermaid graph TD mainARI["MAIN ACTIVATION RECORD
LOCAL VARIABLES AND GLOBALS"] fact3ARI["FACT 3 FRAME
N EQUALS 3
RET ADDR TO MAIN"] fact2ARI["FACT 2 FRAME
N EQUALS 2
RET ADDR TO FACT 3"] fact1ARI["FACT 1 FRAME
N EQUALS 1
RET ADDR TO FACT 2"] returnVal["RETURN VALUE SLOT
CARRIES INTEGER 1"] mainARI --> fact3ARI fact3ARI --> fact2ARI fact2ARI --> fact1ARI fact1ARI --> returnVal ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The arrow direction matches the *push* order. The base of the stack (`mainARI`) is at the bottom, and the most recent frame (`fact1ARI`) is on top — the same convention used in every KTU textbook figure. ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parameter Passing Subsystem ```mermaid graph TD callerScope["CALLER SCOPE
HOLDS ACTUAL ARGUMENTS"] argrEval["ARGUMENT EVALUATION STAGE
LHS TO RHS"] bindingChoice["BINDING SELECTOR
CHOOSES SEMANTICS"] byValue["CALL BY VALUE
COPY OF VALUE"] byRef["CALL BY REFERENCE
ADDRESS ALIAS"] byName["CALL BY NAME
THUNK SUBSTITUTION"] byNeed["CALL BY NEED
MEMOIZED THUNK"] calleeARI["CALLEE ACTIVATION RECORD
FORMAL PARAMETER SLOTS"] sideEffectMonitor["SIDE EFFECT MONITOR
DETECTS GLOBAL MUTATION"] callerScope --> argrEval argrEval --> bindingChoice bindingChoice --> byValue bindingChoice --> byRef bindingChoice --> byName bindingChoice --> byNeed byValue --> calleeARI byRef --> calleeARI byName --> calleeARI byNeed --> calleeARI calleeARI --> sideEffectMonitor ``` ### 4.3 Closure Creation vs. Closure Invocation Flow ```mermaid graph TD defEnv["OUTER FUNCTION DEFINES INNER FN
CAPTURES FREE VARIABLES"] closureObj["CLOSURE OBJECT CREATED
CODE POINTER PLUS CAPTURED ENV"] deepBind["DEEP BINDING
ENV FROZEN AT CREATION"] shallowBind["SHALLOW BINDING
ENV RESOLVED AT INVOCATION"] invoke["CLOSURE INVOKED BY CALLER"] result["RETURN VALUE COMPUTED"] defEnv --> closureObj closureObj --> deepBind closureObj --> shallowBind deepBind --> invoke shallowBind --> invoke invoke --> result ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Deep binding is *safer* (predictable, referentially transparent) and dominates in modern languages (Haskell, Python, JavaScript via `let`). Shallow binding is rarely used in production languages (an exception is early Lisp). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]** #### Q1. Define a *side effect*. Why are pure functions preferred in functional and parallel programming paradigms? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** A *side effect* is any modification of state by a subprogram that is observable outside its local scope — for example, modifying a global variable, mutating a reference parameter, performing I/O, or altering the heap. Pure functions (functions without side effects) are preferred because (i) they depend solely on their inputs, enabling *referential transparency*; (ii) they can be safely executed in any order or in parallel without race conditions; and (iii) they enable powerful compiler optimizations such as common subexpression elimination, memoization, and dead-code elimination. **Marks distribution:** Definition (1), two reasons (1 each) = 3. --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** #### Q2. Differentiate between *static (lexical) scoping* and *dynamic scoping*. Give one example language for each. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** - **Static Scoping:** A name is bound to the declaration in the *nearest enclosing block* in the source code. Resolved at compile time. Example: C, Java, Python. - **Dynamic Scoping:** A name is bound to the *most recent active* declaration at run time on the call stack. Resolved at run time. Example: Original Lisp, SNOBOL4, bash (for some variables). - **Key Distinction:** In `static` the binding is determined by the *program text*; in `dynamic*` it is determined by the *execution history*. **Marks distribution:** Definition of each (1 each), example + key distinction (1) = 3. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Model Paper — Module 3, Internal Choice Pattern]** --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain in detail the **five parameter passing methods** supported by programming languages. For each, describe what the formal parameter receives, whether the caller variable is modified, and the cost/benefit trade-off. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **structure of an activation record instance (ARI)**. Show, step-by-step, the actions performed by the run-time system during a function call and return. Illustrate with a call stack trace of `fact(3)`. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to (a):** 1. **Call by Value:** The actual parameter is evaluated, and a *copy* of its value is bound to the formal. Modifications to the formal do not affect the actual. **Trade-off:** Safe and simple; expensive for large structures (the entire object is copied). Used in C, Java. 2. **Call by Reference:** The *address* of the actual parameter is passed. The formal becomes an *alias* of the actual. Modifications are visible to the caller. **Trade-off:** Cheap for large structures; unsafe and allows aliasing bugs. Used in Fortran, Pascal `var`, C++ `&`. 3. **Call by Value-Result:** A copy is passed in; on return, the final value is copied back to the actual. **Trade-off:** Avoids pointer aliasing pitfalls of call by reference while still allowing output; inconsistent with reference when parameters are aliased. Used in Ada `out`. 4. **Call by Name:** The actual parameter is *textually substituted* into the callee body as an unevaluated *thunk*, re-evaluated on every use. **Trade-off:** Powerful and expressive (lazy semantics); expensive and notoriously hard to implement. Used in Algol 60. 5. **Call by Need:** Like call by name, but each thunk is evaluated *at most once* and the result cached. **Trade-off:** Same lazy semantics as call by name, with O(1) reuse per actual. Used in Haskell. > **Marker Allocation (7 = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 for trade-off and example per mechanism = 7).** --- **Model Solution to (b):** The activation record instance contains, in order: **Return Address → Dynamic Link → Static Link (if nested) → Parameters → Local Variables → Temporaries**. **Call Protocol (each step worth 1 mark):** 1. Evaluate actuals in caller scope; place them in parameter slots. 2. Save return address (PC of next instruction after call). 3. Save dynamic link (caller's base pointer). 4. Update base pointer to current stack top. 5. Allocate locals and temporaries by decrementing SP. 6. Transfer control to callee entry point. **Return Protocol:** 1. Place return value in agreed slot. 2. Restore SP = BP (pop frame). 3. Restore BP = DynamicLink. 4. Jump to RetAddr. 5. Caller resumes; return value is consumed. **fact(3) Call Stack Trace:** - `main` calls `fact(3)` → push frame {n=3, ret=main}. - `fact(3)` calls `fact(2)` → push frame {n=2, ret=fact(3)}; stack height 2. - `fact(2)` calls `fact(1)` → push frame {n=1, ret=fact(2)}; stack height 3. - `fact(1)` returns 1; pop frame. `fact(2)` computes `2*1=2`; pops. `fact(3)` computes `3*2=6`; pops back to `main`. > **Marker Allocation (7 = ARI fields 2 + Call/Return steps 3 + fact trace 2 = 7).** --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Discuss the concept of **subprogram nesting, static links, and dynamic links** with a suitable diagram. How do they differ and why are both required? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **deep binding versus shallow binding** for subprogram closures. Show with an example how the same closure produces *different* outputs under each binding strategy. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to (a):** In block-structured languages, subprograms may be nested within one another. Two distinct pointers are required in every activation record: - **Dynamic Link:** Points to the activation record of the *caller* (the subprogram that *invoked* this one). It is the *runtime* parent and is used to restore the caller's environment on return. - **Static Link:** Points to the activation record of the *static parent* — the subprogram that *textually encloses* this one in the source code. It is used to resolve non-local references under *static scoping*. **Why both are required:** - The dynamic link alone is *insufficient* for static scoping: a function may be called from many different scopes, but its static parent is fixed. - The static link alone is *insufficient* for control flow: returning must go to the *caller*, not the *enclosing scope*. **Diagram of nested subprogram calls (Mermaid):** ```mermaid graph TD mainFn["MAIN ARI
STATIC PARENT IS NONE"] outerFn["OUTER ARI
STATIC LINK TO MAIN
DYNAMIC LINK TO MAIN"] innerFn["INNER ARI
STATIC LINK TO OUTER
DYNAMIC LINK TO OUTER"] mainFn --> outerFn outerFn --> innerFn ``` > **Marker Allocation (7 = definitions 2 + diagram 2 + necessity 3 = 7).** --- **Model Solution to (b):** A **closure** pairs a function body with an *environment* containing the free variables referenced by that body. - **Deep Binding:** The environment captured at the time the closure is *created* is used. The closure carries a snapshot of the bindings that existed when the closure literal was evaluated. - **Shallow Binding:** The environment is *re-resolved* at the time the closure is *invoked*, using whatever binding of the free variable is most recent on the call stack. **Example (pseudo-Pascal-style):** ``` function makeAdder(x) { return function(y) { return x + y; } } a = makeAdder(3) // closure capturing x=3 b = makeAdder(7) // closure capturing x=7 print(a(10)) // Deep: 3+10=13; Shallow: depends on current x print(b(10)) // Deep: 7+10=17 ``` Under **deep binding**, the closures `a` and `b` permanently remember `x=3` and `x=7` respectively, giving **13** and **17**. Under **shallow binding**, both would consult the *current* binding of `x` at the time of the call, potentially yielding the same value — an undesirable, referentially opaque outcome. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write that *deep binding captures the environment at call time* — this is **wrong**. Deep = capture at **creation time**; shallow = at **call time**. Confusing these two is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > **Marker Allocation (7 = closure definition 1 + deep 2 + shallow 2 + example trace 2 = 7).** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers on "Design Issues for Functions" Questions:** > 1. **Stating "Java is call by reference for objects"** — Java is *strictly* call by value (the reference itself is the value copied). This is the most heavily penalized mistake in KTU valuation. > 2. **Omitting the static vs. dynamic link distinction** in nested subprogram questions — both links must appear, *not* one. > 3. **Confusing recursion with iteration complexity** — non-tail recursion is $\Theta(n)$ space, not $\Theta(1)$. > 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark ARI questions — a diagram carries **2 mandatory marks** per the 2024 scheme marking memo. > 5. **Writing `|x|` in tables** — KTU scripts flag this as an "unreadable expression"; use `\vert x \vert`. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Six canonical design issues** for functions: side effects, parameter passing, local referencing environment, recursion, overloading/generics, and first-class functions / closures. - [x] **Five parameter passing methods:** call by value, call by reference, call by value-result, call by name, call by need. Memorize the *cheat sheet table* from Section 2 verbatim. - [x] **Activation Record Instance (ARI)** fields: RetAddr → DynamicLink → StaticLink → Parameters → Locals → Temporaries. - [x] **Java, C, Python use call by value**; **Fortran, Pascal `var`, C++ `&` use call by reference**; **Algol 60 uses call by name**; **Haskell uses call by need**. - [x] **Deep binding = capture at creation; shallow binding = capture at invocation.** Deep is *referentially transparent* and dominant in modern languages. - [x] **Static (lexical) scoping** resolves names by source nesting; **dynamic scoping** resolves by call stack. Static is dominant. - [x] **Recursion is implemented** via separate ARIs on the run-time stack; tail recursion can be optimized to $\Theta(1)$ space. - [x] **Side effects** are the central reason pure functions matter for parallel and functional programming. - [x] **Generic subprograms** (Ada generics, C++ templates, Java generics) provide type-parameterized reuse without inheritance. - [x] **Closures** = code + captured environment; **first-class functions** can be passed, returned, and stored like any value. ---

topics.title: User Defined Overloaded Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** User Defined Overloaded Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Operator Overloading** is a compile-time polymorphism technique in C++ that赋予 user-defined types (classes/structs) the ability to redefine the meaning of existing C++ operators (such as `+`, `-`, `*`, `<<`, `[]`, etc.) so that they operate intuitively on objects of those types. A **user-defined overloaded operator** is a special function, declared with the keyword `operator` followed by the operator symbol, whose purpose is to extend the semantics of a built-in operator to work with operands of class/struct type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Operator overloading is a cornerstone of the *Run-Time / Compile-Time Polymorphism* cluster. The Board expects students to differentiate between *overloading as a member function* and *overloading as a friend (non-member) function*, and to justify the choice for symmetric binary operators such as `+`, `<<`, and `>>`.

Mathematically, if $T$ is a user-defined type and $\oplus$ is an overloaded operator, we model the operation as:

$$ a \oplus b \;\longmapsto\; f_{\oplus}(a, b) \quad \text{where} \;\; a, b \in T $$

Here, $f_{\oplus}$ is the user-defined operator function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you already know the word **"Add"** — it works on numbers: $2 + 3 = 5$. Now, suppose you create a brand-new object called `Complex`. Without overloading, the compiler has no idea what `c1 + c2` means. **Operator overloading is like teaching the `+` sign a new vocabulary** so that when you write `c1 + c2`, the compiler knows to invoke your custom function that adds real parts and adds imaginary parts separately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a multi-tool (Swiss Army knife). The *same* physical tool has a *blade*, *scissors*, and *screwdriver*. The tool's "operator" (the gesture you make) is *overloaded* — one tool, many meanings, depending on context. C++ does the same: one symbol, many behaviors, depending on operand types.

## 1.3 Why We Need User-Defined Overloaded Operators

| Reason | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| **Natural Syntax** | Enables `c3 = c1 + c2;` instead of `c3 = c1.add(c2);` |
| **Readability** | Code becomes self-documenting for domain types (`Matrix`, `Vector`, `Complex`, `Money`) |
| **Type Safety** | Compile-time checked, unlike runtime polymorphism with virtual functions |
| **Symmetry** | `a + b` looks identical to `b + a` when implemented via friend functions |
| **Integration with STL** | STL algorithms and streams expect overloaded `==`, `<`, `<<` |

## 1.4 Operators That CAN and CANNOT Be Overloaded

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Board frequently asks a **2-mark question** distinguishing overloadable from non-overloadable operators.

**Overloadable (the majority):** `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`, `^`, `&`, `\vert`, `~`, `!`, `=`, `<`, `>`, `+=`, `-=`, `<<`, `>>`, `<<=`, `>>=`, `==`, `!=`, `<=`, `>=`, `&&`, `||`, `++`, `--`, `[]`, `()`, `->`, `->*`, `,`, `new`, `new[]`, `delete`, `delete[]`.

**Non-Overloadable (only 5):**
- `.` (member access)
- `.*` (pointer-to-member access)
- `::` (scope resolution)
- `?:` (ternary conditional)
- `sizeof` (size operator)

**Cannot be overloaded (3 special):** `#` (preprocessor), `##` (token pasting), and any operator containing only non-symbol characters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The General Syntax

An operator function has the form:

$$ \text{return\_type} \;\; \texttt{operator} \oplus \;\; (\text{parameter\_list}) $$

There are **two valid scopes** for an operator function:

1. **As a Non-Static Member Function** of the class.
2. **As a Friend (Non-Member) Function** of the class, often declared with the `friend` keyword inside the class.

A third variant — a **non-friend non-member function** — is allowed but cannot access private/protected members.

## 2.2 General Rules (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

- The **precedence**, **associativity**, and **arity** (number of operands) of an operator **cannot be changed**.
- The **short-circuit evaluation semantics of built-in operators cannot be preserved** when overloading `&&` and `||`.
- At least **one operand must be a user-defined type** (you cannot overload an operator for two built-in types).
- It is **illegal to define a new operator symbol** that does not already exist in C++.
- The default meaning of an operator for built-in types **cannot be changed**.

## 2.3 Member vs Friend — The Core Decision Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** If the left operand is *not* an object of your class (e.g., `cout << obj`, `5 + obj`), you **must** use a friend/non-member function. Otherwise, a member function is preferred because it implicitly accesses `this`.

The argument count for the two styles differs by exactly one (since `this` is implicit in members):

$$ \text{Arg}_{\text{member}} = \text{Arg}_{\text{friend}} - 1 $$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operator Category | Examples | Typical Member Signature | Typical Friend Signature | Arity |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Unary Arithmetic | `+`, `-` (unary) | `T operator+() const;` | `friend T operator+(const T&);` | **Unary** |
| Binary Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` | `T operator+(const T&) const;` | `friend T operator+(const T&, const T&);` | **Binary** |
| Relational | `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>` | `bool operator==(const T&) const;` | `friend bool operator==(const T&, const T&);` | **Binary** |
| Stream I/O | `<<`, `>>` | ❌ Cannot be member (left is `ostream`) | `friend ostream& operator<<(ostream&, const T&);` | **Binary** |
| Assignment | `=`, `+=`, `-=` | `T& operator=(const T&);` | ❌ Must be member (left is `T`) | **Binary** |
| Subscript | `[]` | `T& operator[](size_t);` | ❌ Must be member | **Unary (binary-looking)** |
| Function Call | `()` | `R operator()(args...);` | ❌ Must be member | **Variable (functor)** |
| Pre/Post Increment | `++`, `--` | `T& operator++();` / `T operator++(int);` | Possible but unusual | **Unary** |

**Boundary / Special Conditions:**

- **Pre-increment** signature has **no dummy parameter**: `T& operator++();`
- **Post-increment** signature has a **dummy `int` parameter**: `T operator++(int);` — this `int` is a compiler convention, never used.
- **Stream insertion `<<` MUST return `ostream&`** so that chains like `cout << a << b;` work.
- **Assignment `=` MUST return `T&` (reference)** to support `a = b = c;`.
- **`operator[]` MUST be a non-static member function** (per ISO C++ standard).
- **`operator()` MUST be a non-static member function** (used to create *functors*).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Operator overloading is the **backbone of scientific computing libraries** in C++:

- **Eigen & Armadillo:** Linear-algebra `Matrix` types with `+`, `*`, `<<`.
- **Boost.Units:** Physical-dimension arithmetic (force + mass is illegal, but a custom type catches it).
- **Money / Decimal libraries:** `Money` type with safe `+` and `<<` operators.
- **CAD & FEM software:** `Vector3D` types where cross/dot products use `*` and `^`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Overloading `+` for a `Complex` Class (Member Function)

**Problem Statement:** Create a `Complex` class with real and imaginary parts, and overload `+` so that `c3 = c1 + c2` adds complex numbers according to $z_1 + z_2 = (a_1+a_2) + i(b_1+b_2)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the operand type.** Both `c1` and `c2` are of type `Complex`. Hence `+` can be implemented as a **member function**, where `c1` is implicitly passed as `this`.

**Step 2 — Derive the algebraic result.** The complex number is $z = a + ib$.

$$ c_1 = a_1 + i b_1, \quad c_2 = a_2 + i b_2 $$

$$ c_1 + c_2 = (a_1 + a_2) + i (b_1 + b_2) $$

**Step 3 — Translate to code.**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Complex {
private:
    double re, im;
public:
    Complex(double r = 0, double i = 0) : re(r), im(i) {}

    // Operator+ as MEMBER function
    Complex operator+(const Complex& rhs) const {
        Complex result;
        result.re = this->re + rhs.re;
        result.im = this->im + rhs.im;
        return result;
    }

    void display() const {
        cout << re << " + " << im << "i" << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    Complex c1(2.0, 3.0), c2(4.0, 5.0);
    Complex c3 = c1 + c2;   // calls c1.operator+(c2)
    c3.display();            // Output: 6 + 8i
    return 0;
}
```

**Step 4 — Compiler Translation Proof.** The expression `c1 + c2` is silently rewritten by the compiler to:

$$ \texttt{c1.operator+(c2)} $$

This rewriting follows the C++ standard rule: for a binary operator `@` defined as a member, `L @ R` becomes `L.operator@(R)`.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Overloading `<<` as a Friend Function

**Problem Statement:** Overload the stream insertion operator `<<` so `cout << c;` prints the `Complex` object.

**Step 1 — Identify the left operand.** The left operand of `<<` is `ostream` (e.g., `cout`), **not** a `Complex`. Therefore, `operator<<` **cannot** be a member of `Complex`. It must be a non-member — usually a `friend` for access to private data.

**Step 2 — Determine return type.** Returning `ostream&` is mandatory so that cascading works:

$$ \texttt{cout} \ll c1 \ll c2 \;\longmapsto\; (\texttt{cout} \ll c1) \ll c2 $$

**Step 3 — Code implementation.**

```cpp
class Complex {
private:
    double re, im;
public:
    Complex(double r = 0, double i = 0) : re(r), im(i) {}

    // Friend declaration
    friend ostream& operator<<(ostream& out, const Complex& c);
};

ostream& operator<<(ostream& out, const Complex& c) {
    out << c.re << " + " << c.im << "i";
    return out;   // Return the stream to allow chaining
}

int main() {
    Complex c(2.0, 3.0);
    cout << "The complex number is: " << c << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Step 4 — Compiler Translation.**

$$ \texttt{cout} \ll c \;\longmapsto\; \texttt{operator<<(cout, c)} $$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Overloading Pre- and Post-Increment (Member)

**Step 1 — Algebraic difference.** For a `Counter` class with integer value `n`:

- **Pre-increment:** $n \to n+1$ *then* use the new value. Signature: `T& operator++();`
- **Post-increment:** use the *old* value *then* increment. Signature: `T operator++(int);`

**Step 2 — Code implementation.**

```cpp
class Counter {
private:
    int n;
public:
    Counter(int v = 0) : n(v) {}

    // Pre-increment
    Counter& operator++() {
        ++n;
        return *this;
    }

    // Post-increment
    Counter operator++(int) {
        Counter temp = *this;   // Save old state
        ++n;                    // Increment
        return temp;            // Return old state
    }

    int get() const { return n; }
};
```

**Step 3 — Compiler Translation Proof.**

| Expression | Compiler Translation |
| --- | --- |
| `++c` | `c.operator++()` |
| `c++` | `c.operator++(0)` |

The dummy `int` is a **compile-time tag** to distinguish pre- from post-increment. The value `0` is never used.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Member vs Friend

| Scenario | Left Operand | Recommended Style | Reason |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `c1 + c2` | `Complex` | Member | `this` works as left operand |
| `5 + c1` | `int` (built-in) | **Friend** | Cannot add `int::operator+` |
| `cout << c` | `ostream` (built-in) | **Friend** | Cannot modify `ostream` class |
| `c = a` | `Complex` | Member | Assignment must be member |
| `c[i]` | `Complex` | Member (forced) | Standard requires member |
| `c()` | `Complex` | Member (forced) | Function-call operator must be member |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule:** If your class is the *left* operand, prefer a member function. If the *left* operand is built-in (or another class you cannot modify), use a friend function.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Code Skeleton for KTU Exam

```cpp
class Vector {
private:
    int* data;
    int size;
public:
    Vector(int s = 0) : size(s), data(new int[s]) {}

    // Rule of Three / Five (essential for KTU!)
    Vector(const Vector& other);                 // Copy constructor
    Vector& operator=(const Vector& rhs);        // Copy assignment
    ~Vector() { delete[] data; }

    // Binary + as member
    Vector operator+(const Vector& rhs) const;

    // Subscript as member
    int& operator[](int index) { return data[index]; }

    // Stream insertion as friend
    friend ostream& operator<<(ostream&, const Vector&);
};
```

This is the **canonical pattern** the KTU Board expects when students answer "design a class with overloaded operators."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Member vs Friend Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Overloading] --> B{Left operand is built-in orstream?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use friend / non-member function]
    B -- No --> D{Operator is assignment, subscript, function-call, or member-access?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Must be non-static member function]
    D -- No --> F{Both operands are user-defined class type?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Member function preferred - this pointer]
    F -- No --> H[Friend function - symmetry required]

    C --> I[Example: cout less-than less-than obj]
    E --> J[Example: obj bracket index, obj equals obj]
    G --> K[Example: complex1 plus complex2]
    H --> L[Example: scalar plus complex]
```

## 4.2 Compiler Translation Map for `c1 + c2`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["User Code"]
        U1["c1 + c2"]
    end

    subgraph MEMBER["Member-Function Form"]
        M1["c1.operator+(c2)"]
    end

    subgraph FRIEND["Friend-Function Form"]
        F1["operator+(c1, c2)"]
    end

    U1 --> M1
    U1 --> F1

    M1 --> R["Result: Complex object"]
    F1 --> R
```

## 4.3 Architecture of an Overloaded Operator Call

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source code: c3 = c1 + c2] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Parser identifies operator+ and operands]
    C --> D{Lookup: Is operator+ a member of Complex?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Bind this = address of c1]
    D -- No --> F[Look for global/friend operator+]
    E --> G[Generate call to c1.operator+c2]
    F --> H[Generate call to friend operator+ c1, c2]
    G --> I[Execute operator function body]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Return Complex object]
    J --> K[Assign to c3 via copy or move]
```

## 4.4 Operator Categories Map (Revision Aid)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Overloaded Operators))
    Unary
      Arithmetic
        Plus
        Minus
      Increment
        Pre
        Post
    Binary
      Arithmetic
        Plus
        Minus
        Star
        Slash
      Relational
        EqualsEquals
        NotEquals
        LessThan
      Logical
        AndAnd
        OrOr
    Stream
      OutputOp
      InputOp
    Special
      Subscript
      FunctionCall
      Arrow
    Assignment
      Equals
      PlusEquals
      MinusEquals
```

## 4.5 Resolution Hierarchy (Why Friend Cannot Access `this`)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MF["Member Function Context"]
        M1["Implicit this pointer"]
        M2["Access to all private and protected members"]
    end

    subgraph FF["Friend Function Context"]
        F1["No this pointer"]
        F2["Must take both operands explicitly"]
        F3["Friend keyword grants private access"]
    end

    MF --> M3[Receiver is the left operand]
    FF --> F4[Symmetric - both operands equal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: What is operator overloading? List any four operators that cannot be overloaded in C++.**

**Model Answer:**
Operator overloading is a compile-time polymorphism mechanism that赋予 existing C++ operators additional meanings when applied to user-defined types. It allows programmers to define how operators such as `+`, `-`, `<<`, etc., behave with objects of classes/structs.

**Four operators that cannot be overloaded:**
1. `.` (member access operator)
2. `.*` (pointer-to-member operator)
3. `::` (scope resolution operator)
4. `?:` (ternary conditional operator)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between overloading a binary operator as a member function and as a friend function.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Member Function | Friend Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Number of explicit parameters | One (left operand = `this`) | Two (both operands) |
| Left operand | Always an object of the class | Can be any type |
| Use case | `c1 + c2` | `5 + c1`, `cout << c1` |
| Access to private members | Direct (via `this`) | Allowed only if declared `friend` |
| Symmetry | Asymmetric (this is implicit) | Symmetric (both explicit) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]** Design a `Vector2D` class with `double x, y` members. Overload the `+` operator to add two `Vector2D` objects. Demonstrate with a `main()` function.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]** Why is the stream insertion operator `<<` overloaded as a friend function and not as a member function? Write the complete function to overload `<<` to display a `Vector2D` object.

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Vector2D {
private:
    double x, y;
public:
    Vector2D(double xv = 0, double yv = 0) : x(xv), y(yv) {}

    // Overloaded + as MEMBER function
    Vector2D operator+(const Vector2D& rhs) const {
        return Vector2D(x + rhs.x, y + rhs.y);
    }

    void display() const {
        cout << "(" << x << ", " << y << ")" << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    Vector2D v1(3.0, 4.0), v2(1.0, 2.0);
    Vector2D v3 = v1 + v2;   // Equivalent to v1.operator+(v2)
    v3.display();            // Output: (4, 6)
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation:** The expression `v1 + v2` is translated by the compiler to `v1.operator+(v2)`. The new object's `x` and `y` are computed as the sum of the corresponding components, following the standard vector addition formula:

$$ \vec{v_3} = (x_1 + x_2, y_1 + y_2) $$

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

`operator<<` **cannot** be a member of `Vector2D` because the *left* operand is `ostream` (specifically `cout`), and we cannot add member functions to the `ostream` class. Therefore, it must be a non-member function. Declaring it as a `friend` allows it to access the private members `x` and `y`.

```cpp
class Vector2D {
private:
    double x, y;
public:
    Vector2D(double xv = 0, double yv = 0) : x(xv), y(yv) {}
```
