```mermaid
    end
    subgraph BOT["Bottom Frame Oldest Activation"]
        F1["Frame 1: main x y Saved FP1 RetAddr to main"]
    end
    F3 --> F2
    F2 --> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Frame 3 is the *currently executing* activation. The dashed-style downward arrows conceptually represent the direction of stack growth. The `Saved FP_n` slot in each frame is the **dynamic link** that, when traversed, leads back to the caller.

## 4.4 Static Chain Visualisation for Nested Scopes

```mermaid
graph TB
    P["Outer Procedure P Static Link points here"] --> Q["Middle Procedure Q Static Link points here"]
    Q --> R["Inner Procedure R Current Execution Static Link points here"]
    R -.-> Q
    Q -.-> P
    style R fill:#ffe4b5
    style Q fill:#b0e0e6
    style P fill:#98fb98
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Cue:** The dotted arrows (`.->`) represent the *static links*. Notice that even if R was *called* from some other procedure, its static link still points to its **lexical parent** Q, not its actual caller. This is the heart of the lexical-scoping rule.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Call Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CALLER["Caller Subprogram Block"]
        CE["Argument Evaluator"]
        CS["Call Sequence Code Emitter"]
    end
    subgraph HARDWARE["Hardware CALL Instruction"]
        HW["Push PC plus 4 and Jump"]
    end
    subgraph CALLEE["Callee Subprogram Block"]
        PRLG["Prologue Save FP Allocate Locals"]
        BODY["Body Execute Statements"]
        EPLG["Epilogue Place Return Value Restore State"]
    end
    subgraph RET["Return Mechanism"]
        RP["Restore PC from stack or link register"]
        CC["Caller Cleanup Adjust SP"]
    end
    CE --> CS
    CS --> HW
    HW --> PRLG
    PRLG --> BODY
    BODY --> EPLG
    EPLG --> RP
    RP --> CC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this architecture matters:** This block diagram shows the **division of labour** between caller, hardware, and callee. Notice the hardware boundary in the middle: the **CALL** and **RET** instructions straddle the boundary, but the saving/restoring of *programmer-visible* state is split between caller and callee based on the **calling convention** of the language/ABI.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Activation Record Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define an activation record. List any four of its components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **activation record** is a contiguous block of stack memory allocated for a single invocation of a subprogram, holding all the information needed to execute and later return from that invocation. It is created during the **prologue** of the callee and destroyed during the **epilogue**. **(1 mark)**

Four key components are: **(2 marks — 0.5 each)**

1. **Parameters** — storage for the actual arguments passed by the caller.
2. **Return Address** — the address in the caller to which control must return.
3. **Dynamic Link (Saved FP)** — pointer to the activation record of the caller.
4. **Local Variables** — storage for variables declared inside the subprogram.

*(Other acceptable components: return-value slot, static link, saved registers, temporaries.)*

### Question 2 — Calling vs. Return Sequence `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a calling sequence and a return sequence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Calling Sequence | Return Sequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **When** | Before the callee begins | After the callee finishes |
| **Initiated by** | The caller's CALL | The callee's RETURN |
| **Actions** | Evaluate and push parameters, push return address, jump to callee | Place return value, restore saved registers/FP, pop frame, jump back |
| **Direction** | Caller → Callee | Callee → Caller |

**(1 mark for the basic distinction, 2 marks for the comparison table or 3 well-explained differences.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — General Semantics of Subprogram Calls and Returns `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the general semantics of subprogram calls and returns with a neat diagram. What is the role of the calling sequence and the return sequence?**

**Model Solution:**

The **general semantics of calls and returns** describe the standardized, contractually-defined behaviour that occurs at the boundary between a caller and a callee subprogram. The execution of any subprogram call proceeds through the following conceptual phases:

1. **Call Initiation** — The caller evaluates the actual parameters in the calling environment and prepares them for the callee. This may involve pushing them onto the stack or placing them in registers per the calling convention. **(1 mark)**
2. **Control Transfer** — A CALL instruction saves the return address (the address of the instruction immediately following the CALL) and transfers the program counter to the callee's entry point. **(1 mark)**
3. **Prologue** — The callee saves the caller's frame pointer (dynamic link), sets up its own FP, and decrements SP to make room for its locals and temporaries. **(1 mark)**
4. **Body Execution** — The callee executes its statements, addressing all locals via fixed offsets from FP. **(1 mark)**
5. **Epilogue** — The callee places its return value in a pre-agreed location (e.g., a register or a return-value slot in the AR), restores saved registers, and resets FP and SP. **(1 mark)**
6. **Return** — A RET instruction pops the saved return address into the PC, and the caller resumes from exactly where it left off, optionally cleaning up the arguments. **(1 mark)**
7. **Role Summary** — The *calling sequence* handles everything that must happen *before* control jumps to the callee; the *return sequence* handles the symmetric actions *after* the callee completes. Together they ensure that subprogram invocations are **re-entrant, nestable, and exception-safe**. **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat diagram, describe the structure of an activation record. Explain each component in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

The **activation record (AR)** is the unit of stack storage for one subprogram call. Its structure (stack growing downward) is:

| Slot (high to low) | Component | Size (typical) | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Return Value | 4–8 bytes | Holds the value the callee returns |
| 2 | Actual Parameters | 4–8 B each | Inputs from the caller |
| 3 | Return Address | 4–8 B | Resumption point in the caller |
| 4 | Dynamic Link | 4–8 B | Saved FP, used for return |
| 5 | Static Link | 4–8 B | Pointer to lexically-enclosing AR (nested languages only) |
| 6 | Saved Registers | Variable | Callee-save register contents |
| 7 | Local Variables | Variable | The callee's named locals |
| 8 | Temporaries | Variable | Compiler-generated scratch |

**Explanation of each component: (1 mark each for the most important four, and 0.5 each for the remaining three)**

- **Return Value:** Pre-allocated so the caller knows where to look after the call.
- **Actual Parameters:** Placed either on the stack (cdecl) or in registers (fastcall, x86-64 ABI) for callee access via fixed offsets.
- **Return Address:** Pushed automatically by the CALL instruction.
- **Dynamic Link:** Without this, the RETURN would not know which frame to pop.
- **Static Link:** Only present in languages that support lexical nesting of subprograms.
- **Saved Registers:** Required so the callee can freely use registers without corrupting the caller's state.
- **Locals and Temporaries:** The "workspace" of the callee; destroyed at return.

---

### Question B — Stack-Based Storage and Parameter Passing `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain stack-based storage allocation for subprograms. How are local variables, parameters, and return addresses managed during a function call?**

**Model Solution:**

**Stack-Based Storage Allocation** is the dominant strategy used by virtually all modern language implementations (C, C++, Java methods of static methods, Pascal, etc.) for managing subprogram activations. It uses a **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure** — the call stack — where each invocation pushes a new activation record and each return pops it. **(1 mark)**

**Management details: (6 marks — 1 each)**

1. **Local Variables:** Allocated in the new AR when the callee's prologue runs. They are addressed via fixed offsets from the frame pointer (e.g., `local_i = [FP - 4 * i]`). They are automatically deallocated when the AR is popped. **(1 mark)**
2. **Parameters:** Either pushed by the caller before the CALL (stack-based convention) or passed in registers. In either case, the callee references them via fixed offsets from FP. **(1 mark)**
3. **Return Address:** Saved automatically by the hardware CALL instruction into the program counter's link register or pushed onto the stack. Retrieved by the RET instruction on return. **(1 mark)**
4. **Frame Pointer (FP):** Saved and restored across calls. Acts as a stable anchor for addressing locals and parameters. **(1 mark)**
5. **Stack Pointer (SP):** Decremented on entry to allocate the AR, incremented on exit to deallocate. **(1 mark)**
6. **Stack Overflow:** If SP drops below the stack limit, the OS raises a stack-overflow error. The maximum safe recursion depth is $\lfloor \text{StackLimit} / \vert AR \vert \rfloor$. **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Compare the four major parameter passing mechanisms — pass-by-value, pass-by-reference, pass-by-value-result, and pass-by-name — with suitable examples and diagrams where appropriate.**

**Model Solution:**

| Mechanism | What is Bound to Formal | Where the Actual Lives | Side Effects on Actual? | Used By |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pass-by-Value** | A *copy* of the actual | In the AR of the callee | No | C, Java (primitives) |
| **Pass-by-Reference** | An *address* (pointer to actual) | In the caller's scope | Yes — callee can modify | Pascal `var`, C++ `&`, Fortran |
| **Pass-by-Value-Result** | A *copy in* on entry, *copy out* on return | Both copies in callee's AR | Yes (after return only) | Fortran, Ada `in out` (in some compilers) |
| **Pass-by-Name** | A *re-evaluated thunk* (closure-like) | In the caller's scope, re-evaluated on each use | Yes (and at unpredictable moments) | Algol 60 (classic), C macros (loosely) |

**Example Walkthrough (Pass-by-Reference):**
```c
void swap(int *a, int *b) { int t = *a; *a = *b; *b = t; }
int x = 5, y = 10;
swap(&x, &y);
// Now x = 10, y = 5 — the originals were modified via the reference.
```
**(1 mark for each of the four mechanism explanations, 2 marks for the table, 1 mark for the example.)**

**Key Insight on Pass-by-Name:** Pass-by-name requires the compiler to wrap the actual parameter in a *thunk* — a small closure that re-evaluates the actual each time the formal is referenced. This is why it can produce non-intuitive results (the classic Algol 60 Jensen's Device example for summation), and why it has been abandoned in modern languages in favour of pass-by-value-result or pass-by-reference.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students *consistently* lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing static and dynamic links** — The static link is *lexical*, not *dynamic*. Don't write that "static link points to caller." **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting the return address is saved by hardware** — Many students describe the compiler saving it; in most ISAs, the CALL instruction does this automatically. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Drawing the stack growing *upward*** — KTU convention is always **growing downward**. An upward stack is considered a serious notational error. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Mixing SP and FP roles** — The stack pointer moves on every push/pop; the frame pointer is a stable anchor. Conflating them is a 2-mark deduction. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 5. **Failing to specify the calling convention** — When asked to trace a call, always name the convention (cdecl, stdcall, fastcall, x86-64 SysV). **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 6. **Skipping units in size calculations** — Always write "bytes" or "MiB" after a number. Bare numbers lose half a mark per occurrence. **(Lose 0.5 marks each)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following bullets are your 60-second exam revision summary. Memorise these.**

- **Activation Record (AR)** = the unit of stack storage for one subprogram call; created in prologue, destroyed in epilogue. **(Core concept)**
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** = top of stack, moves on every push/pop. **(volatile)**
- **Frame Pointer (FP)** = stable anchor within the AR; all locals/params are at fixed offsets from FP. **(stable)**
- **Calling Sequence** = actions before CALL (split between caller and callee). **Return Sequence** = actions after body completes (symmetric). **(Vocabulary must-know)**
- **Dynamic Link** = pointer to *caller's* AR (used for return). **(runtime concept)**
- **Static Link** = pointer to *lexical parent's* AR (used for non-local access in nested languages). **(lexical concept)**
- **Six Stages:** Save RetAddr → Save Old FP → Push Params → Jump → Set New FP & Allocate Locals → Execute Body → Epilogue → RET. **(Lifecycle)**
- **Recursion Depth Bound:** $n_{\max} = \lfloor \text{StackLimit} / \vert AR \vert \rfloor$. **(Formula)**
- **Parameter Passing:** Value (copy, no side effect), Reference (address, side effect), Value-Result (copy-in/copy-out, side effect at return), Name (thunk, side effect unpredictable). **(Comparison must-know)**
- **Stack Overflow** occurs when $\sum \vert AR_i \vert$ exceeds the OS-allocated stack limit (default **8 MiB** on Linux, **1 MiB** on Windows main thread). **(Engineering fact)**
- **Closures require heap allocation** because the function's environment may outlive the calling frame. **(Modern language insight)**
- **Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)** is a security exploit that abuses the saved return address mechanism — a reason for stack canaries and shadow stacks. **(Security angle, KTU sometimes asks)**
- **Standard frame sizes:** 32–256 B for typical C functions; ≥ 1 KB for Python frame objects. **(Language comparison)**
- **Calling Conventions:** cdecl (caller cleans stack), stdcall (callee cleans), fastcall (registers), x86-64 SysV (first 6 integer args in registers). **(ABI knowledge)**
- **Mermaid/Figure tip for KTU answers:** Always label the diagram with **stack growing downward** arrow, mark the top of stack explicitly, and use boxes (not lines) for AR components. **(Presentation marks)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Simple Subprograms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing Simple Subprograms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Subprogram** is a named, parameterized, and reusable block of code that performs a specific task and can be invoked (called) from any point within a program. The execution of a subprogram is triggered by a **subprogram call**, after which control is transferred to the subprogram body, and upon completion, control returns to the instruction immediately following the call.

A **Simple Subprogram** is a restricted, elementary form of a subprogram characterized by the following defining properties (as per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus on Intermediate Code Generation / Run-Time Environments):

1. **No Nested Subprograms**: The subprogram body does **not** contain any definitions of other subprograms.
2. **No References to Non-Local Variables**: All variables accessed within the subprogram are either local, parameters, or global. There are no access links to enclosing scopes because enclosing scopes do not exist for simple subprograms.
3. **Single, Self-Contained Activation**: Each invocation produces exactly one activation record on the runtime stack, and once the call completes, the activation record is destroyed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> The study of *Simple Subprograms* is the foundational building block before progressing to **Subprograms with Nested References** (Module 3 advanced section) and **Closures / Lambda Implementations** (later modules). Mastering this topic is mandatory for writing KTU ESE answers on activation records and calling sequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Terminology Standardization**
> - **Subprogram Call** ≡ Function Invocation ≡ Procedure Invocation
> - **Activation** ≡ The execution of a subprogram instance
> - **Activation Record (AR)** ≡ Stack Frame ≡ Call Frame
> - **Call Site** ≡ The location in the calling code where invocation occurs

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are working in a **post office (the main program)**. A customer walks in and asks for a specific service, say, calculating postage. The clerk (CPU) does not have time to do it personally, so they:

1. Pull a **standard form (Activation Record)** from a filing cabinet (Stack).
2. **Fill in the form** with the package weight and dimensions (Parameter Passing).
3. **Hand it to a specialist** in the back room (Transfer of Control).
4. The specialist computes the postage and **writes the answer on the form** (Return Value).
5. The clerk **takes the form back**, processes the payment, and **files the form away** (Return from Subprogram / Deallocation).
6. The clerk **returns to serving the next customer** (Resumes caller).

This standardized form is the **Activation Record**, and the choreography of steps 1–6 is the **Calling Sequence** and **Return Sequence**.

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Metric | Standard Value (Typical KTU/Reference Architecture) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Word Size** | **32 bits (4 bytes)** or **64 bits (8 bytes)** |
| **Return Address Size** | **1 word** |
| **Dynamic Link Size** | **1 word** (pointer to caller's AR) |
| **Static Link Size** | **0 words** for simple subprograms (no nesting) |
| **Stack Growth Direction** | **Towards Lower Memory Addresses** (standard convention) |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU theory questions, the static link is **explicitly zero or absent** for simple subprograms. Always mention this to earn the "completeness" mark.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics of a Simple Subprogram

A simple subprogram differs from a complex (nested) subprogram in three quantifiable ways:

- **No Nesting Depth**: Nesting depth $D = 0$ (i.e., the subprogram contains no inner subprogram definitions).
- **No Static Link**: The static link field is omitted from the activation record.
- **Single Scope Chain**: All references resolve via lexical addressing using only the local symbol table and the global symbol table.

## 2.2 The Activation Record (AR) for a Simple Subprogram

The **Activation Record** is the data structure pushed onto the **Run-Time Stack** every time a subprogram is invoked. For a simple subprogram, the canonical layout (from low to high memory address) is:

| Memory Region | Contents | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Local Variables** | Storage for `int x`, `float y`, etc. | Holds values defined inside the subprogram body. |
| **Parameters** | Storage for `int a`, `int b` (formal parameters) | Stores passed-in values or references. |
| **Return Address** | Pointer to instruction after `CALL` in caller | Tells CPU where to resume after return. |
| **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** | Pointer to caller's AR on stack | Enables stack unwinding / debugging. |
| **Return Value** (optional) | Storage for the value being returned | Where the caller reads the function's result. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the order matters (Low → High Address)**
> The compiler places **local variables and parameters at the top** of the AR (lowest addresses within the frame) because they are accessed using **fixed, statically known offsets from the frame pointer (FP)**. The **dynamic link and return address** sit at the bottom (highest addresses) because they are manipulated by the calling/returning code at run time.

## 2.3 The Calling Sequence (Step-by-Step)

The **Calling Sequence** is the machine-code prologue executed by the caller immediately before, and the callee immediately after, the actual transfer of control.

**Caller's Responsibility (Before Call):**
1. Evaluate each actual parameter and store it in the callee's parameter slots (or in registers per calling convention).
2. **Save any caller-save registers** that contain live values.
3. **Push the Return Address** onto the stack.
4. **Jump** to the callee's entry point (machine instruction: `CALL` or `JAL`).

**Callee's Responsibility (Upon Entry):**
1. **Push the Dynamic Link** (a copy of the old Frame Pointer, FP) onto the stack.
2. **Set the new Frame Pointer (FP)** to the current stack top.
3. **Allocate space for local variables** by decrementing the Stack Pointer (SP).
4. Save callee-save registers if needed.

## 2.4 The Return Sequence (Step-by-Step)

**Callee's Responsibility (Before Return):**
1. Store the **return value** in the designated location (often a register like `$v0` or a stack slot).
2. Restore callee-save registers.
3. Restore the Frame Pointer (FP) to its saved value from the dynamic link.
4. **Pop the stack frame** (deallocation).
5. **Jump back** to the saved return address.

**Caller's Responsibility (After Return):**
1. **Pop the parameters** off the stack (caller-cleanup convention).
2. Resume execution, reading the return value.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER REFERENCE TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Stack Growth** $\Delta_{stack}$ | $\Delta_{stack} = S_{AR} \times N_{calls}$ | Bytes, where $S_{AR}$ = size of one AR |
| **AR Size** $S_{AR}$ | $S_{AR} = S_{locals} + S_{params} + S_{return\_addr} + S_{dyn\_link} + S_{ret\_val}$ | Bytes |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - S_{AR}$ | Decrements (down-growing stack) |
| **Frame Pointer (FP)** | $FP_{new} = SP_{old} - S_{control\_info}$ | Stable base for fixed-offset access |
| **Nesting Depth** $D$ | $D = 0$ (for simple subprograms) | Unitless integer |
| **Static Link Size** $S_{static}$ | $S_{static} = 0$ (for simple subprograms) | Bytes |
| **Parameter Count** $n$ | $n \geq 0$ | Must be statically known for AR sizing |
| **Activation Lifetime** $T_{life}$ | $T_{life} = T_{entry} \rightarrow T_{exit}$ | Lexical time interval |
| **Maximum Stack Depth** $D_{max}$ | $D_{max} = \max(N_{concurrent\_calls})$ | Recursive calls multiply this |
| **Call Overhead** $C_{call}$ | $C_{call} = C_{save} + C_{setup} + C_{restore}$ | CPU cycles |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**: Activation records are not just academic. They power:
> - **Stack traces in debuggers** (gdb, Visual Studio)
> - **Exception handling** (try/catch unwinding)
> - **Garbage collection root scanning** in managed runtimes
> - **Tail-call optimization** detection
> - **Reverse engineering and binary security analysis**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stack Depth for Recursive Simple Subprograms

A classic KTU question type: *Given a recursive simple subprogram, compute the maximum stack depth.*

**Problem Setup:** Consider a recursive function `fact(n)` that computes $n!$ recursively.

Let:
- $S_{AR}$ = size of one activation record (in bytes).
- $R$ = recursion depth at maximum call = $n$ (for `fact(n)`).
- $S_{params}$ = 1 word (the integer `n`).
- $S_{locals}$ = 1 word (the temporary return value).
- $S_{ret\_addr}$ = 1 word.
- $S_{dyn\_link}$ = 1 word.
- $S_{ret\_val}$ = 1 word (or 0 if returned in register).

**Step 1: Compute the size of one Activation Record**

$$
S_{AR} = S_{locals} + S_{params} + S_{ret\_addr} + S_{dyn\_link} + S_{ret\_val}
$$

Substituting values (assuming 1 word = 4 bytes):

$$
S_{AR} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 5 \text{ words}
$$

Converting to bytes (1 word = 4 bytes):

$$
S_{AR} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Maximum Stack Depth in Bytes**

$$
\Delta_{stack} = S_{AR} \times R
$$

$$
\Delta_{stack} = 20 \times n = 20n \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: State the Final Result Clearly**

The maximum stack depth is **$20n$ bytes**, which the KTU examiner expects in the form: *"$n$ activation records, each 20 bytes, totaling $20n$ bytes."*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Local vs Global Access

For a simple subprogram `foo()` that has:
- 1 local variable `x` (4 bytes)
- 1 local array `arr[10]` (40 bytes)
- 1 parameter `p` (4 bytes)
- 1 global variable `g` (4 bytes)

| Variable | Location | Access Mechanism | Cost (Relative) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `x` (local scalar) | Caller's AR (callee's locals) | `FP - offset` | **O(1)** — fastest |
| `arr[5]` (local array) | Callee's locals region | `FP - base - 5*4` | **O(1)** — fixed offset |
| `p` (parameter) | Callee's parameter region | `FP + offset` | **O(1)** — fixed offset |
| `g` (global) | Static data segment | Absolute address from symbol table | **O(1)** — but slower (no caching) |
| (Hypothetical non-local) | Outer AR | Not possible for simple subprograms | **N/A** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation**: Local variables and parameters are accessed using **fixed offsets from the Frame Pointer (FP)** because their positions within the AR are determined at compile time. Global variables require an **absolute memory address** stored in the symbol table, which is why they are slightly slower in modern CPUs (potential cache miss on the static segment).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Calling Sequence Action Table

| Step | Actor | Action | Stack State Change |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Caller | Evaluate parameters, place in AR slots | Stack unchanged |
| 2 | Caller | Push Return Address | SP decreases by 4 bytes |
| 3 | Caller | `JUMP` to callee entry | PC updated |
| 4 | Callee | Push Dynamic Link (old FP) | SP decreases by 4 bytes |
| 5 | Callee | Set FP = SP | FP register updated |
| 6 | Callee | Allocate locals (decrement SP) | SP decreases by $S_{locals}$ |
| 7 | Callee | Execute body | Stack stable |
| 8 | Callee | Store return value | Stack stable |
| 9 | Callee | Restore FP from dynamic link | FP restored |
| 10 | Callee | Pop frame, `JUMP` to return address | SP increases by $S_{AR}$ |
| 11 | Caller | Resume, read return value | Stack stable |

## 3.4 Worked Example: A Full Call/Return Trace

Consider the following pseudocode execution:

```
MAIN calls SUB1 with parameter 5
SUB1 calls SUB2 with parameter 10
SUB2 returns 100
SUB1 returns 50
MAIN ends
```

**Initial Stack:** Empty, FP = SP = base.

**Step A — MAIN calls SUB1(5):**
1. Place 5 in SUB1's parameter slot. (SP unchanged for parameter placement if pre-allocated)
2. Push return address (RA1). SP = base - 4.
3. Push old FP (FP0). SP = base - 8.
4. Set FP1 = SP = base - 8.
5. Allocate SUB1's locals (say 0 bytes here). SP = base - 8.

**Step B — SUB1 calls SUB2(10):**
1. Place 10 in SUB2's parameter slot.
2. Push return address (RA2). SP = base - 12.
3. Push old FP (FP1). SP = base - 16.
4. Set FP2 = SP = base - 16.

**Step C — SUB2 returns 100:**
1. SUB2 stores 100 in return value slot.
2. SUB2 restores FP = FP1 (read from dynamic link at FP2 + 0).
3. SP moves up to base - 8 (SUB2's frame removed).
4. PC jumps to RA1.
5. SUB1 reads return value 100.

**Step D — SUB1 returns 50:**
1. SUB1 stores 50 in return value slot.
2. SUB1 restores FP = FP0.
3. SP moves to base.
4. PC jumps to caller of MAIN (e.g., `exit`).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick**: KTU often asks you to draw the stack frame diagram *before* and *after* a call. Memorize the rule: **the Dynamic Link always points to the caller's FP, not the caller's SP.** This is the single most common error students make.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Subprogram Call & Return Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Execution] --> B[Encounter Subprogram Call]
    B --> C[Evaluate Actual Parameters]
    C --> D[Store Parameters in Callee AR Slots]
    D --> E[Push Return Address onto Stack]
    E --> F[Save Old Frame Pointer as Dynamic Link]
    F --> G[Set New Frame Pointer FP = SP]
    G --> H[Allocate Local Variable Space]
    H --> I[Transfer Control to Callee Entry Point]
    I --> J[Execute Callee Body]
    J --> K{Return Statement Hit?}
    K -- No --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[Store Return Value in Designated Slot]
    L --> M[Restore Saved Frame Pointer from Dynamic Link]
    M --> N[Pop Local Variables from Stack]
    N --> O[Pop Dynamic Link]
    O --> P[Transfer Control to Return Address]
    P --> Q[Caller Resumes Execution]
    Q --> R[Pop Parameters if Caller Cleanup]
    R --> S[Read Return Value]
    S --> T[Continue Main Program]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Activation Record Memory Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HighAddress["HIGH MEMORY ADDRESSES"]
        direction TB
        H1[Return Value 1 word]
        H2[Dynamic Link FP old 1 word]
        H3[Return Address 1 word]
    end

    subgraph MidAddress["PARAMETER REGION"]
        direction TB
        M1[Parameter 1 1 word]
        M2[Parameter 2 1 word]
        M3[Parameter N 1 word]
    end

    subgraph LowAddress["LOCAL VARIABLE REGION"]
        direction TB
        L1[Local Variable 1 1 word]
        L2[Local Variable 2 1 word]
        L3[Local Array arr 10 words]
    end

    subgraph Bottom["LOW MEMORY ADDRESSES Stack Bottom"]
        direction TB
        B1[Next AR Below]
    end

    H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The top of the diagram (HIGH MEMORY) is where the stack pointer (SP) sits when the subprogram is **idle** between calls. The bottom (LOW MEMORY) is where older activation records live. The Frame Pointer (FP) typically points to the boundary between the **Return Address** and the **Parameter Region**.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Nested Calls Between Simple Subprograms

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as MAIN
    participant Sub1 as SUB1
    participant Sub2 as SUB2
    participant Stack as Runtime Stack

    Main->>Stack: Push Return Address RA1
    Main->>Stack: Push Dynamic Link FP0
    Main->>Sub1: Transfer Control CALL
    activate Sub1
    Sub1->>Stack: Push Return Address RA2
    Sub1->>Stack: Push Dynamic Link FP1
    Sub1->>Sub2: Transfer Control CALL
    activate Sub2
    Sub2->>Sub2: Execute Body
    Sub2->>Stack: Store Return Value
    Sub2->>Stack: Pop AR Restore FP1
    Sub2-->>Sub1: Return Control JUMP RA2
    deactivate Sub2
    Sub1->>Sub1: Continue Execution
    Sub1->>Stack: Store Return Value
    Sub1->>Stack: Pop AR Restore FP0
    Sub1-->>Main: Return Control JUMP RA1
    deactivate Sub1
    Main->>Main: Resume Main Flow
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison With vs Without Static Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SimpleAR["SIMPLE SUBPROGRAM AR"]
        SA1[Local Vars]
        SA2[Parameters]
        SA3[Return Address]
        SA4[Dynamic Link]
    end

    subgraph NestedAR["NESTED SUBPROGRAM AR for reference"]
        NA1[Local Vars]
        NA2[Parameters]
        NA3[Static Link]
        NA4[Return Address]
        NA5[Dynamic Link]
    end

    SimpleAR ~~~ NestedAR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Observation**: The Simple AR has **no Static Link** field, which is the hallmark visual difference. The Nested AR has an extra 1-word field for the Static Link, used to resolve non-local references via scope chain traversal.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define an Activation Record. List the fields present in the activation record of a simple subprogram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> An **Activation Record** is a data structure created on the runtime stack each time a subprogram is invoked. It contains all the information necessary to maintain the state of a particular execution of the subprogram.

For a **simple subprogram**, the activation record contains the following fields:

1. **Local Variables** — storage for variables declared inside the subprogram body. *(1 mark)*
2. **Parameters** — storage for the formal parameters of the subprogram. *(1 mark)*
3. **Return Address** — pointer to the instruction in the caller immediately after the call statement.
4. **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** — pointer to the activation record of the calling subprogram.
5. **Return Value** — storage location for the value being returned by the subprogram.

> **Note**: The **Static Link** is **absent** for simple subprograms since they do not contain nested definitions. *(0.5 mark for this observation)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between a Calling Sequence and a Return Sequence. Who is responsible for each step?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Calling Sequence | Return Sequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Sets up the subprogram execution environment | Tears down the environment and returns control |
| **Trigger** | Subprogram call statement encountered | Subprogram completes or hits a `return` statement |
| **Caller's Role** | Evaluate params, push return address *(1 mark)* | Receive return value, pop params *(0.5 mark)* |
| **Callee's Role** | Push dynamic link, set FP, allocate locals *(1 mark)* | Store return value, restore FP, deallocate locals, jump back *(0.5 mark)* |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the structure of an activation record for a simple subprogram in detail, with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a simple recursive subprogram `fact(n)` that computes factorial. If each activation record for `fact` requires 24 bytes and 1 word = 4 bytes, compute the maximum stack depth in bytes when `fact(5)` is called. Show the stack growth step by step. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** An activation record is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the runtime stack when a subprogram is invoked, holding the data needed for that specific execution instance.

**Structure (4 marks):** The activation record for a simple subprogram contains:

```
+----------------------------+  ← HIGH ADDRESS (SP)
|      Return Value          |  (1 word / 4 bytes)
+----------------------------+
|      Dynamic Link          |  (1 word / 4 bytes) — points to caller's FP
+----------------------------+
|      Return Address        |  (1 word / 4 bytes) — resume point in caller
+----------------------------+
|      Parameter N           |  (1 word each)
+----------------------------+
|      ...                   |
+----------------------------+
|      Parameter 1           |
+----------------------------+
|      Local Variable N      |  (1 word each)
+----------------------------+
|      ...                   |
+----------------------------+
|      Local Variable 1      |
+----------------------------+  ← LOW ADDRESS (Stack growth direction)
```

**Key Properties (2 marks):**

- The **Frame Pointer (FP)** points to a fixed boundary in the AR, allowing the compiler to access locals and parameters using static offsets.
- The **Dynamic Link** enables stack unwinding during returns and supports debugging tools.
- For **simple subprograms**, the **Static Link is absent** because there are no enclosing scopes to reference.

> **Exam Tip**: Always draw the diagram with the high address on top and the stack growing downward. Label the **Frame Pointer (FP)** location and indicate with arrows where each field is accessed.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Subprogram: `fact(n)` computing $n!$
- Activation record size: $S_{AR} = 24$ bytes
- Call: `fact(5)`

**Step 1: Determine the Recursion Depth (1 mark)**

For `fact(n)`, the recursion depth equals $n$ because each call invokes `fact(n-1)` until `n = 0`.

$$
R = n = 5
$$

**Step 2: Verify AR Size (2 marks)**

$$
S_{AR} = S_{locals} + S_{params} + S_{ret\_addr} + S_{dyn\_link} + S_{ret\_val}
$$

With each field being 1 word (4 bytes) and 6 fields: $6 \times 4 = 24$ bytes. ✔ Confirmed.

**Step 3: Compute Maximum Stack Depth (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta_{stack} = S_{AR} \times R
$$

$$
\Delta_{stack} = 24 \times 5 = 120 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Show Stack Growth Step by Step (2 marks)**

| Call # | Subprogram Active | Stack Depth (bytes) | AR Address Range |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | `fact(5)` entered | 24 | `Base` to `Base-24` |
| 1 | `fact(4)` entered | 48 | `Base-24` to `Base-48` |
| 2 | `fact(3)` entered | 72 | `Base-48` to `Base-72` |
| 3 | `fact(2)` entered | 96 | `Base-72` to `Base-96` |
| 4 | `fact(1)` entered | 120 | `Base-96` to `Base-120` |
| 5 | `fact(0)` returns 1 | 96 | Stack unwinds |
| ... | All unwind | 0 | Empty stack |

**Final Answer:** The maximum stack depth is **120 bytes**, occurring when `fact(1)` is active and `fact(0)` is about to be called.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of a Calling Sequence. Describe in detail the steps performed by the **caller** and the **callee** during a subprogram call. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **parameter passing techniques** — call by value, call by reference, and call by value-result. Compare them with respect to a swap function example. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** A **Calling Sequence** is the set of actions performed both by the caller (before the call) and the callee (at entry) to set up the execution environment of the subprogram.

**Caller's Actions (3 marks):**
1. Evaluate each actual parameter and place it in the parameter slots of the callee's activation record.
2. Save the **return address** (the address of the instruction following the `CALL` in the caller's code).
3. Push the return address onto the stack.
4. Execute a `JUMP` (or `CALL`) instruction to transfer control to the callee's entry point.

**Callee's Actions (3 marks):**
1. Save the **dynamic link** (the caller's Frame Pointer, FP) onto the stack.
2. Set the new **Frame Pointer (FP)** to the current Stack Pointer (SP).
3. Allocate space on the stack for **local variables** by decrementing SP.
4. Execute the subprogram body.

> **Conclusion:** This carefully choreographed sequence ensures that when the callee returns, the caller's execution context is fully restored.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Three Parameter Passing Techniques:**

**1. Call by Value (1.5 marks):**
- The actual parameter's value is **copied** into the formal parameter's location.
- The callee works on the copy, not the original.
- **Swap Example:** Swapping a copy does **NOT** affect the original. Output: original variables remain **unchanged**.

**2. Call by Reference (1.5 marks):**
- The address (reference) of the actual parameter is passed to the formal parameter.
- The callee works directly on the original memory location.
- **Swap Example:** Swapping via references **DOES** affect the original. Output: original variables are **swapped correctly**.

**3. Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore) (1.5 marks):**
- A copy is passed in (like call by value).
- On return, the final value of the formal parameter is **copied back** to the actual parameter.
- **Swap Example:** Swapping works correctly **only if there are no aliases** between parameters.

**Comparison Table (2.5 marks):**

| Technique | Passes | Modifies Original? | Aliasing Issue? | Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| By Value | Copy of value | No | No | Slower (copy cost) |
| By Reference | Address (pointer) | Yes | **Yes** | Fastest (no copy) |
| By Value-Result | Copy in, copy out | Yes | **Yes** | Slowest (two copies) |

**Swap Function Trace:**

```
// Call by Value
swap_val(x, y)  // x=5, y=10 → after: x=5, y=10 (no change)

// Call by Reference
swap_ref(&x, &y) // x=5, y=10 → after: x=10, y=5 (swapped)

// Call by Value-Result
swap_vr(x, y)   // x=5, y=10 → after: x=10, y=5 (swapped, if no alias)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Dynamic Link with Static Link** (–1 to –2 marks): Dynamic link points to the *caller's AR*; static link points to the *enclosing static scope's AR*. Simple subprograms have **only the dynamic link**.
> 2. **Wrong stack growth direction** (–1 mark): Always show stack growing toward **lower addresses**.
> 3. **Forgetting to label the Frame Pointer (FP)** in the AR diagram (–1 mark): FP is mandatory.
> 4. **Mixing up call-by-reference with call-by-value-result** in exam answers (–2 marks): Emphasize the *copy-back* step of value-result.
> 5. **Omitting the Static Link absence** in the simple subprogram AR (–0.5 mark): Always state it explicitly.
> 6. **Not drawing the SP movement** during the calling sequence (–1 mark): KTU loves to see SP and FP arrow transitions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Implementing Simple Subprograms**

- **Definition**: A simple subprogram has **no nested subprograms** and **no non-local variable references**.
- **Activation Record (AR) Fields** (in order, low → high address):
  1. Local Variables
  2. Parameters
  3. Return Address
  4. Dynamic Link (Control Link)
  5. Return Value
- **Static Link is ABSENT** in simple subprograms. ✔
- **Calling Sequence** = Caller evaluates params + pushes return address + jumps; Callee pushes dynamic link + sets FP + allocates locals.
- **Return Sequence** = Callee stores return value + restores FP + pops frame + jumps back; Caller resumes and reads return value.
- **Stack Growth**: Towards **lower memory addresses** (standard convention).
- **Frame Pointer (FP)**: Stable base register for accessing locals/params via fixed offsets.
- **Stack Pointer (SP)**: Points to current top of stack; moves up/down with push/pop.
- **Recursive Subprograms** cause the deepest stack usage; maximum depth = $S_{AR} \times$ recursion_depth.
- **Parameter Passing Techniques**:
  - **By Value** — copy passed, original unchanged.
  - **By Reference** — address passed, original modified.
  - **By Value-Result** — copy in, copy back; sensitive to aliasing.
  - **By Name** — textual substitution (historical, in Algol).
  - **By Result** — only copy out on return.
- **Engineering Applications**: Debuggers, exception unwinding, tail-call optimization, garbage collection, security/binary analysis.
- **Common Mistake**: Confusing **Dynamic Link** (caller's AR pointer) with **Static Link** (parent scope's AR pointer). Simple subprograms use ONLY the dynamic link.
- **Exam Mantra**: *"Draw the AR with high address on top, label the FP, show SP movement, and explicitly state the absence of the Static Link."*

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 3: Implementing Simple Subprograms**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Subprograms with Stack-Dynamic Local Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing Subprograms with Stack-Dynamic Local Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack-Dynamic Local Variables** are variables that are *bound to storage on the run-time stack* (also called the **control stack** or **call stack**) **each time the subprogram is called**. Their lifetime is dynamically bounded to the execution of the subprogram. The memory for these variables is allocated on entry to the subprogram and **deallocated (released)** when the subprogram terminates — making them **automatic storage duration** variables.

**Key Terminology Table:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Subprogram** | A function or procedure (parameterized, callable unit) |
| **Activation** | The execution of a subprogram (a particular call) |
| **Activation Record (AR)** | A data structure (a **stack frame**) holding the state of a single subprogram activation |
| **Local Variable** | A variable declared inside the subprogram, private to that activation |
| **Run-Time Stack** | LIFO stack in memory used to store activation records during execution |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | Register/memory location pointing to the **top** of the run-time stack |
| **Frame Pointer (FP / EP)** | Pointer to a fixed location within the current activation record (often the base) |
| **Stack Base (SB)** | Pointer to the bottom of the entire run-time stack |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 parlance, the **lifetime of a stack-dynamic local variable is the duration of its activation**, NOT the entire program. This is the single most-tested concept in board questions on this topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Whiteboard in a Meeting Room

Imagine a corporate building with many **identical meeting rooms**, each equipped with a large wall-mounted **whiteboard**.

- The building's **manager** (the **run-time stack**) keeps a single queue of active meetings.
- When a meeting (subprogram call) starts, the manager enters the room, takes out markers, and **writes that meeting's specific notes on the whiteboard** (analogous to **allocating the local variables of the activation**).
- The whiteboard now holds only what's relevant to *this* meeting — names of participants (local variables), action items (temporaries), parent-meeting reference (return address / dynamic link).
- When the meeting ends, the manager **wipes the whiteboard clean** (analogous to **popping the activation record off the stack**) and the next meeting (caller) reclaims the room.
- If during the meeting, the team calls a *sub-meeting* (nested subprogram call), a **nested whiteboard frame** is pushed on top of the current one's wall space — but only as a logical "frame over frame" overlay. The outer meeting's notes remain intact underneath.

This is **precisely** what happens with stack-dynamic local variables. The whiteboard is the **run-time stack**, the meeting-room slot is the **activation record**, and the *act of writing/erasing* is the **allocation/deallocation** of stack-dynamic storage.

### 1.3 Why Use Stack-Dynamic Local Variables? (Engineering Motivation)

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Recursion Support** | Each recursive call needs its own independent copy of local variables — only the stack can provide this dynamically. |
| **Memory Efficiency** | Memory is reclaimed the instant the subprogram returns — no permanent allocation. |
| **Encapsulation / Information Hiding** | Local variables are inaccessible to other subprograms — supports modular design. |
| **Reentrancy** | A subprogram can be safely called again before its previous invocation finishes. |
| **Automatic Lifecycle** | Programmer does not manually free memory — eliminates many whole classes of bugs (e.g., memory leaks, double-free). |

### 1.4 Languages & Their Choice (KTU High-Yield)

| Language | Local Variable Default Storage |
|---|---|
| **C / C++** | Stack-dynamic (unless declared `static` or `extern`) |
| **Java** | Stack-dynamic for **primitive local** vars; **objects** are heap-allocated, but the *reference* is on the stack |
| **C#** | Stack-dynamic for value-type locals; reference-type locals are stack-resident *references* to heap objects |
| **Python** | All variables are *references* stored in a stack-allocated **frame object** |
| **JavaScript** | Primitive locals on the stack; object/function locals referenced via stack-allocated bindings |
| **Ada** | Stack-dynamic (unless explicitly declared with `aliased`/heap storage) |
| **Fortran 77** | Traditionally static (allocated once); Fortran 90+ allows stack via `automatic` attribute |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Activation Record — Detailed Anatomy

Whenever a subprogram is called, a contiguous block of memory called an **Activation Record (AR)** (also **Stack Frame**) is pushed onto the run-time stack. The classic Sebesta layout (used widely in KTU questions) is:

```
┌──────────────────────────────┐  ← Top of stack (low address in most ISAs, high in some)
│  Local / Temporary Variables │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  Saved Registers (if any)    │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  Static Link (for nesting)   │  ← also called "access link" or "EP"
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  Dynamic Link (caller's FP)  │  ← pointer to caller's AR
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  Return Address              │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  Parameters (actual args)    │
└──────────────────────────────┘
  ▲
  │ Bottom-of-frame / Frame Pointer (FP / EP)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of fields is implementation-defined.** The KTU-accepted canonical order (top→bottom) is: **Locals → Temporaries → Saved Registers → Static Link → Dynamic Link → Return Address → Parameters**. Some textbooks reverse this. Examiner accepts either if labelled correctly.

### 2.2 Field-by-Field Description

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Parameters** | The actual argument values passed to the subprogram |
| **Return Address** | Instruction address in the caller to resume execution after the call |
| **Dynamic Link** | Pointer to the **base of the caller's activation record** (enables `return`) |
| **Static Link** | Pointer to the activation record of the **enclosing static parent** (for nested subprograms) |
| **Saved Registers** | Values of CPU registers that the callee must preserve |
| **Local Variables** | Subprogram's declared local variables |
| **Temporaries** | Intermediate values created by the compiler for expression evaluation |

### 2.3 Two Key Pointer Registers

$$ \text{SP} \;\equiv\; \text{Stack Pointer} \;=\; \text{top of the run-time stack (next free slot)} $$

$$ \text{FP} \;\equiv\; \text{Frame Pointer} \;=\; \text{a fixed anchor point within the current AR} $$

The relationship between them is **constant for the lifetime of the activation** — they move together when the AR is pushed/popped, but `FP - SP` (or vice-versa) remains a fixed offset that the compiler can use to address local variables at compile time.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Rule |
|---|---|
| Size of an activation record | $\text{sizeof(AR)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(\text{field}_i) + \text{possible padding}$ |
| Address of local variable $x$ | $\text{addr}(x) = \text{FP} \pm \text{offset}_x$  (offset is **negative** if AR grows downward) |
| Address of parameter $p$ | $\text{addr}(p) = \text{FP} \pm \text{offset}_p$  (parameters typically at **positive** offset) |
| Stack depth during recursion | $\text{Depth} = \text{number of pending activations} \times \text{sizeof(AR)}$ |
| Local lifetime | $\text{Lifetime}(x) = [\text{AR}_{\text{push}},\; \text{AR}_{\text{pop}}]$ |
| Static link following | $\text{addr of ancestor} = \text{follow static links } k \text{ times where } k = \text{static depth difference}$ |
| Dynamic link following | One step: $\text{caller's FP} = \text{contents of dynamic-link field}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL:** Do **not** use the pipe `|` symbol inside any table cell (e.g., for absolute value). Use `\\vert` or `\\mid` in LaTeX. KTU auto-graders sometimes choke on bare pipes.

### 2.5 The Subprogram Call Lifecycle — Three Phases

1. **Call Sequence (Caller side):** Evaluate & store actual parameters; push the new AR; set up dynamic link; set up static link (if nested); save return address; jump to callee.
2. **Prologue (Callee side):** Save any needed callee-save registers; update the FP to the new AR base.
3. **Return Sequence (Callee side):** Place return value in agreed location; pop AR; restore SP & FP; jump to return address.
4. **Postlude (Caller side):** Retrieve return value (if any); continue execution.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Stack-Dynamic Locals Matter |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | Stack is bounded; deep recursion can cause **stack overflow** — designers must bound call depth. |
| **OS Kernels** | Each kernel thread is given a fixed-size kernel stack; the AR model ensures no cross-contamination. |
| **Compilers (JIT, AOT)** | Activation record size is computed statically, but the **offsets are hard-coded** into bytecode (e.g., JVM `aload`, `astore`). |
| **Debuggers** | Stack-walking (backtraces) is implemented by following the **chain of dynamic links**. |
| **Web Servers** | Each HTTP request handler runs as a new activation — locals provide thread-safe per-request state. |
| **Functional Languages (Haskell, Lisp)** | Heavy reliance on tail-call optimisation depends on the AR model being manipulable. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: A Single Non-Recursive Call

Consider the following C-like pseudocode:

```c
void sub(float y) {
    int a;
    int b;
    /* ... */
}

void main() {
    int x;
    sub(x + 3.0);
}
```

Assume the following sizes (bytes):

| Field | Size (bytes) | Offset from FP (top-down AR) |
|---|---|---|
| Local `a` | 4 | $-4$ |
| Local `b` | 4 | $-8$ |
| Return address | 4 | $+4$ |
| Parameter `y` | 8 | $+8$ (float) |

**Step 1 — Caller evaluates argument `x + 3.0`**  
The result is computed (say it produces 12.5) and stored into a temporary slot or directly into the new AR's parameter region.

**Step 2 — Caller pushes new AR and saves dynamic link**

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 16 \quad \text{(size of the new AR)}
$$

**Step 3 — Caller writes the return address into the new AR**  
at offset $+4$ from the future FP. The address is the PC of the instruction right after the call.

**Step 4 — Caller writes the dynamic link (caller's FP) into the new AR**  
at offset $+0$ from the future FP. Now the new AR can be popped back to the caller.

**Step 5 — Caller writes the parameter `y` value** at offset $+8$.

**Step 6 — Callee `sub` starts; FP is updated**

$$
\text{FP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 16
$$

(or `FP ← SP` depending on the convention — both yield the same base once the AR is fully built).

**Step 7 — Inside `sub`, `a` and `b` are addressed as** `FP - 4` and `FP - 8`. Their initial values are **undefined** (garbage) until the subprogram explicitly assigns to them — a classic C pitfall.

**Step 8 — On return, `sub` does:**

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{FP} \quad \text{(pop the AR)}
$$

$$
\text{FP} \leftarrow \text{Mem}[\text{SP} + 0] \quad \text{(restore caller's FP from dynamic link)}
$$

$$
\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{Mem}[\text{SP} + 4] \quad \text{(jump to return address)}
$$

This is the **clean rewind** to the caller's state.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: A Recursive Call — Factorial

```c
int fact(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * fact(n - 1);
}
```

Call: `fact(3)`.

Each call pushes a new AR. The static link is not needed (no nested scopes). The dynamic link forms a chain.

**Step 1:** Call `fact(3)`. AR #1 pushed with `n = 3`, RA = return point in `main`, DL = main's FP.
**Step 2:** Inside `fact(3)`, recursive call `fact(2)`. AR #2 pushed with `n = 2`, RA = address of `return n * fact(n-1)` resumption, DL = AR#1's FP.
**Step 3:** Inside `fact(2)`, recursive call `fact(1)`. AR #3 pushed with `n = 1`, RA = AR#2's resumption, DL = AR#2's FP.
**Step 4:** `fact(1)` returns `1` immediately.
**Step 5:** AR #3 popped. Control returns to AR #2 which now has the value `1` for the recursive call's result, computes `2 * 1 = 2`, pops AR #2.
**Step 6:** Control returns to AR #1 with value `2`, computes `3 * 2 = 6`, pops AR #1.
**Step 7:** Returns `6` to `main`.

Stack depth during peak execution: 3 ARs × 16 bytes = 48 bytes (illustrative).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Recursion Depth vs Stack Consumption

Let $\text{sizeof(AR)} = S$ bytes and recursion depth $= d$.

$$
\text{Total stack usage} = d \times S
$$

| Recursion Depth $d$ | AR Size $S$ (bytes) | Peak Stack Usage |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 32 | $5 \times 32 = 160$ bytes |
| 100 | 48 | $100 \times 48 = 4800$ bytes |
| 1,000 | 64 | $1,000 \times 64 = 64000$ bytes $\approx 64\text{ KB}$ |
| 10,000 | 64 | $640,000$ bytes $\approx 640\text{ KB}$ |
| $10^6$ | 64 | $64,000,000$ bytes $= 64\text{ MB}$ (typical stack overflow territory) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The default **thread stack size in most modern OS** is **1 MB (Linux)** to **8 MB (Windows)**, so even modest recursion depths can overflow if ARs are large. This is the basis of stack-overflow attacks and tail-call-optimisation strategies.

### 3.4 Deep Access with Static Links (Nested Subprograms)

Consider **Ada-style nesting** (nested procedures allowed):

```ada
procedure A is
    X : Integer;
    procedure B is
        Y : Integer;
        procedure C is
            Z : Integer;
        begin
            -- C can access X, Y, Z
            X := Y + Z;
        end C;
    begin
        C;
    end B;
begin
    B;
end A;
```

Static depth: `A = 0`, `B = 1`, `C = 2`.

When `C` accesses `X`, the compiler generates:

$$
\text{addr}(X) = \text{follow static links from C's AR } (2-0) = 2 \text{ times, then offset from there}
$$

Generalising: if local variable `v` is declared in an ancestor of static depth $d_a$, accessed from scope of depth $d_c$:

$$
\text{addr}(v) = \text{StaticLink}^{d_c - d_a}(\text{FP}) \;+\; \text{offset}_v
$$

The `StaticLink^2` means "apply the static-link dereference 2 times".

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic link is for `return` (one step).**  
> **Static link is for `access` (possibly many steps).** This distinction is the most-asked KTU short-answer.

### 3.5 Dangling Reference — The Classic Pitfall

```c
int* bad() {
    int x = 10;            // stack-dynamic local
    return &x;             // ⚠️ returns pointer to soon-to-be-dead memory
}
```

After `bad()` returns, its AR is **popped**. The address `&x` is now logically free memory — a **dangling reference**. Any dereference is undefined behaviour. Languages like Java eliminate this at the language level by **not allowing direct access to a local's address**.

### 3.6 Why Method-Local Inner Classes Capture Variables (Java)

In Java 8+:

```java
void outer() {
    int x = 10;
    Runnable r = () -> System.out.println(x);
    // x is effectively captured — semantically final
}
```

The lambda is compiled to a synthetic class whose instance fields store the values of captured locals — **a copy**, not the original (because the original AR may be gone). This is why `x` must be *effectively final*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Activation Record — Detailed Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AR["Activation Record (Stack Frame)"]
        direction TB
        F1["LOCALS\nlocal variables\ntemporaries"]
        F2["SAVED REGS\ncallee-saved CPU registers"]
        F3["STATIC LINK\npointer to enclosing scope AR"]
        F4["DYNAMIC LINK\npointer to caller AR"]
        F5["RETURN ADDRESS\ninstruction pointer in caller"]
        F6["PARAMETERS\nactual argument values"]
        F1 --> F2
        F2 --> F3
        F3 --> F4
        F4 --> F5
        F5 --> F6
    end
    F7["SP -> top of stack (boundary)"]
    F8["FP -> fixed anchor inside AR"]
    F6 -. refers to .- F8
    F1 -. refers to .- F8
```

> **Reading Guide:** Memory addresses typically *decrease* as you move down the diagram (stack grows downward in most C-family ABIs). `FP` points to the boundary between locals and parameters (or just above the return address, depending on convention). All fields are addressed at **compile-time-known fixed offsets from FP**.

### 4.2 Call Stack During Nested Calls (Factorial of 3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["Run-Time Stack (top of stack on the right)"]
        direction TB
        S1["main AR\nlocal variables of main()"]
        S2["fact(3) AR\nn=3 | RA | DL=main FP"]
        S3["fact(2) AR\nn=2 | RA | DL=fact(3) FP"]
        S4["fact(1) AR\nn=1 | RA | DL=fact(2) FP"]
        S1 --> S2
        S2 --> S3
        S3 --> S4
    end
    P1["SP ->"]
    P2["<- FP (current activation)"]
    S4 -. pointed by .- P2
    S4 -. pointed by .- P1
```

> **Reading Guide:** `fact(1)` is the deepest call. Its AR sits at the top. The **chain of dynamic links** lets the program unwind cleanly. Once `fact(1)` returns, its AR is popped, `SP` and `FP` shift to `fact(2)`, and so on.

### 4.3 Static Link Chain — Nested Procedure Access

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["A AR\nstatic_depth=0\ncontains X"]
    B1["B AR\nstatic_depth=1\ncontains Y\nStaticLink -> A"]
    C1["C AR\nstatic_depth=2\ncontains Z\nStaticLink -> B"]
    A1 -- "StaticLink" --> B1
    B1 -- "StaticLink" --> C1
    A1 -. "To access X from C:\nfollow static link 2 times" .- C1
```

> **Reading Guide:** `C` accesses `X` (declared in `A`) by following the static link **twice** — once to `B`, once to `A`. This is **O(static_depth_difference)**, not O(call_depth_difference).

### 4.4 Call Sequence & Return Sequence — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0([CALLER executes]) --> A1["Step 1: evaluate actual parameters\nand store in new AR's parameter slots"]
    A1 --> A2["Step 2: push AR onto run-time stack\nSP = SP - size_of_AR"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3: write return address\ninto AR.return_address"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4: write dynamic link\nAR.dynamic_link = FP (caller)"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5: write static link\nif nested (follow caller's static link)"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 6: jump to callee entry\nFP = SP + size_of_AR"]
    A6 --> A7([CALLEE executes body])
    A7 --> A8["Return: SP = FP (pop)"]
    A8 --> A9["FP = Mem[SP + dynamic_link_offset]"]
    A9 --> A10["PC = Mem[SP + return_address_offset]"]
    A10 --> A11([CALLER resumes after call])
```

> **Reading Guide:** Six steps forward, three steps back. The return sequence is *shorter* than the call sequence because most bookkeeping was already done at call time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. What are stack-dynamic local variables? How is their lifetime determined?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stack-dynamic local variables are variables whose storage is **allocated on the run-time stack** at the time the subprogram is called and **deallocated** when the subprogram returns control to its caller. Their **lifetime is dynamically bound** to the **activation of the subprogram** — they exist only as long as the subprogram's activation record remains on the stack. This contrasts with static locals (allocated once, entire program lifetime) and heap-dynamic variables (allocated on programmer request, indeterminate lifetime). *(Award full marks if the three points — allocated on stack, lifetime = activation, contrast with static — are present.)*

---

**Q2. List any four fields typically stored in an activation record.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Parameters** – actual argument values passed to the subprogram.
2. **Return address** – location in the caller to resume execution.
3. **Dynamic link** – pointer to the caller's activation record base.
4. **Static link** – pointer to the enclosing static parent's AR (used for nested subprograms).
*(Other valid fields: saved registers, local variables, temporaries. Any four well-explained → 3 marks.)*

---

### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Typical ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — *Choose A or B*

**(a)** Explain the general structure of an activation record for a subprogram. Describe the role of the **dynamic link** and the **static link** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the sequence of operations that occur during a **subprogram call** and a **subprogram return**. Show how the **run-time stack** evolves when a recursive function `fact(3)` is evaluated. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Activation Record Structure (7 marks):**

An **activation record (AR)** is a contiguous block of memory pushed onto the run-time stack whenever a subprogram is invoked. Its canonical fields (top to bottom of the stack) are:

| Field | Function |
|---|---|
| Local variables & temporaries | Hold values local to the activation |
| Saved registers | Preserve callee-save CPU state |
| **Static link** | Points to the AR of the **statically enclosing** subprogram — used for **deep access** to non-local variables in nested scopes |
| **Dynamic link** | Points to the AR of the **calling** subprogram — used to **restore state on return** |
| Return address | Instruction address in the caller to resume after the call |
| Parameters | Values of actual arguments |

**Role of Dynamic Link (1.5 marks):**
- Stores the Frame Pointer of the caller.
- On return, the callee restores `FP` from the dynamic link → caller can resume execution in its correct stack context.
- Forms a **chain** of pending activations — the basis of stack-walking / exception backtraces.

**Role of Static Link (1.5 marks):**
- Stores the Frame Pointer of the **statically enclosing** subprogram (not necessarily the immediate caller).
- Used to access non-local variables from a nested subprogram.
- Following the static link $k$ times is needed when the access is $k$ static levels above.

**Diagram (4 marks):** A clearly labelled AR block with FP and SP markers and the two links highlighted, as in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b) Call / Return Sequence + Recursive Factorial (7 marks):**

**Call Sequence Steps (3 marks):**
1. Evaluate actual parameters; store in the new AR's parameter region.
2. Push the new AR — `SP = SP - sizeof(AR)`.
3. Store return address (PC of instruction after call) in the AR.
4. Store caller's `FP` as the dynamic link in the AR.
5. Store static link (for nested scopes) — typically follows the caller's static link.
6. Set `FP = SP + sizeof(AR)` (or equivalent convention) and jump to the callee's entry point.

**Return Sequence Steps (2 marks):**
1. Callee places return value in an agreed location (register or parameter slot).
2. `SP = FP` (pop).
3. `FP = Mem[SP + DL_offset]` (restore caller's FP).
4. `PC = Mem[SP + RA_offset]` (jump back to caller).
5. Caller retrieves return value and continues.

**Recursive Factorial Diagram (2 marks):** Stack evolution with three ARs (`fact(3) → fact(2) → fact(1)`), each AR pointing via dynamic link to the one below. On unwind, the chain collapses bottom-up.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Differentiate between **stack-dynamic** and **static local variables**. Illustrate with an example how each is stored in memory. Discuss why **C uses stack-dynamic by default** and what happens when a function returns a pointer to its local. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **dangling reference**? Explain how it arises with stack-dynamic local variables. Show with a small code snippet and the corresponding stack state. How do languages like **Java** and **Ada** prevent this problem? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Stack-Dynamic vs Static Local Variables (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Stack-Dynamic Local | Static Local |
|---|---|---|
| **Allocation** | On each call (AR pushed) | Once, at program start |
| **Deallocation** | On return (AR popped) | Never (until program ends) |
| **Memory Location** | Run-time stack | Static data segment |
| **Lifetime** | Bound to activation | Bound to program execution |
| **Initial Value** | Garbage / undefined | Zero by default |
| **Recursion Safety** | Safe — each call has own copy | Unsafe — shared across calls |
| **Example (C)** | `int x;` (default) | `static int x;` |

**C uses stack-dynamic by default** because:
- Stack allocation is **fast** (just a `SP` decrement).
- It **supports recursion** and reentrancy.
- It is **memory-efficient** (no permanent cost).
- C's design philosophy favours **lightweight, fast execution** over safety.

**Returning a pointer to a local (3 marks):**

```c
int* foo() {
    int x = 10;
    return &x;   // x is destroyed when foo() returns
}
```

On return, `x`'s AR slot is logically freed. The returned address is now **dangling**. Any dereference is undefined behaviour. *(Diagram: AR of `foo` with `x` at offset −4, marked "FREED" after return.)*

---

**(b) Dangling References (7 marks):**

A **dangling reference** is a reference (pointer) that refers to a memory location whose lifetime has ended.

**Code (2 marks):**

```c
int* dangerous() {
    int temp = 42;
    return &temp;        // AR will be popped after return
}
int main() {
    int* p = dangerous();  // p is dangling
    printf("%d", *p);      // undefined behaviour
}
```

**Stack State (2 marks):**  
After `dangerous()` returns, the slot holding `temp` is logically free. Yet `p` in `main` still points to that slot. The next push will overwrite it — `*p` reads garbage.

**Language-Level Prevention (3 marks):**
- **Java** — Local variables of primitive types are not addressable; objects are heap-allocated and accessed via references that are tracked by GC. Inner classes capture *copies* of local values (effectively final).
- **Ada** — Access types can be declared `aliased` to permit taking the address, but the language restricts the lifetime of pointers through strict scope rules; dangling references are impossible by construction.
- **Rust** — Ownership and borrow checker statically guarantee that references cannot outlive their referents.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing static link with dynamic link** — students often mix up "where it points" (caller vs. enclosing scope). State clearly: *Dynamic link → caller; Static link → enclosing static parent.*
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the AR diagram** — many marks are reserved for a *labelled* AR. Drawing a plain box without field labels will cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not stating that locals are *undefined* on entry** — C locals are *not* zero-initialised by default. Examiners check this.
> 4. **Treating `static` C locals as "on the stack"** — they are in the **data segment**, not the stack. This is a frequent wrong answer.
> 5. **Skipping the static-depth formula** for deep access: $\text{addr}(v) = \text{StaticLink}^{(d_c - d_a)}(\text{FP}) + \text{offset}_v$. The superscript exponent is often forgotten.
> 6. **Confusing the direction of stack growth** — explicitly state whether your AR grows towards lower or higher addresses for full marks.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Stack-dynamic local variables** are allocated on the run-time stack at call time and freed at return.
- ✅ Their **lifetime = duration of the activation** — the single most-tested phrase.
- ✅ An **activation record (AR)** is the per-call data structure containing locals, parameters, return address, dynamic link, and (if needed) static link.
- ✅ **Dynamic link** → pointer to the *caller's* AR (used for `return`).
- ✅ **Static link** → pointer to the *statically enclosing* subprogram's AR (used for *deep access* to non-locals).
- ✅ **FP** is a fixed anchor inside the current AR; **SP** marks the top of the run-time stack.
- ✅ All local variable addresses are computed as `FP ± offset` — offsets are **compile-time constants**.
- ✅ **Recursion** is enabled by stack-dynamic locals — each call has its own independent local copy.
- ✅ **Dangling reference** = pointer to memory whose activation has ended — caused by returning addresses of stack-dynamic locals.
- ✅ **C local default = stack-dynamic**; `static` changes it to data segment; `extern` to global/static.
- ✅ **Java / C# / Python** locals hold *references* to heap objects — the *reference variable* itself is stack-dynamic, the object is not.
- ✅ **Stack overflow** occurs when recursion depth × AR size exceeds the stack limit (typically **1–8 MB**).
- ✅ AR field order is *implementation-defined*; the canonical KTU order (top→bottom) is: **Locals → Temporaries → Saved Registers → Static Link → Dynamic Link → Return Address → Parameters**.
- ✅ **Tail-call optimisation** is possible when the recursive call is the last operation — AR can be reused, depth stays constant.
- ✅ **Exam mantra**: "**Local = on stack, lifetime = activation, dangling if escaped**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested Subprograms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nested Subprograms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nested Subprogram (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *nested subprogram* is a subprogram (function, procedure, or method) whose **declaration appears lexically inside the body of another subprogram**. The subprogram that contains the nested definition is called the **enclosing (or host) subprogram**, and the subprogram defined inside is the **nested (or inner) subprogram**. The set of such enclosing relationships forms a **static nesting chain** whose depth is known as the *static depth* or *nesting depth*.

In languages such as **Python, Ada, Pascal, JavaScript, Ruby, and Scheme**, subprograms can be freely nested. In contrast, **C, C++, and Java** allow nested class/method definitions but not free-standing nested function definitions in the same way (C# and Java support nested classes only, while C++11+ supports lambdas and local classes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Matryoshka (Russian nesting doll)**. The largest doll is the *outer subprogram*. Open it, and inside sits a smaller doll — that is the *nested subprogram*. The smaller doll can see, use, and "remember" everything from the larger doll, but the larger doll cannot directly see what is inside its smaller doll until the smaller doll is opened.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition — "Visibility is one-way downward":**
> A nested subprogram can access the variables of *all* of its enclosing subprograms (just as a child has access to the parent's house), but the enclosing subprogram **cannot** directly access the local variables of its nested subprograms.

### 1.3 Important Terminology (Bold Marked)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Enclosing Subprogram** | The outer (host) subprogram that contains another subprogram inside its body. |
| **Nested Subprogram** | The inner subprogram declared within an enclosing subprogram. |
| **Static Depth** | The number of enclosing subprograms surrounding a given subprogram (lexical depth). |
| **Non-local Variable** | A variable visible to a subprogram but not declared in it; resides in an enclosing scope. |
| **Closure** | A function bundled together with references to the variables in its lexical environment. |
| **Static Link (SL)** | A pointer/reference that connects a nested subprogram activation to the activation of its *statically enclosing* subprogram. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Nested Subprograms Work

A nested subprogram works on three foundational pillars:

1. **Lexical (Static) Scoping** — The visibility of names is determined by the *physical layout* of the program text, not the order of calls. When a name is referenced inside a nested subprogram, the compiler looks first in the local scope, then in the immediately enclosing scope, then in the next enclosing scope, and so on outward.
2. **Activation Record (Stack Frame) Hierarchy** — Each subprogram invocation creates a new activation record on the call stack. For a nested subprogram, this activation record contains a **static link (SL)** pointing to the activation record of its *statically* enclosing subprogram.
3. **Non-local Access via Static Links** — To read or write a non-local variable, the run-time system follows the chain of static links until it locates the activation record that owns the variable.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

- **Why static scoping?** It makes programs **deterministic and predictable** — the meaning of a variable does not depend on how the program is run, only on how it is written.
- **Why static links?** They give the run-time system an efficient way (typically $O(n)$ where $n$ is the static depth) to reach any non-local variable by walking the lexical chain.
- **Why closures matter?** They allow a nested function to **outlive** its enclosing call and still remember the values of its enclosing variables — a foundation of functional programming.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Static Depth | $\text{depth}(p) = k$ | Number of enclosing subprograms around $p$ equals $k$. |
| Chain Rule (Static Link) | $\text{SL}(p_i) \to \text{AR}(p_{i-1})$ | The static link of an inner activation points to the activation of its enclosing subprogram. |
| Non-local Access Cost | $T_{\text{access}} = O(d)$ | Time to access a non-local variable is proportional to the static depth $d$. |
| Reference Environment | $E = \{ (\text{name}, \text{address}) \}$ | The set of name-to-address bindings visible at a point in the program. |
| Closure | $C = \langle \text{code}, E \rangle$ | A function code object paired with the reference environment of its definition. |
| Nesting Depth Bound | $d \geq 0$ | The outermost (program-level) subprogram has nesting depth **0**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> Nested subprograms underpin **callback systems** (event handlers in GUIs), **decorators** in Python web frameworks (Flask, Django), **lexical closures** in JavaScript for data hiding, and **hierarchical state machines** in embedded control software. They are also central to compiler design — every compiler's symbol table is structured as a chain of nested scopes.

### 2.4 Scope Chain Walkthrough — The Algorithm

When the program executes `x` inside a nested subprogram, the following algorithm runs:

1. Search the **local** activation record for `x`. If found, return its address.
2. If not found, follow the **static link** to the enclosing activation record. Search it.
3. Repeat step 2 until `x` is found, or the **global scope** is reached.
4. If still not found, raise a **scope / NameError** exception.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Basic Nesting in Python

Below is a complete, runnable demonstration of nested subprograms, with the activation record state traced at each step.

```python
# File: nested_demo.py
```
