Languages targeting safety-critical domains (Ada, SPARK) maximize $C_i^{safety}$ at the cost of speed. **Example:** Java performs *implicit* array bounds checking on every access, which adds ~5–10% overhead compared to C, but prevents buffer-overflow vulnerabilities. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q:** With a suitable diagram, explain the **exception handling** mechanism. Discuss **built-in vs. user-defined exceptions** with examples. **Model Solution:** **Exception Handling Mechanism:** An exception is an *abnormal event* that disrupts the normal flow of program execution. Modern languages (Java, C++, Ada, Python) provide a structured mechanism consisting of: 1. **Declaration of a handler** — `try { ... } catch (ExceptionType e) { ... }` (Java syntax) 2. **Raising an exception** — `throw new IOException("Disk full");` 3. **Propagation** — If the exception is not handled in the current scope, the runtime unwinds the stack and searches enclosing scopes. **Diagram (textual representation):** ``` [level3] --raises--> [level2] --propagates--> [level1] --propagates--> [main] --handled--> [Handler Block] ``` **Built-in vs. User-Defined Exceptions:** - **Built-in exceptions** are predefined by the language. Examples: - Java: `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `ArithmeticException` - Python: `ZeroDivisionError`, `KeyError`, `TypeError` - C++: `std::out_of_range`, `std::bad_alloc` - **User-defined exceptions** are created by the programmer to model domain-specific errors. Examples: - Java: `class InsufficientFundsException extends Exception { ... }` - Python: `class DatabaseConnectionError(Exception): pass` - C++: `class MyCustomError : public std::exception { ... };` **Advantages of User-Defined Exceptions:** - Improve readability by giving *semantic names* to error conditions - Enable domain-specific error handling - Promote separation of error-handling logic from normal logic --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Design Issues" Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing scope and lifetime** — These are *not* the same. Scope is static (compile-time), lifetime is dynamic (runtime). > 2. **Forgetting to compute the control link and return address** in activation record size — students often compute *only* parameters and locals, losing 2–3 marks. > 3. **Confusing pass-by-value-result with pass-by-reference** — value-result *copies* the value; reference *aliases* the memory. Many students write "both are the same," which is incorrect. > 4. **Mixing name and structural equivalence** — C uses a *hybrid* model. Don't claim it is purely one or the other. > 5. **Omitting examples** — KTU examiners award marks for *examples* alongside definitions. Always pair a definition with a concrete example from C, Java, or Pascal. > 6. **Hand-waving on the orthogonality-regularity trade-off** — both are *good* properties, but the exam expects you to know that over-orthogonality creates pathological cases (e.g., `goto` returning a label from a function). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Design Issues** ### ✅ Core Definitions - [ ] **Design Issues** = the architectural trade-offs a language/software designer must resolve - [ ] **Readability** — ease of *reading* code - [ ] **Writability** — ease of *writing* code - [ ] **Reliability** — conformance to specifications - [ ] **Orthogonality** — free combination of primitives - [ ] **Regularity** — uniform application of rules - [ ] **Aliasing** — two names for one memory location ### ✅ Parameter Passing (Must Memorize) - [ ] **By-Value** — copy, no alias, one-way (in) - [ ] **By-Result** — copy out, one-way (out) - [ ] **By-Value-Result** — copy in and out, two-way, no alias - [ ] **By-Reference** — address, two-way, full alias - [ ] **By-Name** — textual substitution, re-evaluated ### ✅ Type System Essentials - [ ] **Static typing** — checked at compile time (C, Java) - [ ] **Dynamic typing** — checked at runtime (Python, JS) - [ ] **Strongly typed** — strict type rules (Java, Haskell) - [ ] **Weakly typed** — permissive (C, JavaScript) - [ ] **Name equivalence** — based on declared names - [ ] **Structural equivalence** — based on internal structure ### ✅ Activation Record Formula - [ ] $S_{AR} = S_{params} + S_{locals} + S_{control\ link} + S_{return\ addr} + S_{dyn\ ref}$ - [ ] Always count **all** regions, not just params/locals ### ✅ Exception Handling - [ ] **Built-in** — language-provided (e.g., `NullPointerException`) - [ ] **User-defined** — programmer-created (extends base Exception class) - [ ] **Propagation depth** = number of stack frames traversed ### ✅ Implementation Strategies - [ ] **Compilation** — fast execution, slow dev (C, C++) - [ ] **Interpretation** — slow execution, fast dev (early BASIC) - [ ] **Hybrid JIT** — balanced (Java, PyPy, V8) ### ✅ Influences on Language Design - [ ] **Computer architecture** (von Neumann → imperative) - [ ] **Programming methodologies** (OOP → Java, C++) - [ ] **Mathematics** (lambda calculus → LISP/Haskell; logic → Prolog) ### ✅ High-Yield Examples to Quote in Exams - [ ] Pascal → highly orthogonal - [ ] C → fast execution, low safety - [ ] Java → type-safe, JIT-compiled, built-in exceptions - [ ] Algol 60 → pass-by-name - [ ] Ada → in/out/in-out parameter modes ---

topics.title: Language Examples
Parameterized ADT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parameterized ADT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Parameterized Abstract Data Type (Parameterized ADT)**, also widely referred to as a **Generic ADT** or **Template ADT**, is an abstract data type in which one or more **type parameters** are introduced into the specification so that the same logical data structure can be specialized to operate on **any valid data type** without rewriting or duplicating the implementation.

Formally, a parameterized ADT is defined as a **tuple of four elements**:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{ParameterizedADT} \;=\; \langle\, \alpha,\; S(\alpha),\; F(\alpha),\; P(\alpha)\,\rangle\;}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ → a symbolic **type parameter** (placeholder for any concrete type $T$).
- $S(\alpha)$ → a family of **state structures** indexed by the parameter $\alpha$.
- $F(\alpha)$ → a family of **operations / functions** whose signatures are universally quantified over $\alpha$.
- $P(\alpha)$ → a family of **axioms / pre-conditions / post-conditions** that must hold for **every valid binding** of $\alpha$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the module on *Advanced Programming Techniques*, a Parameterized ADT is treated as the formal foundation for **generic programming** — enabling a single, type-safe, reusable specification to generate a family of concrete ADTs (e.g., `Stack<Integer>`, `Stack<String>`, `Stack<Employee>`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Parameterized ADT** as a **"mould" or "cookie cutter"** in a bakery:

- A cookie cutter does **not** make cookies by itself — it only defines the **shape**.
- The **dough** (chocolate, vanilla, ginger) is the **type parameter** $\alpha$.
- The same cutter can produce a chocolate cookie, vanilla cookie, or ginger cookie — without ever redesigning the cutter.
- The recipe (operations: roll, cut, bake) stays **identical** for every dough type.

In programming terms:
- The **ADT specification** is the mould.
- The **type parameter** $\alpha$ is the dough.
- **Binding** $\alpha := \text{Integer}$ produces `Stack<Integer>` — a chocolate cookie.
- **Binding** $\alpha := \text{String}$  produces `Stack<String>`  — a vanilla cookie.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Without parameterization, Java programmers in 2003 had to write `IntegerStack.java`, `StringStack.java`, `EmployeeStack.java` … duplicating hundreds of lines. Parameterized ADTs collapse all of them into **one** specification.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Features (Expressed in Bold Metrics)

- **Type Safety:** Achieved at **compile time**, eliminating `ClassCastException` at runtime.
- **Code Reusability Factor:** A single specification serves **N × M** concrete types (N operations, M type bindings).
- **Erasure Semantics:** In Java, generic type parameters are erased after compilation; the bytecode remains type-agnostic.
- **Bounded Quantification:** Type parameters can be constrained using **upper bounds** (`<T extends Comparable<T>>`), restricting the family of admissible types.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — How a Parameterized ADT Works

The execution pipeline of a Parameterized ADT can be decomposed into **six precise logical steps**:

1. **Specification Phase:** The ADT is defined using a symbolic type variable $\alpha$ (or `T`, `E`, `K`, `V`).
2. **Type Parameter Declaration:** A formal parameter is attached to the class/interface — `class Stack<T> { ... }`.
3. **Binding Phase:** When the client writes `Stack<Integer> s = new Stack<>();`, $\alpha$ is bound to `Integer`.
4. **Type Substitution:** Every occurrence of $\alpha$ inside the ADT body is textually replaced by the bound type.
5. **Type-Checking:** The compiler verifies that **every operation** on $\alpha$ is legal for the bound type.
6. **Code Generation / Erasure:** Either a **new concrete class** is generated per binding (C++ templates) or the type information is **erased** and casts are inserted (Java generics).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Trigger:** *"Explain the difference between a regular ADT and a parameterized ADT."* — Always begin by stating that a regular ADT is **mono-typed**, while a parameterized ADT is **polymorphic at the type level**.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations of Parameterization

The central concept is **universal quantification over types**. A parameterized ADT $G$ with parameter $\alpha$ obeys the following logical statement:

$$
\forall\, T \in \text{ValidTypes} : \;\; G\langle T \rangle \;\text{is a well-formed ADT satisfying the axioms } P(T)
$$

Where **ValidTypes** is either:
- The unrestricted set of all reference types (unbounded `<?>`).
- A bounded subset: $\{ T \mid T \;\text{subtype of}\; B \}$ (bounded `<? extends B>`).

### Substitutivity Rule (Liskov-Style)

If $\alpha$ is substituted by $T$, and $T'$ is a subtype of $T$ ($T' \leq T$), then $G\langle T' \rangle$ must behave as a subtype of $G\langle T\rangle$:

$$
\boxed{\;T' \leq T \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; G\langle T' \rangle \;\leq\; G\langle T \rangle\;}
$$

This property is called **type substitutivity** and is the cornerstone of generic OO design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Notation**        | **Meaning**                                                | **Binding Form**               | **Constraint**                       |
|------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| $G\langle \alpha \rangle$   | Generic ADT named $G$ with type variable $\alpha$          | `class G<T>`                   | None (unbounded)                     |
| $G\langle T \rangle$         | Concrete instantiation with type $T$                       | `G<Integer>`                   | $T$ must be a reference type         |
| $\alpha \leq B$              | Bounded parameter — only subtypes of $B$                   | `<T extends B>`                | $B$ is a class or interface          |
| $\alpha \geq B$              | Lower-bound wildcard (consumer)                            | `<? super B>`                  | Used in `Comparator`, `Consumer`     |
| $G\langle ? \rangle$         | Unbounded wildcard (read-only)                             | `G<?> x;`                      | No write allowed, only `Object` read |
| $\vert \alpha \vert$         | Cardinality of type family instantiable from $\alpha$      | Infinite if unbounded          | Finite if bounded by sealed types    |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** The $\vert \alpha \vert$ notation above uses `\vert` (not the pipe character) to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parameterized ADTs underpin several mission-critical engineering domains:

- **Java Collections Framework (`java.util`)** — `ArrayList<E>`, `HashMap<K,V>`, `PriorityQueue<E>`.
- **C++ Standard Template Library (STL)** — `std::vector<T>`, `std::map<K,V>`, `std::unique_ptr<T>`.
- **Functional Programming** — Haskell’s parametric polymorphism (`Maybe a`, `Either a b`).
- **Database Systems** — Type-safe generic query builders (e.g., Hibernate’s `Query<T>`).
- **Distributed Systems** — Generic message envelopes `Envelope<PayloadType>` for type-safe RPC.
- **Embedded Firmware** — Ring-buffer `Queue<Sample>` for sensor pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Constructing a Parameterized Stack ADT

We now walk through the **exhaustive construction** of a Parameterized `Stack<T>` from a non-parameterized base, recording every logical transition.

### Step 1 — Non-Parameterized Starting Point (Mono-Typed ADT)

Assume we initially only support integers:

$$
\text{Stack}_{\text{int}} = \langle\, \text{int},\; \text{array}[n],\;\{\text{push}(x),\text{pop}()\},\;\{\text{LIFO}\}\,\rangle
$$

This serves only $\alpha = \text{int}$ — **one specific concrete type**.

### Step 2 — Abstract the Type to a Parameter

We observe that the operations `push(x)` and `pop()` only require that $x$ **exists** as an object. The internal logic (LIFO discipline, array indexing) is **independent** of the element type. Therefore, we **lift** `int` to a symbolic placeholder $\alpha$:

$$
\text{Stack}_{\alpha} = \langle\, \alpha,\; \text{array}_{\alpha}[n],\;\{\text{push}(x:\alpha),\text{pop}():\alpha\},\;\{\text{LIFO}\}\,\rangle
$$

This is the **parameterized specification**. No concrete type has been fixed yet.

### Step 3 — Java Implementation (Type-Safe Generic Class)

```java
public class Stack<T> {
    private Object[] data;
    private int top;
    private static final int CAP = 100;

    public Stack() {
        data = new Object[CAP];
        top  = -1;
    }

    public void push(T item) {            // type parameter T used as element type
        if (top == CAP - 1) throw new RuntimeException("Overflow");
        data[++top] = item;
    }

    @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
    public T pop() {                      // return type is T, not Object
        if (top == -1) throw new RuntimeException("Underflow");
        T value = (T) data[top--];        // cast is safe due to push discipline
        return value;
    }
}
```

### Step 4 — Concrete Binding by Client Code

The client performs **three independent bindings**:

$$
\text{Stack}\langle \text{Integer} \rangle \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha := \text{Integer}
$$

$$
\text{Stack}\langle \text{String} \rangle \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha := \text{String}
$$

$$
\text{Stack}\langle \text{Employee} \rangle \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha := \text{Employee}
$$

Each binding produces a **distinct, type-safe** instantiation:

```java
Stack<Integer>  s1 = new Stack<>();
Stack<String>   s2 = new Stack<>();
Stack<Employee> s3 = new Stack<>();

s1.push(10);          // OK
s1.push("hello");     // COMPILE-TIME ERROR — incompatible type
s2.push(10);          // COMPILE-TIME ERROR — incompatible type
```

### Step 5 — Bounded Parameterization (Adding Constraints)

Suppose we need a Stack that **only accepts comparable elements** (so we can later sort). We add an **upper bound**:

$$
\alpha \;\leq\; \text{Comparable}\langle \alpha \rangle
$$

Java syntax:

```java
public class SortedStack<T extends Comparable<T>> {
    private T[] data;
    // ...
    public void insertSorted(T item) {
        // may invoke item.compareTo(other) — guaranteed safe by bound
    }
}
```

Logical implication:

$$
\forall\, T : T \leq \text{Comparable}\langle T \rangle \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{insertSorted}(x) \text{ is well-defined}
$$

### Step 6 — Type-Erasure Semantics (Java Internals)

After compilation, the JVM sees **no** generic types — they are erased:

$$
\text{Stack}\langle T \rangle \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{erasure}}\;\; \text{Stack}_{\text{raw}}
$$

And unchecked casts are inserted at every former `T` site:

$$
\text{push}(T\,x) \;\;\rightarrow\;\; \text{push}(\text{Object}\,x)
$$

$$
T\,\text{value} = (T)\,\text{data}[top--] \;\;\rightarrow\;\; \text{Object value} = \text{data}[top--]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Erasure preserves **binary compatibility** with pre-generic code but trades it for **no reified generics** at runtime. C++ templates do **not** erase — they generate a new class per binding, which is heavier but allows full type introspection.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Regular ADT vs Parameterized ADT

| **Property**                | **Regular ADT (Mono-Typed)**               | **Parameterized ADT (Generic)**                          |
|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| Type binding                | Fixed at design time                       | Deferred to instantiation time                            |
| Code duplication            | One class per type → **N classes**         | One class serves **all** types                            |
| Type safety                 | Achieved by hand or by `Object` casts      | Enforced **at compile time** by compiler                  |
| Polymorphism                | Only subtyping (inclusion)                 | **Parametric + Inclusion** polymorphism                   |
| Discoverable at runtime     | Yes (reflection sees concrete class)       | Java: **No** (erased); C++: **Yes** (template instantiation) |
| Compile cost                | Low                                        | Higher — instantiation may exponentially expand           |
| Runtime cost                | Zero overhead                              | Java: zero (erasure); C++: zero per instantiation         |
| Example (Java)              | `class IntStack`                           | `class Stack<T>`                                          |
| Example (C++)               | `class IntStack`                           | `template<class T> class Stack`                           |
| Bounded constraint          | Not applicable                             | `T extends Number`, `T extends Comparable<T>`             |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Parameterized ADT Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **full operational pipeline** of a Parameterized ADT, from abstract specification to runtime instantiation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    specA["Abstract ADT Specification"] --> parmA["Introduce Type Parameter alpha"]
    parmA --> boundA["Declare Bound on alpha"]
    boundA --> implA["Generic Implementation Stack of T"]
    implA --> bindA["Client Binding alpha := Integer"]
    implA --> bindB["Client Binding alpha := String"]
    implA --> bindC["Client Binding alpha := Employee"]
    bindA --> checkA["Compile-time Type Check"]
    bindB --> checkA
    bindC --> checkA
    checkA -->|Pass| codeA["Generate Bytecode Stack Integer"]
    checkA -->|Fail| errA["Compile Error Type Mismatch"]
    codeA --> runtimeA["Runtime Object Instance"]
    runtimeA --> execA["Execute push pop top"]

    style specA fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000
    style parmA fill:#fef7e0,stroke:#f9ab00,color:#000
    style boundA fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#d93025,color:#000
    style implA fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#34a853,color:#000
    style checkA fill:#f3e8fd,stroke:#9334e6,color:#000
    style codeA fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#34a853,color:#000
    style runtimeA fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000
    style errA fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#d93025,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Type Substitution Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This tree visualizes how a single generic specification branches into multiple concrete types:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Generic Spec Stack of T"] --> b1["T is bound to Integer"]
    rootA --> b2["T is bound to Double"]
    rootA --> b3["T is bound to String"]
    rootA --> b4["T is bound to Employee"]
    b1 --> i1["Stack Integer Class"]
    b2 --> i2["Stack Double Class"]
    b3 --> i3["Stack String Class"]
    b4 --> i4["Stack Employee Class"]
    i1 --> op1["push Integer, pop Integer"]
    i2 --> op2["push Double, pop Double"]
    i3 --> op3["push String, pop String"]
    i4 --> op4["push Employee, pop Employee"]

    style rootA fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#d93025,color:#000
    style b1 fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000
    style b2 fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000
    style b3 fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000
    style b4 fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000
    style i1 fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#34a853,color:#000
    style i2 fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#34a853,color:#000
    style i3 fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#34a853,color:#000
    style i4 fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#34a853,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Bounded vs Unbounded Parameterization (Comparison Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    unbounded["Unbounded Parameter T accepts Any Type"] --> anyRef["Object, String, Integer, Custom Classes"]
    boundedU["Upper Bound T extends Number accepts only Numeric Subtypes"] --> numTypes["Integer, Double, Float, Long"]
    boundedL["Lower Bound super Integer accepts Integer and its Superclasses"] --> supTypes["Integer, Number, Object"]
    multB["Multiple Bounds T extends A and B requires T to subtype both"] --> multTypes["Hybrid Subtypes Only"]

    style unbounded fill:#fef7e0,stroke:#f9ab00,color:#000
    style boundedU fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#34a853,color:#000
    style boundedL fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000
    style multB fill:#f3e8fd,stroke:#9334e6,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define a Parameterized Abstract Data Type. How does it differ from a non-parameterized ADT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Parameterized ADT** is an abstract data type whose specification includes one or more **type parameters** (placeholders for actual data types), allowing the same logical structure to be specialized for different concrete types without code duplication.

| **Aspect**              | **Non-Parameterized ADT**     | **Parameterized ADT**              |
|-------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Type                    | Fixed at design time          | Symbolic — bound at instantiation  |
| Reusability             | One type per class            | One class for all types            |
| Type safety             | Manual or unsafe casts        | Compile-time enforced              |

*(Full marks: definition 1 + difference table 2)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is type erasure in the context of Java generics? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Type erasure** is the compile-time process by which the Java compiler removes all generic type information from the bytecode, replacing type parameters with their **bounds** (or `Object` if unbounded) and inserting **unchecked casts** at former generic sites.

**Reasons for using erasure:**
1. **Binary compatibility** with pre-generic (raw) code.
2. **No runtime overhead** — no per-instantiation class generation.
3. Simpler JVM implementation — no reified generics.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for reasons)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, Module-3 questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below. Each contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define a Parameterized ADT formally. Explain with a neat diagram how a single generic specification produces multiple concrete type-safe classes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design and implement a complete **generic `Stack<T>`** ADT in Java with the operations `push(T)`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `isEmpty()`, and `size()`. Demonstrate its use with **three different type bindings**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Formal Definition & Diagram (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Parameterized ADT is defined as the quadruple:

$$
G\langle \alpha \rangle = \langle\, \alpha,\; S(\alpha),\; F(\alpha),\; P(\alpha)\,\rangle
$$

Where $\alpha$ is a **type variable**, $S(\alpha)$ is the state space family, $F(\alpha)$ is the operation family, and $P(\alpha)$ is the axiom family — all universally quantified over $\alpha$.

**Operational Flow (3 marks):**

- Specification → Introduce parameter → Optionally constrain → Bind at client → Compile-time check → Instantiation.
- (Refer to *Figure 4.1* in Section 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture.)

**Benefits (2 marks):**
- Compile-time type safety.
- No code duplication.
- Reusability across the entire reference-type lattice.

**(b) Java Implementation (7 marks):**

```java
public class Stack<T> {
    private Object[] data;
    private int top;

    public Stack(int capacity) {
        data = new Object[capacity];
        top  = -1;
    }

    public void push(T item) {
        if (top == data.length - 1)
            throw new RuntimeException("Stack Overflow");
        data[++top] = item;
    }

    @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
    public T pop() {
        if (top == -1)
            throw new RuntimeException("Stack Underflow");
        return (T) data[top--];
    }

    @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
    public T peek() {
        if (top == -1) throw new RuntimeException("Empty Stack");
        return (T) data[top];
    }

    public boolean isEmpty() { return top == -1; }

    public int size() { return top + 1; }
}
```

**Three Type Bindings (2 marks):**

```java
Stack<Integer>  intStack    = new Stack<>(10);
Stack<String>   strStack    = new Stack<>(10);
Stack<Double>   doubleStack = new Stack<>(10);

intStack.push(42);       strStack.push("KTU");   doubleStack.push(3.14);
System.out.println(intStack.pop());
System.out.println(strStack.pop());
System.out.println(doubleStack.pop());
```

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **bounded type parameters** with suitable Java examples. What is the difference between `<? extends T>` and `<? super T>` wildcards? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a generic Java method `findMax` that returns the largest element of an array of any type that implements `Comparable`. Show its invocation for `Integer`, `String`, and a custom `Employee` class. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bounded Parameters & Wildcards (7 marks):**

**Bounded Type Parameter (3 marks):**

A type parameter can be constrained using the `extends` keyword to restrict it to subtypes of a given class/interface:

```java
public static <T extends Comparable<T>> T findMax(T[] arr) {
    T max = arr[0];
    for (T x : arr)
        if (x.compareTo(max) > 0) max = x;
    return max;
}
```

Logical constraint:

$$
\alpha \;\leq\; \text{Comparable}\langle \alpha \rangle
$$

**Wildcards — Producer Extends, Consumer Super (4 marks):**

| **Wildcard**         | **Symbol**        | **Use Case**                       | **Mnemonic**            |
|----------------------|-------------------|------------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Upper bounded        | `? extends T`     | Read-only — **producer** of T      | "PECS — Producer Extends" |
| Lower bounded        | `? super T`       | Write-only — **consumer** of T     | "PECS — Consumer Super"   |
| Unbounded            | `?`               | Read as `Object`                   | Safe neutral choice       |

```java
public void readAll(List<? extends Number> list) {
    for (Number n : list) System.out.println(n);
}

public void addIntegers(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(10);  list.add(20);
}
```

**(b) Generic `findMax` with Three Invocations (7 marks):**

```java
public class Util {
    public static <T extends Comparable<T>> T findMax(T[] arr) {
        if (arr == null || arr.length == 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Empty array");
        T max = arr[0];
        for (int i = 1; i < arr.length; i++)
            if (arr[i].compareTo(max) > 0)
                max = arr[i];
        return max;
    }
}

// Demonstration
Integer[] nums   = {3, 7, 2, 9, 5};
String[]  names  = {"Anu", "Bala", "Chitra"};
System.out.println(Util.findMax(nums));    // 9
System.out.println(Util.findMax(names));   // Chitra
```

**Custom Employee Class (4 marks):**

```java
class Employee implements Comparable<Employee> {
    private String name;
    private double salary;
    public Employee(String n, double s) { name = n; salary = s; }

    public int compareTo(Employee other) {
        return Double.compare(this.salary, other.salary);
    }
    public String toString() { return name + ":" + salary; }
}

Employee[] team = {
    new Employee("Anu", 50000),
    new Employee("Bala", 75000),
    new Employee("Chitra", 60000)
};
System.out.println(Util.findMax(team));   // Bala:75000.0
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing type parameter with ordinary parameter.** Type parameters go in angle brackets `<T>`; ordinary parameters in parentheses `(T x)`. Examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks for confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the bound when invoking generic methods.** Writing `findMax(myArray)` without ensuring `T extends Comparable<T>` causes compile errors — examiners expect you to mention this.
> 3. **Missing the `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")` annotation** when casting erased types — a 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Not explaining why erasure happens** (binary compatibility) — losing 1 mark in theory answers.
> 5. **Producing diagrams without arrows/labels** — KTU examiners expect **labeled** flow diagrams, not bare boxes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parameterized ADT = Generic ADT = Template ADT** — all three terms are interchangeable in KTU 2024.
- **Quadruple notation:** $G\langle\alpha\rangle = \langle\alpha, S(\alpha), F(\alpha), P(\alpha)\rangle$.
- **Type parameters** use **angle brackets** and single uppercase letters by convention (`T`, `E`, `K`, `V`).
- **Bounds:** `T extends ClassOrInterface` (upper), `? super T` (lower).
- **Wildcards:** `<?>` (unbounded), `<? extends T>` (producer), `<? super T>` (consumer) — remember **PECS**.
- **Java = Erasure**, **C++ Templates = Code Generation** — both achieve parametric polymorphism but via opposite mechanisms.
- **Compile-time type safety** is the **primary benefit** — eliminates `ClassCastException`.
- **Raw types** (using generics without parameters) are allowed for backward compatibility but **produce compiler warnings**.
- **Bridge methods** are auto-generated by `javac` to preserve polymorphism after erasure — relevant when extending generic classes.
- **Cannot instantiate type parameters directly:** `new T()` is illegal — must use `new Object()` + cast or reflection.
- **Cannot use primitives as type arguments:** `Stack<int>` is invalid; use `Stack<Integer>` (auto-boxing handles conversion).
- **Generic arrays** are not allowed in Java (`new T[10]`) but allowed in C++ — a frequently tested edge case.
- **Liskov substitutivity** holds: if $T' \leq T$, then $G\langle T' \rangle \leq G\langle T \rangle$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"GASP"** → **G**eneric ADT, **A**ngle brackets, **S**ubstitutivity, **P**ECS rule.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encapsulation Constructs
Naming Encapsulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Naming Encapsulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: NAMING ENCAPSULATIONS — FOUNDATIONS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Encapsulation** is a software engineering principle in which a program's identifiers (classes, methods, variables, packages, and constants) are organized, scoped, and structured using systematic naming rules and hierarchical name spaces so that the **logical boundary** of each module is clearly defined, name collisions are eliminated, and the **internal implementation** of a component remains hidden from external code.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Advanced Computer Programming Techniques* (PEBMT746) treats naming encapsulation as a **multi-layered abstraction mechanism** comprising four pillars:

1. **Lexical Naming Conventions** — the standardized *grammar* of identifiers (camelCase, PascalCase, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE, lowercase packages).
2. **Hierarchical Name Spaces (Packages)** — the *directory structure* providing logical grouping.
3. **Fully Qualified Names (FQN)** — the *unique global address* of every type.
4. **Access Modifiers Coupled with Naming** — the *visibility firewall* that protects names from unintended external access.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Apartment Building 🏢

Imagine a massive apartment complex with thousands of residents:

| Programming Concept | Real-World Apartment Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Class Name** | The resident's full legal name (e.g., `John Smith`) |
| **Package** | The building address (`TowerB.Floor5.Apartment12`) |
| **Naming Convention** | The house rule: "All residents use `FirstName LastName` format" |
| **Access Modifier (`private`)** | The locked door of the apartment — only the resident enters |
| **`public` accessor (getter)** | The doorbell — outsiders ring, resident decides to open |
| **FQN (`com.ksrce.csdept.Student`)** | The full postal address with PIN code — unique worldwide |
| **Name Collision** | Two "John"s in the same building causing confusion |
| **`import` statement** | The building directory you carry to find residents quickly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Naming encapsulation is NOT just about "naming variables neatly." It is a **structural design discipline** that combines identifier conventions + package hierarchies + access control to achieve **information hiding**, one of the five SOLID design principles.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Naming Encapsulation

| Pillar | Mechanism | Primary Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Convention Layer** | Identifier syntax (camelCase, etc.) | Readability & team uniformity |
| **Structural Layer** | Packages & directories | Logical grouping & FQN uniqueness |
| **Visibility Layer** | `private`, `protected`, `public`, *package-private* | Information hiding |
| **Resolution Layer** | `import`, `import static`, FQN usage | Conflict-free name resolution |

### 1.4 Standard KTU-Mandated Naming Metrics

The following Java Language Specification (JLS) rules are **non-negotiable** in KTU lab evaluations:

- **Class / Interface Names:** `PascalCase` (e.g., `BinarySearchTree`)
- **Method / Variable Names:** `camelCase` (e.g., `computeSalary()`)
- **Constant Names:** `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` (e.g., `MAX_BUFFER_SIZE = 1024`)
- **Package Names:** All lowercase, reverse-DNS, no underscores (e.g., `edu.ksrce.mca.adbms`)
- **Valid Identifier Characters:** Must begin with a letter, `$`, or `_`; subsequent chars may include digits.
- **Reserved Words Forbidden:** All 50 Java keywords (`class`, `int`, `if`, `new`, etc.) cannot be used as identifiers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Naming Encapsulation

Naming encapsulation addresses **three chronic problems** in large-scale software:

1. **The Collision Problem** — Two developers define a class called `Node` in different modules. Without namespaces, the compiler cannot resolve which to use.
2. **The Ambiguity Problem** — `Date` could mean `java.util.Date` or `java.sql.Date`. Without FQN, the programmer cannot disambiguate.
3. **The Hidden-Dependency Problem** — A class secretly depends on another class's internals. With strict access modifiers, dependencies are forced to be *explicit*.

## 2.2 Layer-1: Lexical Naming Conventions (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 Class & Interface Naming

- Use **PascalCase**: capitalize the first letter of *every word*, no separators.
- Use **nouns** for classes (`Student`, `LinkedList`).
- Use **adjectives** for interfaces (`Serializable`, `Comparable`).
- Avoid abbreviations except universally understood ones (`URL`, `HTML`, `IO`).

### 2.2.2 Method Naming

- Use **camelCase**: first word lowercase, subsequent words capitalized.
- Use **verbs** for action methods (`compute`, `validate`, `execute`).
- Use `get`/`is`/`has` prefix for boolean/accessor methods (`isEmpty()`, `getName()`).
- Use `to` prefix for converters (`toString()`, `toArray()`).

### 2.2.3 Variable Naming

- **Local variables:** `camelCase`, short but meaningful (`idx`, `maxVal`).
- **Instance variables:** `camelCase`, prefixed appropriately (e.g., `mBalance` in Android, plain `balance` in standard Java).
- **Static variables:** Same as instance but reflect shared state (`totalCount`).
- **Constants:** `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE`, declared `static final`.

### 2.2.4 Package Naming

- **All lowercase**, no underscores, no camelCase.
- **Reverse-DNS convention** to guarantee global uniqueness:
  - `com.ktu.btech` (organization: KTU, department: B.Tech)
  - `org.apache.commons.lang3` (organization: Apache, project: Commons)
  - `edu.ksrce.mca.adbms` (institution: KSCE, program: MCA, subject: ADBMS)

## 2.3 Layer-2: Packages as Name Spaces

A **package** in Java is simultaneously:

1. A **file-system directory** (logical grouping on disk).
2. A **name-space** (logical grouping in memory).
3. An **access-control boundary** (package-private access).
4. A **distribution unit** (JAR files group packages).

### 2.3.1 Package Declaration Rules

- Must be the **first non-comment statement** in a `.java` file.
- Only **one** package declaration per file.
- A file without a package declaration belongs to the **unnamed (default) package** — strongly discouraged in KTU submissions.

### 2.3.2 The Package-Private Access Modifier

There are **four** access levels in Java, not three:

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Diff Pkg) | Other Packages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *package-private* (default) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students frequently forget the **default (package-private)** access level. A class member declared *without any modifier* is **NOT** accessible to subclasses in a *different* package — only same-package classes can see it.

## 2.4 Layer-3: Fully Qualified Names (FQN) and Resolution

### 2.4.1 Simple Name vs FQN

A **simple name** is the identifier alone (`Date`).
A **fully qualified name** includes the package path (`java.util.Date`).

The Java compiler resolves names in this **strict order**:

1. **Local scope** (variables defined in the current method/block).
2. **Enclosing class scope** (members of the current class).
3. **Inherited members** (from superclasses).
4. **Imported types** (single-type import, then import-on-demand).
5. **Same package** (default package lookup).
6. **`java.lang` automatic import** (special rule: always available).

### 2.4.2 Import Statements and Name Encapsulation

- `import java.util.List;` → *single-type import* (preferred for clarity).
- `import java.util.*;` → *type-import-on-demand* (use sparingly).
- `import static java.lang.Math.PI;` → *static single import* (for constants).
- `import static java.util.Collections.*;` → *static on-demand import*.

> [!WARNING]
> Importing `java.util.*` and `java.sql.*` together causes **ambiguity** for `Date`. The compiler will reject ambiguous references. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this in lab viva.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Class naming | `PascalCase` | `BinarySearchTree` |
| Method naming | `camelCase` verb | `computeInterest()` |
| Constant naming | `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` | `MAX_RETRIES = 5` |
| Package naming | lowercase reverse-DNS | `in.ktu.btech.cs` |
| Valid identifier start | Letter / `$` / `_` | `_count`, `$flag`, `name7` |
| Reserved keyword | Cannot be identifier | `class`, `int`, `for` |
| FQN format | `pkg1.pkg2...TypeName` | `java.util.ArrayList` |
| Package-private | No modifier keyword | `void helper() { ... }` |
| Encapsulation triplet | `private` field + `public` getter/setter | Standard POJO pattern |
| FQN Uniqueness | Every type has **one** global FQN | Two `Date` classes coexist via FQN |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microservices:** Each service is a separate package hierarchy with its own FQN tree.
- **Android Development:** Package names double as **app IDs** on Google Play Store.
- **Maven/Gradle Build Systems:** `groupId` and `artifactId` directly follow reverse-DNS naming.
- **Enterprise Java (J2EE):** `javax.servlet`, `org.hibernate` packages enforce architectural layers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Name Resolution Algorithm

**Problem:** Given the following file structure, determine the FQN of each class and identify any naming conflicts.

```
src/
└── com/
    └── ktu/
        └── cse/
            ├── data/
            │   ├── Student.java
            │   └── Course.java
            └── util/
                ├── Date.java
                └── Validator.java
```

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the package root directory.**
The root directory is `src/`. Everything under it forms packages.

**Step 2 — Read the package declaration inside each `.java` file.**

The first non-comment line of `Student.java` must read:

$$ \text{package} \; \texttt{com.ktu.cse.data;} $$

**Step 3 — Derive the Fully Qualified Name (FQN).**
The FQN of a class is constructed as:

$$ \text{FQN}(C) = \text{package\_path}(C) \; . \; \text{class\_name}(C) $$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FQN}(\texttt{Student}) &= \texttt{com.ktu.cse.data.Student} \\
\text{FQN}(\texttt{Course}) &= \texttt{com.ktu.cse.data.Course} \\
\text{FQN}(\texttt{Date}) &= \texttt{com.ktu.cse.util.Date} \\
\text{FQN}(\texttt{Validator}) &= \texttt{com.ktu.cse.util.Validator}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify global uniqueness.**
Each FQN is unique because the package paths are distinct (`data` vs `util`). The FQN `com.ktu.cse.util.Date` can coexist with `java.util.Date` because the FQN differs.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
No naming conflicts. The two `Date` classes are resolved using the dot-separated FQN addressing scheme.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Access Modifier Resolution

**Problem:** A class `Employee` in package `com.ktu.payroll` defines a method `calculateTax()`. Class `HRManager` in package `com.ktu.hr` extends `Employee`. Determine if `HRManager` can invoke `calculateTax()`.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the access modifier on `calculateTax()`.**
Suppose the declaration is:

$$ \text{Modifier}(M) = \texttt{protected} \; \texttt{double calculateTax()} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the access decision tree.**
For a `protected` member, access is granted if **any** of the following holds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Access}(M) &= \text{true} \iff \\
&\quad (\text{caller} \in \text{same class}) \;\lor \\
&\quad (\text{caller} \in \text{same package}) \;\lor \\
&\quad (\text{caller} \in \text{subclass, regardless of package})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each condition.**

- **Same class?** `HRManager` is a different class. ❌
- **Same package?** `com.ktu.payroll` ≠ `com.ktu.hr`. ❌
- **Subclass?** `HRManager extends Employee`. ✅

**Step 4 — Resolve.**
Since at least one disjunct is true, access is granted:

$$ \text{Access}(calculateTax(), HRManager) = \text{true} $$

**Step 5 — Important caveat.**
The `protected` access only works through **inheritance**, not by direct instance reference to a parent object. This means `HRManager` can call `this.calculateTax()` or use it internally, but a `HRManager` reference to an `Employee` object can only access `protected` members if they reside in a `com.ktu.payroll` package context. KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests this in viva.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Encapsulation Triplet Construction

**Problem:** Construct a properly encapsulated `BankAccount` class demonstrating the full naming encapsulation triplet.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Declare the class with PascalCase.**

$$ \texttt{public class BankAccount} \; \{ \ldots \} $$

**Step 2 — Apply private modifier to the field (data hiding).**

$$ \texttt{private double balance;} $$

This is the **encapsulation firewall**. External classes cannot read or modify `balance` directly.

**Step 3 — Provide public getter (read-only access through naming).**

$$ \texttt{public double getBalance()} \; \{ \texttt{return balance;} \} $$

The method name `getBalance` follows `get` + PascalCase field convention.

**Step 4 — Provide public setter with validation (write-only controlled access).**

$$ \texttt{public void setBalance(double balance)} \; \{ \ldots \} $$

**Step 5 — Define a class-level constant (naming encapsulation for fixed values).**

$$ \texttt{public static final double MINIMUM\_BALANCE} = 1000.0; $$

The constant name follows `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` and is encapsulated inside the class as a `public static final` member — accessible globally but immutable.

**Step 6 — Final assembled structure.**

```text
Package: com.ktu.bank.model
FQN:     com.ktu.bank.model.BankAccount
Fields:  [private] balance
Methods: [public]  getBalance(), setBalance()
         [private] validateAmount()
Constants:[public static final] MINIMUM_BALANCE
```

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conflict Resolution Strategies

| Conflict Type | Symptom | KTU-Recommended Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Same class name, different packages | `error: reference to Date is ambiguous` | Use FQN: `java.util.Date d1 = new java.util.Date();` |
| Variable shadows field | `this.balance = balance;` confusion | Use `this` keyword + distinct parameter naming |
| Method hides static method | `warning: static method should be qualified` | Call as `ClassName.staticMethod()` not `instance.method()` |
| Import vs `java.lang` clash | `error: incompatible types: String cannot be converted to StringBuilder` | Drop custom `String` class; reuse `java.lang.String` |
| `protected` access denied | `error: calculateTax() has protected access in Employee` | Move caller to same package OR use inheritance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Four-Layer Naming Encapsulation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Layer 1: Lexical Conventions] --> B[Layer 2: Package Hierarchy]
    B --> C[Layer 3: FQN Resolution]
    C --> D[Layer 4: Access Modifiers]
    
    A --> A1[PascalCase Classes]
    A --> A2[camelCase Methods]
    A --> A3[SCREAMING_SNAKE Constants]
    A --> A4[lowercase packages]
    
    B --> B1[com.ktu.cse.data]
    B --> B2[com.ktu.cse.util]
    B --> B3[Reverse DNS Pattern]
    
    C --> C1[Simple Name]
    C --> C2[import single type]
    C --> C3[import on demand]
    C --> C4[static import]
    
    D --> D1[private: class only]
    D --> D2[package-private: package]
    D --> D3[protected: pkg plus subclass]
    D --> D4[public: global]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Name Resolution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Compiler encounters identifier X]) --> L1{Is X a local variable?}
    L1 -- Yes --> UseLocal[Use local binding]
    L1 -- No --> L2{Is X a member of current class?}
    L2 -- Yes --> UseMember[Use class member]
    L2 -- No --> L3{Is X inherited?}
    L3 -- Yes --> UseInherited[Use inherited member]
    L3 -- No --> L4{Is X explicitly imported?}
    L4 -- Yes --> UseImport[Use imported type]
    L4 -- No --> L5{Is X in same package?}
    L5 -- Yes --> UsePkg[Use same-package type]
    L5 -- No --> L6{Is X in java.lang?}
    L6 -- Yes --> UseLang[Use java.lang type]
    L6 -- No --> Error[Compile Error: Cannot find symbol]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Package Hierarchy Tree (KTU Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[com.ktu.cse] --> DataPkg[data]
    Root --> UtilPkg[util]
    Root --> ModelPkg[model]
    Root --> TestPkg[test]
    
    DataPkg --> StudentC[Student.java]
    DataPkg --> CourseC[Course.java]
    UtilPkg --> DateC[Date.java]
    UtilPkg --> ValidatorC[Validator.java]
    ModelPkg --> BankAccountC[BankAccount.java]
    TestPkg --> BankAccountTestC[BankAccountTest.java]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Encapsulation Triplet Class Structure

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class BankAccount {
        -double balance
        -String accountHolder
        +static final double MIN_BALANCE
        +getBalance() double
        +setBalance(double) void
        -validateAmount(double) boolean
        +deposit(double) void
        +withdraw(double) void
    }
    
    class HRManager {
        +calculateTax() double
    }
    
    BankAccount <.. HRManager : uses
    
    note for BankAccount "Encapsulation Triplet:\n1. private fields\n2. public getters/setters\n3. validated access"
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Access Modifier Visibility Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SameClass[Same Class] --> Private[private: YES]
    SamePkg[Same Package] --> Private
    Subclass[Subclass] --> Private
    Other[Other Pkg] --> Private
    
    SameClass --> PkgPrivate[package-private: YES]
    SamePkg --> PkgPrivate
    Subclass --> PkgPrivate
    Other --> PkgPrivate
    
    SameClass --> Protected[protected: YES]
    SamePkg --> Protected
    Subclass --> Protected
    Other --> Protected
    
    SameClass --> Public[public: YES]
    SamePkg --> Public
    Subclass --> Public
    Other --> Public
    
    Private -.denied.- SamePkg
    Private -.denied.- Subclass
    Private -.denied.- Other
    PkgPrivate -.denied.- Subclass
    PkgPrivate -.denied.- Other
    Protected -.denied.- Other
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of "naming encapsulation" in Java. How does it differ from a simple naming convention?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Naming encapsulation is a comprehensive design principle that combines identifier naming rules, hierarchical package structures, and access control modifiers to achieve logical module boundaries and information hiding. While a simple naming convention only dictates *how identifiers should be formatted* (e.g., camelCase methods), naming encapsulation extends this to include *where identifiers live* (packages), *how unique they are* (FQN), and *who can see them* (access modifiers). The full stack ensures that a class's internal representation is hidden while exposing a controlled public interface, satisfying the encapsulation pillar of OOP.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six Java naming conventions with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Convention | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class names use PascalCase | `BinarySearchTree` |
| 2 | Method names use camelCase verbs | `calculateSalary()` |
| 3 | Constant names use SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE | `MAX_BUFFER_SIZE` |
| 4 | Package names use all lowercase reverse-DNS | `in.ktu.btech.cse` |
| 5 | Boolean variables use `is`/`has` prefix | `isActive`, `hasChildren` |
| 6 | Interface names use PascalCase adjectives | `Serializable`, `Runnable` |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With suitable code examples, explain the **four access modifiers** in Java and construct a clear access matrix indicating which modifier is visible from which context. State the **default access level** explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **package naming convention** in Java. Why is the reverse-DNS pattern recommended? How does the concept of a *fully qualified name* (FQN) resolve ambiguity? Provide an example where two classes with the same simple name coexist via FQN. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Step 1 — Define access modifiers.**
Java provides four access levels controlled by three explicit keywords plus a default:

$$
\text{Access Levels} = \{\texttt{private}, \texttt{package-private}, \texttt{protected}, \texttt{public}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the access matrix (4×4).**

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Diff Pkg) | Other Packages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *package-private* (default) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

**Step 3 — State the default access.**
When no access modifier is specified, the member is accessible only within the **same package**. This is called *package-private* or *default* access — it is **not** equivalent to `public` or `protected`.

**Step 4 — Code example.**

```text
package com.ktu.demo;

public class Account {
    private double balance;            // visible only inside Account
    String accountType;                // package-private: visible in com.ktu.demo
    protected String ownerName;        // visible in same pkg + subclasses
    public String getOwner() {         // globally visible
        return ownerName;
    }
}
```

**Step 5 — Concluding remark.**
The tightest encapsulation uses `private` fields with `public` getters/setters, restricting direct field access to the class itself.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Step 1 — Define Java package naming convention.**
- All package names must be **lowercase**.
- Use **reverse-DNS** format: `<tld>.<domain>.<subdomain>...`
- Underscores are forbidden; numbers should be avoided.
- A package name cannot start with a Java keyword.

**Step 2 — Justify the reverse-DNS pattern.**
The reverse-DNS pattern guarantees **global uniqueness** because domain names are uniquely allocated by ICANN. By reversing the organization domain (e.g., `ktu.in` → `in.ktu`), two organizations will never produce identical package names. This prevents the "two companies both wrote a `Date` class" problem.

**Step 3 — Define the Fully Qualified Name (FQN).**

$$ \text{FQN}(C) = \text{package\_path}(C) \; . \; \text{class\_name}(C) $$

**Step 4 — Illustrate coexistence via FQN.**
Two classes both named `Date` can coexist:
- `java.util.Date` — for general date-time representation.
- `java.sql.Date` — for SQL DATE type compatibility.

In the same file:

```text
import java.util.Date;
import java.sql.Date;          // COMPILE ERROR: duplicate import

// Resolution: use FQN
java.util.Date utilDate = new java.util.Date();
java.sql.Date  sqlDate  = new java.sql.Date(System.currentTimeMillis());
```

**Step 5 — Compiler resolution rules.**
The Java compiler resolves names in a fixed order: local scope → class scope → imported types → same package → `java.lang` (auto-imported). When ambiguity arises, the FQN disambiguates it.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Define **encapsulation** as it relates to naming in Java. Construct a complete `Student` class demonstrating the **encapsulation triplet** (private fields, public getters/setters, and validated mutators). Explain why each layer is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the `import` statement in name resolution. Differentiate between **single-type import**, **type-import-on-demand**, and **static import**. Provide a scenario where `import java.util.*` and `import java.sql.*` together cause ambiguity and how the programmer resolves it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Step 1 — Define encapsulation in the naming context.**
Encapsulation in Java is the bundling of data (fields) and the methods that operate on that data within a single class, combined with **access control** that hides the internal state from external manipulation. In the naming context, encapsulation is enforced through three coordinated mechanisms: `private` field names (hidden), `public` accessor names (visible), and validated mutator names (controlled).

**Step 2 — Construct the `Student` class.**

```text
package com.ktu.cse.model;

public class Student {
    // Step 2a: PRIVATE FIELDS — the hidden data layer
    private String rollNumber;
    private String name;
    private double cgpa;
    
    // Step 2b: PUBLIC CONSTANT — encapsulated immutable value
    public static final double MIN_CGPA = 0.0;
    public static final double MAX_CGPA = 10.0;
    
    // Step 2c: PUBLIC CONSTRUCTOR — naming-based initialization gate
    public Student(String rollNumber, String name, double cgpa) {
        this.rollNumber = rollNumber;
        this.name       = name;
        setCgpa(cgpa);   // validation via setter
    }
    
    // Step 2d: PUBLIC GETTERS — naming-convention read access
    public String getRollNumber() { return rollNumber; }
    public String getName()       { return name; }
    public double getCgpa()      { return cgpa; }
    
    // Step 2e: PUBLIC SETTER with validation — controlled write access
    public void setCgpa(double cgpa) {
        if (cgpa < MIN_CGPA || cgpa > MAX_CGPA) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("CGPA out of range");
        }
        this.cgpa = cgpa;
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Explain each layer's necessity.**

- **Private fields** — Prevent direct external modification. A field like `cgpa` could be set to an invalid value (`-5.0`) if exposed.
- **Public getters** — Provide *read* access without exposing mutability. The name `getCgpa` signals the intent.
- **Public setters with validation** — Provide *write* access but with a controlled filter. The name `setCgpa` suggests a side-effect: validation.
- **Public constants** — Encapsulated as `public static final`, they are globally readable but cannot be reassigned. The `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` name signals immutability.

**Step 4 — Concluding principle.**
This design adheres to the **"Tell, Don't Ask"** principle — external classes ask the `Student` object to do something (via method names) rather than reach into its internals.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Step 1 — Role of `import`.**
The `import` statement instructs the compiler where to find types whose simple names will be used in the source file. It does **not** physically include code; it merely resolves names at compile time.

**Step 2 — Differentiate import types.**

| Import Type | Syntax | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-type import** | `import java.util.ArrayList;` | Best practice; explicit and clear |
| **Type-import-on-demand** | `import java.util.*;` | Imports all types from a package; used for many classes |
| **Static single import** | `import static java.lang.Math.PI;` | Import a static member (constant or method) |
| **Static on-demand import** | `import static java.lang.Math.*;` | Import all static members of a class |

**Step 3 — The ambiguity scenario.**

When a file contains both:

```text
import java.util.*;
import java.sql.*;
```

…and references a type named `Date`, the compiler cannot decide whether to use `java.util.Date` (general date-time) or `java.sql.Date` (SQL DATE). Both are present in the imported name spaces, producing:

```text
error: reference to Date is ambiguous
    Date today = new Date();
                  ^
  both class java.util.Date in java.util and class java.sql.Date in java.sql match
```

**Step 4 — Resolution strategies.**

1. **Remove one import** — explicitly import only the one needed.
2. **Use FQN directly** — qualify each usage with the package path:
   ```text
   java.util.Date d1 = new java.util.Date();
   java.sql.Date  d2 = new java.sql.Date(0L);
   ```
3. **Use single-type import** — `import java.util.Date;` makes the compiler prefer this type unless overridden.

**Step 5 — KTU exam tip.**
Always prefer single-type imports for clarity. Type-import-on-demand (`*`) is acceptable in lab code but discouraged in production because it pollutes the namespace.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Naming Encapsulation Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting package-private access (2 marks lost):** Students list only `public`, `private`, `protected` and miss the **default** modifier. Examiners specifically test this in 3-mark questions.
> 
> 2. **Wrong example for FQN (1 mark lost):** Writing `java.Date` instead of `java.util.Date` is a factual error. FQNs must match the actual package path.
> 
> 3. **Mixing camelCase and PascalCase in examples (1 mark lost):** Method names must start with lowercase. `CalculateSalary()` is **wrong**; `calculateSalary()` is **correct**.
> 
> 4. **Using underscores in package names (1 mark lost):** `com.ktu_cse` violates the lowercase-only rule. The correct form is `com.ktu.cse` or `com.ktucse`.
> 
> 5. **Confusing `protected` with `public` for cross-package access (2 marks lost):** `protected` does NOT grant access to non-subclass classes in a different package. Only `public` does.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the `final` keyword for constants (1 mark lost):** A constant must be declared `public static final`, not just `static` or `final` alone.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist for Naming Encapsulations:**

- ✅ **Naming encapsulation** = conventions + packages + FQN + access modifiers.
- ✅ **Class names** → `PascalCase` (e.g., `HashMap`).
- ✅ **Method/Variable names** → `camelCase` (e.g., `getName()`, `firstName`).
- ✅ **Constants** → `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` (e.g., `PI`, `MAX_VALUE`).
- ✅ **Packages** → all lowercase, reverse-DNS (e.g., `in.ktu.btech.cse`).
- ✅ **Four access levels** → `private`, *package-private* (default), `protected`, `public`.
- ✅ **Default access** = package-private; **NOT** `public`.
- ✅ **FQN** uniquely identifies a type as `pkg1.pkg2.TypeName`.
- ✅ **Encapsulation triplet** = `private` field + `public` getter + `public` setter (with validation).
- ✅ **Java resolves names** in fixed order: local → class → import → package → `java.lang`.
- ✅ **`import` does not bundle code** — it is a compile-time name resolution directive.
- ✅ **`import java.util.*` + `import java.sql.*`** = ambiguity for `Date`; use FQN to resolve.
- ✅ **Package declaration** must be the **first** non-comment statement in the file.
- ✅ **Reverse-DNS naming** guarantees global uniqueness across organizations.
- ✅ **`protected` ≠ `public`** — protected is NOT visible to non-subclass code in another package.
- ✅ **Reserved keywords** (50 in Java) cannot be used as identifiers.
- ✅ **Constants** are declared `public static final` and named in `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE`.
- ✅ **Encapsulation goal** = hide implementation, expose interface, control invariants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues for OOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Issues for OOP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Advanced Computer Programming Techniques* (PEBMT746), **Design Issues for Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** refers to the set of architectural, syntactic, semantic, and runtime decisions that a programmer must resolve while modelling a real-world problem using the four pillars of OOP — **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism**.

These design issues dictate:
- How classes are structured and how objects communicate.
- How memory, identity, and lifetime of objects are managed.
- How reuse, extensibility, and maintainability are guaranteed.
- How type safety, binding (early vs late), and substitutability are enforced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"OOP design issues are the recurring decision-points that influence the correctness, efficiency, reusability, and maintainability of object-oriented software. They arise from the conflict between expressive power (e.g., multiple inheritance, dynamic binding) and safety (e.g., type safety, deterministic destruction)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house blueprint (Class)** for a builder to construct actual **houses (Objects)**. Before construction begins, you must answer several questions:

| Design Question (Architect) | OOP Design Issue |
|----------------------------|------------------|
| "Can the owner peek inside the walls?" | **Encapsulation & Information Hiding** |
| "Should the blueprint allow adding new floors easily?" | **Inheritance & Extensibility** |
| "Can one switch (light switch) operate differently based on wiring?" | **Polymorphism & Dynamic Binding** |
| "How much detail should the blueprint show to the contractor vs. owner?" | **Abstraction** |
| "What happens when the house is demolished — who cleans up?" | **Object Lifetime & Garbage Collection** |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a poor blueprint leads to a weak house, poor OOP design choices lead to *rigid, fragile, and immobile* software — the well-known software anti-patterns KTU examiners love to test.

## 1.3 The 10 Canonical OOP Design Issues

The following list represents the **high-yield issues** consistently tested in KTU university exams:

1. **Encapsulation & Data Hiding** — access specifiers and visibility.
2. **Inheritance Models** — single, multiple, multilevel, hierarchical, hybrid.
3. **The Diamond Problem** — ambiguity in multiple inheritance.
4. **Polymorphism & Binding** — early binding vs late binding.
5. **Abstract Classes & Interfaces** — pure virtual functions vs Java interfaces.
6. **Object Identity vs Object Equality** — `==` vs `equals()`.
7. **Memory Management** — stack vs heap, constructors/destructors, GC.
8. **Coupling & Cohesion** — design quality metrics.
9. **Reusability vs Composition** — *is-a* vs *has-a*.
10. **Exception Handling in OOP** — checked vs unchecked, propagation semantics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often frame Part A (3-mark) questions around items 1–5 and Part B (14-mark) questions around items 2, 4, 8, and 9.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Issue 1 — Encapsulation & Data Hiding

**Definition:** *Encapsulation* is the bundling of data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class). *Data hiding* is the restrictive access to internal state using visibility modifiers.

### Access Specifier Comparison Table

| Specifier | Same Class | Derived Class | Same Package (Java) | Global (C++) |
|-----------|:----------:|:-------------:|:-------------------:|:------------:|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (derived) |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| *default* (Java package-private) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | N/A |
| `friend` (C++ only) | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (declared) |

### Design Trade-off
- **Tight encapsulation** → high information hiding, low coupling, easier refactoring.
- **Loose encapsulation** → faster prototyping but high maintenance cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Encapsulation enables **invariant preservation** — once a class invariant is established in the constructor, no external code can violate it.

---

## 2.2 Issue 2 — Inheritance Models

**Definition:** *Inheritance* is the mechanism by which a derived (child) class acquires the attributes and behaviors of a base (parent) class, establishing an **"is-a"** relationship.

### Five Canonical Forms

1. **Single Inheritance** — One base, one derived. Clean, unambiguous.
2. **Multiple Inheritance** — One derived, multiple bases. Powerful but introduces the **Diamond Problem**.
3. **Multilevel Inheritance** — Chain: A → B → C.
4. **Hierarchical Inheritance** — One base, multiple derived.
5. **Hybrid Inheritance** — Combination of two or more types.

### Binding Taxonomy

$$ \text{Binding Time} \in \{ \text{Compile-time},\ \text{Link-time},\ \text{Run-time} \} $$

- **Early (Static) Binding** — resolved by compiler (overloading, non-virtual methods).
- **Late (Dynamic) Binding** — resolved at runtime via v-table (C++ virtual, Java override).

---

## 2.3 Issue 3 — The Diamond Problem

**Definition:** An ambiguity that arises in multiple inheritance when two base classes inherit from the same grandparent, and a class derives from both.

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
       \ /
        D
```

Here, **D** inherits `A::method()` twice (once via B, once via C), causing ambiguity.

### Resolution Techniques

| Language | Resolution Strategy |
|----------|---------------------|
| **C++** | Explicit scope resolution (`D::A::method()`) or **virtual inheritance** (`class B : virtual public A`) |
| **Java** | **Disallowed** — classes cannot have multiple parents; interfaces only |
| **C#** | **Disallowed** for classes; interfaces use explicit implementation |
| **Python** | **MRO (Method Resolution Order)** via C3 linearization |

---

## 2.4 Issue 4 — Polymorphism

**Definition:** The ability of a single interface to represent different underlying forms (data types).

### Polymorphism Matrix

| Type | Mechanism | Resolution | Example |
|------|-----------|:----------:|---------|
| Ad-hoc (Overloading) | Same name, different signatures | Compile-time | `add(int, int)` vs `add(float, float)` |
| Parametric (Generics) | Type parameterization | Compile-time | `template<class T> T sum(T a, T b)` |
| Inclusion (Subtyping) | Subclass overriding base method | Run-time | `Animal* a = new Dog(); a->speak();` |
| Coercion | Implicit type conversion | Compile-time | `int x = 3.14;` (lossy) |

### Late Binding Cost
For a virtual call in C++, the cost is one extra **indirection through the vtable**:

$$ T_{\text{virtual-call}} = T_{\text{load-vptr}} + T_{\text{load-vfn}} + T_{\text{invoke}} $$

---

## 2.5 Issue 5 — Abstract Classes vs Interfaces

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---------|:--------------:|:---------:|
| Can have implemented methods | ✅ | ❌ (Java 7-), ✅ with `default` (Java 8+) |
| Can have state (fields) | ✅ | ❌ (only `static final`) |
| Multiple inheritance allowed | ❌ | ✅ |
| Use case | Common base with shared code | Pure contract / capability |

**In C++:** Abstract class is any class containing at least one **pure virtual function**:

$$ \text{virtual void draw()} = 0; $$

---

## 2.6 Issues 6–10 — Quick Reference

| # | Issue | Core Question | Pitfall |
|---|-------|---------------|---------|
| 6 | Identity vs Equality | Is `obj1 == obj2` reference or value? | Java `==` compares references; `equals()` compares values |
| 7 | Memory Mgmt | Who frees memory? | C++: programmer; Java/C#: GC thread |
| 8 | Coupling/Cohesion | How independent are modules? | Aim for **low coupling, high cohesion** |
| 9 | Inheritance vs Composition | *is-a* or *has-a*? | Prefer composition for flexibility |
| 10 | Exception Handling | Checked vs Unchecked? | Java forces handling of checked exceptions |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Notation | Meaning | Use Case |
|--------------------|---------|----------|
| $C = f(S, M)$ | Class = State + Methods | Encapsulation |
| $\text{is-a}(D, B)$ | D inherits B | Inheritance |
| $\text{has-a}(W, E)$ | W contains E | Composition |
| $T_{\text{dyn}} > T_{\text{stat}}$ | Dynamic binding slower | Performance analysis |
| $C_{\text{coupling}} \downarrow$ | Lower is better | Modularity |
| $C_{\text{cohesion}} \uparrow$ | Higher is better | Reusability |
| $\text{MRO}(C) = L[C]$ | C3 linearization | Python diamond resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These design issues directly map to software quality attributes defined in ISO/IEC 25010 — *maintainability, reliability, performance efficiency, and modifiability*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Coupling & Cohesion Metrics

Coupling and cohesion are not single numbers but **qualitative ranks** with measurable proxies.

### 3.1.1 Coupling — Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

We define coupling between modules $M_i$ and $M_j$ as a function of:

$$ C_{\text{coupling}}(M_i, M_j) = w_1 \cdot N_{\text{data}} + w_2 \cdot N_{\text{control}} + w_2 \cdot N_{\text{external}} + w_4 \cdot N_{\text{stamp}} $$

Where:
- $N_{\text{data}}$ = number of data parameters shared
- $N_{\text{control}}$ = number of control flags passed
- $N_{\text{external}}$ = shared external format dependency
- $N_{\text{stamp}}$ = entire data structure passed
- $w_1 < w_2 < w_3 < w_4$ reflect increasing coupling severity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Best Case (Lowest)} &: C_{\text{coupling}} = w_1 \cdot 1 + w_2 \cdot 0 + w_3 \cdot 0 + w_4 \cdot 0 \\
\text{Worst Case (Highest)} &: C_{\text{coupling}} = w_1 \cdot 0 + w_2 \cdot 0 + w_3 \cdot 0 + w_4 \cdot 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

1. Identify each interface between $M_i$ and $M_j$.
2. Classify the type of information exchanged (data / control / external / stamp).
3. Apply the weight coefficients.
4. Sum to obtain the coupling score.

> [!NOTE]
> **Myers' Coupling Levels (Increasing Strength):**
> 1. Content → 2. Common → 3. Control → 4. Stamp → 5. Data → 6. Message
>
> Aim for **Message coupling** (only data parameters, no shared state).

### 3.1.2 Cohesion — Logical Step Derivation

Cohesion within a module $M$ is the degree to which its responsibilities relate:

$$ \text{Cohesion}(M) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k \cdot \text{rel}(R_k, R_{\text{primary}})}{n} $$

Where:
- $n$ = number of responsibilities of $M$
- $R_k$ = the $k$-th responsibility
- $\text{rel}(R_k, R_{\text{primary}})$ = relevance of $R_k$ to the primary purpose

**From Worst to Best (Yourdon & Constantine):**
1. Coincidental → 2. Logical → 3. Temporal → 4. Procedural → 5. Communicational → 6. Sequential → 7. **Functional**

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

1. List all methods of the class/module.
2. Identify the primary responsibility.
3. Score each method's relevance to the primary responsibility on a scale [0, 1].
4. Average the scores.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Polymorphic Call Cost

In C++, a virtual call through a base pointer involves the following **memory access chain**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Load vptr from object} &\Rightarrow p = *\text{obj} \\
\text{Step 2: Add offset to find vfn} &\Rightarrow \text{addr} = p + \text{offset}(\text{method}) \\
\text{Step 3: Load function pointer} &\Rightarrow \text{fnPtr} = *\text{addr} \\
\text{Step 4: Invoke the function} &\Rightarrow \text{call fnPtr}(\text{obj}, \text{args})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total cost:**

$$ T_{\text{virtual}} = 4 \cdot T_{\text{mem-access}} + T_{\text{call-overhead}} $$

Compared to a static call:

$$ T_{\text{static}} = T_{\text{call-overhead}} $$

**Hence:**

$$ T_{\text{virtual}} - T_{\text{static}} \approx 4 \cdot T_{\text{mem-access}} $$

This explains why **devirtualization** is a common compiler optimization.

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix: Inheritance vs Composition

| Criterion | Inheritance | Composition |
|-----------|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| Relationship | **is-a** | **has-a** |
| Compile-time binding | ✅ | ✅ |
| Runtime type change | ❌ | ✅ (Strategy pattern) |
| Code reuse of implementation | ✅ | ✅ |
| Tight coupling to base | ✅ | ❌ |
| Polymorphism support | ✅ | ✅ (via interfaces) |
| Unit testability | Moderate | High |
| KTU board recommendation | Use sparingly | **Default choice** |

**The "Favor Composition Over Inheritance" Principle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Design Heuristic:} \quad
&\text{If } D \text{ is conceptually a strict subtype of } B, \text{ use inheritance.} \\
&\text{Otherwise, use composition.} \\
&\text{Test: } \forall x (x \in D) \Rightarrow x \in B ?
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners reward students who explicitly state the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** when justifying inheritance decisions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: Resolving the Diamond Problem

Consider:

```
        Animal
        /    \
       Dog    Cat
        \    /
          Pet
```

**Step 1:** Identify duplicate inheritance — `Pet` inherits `Animal` twice.
**Step 2:** Without resolution, calling `pet.speak()` is ambiguous.
**Step 3 (C++ fix):** Use virtual inheritance.

```cpp
class Animal { public: virtual void speak() = 0; };
class Dog : virtual public Animal { ... };
class Cat : virtual public Animal { ... };
class Pet : public Dog, public Cat { ... };
```

**Step 4:** Verify that `Pet` inherits **only one** sub-object of `Animal`.

$$ \text{sizeof(Pet)} = \text{sizeof(Dog fields)} + \text{sizeof(Cat fields)} + \text{sizeof(Animal fields)} \times 1 $$

> [!NOTE]
> Java sidesteps this entirely by **disallowing multiple class inheritance**. Interfaces provide multiple *type* inheritance without state duplication.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: OOP Design Issue Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Problem in OOP] --> B{Is it is-a relationship?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is LSP satisfied?}
    B -- No --> D{Is it has-a relationship?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Inheritance]
    C -- No --> F[Use Composition]
    D -- Yes --> G[Use Composition]
    D -- No --> H[Use Wrapper / Adapter]
    E --> I{Need dynamic behavior?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Use Virtual / Override]
    I -- No --> K[Use Static Binding]
    F --> L[Composition over Inheritance]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    J --> M{Check Diamond Risk?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Apply Virtual Inheritance or Interfaces]
    M -- No --> O[Proceed]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Validate Encapsulation and Coupling]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Diamond Problem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Animal base class with speak method] --> B[Dog inherits Animal]
    A --> C[Cat inherits Animal]
    B --> D[Pet inherits Dog and Cat]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Ambiguous speak call}
    E --> F[Fix via Virtual Inheritance]
    E --> G[Fix via Interface in Java]
    F --> H[Single Animal subobject in Pet]
    G --> I[Multiple interface types but no state duplication]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Polymorphism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Caller Code] --> B[Base Reference or Pointer]
    B --> C{V-Table Lookup}
    C --> D[Virtual Function Pointer Slot]
    D --> E[Derived Class Method]
    E --> F[Return to Caller]
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Coupling and Cohesion Quality Quadrant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Target["Ideal Zone"]
        T[Low Coupling + High Cohesion]
    end
    subgraph Acceptable["Acceptable"]
```
