- **Atomicity** is the cornerstone — achieved via **hardware instructions** (TSL, CAS) or **OS-level interrupt disabling**.
- **The wait queue (L)** is what enables *blocking*; the negative value of `S` indicates the number of processes waiting.
- **Order of P matters in Producer–Consumer:** `P(empty); P(mutex);` — never reverse. Same for `P(full); P(mutex);` in the consumer.
- **Initialization rule:** For a bounded buffer of size $N$, set `empty = N`, `full = 0`, `mutex = 1`.
- **Dining Philosophers deadlock** arises when all 5 pick the left chopstick simultaneously. Fixes: `room = 4` (counting semaphore) or odd/even pickup ordering.
- **Reader–Writer pattern:** First reader locks writers via `P(wrt)`; last reader unlocks via `V(wrt)`.
- **Deadlock-free ≠ Starvation-free** — important distinction; KTU examiners test it.
- **P is also called:** wait, down, proberen, acquire. **V is also called:** signal, up, verhogen, release.
- **Hardware primitives to remember:** Test-and-Set (TSL), Compare-and-Swap (CAS), Fetch-and-Add, Swap.
- **POSIX API to remember:** `sem_init`, `sem_wait`, `sem_post`, `sem_destroy`, `sem_getvalue` (frequently asked in viva).
- **Real-world use cases:** Database connection pools, thread pools, RTOS task synchronization, Linux kernel `struct semaphore`.
- **Common exam weightage:** Module 4 carries **20–25%** of the KTU ESE paper — the Producer–Consumer question is **almost always** a Part B question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Monitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — MONITORS: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **MONITOR (Hoare, 1974):** A monitor is a high-level synchronization construct that encapsulates **shared data**, the **procedures (methods)** that operate on that data, and the **synchronization primitives** (condition variables) required for coordinating concurrent processes/threads, all bundled into a single abstract data type. A monitor guarantees **mutual exclusion** automatically — at most one process can be active inside the monitor at any given instant.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology (PEBMT746), a monitor is treated as a **software module** whose primary responsibilities are:

1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Implicit locking on entry; only one process executes a monitor procedure at a time.
2. **Condition Synchronization** — Processes may suspend themselves on **condition variables** using `wait(cv)` and awaken others using `signal(cv)` / `broadcast(cv)`.

A monitor is typically declared using the following conceptual syntactic template:

```text
monitor MonitorName {
    // Shared Declarations (permanent variables)
    variable x;

    // Permanent Body — initialization code
    initialization_code { ... }

    // Procedure Declarations
    procedure P1( ... ) { ... }
    procedure P2( ... ) { ... }
}
```

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of a Monitor

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Encapsulation** | Shared data and operations are bundled together (like an ADT). |
| **Mutual Exclusion** | Compiler-enforced; only one process at a time inside the monitor. |
| **Condition Variables** | Used for `wait` and `signal` to suspend/resume processes. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Teller Booth 🏦

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a bank branch with **one single cash counter** (a booth with a glass wall) where only **one customer** is allowed inside at a time. Outside the booth, there is a **digital display board** (condition variable) that shows "Token Number 7 may now enter."
>
> - When you enter the booth, the door **automatically locks** behind you — no one else can come in (mutual exclusion).
> - If you need something (say, the manager's signature), you **wait on a chair inside the booth**, and the manager is **signaled** to come.
> - The customer on the chair `wait`s; the manager `signal`s to resume the process.
> - Other customers outside cannot peek into your transactions (encapsulation).

This is **exactly** what a monitor does for concurrent processes.

## 1.4 Why Monitors Exist — The Problem They Solve

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Semaphores are powerful but error-prone. A single `signal(s)` or `wait(s)` left out, duplicated, or swapped can lead to **deadlock**, **race conditions**, or **starvation**. Monitors were proposed to push synchronization into the **language/runtime**, reducing programmer error.

Before monitors, engineers relied heavily on semaphores, but as Brinch Hansen and Hoare pointed out, the correctness of semaphore-based code is **hard to verify** in large systems. Monitors provide a **structured, modular, and verifiable** synchronization primitive.

## 1.5 Condition Variables — The Heart of Synchronization

A condition variable `cv` is associated with a **boolean condition** that a process wishes to be true. It supports only three primitive operations:

| Operation | Effect |
|---|---|
| `wait(cv)` | Suspends the calling process on `cv` and releases the monitor lock. |
| `signal(cv)` | Resumes exactly one process (if any) waiting on `cv`. |
| `broadcast(cv)` | Resumes ALL processes waiting on `cv`. |

> A condition variable **does not store a value** like a semaphore; it is simply a queue of suspended processes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics — How a Monitor Works

A monitor can be visualized as a controlled room with three internal queues:

1. **Entry Queue (Mutex Queue)** — processes waiting to *enter* the monitor.
2. **Condition Queues** — one per condition variable, holding processes that executed `wait`.
3. **Urgent Queue** — present in **Hoare semantics** only, holding the signaler that just woke another process.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. A process calls a monitor procedure `P1`.
2. If the monitor is **busy**, the process joins the **Entry Queue** (blocked).
3. If the monitor is **free**, the process enters and acquires the implicit lock.
4. Inside the monitor, if the process finds a required precondition is **false**, it executes `wait(cv)`. This:
   - **Releases** the monitor lock.
   - **Blocks** the process on `cv`'s condition queue.
5. Another process may enter the monitor and execute `signal(cv)`. This transfers control to the waiting process.
6. The waiting process resumes and reacquires the monitor lock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The mutual exclusion is *implicit* and *compiler-enforced*. The programmer never explicitly writes `wait(mutex)` or `signal(mutex)`.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Monitor Variants (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

| Variant | Signal Semantics | Resumption Rule | Originator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hoare Monitor** | Signal causes immediate transfer of control. Signaler **suspends**; waiter **runs immediately**. | Waiter runs with original preconditions guaranteed true. | C. A. R. Hoare (1974) |
| **Mesa Monitor** | Signal merely **marks** the waiter as runnable. Signaler **continues**. | Waiter runs only after signaler exits or blocks. Preconditions may no longer hold → must re-check (while-loop). | Xerox PARC (Lampson & Redell, 1980) |
| **Hansen Monitor (Brinch Hansen)** | Signal is the **last statement** of a monitor procedure. Signaler exits; waiter runs. | Waiter runs after signaler exits. Re-check of conditions still recommended. | Per Brinch Hansen (1975) |

### 2.2.1 Hoare Monitor — The Original

In Hoare's original formulation, if `P` executes `signal(cv)` and `Q` is waiting on `cv`:

- `Q` immediately resumes execution *inside the monitor*.
- `P` is suspended on a special **signal queue** (urgent queue).
- `Q` will run until it exits or executes `wait`.
- The guarantee: the condition that caused `Q` to wait is **still true** when `Q` runs.

### 2.2.2 Mesa Monitor — The Practical One

In Mesa semantics:

- `signal(cv)` only places `Q` on the **ready queue** — `P` keeps running.
- The condition `Q` was waiting for **may be false again** by the time `Q` actually runs.
- Therefore, **all waits must be inside a `while` loop**, not an `if` statement:

```text
while (!condition)
    wait(cv);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The `while`-loop pattern is the *defining* feature of Mesa monitors. Java's `notify()` follows Mesa semantics. Hoare semantics can be simulated on top of Mesa.

### 2.2.3 Hansen Monitor — The Safe One

In Hansen's design, `signal` is restricted to be the **last statement** of a monitor procedure, ensuring the signaler exits the monitor immediately. This eliminates the urgent queue and avoids the "nested signal" problem.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Construct | Mathematical / Logical Form | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mutual Exclusion Invariant** | $\forall t, \;\; \vert \{P \in \text{active inside monitor} \} \vert \le 1$ | Guarantees safety property |
| **Condition Variable Definition** | $cv_i \subseteq \text{WaitingProcesses}$ | A set, not an integer |
| **Hoare Signal Postcondition** | $P_{waiter}$ resumes $\Rightarrow$ condition $C$ is true | Strong guarantee |
| **Mesa Signal Postcondition** | $P_{waiter}$ eventually scheduled $\Rightarrow$ must recheck $C$ | Weak guarantee |
| **Brinch Hansen Postcondition** | $\text{signal}(cv) \text{ is last stmt} \Rightarrow P_{signaler}$ exits monitor | Structural guarantee |
| **N-Process Mutual Exclusion Counter** | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{in\_monitor}(P_i) \le 1$ | Invariant |
| **Throughput Bound (Hoare)** | $T \le \dfrac{1}{\text{cs\_time} + \text{context\_switch\_overhead}}$ | Performance bound |
| **Condition Variable Invariant** | $\vert cv_i \vert + \text{active} \le N$ | Total processes bounded |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Monitors (or their object-oriented descendants) underpin **virtually every modern language and OS**:

- **Java** — every `synchronized` method + `Object.wait()/notify()` is a Mesa-style monitor.
- **C#** — `lock` + `Monitor.Wait/Pulse` is a Hoare-flavoured monitor.
- **Python** — `threading.Condition` and `with`-statement locks mimic monitor semantics.
- **Ada** — protected objects are formal monitors in the language standard.
- **Pthreads** — `pthread_mutex_lock` + condition variables can implement monitors manually.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 evaluation, students are expected to recognize that **Java's `synchronized` + `wait/notify` ≡ Mesa Monitor**, and **Ada's `protected` ≡ Hoare Monitor** (with requeue on entry).

## 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages Summary

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Mutual exclusion is automatic | Requires language support (or runtime) |
| Less error-prone than semaphores | Nesting monitors / signal-in-other-CV not allowed |
| Encapsulation promotes modularity | Priority / scheduling issues still possible |
| Compiler-verifiable correctness | Semantics differ (Hoare vs Mesa) → portability issues |
| Clear condition-variable abstraction | Single condition variable per waiter may be too coarse |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations, Proofs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of Mutual Exclusion Invariant

**Claim:** At any instant $t$, the number of processes executing inside the monitor body is $\le 1$.

**Proof (Invariant Method):**

Let:
- $E$ = event "a process enters the monitor"
- $X$ = event "a process exits the monitor"
- $A(t)$ = number of processes active inside the monitor at instant $t$

**Step 1.** Define the invariant:

$$I(t) : A(t) \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Step 2.** Base case: $A(0) = 0$. ✓ Trivially holds.

**Step 3.** Induction step — assume $I(t)$ holds at instant $t$. Consider the next event:

**Case (a) — Event $E$ (entry):** A process $P_i$ attempts to enter. The monitor checks a hidden lock $L$. The entry is permitted **only if** $L = \text{unlocked}$:

$$
L = \text{locked} \Rightarrow E \text{ is rejected (process blocked in entry queue)}
$$

$$
L = \text{unlocked} \Rightarrow A(t+1) = A(t) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1
$$

**Case (b) — Event $X$ (exit):** A process $P_i$ leaves the monitor. $A$ decreases by 1:

$$
A(t+1) = A(t) - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0
$$

**Case (c) — Event `wait(cv)`:** Process voluntarily blocks. $A$ decreases by 1, lock released:

$$
A(t+1) = A(t) - 1
$$

**Case (d) — Event `signal(cv)` (Hoare):** Signaler suspends; waiter becomes active. $A$ stays at 1:

$$
A(t+1) = A(t) - 1 + 1 = A(t) = 1
$$

**Case (e) — Event `signal(cv)` (Mesa):** Signaler continues; waiter only marked ready. $A$ stays at 1.

**Step 4.** In all cases, $A(t+1) \le 1$. Therefore, by induction:

$$
\forall t, \quad A(t) \le 1 \qquad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.2 Proof of Absence of Lost Wakeups (Hoare Semantics)

**Claim:** If process $P_j$ is in condition queue of $cv$ and process $P_i$ executes `signal(cv)`, then $P_j$ **will** resume in the monitor while the condition is still true.

**Proof:**

Let $C$ be the boolean condition associated with $cv$.

1. $P_j$ executed `wait(cv)` because $C$ was false at that moment:

$$C \text{ was false at time } t_{\text{wait}(P_j)}$$

2. $P_i$ executes `signal(cv)` at time $t_{\text{signal}} > t_{\text{wait}}$. For $P_i$ to legitimately signal, it must have established $C$:

$$C \text{ is true at time } t_{\text{signal}}$$

3. In Hoare semantics, control is **immediately transferred** to $P_j$. No other process executes inside the monitor between $t_{\text{signal}}$ and $P_j$'s resumption.

4. Therefore, $C$ remains true when $P_j$ resumes. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proof **fails** for Mesa semantics — between `signal` and $P_j$'s actual execution, the signaler and possibly other processes may run and invalidate $C$. Hence the `while`-loop requirement.

## 3.3 Conversion Between Monitor Variants — Analytical Derivation

We derive the equivalence of `if`-wait (Hoare) and `while`-wait (Mesa).

### 3.3.1 Mesa `while`-wait pattern

```text
procedure acquire() {
    while (!condition) {
        wait(cv);
    }
    // Proceed: condition guaranteed true
}
```

**Step-by-step correctness argument:**

Step 1. Initial entry: condition checked. If false, `wait` blocks.
Step 2. After `signal`, process is *marked* runnable, not necessarily running.
Step 3. When process actually runs, `while` re-evaluates condition.
Step 4. If another process invalidated the condition, it waits again. No race.

**Formal invariant maintained:**

$$
I_{\text{Mesa}} : (\text{process exits } \texttt{while}) \Rightarrow C = \text{true}
$$

### 3.3.2 Hoare `if`-wait pattern

```text
procedure acquire() {
    if (!condition) {
        wait(cv);
    }
    // Proceed: condition guaranteed true (by Hoare guarantee)
}
```

**Formal invariant maintained:**

$$
I_{\text{Hoare}} : (\text{process resumes after } \texttt{wait}) \Rightarrow C = \text{true}
$$

### 3.3.3 Equivalence Theorem

> **Theorem:** A Hoare monitor with `if`-wait is **observationally equivalent** to a Mesa monitor with `while`-wait, provided that exactly one signaler is sufficient to make the condition true.

**Proof Sketch:** In Hoare, the condition is true upon waiter's immediate resume. In Mesa, the `while` loop guarantees the condition is true upon exit from the loop. In both cases, the post-condition is identical:

$$
\{C = \text{true}\} \;\; \text{critical\_section} \;\; \{C \text{ may be false}\}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Dining Philosophers Solution Using Monitors

The classic problem with semaphores is deadlock. Monitors give an elegant deadlock-free solution.

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

- $N = 5$ philosophers sit around a table.
- Each philosopher alternates between **thinking** and **eating**.
- Eating requires acquiring **both** adjacent chopsticks (shared resources).

### 3.4.2 State Definition

For each philosopher $i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$:

$$
\text{state}[i] \in \{\text{THINKING}, \text{HUNGRY}, \text{EATING}\}
$$

### 3.4.3 Monitor Code (Hoare-style)

```text
monitor DiningPhilosophers {
    enum State { THINKING, HUNGRY, EATING };
    State state[5];
    condition self[5];

    void pickup(int i) {
        state[i] = HUNGRY;
        test(i);                   // try to eat
        if (state[i] != EATING) {
            wait(self[i]);         // block until neighbors finish
        }
    }

    void putdown(int i) {
        state[i] = THINKING;
        test(LEFT);                // wake up left neighbor
        test(RIGHT);               // wake up right neighbor
    }

    void test(int i) {
        if (state[i] == HUNGRY &&
            state[LEFT]  != EATING &&
            state[RIGHT] != EATING) {
            state[i] = EATING;
            signal(self[i]);
        }
    }

    initialization_code() {
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) state[i] = THINKING;
    }
}
```

### 3.4.4 Correctness Proof

**Mutual Exclusion:** No two neighbors can be EATING simultaneously, because `test(i)` requires $\text{state}[(i-1)\bmod 5] \neq \text{EATING}$ and $\text{state}[(i+1)\bmod 5] \neq \text{EATING}$. ✓

**Freedom from Deadlock:** When a philosopher is HUNGRY but blocked, the only way they are unblocked is by a `signal` from `putdown`, which checks the condition before signaling. Therefore progress is guaranteed. ✓

**Freedom from Starvation:** By induction on ring traversal, a HUNGRY philosopher will eventually be signalled when both neighbors `putdown`. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Semaphores vs. Monitors

| Property | Semaphores | Monitors |
|---|---|---|
| **Primitive Type** | Integer + atomic ops | ADT with encapsulated data |
| **Mutual Exclusion** | Programmer-managed (mutex semaphore) | Compiler/runtime-enforced |
| **Error-Prone?** | Yes (forget signal, deadlock) | Less so |
| **Distributed?** | Easy (no shared memory) | Harder (requires shared memory) |
| **Linguistic Support** | None (OS library) | Required (Java, Ada, C#) |
| **Composability** | Brittle (interleaved signaling) | Modular (one monitor per resource) |
| **Verification** | Difficult | Easier via invariants |
| **Performance** | Slightly faster (no runtime check) | Negligible overhead |

## 3.6 Priority and Scheduling Issues (Frequently Tested in KTU)

When multiple processes are waiting, two questions arise:

1. **Inside the Entry Queue:** Who gets in next when the monitor becomes free?
2. **Inside the Condition Queue:** Who gets signalled when `signal(cv)` is called?

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Bug — "Lost Signal":** If `signal(cv)` is executed when **no process is waiting on `cv`**, the signal is **lost** in both Hoare and Mesa. Semantically, `signal` is **not** a counter increment. Students often confuse this with semaphores.

> **Common Bug — "Nested Signal":** If a process executes `signal(cv1)` while already signalled on `cv2` and reactivated, the urgent queue may overflow. Hansen's solution restricts `signal` to be the last statement of a procedure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Monitor Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EntryQ["ENTRY QUEUE (Mutex Lock)"]
        P1["Process P1 waiting"]
        P2["Process P2 waiting"]
        P3["Process P3 waiting"]
    end

    subgraph MonitorBody["MONITOR BODY (Mutual Exclusion Enforced)"]
        direction TB
        SharedData["SHARED DATA\n(variables, state)"]
        ProcA["Procedure A"]
        ProcB["Procedure B"]
        CondVar1["Condition Variable cv1"]
        CondVar2["Condition Variable cv2"]
    end

    subgraph CondQ1["CONDITION QUEUE for cv1"]
        W1["Waiting Process W1"]
        W2["Waiting Process W2"]
    end

    subgraph CondQ2["CONDITION QUEUE for cv2"]
        W3["Waiting Process W3"]
    end

    EntryQ -->|"P_i acquires lock"| MonitorBody
    MonitorBody -->|"wait(cv1) releases lock"| CondQ1
    MonitorBody -->|"wait(cv2) releases lock"| CondQ2
    CondQ1 -->|"signal(cv1) re-enters"| MonitorBody
    CondQ2 -->|"signal(cv2) re-enters"| MonitorBody
    SharedData -.-> ProcA
    SharedData -.-> ProcB
    CondVar1 -.-> ProcA
    CondVar2 -.-> ProcB
```

## 4.2 Hoare vs Mesa Signal — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant P as Process P (Signaler)
    participant Q as Process Q (Waiter)
    participant MON as Monitor Lock

    Note over P,Q: HOARE MONITOR
    P->>MON: Acquire Lock
    P->>Q: signal(cv) — IMMEDIATE transfer
    Note right of P: P is SUSPENDED on urgent queue
    Q->>MON: Acquires lock, runs immediately
    Q->>Q: Condition C is GUARANTEED true
    Q-->>P: Releases lock to P
    P->>MON: Resumes execution

    Note over P,Q: MESA MONITOR
    P->>MON: Acquire Lock
    P->>Q: signal(cv) — DEFERRRED, just marks runnable
    Note right of P: P CONTINUES running
    P->>MON: Exits monitor
    Q->>MON: Eventually acquires lock
    Q->>Q: Must RE-CHECK condition with while-loop
```

## 4.3 Dining Philosophers — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> THINKING
    THINKING --> HUNGRY: pickup i
    HUNGRY --> EATING: both neighbors NOT eating
    HUNGRY --> HUNGRY: wait self i
    EATING --> THINKING: putdown i
    THINKING --> [*]

    note right of HUNGRY
        If condition fails,
        process blocks on
        condition variable self i
    end note

    note right of EATING
        test LEFT and RIGHT
        neighbors before exit
    end note
```

## 4.4 Resource Allocation Module — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        C1["Client Process C1"]
        C2["Client Process C2"]
        C3["Client Process C3"]
    end

    subgraph MonLayer["MONITOR LAYER"]
        Acquire["Procedure acquire"]
        Release["Procedure release"]
        CV["Condition Variable"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["SHARED RESOURCE LAYER"]
        R1["Resource 1"]
        R2["Resource 2"]
        State["Allocation State Table"]
    end

    C1 -->|call| Acquire
    C2 -->|call| Acquire
    C3 -->|call| Acquire
    Acquire -->|mutates| State
    Acquire -->|blocks on| CV
    Release -->|signals| CV
    Release -->|mutates| State
    State --> R1
    State --> R2
```

## 4.5 KTU Module Flowchart — How a Process Interacts with a Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Process invokes monitor procedure"] --> B{"Is monitor\nfree?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Enter monitor\nlock acquired implicitly"]
    B -- "No" --> D["Join Entry Queue\nBLOCKED"]
    D --> B

    C --> E{"Precondition\nsatisfied?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Execute critical section"]
    E -- "No" --> G["wait cv\nrelease lock\nblock on cv"]
    G --> H["Suspended on condition queue"]
    H --> I{"Another process\nsignals cv?"}
    I -- "No" --> H
    I -- "Yes Hoare" --> J["Resume IMMEDIATELY\nlock reacquired"]
    I -- "Yes Mesa" --> K["Marked runnable\nresumes later\nlock reacquired"]
    J --> F
    K --> E
    F --> L["Exit monitor\nlock released"]
    L --> M["Check Entry Queue\nadmit next process"]
    M --> A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Define a Monitor. List its key features. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **monitor** is a high-level synchronization construct that combines shared data, the procedures that operate on it, and the synchronization mechanism into a single module. Mutual exclusion is automatically enforced — at most one process executes within the monitor at any time.

**Key features (3, for 3 marks):**

1. **Encapsulation** — Data and procedures are bundled into a single ADT.
2. **Mutual Exclusion** — Implicit; only one process active inside at a time.
3. **Condition Variables** — Provide `wait()` and `signal()` for blocking/resuming processes on logical conditions.

---

### Q2. Compare Hoare Monitor and Mesa Monitor. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hoare Monitor | Mesa Monitor |
|---|---|---|
| Signal handling | Immediate transfer of CPU to waiter | Signal just marks waiter as runnable |
| Signaler behaviour | Suspended (urgent queue) | Continues execution |
| Condition check | Single `if` suffices | Must use `while` loop |
| Used in | Theoretical/Hoare's original | Java, real-world languages |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the structure of a Monitor with a neat diagram. Discuss condition variables and their operations in detail. How does a Monitor differ from a Semaphore?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Structure (3 marks):**

A monitor is a software module consisting of:

- **Declaration of permanent variables** (shared state).
- **Procedure declarations** (operations on the state).
- **Condition variable declarations** (`condition cv1, cv2, ...`).
- **Initialization code** (executed once at monitor creation).

**Step 2 — Condition Variables and Operations (3 marks):**

| Operation | Effect |
|---|---|
| `wait(cv)` | Suspends caller on `cv`; releases monitor lock. |
| `signal(cv)` | Resumes one waiting process (if any) on `cv`. |
| `broadcast(cv)` | Resumes all waiting processes on `cv`. |

Condition variables are **not** counters (unlike semaphores). They store only a queue of suspended processes. The condition being waited upon is a programmer-defined boolean.

**Step 3 — Monitor vs Semaphore (1 mark):**

- Semaphores are low-level, error-prone, and have no data encapsulation. Monitors provide structured, modular, automatically mutually exclusive access with explicit condition variables.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Solve the Producer–Consumer (Bounded Buffer) problem using a Monitor. Provide the code and explain correctness.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Problem:** Producer threads insert items into a shared bounded buffer; consumer threads remove them. Buffer has capacity $N$. Need mutual exclusion and two condition variables: `notfull` and `notempty`.

**Monitor Code:**

```text
monitor BoundedBuffer {
    int buffer[N];
    int count = 0, in = 0, out = 0;

    condition notfull, notempty;

    void produce(item x) {
        if (count == N) wait(notfull);
        buffer[in] = x;
        in = (in + 1) mod N;
        count = count + 1;
        signal(notempty);
    }

    void consume(item& x) {
        if (count == 0) wait(notempty);
        x = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) mod N;
        count = count - 1;
        signal(notfull);
    }

    initialization_code() {
        count = 0; in = 0; out = 0;
    }
}
```

**Correctness Argument (key points for 7 marks):**

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** Implicit — only one of `produce`/`consume` runs at a time. ✓
2. **Buffer Bound:** `produce` blocks if `count == N` (waits on `notfull`). ✓
3. **No Empty Consume:** `consume` blocks if `count == 0` (waits on `notempty`). ✓
4. **No Lost Signal:** `signal` is called only when the corresponding condition becomes true. ✓
5. **Liveness:** A producer that fills the buffer will eventually be unblocked by a consumer's `signal(notfull)`. ✓
6. **Note on Mesa semantics:** In practice (e.g., Java), `if` should be replaced by `while` to handle spurious wakeups and Mesa-style re-checking.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain Hoare's Monitor with the Dining Philosophers problem. State and prove the absence of deadlock.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hoare Monitor Recap (2 marks):**

In Hoare's monitor, `signal(cv)` causes **immediate transfer of control** to one waiting process. The signaling process is suspended on the *urgent queue*. The condition that caused the waiter to block is guaranteed to still be true when the waiter resumes.

**Step 2 — Dining Philosophers Monitor Code (3 marks):**

(See full code in Section 3.4.3 of these notes.)

Key points:
- `pickup(i)` marks philosopher $i$ as `HUNGRY`, calls `test(i)`, and waits if not EATING.
- `test(i)` checks both neighbors; if both are not EATING, sets EATING and signals.
- `putdown(i)` sets THINKING and tests both neighbors to wake them.

**Step 3 — Proof of Deadlock-Freedom (2 marks):**

**Claim:** The system cannot deadlock.

**Proof by Contradiction:**

Assume a deadlock state exists. Then every HUNGRY philosopher $P_i$ is blocked on `self[i]`. For $P_i$ to be blocked, `test(i)` must have failed — meaning at least one of $P_{i-1}$ or $P_{i+1}$ is EATING.

Consider the circular sequence of philosophers. If all HUNGRY philosophers are blocked, then every adjacent pair must have one EATING philosopher. By the pigeonhole principle on the cycle of 5, this is impossible — we need at least $\lceil 5/2 \rceil = 3$ EATING philosophers, but each EATING philosopher needs both neighbors to be non-EATING, so at most 2 can be EATING. **Contradiction.** $\blacksquare$

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the Mesa Monitor and the Brinch Hansen Monitor. How is the lost-wakeup problem avoided in each?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mesa Monitor (3 marks):**

In Mesa-style monitors, `signal` does **not** immediately transfer control. The signaler continues, and the waiter is merely placed on the ready queue. Because the condition may become false again before the waiter actually runs, **all `wait` calls must be inside a `while` loop**:

```text
while (!condition)
    wait(cv);
```

**Lost-wakeup avoidance in Mesa:** The `while`-loop forces the awakened process to **re-verify** the condition. If it has become false (e.g., another waiter snatched the resource), the process waits again. This handles the deferred-signal race condition.

**Step 2 — Brinch Hansen Monitor (3 marks):**

In Hansen's design, `signal` is restricted to be the **last statement** of a monitor procedure. This guarantees that the signaler will exit the monitor immediately after signaling, releasing the lock for the waiter.

**Lost-wakeup avoidance in Hansen:** The structural rule (signal must be last) means no race window exists between `signal` and monitor exit. The waiter can run with the condition still true because no other process can enter the monitor and invalidate it in between.

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Mesa | Hansen |
|---|---|---|
| Signal position | Anywhere | Must be last statement |
| Condition re-check | Required (`while`) | Recommended (defensive) |
| Urgent queue | Not needed | Not needed |
| Lost wakeups | Avoided by `while` loop | Avoided by structural rule |
| Used in | Java, C# (pulse) | Original Concurrent Pascal |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing monitor with class/object** — A monitor is *not* a Java class. It is a synchronization construct with implicit mutual exclusion.
> 2. **Treating `signal` like `V(semaphore)`** — `signal` does NOT increment a counter. It is a **no-op** if no process is waiting.
> 3. **Using `if` instead of `while` in Mesa code** — Always lose 1–2 marks in Java-flavored questions if you use `if`.
> 4. **Forgetting `broadcast` vs `signal`** — `signal` wakes **one**; `broadcast` wakes **all**. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 5. **Not stating the invariant explicitly** in correctness proofs — KTU examiners expect invariants like $A(t) \le 1$ to be written, not implied.
> 6. **Confusing the order of `wait` and `signal`** in producer/consumer — always `wait` on the condition that **prevents you from proceeding**, and `signal` on the condition that **allows others to proceed**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Monitor = ADT + Mutual Exclusion + Condition Variables** (the three pillars).
- [x] Mutual exclusion is **implicit** and **compiler-enforced** — not programmer-managed.
- [x] **Condition variables** are **queues of processes**, NOT counters.
- [x] Three classical variants: **Hoare** (immediate transfer), **Mesa** (deferred; use `while`), **Hansen** (signal-last).
- [x] **Hoare guarantee:** Condition is true when waiter resumes → `if` suffices.
- [x] **Mesa reality:** Condition may be false when waiter resumes → MUST use `while`.
- [x] **Java `synchronized` + `wait/notify` ≡ Mesa Monitor.**
- [x] **Ada `protected` ≡ Hoare Monitor** (with requeue).
- [x] **Signal is a no-op** if no process is waiting — *lost wakeup* risk.
- [x] **Dining Philosophers** can be solved **deadlock-free** with a monitor using `test(i)` pattern.
- [x] **Producer-Consumer (Bounded Buffer)** needs **two** condition variables: `notfull` and `notempty`.
- [x] **Priority issues:** The signaling process may have higher or lower priority than the waiter — Hoare handles this with an urgent queue; Mesa does not.
- [x] **Verification:** Monitor code is easier to verify via **invariants** than semaphore code.
- [x] **Modularity:** One monitor per shared resource is the recommended design pattern.
- [x] **Common bug:** Nested monitors / nested signals — avoid by restricting `signal` to the end of a procedure (Hansen rule).

### 🧠 Memory Aid — "**MICE**"
> **M**utual exclusion is implicit
> **I**nvariant: at most 1 inside
> **C**ondition variables for `wait`/`signal`
> **E**ncapsulation of data and code

### 📊 Comparison Mnemonics

- **Hoare** = **H**alt-and-**H**andoff (immediate)
- **Mesa** = **M**ay-**M**iss (re-check needed)
- **Hansen** = **H**and-off at **H**ouse-exit (signal last)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Solve X with a Monitor", always include: (1) the state declarations, (2) the two condition variables, (3) the monitor procedures with proper `wait`/`signal` ordering, and (4) a short correctness argument citing mutual exclusion and liveness. This 4-part structure typically fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Passing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Message Passing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Message Passing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Message Passing** is a fundamental communication paradigm in advanced computer programming wherein independent computational entities (objects, processes, threads, or distributed nodes) exchange data and synchronize their execution by explicitly sending and receiving structured units of information called *messages*, rather than by sharing mutable state in a common memory space.

In the **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** context (Smalltalk, Objective-C, Java, C++), message passing is the mechanism by which one object invokes the behavior of another — the receiver object interprets the message, optionally with arguments, and dispatches to the appropriate method (dynamic binding).

In the **Parallel / Distributed Computing** context (MPI, PVM, Erlang, Go channels), message passing is the explicit *send* and *receive* primitive through which cooperating processes coordinate work across address spaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> "Message passing is a technique in which a sender entity transmits a message (containing data and/or a request) to a receiver entity, which then interprets and acts upon it. It is the cornerstone of both object interaction in OOP and inter-process communication in distributed systems."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🏤 **Real-World Analogy — The Postal Service**
> Imagine two offices in different cities that need to collaborate. They cannot walk into each other's rooms and rearrange papers (that would be *shared memory*). Instead, they write letters, place them in envelopes addressed to a specific recipient, and send them through the postal service. The receiver opens the envelope, reads the message, and may send a reply.
>
> - **Sender** = the object/process that posts the message
> - **Receiver** = the destination object/process
> - **Message** = the envelope (header: address, content: data)
> - **Channel / Mailbox** = the postal system / transport layer
> - **No shared state** = no office can touch the other's papers directly
>
> This perfectly mirrors message passing: there is **no shared mutable memory**; all interaction is mediated through well-defined messages.

### 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standard in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for message passing systems:

- **Message Latency** (**μs to ms**) — time taken for a single message to travel from sender to receiver.
- **Bandwidth** (**MB/s or GB/s**) — rate of data transfer between processes.
- **Message Overhead** (typically **microseconds**) — fixed cost per message, independent of size.
- **MPI Rank** — a unique non-negative integer identifier for each process (e.g., **0 to N−1**).
- **Tag** — an integer used to distinguish message types at the receiver.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Paradigms of Message Passing

#### A. Message Passing in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)

In OOP languages such as **Smalltalk, Objective-C, Ruby, Python,** and to a large extent **Java/C++**, objects interact *only* by sending messages. A message has three parts:

1. **Receiver** — the object that will handle the message.
2. **Selector (Method Name)** — the operation being requested.
3. **Arguments** — zero or more parameters supplying data.

**Operational flow (Why and How):**

- **Step 1 — Sender composes a message:** The client code writes `receiver.messageName(arg1, arg2)`. The runtime packages this into a message envelope.
- **Step 2 — Dispatch:** The runtime of the receiver examines the object's class (and its inheritance chain / method table) to locate a method matching the selector.
- **Step 3 — Dynamic Binding (Why this matters):** The *actual* method executed depends on the *runtime type* of the receiver, not the static type of the variable. This is the heart of polymorphism.
- **Step 4 — Method execution:** The matched method runs in the receiver's context (`self` / `this`), with access to the receiver's private state.
- **Step 5 — Optional return value:** A result (object) is sent back as a "reply message" to the original sender.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why message passing instead of direct function calls?**
> - **Encapsulation:** The receiver's internal state is never exposed; only the public protocol (set of messages it understands) is accessible.
> - **Decoupling:** Sender knows *what* to ask, not *how* it's done.
> - **Dynamic dispatch:** Enables polymorphism and late binding.

#### B. Message Passing in Parallel / Distributed Computing (MPI Model)

The dominant standard is the **Message Passing Interface (MPI)**. The two fundamental operations are:

- **Point-to-Point Communication** — between a specific sender and a specific receiver.
- **Collective Communication** — involving a group of processes (broadcast, scatter, gather, reduce, barrier).

The four basic blocking modes (Karniadakis & Kirby formulation):

| Mode | Send Call | Receive Call | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous send** | `MPI_Ssend` | `MPI_Recv` | Send completes only when receive has started |
| **Buffered send** | `MPI_Bsend` | `MPI_Recv` | Send completes once message is copied to user buffer |
| **Ready send** | `MPI_Rsend` | `MPI_Recv` | Send assumes receive has already been posted |
| **Standard send** | `MPI_Send` | `MPI_Recv` | Implementation-defined; non-local |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated KTU 2024 Scheme formula and notation table for Message Passing. All symbols use clean LaTeX-safe delimiters.

| Concept | Notation / Formula | Description | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Message Latency | $L$ | Time for one zero-length message | **μs** |
| Per-byte cost | $t_b$ | Time to transmit one byte | **ns/byte** |
| Total message time | $T = L + \frac{n}{B}$ | Time to send a message of $n$ bytes over bandwidth $B$ | **seconds** |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | Data rate | **MB/s** |
| Number of processes | $N$ | Total cooperating processes | **integer ≥ 1** |
| MPI Rank | $r \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$ | Unique process identifier | **integer** |
| Message Tag | $\text{tag} \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Used to classify messages | **integer** |
| Blocking receive condition | $\text{recv}(\text{source}, \text{tag})$ | Matches by source rank & tag | — |
| Amdahl's Speedup Limit | $S = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}$ | Maximum parallel speedup with fraction $p$ parallelizable | **scalar** |
| Efficiency | $E = \frac{S}{N}$ | Parallel efficiency | **0 ≤ E ≤ 1** |
| Communication-to-Computation Ratio | $CCR = \frac{T_{comm}}{T_{comp}}$ | Indicates communication intensity | **scalar** |

> **Note on pipes:** Markdown tables break if `|` is used inside cells. All absolute-value / set-membership bars above are rendered via the `\vert` and `\in` math commands so the table parses correctly.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HPC and Scientific Computing** — Climate models, computational fluid dynamics (CFD), molecular dynamics (GROMACS, LAMMPS) all use MPI to scale across thousands of CPU cores.
- **Microservices Architecture** — REST/gRPC message exchanges between services.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems** — AUTOSAR, ROS (Robot Operating System) use message passing between nodes.
- **Telecommunications** — Signaling System No. 7 (SS7) is built on message passing.
- **Operating Systems** — Microkernel architectures (QNX, seL4) rely entirely on IPC message passing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Message Transmission Time (Hockney Model)

The classical *Hockney* communication model treats a message transfer as a startup latency plus a transport time proportional to data size. This derivation is a **high-yield KTU problem**.

**Given:**

- $L$ = network latency (seconds), the time to initiate and traverse the network for one message.
- $n$ = message length in **bytes**.
- $B$ = available bandwidth in **bytes/second**.
- $t_b = \dfrac{1}{B}$ = per-byte transfer cost in **seconds/byte**.

**To derive:** the total time $T$ to deliver a message of length $n$.

**Step 1 — Identify the two additive components.**

A message transfer consists of a *fixed* cost (latency, independent of size) and a *variable* cost (proportional to size):

$$
T(n) = T_{\text{fixed}} + T_{\text{variable}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express each component.**

The fixed cost is the latency $L$ (one-time handshake overhead):

$$
T_{\text{fixed}} = L
$$

The variable cost is size divided by bandwidth (or equivalently, size times per-byte cost):

$$
T_{\text{variable}} = n \cdot t_b = \frac{n}{B}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$
\boxed{\,T(n) = L + \frac{n}{B} = L + n \cdot t_b\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification of limits.**

- When $n = 0$ (zero-length ping-pong): $T(0) = L$. ✔
- When $n \to \infty$: $T \approx n/B$, dominated by bandwidth. ✔
- Units: $L$ is in seconds, $n/B$ is bytes ÷ (bytes/s) = seconds. ✔ (Dimensional consistency holds.)

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style).**

Suppose $L = 2 \ \mu s = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ s, $B = 1 \ \text{GB/s} = 10^9$ bytes/s, and $n = 4 \ \text{KB} = 4096$ bytes.

$$
T = 2 \times 10^{-6} + \frac{4096}{10^9}
$$

$$
T = 2 \times 10^{-6} + 4.096 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T = 6.096 \ \mu s\,}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Message Passing

| Property | Synchronous (Blocking) | Asynchronous (Non-blocking) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sender blocking** | Yes, until receive completes | No, returns immediately with request handle |
| **Receive blocking** | Yes, until message arrives | No, returns immediately; user must later test (`MPI_Wait`/`MPI_Test`) |
| **Buffer requirement** | None (or system-managed) | User or system buffer required |
| **Latency hiding** | Poor | Excellent (overlap compute and communication) |
| **Deadlock risk** | Higher (e.g., cyclic dependencies) | Lower, but requires care with waits |
| **API examples (MPI)** | `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv` | `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv` |
| **Throughput** | Lower for fine-grained messages | Higher when many independent messages are issued |
| **Complexity for programmer** | Lower | Higher |

### 3.3 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to a Message-Passing Program

Amdahl's law bounds the speedup $S$ achievable by parallelizing a fraction $p$ of a program over $N$ processors. KTU frequently tests this in HPC-related questions.

**Step 1 — Define fractions.**

Let the serial portion of execution be a fraction $(1-p)$ and the parallel portion be a fraction $p$, with $0 \le p \le 1$.

**Step 2 — Time on a single processor.**

$$
T_1 = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}} = (1-p) \cdot T + p \cdot T = T
$$

(Normalize so $T_1 = 1$.)

**Step 3 — Time on $N$ processors.**

The serial part is unaffected, and the parallel part divides by $N$:

$$
T_N = (1-p) \cdot 1 + \frac{p \cdot 1}{N} = (1-p) + \frac{p}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup.**

$$
\boxed{\,S(N) = \frac{T_1}{T_N} = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Limit as $N \to \infty$.**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with infinite processors, the speedup is bounded by the inverse of the serial fraction. If $p = 0.95$, then $S_{\max} = 1/0.05 = 20\times$. This is why message-passing implementations must minimize *all* serial regions (including communication setup).

**Step 6 — Worked KTU numerical.**

> If 90% of a program is parallelizable and 10% is serial, find the speedup on 8 processors.

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{(1-0.90) + \frac{0.90}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.1125} = \frac{1}{0.2125}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S(8) \approx 4.706\,}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Point-to-Point Message Passing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process 0 Sender] -->|MPI_Send buffer=msg tag=1| B[MPI Runtime Transport Layer]
    B -->|encapsulation + routing| C[Network / Shared Memory Channel]
    C -->|decode + match tag| D[Process 1 Receiver Mailbox]
    D -->|MPI_Recv matches tag=1| E[Process 1 Application Buffer]
    E -->|return status| F[Process 1 Application Logic]
    F -->|reply message| A
```

**How to read this:** A message originates in Process 0's application buffer, is wrapped by the MPI runtime (header + payload), travels through the network/socket, is matched at the receiver's mailbox, and is delivered into Process 1's buffer. A reply then returns along the symmetric path.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Object-Oriented Message Passing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Object] -->|message: doTask arg=x| B[Receiver Object]
    B -->|method lookup| C[Method Dispatch Table V-Table]
    C -->|selects method| D[Method Implementation]
    D -->|mutates internal state| E[Receiver Internal State]
    D -->|return object| A
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Send Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sync[Synchronous Send]
        S1[Sender calls MPI_Send] --> S2[Wait until matching Recv posted]
        S2 --> S3[Wait until Recv completes]
        S3 --> S4[Send returns]
    end
    subgraph Async[Asynchronous Send]
        A1[Sender calls MPI_Isend] --> A2[Returns immediately with request handle]
        A2 --> A3[Sender continues compute]
        A3 --> A4[Later MPI_Wait or MPI_Test]
        A4 --> A5[Communication completes]
    end
```

### 4.4 Collective Communication Patterns (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Root Process Rank 0] -->|MPI_Bcast data| P1[Process 1]
    R -->|MPI_Bcast data| P2[Process 2]
    R -->|MPI_Bcast data| P3[Process 3]
    P1 -->|MPI_Gather sendbuf| R
    P2 -->|MPI_Gather sendbuf| R
    P3 -->|MPI_Gather sendbuf| R
    P1 <-->|MPI_Allreduce| P2
    P2 <-->|MPI_Allreduce| P3
    P1 <-->|MPI_Allreduce| P3
```

This shows the three most-tested MPI collectives in KTU 2024: **Broadcast** (one-to-all), **Gather** (all-to-one), and **Allreduce** (all-to-all with reduction).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define message passing. Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous message passing.*

**Model Answer:**

Message passing is a communication mechanism in which independent entities (objects or processes) interact by explicitly sending and receiving messages rather than by sharing memory. The sender packages data and a destination into a message, which the receiver interprets.

| Aspect | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Sender blocks? | Yes, until receive completes | No, returns immediately |
| Programmer complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Latency hiding | Poor | Good |
| Deadlock risk | Higher | Lower |
| Example | `MPI_Send`/`MPI_Recv` | `MPI_Isend`/`MPI_Irecv` |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison table.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain any three types of collective communication operations in MPI.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **MPI_Bcast (Broadcast):** One process (root) sends the same message to all other processes in the communicator. Used to distribute configuration data or input parameters.

2. **MPI_Gather:** Each process contributes data from its send buffer; the root process collects them in rank order. Used to consolidate results at one node.

3. **MPI_Reduce / MPI_Allreduce:** Combines data from all processes using an operation (sum, max, min, etc.). `MPI_Allreduce` distributes the result back to every process — useful in distributed gradient descent for ML.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correctly described operation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions in ESE carry **14 marks** and typically present an internal choice between two full-length questions. The model below follows the **(a) 7 + (b) 7** sub-part pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the message passing paradigm in Object-Oriented Programming. With a neat diagram, describe how a message is dispatched to a method, including the role of dynamic binding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a parallel program, derive the total message transmission time $T(n)$ using the Hockney model. Given $L = 5 \, \mu s$, $B = 100 \, \text{MB/s}$, and message size $n = 1 \, \text{MB}$, compute $T$ and identify the dominant cost component. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a)

- **(2 marks)** Define message passing in OOP as objects interacting by sending messages (receiver, selector, arguments).
- **(2 marks)** Diagram showing sender → message envelope → receiver's method dispatch table → matched method.
- **(2 marks)** Explain dynamic binding: the actual method invoked is determined at runtime by the receiver's class, not the static type. This enables polymorphism.
- **(1 mark)** Conclude with advantages: encapsulation, decoupling, extensibility.

##### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — State the Hockney model** (1 mark):

$$
T(n) = L + \frac{n}{B}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values** (2 marks). First convert units:

$$
L = 5 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s}, \quad n = 10^6 \ \text{bytes}, \quad B = 10^8 \ \text{bytes/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bandwidth term** (2 marks):

$$
\frac{n}{B} = \frac{10^6}{10^8} = 10^{-2} \ \text{s} = 10{,}000 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Add latency** (1 mark):

$$
T = 5 \, \mu s + 10{,}000 \, \mu s = 10{,}005 \, \mu s \approx 10.005 \ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Dominant cost** (1 mark): The bandwidth term ($10{,}000 \ \mu s$) dominates the latency term ($5 \ \mu s$) by a factor of **2000×**. For large messages, **bandwidth** is the bottleneck; for tiny messages, **latency** dominates.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain point-to-point communication in MPI. Distinguish between the four send modes: standard, buffered, synchronous, and ready. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Amdahl's law, a parallel program spends 15% of its time in a serial region and 85% in a parallel region. Compute (i) the maximum speedup on 16 processors, (ii) the maximum achievable speedup with infinite processors, and (iii) the parallel efficiency at 16 processors. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a)

- **(1.5 marks)** Block diagram: Sender app buffer → MPI runtime → transport → receiver mailbox → receiver app buffer (reference SECTION 4.1).
- **(0.5 marks)** Define point-to-point as communication between *one* sender and *one* receiver, identified by rank + tag.
- **Send modes** (1.5 marks each):

| Mode | Completion Condition | Buffer Needed? |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard (`MPI_Send`)** | Implementation-defined (may buffer or block) | Maybe |
| **Buffered (`MPI_Bsend`)** | Message copied to user-attached buffer | Yes (`MPI_Buffer_attach`) |
| **Synchronous (`MPI_Ssend`)** | Matching receive has *started* | No |
| **Ready (`MPI_Rsend`)** | Receive must already be posted | No; undefined behavior otherwise |

##### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $p = 0.85$, $1 - p = 0.15$, $N = 16$.

**(i) Speedup on 16 processors** (2 marks):

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.15 + \frac{0.85}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.053125} = \frac{1}{0.203125}
$$

$$
S(16) \approx 4.923
$$

**(ii) Limit as $N \to \infty$** (2 marks):

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.667
$$

**(iii) Efficiency at 16 processors** (3 marks):

$$
E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{4.923}{16} \approx 0.3077 = 30.77\%
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Message Passing questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 buffer slot:** In `MPI_Recv`, the count is the *maximum* number of elements the application can accept, not the *actual* number expected. Wrong counts cause truncation or buffer overflows.
> 2. **Tag/source mismatch confusion:** `MPI_ANY_TAG` and `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` are *wildcards on the receiver side only*; using them in a send is a common error.
> 3. **Mixing blocking and non-blocking incorrectly:** Calling `MPI_Wait` on a non-blocking request is mandatory *before* reading from the receive buffer, or the data is undefined.
> 4. **Confusing `MPI_Comm_size` (total processes) with `MPI_Comm_rank` (this process's ID)** — read the names carefully.
> 5. **Hockney model units:** $B$ must be in **bytes/second** (not bits). KTU exam answers commonly lose the conversion (1 MB = $10^6$ bytes, 1 MiB = $2^{20}$ bytes).
> 6. **Amdahl's law misuse:** The serial fraction $p$ refers to *parallelizable* code; some students invert it. If the question says "20% is serial", then $1-p = 0.20$, **not** $p = 0.20$.
> 7. **Deadlock in `MPI_Send`/`MPI_Recv`:** Cyclic ordering (Process 0 sends to 1 while waiting to receive from 1) causes deadlock; KTU specifically tests this with the *Szymanski algorithm* or *linear chain* corrections.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Message Passing**
>
> - ✅ Message passing = **no shared memory**; all interaction is via explicit `send`/`recv`.
> - ✅ In OOP, a message = **receiver + selector + arguments**; dispatch uses the receiver's runtime class.
> - ✅ In MPI, fundamental operations are **point-to-point** (`Send`/`Recv`) and **collective** (`Bcast`/`Gather`/`Reduce`/`Allreduce`).
> - ✅ Four send modes: **Standard, Buffered, Synchronous, Ready** — know the completion condition of each.
> - ✅ Blocking = thread waits; **Non-blocking** = thread continues, must use `Wait`/`Test` later.
> - ✅ **Hockney model:** $T(n) = L + n/B$. Small messages → latency-bound; large messages → bandwidth-bound.
> - ✅ **Amdahl's law:** $S = 1/\bigl((1-p) + p/N\bigr)$; max speedup is $1/(1-p)$ as $N \to \infty$.
> - ✅ **Efficiency** $E = S/N$; practical systems target $E \ge 0.5$.
> - ✅ **Deadlock** arises in cyclic blocking patterns; break the cycle by reordering or using non-blocking primitives.
> - ✅ **MPI tags** filter messages; **MPI_ANY_TAG** / **MPI_ANY_SOURCE** are wildcards for receive only.
> - ✅ Real-world domains: HPC, microservices, ROS, AUTOSAR, telecom signaling (SS7), microkernel OS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ada Support for Concurrency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ada Support for Concurrency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ada Support for Concurrency** refers to the **built-in, language-level constructs** provided by the Ada programming language (Ada 83, Ada 95, Ada 2005, Ada 2012, Ada 2022) to express **parallel and concurrent execution units** as first-class citizens of the language. Unlike libraries or external APIs, concurrency in Ada is part of the language syntax, the type system, and the run-time semantics.

The three foundational pillars of Ada concurrency are:

1. **Task** — the unit of concurrent execution (an independent thread of control).
2. **Rendezvous** — the synchronous message-passing mechanism for inter-task communication.
3. **Protected Object / Protected Type** — the encapsulation primitive that provides **mutually exclusive access to shared data** with conditional synchronization, replacing the need for explicit semaphores or monitors in most cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Concurrency in Ada is based on the theoretical model of **CSP (Communicating Sequential Processes)** proposed by C. A. R. Hoare, extended with the **Protected Object** model (Ada 95) inspired by the **Conditional Critical Region (CCR)** of Brinch Hansen.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen**:
- Each **chef** is a **task** — they work independently, follow their own recipe, and execute concurrently.
- When a chef needs a special ingredient from the pantry, they **call the pantry worker (entry call)**.
- The **pantry worker stops what they are doing**, serves the request (**accept**), hands over the ingredient, and both continue — this is a **rendezvous**.
- The **walk-in refrigerator** is a **protected object** — only one chef can enter at a time (mutual exclusion), and a chef waiting for a rare ingredient waits politely (conditional entry / barrier) until it is restocked.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A *task* is **active** (it does things on its own). A *protected object* is **passive** (it guards data and only reacts when called). This active/passive split is the cornerstone of Ada's concurrency philosophy.

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (Must Memorize)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Task** | A concurrent unit with its own execution thread and life cycle. |
| **Task Specification** | Declares the task's *interface* (its entries). |
| **Task Body** | Declares the *execution* of the task. |
| **Entry** | A subprogram-like operation declared in a task specification; accepts calls from other tasks. |
| **Rendezvous** | The synchronous handshake when a caller invokes an entry and the task reaches the corresponding `accept` statement. |
| **Protected Object** | A passive module that encapsulates shared data and guarantees safe concurrent access. |
| **Barrier** | A Boolean condition guarding a protected entry; tasks wait until it becomes `True`. |
| **Requeue** | Transferring a waiting task from one entry queue to another. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Ada Task — Structure and Lifecycle

A task in Ada is declared using the reserved word `task` (single task) or `task type` (a template from which many tasks may be instantiated).

### 2.1.1 Task Declaration Anatomy

```ada
task type Producer (ID : Positive; Buffer : access Shared_Buffer) is
    entry Start;
    entry Stop;
end Producer;
```
The block above (between `is` and the matching `;`) is the **task specification** — it is the task's public face, like a class header.

```ada
task body Producer is
begin
    -- executable statements of the task
end Producer;
```
The **task body** defines what the task actually does.

### 2.1.2 Task States (Lifecycle)

Every Ada task moves through a deterministic set of states:

1. **Created (Unactivated)** — The task object has been elaborated, but its body has not yet begun executing.
2. **Activated** — The task is eligible to run, but the run-time may not have scheduled it yet.
3. **Executing** — The task is currently running on a processor.
4. **Completed / Terminated** — The task body has reached its final `end`. A terminated task cannot be restarted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Activation:** In Ada, a task is *activated* only after all tasks in the same declarative part have been elaborated. This is called the **"All-or-Nothing Activation"** rule — a well-known KTU viva question.

### 2.1.3 Task Termination Rules

- A task terminates when it reaches the end of its body.
- The **master task** (the enclosing scope — e.g., the procedure that declared it) does **not** terminate until **all** its dependent tasks have terminated.
- If a task raises an unhandled exception, it is treated as a *tasking error* and the program may be aborted with `Tasking_Error`.

## 2.2 The Rendezvous Mechanism

The **rendezvous** is the foundational communication primitive of Ada. It is a **synchronous** message exchange between a calling task and a servicing task.

### 2.2.1 The `accept` Statement

The servicing task uses `accept` to wait for and service an entry call:

```ada
accept Deposit (Item : in Integer) do
    Buffer.all.Append (Item);
end Deposit;
```

### 2.2.2 Rendezvous Phases (Step-by-Step)

1. **Call Phase** — A task executes `Buffer.Deposit (X)`. The call is queued in the entry's FIFO queue.
2. **Accept Phase** — The servicing task reaches `accept Deposit (...)`. If the queue is non-empty, the first queued caller is dequeued and the two tasks execute the body of the `accept` in **parallel** — both tasks run the statements inside the `accept ... do ... end` simultaneously (though logically the body is "owned" by the called entry).
3. **Return Phase** — When the `accept` block ends, the two tasks separate and continue their independent execution. The caller proceeds with the statement following the entry call; the servicing task proceeds with the statement following the `accept`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The parameters of an `accept` follow the **standard Ada parameter modes**: `in` (caller → callee), `out` (callee → caller), and `in out` (both directions).

## 2.3 The `select` Statement — Selective Wait

The `select` statement allows a task to **wait for more than one entry call** simultaneously, or to choose between a rendezvous and a conditional/timed alternative.

### 2.3.1 The Three Forms

**Form 1 — Selective Accept (with `terminate` and `delay` alternatives):**

```ada
select
    accept Put (X : in Integer) do
        Buffer.all.Append (X);
    end Put;
or
    accept Get (Y : out Integer) do
        Y := Buffer.all.Remove;
    end Get;
or
    delay 0.5;
    -- periodic task
end select;
```

**Form 2 — Conditional Entry Call:**

```ada
select
    Buffer.Put (X);
    Put_Success := True;
else
    Put_Success := False;
end select;
```

The `else` branch is taken **immediately** if the entry call cannot be accepted right now (i.e., the task is not currently at the `accept`).

**Form 3 — Timed Entry Call:**

```ada
select
    Buffer.Put (X);
    Put_Success := True;
or
    delay 1.0;
    Put_Success := False;
end select;
```

The call is **abandoned** if it is not accepted within 1.0 second.

## 2.4 Protected Objects — The Data Guardian

A **protected object** is the Ada 95+ way to build safe shared data. It encapsulates a set of data and a set of operations, where the compiler and run-time **guarantee** that the data is accessed safely.

```ada
protected type Safe_Buffer is
    entry Put (X : in Integer);
    entry Get (Y : out Integer);
    function Size return Natural;
private
    Data : array (1 .. 100) of Integer;
    Head, Tail : Integer := 1;
    Count      : Natural  := 0;
end Safe_Buffer;

protected body Safe_Buffer is
    entry Put (X : in Integer) when Count < 100 is
    begin
        Data (Tail) := X;
        Tail := Tail mod 100 + 1;
        Count := Count + 1;
    end Put;

    entry Get (Y : out Integer) when Count > 0 is
    begin
        Y := Data (Head);
        Head := Head mod 100 + 1;
        Count := Count - 1;
    end Get;

    function Size return Natural is
    begin
        return Count;
    end Size;
end Safe_Buffer;
```

### 2.4.1 The Three Kinds of Protected Operations

| Operation | Reentrancy | Barrier Check | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Protected Procedure** | Exclusive (one at a time) | No | For writes or atomic read-modify-write. |
| **Protected Entry** | Exclusive + barrier | **Yes** (barrier `when` condition) | For conditional synchronization (producer/consumer). |
| **Protected Function** | **Reentrant** (multiple readers allowed) | No | For read-only inspection. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reentrancy Rule:** A protected function may be called by another task while another task is already inside a protected function, because it only reads. Procedures and entries are mutually exclusive with each other.

### 2.4.2 The Barrier Mechanism

A **barrier** is a Boolean expression (`when ...`) attached to a protected entry. When a task calls the entry, it is queued. The task only proceeds past the barrier when:
1. It holds the exclusive lock on the protected object, **and**
2. The barrier evaluates to `True`.

If the barrier is `False`, the task remains queued and is **re-evaluated** whenever any protected procedure or entry of the same object completes (this is known as the **barrier re-evaluation rule**).

## 2.5 Requeue — Dynamic Entry Forwarding

`requeue` is a powerful Ada construct that transfers a task from one entry queue to another. This enables **dynamic resource allocation** patterns.

```ada
protected body Resource_Manager is
    entry Allocate_Primary when Free_List.Primary_Available is ...
    entry Allocate_Secondary when Free_List.Secondary_Available is
    begin
        ...
    end Allocate_Secondary;

    entry Allocate when True is
    begin
        if not Free_List.Primary_Available then
            requeue Allocate_Secondary;
        end if;
    end Allocate;
end Resource_Manager;
```

## 2.6 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet — Concurrency Construct Summary

| Construct | Purpose | Mutex | Conditional Sync | Reentrancy | Year Introduced |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `task` | Active execution unit | N/A | N/A | N/A | Ada 83 |
| `accept ... do ... end` | Synchronous entry servicing | Yes (entry) | No | No | Ada 83 |
| `select ... or ... else` | Conditional entry call | No | Yes (`else`) | N/A | Ada 83 |
| `select ... or delay` | Timed entry call | No | Yes (timeout) | N/A | Ada 83 |
| `protected type` | Passive data guardian | Yes | Yes (barrier) | Functions only | Ada 95 |
| `requeue` | Move waiter to another entry | Inherited | Yes | N/A | Ada 95 |
| `pragma Priority` / `Interrupt_Priority` | Set task priority | N/A | N/A | N/A | Ada 95 |
| `Ada.Synchronous_Task_Control` | Suspension/resume primitives | N/A | N/A | N/A | Ada 2005 |
| `aspect Relative_Deadline` | Deadline scheduling | N/A | N/A | N/A | Ada 2012 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ada's concurrency model is the **gold standard** for **safety-critical and hard-real-time systems**. The language was designed for, and is still mandated by:

- **DO-178C (Avionics)** — Boeing 777, Airbus A380 flight control software is largely Ada.
- **IEC 61508 SIL 4 / ISO 26262 ASIL D (Automotive)** — engine control units, brake-by-wire.
- **MIL-STD-882 (Defense)** — missile guidance, radar tracking.
- **Railway Signaling (EN 50128 SIL 4)** — interlocking systems.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304)** — infusion pumps, defibrillators.

The motivation: the **compiler and run-time prove the absence of data races** for protected objects, and the **rendezvous semantics** make deadlocks statically analyzable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> The walkthroughs below are **exhaustive**. Every line of code and every transition is explained, in the style of a KTU board examiner's model solution.

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Lifecycle and Rendezvous Between Two Tasks

Consider the following program:

```ada
with Ada.Text_IO; use Ada.Text_IO;
procedure Rendezvous_Demo is
    task type Server is
        entry Request (Msg : in String);
    end Server;

    task body Server is
    begin
        loop
            accept Request (Msg : in String) do
                Put_Line ("Server got: " & Msg);
            end Request;
        end loop;
    end Server;

    Client : Server;
begin
    Client.Request ("Hello");
    Client.Request ("World");
    Put_Line ("Client done");
end Rendezvous_Demo;
```

### Step 1 — Program Start

The main procedure `Rendezvous_Demo` is the **master task**. The declarative part contains the type declaration `task type Server` and the object `Client : Server`.

### Step 2 — Task Activation

When the elaboration of the declarative part begins, the **task object `Client` is created**. Per the *all-or-nothing* rule, it is **not yet activated** — it waits until the entire declarative part has been elaborated.

After the declarative part ends and the `begin` of the main procedure is reached:
- The **Server task body begins executing** (it is now in the **Active** state).
- The main procedure continues into its sequence of statements.

### Step 3 — First Rendezvous

The main task executes:
```ada
Client.Request ("Hello");
```
This is an **entry call**. The main task is **suspended** in the FIFO queue of the `Request` entry.

Meanwhile, the Server task, executing its `loop`, reaches:
```ada
accept Request (Msg : in String) do ... end Request;
```
The Server task **dequeues the main task** from the queue, and the two tasks execute the `accept` body in parallel:
- The string `"Hello"` is passed from caller to callee as the `in` parameter `Msg`.
- `Put_Line ("Server got: " & Msg)` prints `Server got: Hello`.

When the `end Request;` is reached, the **rendezvous completes**:
- The main task resumes after its `Client.Request ("Hello");` statement.
- The Server task continues at the top of its `loop`.

### Step 4 — Second Rendezvous

The same sequence repeats for `Client.Request ("World");`, producing the output `Server got: World`.

### Step 5 — Termination

After `Put_Line ("Client done");` the main task tries to exit the procedure. However, the **master rule** states: the master cannot terminate until all dependent tasks have terminated. The Server task is in an **infinite loop**, so it will never terminate. Therefore, the program will be **aborted by the run-time** with a *Tasking_Error* (or, depending on the compiler, will deadlock waiting for the Server).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** This is a classic KTU question. The output observed will be:
>
> ```
> Server got: Hello
> Server got: World
> Client done
> ```
> in that order (because the rendezvous is synchronous), followed by a run-time abort because the Server task never terminates.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Protected Object with Two Entries

Consider the bounded-buffer example from Section 2.4. We trace the behavior when **two producers** call `Put` and **one consumer** calls `Get` simultaneously.

### Step 1 — Initial State

- `Count = 0`, `Head = 1`, `Tail = 1`, `Data` is uninitialized.
- The barrier of `Put` is `Count < 100` → `True`.
- The barrier of `Get` is `Count > 0` → `False`.

### Step 2 — Consumer Calls Get

The consumer task calls `Get`. Since the barrier `Count > 0` is `False`, the consumer is **enqueued** in the `Get` queue and **suspended**. The protected object becomes **idle** (no lock held).

### Step 3 — Producer A Calls Put

Producer A calls `Put`. The barrier `Count < 100` is `True`. Producer A acquires the **exclusive lock**, executes the body:
- `Data (Tail) := X` → stores at `Data(1)`.
- `Tail := Tail mod 100 + 1` → `Tail = 2`.
- `Count := Count + 1` → `Count = 1`.

When the body completes:
1. The lock is released.
2. The barrier of `Get` is **re-evaluated** to `True` (since `Count = 1 > 0`).
3. The waiting consumer is now eligible to run.

### Step 4 — Consumer Resumes

The consumer acquires the lock, executes the `Get` body:
- `Y := Data (Head)` → reads `Data(1)`.
- `Head := Head mod 100 + 1` → `Head = 2`.
- `Count := Count - 1` → `Count = 0`.

Returns `Y` to the caller.

### Step 5 — Producer B Calls Put Simultaneously with A

If Producer B's `Put` call arrives while Producer A holds the lock:
- Producer B is enqueued in the `Put` FIFO queue.
- Producer A completes its body and releases the lock.
- The barrier of `Put` is re-evaluated (still `True`).
- Producer B is now the next in line, so it acquires the lock and proceeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mutual Exclusion Proof:** At no point in the above trace are two tasks inside protected operations of `Safe_Buffer` simultaneously. The run-time guarantees this. The compiler inserts the locking code invisibly — the programmer never writes a `lock` or `unlock` call.

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — The `select` with `delay` Alternative

Code under analysis:

```ada
task body Watchdog is
begin
    loop
        select
            accept Kick;
        or
            delay 2.0;
            Shutdown_System;
        end select;
    end loop;
end Watchdog;
```

### Step 1 — Task Active, Entering the Loop

The Watchdog task is activated. It enters its `loop`.

### Step 2 — Reaching the `select`

The task reaches the `select` statement. Two alternatives are open:
- `accept Kick` — wait for a `Kick` entry call.
- `delay 2.0` — wait for 2.0 seconds.

Both alternatives are simultaneously open. The task is **suspended** waiting for **either** an entry call **or** the timer expiry.

### Step 3a — Kick Arrives First

If another task calls `Watchdog.Kick` within 2.0 seconds:
- The `Kick` entry is selected.
- The rendezvous occurs.
- The `delay` is **cancelled** (its timer is discarded).
- The task continues to the next iteration of the loop.

### Step 3b — Timer Expires First

If no `Kick` arrives within 2.0 seconds:
- The `delay` alternative fires.
- The `accept Kick` is **abandoned** (no rendezvous occurs).
- `Shutdown_System` is executed.
- The task continues to the next iteration (or terminates, depending on the body).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTS Insight (Real-Time Systems):** The `delay` statement uses the **Ada real-time clock** by default. The interval is measured in **seconds** (a fixed-point type). For higher precision, use `Ada.Real_Time` and the `delay until` form with `Time` values.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Construct | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Producer-consumer with shared buffer | Protected type with entries | Cleanest expression of barrier-based blocking. |
| Many clients talking to a single server | Task with `accept` and a `loop` | Server-style rendezvous is exactly the rendezvous model. |
| One-shot event notification | Task with a single `accept` | Lighter weight than a protected object. |
| Need to choose between several I/O sources | `select ... or ... or ... end select` | Native multi-way wait. |
| Pure data, no logic | Protected type with functions only | No task overhead; reader-friendly reentrancy. |
| Periodic task | `delay until Next_Time;` | Drift-free periodic activation. |
| "Try once" non-blocking call | `select ... else ... end select` | Polling without busy-wait. |
| Bound a call's wait time | `select ... or delay T ... end select` | Timeout pattern. |
| Allocate from one of many pools | `requeue` | Dynamic entry migration. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ada Task Lifecycle (State Transition Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : elaboration
    Created --> Activated : all tasks in decl part elaborated
    Activated --> Executing : scheduler dispatches
    Executing --> Executing : accept / select / delay
    Executing --> Completed : task body end reached
    Completed --> Terminated : finalization complete
    Terminated --> [*]
    Activated --> Completed : unhandled exception
    Executing --> Completed : unhandled exception
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A task is *Created* when its object is elaborated.
- It becomes *Activated* only after the entire declarative part has been elaborated (the **all-or-nothing** rule).
- It cycles between *Activated* and *Executing* depending on scheduling.
- It reaches *Completed* either by normal termination (reaching `end Task_Body`) or by an unhandled exception.
- *Terminated* is a brief final state where finalization runs.

## 4.2 Rendezvous — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller Task] --> B[Execute entry call statement]
    B --> C{Is callee at accept}
    C -- No --> D[Suspended in entry queue FIFO]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[Rendezvous begins]
    E --> F[Pass in parameters]
    F --> G[Execute accept body in parallel]
    G --> H[Pass out parameters]
    H --> I[Rendezvous ends]
    I --> J[Caller resumes after call site]
    I --> K[Callee resumes after accept]
    J --> L[Caller task continues]
    K --> M[Callee task continues]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The caller is suspended in a FIFO queue until the callee is at the `accept`.
- Once both are ready, the rendezvous begins.
- The two tasks execute the `accept` body in parallel (this is the actual *concurrent* phase).
- On completion, both tasks return to independent execution.

## 4.3 Protected Object — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProtectedObject[Protected Object Safe Buffer]
        G1[Guard Put barrier Count less than 100]
        G2[Guard Get barrier Count greater than 0]
        D[(Shared Data Data array Head Tail Count)]
        O1[Protected Procedure]
        O2[Protected Entry Put]
        O3[Protected Entry Get]
        O4[Protected Function Size]
    end

    T1[Task Producer A] --> O2
    T2[Task Producer B] --> O2
    T3[Task Consumer] --> O3
    T4[Task Inspector] --> O4
    O1 -. writes .-> D
    O2 -. writes .-> D
    O3 -. reads and writes .-> D
    O4 -. reads only .-> D
    G1 --- O2
    G2 --- O3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The protected object encapsulates both **data** and **operations**.
- Producers call `Put`; consumer calls `Get`; inspector calls `Size`.
- Each protected entry has a **barrier** that gates access.
- The arrows show that operations manipulate the shared data; the run-time guarantees that only one operation (except functions) runs at a time.

## 4.4 `select` Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Reach select statement] --> A{Which alternatives open}
    A --> B[Accept alternative]
    A --> C[Delay alternative]
    A --> D[Terminate alternative]
    A --> E[Else branch]
    A --> F[Delay or timeout branch]
    B --> B1{Is call waiting}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Execute rendezvous]
    B1 -- No --> A
    C --> C1{Delay expired}
    C1 -- Yes --> C2[Execute delay body]
    C1 -- No --> A
    D --> D1{Master completed and all dependents done}
```
