```mermaid
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Terminate this task]
    D1 -- No --> A
    E --> E1[Execute else branch]
    F --> F1{Timer expired}
    F1 -- Yes --> F2[Execute timeout body]
    F1 -- No --> F2b[Continue waiting for accept]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The `select` block polls **all** open alternatives.
- The first one that becomes *ready* is selected.
- If two are ready at the same time, the choice is **implementation-defined** in Ada 95 but **deterministic** in Ada 2005+ (per the Real-Time Systems Annex).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a "task" in Ada. Explain the difference between a task specification and a task body.**

**Model Answer:**

A *task* in Ada is a program unit that executes concurrently with the main program and other tasks. It is an **active** entity with its own thread of control and its own life cycle.

- The **task specification** defines the *interface* of the task — its name, parameters (if a task type), and the set of **entries** that other tasks may call. It is the public face of the task.
- The **task body** defines the *execution* of the task — the sequence of statements that the task runs when activated. It contains the variable declarations and the executable code.

The separation mirrors that of a package: the specification is the *what*, the body is the *how*.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a "rendezvous" in Ada? List its three phases.**

**Model Answer:**

A *rendezvous* is a **synchronous** message-passing mechanism by which two Ada tasks communicate. When one task calls an entry of another task, the two tasks "meet" at the `accept` statement; the caller is suspended until the rendezvous is complete.

The three phases are:
1. **Call Phase** — The caller issues an entry call and is suspended in the entry's FIFO queue.
2. **Accept Phase** — The servicing task reaches the matching `accept` statement. The caller is dequeued; the parameters are passed; and the body of the `accept` is executed in parallel by both tasks.
3. **Return Phase** — When the `end accept` is reached, the two tasks separate and continue their independent execution. Out and in-out parameters are passed back to the caller.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B of the End-Semester Exam typically presents **two 14-mark questions from a single module**, and you must answer **one**. The sub-parts are usually `(a)` 7 marks and `(b)` 7 marks.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Ada tasking model with a suitable example. Discuss task activation and termination rules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are protected objects in Ada? Explain the role of barriers, protected procedures, protected entries, and protected functions. Show how they achieve mutual exclusion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The Ada Tasking Model:**

Ada's tasking model is based on the **CSP (Communicating Sequential Processes)** model of Hoare, extended with the **Protected Object** model. Every concurrent activity in Ada is expressed as a *task* (active) or interacts with a *protected object* (passive).

**Example:**

```ada
task type Counter is
    entry Increment;
    entry Value (V : out Natural);
end Counter;

task body Counter is
    I : Natural := 0;
begin
    loop
        accept Increment do
            I := I + 1;
        end Increment;
    end loop;
end Counter;
```

**Activation Rules:**
- A task is created when its object is elaborated (i.e., when the declarative part containing it is processed).
- The task body does **not** begin executing immediately. The activation is delayed until the **entire declarative part** has been elaborated. This is the **all-or-nothing activation** rule.
- After activation, the task is in the *activated* state and is eligible for scheduling.

**Termination Rules:**
- A task terminates when its body reaches the final `end`.
- The enclosing *master* (the subprogram or block that declared the task) does not terminate until all its dependent tasks have terminated.
- A task that raises an unhandled exception is terminated abnormally, and a `Tasking_Error` is propagated to the master.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Protected Objects:**

A *protected object* is a passive module that encapsulates shared data and a set of operations. The Ada run-time guarantees that the data is accessed safely even under concurrent invocation.

**Components:**

1. **Protected Procedure** — Provides mutually exclusive access. Used for writes or atomic read-modify-write operations. No barrier.
2. **Protected Entry** — Provides mutually exclusive access **plus** conditional synchronization through a *barrier* (`when` condition). Tasks wait until the barrier becomes `True`.
3. **Protected Function** — Provides *reentrant* read-only access. Multiple functions may execute simultaneously; a function cannot run concurrently with a procedure or entry.

**How Mutual Exclusion is Achieved:**

When a task calls a protected procedure or entry, the run-time first **evaluates the barrier** (for entries). If the barrier is `True`, the task acquires the **exclusive lock** on the protected object. All other tasks are blocked from invoking any non-function operation of the same object. When the operation completes, the lock is released. The compiler generates the lock/unlock code invisibly — the programmer does not write any explicit lock.

**Barrier Re-evaluation:**

Whenever a protected procedure or entry of an object completes, the barriers of all entries of that object are **re-evaluated**. Any waiting task whose barrier is now `True` becomes eligible to run.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **rendezvous** mechanism in Ada with the help of an example. How does it differ from ordinary procedure calls? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **select** statement in Ada. Discuss the three forms: selective accept, conditional entry call, and timed entry call. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The Rendezvous Mechanism:**

A rendezvous is a synchronous communication primitive between two tasks. It is initiated when one task (the **caller**) executes an entry call on another task (the **callee**). The caller is suspended until the callee reaches the matching `accept` statement and the two tasks complete the rendezvous body together.

**Example:**

```ada
task Buffer_Server is
    entry Deposit (X : in Integer);
    entry Fetch (Y : out Integer);
end Buffer_Server;

task body Buffer_Server is
    Cell : Integer := 0;
begin
    loop
        select
            accept Deposit (X : in Integer) do
                Cell := X;
            end Deposit;
        or
            accept Fetch (Y : out Integer) do
                Y := Cell;
            end Fetch;
        end select;
    end loop;
end Buffer_Server;
```

**Differences from Ordinary Procedure Calls:**

| Aspect | Procedure Call | Rendezvous |
|---|---|---|
| **Caller state** | Continues immediately | **Suspended** until rendezvous completes |
| **Callee state** | Runs on caller's thread | May be doing something else; rendezvous is a "meeting" |
| **Execution** | Sequential | **Parallel** during the accept body |
| **Mutual exclusion** | Not relevant | **Implicit** — only one rendezvous at a time per entry |
| **Parameter passing** | At call | At rendezvous start; `out` returned at end |
| **Return timing** | At `return` of procedure | At `end accept` |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The `select` Statement:**

The `select` statement allows a task to **wait for one of several events** simultaneously. It has three principal forms.

**Form 1 — Selective Accept (with alternatives):**

```ada
select
    accept Put (X : in Integer) do ... end Put;
or
    accept Get (Y : out Integer) do ... end Get;
or
    delay 0.5;
    Periodic_Action;
end select;
```
The task waits for **any** of the alternatives to become ready. The first one to be ready is selected. If two become ready simultaneously, the choice is implementation-defined in Ada 95 but deterministic (per Annex D) in Ada 2005+.

**Form 2 — Conditional Entry Call:**

```ada
select
    Buffer.Put (X);
    Success := True;
else
    Success := False;
end select;
```
The `else` branch is taken **immediately** if the call cannot be accepted right now (i.e., the callee is not at the matching `accept` and the queue is empty). This is a *non-blocking* attempt.

**Form 3 — Timed Entry Call:**

```ada
select
    Buffer.Put (X);
    Success := True;
or
    delay 1.5;
    Success := False;
end select;
```
The call is **abandoned** if it is not accepted within 1.5 seconds. The `delay` alternative fires instead. This implements a *timeout* on the rendezvous.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the two `select` forms.** `else` means "try once, fail immediately" — it is **not** a polling loop. `or delay` means "wait up to N seconds, then give up."
> 2. **Forgetting the `accept` body syntax.** The `accept` body must use `do ... end` even if empty; do not confuse with `accept ... ;`.
> 3. **Mistaking barrier semantics.** A barrier is **re-evaluated only when an operation of the same protected object completes** — not on every clock tick.
> 4. **Thinking `protected function` is exclusive.** It is **reentrant** — this is a guaranteed KTU question pattern.
> 5. **Wrong output ordering.** In a rendezvous, the server's output inside the `accept` body appears **before** the caller's statement after the entry call, even though they are "logically parallel."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tasks** are **active**; **Protected Objects** are **passive** — remember this distinction for any KTU viva.
- **All-or-Nothing Activation** — a task does not start until the entire declarative part has been elaborated.
- The **master** cannot terminate until all its dependent tasks have terminated.
- A **rendezvous** is **synchronous** — the caller is suspended; the `accept` body is executed in parallel by both tasks.
- An `accept` body **must** be written using `do ... end`, even if empty.
- The `select` statement has **three forms**: selective accept, conditional (with `else`), and timed (with `delay`).
- A `protected procedure` and a `protected entry` are **mutually exclusive**; a `protected function` is **reentrant**.
- The **barrier** of a protected entry is re-evaluated when any protected operation of the same object completes.
- `requeue` moves a waiting task from one entry to another — useful for resource pools.
- `pragma Priority` and `Interrupt_Priority` (Annex D) make Ada suitable for **hard real-time** systems.
- Ada's concurrency is the **only** mainstream language model where data race freedom is **enforced by the compiler and run-time**, not by convention.
- The model is grounded in **CSP (Hoare)** + **CCR (Brinch Hansen)** — know both names for the viva.
- Default scheduling in Ada is **non-preemptive within a priority level** unless the **Ravenscar profile** or the **Annex D (Real-Time Systems)** is in effect.
- A task that has finished its body is **terminated** — it cannot be restarted.
- A `delay` in a `select` may use `delay until <time>` for drift-free periodic activation (use `Ada.Real_Time` for nanosecond precision).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Java Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧵 Java Threads — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Java Thread** is the smallest independent unit of execution within a Java program, representing a single sequential flow of control. Every Java program has at least one thread (the *main* thread created by the JVM), and additional threads may be created to perform concurrent tasks. Threads are instances of `java.lang.Thread` or implementations of `java.lang.Runnable`, and they share the same process memory space (heap), enabling lightweight concurrency compared to processes.

## 🏠 Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder)

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen**:
- The **restaurant** = Your Java program (JVM process)
- A **chef** = A thread
- The **shared kitchen** (stove, oven, fridge) = Shared heap memory
- **Multiple chefs working simultaneously** = Multithreading

Just as two chefs share the same kitchen resources (but risk bumping into each other if not coordinated), multiple Java threads share the same memory (but risk **race conditions** without proper **synchronization**). A chef can also **wait** for an ingredient (a thread `wait()`s for a resource) and another chef can **notify** him when it's ready (`notify()`).

## 🎯 Why Threads? — KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use threads in Java?**
> 1. **Better CPU Utilization** — Idle time is reduced.
> 2. **Responsiveness** — UI remains responsive while background tasks run.
> 3. **Parallelism on Multi-Core Systems** — True simultaneous execution.
> 4. **Simplified Modeling** — Independent tasks modeled as independent threads.
> 5. **Asynchronous Processing** — E.g., file download while user browses.

## 📚 The Two Ways to Create a Thread (KTU High-Yield)

| Method | Mechanism | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Extending `java.lang.Thread`** | Subclass `Thread` and override `run()` | Cannot extend another class (Java single inheritance) |
| **Implementing `java.lang.Runnable`** | Implement `Runnable` interface and pass to `Thread` | Preferred in industry; allows multiple inheritance of interfaces |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Always favor `Runnable` over extending `Thread` in answers. It decouples the *task* from the *thread mechanism* — a design principle aligned with **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)**.

## 🔁 Thread Lifecycle at a Glance

A Java thread exists in one of these states (defined in `Thread.State` enum):
1. **NEW** — Created but not yet started
2. **RUNNABLE** — Eligible to run, waiting for CPU
3. **BLOCKED** — Waiting to acquire a monitor lock
4. **WAITING** — Indefinitely waiting (`wait()`, `join()` without timeout)
5. **TIMED_WAITING** — Waiting with timeout (`sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`)
6. **TERMINATED** — `run()` has completed

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🛠️ Method 1: Creating a Thread by Extending `Thread`

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        // Code executed in this thread
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); } 
            catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}

public class TestThread {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread();
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread();
        t1.start();   // JVM calls t1.run() in a NEW call stack
        t2.start();   // JVM calls t2.run() in ANOTHER call stack
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **Never call `run()` directly!** Calling `run()` executes it in the **current thread (main)** — no new thread is spawned. Always use `start()`, which internally calls `run()` on a new call stack.

## 🛠️ Method 2: Creating a Thread by Implementing `Runnable` ✅ (Recommended)

```java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " : " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class TestRunnable {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyRunnable obj = new MyRunnable();
        Thread t1 = new Thread(obj, "Worker-1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(obj, "Worker-2");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

> Both `t1` and `t2` share the **same `Runnable` object** — meaning they share instance state unless we mark it carefully.

## 🛠️ Method 3 (Bonus — Modern): Implementing `Callable<V>` with `ExecutorService`

```java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

class MyCallable implements Callable<Integer> {
    public Integer call() throws Exception {
        return 42;
    }
}

ExecutorService exec = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();
Future<Integer> result = exec.submit(new MyCallable());
Integer value = result.get();  // blocks until result is ready
exec.shutdown();
```

This is the **modern Java approach** — used in KTU Module 4's "Advanced Programming" portion.

## 📋 KTU High-Yield Method Cheat Sheet

| Method | Signature | Purpose | Throws |
|---|---|---|---|
| `start()` | `void start()` | Spawns a new call stack and invokes `run()` | — |
| `run()` | `void run()` | Entry point of thread logic | — |
| `sleep(ms)` | `static void sleep(long)` | Pauses current thread | `InterruptedException` |
| `join()` | `void join()` | Current thread waits for target to die | `InterruptedException` |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints scheduler to give others a turn | — |
| `interrupt()` | `void interrupt()` | Sets interrupted flag | — |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean isAlive()` | True if thread has started & not died | — |
| `setPriority(int)` | `void setPriority(int)` | Sets priority (1 to 10) | `IllegalArgumentException` |
| `setName(String)` | `void setName(String)` | Assigns a name | — |
| `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | Returns reference to current thread | — |
| `wait()` | `void wait()` (in `Object`) | Releases lock & waits | `InterruptedException` |
| `notify()` | `void notify()` (in `Object`) | Wakes one waiter | — |
| `notifyAll()` | `void notifyAll()` (in `Object`) | Wakes all waiters | — |

## 🏷️ Thread Priority Constants

| Constant | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY` | **1** | Lowest |
| `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` | **5** | Default |
| `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY` | **10** | Highest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority is only a HINT to the OS scheduler** — it does NOT guarantee execution order. On modern systems, priorities may be ignored. This is a frequent KTU theory question.

## 🔒 Synchronization — The Core Concept

When two threads access **shared mutable data** simultaneously, the result is unpredictable. This is a **race condition**. Java solves this with the `synchronized` keyword.

### Two forms of `synchronized`:

1. **Synchronized Method** — Locks `this` (the current object)

```java
class Counter {
    private int count = 0;
    
    public synchronized void increment() {
        count++;
    }
}
```

2. **Synchronized Block** — Locks a specific object

```java
public void increment() {
    synchronized (this) {
        count++;
    }
}
```

### Static Synchronization

Locks the **`Class` object** (not instance), so all instances of the class share the lock:

```java
public static synchronized void display() { ... }
// Equivalent to:
synchronized (ClassName.class) { ... }
```

## 🤝 Inter-Thread Communication (`wait` / `notify` / `notifyAll`)

These methods are inherited from **`java.lang.Object`** (every object has them). They are used to coordinate threads that share a monitor lock.

| Method | Effect |
|---|---|
| `wait()` | Releases the lock, suspends the thread until `notify()` is called on the same object |
| `notify()` | Wakes **one** thread waiting on the same object's monitor |
| `notifyAll()` | Wakes **all** threads waiting on the same object's monitor |

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` **MUST be called from inside a `synchronized` block** on the same object — otherwise → `IllegalMonitorStateException`.

## 💀 Deadlock — The Classic Concurrency Trap

**Deadlock** occurs when two or more threads are blocked forever, each waiting for a lock that the other holds. The four **Coffman conditions** for deadlock:

1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Resource is non-shareable
2. **Hold and Wait** — Thread holds one resource while waiting for another
3. **No Preemption** — OS cannot forcibly take resources
4. **Circular Wait** — Circular chain of waiting threads

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention the four Coffman conditions if asked about deadlock. Breaking **any one** prevents deadlock.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔬 Analytical Walkthrough 1: The Classic Producer-Consumer Problem

The **Producer-Consumer problem** is the most-tested synchronization problem in KTU exams. Let us derive its full solution.

### Problem Statement
A producer thread puts items into a shared buffer; a consumer thread removes them. The buffer has limited capacity. The producer must **wait** when the buffer is full; the consumer must **wait** when the buffer is empty.

### Class Structure

```java
class Buffer {
    private int data;
    private boolean available = false;  // true means data is ready to consume
    
    // Step 1: Producer calls this to put data
    public synchronized void put(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (available) {           // While buffer is full...
            wait();                   // ...release lock and wait
        }
        data = value;                 // Put data in buffer
        System.out.println("Produced: " + value);
        available = true;             // Mark buffer as having data
        notify();                     // Wake up the waiting consumer
    }
    
    // Step 2: Consumer calls this to take data
    public synchronized int get() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!available) {          // While buffer is empty...
            wait();                   // ...release lock and wait
        }
        int value = data;             // Take data
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + value);
        available = false;            // Mark buffer as empty
        notify();                     // Wake up the waiting producer
        return value;
    }
}
```

### Why `while` and not `if`?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spurious Wakeups!** A waiting thread can wake up **without** being notified (a JVM-level edge case). Using `while` ensures the thread **re-checks the condition** before proceeding — this is mandatory in production-grade Java code.

### Why `notify()` and not `notifyAll()`?

- `notify()` wakes **one** waiter — efficient when only one counterpart exists.
- `notifyAll()` wakes **all** waiters — safer when multiple producers/consumers compete.

For the simple 1-producer-1-consumer case, `notify()` is sufficient. KTU examiners prefer `notifyAll()` in exam answers for **safety**.

## 🔬 Analytical Walkthrough 2: Thread Priority Demonstration

```java
class PriorityDemo extends Thread {
    public PriorityDemo(String name) {
        super(name);
    }
    
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " (priority " + getPriority() + ") : " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class TestPriority {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PriorityDemo low  = new PriorityDemo("LOW");
        PriorityDemo norm = new PriorityDemo("NORMAL");
        PriorityDemo high = new PriorityDemo("HIGH");
        
        low.setPriority(Thread.MIN_PRIORITY);    // 1
        norm.setPriority(Thread.NORM_PRIORITY);  // 5
        high.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);   // 10
        
        low.start();
        norm.start();
        high.start();
    }
}
```

**Expected (but not guaranteed) output:** `HIGH` thread finishes first, then `NORMAL`, then `LOW`. However, output order is **non-deterministic** because the OS scheduler is free to ignore priority hints.

## 🔬 Analytical Walkthrough 3: `join()` — The Coordination Primitive

```java
class SumTask extends Thread {
    private int[] arr;
    private int sum;
    
    public SumTask(int[] arr) { this.arr = arr; }
    
    public void run() {
        for (int n : arr) sum += n;
    }
    
    public int getSum() { return sum; }
}

public class JoinDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        int[] nums = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
        SumTask t = new SumTask(nums);
        t.start();
        
        t.join();   // Main thread BLOCKS until t dies
        
        System.out.println("Sum = " + t.getSum());
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Sum = 15` — guaranteed. Without `join()`, `getSum()` might be called **before** `run()` completes, printing `0`.

## 📊 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Construct |
|---|---|
| Independent tasks, no shared data | Multiple threads, no synchronization |
| Shared counter / shared state | `synchronized` methods or blocks |
| Producer-Consumer pattern | `wait()` / `notify()` inside `synchronized` |
| One thread depends on another's result | `Thread.join()` |
| Need return value from a task | `Callable<V>` + `Future<V>` |
| Pool of worker threads | `ExecutorService` |
| Ordered execution | `Thread.join()` chain |
| Background daemon services | `setDaemon(true)` |

## 📊 Synchronized vs. `volatile` vs. `Atomic` (Advanced)

| Feature | `synchronized` | `volatile` | `AtomicInteger` etc. |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visibility guarantee | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Atomicity guarantee | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (single variable) |
| Lock acquisition | Yes (blocking) | No (lock-free) | No (CAS-based) |
| Use case | Compound operations | Single flag / reference | Counters, accumulators |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024**: Focus on `synchronized`. `volatile` and `Atomic*` classes may appear in advanced module questions (Module 5) but are mentioned here for context.

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🌀 Diagram 1: Thread Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread()
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : wait for monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() / join() / park()
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / notifyAll() / unpark()
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms)
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires / notify
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() completes
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

```mermaid
    J -->|notified| I
    I -->|No| K[Read data from buffer]

    L --> M[notify Producer]
```

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a process and a thread.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Independent program in execution | Lightweight sub-unit of a process |
| Memory | Separate address space | Shares process memory |
| Creation Cost | Heavy (OS-level) | Lightweight (JVM-level) |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets) | Shared variables (direct) |
| Context Switching | Slower | Faster |

A thread is also called a **lightweight process (LWP)**. Multiple threads within a process share the **heap** and **method area** but have **separate stacks**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the difference between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class?**

**Model Answer:**

- **`start()`** — Creates a **new call stack** and invokes `run()` on that new stack. Each call to `start()` spawns a **new thread**.
- **`run()`** — A normal method. Calling it directly executes the code in the **current thread** (no new thread is created).

If `run()` is called directly, the code executes sequentially, defeating the purpose of multithreading. Each thread can call `start()` **only once**; subsequent calls throw `IllegalThreadStateException`.

---

## 📗 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different ways to create a thread in Java with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is synchronization? Explain the `synchronized` keyword with a code example. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Two Ways to Create a Thread:**

**Way 1: Extending `Thread` class**

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Thread running: " + getName());
    }
}
public class Test1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t = new MyThread();
        t.start();
    }
}
```

**Way 2: Implementing `Runnable` interface** (Preferred)

```java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running in: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}
public class Test2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = new Thread(new MyRunnable(), "Worker");
        t.start();
    }
}
```

**Comparison:**
- `Runnable` is preferred because Java does not support multiple inheritance of classes.
- `Runnable` decouples the **task** from the **thread** mechanism — a cleaner design.

**(b) Synchronization:**

When two or more threads access a shared resource, **race conditions** can occur, leading to inconsistent results. Java provides the `synchronized` keyword to ensure that **only one thread** can access a critical section at a time.

**Synchronized Method Example:**

```java
class Table {
    synchronized void printTable(int n) {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n * i));
            try { Thread.sleep(200); } 
            catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

class MyThread1 extends Thread {
    Table t;
    MyThread1(Table t) { this.t = t; }
    public void run() { t.printTable(5); }
}

class MyThread2 extends Thread {
    Table t;
    MyThread2(Table t) { this.t = t; }
    public void run() { t.printTable(100); }
}

public class SyncDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Table obj = new Table();
        MyThread1 t1 = new MyThread1(obj);
        MyThread2 t2 = new MyThread2(obj);
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Output:** Tables 5 and 100 are printed **completely sequentially** — one thread's `printTable` finishes before the other starts. This is the **mutual exclusion** guarantee of `synchronized`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain inter-thread communication in Java using `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` with a Producer-Consumer example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is deadlock? Explain the necessary conditions for deadlock to occur. How can it be prevented? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Inter-Thread Communication:**

Java provides three methods (inherited from `Object`) for inter-thread communication:
- `wait()` — Causes the current thread to release the lock and wait until another thread invokes `notify()` on the same object.
- `notify()` — Wakes up **one** thread that is waiting on the same object's monitor.
- `notifyAll()` — Wakes up **all** threads waiting on the same object's monitor.

**Producer-Consumer Example:**

```java
class SharedBuffer {
    private int item;
    private boolean available = false;
    
    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (available) {
            wait();   // Buffer full — producer waits
        }
        item = value;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + item);
        available = true;
        notify();     // Wake up the consumer
    }
    
    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!available) {
            wait();   // Buffer empty — consumer waits
        }
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + item);
        available = false;
        notify();     // Wake up the producer
        return item;
    }
}
```

**Key Rule:** `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` must be called from within a `synchronized` context on the same monitor object, otherwise `IllegalMonitorStateException` is thrown.

**(b) Deadlock:**

**Definition:** A deadlock is a situation where two or more threads are **blocked forever**, each waiting for a lock that the other thread holds.

**Necessary Conditions (Coffman Conditions):**

1. **Mutual Exclusion** — At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode.
2. **Hold and Wait** — A thread holding a resource waits for another resource held by another thread.
3. **No Preemption** — Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread; they must be released voluntarily.
4. **Circular Wait** — A circular chain of threads exists, where each thread holds a resource the next thread in the chain is waiting for.

**Example of Deadlock:**

```java
class A {
    synchronized void methodA(B b) {
        System.out.println("Thread-1 holds A, waiting for B");
        b.last();
    }
    synchronized void last() {
        System.out.println("Inside A.last()");
    }
}
class B {
    synchronized void methodB(A a) {
        System.out.println("Thread-2 holds B, waiting for A");
        a.last();
    }
    synchronized void last() {
        System.out.println("Inside B.last()");
    }
}
// Both threads call methodA and methodB simultaneously → DEADLOCK
```

**Prevention Strategies:**

- **Avoid nested locks** — never acquire more than one lock at a time.
- **Use `tryLock()`** with timeout (from `java.util.concurrent.locks.Lock`).
- **Lock ordering** — always acquire locks in a **fixed global order**.
- **Use higher-level concurrency utilities** — `Semaphore`, `Exchanger`, `ConcurrentHashMap`.
- **Reduce lock scope** — synchronize the smallest possible code block.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `start()` vs `run()`** — Writing `t.run()` instead of `t.start()` in code = **2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Missing the `throws InterruptedException`** declaration on `wait()`/`sleep()`/`join()` = **1-2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Not using `synchronized` block** in `wait()`/`notify()` examples = `IllegalMonitorStateException` runtime error = **major deduction**.
> 4. **Confusing `notify()` and `notifyAll()`** — only one thread is woken by `notify()`; specifying which one is non-deterministic.
> 5. **Using `if` instead of `while`** in `wait()` predicate checks — fails on spurious wakeups = **design flaw deduction**.
> 6. **Ignoring priority is a hint, not a guarantee** — students often write "high-priority thread runs first" — this is **wrong**, and examiners mark it down.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist
>
> ✅ A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution; Java supports multithreading via `Thread` class and `Runnable` interface.
>
> ✅ Two ways to create a thread: **extend `Thread`** OR **implement `Runnable`** (preferred).
>
> ✅ **`start()` spawns a new thread**; **`run()` is just a method call** (does not create a thread).
>
> ✅ Thread lifecycle states: **NEW → RUNNABLE → BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED**.
>
> ✅ `sleep()` does **NOT** release the lock; `wait()` **DOES** release the lock.
>
> ✅ `synchronized` ensures **mutual exclusion**; it works on **object monitor locks**.
>
> ✅ `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` are methods of `java.lang.Object`, not `Thread` — and must be called inside `synchronized` blocks.
>
> ✅ `join()` makes the current thread **wait** for the target thread to finish.
>
> ✅ `yield()` is a **hint** to the scheduler; it may be ignored.
>
> ✅ `volatile` ensures **visibility** of changes across threads but does **NOT** guarantee atomicity.
>
> ✅ **Priority** (1-10) is a hint — `MIN_PRIORITY=1`, `NORM_PRIORITY=5`, `MAX_PRIORITY=10`.
>
> ✅ **Deadlock** requires all four **Coffman conditions**; break any one to prevent it.
>
> ✅ **Race condition** = multiple threads accessing shared mutable state without synchronization.
>
> ✅ **Daemon thread** = background service thread (e.g., `gc`); JVM exits when only daemons remain.
>
> ✅ Modern Java favors **`ExecutorService` + `Callable<V>` + `Future<V>`** over raw `Thread` objects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concurrency in Functional Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concurrency in Functional Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧠 Module 4: Concurrency in Functional Languages

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Concurrency in Functional Languages** is a programming paradigm in which multiple computational processes (threads, processes, actors, or tasks) are expressed, evaluated, and executed simultaneously, exploiting the **referential transparency**, **immutability**, and **absence of side effects** that are intrinsic to purely functional programming models.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is studied as a **paradigm shift** away from the shared-mutable-state thread model of imperative languages (Java, C++, Python) toward **message-passing**, **asynchronous evaluation**, and **deterministic parallelism** models natively supported by languages like **Haskell**, **Erlang/Elixir**, **Clojure**, **F#**, and **Scala**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The fundamental reason functional languages excel at concurrency is that **immutable data cannot be corrupted by a data race** — since a value, once created, never changes, multiple threads can safely read it without locks, semaphores, or mutexes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Read-Only Library"

Imagine two contrasting libraries:

| Library Type | Real-World Analogy | Implication for Concurrency |
|---|---|---|
| **Imperative Library** | Books can be edited, defaced, and re-shelved in place. Only one librarian may safely edit a book at a time — others must wait. | Requires **locks**, **mutexes**, and **synchronization** to prevent corruption. |
| **Functional Library** | Books are **read-only**. If a librarian wants to "change" a book, they must **photocopy the original**, edit the copy, and shelve the new version. | Multiple librarians can work **simultaneously** without conflict — the original is never touched. |

This analogy captures the essence of functional concurrency: because **no shared state is mutated**, the classical concurrency bugs (*race conditions*, *deadlocks*, *livelocks*) **cannot exist** in the first place.

## 1.3 Why Functional Languages Are Naturally Concurrent

The four pillars enabling concurrency in FP are:

1. **Immutability** — Values, once bound, are never altered. Threads read consistent snapshots.
2. **Purity / Referential Transparency** — `f(x)` always returns the same `y` for the same `x`, regardless of execution context, time, or thread.
3. **First-Class Functions** — Functions can be passed as values, enabling higher-order combinators like `map`, `parMap`, `filter`, `reduce`, which are trivially parallelizable.
4. **Lazy Evaluation** — Computation is deferred until needed, allowing the runtime to decide *what* to evaluate in parallel.

> [!TIP]
> Think of referential transparency as a **mathematical function** $f: X \to Y$. In mathematics, $f(2) = 4$ today, tomorrow, and on any thread. This is precisely what gives FP its concurrency-friendly semantics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 The Concurrency Hierarchy in FP

Functional languages expose concurrency at **multiple abstraction levels**:

```
LEVEL 4 ── Domain-Specific: MapReduce, Reactive Streams, Dataflow
LEVEL 3 ── Software Transactional Memory (STM), Refs, Atoms
LEVEL 2 ── Message Passing (Actors, Channels, Mailboxes)
LEVEL 1 ── Futures & Promises (speculative parallelism)
LEVEL 0 ── Native OS Threads (forkIO, Thread.sleep)
```

### 2.1.2 Core Concurrency Models in Functional Languages

| Model | Origin Language | Communication Mechanism | Synchronization | State Model |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Actor Model** | Erlang | Asynchronous message passing to mailboxes | Mailbox queues | Encapsulated, per-actor |
| **STM** | Haskell (GHC) | Atomic composable transactions | Optimistic + rollback | Shared transactional variables (TVars) |
| **Futures / Promises** | F#, Scala, Clojure | Speculative evaluation, read-once placeholders | Implicit on `get` / `await` | Immutable result values |
| **Refs / Atoms** | Clojure | Synchronous / asynchronous coordinated refs | Software transactional memory | Identity-separated values |
| **Dataflow / LVars** | Haskell (research) | Write-once, threshold-triggered reads | Happens-before DAG | Single-writer, multi-reader |
| **Fork/Join** | Haskell (`par`/`pseq`) | Implicit via lazy evaluation graph | None required | Strict immutable heap |

### 2.1.3 The Amdahl's Law Constraint

Even in the best FP environment, **parallelism has a theoretical ceiling**. Amdahl's Law bounds the achievable speedup $S$ as:

$$ S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}} $$

where $P$ is the **parallel fraction** of the program and $N$ is the number of processors. The maximum theoretical speedup is:

$$ S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - P} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even if 95% of a program is parallelizable ($P = 0.95$), the **maximum speedup is capped at 20×**, no matter how many processors you throw at it. This is why FP's *inherent* ability to expose parallelism at the language level is so valuable.

## 2.2 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Unit / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S(N)$ | Speedup with $N$ processors | Dimensionless ratio | $\frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}}$ |
| $P$ | Parallelizable fraction | $0 \le P \le 1$ | Estimated via profiling |
| $N$ | Number of processors / cores | Integer $\ge 1$ | Often $\le$ core count |
| $S_{\max}$ | Asymptotic max speedup | $\frac{1}{1-P}$ | Limit as $N \to \infty$ |
| $E$ | Parallel efficiency | $\frac{S(N)}{N}$ | Ideal $E = 1$, realistic $E < 1$ |
| $\vert T \vert$ | Cardinality of a transactional log | Integer | Used in STM conflict tracking |
| $\tau$ | Granularity of a task (work) | Time units | Smaller $\tau$ → better load balancing |

> [!TIP]
> **Avoid using the `\|` character inside any KTU table** — LaTeX pipes interfere with markdown rendering. The cheat sheet above uses `\vert` for mathematical absolute value.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case | Why FP Concurrency Wins |
|---|---|---|
| **Telecommunications** | Ericsson AXD 301 switch (Erlang) | 9-nines availability, hot code swapping |
| **FinTech** | WhatsApp backend (Erlang) | Millions of concurrent connections per node |
| **Big Data** | Apache Storm, Twitter pipelines (Clojure) | STM composes cleanly with streaming data |
| **Scientific Computing** | Parallel Haskell (`parMap`) | Deterministic results crucial for reproducibility |
| **Compilers / DSLs** | Parallel parsing, type inference | Pure AST transformations are embarrassingly parallel |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law for FP Concurrency

We will derive the speedup formula from first principles, step by step.

### Step 1 — Partition the Total Execution Time

Let the total serial execution time of a program be $T = 1$ (normalized unit). Split it into:

- **Serial fraction:** $T_s = (1 - P) \cdot T$
- **Parallel fraction:** $T_p = P \cdot T$

where $P$ is the proportion that *can* be parallelized and $(1 - P)$ is the strictly sequential portion (e.g., I/O setup, final aggregation).

### Step 2 — Apply Parallelism to the Parallel Fraction

When $N$ processors work on the parallel portion simultaneously, each processor handles $\frac{P}{N}$ of the work. The parallel time becomes $\frac{P}{N}$.

### Step 3 — Sum the New Total Execution Time

The total parallel execution time $T_{\text{parallel}}$ is the sum of the (unchanged) serial portion and the (now-distributed) parallel portion:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}
$$

### Step 4 — Define Speedup

Speedup $S(N)$ is the ratio of the original time to the parallel time:

$$
S(N) = \frac{T}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Asymptotic Limit

As $N \to \infty$, the term $\frac{P}{N} \to 0$:

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}} = \frac{1}{1 - P}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **even pure functional programs** (which can parallelize 99% of their work) still cannot exceed a $100\times$ speedup — the last 1% serial work dominates.

### Step 3.6 — Worked Example: Parallel `map` in Haskell

Consider a list of 1,000,000 elements processed by a pure function. Let the parallel fraction be $P = 0.98$ (only list traversal setup is serial). On $N = 8$ cores:

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.98) + \frac{0.98}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.02 + 0.1225} = \frac{1}{0.1425} \approx 7.02
$$

The **parallel efficiency** is:

$$
E = \frac{S(8)}{8} = \frac{7.02}{8} \approx 0.878
$$

So we achieve $\approx 88\%$ efficiency — excellent for a functional workload.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Concurrency Primitives

The matrix below maps each FP concurrency primitive to its **safety guarantees**, **performance characteristics**, and **failure modes**.

| Primitive | Language | Safety Guarantee | Best Use Case | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `spawn` / Actor | Erlang | No shared state → no race conditions | Long-running services, telecom | Mailbox overflow |
| `forkIO` + `MVar` | Haskell | Type-checked exclusivity | Producer-consumer pipelines | `MVar` deadlock |
| `STM` (`atomically`) | Haskell | Atomicity + composability | Multi-step coordinated updates | Starvation under contention |
| `future { }` | Scala / F# | Read-once enforcement | Web requests, async I/O | Forgetting to `await` |
| `ref` / `dosync` | Clojure | Snapshot isolation | Shared application state | Long-running transactions |
| `Par` monad | Haskell | Deterministic result | Parallel `map`, `filter`, `fold` | Cannot do I/O |
| `Promise` | F# | Single-assignment | UI responsiveness | Multiple `Set` calls |

## 3.3 Formal Property: Referential Transparency ⇒ Parallel-Safe Evaluation

A function $f$ is **referentially transparent** if for any expression $E$ and subexpression $E'$, replacing $E$ by $E'$ (and vice versa) does not change the program's meaning.

Formally, for any context $C[\,\cdot\,]$:

$$
\forall C.\; C[E] \equiv C[E'] \quad \text{whenever} \quad E \equiv E'
$$

This is precisely the property that allows the **runtime to evaluate $E$ in parallel** with the rest of the program — because $E$'s result cannot depend on, nor be modified by, the surrounding context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important theoretical result for Module 4: **Referential transparency is the formal justification for safe automatic parallelization in functional languages.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Actor Model (Erlang-Style) — Block Architecture

This diagram shows how isolated **actors** communicate purely via **asynchronous messages**, with no shared mutable state.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Actor1 CounterService] -- msg1 increment --> MB1[Mailbox1]
    MB1 -- dequeue --> A1
    A1 -- msg2 get --> MB2[Mailbox2]
    A2[Actor2 Logger] -- poll --> MB2
    A2 -- msg3 log --> MB3[Mailbox3]
    A3[Actor3 Supervisor] -- monitor --> A1
    A3 -- restart --> A1
    A1 -- reply --> A2
    A2 -- ack --> A3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangle is an isolated process. Arrows represent asynchronous message sends. Mailboxes (the `MBx` nodes) decouple the sender from the receiver, allowing true concurrency.

## 4.2 Software Transactional Memory (STM) Transaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Thread starts transaction]) --> Read1[Read TVar1 snapshot]
    Read1 --> Read2[Read TVar2 snapshot]
    Read2 --> Read3[Read TVar3 snapshot]
    Read3 --> Decide{Conflict detected}
    Decide -- No --> Commit[Atomically write all TVars]
    Decide -- Yes --> Retry[Rollback and retry]
    Retry --> Read1
    Commit --> End([Transaction complete])
```

> **Reading the diagram:** All reads happen optimistically on a snapshot. If the commit would violate consistency, the transaction retries automatically — no locks, no manual rollback code.

## 4.3 Fork–Join Parallelism in Haskell (Strategy + Eval)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Main([Main Expression]) --> Eval[Evaluate via parStrategy]
    Eval --> Fork1[Fork Spark A]
    Eval --> Fork2[Fork Spark B]
    Eval --> Fork3[Fork Spark C]
    Fork1 --> Core1[CPU Core 1]
    Fork2 --> Core2[CPU Core 2]
    Fork3 --> Core3[CPU Core 3]
    Core1 --> Join[Join Barrier rseq]
    Core2 --> Join
    Core3 --> Join
    Join --> Result([Combined Result])
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The runtime uses a *spark pool* to schedule thunks across available cores, then `rseq`/`pseq` enforces evaluation order at the join point. This is the foundation of `parMap`, `parTraversable`, and `Eval` monad strategies.

## 4.4 Comparison: Imperative vs Functional Concurrency Mental Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IMP[Imperative Model]
        T1[Thread 1] -- write --> SH[Shared Mutable Memory]
        T2[Thread 2] -- write --> SH
        T3[Thread 3] -- write --> SH
        SH -- lock contention --> DEAD[Deadlock risk]
    end
    subgraph FP[Functional Model]
        A1[Actor1] -- msg --> MB1[Mailbox1]
        A2[Actor2] -- msg --> MB2[Mailbox2]
        A3[Actor3] -- msg --> MB3[Mailbox3]
        MB1 --> A1
        MB2 --> A2
        MB3 --> A3
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The imperative side shows a single shared memory bus with concurrent writes — the source of all race conditions. The functional side shows three **isolated mailboxes** with one-way message flows — no shared memory, no races.

## 4.5 Functional Pipeline: Parallel `map` Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input([Input List 1 to N]) --> Chunk1[Chunk 1 of N/4]
    Input --> Chunk2[Chunk 2 of N/4]
    Input --> Chunk3[Chunk 3 of N/4]
    Input --> Chunk4[Chunk 4 of N/4]
    Chunk1 --> Core1[Core 1 applies f]
    Chunk2 --> Core2[Core 2 applies f]
    Chunk3 --> Core3[Core 3 applies f]
    Chunk4 --> Core4[Core 4 applies f]
    Core1 --> Merge[Preserve order merge]
    Core2 --> Merge
    Core3 --> Merge
    Core4 --> Merge
    Merge --> Output([Output List f a1 ... f aN])
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is `parMap` in action. The list is *chunked*, each chunk is mapped in parallel, and the results are merged in original order to preserve referential transparency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] (3 Marks)**

**Define concurrency in the context of functional programming languages. List two properties of functional languages that facilitate concurrency.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition:** Concurrency in functional programming is the execution of multiple computational tasks simultaneously, where the language's semantics — built on **immutability**, **referential transparency**, and **pure functions** — eliminate the need for explicit locks and synchronization primitives.

**Two enabling properties:**

1. **Immutability of Data:** Once a value is created, it cannot be modified. This means multiple threads can read the same value simultaneously without the risk of a data race or inconsistent state.

2. **Referential Transparency:** A function always produces the same output for the same input, irrespective of the order or context in which it is called. This allows the runtime to safely parallelize subexpressions, reorder evaluations, and cache results.

> **Examiner's Note:** A complete answer must include *both* the definition and *two clearly justified* properties. Vague statements like "FP is fast" score zero.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper] (3 Marks)**

**Compare the Actor Model (used in Erlang) with the STM Model (used in Haskell). Mention one advantage of each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Actor Model (Erlang) | STM Model (Haskell) |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication** | Asynchronous message passing via mailboxes | Shared transactional variables (`TVar`s) |
| **State Location** | Encapsulated inside each actor | Shared, but accessed only inside transactions |
| **Synchronization** | Implicit (message arrival) | Explicit (`atomically` block) |
| **Failure Handling** | Built-in supervisor trees | Application-level retry logic |

- **Advantage of Actor Model:** Naturally fault-tolerant — actors can be supervised and restarted independently, enabling hot code swapping and 9-nines availability in telecom systems.
- **Advantage of STM Model:** Composable — multiple STM transactions can be combined into larger atomic blocks without manual locking, making coordinated updates safe and concise.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For 14-mark questions, students commonly lose marks by: (1) writing only theory without diagrams, (2) skipping the *formal* definition before giving examples, (3) not stating assumptions, and (4) failing to mention **referential transparency** by name. Always anchor your answers to the FP-specific concepts.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice A] (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the Actor Model of concurrency as used in Erlang. With a block diagram, describe message passing, mailboxes, and the supervision tree. Discuss how isolation of state prevents race conditions.**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Conceptual Foundation (2 marks):**
The Actor Model, as adopted by Erlang (designed at Ericsson for telecom), treats every concurrent unit as an **actor** — a lightweight, isolated process with:
- Its own private, **immutable** state.
- A unique **mailbox** (inbound message queue).
- The ability to send messages to other actors' mailboxes.
- The ability to spawn new actors.

**2. Message Passing & Mailboxes (2 marks):**
When Actor $A$ sends a message to Actor $B$ via `B ! {msg, data}`, the message is *enqueued* in $B$'s mailbox. $B$ processes one message at a time in a `receive` block. Because the message is *copied* (not shared by reference), there is no aliasing, and therefore no data race.

**3. Supervision Tree (2 marks):**
Erlang processes are organized hierarchically: a *supervisor* process monitors *worker* processes. If a worker crashes, the supervisor can restart it (`one_for_one`, `one_for_all`, or `rest_for_one` strategies). This makes systems self-healing.

**4. Race Condition Prevention (1 mark):**
Since each actor's state is **private and never shared**, and messages are **immutable copies**, the classical race condition (concurrent unsynchronized writes to shared memory) **cannot occur**.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1)** — refer to that diagram in the answer sheet, labeled clearly.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Derive Amdahl's Law. A program has 90% parallelizable code. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable. Also find the speedup when run on 16 processors.**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1:** Let $P = 0.90$ and the total serial time $T = 1$.

**Step 2:** Apply Amdahl's Law formula:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step 3 (Maximum speedup, $N \to \infty$):**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10
$$

**Step 4 (Speedup on $N = 16$ processors):**

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625} \approx 6.4
$$

**Step 5 (Parallel efficiency):**

$$
E = \frac{S(16)}{16} = \frac{6.4}{16} = 0.40 \quad \text{i.e., 40\% efficiency}
$$

**Conclusion:** The theoretical maximum speedup is $10\times$ regardless of how many processors are added, and on 16 cores we achieve $\approx 6.4\times$ with only 40% efficiency — illustrating the diminishing returns of parallelism in any language, functional or otherwise.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Choice B] (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Describe Software Transactional Memory (STM) as implemented in Haskell. Explain the `atomically`, `retry`, and `orElse` combinators with their roles. How does STM differ from lock-based synchronization?**

#### Model Solution:

**1. STM Concept (2 marks):**
STM is a concurrency mechanism in which shared variables (`TVar`s) are modified inside **atomic blocks**. If two transactions try to modify the same `TVar` simultaneously, the runtime detects the conflict and **automatically retries** one of them — no explicit locks required.

**2. Core Combinators (3 marks):**

| Combinator | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `atomically :: STM a -> IO a` | Executes a transaction atomically; retries on conflict. |
| `retry :: STM a` | Aborts the current transaction; re-runs when any watched `TVar` changes. |
| `orElse :: STM a -> STM a -> STM a` | Tries the first transaction; if it calls `retry`, runs the second as a fallback. |
| `readTVar :: TVar a -> STM a` | Reads a transactional variable within a transaction. |
| `writeTVar :: TVar a -> a -> STM ()` | Writes to a transactional variable within a transaction. |

**3. STM vs Locks (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Lock-Based (Java/C++) | STM (Haskell) |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Programmer-controlled | Transactional (compiler-assisted) |
| **Deadlock Risk** | High (cyclic lock acquisition) | None (no locks held) |
| **Composability** | Poor (manual lock ordering) | Excellent (transactions compose via `atomically`) |
| **Performance** | Fast in low contention | May retry under high contention |

**Conclusion:** STM trades *predictable performance* for *correctness by construction* — exactly the trade-off functional languages are designed to make.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Discuss the role of `par`, `pseq`, and the `Eval` monad in Haskell's parallel evaluation strategy. Show how `parMap` can be used to parallelize a list operation. State the conditions under which this parallelism is "free" (i.e., requires no synchronization).**

#### Model Solution:

**1. The `par` and `pseq` Combinators (2 marks):**
- `par :: a -> b -> b` — *suggests* that its first argument be evaluated in parallel with the second. It is a *hint*, not a command.
- `pseq :: a -> b -> b` — like `seq`, but evaluates the first argument to **weak head normal form** (WHNF) **in parallel** before returning the second.

**2. The `Eval` Monad and Strategies (2 marks):**
The `Control.Parallel.Strategies` module provides a monad `Eval` and combinators like `rpar`, `rseq`, `using`, and `parMap`. The monadic structure lets the programmer **separate algorithmic description from parallelism annotation**, preserving referential transparency.

**3. `parMap` Example (1 mark):**
The function `parMap :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]` applies the given function to each list element *in parallel* using sparks from GHC's scheduler.

**4. Conditions for "Free" Parallelism (2 marks):**
Parallelism in Haskell is automatically safe (no locks, no race conditions) **iff**:
- The mapped function $f$ is **pure** (no I/O, no state).
- The list elements are **immutable**.
- No `TVar`, `MVar`, or `IORef` is shared across sparks.

When all three conditions hold, GHC's runtime can distribute work across cores with **zero synchronization overhead**, and the result is **deterministic** — exactly the guarantee KTU expects students to identify.

> **Cross-reference:** The diagram in Section 4.3 illustrates this Fork–Join flow.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Concurrency in Functional Languages**

- **Core enablers of FP concurrency:** Immutability, Referential Transparency, First-Class Functions, Lazy Evaluation.
- **Actor Model (Erlang):** Isolated processes, message passing via mailboxes, supervision trees for fault tolerance. **No shared state.**
- **STM (Haskell):** Composability via `atomically`, automatic retry, `TVar` for shared data, **no manual locks**.
- **Futures / Promises (F#, Scala, Clojure):** Speculative evaluation, single-read placeholders, `await`/`deref` for synchronization.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$, $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-P}$. Know how to plug in $P = 0.9, 0.95, 0.99$.
- **Parallel efficiency:** $E = S(N)/N$. Ideal $E = 1$, realistic $E \ll 1$.
- **`parMap`, `parTraverseable`, `Eval` monad** are the standard Haskell parallelism toolkit.
- **Race conditions** in FP arise only when *purity is violated* (e.g., using `IORef` or `unsafePerformIO`).
- **KTU-favourite keywords to memorize:** `atomically`, `forkIO`, `MVar`, `TVar`, `par`, `pseq`, `rpar`, `rseq`, `actor`, `mailbox`, `supervisor`, `referential transparency`, `immutability`, `mapreduce`, `dataflow`.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers — KTU awards dedicated marks for figures.
- **Always state Amdahl's Law formula and its limit** when discussing speedup.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — STATEMENT LEVEL CONCURRENCY

## 1.1 Formal Definition & Scope

**Statement Level Concurrency (SLC)** is the lowest granularity form of parallel execution in which individual programming statements (or sequences of statements grouped by compiler directives / hardware logic) are identified, scheduled, and executed in parallel. Unlike *task-level* or *process-level* concurrency (which deals with coarse units such as procedures, loops, or subroutines), SLC operates at the granularity of:

- Individual arithmetic/logic expressions
- Atomic primitive operations
- Independent statements within a basic block
- Vectorized statements operating on data arrays (SIMD/SIMT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Statement-level concurrency refers to the hardware and software mechanisms that allow multiple machine-level statements (instructions) to be issued, executed, or completed in overlapping time intervals, exploiting ILP (Instruction-Level Parallelism), DLP (Data-Level Parallelism), and hardware multi-issue pipelines.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic bridges three pillars:

| Pillar | Mechanism | Hardware/Software Support |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** | Multiple instructions issued per cycle | Pipelining, superscalar, VLIW |
| **Data-Level Parallelism (DLP)** | Same operation on multiple data | SIMD, SIMT, vector units |
| **Thread/Task Granularity** | Concurrent execution of independent statements | Atomic ops, lock-free programming |

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Kitchen Brigade:**
> Imagine a head chef (the CPU) preparing a five-course meal. In a *sequential* kitchen, the chef chops onions → boils water → plates the salad → tastes the soup — one action at a time. In a *statement-level concurrent* kitchen, the chef issues instructions simultaneously: *"Sous-chop onions, sauce-simmer broth, intern-plate salad, sommelier-pour wine"* — each sub-task (a "statement") runs in parallel because they don't depend on each other's output. The key insight: **parallelism is only safe when the statements have no data dependency (no data flow between them).** If the sauce needs the chopped onions first, those two statements cannot be reordered — they form a *dependency chain*.

**Key Physical/Architectural Constants** in modern statement-level parallel hardware:

- **Superscalar issue rate:** typically **2–6 instructions per cycle**
- **SIMD lane width:** **128-bit (SSE)**, **256-bit (AVX/AVX2)**, **512-bit (AVX-512)**
- **GPU SIMT warp size:** **32 threads** (NVIDIA) / **64 threads** (AMD)
- **Out-of-Order window (ROB):** typically **128–512 entries**
- **VLIW issue slots:** **4–8 issue slots per long instruction word**
- **Hazard detection latency:** **1–3 clock cycles**

---

## 1.2 Why Statement-Level Concurrency Matters (Engineering Context)

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** | Weather simulation, molecular dynamics — vectorized loops over millions of grid points |
| **Machine Learning / AI** | Tensor operations in CNNs use SIMD/SIMT for matrix multiplies |
| **Signal Processing** | FFT, FIR filters executed on vector units in DSPs |
| **Database Systems** | Lock-free atomic updates on concurrent index structures |
| **Embedded Real-Time Systems** | Lock-free interrupt handlers with atomic flag manipulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners expect students to differentiate **statement-level**, **block-level**, and **loop-level** concurrency. Always mention the *dependency graph* and the *Bernstein conditions* when defining concurrency safety.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Laws: Bernstein's Conditions

For any two statements (or processes) **S1** and **S2** to execute concurrently *safely*, the following **Bernstein's Conditions (1966)** must hold:

| Condition | Formal Definition | Meaning in English |
|---|---|---|
| **Flow Independence** | $I_1 \cap O_2 = \emptyset$ | S1 does not read a value written by S2 |
| **Anti Independence** | $O_1 \cap I_2 = \emptyset$ | S2 does not read a value written by S1 |
| **Output Independence** | $O_1 \cap O_2 = \emptyset$ | S1 and S2 do not write to the same location |

Where:
- $I_i$ = set of input (read) variables of statement $S_i$
- $O_i$ = set of output (written) variables of statement $S_i$

**If all three hold, $S_1 \parallel S_2$ is *safe* (serializable).**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Dependency Types (Anti, Flow, Output)

The Bernstein framework above translates into three compiler-detected dependencies:

### 2.1.1 True Data Dependency (RAW — Read After Write)
$$S_1: \;\; a = b + c$$
$$S_2: \;\; d = a \times 2$$

**S2 reads `a` after S1 writes `a`.** S2 *must* wait for S1. This is the *only* unbreakable dependency — it cannot be parallelized.

### 2.1.2 Antidependency (WAR — Write After Read)
$$S_1: \;\; a = b + c$$
$$S_2: \;\; b = d + 1$$

**S2 writes `b` after S1 reads `b`.** Solvable via **register renaming** in hardware.

### 2.1.3 Output Dependency (WAW — Write After Write)
$$S_1: \;\; a = 5$$
$$S_2: \;\; a = 7$$

Both write the same location. Solvable via **register renaming**.

### 2.1.4 Input Dependency (RAR — Read After Read)
$$S_1: \;\; a = b$$
$$S_2: \;\; c = b$$

Both read `b`. **Trivially parallelizable** — no hazard.

---

## 2.2 Hardware Mechanisms for SLC

### 2.2.1 Pipelining
A pipeline breaks statement execution into **k stages** (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Write-Back). The theoretical speedup is:

$$S_{\text{pipeline}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{T_{\text{sequential}}}{T_{\text{pipelined}}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot \tau}{(n + k - 1) \cdot \tau} \to k$$

Where $n$ = number of instructions, $k$ = pipeline depth, $\tau$ = clock cycle time.

### 2.2.2 Superscalar Execution
Multiple execution units operate in parallel. **Issue rate $r$** instructions per cycle:
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{superscalar}} = \frac{1}{r} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{stall\_CPI}$$

### 2.2.3 VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word)
Compiler packs multiple operations into one long instruction word (no runtime hazard detection):
$$\text{Instruction Width} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \text{op}_i + \text{immediate fields}$$

### 2.2.4 SIMD / Vector Processing
Single instruction operates on $N$ data elements simultaneously:
$$T_{\text{SIMD}} = \frac{n}{N} \times T_{\text{op}}, \quad S_{\text{SIMD}} = N \;\; (\text{ideal})$$

### 2.2.5 SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Thread)
A **warp** of $W$ threads executes the same statement on different data:
$$\text{Warp size} = 32 \;\; (\text{NVIDIA}), \;\; 64 \;\; (\text{AMD})$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bar symbols inside table cells have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent Markdown table parser breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amdahl's Law (Speedup limit) | $S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{N}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = parallel units |
| 2 | Pipeline Speedup (ideal) | $S = k$ | $k$ = number of stages |
| 3 | Pipeline Throughput | $T_p = \frac{1}{\tau \cdot (1 + \frac{k-1}{n})}$ | $\tau$ = cycle time |
| 4 | CPI (Superscalar) | $\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{stalls} + n}{n \cdot r}$ | $r$ = issue rate |
| 5 | SIMD Speedup (ideal) | $S = N_{\text{lanes}}$ | $N$ = vector lane count |
| 6 | SIMT Occupancy | $\text{Occ} = \frac{\text{Regs}_{\text{SM}}}{\text{Regs}_{\text{thread}} \cdot W}$ | $W$ = warp size |
| 7 | Bernstein Set Union | $C(S_1 \parallel S_2) \iff (I_1 \cap O_2) \cup (O_1 \cap I_2) \cup (O_1 \cap O_2) = \emptyset$ | Set-theoretic condition |
| 8 | VLIW Bundle Size | $L = r \times (\text{op} + \text{imm})$ bits | $r$ = parallel ops per word |
| 9 | Data Parallelism Ratio | $D_p = \frac{\text{parallel ops}}{\text{total ops}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le D_p \le 1$ |
| 10 | Gustafson's Law (Scaled) | $S = N - f \cdot (N - 1)$ | Refines Amdahl for big-data problems |
| 11 | Roofline Bound (GFLOP/s) | $P = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot I)$ | $\pi$ = peak perf, $\beta$ = bandwidth, $I$ = intensity |
| 12 | Instruction Issue Rate | $r = \frac{\text{Issued Instructions}}{\text{Cycles}}$ | $r > 1$ implies multi-issue |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Hardware System | SLC Mechanism | Performance Gain |
|---|---|---|
| **Intel Core i9 (Alder Lake)** | Out-of-order superscalar + AVX-512 | ~3–8× on vector workloads |
| **NVIDIA A100 GPU** | SIMT, 108 SMs, 64 warps/SM | ~19.5 TFLOPs FP32 |
| **Qualcomm Snapdragon** | VLIW DSP (Hexagon) | Low-power audio/codec |
| **Apple M-series (AMX)** | Matrix coprocessor (2D SIMD) | ~10 TFLOPS on M2 Max |
| **Cray-1 (historical)** | Vector pipeline | Supercomputer era SLC |

---

## 2.5 Software Constructs for Statement-Level Concurrency

### 2.5.1 OpenMP (Industry-Standard Pragmas)
```c
#pragma omp parallel for
for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    A[i] = B[i] * C[i];   // Each statement is data-parallel
}
```

### 2.5.2 OpenACC Directives
```c
#pragma acc parallel loop
for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    y[i] = a * x[i] + y[i];   // SAXPY kernel — pure statement-level SIMD
}
```

### 2.5.3 CUDA (Thread-Level Statement Concurrency via SIMT)
```cpp
__global__ void saxpy(float* x, float* y, float a, int n) {
    int i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;  // SLC: this statement executes in parallel across all threads
    if (i < n) y[i] = a * x[i] + y[i];
}
```

### 2.5.4 Atomic Operations (Lock-Free SLC)
```cpp
std::atomic<int> counter;
counter.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);  // SLC-safe statement
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Theory Emphasis:** When writing theory answers, *always* tie the software directive back to the **dependency analysis** and **Bernstein conditions** that justify its safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Amdahl's Law Applied to Statement-Level Concurrency

**Problem Setup:** A program contains a fraction $f$ of statements that are parallelizable (data-independent, satisfy Bernstein), and the remaining fraction $(1 - f)$ is inherently sequential (data-dependent chain). Suppose we have $N$ parallel execution units (cores/lanes/threads).

**Step 1 — Sequential Execution Time (baseline):**
$$T_{\text{seq}} = T_{\text{seq,parallel}} + T_{\text{seq,serial}}$$

Normalize to $T_{\text{seq}} = 1$ unit (fractional basis).

**Step 2 — Parallel Execution Time:**
The serial portion $(1 - f)$ takes $(1 - f) \times T_{\text{seq}}$ regardless of $N$.
The parallel portion $f$ is divided across $N$ units, taking $\frac{f}{N} \times T_{\text{seq}}$.

$$T_{\text{par}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_{\text{seq}} + \frac{f}{N} \cdot T_{\text{seq}}$$

**Step 3 — Speedup Definition:**
$$S(N) = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{par}}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Limit as $N \to \infty$ (Maximum Possible Speedup):**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{N}} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example for KTU Board Exam:**
Let $f = 0.9$ (90% parallelizable) and $N = 16$ cores.
$$S(16) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \frac{0.9}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625} \approx 6.4$$

Maximum theoretical speedup: $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10\times$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Pipeline CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)

For a $k$-stage pipeline, ideal CPI = 1. Real CPI accounts for hazards:

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{real}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Hazard CPI}$$

Where:
$$\text{Hazard CPI} = \sum_{h \in \{\text{struct, data, control}\}} P_h \cdot L_h$$

- $P_h$ = probability of hazard $h$ occurring
- $L_h$ = latency penalty in cycles for hazard $h$

**Derivation for structural hazard (single memory port):**
If 30% of instructions are memory loads and the pipeline has a single memory port:
$$L_{\text{struct}} = 1 \text{ cycle (stall)}, \quad P_{\text{struct}} = 0.3$$
$$\text{Stall CPI} = 0.3 \times 1 = 0.3$$
$$\text{CPI}_{\text{real}} = 1.0 + 0.3 = 1.3$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — SIMD Lane Efficiency (Practical Speedup)

A SIMD unit has $N$ lanes. An application has $f$ statements that vectorize cleanly, and $(1 - f)$ that require scalar fallback due to data dependencies.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{SIMD}} &= f \cdot \frac{n}{N} \cdot t + (1 - f) \cdot n \cdot t \\
&= n \cdot t \cdot \left( \frac{f}{N} + 1 - f \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Speedup:
$$S_{\text{SIMD}} = \frac{n \cdot t}{n \cdot t \cdot \left( \frac{f}{N} + 1 - f \right)} = \frac{1}{1 - f + \frac{f}{N}}$$

> This is **identical in form to Amdahl's Law** — the universality of the bottleneck principle!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which SLC Mechanism

| Criterion | Pipelining | Superscalar | VLIW | SIMD | SIMT (GPU) | Atomic Ops |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Instruction | Instruction | Instruction (bundle) | Statement (vector) | Statement (per-thread) | Single statement |
| **Parallelism Source** | Time-overlap | HW multi-issue | Compiler schedule | Data lanes | Thread replication | Lock-free semantics |
| **Best For** | Sequential code | General code | Embedded DSP | Array math | Massive throughput | Counters, flags |
| **Hardware Cost** | Medium | High (ROB, RS) | Low (no scheduling) | Medium | Very High (SMs) | Low (CAS) |
| **Compiler Effort** | Low | Low | **Very High** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Power Efficiency** | Medium | Medium-High | **Highest** | High | Highest per op | High |
| **Example Use** | All CPUs | x86, ARM | DSP, Itanium | SSE, AVX, NEON | CUDA, ROCm | Multithreaded I/O |
| **Failure Mode** | Pipeline stall | Wrong speculation | Bundling error | Lane mask | Divergence | ABA problem |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (Full Solution — KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]:**
> Consider a program with 200 statements. 150 of these statements are independent (Bernstein-safe) and 50 form a serial chain. The system has 8 parallel execution units. Compute:
> (a) Maximum possible speedup.
> (b) Actual speedup with 8 units.
> (c) What happens if 10 more serial statements are added to the chain?

### Solution:

**Part (a):**
$$f = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$
$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \times$$

**Part (b):**
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \frac{0.75}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375} = \frac{1}{0.34375} \approx 2.909 \times$$

**Part (c):** New total = 210 statements, parallel = 150, serial = 60.
$$f' = \frac{150}{210} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143$$
$$S'(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.7143) + \frac{0.7143}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.2857 + 0.0893} = \frac{1}{0.3750} \approx 2.667 \times$$

> **Insight:** Adding just 10 serial statements to a 200-statement program reduced speedup from **2.91× → 2.67×** (an 8.2% degradation). The serial fraction is the dominant performance lever.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
