<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Statement Level Concurrency. List any three conditions that determine whether two statements can execute concurrently.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Statement Level Concurrency is the form of parallel execution in which individual programming statements or machine instructions are identified and executed in overlapping time intervals, exploiting Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP), Data-Level Parallelism (DLP), or thread-level hardware support.

**Three Conditions (Bernstein's Conditions — 2 marks):**
1. **Flow Independence (RAW safe):** $S_1$ should not read a variable that $S_2$ writes, i.e., $I_1 \cap O_2 = \emptyset$.
2. **Anti Independence (WAR safe):** $S_2$ should not read a variable that $S_1$ writes, i.e., $O_1 \cap I_2 = \emptyset$.
3. **Output Independence (WAW safe):** Both statements should not write to the same memory location, i.e., $O_1 \cap O_2 = \emptyset$.

If all three hold, the statements are *safe* for concurrent execution.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between SIMD and SIMT execution models with a one-line example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SIMD | SIMT |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Single Instruction, Multiple Data | Single Instruction, Multiple Threads |
| **Execution Unit** | One wide ALU with N lanes | Many scalar threads grouped in warps |
| **Control** | Single instruction stream | Single instruction stream per warp, but each thread has its own PC/registers |
| **Example Statement** | `ymm0 = ymm1 + ymm2` (AVX add) | `y[i] = a*x[i] + y[i];` in CUDA with `i` varying per thread |
| **Hardware Example** | Intel AVX-512 | NVIDIA A100 GPU |

(2 marks for the table; 1 mark for the one-line example of each.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Pattern]**
> *(a)* Explain the three types of data dependencies between statements with a clear example for each. Show how a compiler can eliminate antidependencies and output dependencies using **register renaming**. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> *(b)* A scientific kernel contains **80% data-parallel statements** and **20% sequential statements** (Bernstein-incompatible). Compute and tabulate the speedup $S(N)$ for $N = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32$ cores. What is the maximum achievable speedup? Justify the result with Amdahl's Law. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**1. True Data Dependency (RAW) — 2 marks**
A RAW dependency occurs when a later statement reads a value produced by an earlier statement.
```
S1:  a = b + c
S2:  d = a * 3
```
Here $S_2$ reads `a` which $S_1$ writes. **This is unbreakable** — $S_2$ must wait for $S_1$ to complete.

**2. Antidependency (WAR) — 2 marks**
A WAR dependency occurs when a later statement writes to a variable that an earlier statement still needs to read.
```
S1:  a = b + c
S2:  b = d + 1
```
Here $S_2$ writes `b` which $S_1$ reads. **Eliminable** via renaming `b` in $S_2$ to a new physical register `b'`.

**3. Output Dependency (WAW) — 2 marks**
A WAW dependency occurs when two statements write to the same location in program order.
```
S1:  a = 5
S2:  a = 7
```
**Eliminable** via register renaming — compiler/HW maps $S_1$'s `a` and $S_2$'s `a` to distinct physical registers, then the architectural `a` is updated only with $S_2$'s final value at retirement.

**4. Register Renaming Mechanism — 1 mark**
The compiler (or hardware Reorder Buffer) maintains a *rename table* mapping architectural registers to a larger pool of physical registers. Each write is assigned a fresh physical register, eliminating false dependencies. This expands the *instruction window* for parallel scheduling.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $f = 0.8$, $N \in \{1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32\}$.

**Formula:**
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.8) + \frac{0.8}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{N}}$$

**Step-by-step calculations (one row per N — 3 marks):**

| $N$ | $0.8 / N$ | $0.2 + 0.8/N$ | $S(N)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8000 | 1.0000 | 1.000 |
| 2 | 0.4000 | 0.6000 | 1.667 |
| 4 | 0.2000 | 0.4000 | 2.500 |
| 8 | 0.1000 | 0.3000 | 3.333 |
| 16 | 0.0500 | 0.2500 | 4.000 |
| 32 | 0.0250 | 0.2250 | 4.444 |

**Maximum speedup (1 mark):**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) \\
&= \frac{1}{0.2} \\
&= 5 \times
\end{aligned}$$

**Justification with Amdahl's Law (3 marks):**
Amdahl's Law states that the speedup of a parallel system is bounded by the serial fraction $(1-f)$ of the program, regardless of how many cores we add. In this kernel, $1 - f = 0.2$ (the 20% sequential statements) acts as the **irreducible bottleneck**. As $N$ grows, the parallel fraction $0.8/N$ shrinks toward zero, so $S(N)$ asymptotes to $1/0.2 = 5\times$. **Even with 32 cores, we only achieve 4.444×** — within 11% of the theoretical limit. This is why *Amdahl-aware algorithm design* (reducing the serial fraction) is more important than merely adding cores.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes observed in valuation:
> 1. Forgetting that $f$ is the **parallelizable fraction**, not the total statement count.
> 2. Conflating Amdahl's Law with Gustafson's Law (the latter assumes the *problem size scales* with $N$).
> 3. Noting only the formula but failing to **interpret** why the curve flattens — examiners allocate 2–3 marks for the *qualitative interpretation* of the asymptote.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a **5-stage pipelined processor** (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB). Explain how statement-level concurrency is achieved through **pipelining**, and derive the ideal speedup for $n$ statements. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> *(b)* Compare **VLIW** and **Superscalar** architectures for statement-level concurrency. Discuss their **advantages**, **limitations**, and the role of the **compiler** in each. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**5-Stage Pipeline Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
IF -> ID -> EX -> MEM -> WB
```
- **IF (Instruction Fetch):** Reads next instruction from I-cache.
- **ID (Instruction Decode):** Decodes opcode, reads register file.
- **EX (Execute):** ALU operation, branch target calculation.
- **MEM (Memory Access):** Loads/stores to D-cache.
- **WB (Write Back):** Result written back to register file.

Each pipeline register between stages holds the intermediate state, allowing a new instruction to enter IF every clock cycle.

**Statement-Level Concurrency via Pipelining (2 marks):**
Pipelining achieves SLC by **temporal overlap** of *different statements in different stages* at the same clock cycle. At steady state, 5 statements are simultaneously in the pipeline, each in a different stage. The same physical hardware (ALU, memory port) is *time-shared* across multiple statements.

**Ideal Speedup Derivation (2 marks):**
Sequential execution of $n$ instructions on a $k$-stage pipeline:
$$T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau$$

Pipelined execution (each subsequent instruction starts one cycle after the previous):
$$T_{\text{pipeline}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{ideal}} &= \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{pipeline}}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot \tau}{(k + n - 1) \cdot \tau} \\
&= \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}
\end{aligned}$$

As $n \to \infty$, $S_{\text{ideal}} \to k$ — the pipeline executes 1 instruction per cycle, versus $k$ cycles per instruction sequentially. **This is the asymptotic speedup limit equal to the number of stages.**

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

| Aspect | VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) | Superscalar |
|---|---|---|
| **Scheduling** | **Static (compile-time)** — compiler packs ops into long instruction word | **Dynamic (runtime)** — hardware schedules |
| **Hazard Detection** | None at runtime — compiler guarantees no conflicts | Hardware Reorder Buffer (ROB) detects hazards |
| **Hardware Complexity** | **Low** — no schedulers, simple issue logic | **Very High** — reservation stations, renaming, ROB |
| **Power Efficiency** | **High** (no runtime check logic) | Lower (complex dispatch) |
| **Compiler Burden** | **Very High** — must know exact latencies & issue rules | Moderate (HW handles it) |
| **Code Density** | Low (NOPs needed in bundles) | High (compact) |
| **Bin Portability** | Poor (code is target-specific) | Good (ISA-stable) |
| **Examples** | Intel Itanium, TI TMS320 DSP, Qualcomm Hexagon | Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex-A |
| **Failure Mode** | Mis-scheduled bundle → wrong result | Speculation miss → pipeline flush |
| **ILP Window** | Whole program (compiler scope) | Limited by ROB (128–512 entries) |

**Role of the Compiler (2 marks for discussion):**
- In **VLIW**, the compiler is the *brain* — it must perform instruction scheduling, bundling, register allocation, and software pipelining. Any miscalculation of latency produces incorrect code at runtime with no hardware safety net.
- In **Superscalar**, the compiler mainly optimizes for *cache locality* and *branch predictability*; the hardware handles dynamic issue and renaming. This trades power/area for ease of programming.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
VLIW is ideal for **embedded DSP** workloads with known latencies and power constraints. Superscalar dominates **general-purpose computing** where code variability and backward compatibility matter.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **Diagrams are mandatory** for the 5-stage pipeline part — students who draw only a textual description lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. Don't confuse *pipelining* (single instruction stream, time-overlap) with *superscalar* (multi-issue, spatial+ temporal). Examiners penalize this conflation.
> 3. VLIW vs Superscalar comparison must mention *at least 4 distinguishing parameters* — a 3-row table is below the expected depth for a 7-mark sub-part.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — STATEMENT LEVEL CONCURRENCY**

✅ **Core Definition:** Concurrency at the granularity of *individual statements* (not blocks, not loops, not processes).

✅ **Bernstein's Three Conditions (MUST MEMORIZE):**
- $I_1 \cap O_2 = \emptyset$ (Flow)
- $O_1 \cap I_2 = \emptyset$ (Anti)
- $O_1 \cap O_2 = \emptyset$ (Output)
- All three satisfied ⇒ Safe to parallelize.

✅ **Dependency Types:**
- **RAW (True)** — Unbreakable. Compiler must preserve.
- **WAR (Anti)** — Breakable via register renaming.
- **WAW (Output)** — Breakable via register renaming.
- **RAR (Input)** — Trivially parallel.

✅ **Hardware Mechanisms:**
- **Pipelining** — temporal overlap, ideal speedup = $k$ stages.
- **Superscalar** — dynamic multi-issue, CPI = 1/r at peak.
- **VLIW** — static compiler-scheduled, low HW complexity.
- **SIMD** — wide vector ALU, N lanes.
- **SIMT** — warp-based GPU threads (32/64 threads).

✅ **Key Formulas:**
- Amdahl: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}}$
- Pipeline speedup: $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$
- SIMD speedup: $S = \dfrac{1}{1 - f + \frac{f}{N}}$

✅ **Software Constructs:** OpenMP `#pragma omp parallel`, OpenACC `#pragma acc parallel`, CUDA `__global__`, `std::atomic`.

✅ **Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- Confusing *concurrency* (logical simultaneity) with *parallelism* (physical simultaneity).
- Forgetting that register renaming is done by *HW* in superscalar, by *compiler* in VLIW.
- Missing the **Bernstein set union** form: $C \iff (I_1 \cap O_2) \cup (O_1 \cap I_2) \cup (O_1 \cap O_2) = \emptyset$.

✅ **Constants to Remember (for numerical problems):**
- Superscalar issue: 2–6 inst/cycle
- SIMD widths: 128/256/512 bit
- GPU warp: 32 threads (NVIDIA), 64 (AMD)
- Typical ROB size: 128–512 entries
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exception Handling and Event Handling: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exception Handling and Event Handling: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Exception Handling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exception Handling** is a powerful, object-oriented mechanism in Java used to **detect, intercept, and recover from runtime errors** (anomalies) that disrupt the normal flow of program execution. An *exception* is an abnormal condition that arises during program execution and disrupts the program's normal sequential flow.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, exception handling is positioned as a critical reliability engineering concept — a method to **separate error-handling code from regular business logic**, leading to robust, fault-tolerant applications.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Event Handling

> [!NOTE]
> **Event Handling** refers to the mechanism that controls **when and how an event (user action or system action) is processed** by a program. In Java's AWT/Swing (and the Delegation Event Model), an *event* is an object that describes a state change in the GUI component (e.g., button click, mouse hover, key press).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Exception Handling as an Airbag System:**

Imagine you are driving a car at 100 km/h on a highway. The car represents your program executing instructions sequentially. A sudden unexpected event (a crash) occurs. The **airbag system** activates automatically, absorbs the impact, and protects the driver — allowing the journey to safely conclude (or at least log a controlled failure).

In programming:
- The **car** = your main program flow
- The **crash** = an exception (e.g., divide by zero, file not found)
- The **airbag** = the `catch` block that handles the exception
- The **seatbelt reminder** = the `finally` block (always executes)

**Analogy 2 — Event Handling as a Doorbell System:**

When a visitor presses your **doorbell** (the *event source* generates an *event*), the bell rings (the *event object* is created), and you (the *event listener*) open the door (the *event handler method* executes). You don't stand glued to the door — the doorbell **notifies** you only when action is needed.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Throwable** is the **superclass of all errors and exceptions** in Java.
- The three standard keywords: `try`, `catch`, `throw`, `throws`, `finally`.
- Event handling uses **Listener interfaces** (e.g., `ActionListener`, `KeyListener`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Exception Hierarchy (Java's Throwable Tree)

Java's exception framework is built on a clean class hierarchy rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`. The hierarchy is logically divided into three branches:

- **Error** — Severe, unrecoverable problems (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`). Applications **should not** catch these.
- **Exception** — Recoverable conditions that applications **should** catch.
  - **Checked Exceptions** — Checked at **compile-time** (e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException`).
  - **Unchecked Exceptions (RuntimeException)** — Checked at **run-time** (e.g., `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`).

### 2.2 The Five Keywords of Exception Handling

1. `try` — Defines a block of code where an exception may occur.
2. `catch` — Defines a block to handle the exception.
3. `finally` — Defines a block that **always executes** (cleanup code).
4. `throw` — Used to **explicitly throw** an exception.
5. `throws` — Declares exceptions that a method **might** throw.

### 2.3 Event Handling — The Delegation Event Model (DEM)

Java's modern event handling uses the **Delegation Event Model**, which has four key participants:

| Component | Role | Example |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **Event Source** | The GUI component that generates the event | `JButton`, `JTextField` |
| **Event Object** | The object encapsulating event details | `ActionEvent`, `KeyEvent` |
| **Event Listener** | Interface implemented to receive events | `ActionListener` |
| **Event Handler** | The method that processes the event | `actionPerformed()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Delegation"?** The source component **delegates** the responsibility of handling an event to a separate listener object. This decouples the GUI from the business logic, following the **Observer design pattern**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Memory Hook |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| Try block | `try { ... }` | Risk zone |
| Catch block | `catch(ExceptionType e) { ... }` | Rescue zone |
| Finally block | `finally { ... }` | Always runs |
| Throw | `throw new Exception("msg");` | Explicit trigger |
| Throws | `void m() throws IOException { }` | Declaration |
| Register listener | `btn.addActionListener(this);` | Subscribe to event |
| Handle event | `public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { }` | Callback method |
| Event source | The component that **fires** the event | Generator |
| Event listener | The object that **receives** the event | Observer |

> **Real-world utility:** Exception handling is used in **banking software** (transaction rollback), **aerospace systems** (mission abort procedures), and **embedded firmware** (sensor fault recovery). Event handling drives every GUI application — from ATM screens to cockpit displays.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a programming concept topic, "derivations" translate into **step-by-step code walkthroughs** that demonstrate execution flow, scope resolution, and control transfer between blocks.

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Try-Catch-Finally Flow Control

Consider the following Java program. We will trace the **exact execution path** step-by-step.

```java
public class ExceptionFlow {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("STEP 1: Before try");
        try {
            System.out.println("STEP 2: Inside try, before division");
            int result = 10 / 0;   // ArithmeticException occurs here
            System.out.println("STEP 3: After division (NOT executed)");
        }
        catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("STEP 4: Caught exception: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("STEP 5: Finally block (ALWAYS executes)");
        }
        System.out.println("STEP 6: Program continues normally");
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace:**

| Step | Action | Output / Result |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Print statement before try | `STEP 1: Before try` |
| 2 | Enter try block | `STEP 2: Inside try, before division` |
| 3 | `10 / 0` is evaluated | **ArithmeticException thrown** by JVM |
| 4 | Control jumps to matching `catch` | `STEP 4: Caught exception: / by zero` |
| 5 | `finally` block executes | `STEP 5: Finally block (ALWAYS executes)` |
| 6 | Resume after try-catch-finally | `STEP 6: Program continues normally` |

**Logical Conclusion:**

$$
\text{Execution Path} = \text{try} \rightarrow \text{(exception)} \rightarrow \text{matched catch} \rightarrow \text{finally} \rightarrow \text{normal flow}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Multiple Catch Blocks

When different exception types may arise, multiple `catch` blocks can be chained. The **most specific** exception class must appear **first** (subclass before superclass), or a compile-time error occurs.

```java
try {
    int arr[] = new int[5];
    arr[10] = 50;        // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
}
catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Arithmetic error");
}
catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
    System.out.println("Array index error");
}
catch (Exception e) {
    System.out.println("Generic error");
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Putting `catch(Exception e)` **before** `catch(ArithmeticException e)` will cause a **compile-time error** — *"exception ArithmeticException has already been caught"* — because the superclass catch would always match first.

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: Event Handling Sequence

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class DemoEvent extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JButton btn;
    DemoEvent() {
        btn = new JButton("Click Me");
        btn.addActionListener(this);    // Step A: Register listener
        add(btn);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {  // Step B: Event handler
        btn.setText("Clicked!");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new DemoEvent();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Event Flow:**

1. **Construction phase:** `new DemoEvent()` creates the JFrame and button.
2. **Registration phase:** `btn.addActionListener(this)` — the button *delegates* future clicks to `this` object.
3. **Idle phase:** Program waits for user interaction.
4. **Event generation:** User clicks the button → JVM creates an `ActionEvent` object.
5. **Dispatch phase:** JVM calls `actionPerformed(e)` on the registered listener.
6. **Handling phase:** Button label is changed to `"Clicked!"`.

The mathematical representation of event propagation:

$$
\text{Event Source} \xrightarrow{\text{generates}} \text{Event Object} \xrightarrow{\text{delegated to}} \text{Listener} \xrightarrow{\text{calls}} \text{Handler Method}
$$

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions

| Property | Checked Exception | Unchecked (Runtime) Exception |
|----------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| Checked at | Compile-time | Run-time |
| Compiler enforcement | Must handle or declare | Optional |
| Extends | `Exception` | `RuntimeException` |
| Examples | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |
| Indicates | External resource issues | Programming bugs |
| Handling | `try-catch` or `throws` | Optional, often fixed via code review |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Java Exception Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.lang.Throwable]
    B --> C[java.lang.Error]
    B --> D[java.lang.Exception]
    C --> C1[OutOfMemoryError]
    C --> C2[StackOverflowError]
    D --> E[java.io.IOException]
    D --> F[java.sql.SQLException]
    D --> G[java.lang.RuntimeException]
    G --> G1[ArithmeticException]
    G --> G2[NullPointerException]
    G --> G3[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    G --> G4[NumberFormatException]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Try-Catch-Finally Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Program Start] --> P2[Enter try block]
    P2 --> P3{Exception thrown?}
    P3 -- No --> P4[Execute remaining try code]
    P3 -- Yes --> P5[Skip remaining try code]
    P4 --> P6[Skip catch blocks]
    P5 --> P7{Matching catch found?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Execute matched catch block]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Propagate exception upward]
    P6 --> P10[Execute finally block]
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Continue program execution]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Delegation Event Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[Event Source e.g. JButton] -->|addActionListener| Lst[Event Listener Interface e.g. ActionListener]
    Lst -->|implements| Hdl[Event Handler Method e.g. actionPerformed]
    Usr[User Action e.g. Mouse Click] -->|triggers| Src
    Src -->|fires| EvObj[Event Object e.g. ActionEvent]
    EvObj -->|passed to| Hdl
    Hdl -->|updates| UI[GUI Component State]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Exception Propagation Through Call Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[main method] --> M2[methodA]
    M2 --> M3[methodB]
    M3 --> M4[methodC]
    M4 --> X{Exception occurs}
    X -- Caught in methodC --> C1[Handle locally, stop propagation]
    X -- Not caught --> P1[Propagate to methodB]
    P1 -- Caught in methodB --> C2[Handle in methodB]
    P1 -- Not caught --> P2[Propagate to methodA]
    P2 -- Caught in methodA --> C3[Handle in methodA]
    P2 -- Not caught --> P3[Propagate to main]
    P3 -- Not caught --> P4[JVM default handler, program terminates]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Insight:** Exception propagation follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** call stack unwinding principle. If no method in the call chain catches the exception, the **JVM's default exception handler** prints the stack trace and terminates the program.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]`**
> **Q1. Define an exception. Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An *exception* is an abnormal condition that occurs during program execution and disrupts the normal flow of instructions. In Java, exceptions are objects derived from the `Throwable` class.

| Feature | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Checked at | Compile-time | Run-time |
| Inherits from | `Exception` | `RuntimeException` |
| Handling | Mandatory (try-catch or throws) | Optional |
| Example | `IOException` | `ArithmeticException` |

> **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q2]`**
> **Q2. What is an event in Java? List any two event listener interfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An *event* is an object that represents a state change in a GUI component, typically triggered by user interaction such as clicking a button or pressing a key.

Two event listener interfaces:
1. **`ActionListener`** — handles button clicks and menu selections.
2. **`KeyListener`** — handles key press, release, and type events.

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Full Module Q]`**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the exception handling mechanism in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the role of `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the Delegation Event Model? Describe its components with a Java code example that handles a button click event. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** Java's exception handling is a robust mechanism to handle runtime errors, ensuring the program does not terminate abruptly. It uses five keywords: `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws`.

**Explanation of keywords (5 marks):**

- **`try`:** Wraps the code that might throw an exception. The block is "monitored" for errors.
- **`catch`:** Catches and handles the exception thrown from the try block. Multiple catch blocks can handle different exception types.
- **`finally`:** Always executes, regardless of whether an exception occurred. Used for cleanup (e.g., closing files, releasing resources).
- **`throw`:** Explicitly throws an exception object from a method or block. Syntax: `throw new ArithmeticException("msg");`
- **`throws`:** Declares that a method may throw one or more exceptions, transferring responsibility to the caller. Syntax: `void read() throws IOException`.

**Example code (1 mark):**

```java
import java.io.*;
class Demo {
    void readFile() throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader("test.txt");
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(fr);
        try {
            System.out.println(br.readLine());
        }
        catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
            throw e;
        }
        finally {
            br.close();
        }
    }
}
```

**Conclusion:** The five keywords together provide a structured way to detect, propagate, and recover from errors.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is Java's modern approach to event handling, where the event source (e.g., a button) *delegates* the task of event processing to a separate listener object. This model is based on the **Observer design pattern**.

**Components (3 marks):**

1. **Event Source** — The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`) that generates the event.
2. **Event Object** — Created when an event occurs (e.g., `ActionEvent`). Encapsulates event details.
3. **Event Listener Interface** — Implemented by classes that want to receive notifications (e.g., `ActionListener`).
4. **Event Handler Method** — The method declared in the listener interface that contains the response logic (e.g., `actionPerformed`).

**Code Example (2 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;
class MyFrame extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JButton b;
    MyFrame() {
        b = new JButton("Submit");
        b.addActionListener(this);
        add(b);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        b.setText("Submitted!");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MyFrame();
    }
}
```

**Conclusion:** DEM promotes loose coupling between GUI and logic, making code modular and maintainable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the exception class hierarchy in Java with a neat diagram. Differentiate between `Error` and `Exception`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate multiple catch blocks and nested try-catch statements. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Hierarchy Explanation (4 marks):** The root of Java's exception hierarchy is `java.lang.Throwable`. It has two direct subclasses:

- **`Error`:** Represents serious, unrecoverable problems (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`). Programs typically do not catch these.
- **`Exception`:** Represents recoverable conditions. Has two main subcategories:
  - **Checked Exceptions** (compile-time): `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`.
  - **Unchecked Exceptions** (run-time, extend `RuntimeException`): `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `IndexOutOfBoundsException`.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
              java.lang.Object
                    |
              java.lang.Throwable
              /                 \
       java.lang.Error      java.lang.Exception
       (Unrecoverable)           |
                          +------+------+
                          |             |
                  Checked Exceptions   java.lang.RuntimeException
                  (e.g. IOException)        (Unchecked)
                                       e.g. ArithmeticException
```

**Difference Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Error | Exception |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| Recoverable? | No | Yes |
| Caught by app? | Should not | Should |
| Example | OutOfMemoryError | IOException |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Program (4 marks):**

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int a[] = new int[5];
            try {
                a[5] = 30 / 0;   // ArithmeticException occurs
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch: " + e);
            }
            String s = null;
            System.out.println(s.length());   // NullPointerException
        }
        catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Array error: " + e);
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic: " + e);
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("Finally always runs");
        }
    }
}
```

**Output Explanation (3 marks):**

1. First, `30 / 0` throws `ArithmeticException` → caught by **inner catch** → prints `Inner catch: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero`.
2. Then `s.length()` throws `NullPointerException` → caught by **outer catch** → prints `Outer catch: java.lang.NullPointerException`.
3. Finally, **finally block** always executes → prints `Finally always runs`.

**Conclusion:** Nested try-catch allows granular error handling at different levels of code complexity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to declare `throws`** for checked exceptions in method signatures → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Placing superclass catch before subclass catch** → compile-time error → **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing `throw` (keyword) with `throws` (declaration)** → **−1 mark** per instance.
> 4. **Omitting the `implements ActionListener`** in event programs → **−2 marks** (program won't compile).
> 5. **Forgetting to call `addActionListener(this)`** in GUI event programs → **−1 mark** (event never fires).
> 6. **Not drawing the hierarchy diagram in Q.B(a)** → **−1 to −2 marks** (diagrams carry weightage).
> 7. **Generic `catch(Exception e)` placed first** in multiple-catch blocks → **−2 marks** (unreachable subclass catch).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **exception** is an object representing a runtime anomaly; it descends from `java.lang.Throwable`.
- Java exceptions are categorized into **Checked (compile-time)** and **Unchecked (run-time)** exceptions.
- The **five keywords** are `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws` — memorize their distinct roles.
- `finally` block executes **always**, even if `System.exit(0)` is *not* called.
- Multiple `catch` blocks must be ordered from **most specific (subclass) to most general (superclass)**.
- The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** separates event generation from event handling.
- The **four DEM components** are: *Event Source, Event Object, Event Listener, Event Handler*.
- The **listener interface** must be implemented by the class that wants to receive events (e.g., `implements ActionListener`).
- Event registration happens via `component.addXxxListener(listenerObject)`.
- Exception propagation follows the **call stack** — uncaught exceptions terminate the program via JVM's default handler.
- **Errors** (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) are **not meant to be caught** by applications.
- Common exceptions to know: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`, `IOException`.
- Common event listeners: `ActionListener`, `KeyListener`, `MouseListener`, `WindowListener`.
- Always **close resources** in the `finally` block (pre-Java 7 best practice).
- A single `catch` block can catch multiple exceptions using `catch (IOException \vert SQLException e)` (Java 7+).
- **Custom exceptions** are created by extending the `Exception` class.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exception Handling in Ada
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exception Handling in Ada

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exception Handling in Ada** is a structured, declarative mechanism used to detect, raise, and recover from *abnormal conditions* (runtime anomalies) that disrupt the normal flow of program execution. An *exception* in Ada is a **named entity** declared in a declarative region, which is *raised* when an exceptional condition occurs and subsequently *handled* by an enclosing construct containing a corresponding `exception handler`.

In the Ada Reference Manual (ARM) terminology adopted by the KTU 2024 syllabus, exception handling is built around **three primary language constructs**:

1. The **declaration** of an exception (a named entity of type `Exception`).
2. The **raising** of an exception (either implicitly by a runtime check, or explicitly by a `raise` statement).
3. The **handling** of an exception (via a sequence of *exception handlers* in the optional `exception` part of a block, subprogram body, package body, or task body).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** Students must distinguish between *predefined exceptions* (raised automatically by the language runtime) and *user-defined exceptions* (raised explicitly by the programmer). Both follow the same propagation and handling rules.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway:

- **Normal Flow** → You continue smoothly along your route.
- **Abnormal Condition (Exception)** → A tyre suddenly punctures, the engine overheats, or fuel runs out.
- **Detection** → The dashboard warning light blinks (analogous to Ada's runtime check).
- **Propagation** → The car pulls itself over to the shoulder — control *transfers* to the emergency protocol.
- **Handler** → You take out the spare tyre, fix the issue, and resume driving (or call for help).

In Ada, the *handler* does **not** return execution to the point of failure (unlike some languages). Instead, it resolves the abnormal condition and continues execution *after* the block, subprogram, or unit in which the exception was handled. This makes Ada's exception model one of the most reliable in the industry — used in **avionics (DO-178C)**, **railways**, and **defence systems**.

---

## 1.3 The Ada Exception Architecture (One-Glance View)

| Component | Ada Construct | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Exception Declaration | `<name> : exception;` | Defines a new exception identity |
| Implicit Raise | Runtime check failure (e.g., array bound) | Signals abnormal condition |
| Explicit Raise | `raise <name>;` | Programmer-triggered signal |
| Handler | `when <name> => ...` | Recovery code for a specific exception |
| Catch-All | `when others => ...` | Catches any un-named exception |
| Re-raise | `raise;` (no argument) | Propagates same exception to enclosing scope |
| Information | `Ada.Exceptions.Exception_Name`, `Exception_Message` | Retrieves the identity & message of an Ada 2005+ exception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metric / Standard:** Ada is the **only** ISO-standardized language (ISO/IEC 8652) with mandatory, language-level exception handling integrated with strong typing. This is why KTU includes it in the "Advanced Computer Programming Techniques" course — it reinforces structured reliability engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Ada Block with Exception Handling

The general skeleton accepted by the Ada compiler (GNAT, ObjectAda, Janus/Ada) is:

```ada
declare
   -- Declarative part:
   --   variables, constants, subprograms,
   --   and **exception declarations**
   <var_list>;
   <My_Error> : exception;       -- user-defined exception
begin
   -- Sequence of statements
   --   (may contain explicit `raise` statements)
   if <condition> then
      raise <My_Error>;          -- explicit raise
   end if;
exception
   -- Exception part (optional, but mandatory here)
   when <My_Error>      => <handler_statements>;
   when Constraint_Error => <handler_statements>;
   when others          => <handler_statements>;
end;
```

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (The 'Why' and 'How')

### Step 1 — Declaration Stage
- Exceptions are declared like any other entity: `<name> : exception;`
- Scope rules apply: an exception declared in a subprogram is *only* visible inside that subprogram (and any nested block, unless shadowed).
- An exception is a *first-class named entity* in the declarative region.

### Step 2 — Raising Stage
- **Implicitly**: by the language runtime when a *check* fails. Examples:
  - `Constraint_Error` — index out of range, null access dereference, range violation.
  - `Program_Error` — calling an uninitialized variable as a subprogram, abnormal function return.
  - `Storage_Error` — dynamic memory exhaustion (allocator failure).
  - `Tasking_Error` — task activation / communication failure.
  - `Numeric_Error` — legacy (Ada 83) analogue of `Constraint_Error` for numeric overflow; obsolete in modern Ada.
- **Explicitly**: the programmer writes `raise <exception_name>;`.

### Step 3 — Propagation Stage
- When an exception is raised and there is **no matching handler** in the current block, the block is **abandoned**, all objects created within the block's elaboration are finalized, and the exception is **propagated to the enclosing block/subprogram/package body**.
- Propagation continues dynamically upward until a matching handler is found.
- If propagation reaches the main program and remains unhandled, the runtime terminates the program with a diagnostic dump.

### Step 4 — Handling Stage
- A handler is selected if its `when` clause names the raised exception **or** if the `when others` clause is present.
- Once chosen, the corresponding statement sequence executes; the handler then exits the block (or subprogram) at the end of the handler — *not* at the raise point.
- If multiple `when` clauses could match, **only the first matching one in textual order** is selected.

### Step 5 — Re-raise (Optional)
- A handler may re-raise the same exception to pass it to the *outer* scope using the parameter-less form: `raise;`
- This is critical for layered architectures (e.g., low-level module detects, middle layer logs, top layer informs user).

---

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Syntax / Formula | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declare exception | `E : exception;` | Marks awarded only if colon-`exception` syntax is exact |
| 2 | Explicit raise | `raise E;` | Used inside an `if` or procedure body |
| 3 | Single handler | `when E => <seq_of_stmts>;` | Must end with semicolon before the next `when` |
| 4 | Multiple handlers | Sequence of `when` clauses, optional `when others` last | `others` must come **last** |
| 5 | Catch-all | `when others => <seq_of_stmts>;` | Single `others` per `exception` part |
| 6 | Re-raise | `raise;` | No identifier — re-raises the current exception |
| 7 | Predefined | `Constraint_Error, Program_Error, Storage_Error, Tasking_Error` | Asked frequently as 3-mark questions |
| 8 | Block construct | `declare ... begin ... exception ... end;` | Almost every KTU 14-mark question uses a block |
| 9 | Information (Ada 2005) | `Ada.Exceptions.Exception_Message(X)`, `Exception_Name(X)` | New in KTU 2024 syllabus (2005+ semantics) |
| 10 | Bundled exception | `raise E with "message";` | Ada 2005+ feature; KTU asks about it |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes used** in the table above. Set-theoretic or magnitude notation is intentionally avoided to keep the markdown table intact.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing 777, Airbus A380):** Ada's exception model ensures that a sensor failure *cannot* silently corrupt state; the system either recovers or enters a known safe state.
- **Railway Signalling (Alstom, Siemens):** Exception propagation gives a deterministic, traceable failure path — mandatory for SIL-4 certification.
- **Embedded Defence (US Army, BAE Systems):** The `when others` clause is used as the fail-safe catch-all in mission-critical software.
- **Industrial Control (Schneider, ABB PLCs):** `raise` with informative messages (Ada 2005) is used to log actuator failures in real time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Predefined Exception Triggers

The KTU 2024 board expects students to **map** specific runtime conditions to predefined exceptions. The mapping is derived directly from the Ada Reference Manual §11.1.

| Source of Runtime Check | Exception Raised | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Array index out of range | `Constraint_Error` | Subscript falls outside `'First .. 'Last` |
| Slice bounds mismatch | `Constraint_Error` | Discrete range violation |
| Discriminant constraint violation | `Constraint_Error` | `unconstrained` discriminant failed |
| Dereference of `null` access value | `Constraint_Error` | Null-pointer analogue |
| Range check failure (numeric) | `Constraint_Error` | Value out of subtype range |
| Division by zero (integer) | `Constraint_Error` | Only on machine overflow trap |
| Calling a value of access type as subprogram, when null | `Program_Error` | Elaboration aborted / uninitialized |
| Elaboration of a body fails | `Program_Error` | Incomplete package state |
| Failure of allocator (`new`) | `Storage_Error` | No more memory in heap |
| Subprogram activation out of resources | `Tasking_Error` | Task pool exhausted |

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Exception Propagation in Nested Blocks

Consider a program with three nested `declare ... begin ... exception ... end;` blocks. We will trace exception propagation **step-by-step**, since this is a high-yield KTU 14-mark question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical rule:** An exception propagates to the **nearest enclosing construct** that contains a matching handler. Block finalization happens in LIFO order at each scope exit.

### Derivation (nested-block trace)

**Step 1** — Define the exception identity.

```ada
A, B, C : exception;       -- three distinct exception names
```

**Step 2** — The innermost block raises exception `B`.

```ada
declare
begin
   declare
   begin
      raise B;             -- (i) raise point: B is now "in flight"
   exception
      when A => ... ;      -- (ii) handler for A: NOT matched
      -- (iii) no handler for B, no `when others`
      -- (iv) implicit re-propagation to outer block
   end;                    -- (v) inner block exits via propagation
exception
   when B => ... ;         -- (vi) outer block's handler matches B
   when others => ... ;
end;
```

**Step 3** — Decision logic at the inner `exception` part.

- `when A` is evaluated. The currently active exception is `B`, not `A`. Therefore, `A`'s handler is **not** chosen.
- Since no `when others` clause is present, the inner block has **no applicable handler**.
- The exception `B` is therefore *implicitly re-raised* into the dynamically enclosing block.

**Step 4** — Decision logic at the outer `exception` part.

- `when B` matches. Handler executes.
- Execution continues immediately **after the outer `end;`**, *not* at the original raise point.

**Step 5** — Finalization invariant.

At step (v), any controlled (finalizable) objects declared in the inner block have their `Finalize` calls (Ada 95+) invoked *before* propagation to the outer scope. This is the LIFO invariant: deeper objects finalize first.

### Resulting Behaviour

$$
\text{Result}(B) \;=\; \text{handler of outer block executes} \;\mid\; \text{inner block is abandoned}
$$

Written more formally:

$$
P(B) \;=\; \text{OuterScopeMatch}(B) \quad \text{where} \quad \text{OuterScopeMatch}(X) \;=\; \bigvee_{i=1}^{n}\;\big[\,X = H_i\,\big]
$$

with $H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_n$ being the handlers in textual order of the outer block, and $H_n$ being `others` (if present).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — `raise` with Message (Ada 2005+)

**Problem (frequently asked in KTU ESE):** Write an Ada procedure that reads an integer and raises a user-defined exception `Out_Of_Range` with a meaningful message if the value is not within $\left[1,\; 100\right]$.

**Step-by-step model solution:**

```ada
with Ada.Text_IO;            use Ada.Text_IO;
with Ada.Integer_Text_IO;    use Ada.Integer_Text_IO;
with Ada.Exceptions;         use Ada.Exceptions;

procedure Check_Range is
   Out_Of_Range : exception;
   N            : Integer;
begin
   Put("Enter an integer: ");
   Get(N);

   if N < 1 or N > 100 then
      raise Out_Of_Range with
         "Value " & Integer'Image(N) & " is outside [1, 100]";
   end if;

   Put_Line("Accepted: " & Integer'Image(N));
exception
   when Out_Of_Range =>
      Put_Line("Caught: " & Exception_Message(Out_Of_Range'Identity));
      --  Note: a raised value carries the message; the bare
      --  identifier in the handler is the exception type itself.
end Check_Range;
```

**Logic walk-through (each line in the model answer):**

1. `with Ada.Exceptions;` imports the language-level introspection package (added in Ada 2005).
2. `Out_Of_Range : exception;` declares the user-defined identity.
3. `raise Out_Of_Range with "...";` constructs an *exception occurrence* (an internal object) carrying both the identity and a payload string.
4. In the `when Out_Of_Range =>` handler, `Exception_Message` retrieves the message of the *occurrence* that was raised.
5. After the handler runs, the procedure `Check_Range` returns to its caller normally — no re-raise occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 trap:** Many students write `raise Out_Of_Range("...");` (function-call syntax) which is **illegal**. The correct syntax is the keyword `with` introducing the message string.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — `when others` Position Rules

| Position of `when others` | Validity | KTU Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Last handler in `exception` part | ✅ Legal | Standard form, ARM §11.2(6) |
| First handler | ❌ Illegal | No later handler can ever be reached |
| Multiple `when others` in one part | ❌ Illegal | Only one catch-all allowed per `exception` part |
| `when others` in inner block but not outer | ✅ Legal | Each scope has its own handler set |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common board mistake:** Writing `when others =>` *before* a specific `when Constraint_Error =>` handler. The compiler accepts neither placement; the `others` must be the *last* handler in textual order. This single issue costs **2 marks** in KTU valuations.

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Expression of Exception Selection

Let $E$ be the set of exception identities in a single `exception` part, $E = \{ e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_{n-1}, e_n\}$ where $e_n$ denotes `others`. The selection function is:

$$
\text{Choose}(E, x) \;=\; e_i \quad \text{such that} \quad i \;=\; \min\Bigl\{k \;\bigm|\; x \in \bigl\{\,e_k\,\bigr\} \cup \bigl\{\,e_n \,\text{if}\, k = n\,\bigr\}\Bigr\}
$$

That is, **textually first matching handler wins**. If no element of $E$ matches $x$ (and $e_n$ is not present), the function is undefined and the exception propagates further.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ada Exception Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Program Entry]) --> B[Declarative Part of Block/Subprogram]
    B --> C[Executable Statements Begin]
    C --> D{Check or explicit raise?}
    D -- "Normal" --> E[Continue sequential execution]
    D -- "Raise" --> F[Construct exception occurrence]
    F --> G{Current block has matching handler?}
    G -- "Yes: when X or when others" --> H[Execute handler statements]
    H --> I[Finalize block-local controlled objects]
    I --> J([Exit block; continue after `end`])
    G -- "No" --> K[Finalize block-local controlled objects LIFO]
    K --> L[Propagate to enclosing block/subprogram]
    L --> G
    E --> M{Block complete?}
    M -- "No" --> C
    M -- "Yes" --> N[Finalize local objects]
    N --> O([Normal exit])
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Handler Selection Algorithm (textually first match)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Exception occurrence X raised]) --> S1[Initialize handler index i = 1]
    S1 --> S2{i exceeds number of handlers?}
    S2 -- "Yes" --> S3[Propagate X to enclosing scope]
    S2 -- "No" --> S4{Handler i is `when X`?}
    S4 -- "Yes" --> S5[Execute handler i]
    S4 -- "No" --> S6{Handler i is `when others`?}
    S6 -- "Yes" --> S5
    S6 -- "No" --> S7[i = i + 1]
    S7 --> S2
    S5 --> S8[Finalize block]
    S8 --> S9([Continue after block])
    S3 --> S10([Outer scope receives X])
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of Information Retrieval (Ada 2005+)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAISE[raise E with 'message text'] --> OCC[Exception Occurrence]
    OCC --> HANDLER[when E => handler body]
    HANDLER --> GETMSG[Ada.Exceptions.Exception_Message]
    HANDLER --> GETNAM[Ada.Exceptions.Exception_Name]
    GETMSG --> OUT1[Print / log to operator]
    GETNAM --> OUT2[Route to category-specific recovery]
    RERAISE[raise;] --> OCC2[Same occurrence re-propagated]
    OCC2 --> OUTER[Outer scope handler]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a flow diagram instead of UML?** KTU 2024 valuations expect conceptual clarity over UML class diagrams. The flowchart emphasises the *data flow* of the exception occurrence, which is the key concept examined in board questions on Ada 2005+ semantics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q:** Define the term *exception* in Ada. List any **four** predefined exceptions with a one-line description of when each is raised.

**Model Answer:**

> An *exception* in Ada is a named entity declared in a declarative region which represents an abnormal condition that may arise during program execution. When the condition is detected, the exception is *raised*, and control transfers to a matching *exception handler*.

The four predefined exceptions are:

| Exception | When Raised |
|---|---|
| `Constraint_Error` | Out-of-range subscript, range violation, null access dereference |
| `Program_Error` | Elaboration of a subprogram body fails; calling an uninitialized access value as a subprogram |
| `Storage_Error` | Dynamic memory (`new`) allocator fails |
| `Tasking_Error` | Task activation, termination, or communication fails |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the table with correct triggers.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Differentiate between the *implicit* and *explicit* raising of an exception in Ada. Provide a one-line Ada code snippet for each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Implicit raising** is performed automatically by the Ada runtime when a language-defined check (subscript, range, discriminant, null access) fails. The programmer does not write a `raise` statement.
- **Explicit raising** is performed by the programmer using the `raise` statement when a custom abnormal condition is detected.

**Code snippets (1 line each):**

```ada
-- Implicit: when I > 10, Constraint_Error is raised automatically
Put (A(I));

-- Explicit: programmer triggers the user-defined exception
if Temperature > 150 then raise Overheat; end if;
```

*(3 marks: 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark per code snippet.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** The 14-mark question in Module 4 is typically split into **(a) 7 marks — descriptive/conceptual** and **(b) 7 marks — full Ada program with a working exception handler.** Both alternatives are provided below for full preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **exception propagation** mechanism in Ada. Describe, with the help of nested blocks, how an exception raised in an inner block is handled by an outer block's handler.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Ada program that declares a user-defined exception `Divide_By_Zero`. The program should read two integers, perform division inside an inner block, raise `Divide_By_Zero` if the divisor is **0**, and handle it in the outer block by printing a recovery message. Use the `raise;` re-raise statement to propagate further if the divisor is negative.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Propagation Rule.** When an exception is raised inside a construct (block, subprogram, package body, task body) and *no handler* in that construct's `exception` part matches, the construct is **abandoned** in an orderly manner — all controlled (finalizable) objects declared within are finalized, in LIFO order — and the exception is *implicitly re-raised* into the dynamically enclosing construct.
2. **Search Algorithm.** The runtime searches for a handler in textual order within the current `exception` part. If none matches and `when others` is absent, the search escalates to the enclosing construct.
3. **Handler Selection Rule.** The *first* `when` clause (in textual order) that either names the exception or is `when others` is selected.
4. **Termination of Search.** Once a handler executes, the construct *exits* via the normal end of the handler (not the raise point), and execution continues in the caller / enclosing scope *after* the construct.

**Nested-block illustration (7 marks-worth of content):**

```ada
with Ada.Text_IO; use Ada.Text_IO;

procedure Propagation_Demo is
   Fatal : exception;          -- declared in outer scope (visible to all inner blocks)
begin
   Put_Line("[outer] start");
   declare
      Local : Integer := 0;
   begin
      Put_Line("  [inner] start");
      raise Fatal;             -- raise in inner block
      Put_Line("  [inner] never reached");
   exception
      -- (A) No handler for Fatal here; no `when others`.
      -- The exception is *implicitly* re-raised to the outer block.
      Put_Line("  [inner] abandoned");
   end;
   Put_Line("[outer] never reached");
exception
   when Fatal =>
      Put_Line("[outer] caught Fatal in outer handler");
end Propagation_Demo;
```

**Expected output (each line, with mark allocation):**

```
[outer] start
  [inner] start
  [inner] abandoned
[outer] caught Fatal in outer handler
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 2-mark deduction:** Many students write handlers in the **outer** block first and then in the **inner** block in a way that suggests the inner block's `exception` part is "skipped". The correct mental model: the inner block's `exception` part is searched *first*, and only when *no* handler matches does propagation escalate. Reverse the explanation and the examiner will deduct marks for conceptual inversion.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```ada
with Ada.Text_IO;        use Ada.Text_IO;
with Ada.Integer_Text_IO; use Ada.Integer_Text_IO;

procedure Safe_Divide is
   Divide_By_Zero : exception;
   A, B, Q        : Integer;
begin
   Put("Enter dividend: ");   Get(A);
   Put("Enter divisor:  ");   Get(B);

   declare
      Local_Q : Integer;
   begin
      if B = 0 then
         raise Divide_By_Zero;        -- (i) explicit raise on zero divisor
      end if;
      if B < 0 then
         raise Divide_By_Zero;        -- (ii) explicit raise; will be re-raised
      end if;
      Local_Q := A / B;
      Q := Local_Q;
      Put_Line("Quotient = " & Integer'Image(Q));
   exception
      when Divide_By_Zero =>
         Put_Line("Inner handler: invalid divisor.");
         raise;                       -- (iii) re-raise to outer block
   end;

   Put_Line("Program completed normally.");

exception
   when Divide_By_Zero =>
      Put_Line("Outer handler: divisor was invalid; aborting computation.");
   when others =>
      Put_Line("Outer handler: unexpected exception.");
end Safe_Divide;
```

**Step-by-step walk-through (each line earns partial credit):**

1. **Lines 1–3** — Imports standard text I/O packages (1 mark).
2. **Line 5** — Declaration of the user-defined exception `Divide_By_Zero` (1 mark).
3. **Lines 7–8** — Reading the two operands using `Get` (1 mark).
4. **Line 11** — Opening of the **inner block** where division occurs (1 mark).
5. **Line 13** — `if B = 0 then raise Divide_By_Zero;` demonstrates explicit raise on zero divisor (1 mark).
6. **Line 17** — `if B < 0 then raise Divide_By_Zero;` to demonstrate that the same exception is raised on a negative divisor (1 mark).
7. **Line 24** — `raise;` re-raises the active exception to the outer block (1 mark).
8. **Lines 32–33** — The outer block's `when Divide_By_Zero` handler intercepts the re-raised exception and reports the failure (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 2-mark deduction:** A frequent error is to write `raise Divide_By_Zero;` inside the outer `when Divide_By_Zero` handler, mistakenly thinking this re-raises it. The correct re-raise form is the *parameter-less* `raise;`. Using the full identifier is a *new raise*, not a propagation, and most boards deduct 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the difference between **predefined** and **user-defined** exceptions in Ada. With a labelled diagram (or schematic), illustrate how an exception is propagated across *three* levels of nested subprograms (`Proc1` → `Proc2` → `Proc3`), where `Proc3` raises an exception that is handled in `Proc1`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an Ada program that uses the `Ada.Exceptions` package (Ada 2005+ semantics) to raise a `Login_Failed` exception *with a message* indicating the number of failed attempts. The handler should print the exception name and message using `Exception_Name` and `Exception_Message`.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Aspect | Predefined Exceptions | User-Defined Exceptions |
|---|---|---|
| Defined by | The Ada language standard (ARM §11.1) | The application programmer |
| Raising | Implicitly by the runtime when a check fails | Explicitly by the programmer's `raise` statement |
| Visibility | Visible in every scope (no `with` needed) | Visible only within the declarative region of declaration |
| Common examples | `Constraint_Error`, `Program_Error`, `Storage_Error`, `Tasking_Error` | `Overheat`, `Login_Failed`, `Out_Of_Range` |
| Override allowed? | Cannot be redeclared in the same scope | May be redeclared in inner scopes (shadowing) |

**Schematic — Propagation across three subprograms:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1([Proc1 begins]) --> P1H{Handler for E present?}
    P1H -- "Yes" --> P1CALL[Call Proc2]
    P1CALL --> P2[Proc2 executes]
    P2 --> P2CALL[Call Proc3]
    P2CALL --> P3[Proc3 executes]
    P3 --> P3RAISE[raise E]
    P3RAISE --> P3FIN[Finalize Proc3 locals LIFO]
    P3FIN --> P2PROP[Exception propagates to Proc2]
    P2PROP --> P2H{Handler in Proc2?}
    P2H -- "No" --> P2FIN[Finalize Proc2 locals]
    P2FIN --> P1PROP[Exception propagates to Proc1]
    P1PROP --> P1H
    P1H -- "Match" --> P1HANDLER[Execute Proc1's handler for E]
    P1HANDLER --> P1DONE([Proc1 returns to caller])
```

**Narrative walk-through (each arrow mapped to marks):**

1. `Proc1` is the outermost frame. It declares a handler for exception `E`.
2. `Proc1` calls `Proc2`, which in turn calls `Proc3` (2 marks for understanding dynamic chain).
3. `Proc3` executes `raise E;`. Since `Proc3` has *no* handler, control unwinds (2 marks).
4. `Proc2` is searched for a handler for `E`; it has *none* in this scenario, so control unwinds to `Proc1` (2 marks).
5. `Proc1` matches `E` and runs its handler; the program resumes after `Proc1` returns (1 mark for terminator logic).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```ada
with Ada.Text_IO;        use Ada.Text_IO;
with Ada.Exceptions;     use Ada.Exceptions;

procedure Login_Demo is
   Login_Failed : exception;
   Attempts     : Natural := 0;
   Max_Allowed  : constant := 3;
begin
   loop
      Attempts := Attempts + 1;
      Put("Attempt " & Integer'Image(Attempts) & " - press Enter: ");
      Skip_Line;

      if Attempts > Max_Allowed then
         raise Login_Failed with
            "Exceeded " & Integer'Image(Max_Allowed) & " attempts.";
      end if;

      Put_Line("Login attempt recorded.");
   end loop;
exception
   when Login_Failed =>
      Put_Line("Exception Name   : " & Exception_Name(Login_Failed'Identity));
      Put_Line("Exception Message: " & Exception_Message(Login_Failed'Identity));
end Login_Demo;
```

**Step-by-step explanation (partial-credit mapping):**

1. **Lines 1–2** — Importing `Ada.Text_IO` and `Ada.Exceptions` (1 mark).
2. **Line 4** — Declaration of the user-defined exception `Login_Failed` (1 mark).
3. **Lines 5–6** — Local counter and the maximum-allowed threshold (1 mark).
4. **Lines 10–12** — The `if` block raising `Login_Failed` *with* a message — note the keyword `with`, not a function call (2 marks).
5. **Lines 19–20** — Use of `Exception_Name` and `Exception_Message` to print the metadata of the raised exception (2 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 2-mark deduction:** A common mistake is to write `raise Login_Failed("Exceeded attempts")` using function-call syntax. This is **not** valid Ada. The language syntax mandates the keyword `with` between the exception name and the message expression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4):**

- [ ] An **exception** is a *named entity* declared with `exception`; it is a separate kind of declarative item, not a variable.
- [ ] The `raise` statement has **two forms**: `raise <name>;` (initial raise) and `raise;` (re-raise in a handler).
- [ ] Ada 2005+ adds `raise <name> with <string_expression>;` to attach a *message* to the exception occurrence.
- [ ] **Predefined** exceptions (`Constraint_Error`, `Program_Error`, `Storage_Error`, `Tasking_Error`) are raised *implicitly* by the runtime; they are visible everywhere.
- [ ] **User-defined** exceptions must be raised *explicitly* by the programmer.
- [ ] An exception handler is selected by the **first** matching `when` clause in textual order.
- [ ] The `when others` clause must appear **last** in an `exception` part and is **the only catch-all** permitted.
- [ ] If a block has no matching handler, the exception is **implicitly re-raised** to the enclosing scope after LIFO finalization of local controlled objects.
- [ ] Once a handler executes, the block *exits*; control does **not** return to the original raise point.
- [ ] `Ada.Exceptions.Exception_Name(X)` returns the full name (e.g., `LOGIN_FAILED`); `Exception_Message(X)` returns the message string.
- [ ] Exception handling in Ada is part of the **ISO/IEC 8652** language standard — there are no vendor-specific variants in the KTU syllabus.
- [ ] Avoid using `when others` *before* specific handlers; the compiler will reject the construct and KTU valuations will deduct marks for the same conceptual error.
- [ ] `raise;` (parameter-less) is the **only** correct way to re-raise from inside a handler.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT746 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Java

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview — Advanced Java (Module 4)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module 4 - Advanced Java Constructs)**
> Module 4 of *Advanced Computer Programming Techniques (PEBMT746)* deals with the **modern, object-oriented, type-safe, and functional programming features of Java (JDK 8 and above)**. The core pillars covered are: **Generics** (parameterized types for compile-time type safety), the **Collections Framework** (a unified architecture for storing and manipulating groups of objects), **Lambda Expressions** (treating functionality as a method argument), **Functional Interfaces** (single-abstract-method interfaces that enable lambda targets), **Method References** (compact lambda shorthand), the **Stream API** (declarative bulk data processing pipelines), and the **Optional class** (a null-safe container).

These features collectively transform Java from a purely imperative OOP language into a **hybrid object-functional programming paradigm**, enabling concise, parallelizable, and less error-prone code — essential for KTU-aligned industry-grade software development.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Toolbox Upgrade" Viewpoint**
> Imagine Java prior to JDK 8 as a **basic carpenter's toolbox**: it has a hammer, saw, and screwdriver (classes, inheritance, polymorphism) — useful, but every job requires manual, repetitive setup. **Module 4 introduces power tools**:
>
> - **Generics** = A *labeled, sized toolbox*. You specify upfront that this box is "for screws only, M6 size". The compiler enforces this — you cannot shove a wrong-sized screw in, avoiding damage (runtime errors).
> - **Collections Framework** = A *modular shelving system* with standardized drawers (List, Set, Map, Queue). Every drawer has the same interface but specialized internal behavior.
> - **Lambda Expressions** = A *one-line instruction slip* you hand to a worker, instead of writing a full formal memo (anonymous class). The slip says *"do this small task"* and the worker figures it out.
> - **Stream API** = An *assembly-line conveyor belt*. Items go in one end, pass through a series of stations (filter, sort, transform), and a finished product rolls out the other end — without you touching each item manually.
> - **Optional** = A *sealed box that may or may not contain an item*. You must explicitly open it and check before using — preventing the dreaded `NullPointerException` "lemon surprise."

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & High-Yield Constants (Bolded)

| Metric / Constant | Value / Specification |
|---|---|
| **Type Erasure** | Generics exist only at **compile-time**; erased to `Object` (or bound) at runtime |
| **Diamond Operator** | `<>` — introduced in **Java SE 7** for type inference |
| **Lambda Target Type** | Must be a **functional interface** (single abstract method) |
| **Stream Operations** | **Two categories**: *intermediate* (return Stream, lazy) and *terminal* (produce result, eager) |
| **`Optional.empty()` Cardinality** | A singleton representing **absent value** |
| **Default Capacity (ArrayList)** | Initial capacity **10**, grows by **1.5×** when full |

---

## 1.4 Why Module 4 Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: Module 4 constitutes **~20-25% of the End Semester Exam (ESE)** weightage. Questions regularly test (a) Generics syntax and wildcards, (b) Collections hierarchy and selection rationale, (c) Stream pipelines, and (d) lambda-functional interface pairing. Mastering this module is non-negotiable for a high GPA.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar I — Generics (Parameterized Types)

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps

1. **Declaration Phase**: A class/interface declares one or more **type parameters** using angle brackets `<T>`, `<K, V>`, etc.
   - Convention: `T` (Type), `E` (Element), `K` (Key), `V` (Value), `N` (Number).
2. **Invocation Phase**: The client supplies **actual type arguments**, e.g., `Box<Integer>`, locking the box to that type.
3. **Compile-Time Checking**: The compiler inserts **type checks** at every get/put — eliminates `ClassCastException` at runtime.
4. **Type Erasure**: The compiler **erases** generic info post-compilation, replacing `T` with `Object` (or the upper bound). This guarantees **binary compatibility** with pre-generics code.
5. **Bounded Types**: `<T extends Comparable<T>>` restricts allowed types — enables method invocation on the bound type.

### 2.1.2 Wildcard System (Critical for KTU)

| Wildcard | Meaning | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| `<?>` | Unbounded — any type | Reading values of unknown type |
| `<? extends T>` | Upper bounded — T or subtype | **Producer** — you only read |
| `<? super T>` | Lower bounded — T or supertype | **Consumer** — you only write |
| `<T>` | Bounded type parameter | Declaration inside generic class/method |

> **PECS Rule (Producer Extends, Consumer Super)** — memorize for KTU.

---

## 2.2 Pillar II — Collections Framework

### 2.2.1 Hierarchy Logic

- **`Collection<E>`** (root for single-element groups)
  - **`List<E>`** → Ordered, allows duplicates, indexed access
    - `ArrayList`, `LinkedList`, `Vector`, `Stack`
  - **`Set<E>`** → No duplicates
    - `HashSet` (hashed), `LinkedHashSet` (insertion-ordered), `TreeSet` (sorted)
  - **`Queue<E>`** → FIFO, head-access
    - `PriorityQueue`, `ArrayDeque`
- **`Map<K, V>`** (root for key-value pairs — **separate hierarchy**)
  - `HashMap`, `LinkedHashMap`, `TreeMap`, `Hashtable`, `ConcurrentHashMap`

### 2.2.2 Selection Logic (Why & How)

- **Random access heavy** → `ArrayList` (O(1) get, O(n) insert in middle)
- **Insert/delete heavy** → `LinkedList` (O(1) at ends, O(n) random access)
- **Unique elements, fast lookup** → `HashSet` (O(1) avg, O(n) worst)
- **Sorted iteration** → `TreeSet` (O(log n), Red-Black tree backed)
- **Key-value with ordering** → `LinkedHashMap`
- **Thread-safe legacy** → `Hashtable` (avoid in new code)
- **High-concurrency** → `ConcurrentHashMap`

---

## 2.3 Pillar III — Lambda Expressions & Functional Interfaces

### 2.3.1 Functional Interfaces (FI)

A functional interface has **exactly one abstract method** (SAM — Single Abstract Method). It may have default and static methods.

```java
@FunctionalInterface
interface MyCalculator {
    int operate(int a, int b);  // single abstract method
}
```

### 2.3.2 Built-in Functional Interfaces (java.util.function package)

| Interface | Method Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `Predicate<T>` | `boolean test(T t)` | Boolean condition / filter |
| `Function<T, R>` | `R apply(T t)` | Transform T → R |
| `Consumer<T>` | `void accept(T t)` | Consume T, no return |
| `Supplier<T>` | `T get()` | Factory / lazy producer |
| `BiFunction<T,U,R>` | `R apply(T t, U u)` | Two-arg transformation |
| `UnaryOperator<T>` | `T apply(T t)` | Special Function (T→T) |
| `BinaryOperator<T>` | `T apply(T t1, T t2)` | Special BiFunction (T,T→T) |

### 2.3.3 Lambda Syntax Forms

```java
(parameters) -> expression
(parameters) -> { statements; }
() -> expression
x -> x * 2                          // single param, single expr
(a, b) -> a + b                     // multi param, expr body
(String s) -> { System.out.println(s); }  // block body
```

---

## 2.4 Pillar IV — Stream API

### 2.4.1 Pipeline Anatomy

$$ \text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{zero or more intermediate ops}} \text{Stream} \xrightarrow{\text{one terminal op}} \text{Result} $$

- **Source**: Collection, array, I/O channel, generator function.
- **Intermediate ops**: `filter`, `map`, `sorted`, `distinct`, `limit`, `skip`, `flatMap` — return Stream, **lazy** (not executed until terminal).
- **Terminal ops**: `collect`, `forEach`, `reduce`, `count`, `anyMatch`, `allMatch`, `findFirst`, `min`, `max` — trigger execution, return non-Stream result.

### 2.4.2 Key Methods

- `filter(Predicate<T>)` → keeps elements matching predicate
- `map(Function<T,R>)` → transforms each element
- `reduce(BinaryOperator<T>)` → aggregates to single value
- `collect(Collectors.toList())` → mutates into List
- `parallelStream()` → enables parallel execution via ForkJoinPool

---

## 2.5 Pillar V — Optional Class

`Optional<T>` is a **container object** that may or may not contain a non-null value.

| Method | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `Optional.of(value)` | Throws NPE if value is null |
| `Optional.ofNullable(value)` | Allows null; returns empty Optional |
| `isPresent()` | True if value exists |
| `orElse(T other)` | Returns value or default |
| `orElseGet(Supplier)` | Lazy default |
| `map(Function)` | Transform if present |
| `ifPresent(Consumer)` | Execute if present |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Generics** → Used in every collection, custom DAO layer, type-safe REST clients.
- **Collections** → Backbone of caching layers, in-memory queues, dependency graphs.
- **Lambdas** → Event handlers (JavaFX/Swing), comparator definitions, callback mechanisms.
- **Streams** → ETL pipelines, log analytics, bulk DB result processing — expressible in 3 lines vs 15 imperative lines.
- **Optional** → Spring Data JPA `Optional<T>` return types, REST API responses, defensive coding.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| `ArrayList.get(i)` | Direct index | **O(1)** |
| `LinkedList.addFirst/Last` | Pointer manipulation | **O(1)** |
| `LinkedList.get(i)` | Sequential traversal | **O(n)** |
| `HashSet.add(e)` | Hash bucket insert | **O(1)** avg, O(n) worst |
| `TreeSet.add(e)` | Red-Black tree | **O(log n)** |
| `HashMap.get(k)` | Hash bucket lookup | **O(1)** avg, O(n) worst |
| `Stream.filter().count()` | Lazy intermediate + terminal | **O(n)** |
| `ArrayList` growth | `newCap = oldCap + (oldCap >> 1)` | **1.5× factor** |
| `HashMap` capacity | Always power of **2** | — |
| Stream terminal count | Exactly **1 per pipeline** | — |
